text: ## Purpose This runbook describes the operational procedures to deploy, upgrade, roll back, and safely disable the **perf-canary** service across regions. perf-canary is a lightweight, always-on synthetic workload that calls internal inference endpoints for a curated model set and emits performance metrics (p50/p95/p99, tokens/sec, prefill/decode, queueing time, batch stats). The service must stay under the defined overhead budget and must not capture or emit customer data. **Primary users:** Eng-Infra on-call and Release Engineering during rollouts. --- ## Quick reference **Service name:** perf-canary **Deployment mechanism:** - Terraform: service account / IAM / secrets / region wiring - Helm: workload deployment and per-region config **Primary dashboards:** - Grafana: "Perf Canary – Per Model" (p95, tokens/sec, breakdowns) - Capacity/overhead: GPU utilization + canary QPS counters **Kill switch:** control-plane config (disable flag) + Helm scale-to-zero (last resort) **Slack channels:** - #eng-runtime (warnings / runtime attribution) - #eng-oncall (paging / incident coordination) - #eng-releases (rollout sequencing) **Owners:** - Infra/rollout coordination: Vanessa Ortiz - Runtime canary TL: Noah Patel - SRE readiness: Sean Gallagher / Rafael Mendes --- ## Safety and guardrails (must-read) 1) **Overhead budget:** perf-canary must remain below the region budget (target <0.5% of GPU capacity for covered models). If overhead is uncertain, do not expand coverage. 2) **No customer
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: (must-read) 1) **Overhead budget:** perf-canary must remain below the region budget (target <0.5% of GPU capacity for covered models). If overhead is uncertain, do not expand coverage. 2) **No customer data:** only synthetic prompts. perf-canary must not log prompts, user identifiers, request ids from customer traffic, or raw outputs. 3) **Least privilege:** perf-canary uses a restricted service account with inference read access and metrics write access only. 4) **Cardinality control:** metrics must not include high-cardinality labels (prompt id, request id, full model string variants). Use canonical model/version identifiers. If any of the above is violated or suspected, **disable perf-canary immediately** (see “Safe-disable procedures”). --- ## Preconditions Before deploying or upgrading perf-canary in a region, confirm: - You have the correct change window and announcement in #eng-releases (or an approved emergency change). - The control-plane canary config for the target region has been reviewed/validated (model allowlist and thresholds). - Telemetry pipeline is healthy (no known ingestion lag that would delay alerts). - Dashboards exist for the region and links in alert payloads resolve. - Warning alerts are enabled first; paging is enabled only after calibration and sign-off. Required access: - Kubernetes deploy permissions for the region’s perf-canary namespace - Terraform apply permissions for the relevant env (prod) - Ability to view Grafana, logs, and alert routing
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: sign-off. Required access: - Kubernetes deploy permissions for the region’s perf-canary namespace - Terraform apply permissions for the relevant env (prod) - Ability to view Grafana, logs, and alert routing configuration --- ## Deployment topology (conceptual) perf-canary runs as a small Deployment in each region: - Calls internal inference endpoints via restricted auth scope - Uses a seedable synthetic prompt-shape generator - Emits metrics to the standard metrics pipeline (histograms + counters) - Fetches baseline artifacts / config from baseline store and control-plane config Key runtime attribution tags expected in metrics: - runtime_build_id - kernel_selection_id (or kernel version tag) - model_id + model_version - hardware_sku - region --- ## Standard rollout sequence Use this sequence for net-new region enablement or for major upgrades: 1) Deploy/upgrade **with alerts disabled** (metrics only) 2) Validate metrics emission and overhead 3) Enable **warning** alerts 4) Run 3–7 days; tune thresholds if needed 5) Enable **paging** only after SRE/runtime sign-off for top models Avoid simultaneous risky changes: - Do not expand model coverage and change runtime version in the same window. - Do not enable paging on the same day you add a new region. --- ## Procedure A — New region enablement (first-time) ### A1. Confirm regional prerequisites - Verify the region has: - inference endpoints reachable from perf-canary namespace - required secrets provisioned (auth scope token material)
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: enablement (first-time) ### A1. Confirm regional prerequisites - Verify the region has: - inference endpoints reachable from perf-canary namespace - required secrets provisioned (auth scope token material) - dashboards pre-created or templated panels available ### A2. Terraform apply (IAM + secrets wiring) 1) Pull latest main in the infra repo. 2) Confirm the perf-canary service account exists and is scoped correctly. 3) Apply the region module changes. Expected outcomes: - Service account is created/updated - Secrets are provisioned/rotated as needed - Network policies allow egress to inference endpoints and metrics ingestion ### A3. Helm install (metrics-only) 1) Install the perf-canary chart into the region namespace with: - conservative QPS caps - limited model list (2–3 models) - warnings/paging disabled (or alert rules muted) 2) Wait for the Deployment to become Ready. ### A4. Validate Within 30 minutes, confirm: - perf-canary pods are stable (no crash loops) - Metrics appear in Grafana: - latency histograms for e2e and runtime-only - tokens/sec counters - queueing time if enabled - Labels are correct and low-cardinality. - Overhead remains within budget. ### A5. Enable warning alerts - Unmute/enable warning rules for the region and model set. - Confirm incident-bot threads route to #eng-runtime (not paging). ### A6. Post-enable monitoring checklist For the next 24 hours: - Watch for noisy alerts (flapping) and ingestion lag. - Confirm baseline lookups are succeeding (no
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: route to #eng-runtime (not paging). ### A6. Post-enable monitoring checklist For the next 24 hours: - Watch for noisy alerts (flapping) and ingestion lag. - Confirm baseline lookups are succeeding (no “baseline missing” warnings). --- ## Procedure B — Standard upgrade (same region) Examples: perf-canary image bump, config schema update, or dependency update. ### B1. Pre-flight - Identify what is changing: - Image tag only vs chart changes vs config changes - Check if the change affects: - metrics schema/labels - baseline fetch logic - QPS / overhead - Announce upgrade start time in #eng-releases if it could affect alerting noise. ### B2. Upgrade via Helm 1) Confirm current version: - helm list / helm history (region) 2) Apply upgrade: - helm upgrade with the new image tag/chart version 3) Watch rollout: - ensure pods drain/replace cleanly ### B3. Validate after upgrade - Confirm metrics continuity: - no gaps beyond expected rollout window - histogram buckets unchanged unless explicitly approved - Confirm alert payload links still point to correct dashboards/runbooks. - Confirm overhead hasn’t increased. ### B4. Roll forward / stop criteria If any of the following occurs, stop further upgrades and consider rollback: - sustained error rate calling inference endpoints - missing baseline artifacts causing continuous alert noise - overhead increase >20% relative to prior canary overhead in the same region - new high-cardinality labels introduced (metrics volume spike) --- ## Procedure
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: artifacts causing continuous alert noise - overhead increase >20% relative to prior canary overhead in the same region - new high-cardinality labels introduced (metrics volume spike) --- ## Procedure C — Rollback (Helm) Use when a perf-canary deploy/upgrade causes instability, overhead spikes, metrics breakage, or alert storms attributable to canary itself. ### C1. Decide rollback vs disable - If perf-canary is **functioning but noisy**: prefer to mute alerts / adjust config, not rollback. - If perf-canary is **malfunctioning** (crash loop, spamming endpoints, violating safety constraints): rollback immediately. - If safety/privacy is at risk: disable immediately (see “Safe-disable procedures”), then rollback. ### C2. Execute rollback 1) Identify last known good revision: - helm history perf-canary 2) Roll back: - helm rollback perf-canary <REVISION> 3) Watch: - pods become Ready - error logs subside - metrics return to expected shape ### C3. Post-rollback tasks - Confirm alert rules are in the desired state (muted/unmuted). - Update #eng-releases and #eng-runtime with: - what was rolled back - why - whether model coverage or alerting was impacted - File follow-up ticket referencing rollback revision and observed symptoms. --- ## Procedure D — Safe-disable procedures (kill switch) Use to stop canary load quickly without impacting inference endpoints. ### D1. Preferred: disable via control-plane config This is the safest path because it preserves Deployment health while stopping
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: Use to stop canary load quickly without impacting inference endpoints. ### D1. Preferred: disable via control-plane config This is the safest path because it preserves Deployment health while stopping workload generation. 1) Set region enablement to disabled for perf-canary (control-plane flag). 2) Confirm perf-canary acknowledges config refresh (logs/metric indicating “targets=0” or “disabled=true”). 3) Verify: - QPS to inference endpoints drops to ~0 - metrics stop changing except for heartbeat/health ### D2. Secondary: set model list empty / QPS cap to zero If full disable is not available but config is reachable: - Set canary target models to an empty allowlist for that region OR set QPS cap to 0. - Validate the request loop stops. ### D3. Last resort: scale Deployment to zero Use only if config plane is unavailable or the service is misbehaving. 1) kubectl scale deployment/perf-canary --replicas=0 2) Confirm traffic stops (endpoint QPS counters drop). 3) Leave a note in the incident thread explaining why last resort was required. ### D4. After disabling - If this was due to privacy/safety concerns: open a Sev2 incident (minimum) and notify Security & Compliance. - If due to overhead: capture before/after GPU utilization and canary QPS for later tuning. --- ## Verification checklist (what “good” looks like) ### Health - Pods Running/Ready; restarts stable - No sustained inference call errors ### Metrics - E2E latency histogram present - Runtime-only latency histogram present
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: checklist (what “good” looks like) ### Health - Pods Running/Ready; restarts stable - No sustained inference call errors ### Metrics - E2E latency histogram present - Runtime-only latency histogram present (if instrumented) - tokens/sec present - Queueing time present (if enabled in runtime) - kernel/runtime/model version tags present ### Cardinality - Labels limited to: model_id, model_version, region, hardware_sku, runtime_build_id, kernel_selection_id, tier (optional) - No prompt_id, request_id, pod_uid labels ### Overhead - Canary QPS respects configured caps - Estimated GPU overhead within regional budget --- ## Common failure modes and mitigations 1) **Baseline missing / baseline fetch failing** - Symptom: alerts or logs indicate “no baseline” and the evaluator can’t compute regression. - Mitigation: disable alerts for affected model/region; restore baseline store access; verify model_id/model_version match canonical registry identifiers. 2) **Telemetry ingestion lag** - Symptom: alerts fire late; dashboards show step-like delays. - Mitigation: do not tune thresholds based on delayed data; coordinate with Telemetry (Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman) to resolve lag. 3) **Cardinality explosion / metrics cost spike** - Symptom: metrics backend cost/volume alerts; Grafana queries slow. - Mitigation: disable offending metrics emission or rollback; remove high-cardinality labels; follow the metrics cardinality audit guidance. 4) **Overhead too high** - Symptom: GPU utilization rises;
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: - Mitigation: disable offending metrics emission or rollback; remove high-cardinality labels; follow the metrics cardinality audit guidance. 4) **Overhead too high** - Symptom: GPU utilization rises; inference queueing increases correlated with canary. - Mitigation: reduce QPS caps; reduce model coverage; reduce concurrency; consider region-specific schedules. 5) **Alerts flapping** - Symptom: warning/paging repeatedly triggers and resolves. - Mitigation: increase min sample size; extend dampening window; ensure region/SKU-aware thresholds if variance differs. --- ## Escalation and communication - **If customer impact is suspected:** follow the on-call incident process; page SRE on-call and notify #eng-oncall. - **If regression is real (not canary artifact):** engage Serving Runtime owners; include runtime_build_id and kernel_selection_id in the handoff. - **If infra/fleet correlation is suspected:** engage Fleet/Infra; include hardware_sku, region, and recent fleet change ids. In any escalation, include: - Region - Model + model version - Observed vs baseline metrics (p95, tokens/sec) - Sample size + evaluation window - Runtime build id + kernel selection id - Dashboard links and recent deploy/rollout context --- ## Change management notes - Prefer weekday business hours for region expansion and for enabling paging. - Use phased rollout: one region, limited models, warnings first. - Keep a clear revert path: last-known-good Helm revision and the disable switch confirmed. ---
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: region expansion and for enabling paging. - Use phased rollout: one region, limited models, warnings first. - Keep a clear revert path: last-known-good Helm revision and the disable switch confirmed. --- ## Appendix: Minimal rollback decision tree 1) Is perf-canary violating safety/privacy constraints? - Yes → Disable immediately (D1/D3), then rollback (C2). 2) Is perf-canary generating excessive load/overhead? - Yes → Disable (D1/D2). If still present → scale to zero (D3). Then tune and redeploy. 3) Are alerts noisy due to thresholds/baseline drift? - Yes → mute paging, adjust thresholds/min-samples/dampening; avoid rollback unless a code change caused it. 4) Are metrics broken (missing tags/histograms) after upgrade? - Yes → rollback to last known good; validate schema compatibility before retry. --- ## Revision history - 2025-12-18: Initial version for us-east phase-1 rollout. - 2026-02-14: Added control-plane disable path as preferred kill switch; expanded validation and overhead stop criteria.
title: Runbook: Deploy / Upgrade / Roll Back perf-canary (Prod)

text: Summary Overview: This document defines the operational contract (SLOs, quotas, billing guardrails) and the CI/CD lifecycle for the cross-account GPU burst fabric. It is intended for platform engineers, tenant owners, and on-call responders who coordinate cross-account bursting between Redwood-managed GPU pools and partner or tenant cloud accounts. Goals and scope: - Define a minimal, enforceable SLO contract that tenant teams and platform teams can rely on during bursts. - Specify Terraform/IaC module shape and CI/CD checks required for enrolling an account in cross-account bursting. - Provide an operator runbook for common failure modes (burst denied, egress exhaustion, billing disputes). - Describe test, canary, and rollback requirements for rollouts that change allocation or billing behavior. Key definitions: - Burst: short-lived use of extra GPU capacity in another account or region to satisfy a spike. Typically preemptible/spot/borrowed nodes. - Borrower: tenant account consuming burst capacity. - Lender: Redwood-managed account providing burst capacity. - SLO contract: set of measurable targets (latency, success rate, cost cap) enforced by telemetry + circuit breakers. SLO contract (high level): 1) Availability SLO: 99.8% request success for burst path (30d rolling window). 2) Latency SLO: 95th-percentile tail latency for bursted requests must be <= 1.5x local-pool P95 for the same model class. 3) Cost safety cap: each borrower sets a daily burst spend cap; the cross-account
title: Cross-account GPU burst SLO contract and CI lifecycle

text: Latency SLO: 95th-percentile tail latency for bursted requests must be <= 1.5x local-pool P95 for the same model class. 3) Cost safety cap: each borrower sets a daily burst spend cap; the cross-account router enforces hard-stop at cap + 10% buffer. 4) Fairness SLO: lender pool should reserve a minimum of 20% capacity for local tenant commits; cross-account allocator must not exceed 80% burst saturation. SLO measurement signals and owners: - Request success rate: measured with per-route status codes in "burst-router" trace spans. Owner: eng-platform (metrics). - Tail latency: measured by p95/p99 spans, stored in trace-store. Owner: eng-serving-runtime. - Spend cap enforcement: measured by cost-exporter and billing-allocator. Owner: finance+infra. - Pool saturation: measured by allocator telemetry (allocator-exporter). Owner: gpu-fleet-and-capacity. SLO violation responses (automated + manual): - On minor violations (in the 24h window), throttle new burst requests by 30% across all borrowers for 30 minutes and notify the borrower owners. - On repeated violations (>3 in 7 days) escalate to capacity planning and trigger a temporary ban of non-critical bursts for offending borrowers until mitigation. - Persistent fairness violations lead to reconfiguration of tenant priority buckets and invoicing adjustments. Enrolling an account: IaC and CI requirements All borrower accounts must be enrolled via the cross-account IaC module and pass a CI gate before they can request burst capacity.
title: Cross-account GPU burst SLO contract and CI lifecycle

text: invoicing adjustments. Enrolling an account: IaC and CI requirements All borrower accounts must be enrolled via the cross-account IaC module and pass a CI gate before they can request burst capacity. The enrollment pipeline contains three stages: preflight, canary, and promote. Required artifacts: - Terraform module: redwood-gpu-burst-enroll (variables defined below). - IAM template: minimal role with AssumeRole policy and scoped permissions. - Network check manifest: list of allowed egress CIDRs, NAT config, and verification probe. - Cost cap config: daily_limit_usd and emergency_hard_stop flag. Preflight checks (automated): 1) Terraform plan must be clean with no drift. 2) IAM role can be assumed by lender test harness. 3) Egress probe from borrower to lender control plane succeeds (latency < 200ms). 4) Cost cap configured and billing-family contact set. 5) CI-run smoke generation: 100 synthetic requests against a small model to validate end-to-end prefix caching and KV fill. Canary phase (7 days): - Canary scope: 1% of production traffic or synthetic schedules occupying up to 5% of a small lender pool. - Telemetry to monitor: p95 latency, error rate, billing deltas, KV cache miss rate. - Canary acceptance criteria: no SLO violations and cost deltas within +/- 15% forecast. Promote: - After passing canary, account is automatically promoted by CI to full enrollment. Promotion creates a BurstPolicy CR and final Terraform apply in the lending account. CI/CD gating rules (must
title: Cross-account GPU burst SLO contract and CI lifecycle

text: Promote: - After passing canary, account is automatically promoted by CI to full enrollment. Promotion creates a BurstPolicy CR and final Terraform apply in the lending account. CI/CD gating rules (must be enforced in the pipeline): - Terraform plan must include a cost_cap variable. Missing cost caps cause pipeline failure. - Any changes to burst routing or allocator policy require a staged rollout with a mandatory canary pipeline and a signed plan by gpu-fleet owner. - Module updates that change default priority buckets require a migration job runbook and data-backfill pre-approve step. Operator runbook: common failure modes 1) Burst denied / AssumeRole failure: - Symptoms: immediate 403s from lender AssumeRole API, burst requests return 403. - Quick checks: a) Verify IAM role ARN and trust policy is unchanged (terraform state vs remote). b) Check STS assume role logs and recent IAM policy changes. c) Confirm that the borrower has not exceeded daily_limit_usd. - Remedy: i) If IAM trust changed, restore from audit log and reapply terraform. ii) If cost cap exceeded, contact borrower owner to raise cap or wait until reset. iii) If transient STS errors, retry with exponential backoff and alert. 2) Egress exhaustion / NAT saturation: - Symptoms: high packet drops, elevated egress latency, failed health probes. - Quick checks: a) NAT gateway metrics, connection tracking exhaustion, and SNAT port usage. b) Check egress billing spikes for partner account. - Remedy: i) Shift scheduled
title: Cross-account GPU burst SLO contract and CI lifecycle

text: latency, failed health probes. - Quick checks: a) NAT gateway metrics, connection tracking exhaustion, and SNAT port usage. b) Check egress billing spikes for partner account. - Remedy: i) Shift scheduled canaries to alternate egress path (operator toggle). ii) Apply temporary egress shunting policy to limit concurrent burst sessions (rate-limit config). iii) Open a ticket to networking ops for NAT scaling and attach for capacity. 3) Billing reconciliation disputes: - Symptoms: borrower disputes invoice line items tied to burst usage. - Resolution path: 1) Pull per-request cost attribution logs (cost-exporter). 2) If root cause is metered misattribution, replay allocations from allocator-audit logs. 3) Apply credit or invoice adjustment within 3 business days; notify finance and borrower owner. Runbook escalation matrix: - Pager: gpu-fleet-oncall for allocator and pool issues. - Pager: eng-platform on config and CI gating errors. - Slack incident channel: #gpu-cross-account-incidents (primary). - Escalation timeline: 15m initial ack, 1h mitigation plan, 4h customer comms update. Capacity forecasting and billing guardrails: - Weekly forecast cadence: run a ten-day rolling capacity forecast for lender pools using prior 14-day peak metrics + scheduled bursts. - Forecast outputs: expected spare GPU count per region, expected burst revenue, and risk band (low/med/high). - Action thresholds: - If spare GPUs < 25 in a lender pool -> place a soft cap on new canaries for 48 hours. - If
title: Cross-account GPU burst SLO contract and CI lifecycle

text: expected spare GPU count per region, expected burst revenue, and risk band (low/med/high). - Action thresholds: - If spare GPUs < 25 in a lender pool -> place a soft cap on new canaries for 48 hours. - If expected burst revenue delta > 20% month-over-month -> schedule a capacity planning sync. Testing, canaries and chaos simulation: - Synthetic storm harness: used in CI to simulate 2x traffic spikes to validate allocator throttles and cost caps. - Chaos tests to run quarterly: IAM revocation simulation, NAT failure, allocator leader failover. - Required test artifacts committed with any IaC change: plan summary, smoke logs, and canary dashboard link. Rollout checklist (pre-promotion): 1) IaC module version pinned and reviewed. 2) Cost cap and billing contact set. 3) Canary plan documented with duration and telemetry thresholds. 4) Runbook updated with ownership and runbook play IDs. 5) Observability dashboards and alerts defined and attached to the CI run. Appendix A — Example Terraform module variables (excerpt): ```hcl variable "borrower_account_id" { type = string description = "Target account id for borrower" } variable "daily_limit_usd" { type = number description = "Hard stop for borrower burst spend per day (USD)" } variable "emergency_hard_stop" { type = bool default = false description = "If true, lender will refuse all new bursts immediately" } output "burst_policy_cr" { value = local.policy_cr } ``` Appendix B — Minimal IAM trust policy (description): - Role: redwood-burst-borrower-assume-role
title: Cross-account GPU burst SLO contract and CI lifecycle

text: lender will refuse all new bursts immediately" } output "burst_policy_cr" { value = local.policy_cr } ``` Appendix B — Minimal IAM trust policy (description): - Role: redwood-burst-borrower-assume-role - Trust scope: lender-control-plane-principal only. - Permission scope: STS:AssumeRole, logs:PutLogEvents (only for audit), ec2:DescribeInstances (read-only), monitoring:GetMetricData. Appendix C — Example monitoring table: | Signal | Source | Owner | Alert threshold | Response play | |---|---|---:|---|---| |Burst request failures|burst-router|eng-platform|>1% error rate over 10m|Throttle + notify borrower| |Burst P95 latency|traces|eng-serving-runtime|>1.5x local-p95|Canary rollback + investigate| Related artifacts and links: - Enrollment IaC module: repo://infra/modules/redwood-gpu-burst-enroll - Canary dashboard: dashboards/gpu/burst-canary (internal) - Allocator audit logs location: s3://redwood-allocator-audit/ Ownership and maintenance: - Primary owners: gpu-fleet-and-capacity (policy & allocator), eng-platform (CI + metrics), cloud-accounts-and-networking (egress & NAT). - Quarterly review: owners must review SLO thresholds and cost caps each quarter and publish changes to this doc. Revision history: - 2024-02-15: Initial draft by Marcus Chen. - 2024-06-12: Added canary requirements and cost cap enforcement (reviewers: Priya Iyer). - 2025-11-02: Formalized escalation timeline; updated IAM recommendations (reviewers: Elena Gomez). If you are a borrower owner preparing to

text: Overview Purpose: This playbook documents the recommended coordination model, validation checks, and runbook steps for engaging third-party vendors and external suppliers during an enterprise customer onboarding at Redwood Inference. It covers technical handoffs (SSO, SCIM, KMS), network coupling (VPC peering / firewall rules), telemetry & cost contracts, compliance artifacts, and cross-team escalation flows. Audience: - Customer Success Managers (CSMs) - Solutions Engineers and Onboarding Engineers - Security & Compliance reviewers - SRE/Platform contacts - Third-party vendor leads and integrators Scope and Assumptions: - Applies to Dedicated and Private deployments where external parties require access or service integration. - Assumes an executed contract that approves vendor access scope and data classification. - Does not replace vendor security questionnaires or legal NDAs; those must be completed pre-engagement. Key SLA / Acceptance Criteria (customer-facing): - End-to-end onboarding runway target: 6–8 weeks for standard integrations. - Security gate acceptance: all required artifacts provided and verified within 10 business days of evidence request. - Telemetry completeness: 95% of required ingestion metrics flowing into the tenant’s monitoring workspace at canary. - Post-cutover error rate: <1% p95 error rate for tenant API calls over first 72 hours. Roles & Responsibilities (quick reference): - Customer Success (Owner): program management, stakeholder comms, acceptance
title: Third-Party & Vendor Coordination Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: Post-cutover error rate: <1% p95 error rate for tenant API calls over first 72 hours. Roles & Responsibilities (quick reference): - Customer Success (Owner): program management, stakeholder comms, acceptance criteria signoff. - Onboarding Eng / Solutions Eng: integration templates, test harnesses, runbook execution. - Security & Compliance: artifact review, risk acceptance, access reviews. - SRE / Platform: networking (peering, firewall), ingress tuning, throttles. - Vendor / Third Party: provide integration adapters, required artifacts, point-of-contact for remedial work. Pre-Onboarding Checklist (must be completed before technical work begins): 1) Contract & Scope - Confirm contract includes vendor access, data handling terms, and SLAs. - Confirm approved data classification for the integration (PII / Sensitive / Internal). 2) Primary Contacts & RACI - Collect primary POC, backup, and escalation contacts (name, role, email, mobile). - Share RACI matrix to all parties. 3) Access Requests - Vendor must submit an access request ticket in our onboarding tracker with required details: IP ranges, hostnames, SSH keys/public certs, expected egress, and data types. 4) Security Artifacts (deliverables) - Vendor SOC2 Type II or equivalent attestation (if applicable). - Data processing agreement (DPA) and required clauses executed. - Pen test report or summary (last 12 months) or vendor self-attestation. 5) Legal & Procurement signoff documented in the contract binder. Integration Validation
title: Third-Party & Vendor Coordination Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: (DPA) and required clauses executed. - Pen test report or summary (last 12 months) or vendor self-attestation. 5) Legal & Procurement signoff documented in the contract binder. Integration Validation Matrix (examples) | Integration Type | Minimum Validation Steps | Owner | Pass Criteria | |------------------|--------------------------|-------|---------------| | SSO / SAML / OIDC | Exchange metadata, test auth flow, map roles | Onboarding Eng | Successful login, role mapping test users | | SCIM provisioning | Provision test user, de-provision, attribute mapping | Solutions Eng | Provisioned user appears in tenant, de-provision removes user | | VPC peering / Firewall | Establish peering, verify routes, egress checks | SRE/Platform | 3x pings, TCP port checks, data path validated via traceroute | | KMS integration | Key rotation policy, test encrypt/decrypt | Security | Keys accessible with least privilege, decrypt success | | Event webhooks / Ingest | Replay test events, backpressure handling | Vendor | 99% of events accepted in test window, retry semantics validated | Detailed Runbook: Cutover Phases (recommended) 1. Preparation Phase (Runway weeks -4 to -1) - Week -4: Kickoff meeting with vendor + CSM + Onboarding Eng + Security. Share timelines and acceptance criteria. - Week -3: Deliver artifacts (SOC2, DPA, IP ranges). Security begins evidence review and schedules any deep-dive if needed. - Week -2: Test environment integration. Establish telemetry streaming (metrics + traces)
title: Third-Party & Vendor Coordination Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: Deliver artifacts (SOC2, DPA, IP ranges). Security begins evidence review and schedules any deep-dive if needed. - Week -2: Test environment integration. Establish telemetry streaming (metrics + traces) to the customer’s observability workspace. Validate basic SSO logins and SCIM provisioning for 2 test accounts. - Week -1: Dry-run of cutover steps in staging. Run synthetic traffic (at scaled-down level) for 24 hours. Capture telemetry baseline. 2. Canary Cutover (day 0) - Start small: route 2–5% of production traffic (or a small tenant slice) to the new integration. - Monitor these signals in real-time: 1) request latency p95 < target (customer-specific; default target 300ms), 2) error rate <1% p95, 3) token consumption / cost anomalies < +15% of baseline. - Hold for 60–120 minutes of steady state. If thresholds breach, execute rollback procedure (see below). 3. Gradual Ramp (day 0–3) - 25% → 50% → 100% increments with 30–60 minute steadiness windows between steps. - Re-run provisioning sanity checks and verify no CVE or credential exposure alerts. 4. Post-Cutover Stabilization (day 3–14) - Run a focused diagnostics sweep at 24h, 72h, and day 14. Verify: billing signals, latency, error rates, and telemetry coverage. - Security to re-run access review and close temporary access granted for cutover. Rollback Procedure (abbreviated) 1) Immediate trigger conditions: - sustained error rate > 2% p95 for 10 consecutive minutes, - latency p95 > 2x baseline for 10 consecutive minutes,
title: Third-Party & Vendor Coordination Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: granted for cutover. Rollback Procedure (abbreviated) 1) Immediate trigger conditions: - sustained error rate > 2% p95 for 10 consecutive minutes, - latency p95 > 2x baseline for 10 consecutive minutes, - evidence of data leakage or unauthorized access. 2) Actions: 1. CSM alerts stakeholders and opens an incident in the tracker. 2. Onboarding Eng toggles routing to previous integration (or blocks vendor egress IPs) via routing console. 3. SRE verifies route rollback and runs smoke tests. 4. Security initiates a focused log and access audit. Escalation Matrix (primary fast path) - Level 1 (On-call Onboarding Eng): 30 min response, primary contact Marco Nguyen (eng pager). - Level 2 (SRE): 60 min response, primary contact SRE rotation (sre-oncall@redwood.ai). - Level 3 (Security): 2 hour response for evidence review and containment, primary contact Evelyn Park. - Executive notification: if customer impact > 2 hours or SLA credit likely. Telemetry & Observability Contract (minimum requirements) - Metrics: ingest_rate, request_latency_ms.p50/p95/p99, request_error_rate, kv_cache_hit_ratio, token_consumption_per_request. - Traces: sampled end-to-end traces for 0.1% of requests with correlation_id. - Logs: structured JSON logs with fields (timestamp, request_id, tenant_id, route, status_code, latency_ms). - Alerting: on error rate > 1% p95 sustained 5m; on latency p95 > 2x baseline sustained 5m. - Cost telemetry: daily token consumption and projected 7-day burn rate. Security & Compliance
title: Third-Party & Vendor Coordination Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: latency_ms). - Alerting: on error rate > 1% p95 sustained 5m; on latency p95 > 2x baseline sustained 5m. - Cost telemetry: daily token consumption and projected 7-day burn rate. Security & Compliance Gate Checklist (Required Evidence) - Vendor attestation: SOC2 or equivalent (pdf). - DPA signed and stored in Contract binder (link to doc repository). - Network diagram showing peering and ingress/egress flow. - Access list: issued credentials, service accounts, and scope with TTL and approval stamps. - Key management proof: KMS integration docs and sample encrypt/decrypt workflow. Operational Templates (snippets) - Sample SSH key request text: "Please provide the vendor public key (ED25519) for on-call access. Key will be uploaded to the onboarding vault with a 30-day TTL; renewal required after expiration" - Minimal SCIM provisioning test payload (example): { "schemas": ["urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:User"], "userName": "test.vendor@example.com", "name": {"givenName": "Test", "familyName": "Vendor"} } - Telemetry validation curl (example): curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <TEST-API-KEY>" https://ingest.redwood-internal.example/tenants/<TENANT>/metrics/status Acceptance & Post-Go-Live Tasks - Day 1: Confirm telemetry completeness and backfill any missing events. - Day 3: Run customer-facing functionality smoke tests and collect signoff from Product/Customer. - Day 7: Security completes access review; revoke temporary emergency credentials. - Day 14: CSM prepares QBR inputs:
title: Third-Party & Vendor Coordination Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: customer-facing functionality smoke tests and collect signoff from Product/Customer. - Day 7: Security completes access review; revoke temporary emergency credentials. - Day 14: CSM prepares QBR inputs: onboarding timeline, integration quality metrics, cost delta, and open action items. Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) Q: What if the vendor cannot provide SOC2? A: We accept equivalent attestations (ISO 27001) or an agreed compensating control plan with a documented timeline and Security risk acceptance. Q: Who owns the cost delta introduced by vendor traffic? A: Cost responsibility is contractually defined. During cutover, we enable cost-guardrails and communicate projected overages. CSMs must escalate to finance if projected burn exceeds 15% of baseline. Appendix: Links & Templates - Onboarding tracker template: /ops/onboarding/tracker/template - Contract binder (legal): /legal/contracts/vendor-binder - Security evidence upload: /secure/uploads/vendor-evidence - Observability handbook: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/observability-handbook Contact & Ownership - Playbook owner: Aisha Kapoor (CSM) — aisha.kapoor@redwood.ai - Onboarding eng lead: Marco Nguyen — marco.nguyen@redwood.ai - Security reviewer: Evelyn Park — evelyn.park@redwood.ai Change log: - 2026-03-15: Created initial draft. - 2026-07-20: Added telemetry contract and sample SCIM payload. - 2026-11-02: Hardened rollback criteria and added restricted confidentiality classification.
title: Third-Party & Vendor Coordination Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: ## Purpose This page defines the **canonical step catalog** for the “Guided onboarding + First Production Launch” checklist. This template is the **single source of truth** for: - Step IDs (stable identifiers used by Console + progress service + telemetry) - Step ordering, dependencies, and completion criteria - Deep links in the Redwood Console - Canonical docs + quickstart/example links (with language parity expectations) This template is used in: - **Redwood Console** checklist UI - **Docs** (mirrors the same steps and order) - **Support playbooks** (triage by step state) --- ## Template versioning and change control **Template version:** 1.0 **Rules** 1. **Step IDs are immutable.** Do not reuse an ID for a different meaning. 2. Any change that affects completion logic (criteria or event mapping) requires review from: - DevEx (Docs/Quickstarts): Wei Chen / Elliot Price - Console PM/Eng: Marissa Cole / Dylan Brooks - Telemetry: Logan Wright - Support enablement: Dev Patel 3. Backward compatibility: existing tenants’ progress must not regress due to template edits. **Update cadence** - Minor copy/link changes: anytime (no code changes required). - Criteria/event changes: batched; target 1–2 releases/month. --- ## Definitions - **TTFS (Time to First Success):** elapsed time from tenant creation → first successful (2xx) Hosted API response for a supported endpoint. - **TTFP (Time to First Production Traffic):** elapsed time from tenant creation → first request classified as production
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: creation → first successful (2xx) Hosted API response for a supported endpoint. - **TTFP (Time to First Production Traffic):** elapsed time from tenant creation → first request classified as production traffic per telemetry rules. **Checklist step states** - Not started: no evidence yet - In progress: partial evidence (where applicable) - Completed: acceptance criteria met - Blocked: step cannot complete due to detected error state (Console may show guidance) --- ## Supported environments (MVP) This template is authored for **Hosted API** onboarding (self-serve and sales-assisted). Dedicated/Private steps will be added later. Supported language tracks for “Quickstart/Examples” links: - Python - TypeScript/Node - Go - cURL Parity expectation: each step should have at least one canonical docs URL and at least two language tracks with working code samples by GA. --- ## Canonical step catalog (ordered) ### Step 1 — Create an API key **Step ID:** ONB_API_KEY_CREATED **Goal:** Ensure the developer has a valid key and knows where to rotate/revoke it. **Acceptance criteria (completion):** - At least one active API key exists for the tenant in the Console. **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/settings/api-keys **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/api-keys - Quickstart (cURL): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl **Common pitfalls to address in step detail UI:** - Using the wrong environment variable name - Copying a key
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/api-keys - Quickstart (cURL): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#curl **Common pitfalls to address in step detail UI:** - Using the wrong environment variable name - Copying a key with extra whitespace - Using an org admin key in a restricted project context **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: api_key.created (source=console) --- ### Step 2 — Configure environment (SDK + env vars) **Step ID:** ONB_ENV_CONFIGURED **Goal:** Ensure the key is stored safely and the SDK can authenticate reliably. **Acceptance criteria:** - Tenant has at least one successful authenticated request (2xx) OR explicit verification event from Console “Test connection”. **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/quickstart **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/authentication#environment-variables - Quickstart (Python): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#python - Quickstart (TypeScript): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#typescript - Quickstart (Go): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/first-call#go **Notes:** - Step detail should recommend using a secrets manager for production deployments. **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: auth.success (first occurrence) - Optional client hint: sdk.initialized (no secrets; SDK name/version only) --- ### Step 3 — Make your first successful request (non-streaming) **Step
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: Server-side event: auth.success (first occurrence) - Optional client hint: sdk.initialized (no secrets; SDK name/version only) --- ### Step 3 — Make your first successful request (non-streaming) **Step ID:** ONB_FIRST_2XX_REQUEST **Goal:** Achieve TTFS and validate basic request/response. **Acceptance criteria:** - First 2xx response from one of: /v1/chat/completions, /v1/responses, /v1/embeddings, /v1/rerank (Hosted API). **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/requests (filtered to last 15 minutes) **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/first-request - Example (TypeScript): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/first-request-ts) - Example (Python): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/first-request-py) **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: request.2xx.first --- ### Step 4 — Try streaming (and handle disconnects) **Step ID:** ONB_STREAMING_ESTABLISHED **Goal:** Validate streaming works end-to-end and the client behaves correctly on disconnect. **Acceptance criteria:** - At least one streaming response established (server confirms stream opened and at least one token chunk delivered). **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/streaming **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming - Troubleshooting: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming#disconnects - Example (prod-ready): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming - Troubleshooting: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/streaming#disconnects - Example (prod-ready): https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-examples (folder: examples/streaming-chat-prod-ready) **Implementation guidance to surface in step detail:** - Recommend read timeouts appropriate for long generations - Recommend reconnect pattern (resume vs retry) and how to avoid duplicate side effects **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: streaming.opened.first - Server-side event: streaming.chunk.sent.first --- ### Step 5 — Set production-safe timeouts and retries **Step ID:** ONB_RETRIES_TIMEOUTS_CONFIGURED **Goal:** Prevent common early-production failures (hanging requests, spiky error rates, retry storms). **Acceptance criteria:** - Tenant has generated at least one request using an SDK client configured with recommended presets OR Console “Prod defaults acknowledged” action (for non-SDK use cases). **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/developer/production-defaults **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/production-readiness/timeouts-retries - Python SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/python#retries-timeouts - TypeScript SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/typescript#retries-timeouts - Go SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/go#retries-timeouts **Recommended defaults (for docs + SDK presets):** - Connect timeout: 2–5s - Overall
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/typescript#retries-timeouts - Go SDK guide: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/sdk/go#retries-timeouts **Recommended defaults (for docs + SDK presets):** - Connect timeout: 2–5s - Overall request timeout: 30–90s (non-streaming); streaming uses read timeout/keepalive guidance - Retry policy: exponential backoff with jitter; retry on 429/408/409/5xx where safe - Max attempts: 3–5 (cap total retry time) **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Client hint (preferred): sdk.retry_policy.enabled (no config values, only preset name) - Server-side fallback: request.retry_observed (inferred; less reliable) --- ### Step 6 — Review usage and latency in the Console **Step ID:** ONB_USAGE_DASHBOARD_VIEWED **Goal:** Ensure the user knows where to see token usage, latency, and errors; reduce “usage dashboard mismatch” tickets. **Acceptance criteria:** - User visits usage dashboard page OR API call to /usage summary is made. **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/usage/overview **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/usage-dashboard - Notes on data freshness: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing#data-freshness **Copy requirements (Console step detail):** - Clearly state expected ingestion delay (e.g., “Usage may take up to ~5 minutes to fully reflect in dashboards.”) **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: console.page_view (page=usage_overview) --- ### Step 7 — Configure
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: delay (e.g., “Usage may take up to ~5 minutes to fully reflect in dashboards.”) **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: console.page_view (page=usage_overview) --- ### Step 7 — Configure quotas and budget alerts **Step ID:** ONB_QUOTAS_BUDGETS_SET **Goal:** Prevent surprise spend and reduce 429 confusion by making limits explicit. **Acceptance criteria:** - Tenant has set at least one of: - Monthly budget alert threshold, OR - Per-minute request/token limit policy, OR - Project-level quota override **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/settings/quotas-budgets **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/usage-and-billing/quotas-budgets - Terraform snippet: https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-terraform (path: examples/budget-alerts) **Guidance to surface in step detail:** - Explain how 429s work (quota vs rate limit vs concurrency) - Recommend adding client-side backoff and server-side circuit-breakers **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: quotas.updated - Server-side event: budgets.updated --- ### Step 8 — Pin a model version (avoid surprise changes) **Step ID:** ONB_MODEL_PINNED **Goal:** Stabilize production behavior by pinning a model and (where applicable) a revision. **Acceptance criteria:** - Tenant has set a default model for the project and pinned to a specific version/revision OR explicitly acknowledged “floating model” risk. **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/models (then “Set default”)
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: set a default model for the project and pinned to a specific version/revision OR explicitly acknowledged “floating model” risk. **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/models (then “Set default”) **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/models/model-versioning - Quickstart section: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#model-pinning **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: model.default_set - Server-side event: model.version_pinned --- ### Step 9 — Configure fallback policy (reliability under load) **Step ID:** ONB_FALLBACK_POLICY_CONFIGURED **Goal:** Ensure the application stays online during capacity constraints or model-specific incidents. **Acceptance criteria:** - A fallback policy exists for at least one route (same family or compatible variant), OR the tenant acknowledges “no fallback” for the project. **Recommended Console deep link:** /console/routing/fallbacks **Docs / quickstarts:** - Docs: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/guides/reliability/fallbacks - Quickstart section: https://docs.redwoodinference.com/quickstarts/production-readiness#fallback-policy **Telemetry / progress evidence:** - Server-side event: routing.fallback_policy.updated --- ### Step 10 — Add basic observability hooks (request IDs + tracing) **Step ID:** ONB_OBSERVABILITY_ENABLED **Goal:** Make debugging first production incidents fast (correlate customer logs ↔ Redwood request IDs, add traces/metrics). **Acceptance
title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)

text: Overview This playbook defines the technical and operational runways for an enterprise customer go-live and the stabilization activities leading up to the end of week three post-cutover. It is intended for Customer Success Engineers (CSEs), Solutions Engineering, SRE, and the customer's technical stakeholders. The goal is to ensure predictable availability, cost controls, and measurable quality during the high-risk window immediately after go-live. Scope - Applies to Dedicated and Private deployment modes and hosted customers with enterprise SLAs. - Covers pre-go-live readiness, day-of execution, and week-1 through week-3 stabilization. - Excludes long-term feature rollouts (see related roadmap/feature-release pages). Quick summary of ownership - CSE (Primary runbook owner): coordinates customer comms, activation gating, and weekly status. - SRE: infra monitoring, incident response, capacity adjustments. - Solutions Engineering: test harness, integration verification, and debug. - Customer Engineering Lead: on-site or designated technical contact for approvals. Pre-Go-Live (T minus 14 to T minus 1 days) — readiness gate checklist 1) Technical validation (owners: SolutionsEng, CustomerEng) a) End-to-end smoke test passes using production-like traffic profile (see test matrix). b) Authentication and network connectivity validated (IDs, firewall rules, VPC peering). c) Model variant pinning and fallback policy confirmed. 2) Capacity & performance (owners: SRE, InfraOps) - Reserved
title: Go-Live Runway and Week-3 Stability Playbook

text: Authentication and network connectivity validated (IDs, firewall rules, VPC peering). c) Model variant pinning and fallback policy confirmed. 2) Capacity & performance (owners: SRE, InfraOps) - Reserved capacity validated for peak QPS; target headroom >= 30%. - Batching and KV cache configuration reviewed against expected median sequence length. - Latency SLO target listed: p95 <= 250ms for single-token probes; p99 <= 800ms. 3) Cost controls and quotas (owners: Finance, CSE) - Per-route quota set for first 72 hours: default 50% of expected peak to avoid runaway costs. - Alerting on daily cost burn rate > 150% of forecast. 4) Observability & alerting (owners: SRE, Observability) - Traces, metrics, logs are routed to central stack; sampling rules verified. - Key dashboards created: tokens/day, tokens/second, error-rate, avg-latency-by-model. Pre-Go-Live checklist table (copy/paste to customer kickoff) | Area | Criteria | Owner | Pass/Fail | |------|---------|-------|----------| | Network | VPC peering, IP allowlists configured | InfraOps | ____ | | Auth | API key exchange and rotation verified | CustomerEng | ____ | | Runtime | Model pin and fallback configured | SolutionsEng | ____ | | Observability | Dashboards live + alerting enabled | SRE | ____ | | Billing | Quotas + budget alerts set | Finance/CSE | ____ | Day-of Go-Live — hour-by-hour run of show (owners annotated) - T-2h: Final smoke tests (SolutionsEng) — run 3 synthetic scenarios at production rate. - T-1h: Enable throttling
title: Go-Live Runway and Week-3 Stability Playbook

text: set | Finance/CSE | ____ | Day-of Go-Live — hour-by-hour run of show (owners annotated) - T-2h: Final smoke tests (SolutionsEng) — run 3 synthetic scenarios at production rate. - T-1h: Enable throttling and quotas; confirm monitoring ingestion (SRE). - T-15m: Stakeholder sync in #cust-<customer>-go-live Slack channel (CSE). - T+0: Switch traffic per agreed plan (blue/green or DNS cutover) (InfraOps + CustomerEng). - T+15m: Run quick sanity probes (latency, error-rate, 10 sample user sessions) (SolutionsEng). - T+60m: First-hour stabilization review — share dashboards and any rate anomalies (All). Immediate post-go-live checks (first 72 hours) - Checkpoint cadence: 15m, 1h, 4h, 12h, 24h, then every 24h until day 7. - Probing script (run as CI job or adhoc): curl -sS -X POST https://api.redwood.example/v1/generate -H 'Authorization: Bearer <KEY>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"rwd-3b-infer","input":"healthcheck prompt"}' | jq '.status, .latency' - What to watch: sudden jump in token generation time, p50->p95 increase > 3x, rising error-rate > 1%. Week 1 stabilization (days 1–7) - Focus: eliminate recurring errors, tune batching and cache settings, tighten cost controls. - Tasks: - SRE: Set adaptive sampling for traces to capture errors at p99. - SolutionsEng: Increase KV cache TTL for repeated prefixes if cache-miss ratio > 40%. - CSE: Run a QBR-style health call on day 3 with customer technical leadership. Week 2 stabilization (days 8–14) - Focus: performance
title: Go-Live Runway and Week-3 Stability Playbook

text: Increase KV cache TTL for repeated prefixes if cache-miss ratio > 40%. - CSE: Run a QBR-style health call on day 3 with customer technical leadership. Week 2 stabilization (days 8–14) - Focus: performance tuning, model quality checks, and rollback readiness. - Tasks: - Run model quality spot checks against customer prompt set (10–50 prompts). - Revisit autoscaling policies — target concurrency headroom and warm-up thresholds. - Finance: Convert provisional quotas to target production quotas where safe. Week 3 stability & final handback (days 15–21) - Focus: operational handback to BAU and reduced monitoring cadence. Criteria to move to BAU: 1) Error-rate <= 0.3% for 7 consecutive days. 2) Latency SLOs met p95 <= 250ms and p99 <= 800ms for 7 consecutive days. 3) Cost variance <= +/- 20% of forecast for 7 consecutive days. 4) No active escalations in last 72 hours. - If all criteria met, schedule BAU transition meeting and update owners to default rotations. Escalation matrix and rapid contacts (use in incident communication) - Level 1 (Customer-facing): CSE on-call — Slack: @cse-primary, Email: cse-primary@redwood.ai, PagerDuty: PD-1234. - Level 2 (SRE / Infra): SRE on-call — Slack: #sre-oncall, PagerDuty: PD-2001. - Level 3 (Engineering / Runtime): Runtime eng lead — PagerDuty: PD-3004, Phone: +1-415-555-0167. Escalation steps (numbered) 1) Triage in Slack channel and attach initial triage template (incident type, impact, first seen). 2) If impact > P1 (complete customer outage)
title: Go-Live Runway and Week-3 Stability Playbook

text: PD-3004, Phone: +1-415-555-0167. Escalation steps (numbered) 1) Triage in Slack channel and attach initial triage template (incident type, impact, first seen). 2) If impact > P1 (complete customer outage) escalate to Level 2 within 10 minutes. 3) For persistent degradation beyond 30 minutes, call Level 3 and create incident bridge. Sample incident triage template to paste into channel: --- Incident type: (outage / degraded / cost-anomaly) First observed: (timestamp UTC) Impacted routes/models: (list) Customer impact: (all traffic / subset / background jobs) Current mitigation attempted: (quarantine model / reduce QPS / fallback variant) Next actions: (owner + ETA) --- Operational playbook snippets 1) Temporarily reduce model concurrency via admin API: curl -X POST https://console.redwood.example/api/admin/limit -H 'Authorization: Bearer <ADMIN_KEY>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"customer_id":"cust-1234","model":"rwd-3b-infer","concurrency_limit":10}' 2) Enable fallback to smaller model variant (for graceful degradation): PATCH /v1/customers/cust-1234/policies {"fallback_policy":"rwd-1b-infer"} Telemetry and queries (examples you can run) - PromQL: error-rate across customer routes (1m): sum(rate(redwood_requests_failed_total{customer="cust-1234"}[1m])) / sum(rate(redwood_requests_total{customer="cust-1234"}[1m])) - SQL (usage billing table): SELECT day, SUM(tokens) as tokens, SUM(cost) as cost FROM billing.usage WHERE customer_id='cust-1234' GROUP BY day ORDER BY
title: Go-Live Runway and Week-3 Stability Playbook

text: sum(rate(redwood_requests_total{customer="cust-1234"}[1m])) - SQL (usage billing table): SELECT day, SUM(tokens) as tokens, SUM(cost) as cost FROM billing.usage WHERE customer_id='cust-1234' GROUP BY day ORDER BY day DESC LIMIT 14; - Synthetic probe example (k6 script snippet): import http from 'k6/http'; export default function () { const res = http.post('https://api.redwood.example/v1/generate', JSON.stringify({model:'rwd-3b-infer',input:'healthcheck'}), {headers:{Authorization:'Bearer ${__ENV.API_KEY}', 'Content-Type':'application/json'}}); check(res, {'status is 200': (r) => r.status === 200}); } Rollback plan (when to trigger and steps) - Trigger criteria: - Unrecoverable p99 latency > 2x target for 2 hours and mitigation fails. - Persistent error-rate > 5% for 1 hour despite scaling and throttling. - Regressions in model quality affecting customer’s business metrics. - Rollback steps (owners annotated): 1) Notify stakeholders and open an incident bridge (CSE). 2) Switch traffic to previous deployment (InfraOps) using blue/green or model pin rollback. 3) Verify successful rollback via synthetic probes (SolutionsEng). 4) Perform postmortem and remediation plan within 72 hours (SRE + SolutionsEng). Release sign-off (checklist before BAU handoff) - All pre-go-live checklist items are green. - No P1/P0 incidents in the last 72 hours. - Customer acceptance test signed by Customer Engineering Lead. - Finance confirms quotas and billing alerts are in place. Postmortem and learning
title: Go-Live Runway and Week-3 Stability Playbook

text: are green. - No P1/P0 incidents in the last 72 hours. - Customer acceptance test signed by Customer Engineering Lead. - Finance confirms quotas and billing alerts are in place. Postmortem and learning capture - For any incident with customer impact greater than 1 hour or cost > $5k unexpected, open a postmortem in the incident channel and publish within 7 business days. Include root cause, timeline, action items, and owner for each action. Appendix: common troubleshooting commands and config snippets - Check active model variants for customer: GET /v1/customers/cust-1234/config - Example model cache config YAML: cache: enabled: true ttl_seconds: 3600 max_keys: 250000 - Typical autoscaler min/max for dedicated pool (example): min_replicas: 2 max_replicas: 12 target_utilization: 0.7 FAQ Q: How long should we keep quotas conservative after go-live? A: Conservative quotas are recommended for the first 72 hours. If metrics are stable, move to target quotas in week 2. Q: Can we change model variants after go-live? A: Yes, but treat model changes as a deployment. Validate in staging or use canary rollouts. Related pages and templates - Customer-specific runbooks, integration test harness, and billing forecast template (links available in the related_pages list). Document maintenance - Owner: CSE Runbooks (Ava Martinez). Update cadence: after each enterprise go-live or quarterly.
title: Go-Live Runway and Week-3 Stability Playbook

text: Title: Federated Enterprise Onboarding Playbook Overview: This playbook outlines the recommended onboarding process for enterprise customers adopting Redwood Inference in federated deployment modes (Dedicated and Private). It covers pre-sales handoff, technical intake, network and security gating, initial runbook and health checks, first 90‑day success metrics, escalation paths, and QBR-ready artifacts. The intention is to standardize onboarding for customers who require private control plane, VPC peering, or on‑prem packaging while preserving predictable SLAs and operational readiness. Goals: - Establish production-ready deployment within 4–8 weeks for standard enterprise patterns. - Verify SLO alignment: 99.9% availability for control plane; 95th‑pct latency SLOs documented per workload. - Ensure secure connectivity, auditing, and access controls before traffic cutover. - Provide documented runbooks and monitoring for customer-run teams and Redwood support. Audience: - Customer Success Manager (CSM) - Solutions Engineer (SE) / Solutions Architect (SA) - Customer platform / SRE contacts - Redwood Onboarding Engineers and Support Pre-Onboarding Checklist (to complete before kickoff): 1. Signed contract with deployment mode (Dedicated / Private / On‑prem) and capacity commitments. 2. Primary technical contact + secondary contact + Slack/Teams escalation channel. 3. Customer firewall / network point of contact and IP ranges for allowlist. 4. Confirmation of identity provider (IdP)
title: Federated Enterprise Onboarding Playbook

text: Primary technical contact + secondary contact + Slack/Teams escalation channel. 3. Customer firewall / network point of contact and IP ranges for allowlist. 4. Confirmation of identity provider (IdP) for SSO (SAML/OIDC) and required attributes. 5. KMS/HSM ownership model and key lifecycle policy (if using customer-managed keys). 6. Data residency and retention requirements documented. 7. Initial expected token throughput and peak concurrency estimates. Kickoff Agenda (1-hour): - Introductions and roles - High-level architecture and deployment variant - Security and compliance signoffs required - Network connectivity plan and timelines - SSO and provisioning plan (SCIM) - Rollout plan: pilot -> canary -> cutover - Success metrics and QBR cadence (monthly first quarter) Technical Intake (deliverables): - Network diagram (customer side) with NAT/GW, proxies, and egress constraints. - CIDR and static IPs for allowlist; expected ports (443, 8443 for control plane). - Example SSH bastion or jumpbox requirements for on‑prem package installation. - IdP metadata XML (SAML) or OIDC discovery endpoint. - SCIM provisioning scope: groups mapping + service account email. - KMS key ARN and rotation window (if applicable). Network and Security Requirements (summary table): | Item | Requirement | Notes | |------|-------------|-------| | Allowlist IPs | Customer must allow outbound to Redwood control plane CIDR | Provide final CIDR in pre-kickoff email | | Ports | 443 (HTTPS), 5432 (optional for
title: Federated Enterprise Onboarding Playbook

text: Notes | |------|-------------|-------| | Allowlist IPs | Customer must allow outbound to Redwood control plane CIDR | Provide final CIDR in pre-kickoff email | | Ports | 443 (HTTPS), 5432 (optional for metrics forwarding) | Metrics forwarding optional via TLS | | TLS | mTLS for private deployments recommended | TLS 1.2+; cert chain required for on‑prem installers | | Audit logging | Syslog/Splunk endpoint or S3 bucket | Provide destination details and retention policy | SSO and Provisioning Steps: 1. Customer provides IdP metadata and test user accounts. 2. Redwood SE configures SAML/OIDC in the customer tenant and shares test SP endpoints. 3. Validate attributes: email, name, groups. Ensure group names map to Redwood RBAC roles (admin, ops, developer). 4. Enable SCIM if available for automated provisioning; run dry‑run sync and validate. 5. Document expected session lengths and reauth policy for audits. RBAC Mapping (recommended): - admin -> full project and model management; allowed to create keys - ops -> deploy/rollback and monitoring access - developer -> inference keys, logs, and limited model management Deployment Phases and Acceptance Criteria: Phase 0 — Preparation (1–2 weeks): - All network prerequisites completed; test connectivity verified. - IdP integration validated with 2 test users. - KMS and key policies agreed. Phase 1 — Pilot (2–3 weeks): - Deploy a single model endpoint (low QPS) to dedicated/private environment. - Verify baseline metrics: latency P50/P95,
title: Federated Enterprise Onboarding Playbook

text: with 2 test users. - KMS and key policies agreed. Phase 1 — Pilot (2–3 weeks): - Deploy a single model endpoint (low QPS) to dedicated/private environment. - Verify baseline metrics: latency P50/P95, token throughput, and error rate. - End-to-end tracing enabled and validated. - Customer sign‑off on functional correctness. Phase 2 — Canary (1–2 weeks): - Scale traffic to 10–20% of expected production load with autoscaling policies in place. - Observe slow path behavior, cache warming, and batching efficiency. - Perform failure injection: control plane restart and model fallback test. Phase 3 — Cutover (1 week): - DNS or routing change to send production traffic. - Run ramp script (see "Ramp Plan" section). - 72‑hour elevated monitoring window and daily touchpoint with SE/CSM. Ramp Plan (example CLI verbs): - Phase A: send 5% traffic for 12 hours, verify: error rate < 1%, P95 within target. - Phase B: 25% traffic for 24 hours, run load profiling via /internal/loadtest endpoint. - Phase C: 50% traffic for 24 hours. - Final: 100% after green-checks. First‑90‑Day Success Metrics (measured weekly): - Availability: control plane and inference endpoints >= 99.9% - Client observed latency: P95 must not exceed pre-agreed latency by more than 20% - Cost per 1k tokens within 15% of estimated projection - Number of critical incidents: 0; high-severity incidents <= 1 Operational Runbooks (examples): - Rolling restart of model workers (Private): 1. Put model variant into maintenance mode via
title: Federated Enterprise Onboarding Playbook

text: projection - Number of critical incidents: 0; high-severity incidents <= 1 Operational Runbooks (examples): - Rolling restart of model workers (Private): 1. Put model variant into maintenance mode via admin UI/API: POST /admin/models/{id}/maintenance 2. Scale down worker pool by 10% every 30s to drain connections 3. Apply binary/OS patch on node; verify service health on /healthz 4. Scale pool back up and run smoke test (sample prompts) - Cache warming after deploy: 1. Run batch warmup script: curl -X POST -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' "https://<endpoint>/internal/warmup" -d '@warmup_prompts.json' 2. Monitor cache hit ratio in dashboard; target > 60% within first 1 hour for chat workloads Common Known Issues & Workarounds (priority list): 1. Error: 429 Too Many Requests on control plane during peak onboarding traffic - Cause: rate limiter default thresholds on customer tenant - Workaround: SE can request temporary quota increase; advise staged ramp and use dedicated capacity reservations 2. SAML assertion mapping missing groups - Cause: attribute mapping mismatch in IdP - Workaround: add custom attribute map or use SCIM provisioning; test with a debug assertion tool provided by Redwood 3. Increased tail latency after canary deploy - Cause: new kernel/quantization profile mismatch for model variant - Workaround: rollback to previous model variant; open a performance triage with eng-serving-runtime and attach profile traces Escalation Matrix (contact order + expected response
title: Federated Enterprise Onboarding Playbook

text: mismatch for model variant - Workaround: rollback to previous model variant; open a performance triage with eng-serving-runtime and attach profile traces Escalation Matrix (contact order + expected response SLAs): - Level 1 (Customer SRE -> Redwood Oncall): 15 minutes to acknowledge, 1 hour to triage - Level 2 (Oncall -> Onboarding + SE): 30 minutes to action plan, 4 hours to remediation plan - Level 3 (Product + Eng + SRE): 2 hours to assembly, 24 hours for remediation window or agreed workaround Contacts (example): - Redwood Customer Success: maya.chen@redwoodlabs.com (CSM) - Onboarding SE: alejandro.ruiz@redwoodlabs.com - Oncall PagerDuty: team-redwood-oncall (link internal) QBR Artifacts and Suggested Agenda (monthly first quarter): - Rolling metrics: availability, P50/P95 latency, error budget burn, token usage, cost per 1k tokens - Incident review (if any) and remediation status - Model performance regressions and evals summary - Roadmap alignment: upcoming infra changes that may affect SLAs - Action items and owners for next quarter Template: Health Check Checklist (to run at end of each week during onboarding) - Control plane reachability: PASS/FAIL - Inference endpoint smoke tests (5 prompts): PASS/FAIL - Error rate last 24h < 1%: PASS/FAIL - Disk and memory pressure on worker nodes: OK/WARN/CRITICAL - Audit log delivery verified: PASS/FAIL Post-Onboarding Handoff: - Deliver runbooks and role-based access lists to customer SRE and CSM - Schedule knowledge transfer session
title: Federated Enterprise Onboarding Playbook

text: worker nodes: OK/WARN/CRITICAL - Audit log delivery verified: PASS/FAIL Post-Onboarding Handoff: - Deliver runbooks and role-based access lists to customer SRE and CSM - Schedule knowledge transfer session covering day‑2 ops and scheduled upgrades - Agree on ongoing QBR cadence (monthly -> quarterly) and alert playbooks Appendix A — Example firewall allowlist snippet (customer to paste into security team request): ``` Allow outbound TLS to Redwood control plane CIDR: 34.120.0.0/20 (example) Ports: 443 (control plane), 8443 (metrics), optionally 22 for bastion during install window Destination: control-plane.redwood.company ``` Appendix B — Minimal SCIM mapping (example attributes): ``` { "userName": "user.email", "name.familyName": "user.lastName", "name.givenName": "user.firstName", "groups": "memberOf" } ``` Appendix C — Troubleshooting checklist for high tail latency: 1. Collect kernel traces and quantization profile ID from /internal/trace 2. Check batching metrics (tokens/batch, requests/batch) in dashboard 3. Confirm KV cache hit ratio and warmup status 4. If new model build: rollback to previous variant and open perf triage ticket References and Related Pages: - SSO Setup Guide: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/integration-guides/sso-setup - Private Deployment Networking Checklist: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/networking-checklist - Runtime Performance Triage: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook Ownership and Review Cycle: - Owner:
