text:
Summary:
This playbook defines the acceptance criteria and step-by-step verification procedures Customer Success uses to validate a Redwood Private deployment against our zero-trust baseline before the final go-live handoff. It is intended for use by Customer Success, Solutions Engineering, Security Eng, and the Customer's SRE team during pre-production and production cutover.

Goals:
- Ensure network, auth, and cryptographic controls meet Redwood and customer policy.
- Produce a reproducible evidence bundle that can be attached to the go/no-go decision.
- Establish operational checks and automated probes for day-2 validation.

Who should use this doc:
- Customer Success Onboarding Lead (primary)
- Solutions Engineer (technical lead)
- Customer SRE / Security contact
- Redwood Private Eng on-call (for triage)

Acceptance Criteria (high level):
1) Mutual TLS enforced for all inbound control-plane connections and node-to-node traffic.
2) All persistent secrets are stored in the customer-managed KMS/HSM and never in plaintext on hosts.
3) Workloads run with least privilege: no default admin containers, restricted service accounts.
4) Network policy enforces deny-by-default egress with explicit allow rules.
5) Audit logging (control plane + node-level) forwarded to customer SIEM with retention policy >= 90 days (or customer's required retention).
6) SPIFFE/SVID or equivalent identity issuance is functional and rotated per policy.
7) Signing and attestation for
title: Zero-Trust Private Deployment: Acceptance & Verification Playbook

text:
customer SIEM with retention policy >= 90 days (or customer's required retention).
6) SPIFFE/SVID or equivalent identity issuance is functional and rotated per policy.
7) Signing and attestation for images (Notary/ Cosign) validated on deploy path.
8) Automated health probes (connectivity, auth, and inference smoke tests) pass for 48 hours under simulated production load.
9) Evidence bundle produced and stored in onboarding artifact store.

Pre-check checklist (run before the acceptance window):
- [ ] Deployment manifests and bootstrap scripts committed to customer repo and reviewed by Solutions Eng.
- [ ] KMS/HSM access validated for Redwood control-plane service principal.
- [ ] Customer has provided allowed CIDR ranges and egress policy list.
- [ ] Customer SIEM endpoints (Syslog/OTLP/HTTPS) provided and reachable.
- [ ] SRE contact roster and escalation path documented.

Verification procedures (numbered steps to produce evidence):
1) mTLS & Identity
 - Purpose: Verify TLS mutual authentication and workload identity issuance.
 - Steps:
 a) Using the control-plane API endpoint, run:
 curl -vk --cert /tmp/client.crt --key /tmp/client.key https://CONTROL_PLANE_FQDN/healthz
 Expected: HTTP 200 and header X-Spiffe-Identity present.
 b) Validate SVIDs from the workload node:
 ssh CUSTOMER_NODE && /usr/local/bin/spire-agent api fetch x509 SVID.pem && openssl x509 -noout -text -in SVID.pem | grep "SPIFFE ID"
 Expected: SPIFFE ID format and expiry within rotation
title: Zero-Trust Private Deployment: Acceptance & Verification Playbook

text:
workload node:
 ssh CUSTOMER_NODE && /usr/local/bin/spire-agent api fetch x509 SVID.pem && openssl x509 -noout -text -in SVID.pem | grep "SPIFFE ID"
 Expected: SPIFFE ID format and expiry within rotation window.
 c) Evidence: Save the curl output and SVID text to evidence/mtls-check.txt.

2) KMS/HSM proof of key usage
 - Purpose: Ensure keys are used for envelope encryption and not stored locally.
 - Steps:
 a) From a host, attempt to retrieve a wrapped key:
 aws kms decrypt --ciphertext-blob fileb://wrapped.bin --output text --query Plaintext | base64 --decode > testkey
 (adjust for GCP/Azure HSM CLI as appropriate)
 b) Verify that private keys in use are not present on filesystem:
 sudo find / -type f -name "*private*" -o -name "*.pem" | grep -v "/etc/ssl" || true
 Expected: No unapproved key files; any exceptions must map to approved CA bundles.
 c) Evidence: add CLI transcript and HSM audit entry to evidence/kms-proof.txt.

3) Network policy & Egress deny-by-default
 - Purpose: Validate deny-all egress baseline with controlled exceptions.
 - Steps:
 a) From a workload pod/container run an egress test to a disallowed host:
 kubectl exec -it pod-name -- curl -sS --connect-timeout 5 http://93.184.216.34 || echo "blocked"
 Expected: blocked or connection refused.
 b) Validate that allowed egress to telemetry endpoints succeeds.
 c) Evidence: capture iptables/egress-policy rules and successful/failed curl attempts in evidence/network-policy.txt.

4)
title: Zero-Trust Private Deployment: Acceptance & Verification Playbook

text:
refused.
 b) Validate that allowed egress to telemetry endpoints succeeds.
 c) Evidence: capture iptables/egress-policy rules and successful/failed curl attempts in evidence/network-policy.txt.

4) Image signing and attestation
 - Purpose: Ensure only signed images are deployed.
 - Steps:
 a) Verify cosign verification on deployed image:
 cosign verify --key cosign.pub registry.example.com/release/model-server:sha256
 Expected: verification PASS and verified identity payload.
 b) If using a CI attestation system, confirm the attestation exists in the note store (e.g., Grafeas).
 c) Evidence: attach cosign verification output to evidence/image-attestation.txt.

5) Audit logging pipeline
 - Purpose: Confirm control-plane and node logs are forwarded and contain required fields (timestamp, principal, action).
 - Steps:
 a) Trigger a control-plane action (create a test route or rollout).
 b) Confirm the event appears in customer SIEM within configured latency window (default <= 60s).
 c) Evidence: SIEM event ID(s) and screenshots or exported JSON in evidence/audit-log.json.

6) Operational smoke tests (functional + load validation)
 - Purpose: Verify basic inference, KV cache behavior, and autoscaling under controlled load.
 - Steps:
 a) Baseline inference test (latency & correctness):
 curl -H "Authorization: Bearer TOKEN" -d '{"input":"hello"}' https://TENANT_API/generate | jq .
 Expected: 200, latency < 200ms p50 (baseline), no error tokens.
 b)
title: Zero-Trust Private Deployment: Acceptance & Verification Playbook

text:
(latency & correctness):
 curl -H "Authorization: Bearer TOKEN" -d '{"input":"hello"}' https://TENANT_API/generate | jq .
 Expected: 200, latency < 200ms p50 (baseline), no error tokens.
 b) KV cache correctness: run a multi-step prompt that relies on prefix caching. Validate subsequent call uses cache via tracing header X-Cache-Hit: true.
 c) Load ramp: run a 30-minute synthetic at expected go-live RPS (use locust or k6). Observe SLOs: p95 latency < 800ms, error rate < 0.5%.
 d) Evidence: attach synthetic run logs and a short video or export of Grafana dashboards to evidence/load-test-archive.zip.

7) SPIFFE rotation and CA chain validation
 - Purpose: Confirm rotation and rollback behavior for identity certs.
 - Steps:
 a) Trigger a certificate rotation in non-prod or staging via the documented rotation procedure.
 b) Ensure no packet drops or auth failures; validate that staggered rotation occurred and clients accepted the new chain.
 c) Evidence: rotation logs and pre/post metrics in evidence/cert-rotation.txt.

8) End-to-end telemetry & synthetic alerting
 - Purpose: Ensure synthetic probes surface failures and are sent to incident platform.
 - Steps:
 a) Configure a synthetic probe for the tenant (sample generator + health check).
 b) Intentionally create a controlled failure (block egress to the model shard) and validate alerting and incident creation.
 c) Evidence: incident ID, probe traces, and on-call acknowledgement in evidence/synthetic-alerting.txt.

Evidence
title: Zero-Trust Private Deployment: Acceptance & Verification Playbook

text:
failure (block egress to the model shard) and validate alerting and incident creation.
 c) Evidence: incident ID, probe traces, and on-call acknowledgement in evidence/synthetic-alerting.txt.

Evidence bundle contents (artifact produced at the end of verification window):
- mtls-check.txt
- kms-proof.txt
- network-policy.txt
- image-attestation.txt
- audit-log.json
- load-test-archive.zip
- cert-rotation.txt
- synthetic-alerting.txt
- acceptance-decision.md (signed by customer and Redwood onboarding lead)

Decision flow (Go/No-Go):
1) If all acceptance criteria pass and synthetic load run shows stability for 48 hours -> GO.
2) If any high-severity control fails (mTLS, KMS, audit forwarding) -> NO-GO.
3) For medium severity failures (single non-critical egress rule, minor trace gaps) -> go to mitigation plan with listed owners and defects scheduled within SLA window (default 14 days).

Common failure modes and mitigations:
- Problem: SPIFFE SVIDs expire too quickly on the first rotation.
 Mitigation: Roll back rotation, increase renewal window, coordinate orchestration stagger with Solutions Eng.
- Problem: SIEM ingest rejects log format.
 Mitigation: Switch to fallback delivery (S3 + SIEM ingestion job) and open a ticket with telemetry team.
- Problem: Cosign verification fails for a single image tag.
 Mitigation: Block the tag, verify CI signing pipeline, and promote a validated build.

Post-acceptance tasks (Day 1–7):
- Finalize runbook additions
title: Zero-Trust Private Deployment: Acceptance & Verification Playbook

text:
Cosign verification fails for a single image tag.
 Mitigation: Block the tag, verify CI signing pipeline, and promote a validated build.

Post-acceptance tasks (Day 1–7):
- Finalize runbook additions for customer on-call.
- Schedule a knowledge transfer session and QBR sync for 30/90/180 days.
- Set up automatic synthetic probes and tie to customer incident channel.
- Confirm retention and log access IAM roles are functional for customer auditors.

Owners and contacts:
- Customer Success Onboarding Lead: Priya Raman (priya@redwood.ai)
- Solutions Engineering: Ethan Cole (ethan@redwood.ai)
- Security Eng Liaison: Mariana Lopez (mariana@redwood.ai)
- Private Deployment Eng On-call: paged via PagerDuty "private-deploy"

Appendix A — Acceptance checklist (copyable)
- [ ] mTLS verified between control-plane and agents
- [ ] SVID issuance + rotation validated
- [ ] KMS/HSM key usage validated and no plaintext keys present
- [ ] Egress deny-by-default enforced with allowed list
- [ ] Image signing verified for deployed artifacts
- [ ] Audit logs forwarded to SIEM and retained
- [ ] Synthetic probes and alerting configured
- [ ] Load run p95/p99 within SLOs for 48 hours
- [ ] Evidence bundle uploaded to onboarding artifact store
- [ ] Acceptance decision documented and signed

Appendix B — Useful commands & snippets
- mTLS health check (example):
 curl -vk --cert /tmp/client.crt --key /tmp/client.key https://CONTROL_PLANE_FQDN/healthz | tee mtls-out.txt

Appendix B — Useful commands & snippets
- mTLS health check (example):
 curl -vk --cert /tmp/client.crt --key /tmp/client.key https://CONTROL_PLANE_FQDN/healthz | tee mtls-out.txt

- Cosign verify example:
 cosign verify --key cosign.pub registry.example.com/release/model-server:sha256 | tee cosign.txt

- Example SPIRE cert fetch (if using SPIRE):
 spire-agent api fetch x509 SVID.pem && openssl x509 -noout -text -in SVID.pem > svid.txt

- Basic SIEM probe using netcat (syslog):
 echo "{\"ts\": \"$(date -Iseconds)\", \"svc\": \"control-plane\", \"event\": \"test\" }" | nc -u -w1 SIEM_HOST 514

title: Zero-Trust Private Deployment: Acceptance & Verification Playbook

text: Summary: This playbook defines a repeatable, measurable game-day simulation designed to validate enterprise onboarding readiness for Redwood Inference customers. The exercise exercises critical success paths (go‑live, degraded throughput, model failover, networking incidents, and billing/quotas) and verifies cross-functional coordination between Customer Success, Support, SRE, and Engineering. Goals: - Verify end-to-end customer go-live checklist can be executed under stress. - Validate monitoring & alerting triggers, escalation paths, and on-call handoffs. - Surface integration edge cases (VPC networking, KMS access, streaming webhooks). - Measure response time to simulated incidents and close the loop with concrete action items. - Create repeatable artifacts: post‑game report, RCA action items, and knowledge base updates. Audience and Scope: - Primary audience: Customer Success Managers (CSM), Support Engineers, SRE oncall, Solutions Engineers, and customer's technical contacts. - Scope: Enterprise Dedicated and Private deployments. Hosted API smoke tests included when relevant. - Out of scope: Model training jobs, billing disputes unrelated to onboarding config. Prerequisites (must be completed before scheduling a game day): 1. Signed onboarding checklist present in the customer record (owner: CSM). 2. Dedicated or Private environment accessible to Redwood SRE and the customer's technical lead. 3. Test API keys and a sandbox model pinned to a known, small test-model (e.g.,
title: Customer Success: Game Day Simulation Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: customer record (owner: CSM). 2. Dedicated or Private environment accessible to Redwood SRE and the customer's technical lead. 3. Test API keys and a sandbox model pinned to a known, small test-model (e.g., m-small-test-v1). 4. Observability access: dashboards shared, alert notification channels confirmed (PagerDuty, Slack channels). 5. Contact roster: primary/secondary oncall for customer, CSM, SE, SRE lead, and Support manager. 6. Pre-loaded test prompts and evaluation prompts for quality checks. Schedule and Timebox: - Total duration: 3.5 hours (recommended). - 00:00–00:30: Kickoff and validation of prerequisites. - 00:30–01:15: Planned scenario A (Go-live smoke + capacity ramp). - 01:15–02:00: Planned scenario B (Model failover + quantization mismatch). - 02:00–02:30: Planned scenario C (Network partition / VPC routing). - 02:30–03:00: Unplanned surprise scenario (simulated production alert). - 03:00–03:30: Debrief and immediate action items. Roles & Responsibilities: - CSM (owner): Coordinates schedule, confirms business objectives, communicates outcomes to customer stakeholders. - Support Engineer (responder): Triage alerts, execute customer-facing mitigation steps, document steps in ticket. - SRE (technical lead): Owns infrastructure actions (scaling, routing changes, failover), runs diagnostic queries. - Solutions Engineer (integration lead): Validates config, manifests, and client SDK behavior. - Incident Commander (rotating): Runs timeline, assigns tasks, ensures post-game
title: Customer Success: Game Day Simulation Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: runs diagnostic queries. - Solutions Engineer (integration lead): Validates config, manifests, and client SDK behavior. - Incident Commander (rotating): Runs timeline, assigns tasks, ensures post-game deliverables. Pre-built Scenarios (examples): 1) Scenario A — Controlled Go-live & Capacity Ramp - Objective: Validate autoscaling, rate-limit configs, and initial cost telemetry. - Steps: 1. Switch customer from pre-production API key to go-live API key (SE verifies). 2. Ramp request rate from 5 RPS to 200 RPS across 10 minutes using load script. 3. Observe autoscaler behavior for Dedicated pool and record scale events. 4. Verify token-level cost breakdown appears in Redwood Console and matches expected model units. - Success criteria: p95 latency under 350ms; autoscaler scales to expected instance count within target window; no 5xx spikes above 0.5%. 2) Scenario B — Model Variant Failover + Quantization Mismatch - Objective: Exercise model-router fallback and quality regression detection. - Steps: 1. Intentionally introduce an incompatible quantization config on the primary model variant (simulate misconfigured model binary). 2. Trigger a burst of inference that routes to the primary; observe fallback to a verified variant (fallback window: 30s). 3. Run prompt set with key quality tests; alert if BLEU/ROUGE-like metrics drop beyond threshold. - Success criteria: Traffic reroutes to verified variant within 60s; quality monitoring detects regression and creates an evaluation ticket.
title: Customer Success: Game Day Simulation Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: tests; alert if BLEU/ROUGE-like metrics drop beyond threshold. - Success criteria: Traffic reroutes to verified variant within 60s; quality monitoring detects regression and creates an evaluation ticket. 3) Scenario C — VPC Routing and KMS Latency Spike - Objective: Validate customer private networking and KMS access under intermittent latency. - Steps: 1. Simulate increased latency to KMS (inject 300–700ms latency at the customer VPN boundary). 2. Observe request timeouts, queuing behavior, and rate limiting. 3. Run mitigation: enable local TTL-based KMS caching (if configured) and re-run traffic. - Success criteria: Reduced end-to-end failures after caching enabled; no customer data exposed in logs; SLO for request success maintained above 98% during simulated window. 4) Surprise Scenario — Observability Alert + Paging - Objective: Validate operator escalation flow, oncall handoff, and customer communication cadence. - Steps: 1. Fire synthetic alert: p99 latency > 1.5x SLO for 5 minutes. 2. Confirm PagerDuty page fires, escalation proceeds to the correct rotation. 3. Incident Commander opens incident ticket, CSM crafts initial customer notification using template. - Success criteria: Page acknowledged within 5 minutes, incident communications sent within 15 minutes, runbook tasks initiated. Execution Playbook (step-by-step): 1. Kickoff (CSM): Share agenda, objectives, and success criteria. Confirm observation points and who will own each scenario. 2. Baseline capture (SRE):
title: Customer Success: Game Day Simulation Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: tasks initiated. Execution Playbook (step-by-step): 1. Kickoff (CSM): Share agenda, objectives, and success criteria. Confirm observation points and who will own each scenario. 2. Baseline capture (SRE): Collect baseline dashboards (latency, error-rate, CPU/GPU utilization, queue depth) and snapshot logs. 3. Execute scenario (Incident Commander assigns tasks): Use validated scripts from /contrib/game-day-scripts. Capture timestamps for key events. 4. Immediate mitigation (Support / SRE): Apply documented mitigations; if not available, escalate to Engineering and note time-to-escalate. 5. Document (Support Engineer): Open post-game ticket with timestamps, logs, and screenshots. 6. Debrief (All): 15–30 minute structured debrief in which each role provides findings, blockers, and proposed fixes. 7. Post-game artifacts (CSM owner): Post a short report to customer record and update the knowledge base article referenced in the ticket. Post-Game Report Template (must be completed within 48 hours): - Executive summary (1 paragraph). - Timeline of events (table with timestamps, actor, action). - Metrics observed vs. expected (latency p50/p95/p99, error rates, instance counts). - Root cause hypotheses and confirmed root cause (if any). - Action items (owner, priority, ETA). - Knowledge base updates (links). Sample Timeline Table (ASCII): | Time (UTC) | Actor | Event | Notes | |------------|-------|-------|-------| | 15:02 | Support | Customer switched to go-live key | SE verified config
title: Customer Success: Game Day Simulation Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text: updates (links). Sample Timeline Table (ASCII): | Time (UTC) | Actor | Event | Notes | |------------|-------|-------|-------| | 15:02 | Support | Customer switched to go-live key | SE verified config | | 15:08 | SRE | Autoscaler triggered +3 nodes | scale latency 75s | | 15:22 | SRE | Model failover initiated | fallback to variant v1-stable | Metrics and SLOs to validate during game day: - API success rate (2xx): target >= 99.0% during simulation window. - p95 latency: target <= 350ms for short-form text models; p99 <= 800ms. - Autoscaler reaction time: scale-up start < 120s after threshold breach. - PagerDuty page-to-ack: < 5 minutes. - Mean time to mitigation for mitigatable incidents: < 30 minutes. Common failure modes and recommended mitigations: - Failure mode: KV cache invalidation after model variant swap. - Mitigation: Warm caches using replay of peak traffic; pre-warm sequence cache for pinned variants. - Failure mode: KMS timeouts in high-latency regions. - Mitigation: Enable regional KMS proxy and client-side retry/backoff. - Failure mode: Misconfigured rate limits cause 429 storm. - Mitigation: Gradual ramp plan with automated throttling and synthetic traffic gating. Artifacts and Repositories: - Game-day traffic scripts: git@redwood:infra/game-day-scripts.git (branch: enterprise-onboarding). - Templates: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/health-and-qbr/qbr-template-and-operational-checklist-2026. - Runbooks referenced: /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/incident-process

You may see elevated traffic and controlled rollbacks during the window. We will keep you updated every 15 minutes. - Incident status update (30min): Current status: Investigating elevated p99 latency. Impact: API errors for 2% of requests. Next update in 30 minutes. Owner: <SRE Lead>. Debrief & Continuous Improvement: - All post-game action items must be tracked in the customer onboarding Jira board and closed within 30 days (CSM owner). - Quarterly re-run recommended for customers on Dedicated/Private who cross 10% month-over-month usage growth. - Add new scenarios to the playbook when we encounter gaps; maintain change log in the page history. Appendix A — Example CLI snippets: - Run load script (local): ./scripts/ramp_load.sh --api-key $TEST_KEY --rps 200 --duration 600 - Capture baseline traces (SRE): curl -sS "https://api.redwood.dev/metrics?env=staging&customer=<id>" | jq . > baseline.json Appendix B — Known limitations: - This playbook cannot safely simulate hardware failures (NVLink/GPU hang) in customer Dedicated pools without explicit engineering signoff.

text: Summary: This playbook describes a coordinated technical sweep and SLA readiness checklist for enterprise go-lives during the first 14 days after cutover. It is designed for Customer Success Engineers (CSEs), Platform SRE, Product Ops, and the customer's technical leads to validate production readiness, confirm SLO alignment, and establish a backstop for fast mitigation of any regression in availability, latency, or cost. Intended audience: - Customer Success (primary) - Eng SRE and Platform teams - Onboarding Solutions Engineers - Customer technical contacts and architecture owners Goals: - Verify telemetry, alerting, and dashboards are accurately reflecting tenant traffic within 48 hours of cutover. - Confirm SLA eligibility and billing flags are correct prior to first billing cycle. - Execute lightweight smoke and regression checks for core flows (generation, embeddings, streaming) at production scale for the customer traffic profile. - Establish a documented mitigation and rollback path if SLOs are breached. When to run this playbook: 1) Immediately after tenant cutover in staging -> production (day 0) for an initial smoke sweep. 2) Full technical sweep during day 1–3 to validate telemetry, throttles, and model fallbacks. 3) SLA readiness confirmation and billing gating on day 7 and day 14. Ownership and contacts: - CSE Onboarding Lead: Asha Patel (owner) - Platform SRE Incident Lead: Marcus Nguyen - Product Ops (billing gate): Lena Ortiz - Escalation path: eng-sre-oncall
title: Enterprise Stabilization: Technical Sweep & SLA Readiness Checklist

text: day 7 and day 14. Ownership and contacts: - CSE Onboarding Lead: Asha Patel (owner) - Platform SRE Incident Lead: Marcus Nguyen - Product Ops (billing gate): Lena Ortiz - Escalation path: eng-sre-oncall -> platform-incident -> customer-success-manager High-level coordination checklist (roles): - CSE: coordinate customer runbook, run smoke tests, validate sample prompts, confirm token accounting. - SRE: confirm alert routing, synthetic checks, latency percentiles, and circuit-breakers. - Product Ops: verify usage metering, free-credit accounting, and SLA flags. - Customer: provide production traffic pattern, hold a 30-minute daily sync for first 72 hours. Pre-sweep prerequisites (verify these before starting): - Deployment flag for tenant set to PRODUCTION and correct model pins applied. - Ingress certificates valid and client certs authenticated (if mutual TLS used). - Observability forwarding configured for tenant (metrics, traces, logs). - Billing tenant_id set and test charge accepted in sandbox. - SLO definitions imported into the tenant record (latency P50/P95, availability). Technical sweep procedure (Step-by-step): 1) Smoke tests (immediate, ~30–60 minutes): a) Validate health endpoints: curl -sS https://api.redwood.example.com/v1/health | jq . b) Run 10 production-like generation requests using customer's sample prompts. Capture latency and tokens used. c) Confirm streaming connection setup (websocket/wss) and EOF behavior with 5 streaming requests. 2) Telemetry and sampling
title: Enterprise Stabilization: Technical Sweep & SLA Readiness Checklist

text: generation requests using customer's sample prompts. Capture latency and tokens used. c) Confirm streaming connection setup (websocket/wss) and EOF behavior with 5 streaming requests. 2) Telemetry and sampling validation (within 4 hours): a) Confirm traces appear in X-Ray/Jaeger for these requests with span names: request.receive, runtime.batch, kv.cache.hit, runtime.render. b) Check metrics in Grafana: tenant.<tenant_id>.requests_total, token_count, latency_ms.p50, latency_ms.p95, error_rate. Expected baselines should be within +/-20% of staging profile. 3) Rate limit and quota exercise (within 8 hours): a) Simulate burst at 1.5x expected peak to validate adaptive rate-limiter behavior. b) Validate 429 handling and graceful backoff behavior in client. 4) Model fallback and compatibility (24 hours): a) Trigger a model variant cutoff (simulate an out-of-capacity fallback) and confirm auto-fallback to pinned-compatible-model returns correct format and acceptable quality. 5) Cost and meter sanity (day 1–3): a) Inspect token accounting for test requests vs. expected cost models. b) Verify the usage connector has forwarded billing rows to Billing pipeline with tenant tag. 6) SLO and alert confirmation (day 1): a) Confirm alerting rules fire on synthetics when latency or error budget thresholds are crossed. b) Validate escalation flow triggers pager when a high-severity alert threshold is breached. SLA readiness gating table (to be signed by Product Ops and CSE): | Gate | Criterion
title: Enterprise Stabilization: Technical Sweep & SLA Readiness Checklist

text: thresholds are crossed. b) Validate escalation flow triggers pager when a high-severity alert threshold is breached. SLA readiness gating table (to be signed by Product Ops and CSE): | Gate | Criterion | Pass Signal | Owner | Deadline | |-----|----------|------------|-------|---------| | Telemetry | Metrics and traces visible for tenant | Grafana and Jaeger traces with tenant tag | SRE | Day 1 EOD | | Functionality | Core API requests succeed at expected success rate (>99%) | Synthetic pass rate >= 99% for 1hr | CSE | Day 1 | | Latency | P95 latency within SLO target (configurable by customer) | P95 < configured threshold for 30m window | SRE | Day 3 | | Billing | Usage rows present and mapped to tenant with expected token accounting | Billing pipeline shows non-zero rows for test traffic | Product Ops | Day 7 | | SLA Flag | Tenant eligible flag set for SLA and billing protections | Manual approval in Billing console | Product Ops | Day 14 | NOTE: If any gate fails, follow the Mitigation and Rollback section below before approving SLA flag. Common validation commands and snippets: - Check recent metrics: kubectl exec -it grafana-query -- curl -s "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query?query=rate(tenant_requests_total[5m])" | jq . - Sample generation request (curl): curl -X POST https://api.redwood.example.com/v1/generate \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"model":"redwood-xl-v1","input":"Summarize customer invoice records for April.","max_tokens":200}'
title: Enterprise Stabilization: Technical Sweep & SLA Readiness Checklist

text: https://api.redwood.example.com/v1/generate \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"model":"redwood-xl-v1","input":"Summarize customer invoice records for April.","max_tokens":200}' | jq . - Streaming test (node example): const ws = new WebSocket('wss://stream.redwood.example.com/v1/stream?tenant=$TENANT'); ws.on('open', () => ws.send(JSON.stringify({model:'redwood-xl-v1',input:'Hello'}))); Telemetry assertions (quick checklist): - Tenant tag present on request metrics and billing metrics. - KV cache hit ratio reported in telemetry (should be between 10%–60% depending on workload). - CPU/GPU utilization per node under 85% for sustained 5-minute window. - Error rate per route < 1% (excluding expected 429s during rate-limit tests). Mitigation and rollback options (ordered by preference): 1) Feature-level rollback: disable new feature flags (CSE/Product) and continue traffic on previous path. 2) Model variant switch: pin customer to previous model version with known stable profile. 3) Traffic diversion: redirect subset of tenant traffic to a safe capacity pool (dedicated to burst/fallback). 4) Tenant freeze: throttle requests to a safe limit while extended investigation completes. 5) Emergency rollback: revert cutover and return to the old endpoint (requires coordinated release and database state verification). Escalation playbook (if SLO breached): 1) Declare SEV-1 if availability < 99% for > 15 minutes or latency > 2x SLO for > 30 minutes.
title: Enterprise Stabilization: Technical Sweep & SLA Readiness Checklist

text: endpoint (requires coordinated release and database state verification). Escalation playbook (if SLO breached): 1) Declare SEV-1 if availability < 99% for > 15 minutes or latency > 2x SLO for > 30 minutes. 2) Notify oncall SRE, CSE owner, Product Ops, and customer primary via designated Slack channel and incident bridge. 3) Run triage checklist: gather logs, top 10 error stacks, recent deploys, quota and KVM cache metrics. 4) If mitigation above cannot restore service within 30 minutes, initiate traffic diversion. Communication plan with customer: - Day 0: immediate post-cutover email with links to dashboards, daily sync times, and escalation contacts. - Day 1–3: 30-minute daily sync (standup) to review telemetry and runbook actions. - Day 7: SLA readiness summary and ask for sign-off on billing gate. - Day 14: Final stabilization review and transition to standard support cadence. Acceptance signoff template (copy into meeting notes): - CSE confirms telemetry and functionality gates: YES/NO - SRE confirms alerting and latency gates: YES/NO - Product Ops confirms billing and SLA flag: YES/NO - Customer confirms acceptance and operational readiness: YES/NO Appendices: A) Quick reference: typical SLO defaults - Availability: 99.9% monthly for enterprise Dedicated tenants (changeable by contract) - Latency: P50 50ms, P95 300ms (depends on model and region) - Error budget: 0.1% monthly B) Example synthetic test payloads - Short generation: {"model":"redwood-small","input":"Give three
title: Enterprise Stabilization: Technical Sweep & SLA Readiness Checklist

text: contract) - Latency: P50 50ms, P95 300ms (depends on model and region) - Error budget: 0.1% monthly B) Example synthetic test payloads - Short generation: {"model":"redwood-small","input":"Give three bullet points about product packaging."} - Embedding batch sample (curl): curl -X POST https://api.redwood.example.com/v1/embeddings \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"model":"embed-compact-v1","inputs":["invoice text 1","invoice text 2"]}' | jq . C) Ops contacts and escalation channels - Slack: #oncall-sre, #cse-enterprise, #platform-ops - Pager: +1-555-0100 (primary oncall) - Incident bridge: https://meet.example.com/incident-bridge Revision history: - 2027-01-05: Created by Asha Patel (initial draft). - 2027-02-10: Expanded model fallback tests, added SLA gate table, updated owners. Related templates and further reading: - Tenant acceptance testing template: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/enterprise-onboarding/tenant-onboarding-acceptance-testing-and-telemetry-assertions-2027
title: Enterprise Stabilization: Technical Sweep & SLA Readiness Checklist

text:
Overview:

This playbook documents the end-to-end (E2E) evidence collection, verification, and signoff process required for enterprise deployments and compliance-driven cutovers. It is intended for Customer Success, Solutions Engineering, Platform Operations, and the customer's compliance or security stakeholders. Use this playbook to produce a reproducible audit trail for regulatory review, internal compliance checks, and third-party audits.

Scope:
- Applies to Dedicated and Private (VPC / on-prem) deployments where documented evidence is required prior to go-live.
- Covers technical evidence (telemetry, access logs, configuration snapshots), organizational signoffs, and the post-cutover audit window.
- Does NOT replace security penetration tests or SOC/ISO certification artifacts; it aggregates the project-specific evidence package for release.

Owner and roles:
- Playbook owner: Customer Success (Riley Chen)
- Primary technical owner: Platform Operations (on-call platform lead)
- Security reviewer: Security & Compliance (security reviewer assigned per-customer)
- Customer liaison: Solutions Engineering / Customer Technical Lead
- Audit contact: Legal / Compliance (named per account)

Service & SLO baseline to validate before signoff:
- Availability: >= 99.95% measured over 24h pre-cutover window
- Request success rate: >= 99.9% for production-like traffic pattern
- Token latency (p99): <= 300ms for hosted model variants; for Dedicated p99 should match contract
title: Enterprise Deployment Compliance Evidence Playbook and E2E Signoff

text:
measured over 24h pre-cutover window
- Request success rate: >= 99.9% for production-like traffic pattern
- Token latency (p99): <= 300ms for hosted model variants; for Dedicated p99 should match contract (documented per-customer)
- Telemetry ingress latency: <= 30s for events from gateway to observability stack

Key systems referenced (examples):
- ingress-gateway (TLS termination, mTLS to backend)
- tenant-isolator (network and quota enforcement)
- model-router (routing to model variants and fallbacks)
- kvcache-service (prefix cache and KV eviction metrics)
- telemetry-gatekeeper (contract validation & forwarding)
- obs-collector (metrics/traces/logs forwarder)

Pre-cutover checklist (technical):
1. Configuration snapshot: export and store JSON/YAML of: ingress-gateway, tenant-isolator, model-router, and quota rules.
2. Access control review: confirm RBAC roles and service accounts; audit log enabled for key services.
3. Telemetry contract verification: run telemetry-contract-check.sh (see snippet) and confirm schema compliance.
4. Synthetic traffic run: execute 6h synthetic run matching 75–100% expected QPS and validate SLOs.
5. KVCache warmup: run cache-warmup micro-batch with representative prefixes.
6. Model variant smoke: exercise primary and fallback variants, verify identical response schema and function calling behavior.
7. Backup & rollback snapshot: store infra state, TLS certs (references), and database snapshot where applicable.

Evidence
title: Enterprise Deployment Compliance Evidence Playbook and E2E Signoff

text:
variants, verify identical response schema and function calling behavior.
7. Backup & rollback snapshot: store infra state, TLS certs (references), and database snapshot where applicable.

Evidence artifacts required (store in customer-audit-bucket):
| Artifact ID | Description | Required format | Owner |
|------------|-------------|----------------|-------|
| CFG-001 | Configuration snapshot (pre-cutover) | JSON/YAML | Platform Ops |
| ACC-002 | RBAC & service-account export | CSV/JSON | Security |
| TEL-003 | Telemetry contract validation report | JSON + log tail | Platform Ops |
| SYN-004 | Synthetic run report (6h) | HTML/PDF + metrics snapshot | Customer Success |
| KV-005 | KV cache hit-rate and eviction snapshot | CSV/JSON | Platform Ops |
| LOG-006 | Access & audit logs (rolling 7d) | gzipped logs | Customer Success / Security |
| SMK-007 | Model smoke test results and example payloads | JSON | Solutions Eng |
| ROLLBACK-001 | Rollback plan & snapshot pointers | Markdown | Platform Ops |

Step-by-step E2E signoff workflow:
1. Prepare artifacts: Platform Ops and Customer Success ensure all artifacts from the table above are uploaded to the customer-audit-bucket with consistent naming: <customer>-<artifact-id>-<timestamp> (e.g., acme-CFG-001-20260601T0300Z).
2. Automated verification: Trigger the evidence-verifier pipeline (CI job: ci/evidence-verifier) which runs schema validations and smoke-test replays. Pipeline must return green with zero critical failures.
3.
title: Enterprise Deployment Compliance Evidence Playbook and E2E Signoff

text:
Automated verification: Trigger the evidence-verifier pipeline (CI job: ci/evidence-verifier) which runs schema validations and smoke-test replays. Pipeline must return green with zero critical failures.
3. Security review: Security reviewer performs RBAC and audit-log spot checks within 24 hours. Reviewer documents findings in SECURITY-REVIEW.md and attaches to the artifact set.
4. Customer acceptance: Customer Technical Lead validates the synthetic run and model smoke tests. A signed acceptance email or JIRA approval is required.
5. Legal & Compliance: Legal confirms contractual obligations are represented; Compliance approves retention and evidence redaction plan.
6. Executive signoff: Customer Success Lead and Platform Lead confirm readiness and set the go-live window.
7. Cutover execution: Follow cutover runbook (link below). During cutover, collect pre-defined live telemetry and produce the live-run evidence package.
8. Post-cutover validation: Run 24h high-fidelity validation and compare to pre-cutover SLOs. If any critical SLO fails, follow rollback criteria below.
9. Archive & retention: After 30 days of stable operation, move evidence package to long-term archive and produce an Audit Manifest.

Rollback criteria (abort or roll back immediately if any of the following occur during the 24h post-cutover window):
- Availability drops below 99.0% for a sustained 15 minute window.
- Request success rate drops below 98.5% for 10m with customer-visible errors.
-
title: Enterprise Deployment Compliance Evidence Playbook and E2E Signoff

text:
following occur during the 24h post-cutover window):
- Availability drops below 99.0% for a sustained 15 minute window.
- Request success rate drops below 98.5% for 10m with customer-visible errors.
- Model correctness regressions exceed the evaluated threshold (as determined by solutions eng eval harness).
- Unauthorized access or privilege escalation detected.

Cutover smoke tests (run as part of the live window):
- Functional: 50 representative user flows against the production tenant (sample payloads stored in SMK-007).
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 token latencies captured and compared to baseline.
- Cost-safety: per-minute token emission rate validated against burst limits and quota controllers.

Telemetry verification snippet (example):
# Telemetry contract check (bash)
# Usage: ./telemetry-contract-check.sh acme-staging
cat <<'JSON' > sample-event.json
{
 "tenant_id": "acme",
 "event_type": "inference.request",
 "timestamp": "2026-06-01T03:00:00Z",
 "payload_size": 832
}
JSON

curl -s -X POST https://telemetry.example.internal/contract-validate \n -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \n --data @sample-event.json | jq .

Expected output: {
 "status": "ok",
 "schema_version": "v2.1",
 "errors": []
}

Traceability matrix (how artifacts map to compliance controls):
- CFG-001 -> Control INF-CONFIG-1 (configuration immutability)
- ACC-002 -> Control ACC-AUDIT-3 (role separation & access logging)
- TEL-003 -> Control MON-INGEST-2
title: Enterprise Deployment Compliance Evidence Playbook and E2E Signoff

text:
artifacts map to compliance controls):
- CFG-001 -> Control INF-CONFIG-1 (configuration immutability)
- ACC-002 -> Control ACC-AUDIT-3 (role separation & access logging)
- TEL-003 -> Control MON-INGEST-2 (telemetry contract compliance)

Communication templates (abbreviated):
- Customer announcement: brief summary of go-live window, expected impact, rollback policy, and contacts.
- Internal incident slack template: pre-filled with artifact pointers and runbook links.
- Audit submission email: to auditor@example.com with artifact manifest and retention policy.

Post-go-live audit & retrospective (30 days):
- Compile Audit Manifest: list of artifacts, retention dates, hashes, and redaction notes.
- 30-day stability report: metrics, incidents, cost delta, and customer feedback.
- Lessons learned: 1-page runbook changes and owners for follow-up.

Appendix A — Sample evidence manifest (JSON snippet):
{
 "customer": "acme",
 "cutover_time": "2026-06-01T03:00:00Z",

text: summary: A structured acceptance-testing playbook to validate tenant readiness after provisioning and before hand-back. Defines telemetry assertions, synthetic scenarios, and safe rollback/fallback steps tailored to Dedicated and Private tenants. body: Purpose This playbook provides a repeatable acceptance-testing and telemetry-assertion checklist for enterprise tenant cutovers (Dedicated and Private modes). Its goal is to ensure customers meet their SLOs and observability expectations before the CX hand-off. Scope and audience - Audience: Customer Success Engineers (CSE), Solutions Engineers, Oncall SREs, and Platform Engineers executing go-live validation. - Scope: Post-provisioning validation window (first 72 hours), focused on latency, error rates, throughput, billing signals, and data-plane connectivity. Not intended for long-term performance tuning. Prerequisites 1. Tenant is provisioned and accessible; operator has admin token and tenant ID. 2. Telemetry forwarding (metrics/traces/logs) enabled to central telemetry workspace or customer-managed forwarder. 3. Canary API key created and injected into test harness. Quick checklist (high level) - [ ] DNS / network reachability verified (ping, curl). - [ ] TLS certificate chain validated for private endpoints. - [ ] Basic API call succeeds and returns expected schema. - [ ] Ingress egress firewall rules allow control-plane callbacks (if Private). - [ ] Telemetry streams ingest baseline synthetic traffic. Acceptance criteria
title: Tenant Onboarding: Acceptance Testing & Telemetry Assertions

text: call succeeds and returns expected schema. - [ ] Ingress egress firewall rules allow control-plane callbacks (if Private). - [ ] Telemetry streams ingest baseline synthetic traffic. Acceptance criteria (signal-level) Provide these pass/fail assertions to the customer and engineering stakeholders. If any assertion fails, execute the rollback/fallback procedure below. | Signal | Metric | Assertion (pass) | |---|---:|---| | Availability | 5m rolling availability | >= 99.5% during acceptance window | | Latency (generate) | p95 gen latency | <= 600ms for short prompts (<=128 tokens) | | Error rate | 1m error rate (5xx + 429) | < 0.5% | | Throughput | steady-state rps | Sustains expected commit profile ±10% | | Cost signal | estimated projected daily token cost | Within agreed finance cap ±15% | | Telemetry lag | metric ingestion lag | < 60s for critical alerts | Telemetry assertions (concrete checks) - Metrics to assert in the first 72 hours: - request.count by route and model variant - request.latency.p50/p95/p99 by route - request.errors.5xx and request.errors.ratelimit - kvcache.hit_ratio and kvcache.miss_ratio (if KV cache enabled) - pod.resource.cpu_util and pod.resource.gpu_util per node pool - billing.estimate.daily_tokens and billing.estimate.24h_projection - Alert thresholds to temporarily enable for acceptance: - latency.p95 > 800ms for 2 consecutive 1m windows -> Sev2 - errors.5xx >= 1% for 3 consecutive 1m windows -> Sev2 - telemetry.lag > 120s -> Sev3 Synthetic test scenarios
title: Tenant Onboarding: Acceptance Testing & Telemetry Assertions

text: temporarily enable for acceptance: - latency.p95 > 800ms for 2 consecutive 1m windows -> Sev2 - errors.5xx >= 1% for 3 consecutive 1m windows -> Sev2 - telemetry.lag > 120s -> Sev3 Synthetic test scenarios (run in order) 1) Smoke: Basic generation and embeddings - Command: curl -X POST https://{tenant-host}/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer {canary_key}" -d '{"prompt": "Hello world","max_tokens": 20}' - Expected: 200 OK, model output length <= 40 tokens, schema contains request_id and latency_ms 2) Latency profile: Burst of short prompts - Run: 100 short prompt requests over 60s (ramp 0->100rps) using our load harness - Expected: p95 latency within asserted bound; no tail errors or rate-limit biasing normal routes 3) Throughput profile: Sustained throughput at committed RPS - Run: sustained RPS for 15 minutes equal to tenant commit (or 50% of commit if in dry-run) - Expected: no autoscaler thrash; no OOMs on pods; stable CPU/GPU util curves 4) KV cache warm/cold cycle (if enabled) - Run a prefix-heavy scenario, measure kvcache.hit_ratio rising above 40% within 5 minutes for typical workloads 5) Failure injection: Simulate model fallback - Trigger: mark primary variant unhealthy via platform toggle; confirm automatic routing to fallback variant within policy window and acceptable quality degradation metrics tracked Operational commands and tooling - Test harness quickstart (example): - docker run --rm -e TENANT_HOST={tenant-host} -e API_KEY={canary_key} redwood/load-harness:latest
title: Tenant Onboarding: Acceptance Testing & Telemetry Assertions

text: quality degradation metrics tracked Operational commands and tooling - Test harness quickstart (example): - docker run --rm -e TENANT_HOST={tenant-host} -e API_KEY={canary_key} redwood/load-harness:latest --script short-burst.json - Curl example for telemetry assertion (prometheus query): - curl -s "https://telemetry.redwood.internal/api/v1/query?query=request_latency_p95{tenant=\"{tenant_id}\"}" | jq .data.result Rollback and fallback (safe steps) If acceptance fails for any critical assertion, follow the safe rollback below in order. Document each step in the ticket. 1. Pause customer traffic (edge ACL or routing flag). Record timestamp. 2. If degraded due to model variant, revert routing to the last verified variant (platform toggle): - UI: Console -> Tenants -> {tenant} -> Traffic routing -> Revert to stable - API: POST /api/v1/tenants/{tenant}/routing?action=revert&to=stable 3. If resource saturation (OOMs/GC spikes): scale up GPU pool by +1 node or apply temporary burst capacity policy. 4. If networking (Private) issues: re-run firewall handshake and verify peering configurations; escalate to network infra. 5. If cost projection exceeded: engage FinOps and set soft quota throttle at 90% of projected cap. Ownership and escalation matrix | Role | Primary | Secondary | Pager | Escalation criteria | |---|---|---|---|---| | CSE | Aisha Kaur | Ruth Chen | N/A | Any acceptance failure requiring customer comms | | Platform SRE | Owen Patel | Diego Martinez | oncall-sre@redwood.com
title: Tenant Onboarding: Acceptance Testing & Telemetry Assertions

text: Escalation criteria | |---|---|---|---|---| | CSE | Aisha Kaur | Ruth Chen | N/A | Any acceptance failure requiring customer comms | | Platform SRE | Owen Patel | Diego Martinez | oncall-sre@redwood.com | Resource OOMs, autoscaler thrash | Network | Priya Sharma | Lukas Berg | network-oncall@redwood.com | Private peering / firewall failures | FinOps | Maya Singh | Jonah Park | finops@redwood.com | Projected spend > cap + 10% Post-acceptance handback checklist - Confirm all temporary high-sensitivity alerts are returned to normal thresholds. - Remove canary API key and rotate if requested. - Share a short acceptance report with: customer, AE, CSE, and Platform owner. Report should include: acceptance window metrics snapshot, synthetic script outputs, and any remediation performed. - Schedule QBR readiness meeting within 30 days to review long-term SLOs and cost profile. Telemetry dashboard templates (recommended panels) - Tenant Overview: availability, p95/p99 latency, error rate, synthetic test pass/fail - Model Variants: per-variant request count, fallbacks, average latency - Resource Utilization: per-node GPU/CPU, pod restarts, OOM counts - Cost: 24h token estimate, projected 7d and 30d spend Appendix: Example acceptance report snippet - Acceptance window: 2026-02-10 02:00 -> 2026-02-10 08:00 UTC - p95 latency (generate, short prompts): 412ms - Availability: 99.92% - kvcache.hit_ratio: 46% after warm-up - Actions performed: Increased burst capacity by 1 node at 02:45 UTC; reverted

Summary: [key metrics]. Next: schedule QBR" Notes and version history - v1.0 (2025-11-12) initial creation by Aisha Kaur - v1.1 (2025-12-08) added KV-cache warm/cold assertions (reviewer: Diego Martinez) - v1.2 (2026-02-15) tightened latency assertions for p95 and clarified rollback step 2 (reviewer: Ruth Chen) End of document

text: Purpose This playbook documents a standardized approach for identifying, preventing, and remediating failures caused by third-party or internal dependency outages during enterprise onboarding and early go-live. It focuses on the technical mitigations, telemetry assertions, and communication patterns Customer Success (CS), SRE, and Platform teams should follow when dependencies degrade or fail. Scope - Applies to enterprise onboarding windows, canary rollouts, and first-72-hour post-go-live stability runs. - Covers external partners (cloud provider APIs, SCM, identity providers, SaaS connectors) and internal services we rely on (ingress-gateway, fanout-proxy, telemetry-gatekeeper, embedding-indexer, kvcache, model-router). - Does NOT cover purely customer-side outages (e.g., customer DB down) except where we coordinate cutover. Owners and RACI - Primary owner: Customer Success (Onboarding) — Ava Chen - Technical owner (SRE escalation): Eng SRE — Diego Morales - Platform mitigations and config changes: Eng Platform — Priya Natarajan - Observability contract owner: Eng DevEx — Samir Gupta RACI summary: 1) Detection: Eng SRE (R), Eng DevEx (C), CS (I) 2) Triage/Customer comms: CS (R), Eng SRE (A) 3) Mitigation (routing/fallback): Eng Platform (R/A) 4) Postmortem & action items: Eng SRE (R), CS (C) Pre-Go-Live Dependency Map Checklist (must be completed before go-live gate) 1. Dependency inventory: maintain a single table with service name, owner, SLA (external), API endpoints, auth
title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)

text: (R), CS (C) Pre-Go-Live Dependency Map Checklist (must be completed before go-live gate) 1. Dependency inventory: maintain a single table with service name, owner, SLA (external), API endpoints, auth method, and last verified test run. 2. Telemetry assertions: add at least three assertion checks for each dependency (latency, error rate, presence of expected metrics). 3. Fallback compatibility: confirm an alternate path exists for each critical dependency (e.g., cached read, degraded feature, queued processing). 4. Runbook verification: ensure a runbook paragraph exists for each item with detection signal and mitigation steps. 5. Communication template: verify customer comms and internal incident templates are available and signed-off. Dependency Resilience Matrix (example table) | Dependency | Criticality | SLO tie | Failure modes | Primary detection signal | Immediate mitigation | Owner | |-----------|-------------|---------|---------------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------| | ingress-gateway | P0 | 99.9% availability | TLS handshake failures, 502 spikes | 5xx rate > 0.5% over 5m, TLS errors spike | Reroute to standby gateway, enable traffic-shaping to slow new sessions | Eng Platform (Priya) | | telemetry-gatekeeper | P0 | Inference telemetry 1h TTL | Missing spans, dropped metrics | Missing ingestion heartbeat for 2m, sample rate drop | Fallback to passive buffering sidecar; enable synchronous flush | Eng DevEx (Samir) | | fanout-proxy | P1 | 99% throughput
title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)

text: spans, dropped metrics | Missing ingestion heartbeat for 2m, sample rate drop | Fallback to passive buffering sidecar; enable synchronous flush | Eng DevEx (Samir) | | fanout-proxy | P1 | 99% throughput | Queue growth, per-tenant blocking | Queue depth > 80% capacity for 3m | Enable per-tenant throttles, route low-priority tenants to degraded path | Eng Platform | | external-idp (SCIM) | P2 | External SLA 99.5% | 429/403 from idp | auth failures or 429s > 1% | Switch to cached user mapping; pause provisioning jobs | Customer Success | | embedding-indexer (internal) | P1 | Indexing SLA 95% | Index lag, OOM | indexing-lag > 10m | Apply incremental backfill window, drop non-critical batches | Eng Serving Runtime | | model-router | P0 | Serving SLA 99.95% | Model load failures, KV cache misses | model load errors, cache miss rate > 15% | Switch to compatible quantized variant, enable cache warmup | Eng Serving Runtime | Operational Runbook (detection -> mitigation -> validate) 1. Detection (first 5 minutes) - Watchers: SRE alerting channels receive automated signals (PagerDuty, Slack #sre-ops). - Initial checks: run ./tools/check-dependency-health --service <name> to confirm alarm. 2. Triage (5–20 minutes) - Assign a triage lead (SRE) and a customer liaison (CS). - Confirm blast radius: run tenant impact query (example): curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $MONITOR_API" "https://telemetry-api.prod/tenant-impact?service=fanout-proxy&window=10m" | jq . - If impact > threshold (defined
title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)

text: radius: run tenant impact query (example): curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $MONITOR_API" "https://telemetry-api.prod/tenant-impact?service=fanout-proxy&window=10m" | jq . - If impact > threshold (defined below) escalate to on-call Eng Platform. 3. Immediate mitigations (20–60 minutes) - If ingress-gateway is degraded: flip to standby gateway with the CLI: rwdctl gateway failover --from ingress-gw-us-west-2 --to ingress-gw-us-east-1 --reason "onboarding surge" - If telemetry-gatekeeper ingestion is missing: enable buffering and start a fast-backfill: kubectl -n telemetry apply -f buffering-sidecar-override.yaml ./tools/backfill-telemetry --start $(date -u -d '15 minutes ago' +%s) --end $(date -u +%s) - If model-router fails to load a quantized variant: re-route traffic to fallback model: rwdctl model route --from model:prod:v2 --to model:prod:quantized-v1 --percent 100 --force 4. Validate (after 60 minutes) - Confirm telemetry asserts: all dependency assertions pass for 10 consecutive minutes. - Confirm SLO posture re-evaluated for impacted tenants and document customer-facing impact. Escalation thresholds (examples) - Severity P0: Any customer-facing error rate causing >1% 5xx across the tenant fleet or single large tenant with >0.5% 5xx for 10m. PagerDuty P0, incident bridge, executive notify. - Severity P1: Throughput drops causing queue growth beyond 50% for >15m. PagerDuty P1; CS alerts impacted customers. - Severity P2: Auxiliary systems (SCIM, billing connector) errors
title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)

text: executive notify. - Severity P1: Throughput drops causing queue growth beyond 50% for >15m. PagerDuty P1; CS alerts impacted customers. - Severity P2: Auxiliary systems (SCIM, billing connector) errors causing background job failures. Email + Slack notify; CS to log and monitor. Customer Communication templates - Initial acknowledgement (under 15 minutes): "We’ve detected a degradation affecting [feature/service]. Our engineering team is triaging. We will provide an update within 30 minutes and a root-cause timeline within 4 hours. Impact: [brief impact]. Mitigation: [what we’re doing]. Contact: your CS rep, [name], + link to status page." - Mitigation update (30–90 minutes): "Summary: We identified [dependency] as the primary cause. We’ve applied [mitigation] which restored service for X% of requests. Engineers continue to monitor and will follow up with next steps by [time]." Telemetry Assertions (examples to add to onboarding checks) 1. Ingestion heartbeat: telemetry-gatekeeper.heartbeat == true in last 1m 2. Model load success rate: model-router.load_success_rate >= 99.9% over 5m 3. KV cache hit ratio: kvcache.hit_ratio >= 85% over 10m Add these to the onboarding synthetic suite; example JSON snippet for assertion: { "assertion_name": "kvcache_hit_ratio_onboarding", "expr": "avg(kvcache_hit_ratio{env=\"prod\",stage=\"onboarding\"}) > 0.85", "window": "10m", "owner": "eng-serving-runtime" } Canary Failure Simulation (run prior to go-live) 1. Simulate partial dependency failure:
title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)

text: "avg(kvcache_hit_ratio{env=\"prod\",stage=\"onboarding\"}) > 0.85", "window": "10m", "owner": "eng-serving-runtime" } Canary Failure Simulation (run prior to go-live) 1. Simulate partial dependency failure: throttle upstream responses to 50% for 10 minutes using traffic-shaper job. 2. Verify that fallback paths engage within target time (<= 30s) and degraded responses are metric-tagged. 3. Run customer communication dry-run: ask CS to publish the initial acknowledgement and verify internal observers see it. Post-Incident Walkthrough and Action Items (within 72h) - Convene triage + CS + platform; capture timeline and mitigation chronology. - Generate action items: code fix, observability gaps, config changes, customer compensation if SLA breach. Assign owners and target dates. - Update dependency map and onboarding checklist with any missed validations. Common Failure Mode Playbooks (short form) - Telemetry ingestion drop 1) Buffer sidecar enablement 2) Backfill telemetry for the gap window 3) Postmortem: add assertion and test to synthetic suite - External SCIM throttling 1) Pause provisioning 2) Switch to cached identity mapping for auth flows 3) Retry queue with exponential backoff and jitter - KV cache cold start leading to latency spike 1) Warm cache using sample keyset: ./tools/warm-kvcache --tenant <tenant-id> --keys-file warm-keys.json 2) If warmfail, route to model variant with smaller memory footprint Appendix: Useful commands and references - rwdctl status dependencies
title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)

text: ./tools/warm-kvcache --tenant <tenant-id> --keys-file warm-keys.json 2) If warmfail, route to model variant with smaller memory footprint Appendix: Useful commands and references - rwdctl status dependencies --tenant <id> --since 15m\- shows dependency health summary\- - ./tools/check-dependency-health --service telemetry-gatekeeper --verbose\- detailed probe for the service\- - Example buffering-sidecar-override.yaml (snippet): apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap metadata: name: telemetry-buffer-override data: buffer.max_bytes: "50000000" flush.interval_s: "10" Appendix: Postmortem checklist template (to attach to incident) - Incident title and severity - Detection time, mitigation applied, time to recovery - Impacted tenants (list) and customer-facing communication timeline - Root cause and contributing factors - Concrete action items (owner, due date, verification test) FAQ (short) Q: When should CS ask for credits or SLA remedies? A: Follow the existing SLA reconciliation playbook — if the incident breaches the contracted uptime or per-tenant SLO defined in the contract, CS should initiate the billing remediation flow after the postmortem is complete. Q: Can we always failover to another region? A: Not always. Region-based failover is a last-resort for data residency or private deployment customers. Follow the regional failover checklist and confirm customer consent where required. Related pages and references - Onboarding failure mode scenarios (linked internally) - Canary simulation
title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)

text: customers. Follow the regional failover checklist and confirm customer consent where required. Related pages and references - Onboarding failure mode scenarios (linked internally) - Canary simulation playbook (Eng SRE) - SLA remediation and billing process This playbook is intended to be a living document. Update the dependency matrix and telemetry assertions after every simulation and postmortem.

text: Summary: This playbook provides a prescriptive, low-risk process for performing large-scale data ingest and backfill work for enterprise customers, followed by the live cutover to Redwood inference pipelines (hosted, dedicated, or private). It codifies roles, pre-cutover validation, dry-run and backfill strategies, SRE guardrails, escalation paths, rollback criteria, and post-cutover checks. Scope: - Target audience: Customer Success (CS), Solutions Engineering (SE), Customer SRE, Redwood Platform Ops, and customer engineering teams. - Applies to: bulk ingestion of telemetry, prompt sets, embedding indices, or any dataset used by inference features where size or throughput requires coordinated cutover. - Out of scope: routine small-scale batch imports (<100k records) — use standard integration guide. Goals and success criteria: - Cutover completes with <1% data loss and no more than 5 minutes of degraded inference latency compared to baseline. - Backfill throughput sustained to complete within agreed SLA (example: 48 hours for 100M rows). - No customer-visible errors beyond planned degradation windows; all incidents resolved or rolled back per runbook. Roles & ownership: - Customer Success (CS): overall cutover coordination, stakeholder comms, runbook lead. - Solutions Engineering (SE): integration plan, test harnesses, schema mappings. - Customer SRE: responsible for customer's production environment, networking, and ACLs. - Redwood Platform Ops: capacity reservation, dedicated
title: Enterprise bulk data ingest, backfill, and live cutover playbook

text: (SE): integration plan, test harnesses, schema mappings. - Customer SRE: responsible for customer's production environment, networking, and ACLs. - Redwood Platform Ops: capacity reservation, dedicated pool config, rollback hooks. - Redwood SRE/Oncall: live monitoring, alerts, and incident triage. Contact matrix (primary -> fallback): - CS lead: alyssa.chen@redwood.ai -> jordan.patel@redwood.ai - SE lead: ravi.narayanan@redwood.ai -> sofia.martinez@redwood.ai - Platform Ops pager: #platform-ops -> escalation to oncall@redwood.ai Pre-cutover checklist (required before any dry run): 1) Contract & SOW confirmation: confirm ingestion SLA, cost model, and storage retention with customer procurement. 2) Capacity and quota reservation: Platform Ops must reserve GPU/CPU pools and token quota for estimated peak load + 30% buffer. 3) Network & permissions validated: firewall rules, VPC peering, service accounts, and KMS/HSM access verified. 4) Schema & mapping validation: SE confirms schema compatibility with runtime and embedding pipelines. 5) Observability hooks enabled: request/response tracing, token-level cost tagging, custom metrics for ingest rate and validation failures. 6) Rollback criteria agreed and documented with sign-off by CS and Customer SRE. Configuration matrix (example): | Item | Recommended value | Owner | |------|-------------------|-------| | Dedicated pool size | 8x A100 equivalent | Platform Ops | | Backfill batch size | 5k - 50k records (tune) | SE/Customer | |
title: Enterprise bulk data ingest, backfill, and live cutover playbook

text: | Item | Recommended value | Owner | |------|-------------------|-------| | Dedicated pool size | 8x A100 equivalent | Platform Ops | | Backfill batch size | 5k - 50k records (tune) | SE/Customer | | Max concurrent streams | 200 | Platform Ops | | Retention during backfill | 30 days (temp) | Customer | Pre-run validation tests (execute in staging or shadow environment): - Synthetic ingest at 1.5x expected rate for 30 minutes to validate autoscaling and queueing. - Schema edge-case validation (nulls, UTF-8, truncation). - End-to-end inference correctness checks on representative subset. Dry-run procedure (recommended and must be green before full backfill): 1) Schedule a 2–4 hour maintenance window with stakeholders and publish comms. 2) Run a limited backfill on a 1–5% subset of data that mirrors distribution. 3) Metrics to observe: ingest success rate >= 99.5%, average token latency within SLO ±10%, error budget < 1%. 4) Run data reconciliation: sample hash checks between source and target for 1000 randomly selected rows. 5) Post-dry-run sign-off meeting: SE, CS, Customer SRE, Platform Ops. If any gate fails, remediate and rerun. Backfill execution strategy options (choose one): A) Continuous streaming backfill: best for near-live pipelines, low-latency requirements. B) Batch windowing with checkpointing: recommended for very large datasets (>=50M rows) to limit peak load. C) Hybrid (fast-forward then streaming): seed the index with a bulk snapshot, then stream real-time deltas.
title: Enterprise bulk data ingest, backfill, and live cutover playbook

text: windowing with checkpointing: recommended for very large datasets (>=50M rows) to limit peak load. C) Hybrid (fast-forward then streaming): seed the index with a bulk snapshot, then stream real-time deltas. Example backfill batch configuration (YAML): --- batch_size: 20000 parallel_workers: 12 checkpoint_interval: 100000 retry_policy: max_retries: 5 backoff_seconds: 60 validation_checks: - checksum - schema_match - sample_inference_compare --- Operational monitoring and SLOs during backfill: - Ingest success rate: target 99.9% (alert if <99.5% for 10m). - Average per-request latency (inference path): SLO depends on customer tier; e.g., Dedicated <= 150ms p50, <= 500ms p95. Alert if p95 increases >2x baseline for 5 minutes. - Token cost inflation: track cost-per-thousand-tokens; alert if >30% above forecasted. - Queue depth and backpressure: alert if queue length > 5x normal for 10 minutes. Incident detection and immediate mitigation steps (triage flow): 1) Detect: Alert fires (one of the SLO alerts above). 2) Triage: Platform Ops and Redwood SRE examine logs, traces, and queue metrics within 10 minutes. 3) Contain: Throttle backfill by reducing parallel_workers or switching to smaller batch_size. 4) Mitigate: Route high-priority customer traffic to fallback model variant or alternative region (see routing policy). 5) Escalate: If containment fails within 20 minutes, page engineering lead and open an incident in PagerDuty. Escalation matrix (severity mapping): - Sev3 (performance
title: Enterprise bulk data ingest, backfill, and live cutover playbook

text: alternative region (see routing policy). 5) Escalate: If containment fails within 20 minutes, page engineering lead and open an incident in PagerDuty. Escalation matrix (severity mapping): - Sev3 (performance degradation): CS lead notified, Platform Ops mitigates, 1-hour P1 work window if unresolved. - Sev2 (partial loss / repeated failures): Page Redwood SRE and SE, require immediate rollback gating. - Sev1 (customer outage / data loss): Full incident protocol, immediate rollback, legal and exec comms. Rollback criteria and procedure (pre-agreed): - Rollback triggers: - Data loss detected in reconciliation > 0.5% of sampled rows. - Sustained latency >3x SLO for 15+ minutes with no mitigation. - Unrecoverable consumer queue corruption. - Rollback steps: 1) Stop all ingestion jobs and place system in read-only mode. 2) Revert model routing to previous stable variant. 3) Restore snapshot from pre-backfill checkpoint (store snapshots in S3 or customer-provided bucket). 4) Run reconciliation checks and issue post-mortem. Post-cutover validation checklist (24–72 hours): - Full reconciliation completed for initial import window and sampled full dataset. - Latency and error metrics stable within thresholds. - Cost report generated and reviewed with CS and customer. - Retention policy and cold-storage lifecycle rules applied. - Runbook: close action items and create any follow-ups for engineering or infra. Telemetry and observability (required tags and dashboards): - Required metric
title: Enterprise bulk data ingest, backfill, and live cutover playbook

text: and cold-storage lifecycle rules applied. - Runbook: close action items and create any follow-ups for engineering or infra. Telemetry and observability (required tags and dashboards): - Required metric tags: customer_id, ingest_job_id, batch_id, dataset_name, environment. - Dashboards to create prior to run: Backfill Overview, Per-job Errors, Cost vs Forecast, Model Latency Heatmap. Sample CLI commands and checks: - Kick off a dry-run (example): curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/ingest/run \n -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' \n -d '{"dataset":"customer_xyz_sample","mode":"dry_run","workers":4}' - Check backfill job status (example response): { "job_id": "bf-20260301-01", "status": "running", "processed": 1200000, "failed": 12, "eta_minutes": 360 } - Reconciliation hash check (pseudo-command): redwood-reconcile --source s3://cust-bucket/2026-02-01/ --target redwood://ingest/datasets/customer_xyz --sample 1000 --output reconciliation-report.csv Cost & billing notes: - Enable per-job cost tagging before backfill so cost is attributed to customer SOW line items. - Notify Finance if estimated credit consumption during backfill exceeds committed usage by >20%. Run completion & sign-off: - Required sign-offs: SE (schema/mapping), Customer SRE (network/permissions), CS (stakeholder comms), Platform Ops (capacity and final state). - Closing artifacts to attach to this page: job logs, reconciliation report, cost worksheet, post-mortem if any.
