text: Summary: This playbook defines the governance, telemetry contracts, safety gates, and SLA carveouts for enterprise model migrations and cutovers. It is intended for Customer Success (CS), SRE, Product, and Security stakeholders who jointly own customer-facing model upgrades or tenant re-allocations (hosted, dedicated, or private). Why this exists: - Model migrations change output fidelity, token cost, and performance characteristics. Customers expect minimal disruption and an auditable trail for decisions that affect SLA eligibility and service credits. - This document standardizes pre-cutover readiness checks, telemetry contracts, launch gates, and a clear escalation matrix to reduce surprise rollbacks and disputes. Scope: - Applies to: enterprise tenant cutovers, major model version rollouts (e.g., gen-1 -> gen-2), quantization or architecture changes that affect response semantics, and high-risk routing changes (regional failover). - Excludes: routine minor model config tweaks that are covered by auto-canary thresholds (see auto-canary spec). Owners and roles: - CS Owner: Aisha Patel — customer comms and consent - Cutover Lead (SRE): Miguel Fernandez — orchestration, telemetry gating - Product Lead: Emily Chen — model selection, rollback criteria - Security/Compliance: Rajesh Kumar — data residency and KMS checks - Billing/FinOps reviewer: Priya Menon — cost impact and SLA carveouts Pre-cutover checklist (gates that must be green before customer-facing switch): 1) Telemetry
title: Model Migration & Cutover Governance: telemetry contracts, safety gates, and SLA carveouts

text: Kumar — data residency and KMS checks - Billing/FinOps reviewer: Priya Menon — cost impact and SLA carveouts Pre-cutover checklist (gates that must be green before customer-facing switch): 1) Telemetry contract signed (CS/SRE/Product) and baselines captured (see telemetry snippet). 2) Synthetic regression suite pass: latency, p99, token-per-request, and quality checks (at least 95% of prompt set within expected metric bands). 3) Observability enabled: tenant-level traces, sample output capture, and per-request model-id tagging. 4) Backout plan documented: automated switchback playbook, expected RTO (<= 5 mins) and RPO (<= 0 requests). 5) SLA carveout agreement noted for migration window (timeboxed with explicit credit formula). 6) Security & compliance signoff for any new network egress or KMS usage. Telemetry contract (example JSON snippet to be checked in with cutover): { "contract_version": "1.0", "signals": [ {"name": "latency_p95_ms", "sampling": "100% request-level", "owner": "sre"}, {"name": "token_rate_per_min", "sampling": "aggregate per-minute", "owner": "billing"}, {"name": "semantic_regression_pct", "sampling": "per-prompt-suite daily", "owner": "product"}, {"name": "kv_cache_hit_rate", "sampling": "1s sliding window", "owner": "runtime"} ], "retention": "90 days", "access": ["cs-team", "sre-oncall", "product"] } Minimum signal thresholds and actions (table): | Signal | Threshold (migration baseline) | Immediate action if breached | Owner | |---|---:|---|---| | latency_p95_ms
title: Model Migration & Cutover Governance: telemetry contracts, safety gates, and SLA carveouts

text: ["cs-team", "sre-oncall", "product"] } Minimum signal thresholds and actions (table): | Signal | Threshold (migration baseline) | Immediate action if breached | Owner | |---|---:|---|---| | latency_p95_ms | <= 1200 ms | Abort migration, switchback within 5m | SRE | | latency_p99_ms | <= 3000 ms | Trigger L2 paging and investigate | SRE/Product | | semantic_regression_pct | <= 7% deviation vs baseline | Hold rollout, run extended evals | Product/CS | | token_rate_per_min | < 3x expected | Auto-scale or throttle; notify FinOps | Runtime/FinOps | | kv_cache_hit_rate | >= 60% | Proceed; if <40% abort and investigate cache warming | Runtime | Cutover day runbook (high-level steps): 1. T-minus 4 hours: Finalize customer comms (scheduled window, expected impacts, on-call roster). CS sends templated notice (see Slack template below). 2. T-minus 2 hours: Run a full synthetic pass and upload results artifact to cutover ticket. 3. T-minus 30 minutes: Confirm telemetry contract live, sampling turned on, and retention set to 90 days. 4. T-minus 5 minutes: Set circuit-breaker thresholds to conservative values. 5. T0: Execute canary at 1% of traffic for 15 minutes. Monitor signals every minute. 6. T0 + 15m: If canary green, ramp to 10% for 20 minutes (automated step). 7. Progressive ramps: 10% -> 50% -> 100% with hold windows and explicit approvals from Cutover Lead. 8. Post-switch: Run end-to-end customer acceptance prompts and log outputs to the customer audit stream. Rollback criteria (explicit,
title: Model Migration & Cutover Governance: telemetry contracts, safety gates, and SLA carveouts

text: -> 100% with hold windows and explicit approvals from Cutover Lead. 8. Post-switch: Run end-to-end customer acceptance prompts and log outputs to the customer audit stream. Rollback criteria (explicit, must be documented before T-minus 2h): - Any threshold in "Immediate action" column breached. - >10% of customer-critical prompt-suite failures in a 10-minute window. - p99 latency > 2x baseline for 5 consecutive minutes. - Unexpected increase in token billing estimate > 25% over projection for the hour. If rollback is triggered: execute automated switchback, open an incident in the Incident Tracker, and initiate a post-mortem owner assignment within 24 hours. Escalation matrix (who to page in order): 1) Cutover Lead (SRE) -> Miguel Fernandez (primary), paging channel: #sre-cutover-paging 2) Product (model quality) -> Emily Chen (primary) 3) CS escalation -> Aisha Patel (customer updates & acceptance) 4) Security -> Rajesh Kumar (if data-residency/KMS issues suspected) 5) FinOps -> Priya Menon (billing anomalies) 6) Exec on-call -> Engineering Director (if customer outage > 60 mins or material SLA dispute) Slack templates (use before/after cutover): - Pre-cutover notice (CS -> customer): Hi <customer-team>, We will start a scheduled model upgrade at <start-time UTC>. Expected user-visible changes: - latency: +/- small variance - improved semantic accuracy for X prompts We expect a <duration> window; if you observe regressions after <T+30m> please open a support ticket and we will
title: Model Migration & Cutover Governance: telemetry contracts, safety gates, and SLA carveouts

text: user-visible changes: - latency: +/- small variance - improved semantic accuracy for X prompts We expect a <duration> window; if you observe regressions after <T+30m> please open a support ticket and we will prioritize. - Internal rapid update (SRE -> stakeholders): Cutover update: stage=<canary|ramp|complete|rollback>, traffic=<percent>, key_signals={latency_p95: 420ms, semantic_regression: 1.2%}. Next decision in 10m. Post-cutover validation checklist (within 24h): - Confirm autoscaling behaved as expected and no abnormal token spikes. - Confirm synthetic regression suite still within expectations. - Execute run of customer-critical prompts and collect adjudicated pass/fail. - Prepare a short runbook for customer-facing operational quirks observed. - Schedule QBR slot to review migration metrics and SLA carveouts if any. SLA carveout template (example language): - Time window: <start> to <end> (migration window) - Carveout scope: latency and availability credit eligibility paused for customer-impact incidents directly caused by model semantic regressions discovered during the window (requires documented trial and rollback attempts). - Credit formula: prorated daily uptime * 0.5 for migration window if rollback executed; otherwise standard credit rules apply. Decisions require signoff from CS + FinOps. Dispute resolution steps (if customer rejects migration outcome): 1. CS collects adjudicated evidence: sampled outputs, synthetic suite results, and telemetry. 2. Convene 3-way
title: Model Migration & Cutover Governance: telemetry contracts, safety gates, and SLA carveouts

text: signoff from CS + FinOps. Dispute resolution steps (if customer rejects migration outcome): 1. CS collects adjudicated evidence: sampled outputs, synthetic suite results, and telemetry. 2. Convene 3-way review (CS/Product/SRE) within 48 hours to attempt remediation or offer rollback. 3. If unresolved, escalate to Legal & Finance for SLA credit arbitration. Postmortem and QBR integration: - Any migration with rollback or with a carveout invoked must produce a 1-page incident summary and a 45-minute QBR deep-dive within the next quarterly QBR cycle. - Include the following QBR artifacts: baseline vs actual telemetry charts, adjudicated prompt examples, cost delta analysis, and customer sentiment summary. Appendix A: Example queries to validate telemetry (CLI): # p95 latency for tenant-123 in last 15m redwood metrics query --tenant tenant-123 --metric latency_p95_ms --range 15m # sample output capture for 10 requests containing prompt-set id 987 redwood audit fetch --tenant tenant-123 --prompt-set 987 --limit 10 --output sample.json Appendix B: Sample automated canary policy (YAML): policy: name: model-migration-canary steps: - percent: 1 duration: 15m pass_criteria: latency_p95_ms: <1200 semantic_regression_pct: <7 - percent: 10 duration: 20m approval: cutover_lead - percent: 50 duration: 10m approval: cs_owner Appendix C: Contacts and paging channels: - SRE Cutover: miguel.fernandez@redwood.ai, slack: @miguel_f (channel #sre-cutover-paging) - Product: emily.chen@redwood.ai, slack:

text: Overview This playbook defines the post-provisioning stability and fallback verification steps we run for enterprise customers after Dedicated or Private (VPC / on-prem) environments are provisioned and before handing back to Product/Engineering for live traffic. The goal is to validate functional correctness, confirm SLOs and capacity assumptions, exercise fallback and routing policies, and confirm monitoring/alerting is wired end-to-end. This document is intended for Customer Success Engineers (CSEs), Solutions Engineers, and the Redwood SRE/Platform teams. Key objectives - Verify baseline latency and throughput for representative workloads. - Confirm automatic fallback paths and model variant switchover behave as expected. - Validate KV/prefix cache priming and persistence (where applicable). - Ensure observability: dashboards, tracing, and alerting triggerable and readable by customer ops. - Document owners, rollback criteria, and escalation steps. When to run - Immediately after dedicated/private environment provisioning completes and the control plane shows "provisioned" state. - After canary deploys or model version upgrades prior to 100% traffic. - After network configuration changes, routing policy updates, or capacity reservations change. Prerequisites - Customer tenant ID and API credentials with a staging/test API key. - Access to the customer's VPC peering / VPN details (for Private deployments). - A canonical set of prompts (prompt-set) representative of expected
title: Enterprise Onboarding — Post-Provisioning Stability & Fallback Checklist

text: ID and API credentials with a staging/test API key. - Access to the customer's VPC peering / VPN details (for Private deployments). - A canonical set of prompts (prompt-set) representative of expected traffic; include edge cases. - Access to Redwood Console for the tenant and to internal SRE dashboards. - Test VM or container with curl / python / k6 installed for load testing. Stability checklist (high-level) 1) Connectivity and auth checks - Confirm control-plane API responds: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <STAGING_KEY>" https://api.redwood.ai/v1/account/status - Verify TLS certificates are valid and CN/SANs match the customer's domain where relevant. - For Private: validate VPC routes and firewall rules allow traffic to inference nodes on ports 443 and internal gRPC ports (if used). 2) Basic functional smoke tests (AND golden outputs) - Run 10 deterministic prompts from the provided prompt-set, assert no 5xx errors. - Capture token counts and ensure shape matches expected model behavior. Example curl: curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $STAGING_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \n -d '{"model":"redwood-rt-1","input":"Translate to French: The server is healthy."}' \n https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate | jq '.' 3) Latency and tail latency verification - Run 50 sequential single-request latency samples. Compute P50/P90/P99. Acceptable pass thresholds: P50 < 120ms, P90 < 250ms, P99 < 800ms for short-context workloads (<=256 tokens). - Document results in the Handback Report
title: Enterprise Onboarding — Post-Provisioning Stability & Fallback Checklist

text: single-request latency samples. Compute P50/P90/P99. Acceptable pass thresholds: P50 < 120ms, P90 < 250ms, P99 < 800ms for short-context workloads (<=256 tokens). - Document results in the Handback Report (see "Handback template" section). 4) Throughput and concurrency test - Use k6 or a simple Python worker to simulate concurrent clients. Start with concurrency=8, ramp to concurrency=64 over 5 minutes. Goal: No 5xx errors and <5% request error budget. - Observe autoscaler behavior (Dedicated only): confirm new worker pods/instances spawn and throughput increases. 5) KV / prefix cache priming and persistence - For workloads that benefit from KV cache (chat rerendering or repeated prefix expansions), run a session workload that primes the cache. Note cache hit rate in runtime metrics (target > 60% after priming for representative sessions). - Restart one serving process (or node) to verify cache recovery and TTL settings. 6) Model variant fallback and routing exercise - Intentionally create a synthetic capacity constraint: apply a routing policy that returns the model to a "degraded" variant (e.g., quantized lower-cost variant). Validate end-to-end: requests succeed, outputs remain within quality guardrails, and customer-visible headers indicate fallback. - Confirm automated rollback triggers fire if quality/regression monitors cross threshold (see "Quality thresholds"). 7) Observability and alerting verification - Confirm dashboards show: request rate, latency percentiles, error
title: Enterprise Onboarding — Post-Provisioning Stability & Fallback Checklist

text: triggers fire if quality/regression monitors cross threshold (see "Quality thresholds"). 7) Observability and alerting verification - Confirm dashboards show: request rate, latency percentiles, error rate, KV cache hit rate, token cost per request. - Trigger a synthetic alert by causing a controlled 5xx surge and confirm SRE-oncall receives notifications and that pager routing matches the runbook. 8) Cost and billing sanity check - Run a short burst of typical workload and compare estimated cost vs. forecasted unit economics. Flag >20% deviation to finance/CS ops. Quality thresholds (automated regression gates) | Metric | Acceptable | Warning | Failure | |---|---:|---:|---:| | P50 latency (short context) | <120ms | 120-250ms | >250ms | | P99 latency | <800ms | 800-1500ms | >1500ms | | 5xx error rate | <0.5% | 0.5%-2% | >2% | | Prompt-level BLEU/ROUGE change (sample eval) | <1% delta | 1%-3% | >3% | | KV cache hit rate (primed) | >60% | 40%-60% | <40% | Handback template (required for sign-off) - Customer: <tenant-name> - Environment: Dedicated / Private (VPC) - Provisioned at: <timestamp> - Test key used: staging-xxxxx - Test prompt-set summary: (attach file or link) - Latency P50/P90/P99: <values> - Peak throughput achieved (rps): <value> - Errors: 5xx count and % - Cache hit rates: <values> - Fallback exercised: yes/no and notes - Open action items: list owners and due dates - Acceptance: CSE signoff and customer technical contact signoff (names & signatures) Runbook — rapid
title: Enterprise Onboarding — Post-Provisioning Stability & Fallback Checklist

text: rates: <values> - Fallback exercised: yes/no and notes - Open action items: list owners and due dates - Acceptance: CSE signoff and customer technical contact signoff (names & signatures) Runbook — rapid remediation steps (triage first 15 minutes) 1) If >2% 5xx errors or P99 > 1.5x expected: immediately switch traffic to fallback model variant or lower-load region according to routing policy. Owner: SRE (pager). 2) If autoscaler fails to add capacity under load: scale to reserved capacity (Dedicated) or request capacity burst via capacity API. Owner: Platform Ops. 3) If KV cache hit rate drops >30% after restart: check cache mount points, persistence config, and system logs (service: redwood-kv). Owner: Runtime team. 4) If customer cannot authenticate: verify API key rotation and KMS/HSM integration for Private. Owner: Customer Success & Security. Escalation matrix - Customer-facing CSE: first responder for customer communication. - Platform SRE (pager): handles capacity, autoscaling, and high-traffic incidents. - Runtime engineering on-call: kernel/runtime regressions, KVCACHE corruption. - Security on-call: suspected credential compromise or data exfiltration. - Escalation channels: Slack channel #cs-enterprise-ops, PagerDuty service "redwood-dedicated-prod", and email enterprise-ops@redwood.ai. Example troubleshooting commands and snippets - Check API health: curl -I -H "Authorization: Bearer $STAGING_KEY" https://api.redwood.ai/v1/health - Query runtime metrics (internal)
title: Enterprise Onboarding — Post-Provisioning Stability & Fallback Checklist

text: enterprise-ops@redwood.ai. Example troubleshooting commands and snippets - Check API health: curl -I -H "Authorization: Bearer $STAGING_KEY" https://api.redwood.ai/v1/health - Query runtime metrics (internal) via Prometheus API: curl -s "https://prometheus.internal/api/v1/query?query=redwood_request_duration_seconds_p99{tenant=\"<tenant>\"}" | jq . - Priming a chat session (Python): import requests resp = requests.post('https://api.redwood.ai/v1/chat', headers={'Authorization':f'Bearer {KEY}'}, json={'model':'redwood-rt-1','messages':[{'role':'user','content':'Hello world'}]}) print(resp.json()) Healthchecks config example (YAML) apiHealthcheck: path: /v1/health expected_status: 200 timeout_ms: 1500 latencySloProbe: percentile: p99 threshold_ms: 800 sample_interval_s: 30 Acceptance and sign-off process 1) CSE runs checklist and fills Handback template. 2) Share results with customer technical contact and SRE for review. 3) Hold a 30-minute go/no-go call: review anomalies and mitigation plans. 4) Once customer and CSE sign off, mark the environment as "Customer Ready" in the provisioning system and schedule a production cutover window. Post-handback operational recommendations (first 30 days) - Weekly: compare observed unit costs vs. forecast. - Twice weekly: run the smoke prompt-set and store results in the evaluation harness. - Daily for first 72 hours of live traffic: monitor P99 and 5xx errors on Redwood Console and keep an open incident channel. Ownership and contact info
title: Enterprise Onboarding — Post-Provisioning Stability & Fallback Checklist

text:
Summary:
This playbook defines steps, telemetry gate criteria, and cost-gating policies for cross-regional enterprise tenant cutovers. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, Solutions Engineers, SRE on-call, and the customer’s cloud/networking contact. The goal is a safe, observable cross-region activation with automatic failback triggers and cost mitigation if unexpected billing or token-rate anomalies occur.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to enterprise customers migrating from single-region hosted or Dedicated deployments to multi-region (active-primary + passive-secondary) configurations during go-live windows.
- Covers pre-cutover verification, telemetry gate definitions, automated gate enforcement, manual and automatic failback, cost gating thresholds, and post-cutover QBR metrics.
- Does NOT replace tenant-specific security acceptance tests (see security gates playbook).

Roles and ownership:
- Customer Success (CS): runbook owner, coordinates stakeholders and business signoffs.
- Solutions Engineering (SE): pre-cutover config and ingress verification.
- Eng-SRE: on-call for capacity and traffic shaping; owns automated failback tooling.
- Customer (networking/security): provide firewall, routing, and cert rotations.
- Escalation: follow standard escalation matrix; primary CS owns customer comms.

Pre-cutover checklist (must be completed no earlier than 72 hours before scheduled cutover):
1) Capacity & model pinning
 - Verify dedicated pools
title: Cross-Regional Cutover, Telemetry Gates, and Cost-Gating Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text:
escalation matrix; primary CS owns customer comms.

Pre-cutover checklist (must be completed no earlier than 72 hours before scheduled cutover):
1) Capacity & model pinning
 - Verify dedicated pools or reserved capacity are provisioned in target region(s) with 20% buffer over expected steady-state TPS.
 - Confirm model versions and quantization profiles are pinned in tenant config (model_id + version).
2) Network & certs
 - Confirm peering or Transit Gateway is established; test 3-way TCP handshake to each inference proxy IP.
 - Ensure client certs and ingress certs rotate with no downtime (test cert chain).
3) Telemetry gating assertions deployed in gatekeeper service
 - Install telemetry assertions bundle (JSON spec) to customer-facing gatekeeper namespace.
4) Cost guard setup
 - Define billing thresholds and emergency cost caps in Tenant FinOps panel; add CS and finance contacts.
5) Synthetic workload
 - Create tenant-specific synthetic journeys that emulate common request shapes (chat, streaming, embeddings).

Telemetry Gate Criteria (these must be green to proceed):
- Latency SLOs (per-route and per-model): 99th percentile latency for short-chat (<=128 tokens) must be <= 350ms.
- Token success rate: >= 99.5% for tokenized responses (no stream resets, 4xx/5xx).
- Error budget burn rate: must be < 2x baseline for the 30-minute rolling window.
- Cost per 1k tokens deviation: <= +25% vs expected unit cost baseline for the first 60 minutes.
- Synthetic fidelity:
title: Cross-Regional Cutover, Telemetry Gates, and Cost-Gating Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text:
Error budget burn rate: must be < 2x baseline for the 30-minute rolling window.
- Cost per 1k tokens deviation: <= +25% vs expected unit cost baseline for the first 60 minutes.
- Synthetic fidelity: synthetic journeys must achieve >= 95% success (passes semantic assertions).

Telemetry Gate Table (quick reference - assertions enforced by Gatekeeper):
- "latency_p99_short_chat": threshold 350ms, evaluation_window 30m, action "hold"
- "token_success_rate": threshold 99.5%, evaluation_window 15m, action "alert"
- "error_budget_burn": threshold 2x, evaluation_window 30m, action "failback"
- "cost_delta_1k": threshold +25%, evaluation_window 60m, action "cost-cap"
- "synthetic_journey_pass_rate": threshold 95%, evaluation_window 15m, action "hold"

Cutover procedure (step-by-step):
Pre-cutover (T-minus 2 hours):
1. Notify stakeholders via Cutover channel and update status page.
2. Run a full synthetic run (3 iterations) from multiple regions; validate gate assertions locally.
3. Ensure autoscaling groups are warm and KV caches are primed with representative prefixes.

At cutover (execution window):
1. Gradual DNS / routing change: shift 10% of production traffic to target region for 10 minutes. Observe telemetry gates in real time.
2. Increase to 30% for 15 minutes if gates remain green; run synthetic journeys.
3. Proceed to 60% for 20 minutes; monitor cost delta closely.
4. Final 100% switchover only allowed if all gate assertions remained
title: Cross-Regional Cutover, Telemetry Gates, and Cost-Gating Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text:
30% for 15 minutes if gates remain green; run synthetic journeys.
3. Proceed to 60% for 20 minutes; monitor cost delta closely.
4. Final 100% switchover only allowed if all gate assertions remained green across the incremental windows.

Automated failback and remediation rules:
- Immediate automatic failback to previous region (or queued fallback model pool) if any gate with action "failback" trips (e.g., error_budget_burn).
- Throttled rollback (graceful): if "hold" gates trip, cut traffic back by 25% and run diagnostics.
- Cost-cap trigger: when cost_delta_1k > threshold, automatically enable token-rate limiters for non-critical routes and notify CS/FinOps.

Failback runbook (SRE execution):
1) On alert, acknowledge and open incident in PagerDuty with tag "cross-regional-cutover".
2) Execute automated rollback script: /opt/redwood/bin/region-failback --tenant $TENANT --target-region $PRIMARY --reason $ALERT_TAG
3) If rollback script fails, perform manual routing rollback via the routing console (steps included in appendix).
4) Post-rollback: collect the following artifacts within first 30 minutes: trace slices (x-trace ids), pod logs, gatekeeper assertion logs, synthetic journey results. Attach to incident.

Cost-gating and emergency billing protection:
- Emergency cost cap: default = 200% of daily expected spend (tenant-configurable). When reached: (a) pause low-priority embedding jobs and batch ingestion, (b) enable per-API token caps, (c) notify FinOps
title: Cross-Regional Cutover, Telemetry Gates, and Cost-Gating Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text:
Emergency cost cap: default = 200% of daily expected spend (tenant-configurable). When reached: (a) pause low-priority embedding jobs and batch ingestion, (b) enable per-API token caps, (c) notify FinOps and customer billing contact.
- Tiered actions by severity:
 - Severity 1 (blocker): immediate pause of all non-essential routes and rollback to previous topology.
 - Severity 2 (high): apply 50% token rate reduction to best-effort routes, keep primary customer transactions.
 - Severity 3 (medium): raise alert to CS and schedule QBR follow-up to review FinOps anomalies.

Communication templates (use in Cutover channel and customer emails):
- Short update (while cutover in progress): "Cutover to region eu-west-1 at 10:10 UTC: currently 30% traffic, gates nominal. Will update in 15m or on exception."
- Failback notice: "Automated failback executed for tenant <TENANT>. Reason: <GATE_NAME> tripped. CS lead <NAME> coordinating next steps. Incident: <PD_LINK>"

Post-cutover QBR & SLA evaluation (first 72 hours):
- Metrics to collect and include in QBR: 99/95/50 latency percentiles by route, token success rate, per-model cost per 1k tokens, synthetic journey pass rate, error budget burn curves, and customer-facing error traces.
- Evaluation framework: compare each metric to pre-cutover baseline and flag regressions >10% for prioritized RCA.
- Suggested agenda items (30–45 minute review): cutover timeline, telemetry gate events, cost delta analysis, customer impact summary,
title: Cross-Regional Cutover, Telemetry Gates, and Cost-Gating Playbook for Enterprise Onboarding

text:
pre-cutover baseline and flag regressions >10% for prioritized RCA.
- Suggested agenda items (30–45 minute review): cutover timeline, telemetry gate events, cost delta analysis, customer impact summary, action items and owners.

Appendix A — Example Gatekeeper assertion JSON (deploy via gatekeeper-api):
{
 "assertions": [
 {"name": "latency_p99_short_chat", "type": "p99_latency", "threshold_ms": 350, "window_minutes": 30, "action": "hold"},
 {"name": "cost_delta_1k", "type": "unit_cost_delta", "threshold_pct": 25, "window_minutes": 60, "action": "cost-cap"}
 ]
}

Appendix B — Routing console quick commands:
- Check active weights: curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' https://routing-console.prod/api/tenants/$TENANT/weights
- Set weight: POST to /api/tenants/$TENANT/weights {"region":"eu-west-1","weight":30}

Appendix C — Escalation contacts (order):

text: Purpose This document is a practical, sequence-driven checklist to validate network, firewall, and peering configurations during enterprise private deployment onboarding (VPC and on-prem). It consolidates recommended rules, verification steps, and escalation paths to minimize go-live delays caused by connectivity issues between customer networks and Redwood Inference Private control and data planes. Audience - Customer network and security teams - Redwood Solutions Engineering (SE) and Customer Success (CS) onboarding leads - Redwood Private deployment engineers Scope and intent This checklist covers: - Outbound and inbound firewall rules for control plane and data plane services - VPC peering and route validation patterns - Basic NAT and proxy considerations - Diagnostic commands and test vectors to validate traffic flows - Acceptance criteria and how to escalate unresolved issues Summary of assumptions 1. Customer environment: VPC (AWS/Azure/GCP) or equivalent private network with ability to add routes/security-group/firewall rules. 2. Customer will provide a network contact, CIDR ranges, and preferred peering method (VPC peering, Transit Gateway, PrivateLink, VPN, or dedicated circuit). 3. Private deployment uses two logical planes: control-plane endpoints (API/management) and data-plane endpoints (inference endpoints, egress to telemetry). Network ownership and run roles - Customer network owner: single point-of-contact for rule changes and routing (required). - Redwood SE
title: Private Network: Firewall & Peering Checklist for Enterprise Onboarding

text: data-plane endpoints (inference endpoints, egress to telemetry). Network ownership and run roles - Customer network owner: single point-of-contact for rule changes and routing (required). - Redwood SE onboarding lead: coordinates validation steps and performs Redwood-side checks. - Redwood Private ops: accepts packet captures / logs and performs further investigation when escalated. Checklist: Pre-provision (customer to confirm before scheduling provisioning) 1) Provide required values (email these to SE): - Primary VPC CIDR(s) and subnets (e.g., 10.100.0.0/16) - NAT / egress architecture details (NAT Gateway or egress proxies) - Peering preference: VPC Peering / Transit Gateway / PrivateLink / VPN / Direct Connect - Egress to 3rd-party KMS or identity provider endpoints (if used) - Internal DNS resolution strategy (will customer resolve Redwood hostnames internally?) 2) Schedule a 60m network validation call with network owners and Redwood SE. Firewall and routing rules (required) Apply these rules to customer perimeter firewalls / security groups / network appliances. Replace example CIDRs and hostnames with the values provided by Redwood in the deployment manifest. | Purpose | Protocol | Direction | Destination / Port | Notes | |---|---:|---|---:|---| | Control plane API (management) | TCP | Outbound | redwood-control.example.com:443 | TLS 1.2+; allow SNI "redwood-control.example.com" | | Data plane inference endpoints | TCP | Outbound / Peered | 10.200.0.0/22:443 | Customer
title: Private Network: Firewall & Peering Checklist for Enterprise Onboarding

text: (management) | TCP | Outbound | redwood-control.example.com:443 | TLS 1.2+; allow SNI "redwood-control.example.com" | | Data plane inference endpoints | TCP | Outbound / Peered | 10.200.0.0/22:443 | Customer should allow peered subnet range for inference requests | | KV cache and internal telemetry | TCP | Outbound | 10.201.0.0/24:443 | Encryption required; avoid deep packet inspection | | Healthcheck probes (from Redwood) | TCP | Inbound (when enabled) | <customer-health-probe-ip>:443 | Optional; customer may whitelist Redwood probe IPs | | NTP (time sync) | UDP | Outbound | 0.pool.ntp.org:123 or NTP server IPs | Time skew > 2s can break TLS session validation | | DNS resolution | UDP/TCP | Outbound | <customer-dns-ips>:53 | If private DNS resolvers used, confirm recursive replies for redwood hostnames | Note: If the deployment uses PrivateLink/Private Endpoint, data-plane traffic does not require NATed egress but still requires Security Group/ACL rules permitting the private endpoint. Peering and route validation - VPC Peering: Ensure both sides have explicit route table entries for each other's CIDRs pointing to the peering attachment. No overlapping CIDRs allowed. - Transit Gateway: Confirm route propagation and attachment associations include the subnets in scope. - VPN / Direct Connect: Verify BGP prefixes and BGP session state are UP (use show ip bgp summary / cloud console BGP diagnostics). - PrivateLink: Validate ENI creation in the customer subnet and that security
title: Private Network: Firewall & Peering Checklist for Enterprise Onboarding

text: Direct Connect: Verify BGP prefixes and BGP session state are UP (use show ip bgp summary / cloud console BGP diagnostics). - PrivateLink: Validate ENI creation in the customer subnet and that security groups allow traffic to the service endpoint. Test vectors and verification steps (execute with SE) 1) DNS validation - From a host in-customer-VPC: nslookup redwood-control.example.com <customer-dns-ip> - Expected: resolves to Redwood-provided control-plane IPs (or private endpoint IPs) 2) TCP handshake and TLS validation - curl -v --resolve 'redwood-control.example.com:443:<ip>' https://redwood-control.example.com/health - Expected: HTTP 200 and a valid TLS certificate matching the hostname. If certificate is customer-mTLS, provide client certs. 3) Peering route test (ICMP/TCP) - ping <data-plane-ip> (if ICMP allowed) or - nc -vz <data-plane-ip> 443 - Expected: connection establishes within 50ms for same-region peering or within agreed latency SLO. 4) End-to-end inference call test - curl -X POST https://<inference-host>/v1/generate -d '{"prompt":"ping"}' -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' - Expected: 200 with model response; token auth validated. Record request latency and token usage. 5) Telemetry and metrics egress - Confirm outbound connectivity to telemetry endpoints (if using SaaS telemetry) or ensure logs are forwarded to provided collector address. Acceptance criteria (must be green to proceed to provisioning) - Control-plane API resolves
title: Private Network: Firewall & Peering Checklist for Enterprise Onboarding

text: to telemetry endpoints (if using SaaS telemetry) or ensure logs are forwarded to provided collector address. Acceptance criteria (must be green to proceed to provisioning) - Control-plane API resolves and TLS handshake succeeds from a customer-hosted instance. - Data-plane CIDRs are routable across peering attachment and security groups allow TCP/443. - End-to-end inference call completes successfully from a customer test VM. - Time synchronization is within tolerance (+/- 2s). - Agreed monitoring/probe IPs are whitelisted if customer requires inbound probes. Common failure modes and quick mitigations 1) DNS resolves to public IP but customer expected private endpoint - Cause: split-horizon DNS not configured or DNS forwarding missing. - Mitigation: Update private DNS zone with records provided by Redwood or configure conditional forwarding to Redwood DNS resolver. 2) Connection times out to data plane IP - Cause: Route table missing peering route or security group denying egress. - Mitigation: Validate route table entries and security group/ACLs; run traceroute to identify hop where packets drop. 3) TLS handshake fails with certificate name mismatch - Cause: using IP address instead of hostname or incorrect SNI through proxy. - Mitigation: Use hostname in SNI; update proxy to forward Host header and SNI. 4) Intermittent failures under load - Cause: asymmetric routing, stateful firewall timeouts, or SNAT port exhaustion. - Mitigation: Use connection tracking tuning, increase
title: Private Network: Firewall & Peering Checklist for Enterprise Onboarding

text: forward Host header and SNI. 4) Intermittent failures under load - Cause: asymmetric routing, stateful firewall timeouts, or SNAT port exhaustion. - Mitigation: Use connection tracking tuning, increase ephemeral port range, or enable NAT gateway autoscaling patterns. Diagnostic commands matrix (copy/paste friendly) - DNS: dig +short redwood-control.example.com @<customer-dns> - TLS: openssl s_client -connect <ip>:443 -servername redwood-control.example.com - TCP: nc -vz <ip> 443 - Traceroute: traceroute <ip> (Linux) / tracert <ip> (Windows) - BGP: (on-customer-router) show ip bgp summary Escalation path (when checklist verification fails) 1) Network owner coordinates immediate troubleshooting with Redwood SE during the validation call. 2) If unresolved within 60 minutes, SE opens a Customer Incident with level: "network-blocking" and notifies Redwood Private Ops. 3) Private Ops will collect packet capture (pcap) from both sides where possible and run a 1:1 session diagnostic. (Provide pcap, timestamps, and test vectors.) 4) If vendor appliance or carrier issue suspected (Transit GW, Direct Connect), escalate to cloud provider support with session history and BGP details. On-call and SLAs - Redwood will aim to respond to network-blocking incidents within 30 minutes during business hours and 60 minutes outside business hours per the Enterprise onboarding commitment. - Target resolution time depends on root cause: misconfiguration (<= 8 hours), provider/carrier (<72 hours with provider
title: Private Network: Firewall & Peering Checklist for Enterprise Onboarding

text: and 60 minutes outside business hours per the Enterprise onboarding commitment. - Target resolution time depends on root cause: misconfiguration (<= 8 hours), provider/carrier (<72 hours with provider support). Customer checklist sign-off (to be completed by customer network owner) 1) I confirm the fields in the provisioning manifest are accurate. 2) I confirm the firewall and route table entries listed above are applied and verified. 3) I confirm the end-to-end inference test succeeded and recorded latency is within acceptable bounds. Signed: _______________________ Name: _______________________ Date: _______________________ Appendix: Example iptables rules (customer Linux NAT host) # Allow outbound TLS to Redwood control plane iptables -A OUTPUT -p tcp -d <redwood-control-ip> --dport 443 -m conntrack --ctstate NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT # Allow established/related inbound for return traffic iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT Appendix: When using an egress proxy (common enterprise pattern) - Ensure SNI passthrough (not SNI rewrite) for redwood-control.example.com or configure explicit proxy bypass for the Redwood control/data-plane domains. - If mTLS is used, ensure the proxy allows client certificate forwarding or use a sidecar that terminates mTLS locally and forwards plaintext to the proxy only within the VPC. References and related artifacts - Deployment manifest template (provided during provisioning): includes IP/CIDR map, hostnames,
title: Private Network: Firewall & Peering Checklist for Enterprise Onboarding

text: terminates mTLS locally and forwards plaintext to the proxy only within the VPC. References and related artifacts - Deployment manifest template (provided during provisioning): includes IP/CIDR map, hostnames, and required probe IPs. - Troubleshooting playbook: /confluence/customer-success-and-support/support-process/enterprise-ratelimit-quota-adjustment-and-cost-protection-playbook-2026 (for non-network related failures). - Eng private deployments reference: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/reference-architectures/vpc-peering-patterns Document change log - 2026-02-10: Created by Ava Martinez (initial checklist and tests). - 2026-03-02: Reviewed and expanded failure modes and diagnostic commands (reviewers Marcus Chen, Priya Nair).

text: Overview This playbook describes the operational steps, monitoring, and escalation paths to ensure a stable migration/cutover for enterprise customers moving traffic to Redwood (Hosted / Dedicated / Private). It is intended for Customer Success, SRE, and the customer's technical owners. The document covers pre-cutover validation, immediate cutover actions (0–2 hours), degradation thresholds and automated mitigations, rollback criteria, and the 72-hour stabilization window. When to use - Planned migrations from another provider or self-hosted inference to Redwood Dedicated or Private. - Large traffic shifts (>= 50% traffic change) or model variant swaps for production customers. - Post-contract go-lives where SLAs and cost constraints are in effect. Key goals - Maintain SLOs: p95 latency < 500 ms (generation endpoints), error rate < 0.5% (5xx + client failures), embedding throughput within agreed baseline. - Detect and contain regressions within 5–15 minutes. - Provide deterministic rollback when criteria are exceeded. Stakeholders and primary contacts - Customer Success (primary owner): Aisha Patel (lead) — oncall: +1-415-555-0172, pager: @cs-oncall - SRE/Platform: Carlos Rivera — slack: #sre-redwood, pager: @sre-oncall - Serving Runtime Lead: Sophie Nguyen — slack: @sophie-nguyen - Customer Technical Contact: (populate from onboarding worksheet) — recommended escalation cadence included below Pre-cutover checklist (complete 72–1 hours before cutover) 1. Configuration & access
title: Migration cutover stability playbook

text: - Customer Technical Contact: (populate from onboarding worksheet) — recommended escalation cadence included below Pre-cutover checklist (complete 72–1 hours before cutover) 1. Configuration & access - Confirm API keys and IP allowlists are provisioned. Example allowed CIDR list for customer egress: 10.2.0.0/16, 198.51.100.0/24. - If Private: validate VPC peering and firewall rules; test TCP handshake to inference endpoints (TLS 443). 2. Capacity & model readiness - Ensure dedicated capacity pool size matches forecasted TPS + 30% buffer. Documented in order: gpu-pool-size and expected token/sec. - Model variant pinned for cutover (model-id, quantization profile, kernel tag). 3. Test plan and synthetic traffic - Prepare a synthetic traffic generator with representative prompt mixes (short/long sequences, embeddings, chat). Sample CLI: rcli simulate traffic --target https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate --concurrency 50 --profile enterprise-cutover.json - Validate KV cache priming: run warmup scripts that exercise common prefixes and expected session lengths. 4. Observability preps - Create a cutover dashboard (p90/p95/p99 latency, error rate, request rate, GPU util, queue depth per pool). - Ensure tracing is enabled for customer requests; add sample span tag customer_id and route. Immediate cutover steps (0–2 hours) 1. Communicate T-0 to stakeholders (status channel, customer contact). 2. Switch routing: update traffic router (or DNS) to send 10% of traffic to Redwood for 10 minutes,
title: Migration cutover stability playbook

text: Immediate cutover steps (0–2 hours) 1. Communicate T-0 to stakeholders (status channel, customer contact). 2. Switch routing: update traffic router (or DNS) to send 10% of traffic to Redwood for 10 minutes, then 50% for 15 minutes, then 100% if green (recommended gradual ramp). 3. During each ramp phase execute checklist: - Verify p95 latency remains < 500 ms. - Verify error rate < 0.5%. - Confirm no queue depth exceeding threshold (see Alert thresholds table below). Monitoring and health indicators Primary metrics (track in dashboard): - request.rate (rps) — baseline and delta - request.p95_latency_ms — target < 500 ms - request.error_rate_percent — target < 0.5% - gpu.utilization_percent — avoid sustained > 90% for more than 5 minutes - serving.queue_depth — alert when > 250 requests in queue per pool - kv_cache.hit_rate_percent — expected > 70% after warmup for session-heavy apps Alert thresholds (recommended) | Alert name | Trigger | Severity | Notes | |---|---:|---|---| | High error rate | error_rate_percent > 1.5% for 2m | P1 | Consider immediate rollback if sustained | | Latency spike | p95_latency_ms > 1200 for 3m | P1 | Investigate model or kernel bottleneck | | Queue saturation | queue_depth > 500 for 5m | P1 | Autoscale/limit ingestion; see mitigations | | GPU saturation | gpu.utilization_percent > 95% for 10m | P2 | Trigger capacity expansion/eviction policies | | KV cache missstorm | kv_cache.hit_rate_percent < 30% for 10m | P2 | May indicate warmup needed or routing
title: Migration cutover stability playbook

text: gpu.utilization_percent > 95% for 10m | P2 | Trigger capacity expansion/eviction policies | | KV cache missstorm | kv_cache.hit_rate_percent < 30% for 10m | P2 | May indicate warmup needed or routing mix issue | Degradation behavior and automated mitigations - For minor degradation (error_rate 0.5%–1.5% or p95 500–1200 ms): automatically throttle non-critical requests, reduce batch size, and enable fallback model variant with smaller footprint. - For severe degradation (error_rate > 1.5% or sustained queue_depth > 500): engage rollback to previous provider routing or previous model version. - Built-in automated actions (what Redwood platform can perform): - route.rebalance --move 50% traffic to fallback pool - model.variant_switch --to model-id:golden-small-quant Rollback criteria and procedure Rollback should be considered when ANY of the following are true for 10 minutes: - request.error_rate_percent > 2.0% AND increasing - p95_latency_ms > 1500 ms AND user impact confirmed - queue_depth > 800 or sustained GPU OOM errors Rollback steps (execute in order) 1. Notify stakeholders: open a migration incident and ping P1 channels. 2. Execute route rollback: revert routing to previous provider or traffic mirror. Command example: rcli traffic update --route customer-123 --percent 0 --target previous-provider 3. If rollback is to a previous model version in Redwood: rcli model promote --customer customer-123 --model-id "model-v1-stable" 4. Scale down any warm pool to reduce cost and
title: Migration cutover stability playbook

text: --target previous-provider 3. If rollback is to a previous model version in Redwood: rcli model promote --customer customer-123 --model-id "model-v1-stable" 4. Scale down any warm pool to reduce cost and surface residual errors for investigation. Escalation matrix and communication - Phase 0 (informal): Customer Success lead + customer tech contact (first 5 minutes). - Phase 1 (operational): Add SRE and Serving Runtime lead (10–30 minutes). - Phase 2 (critical): Engage Engineering on-call, Platform PM, SOC if security incident suspected (>30 minutes). Contact table (primary means) - Customer Success lead: Aisha Patel — slack: @aisha-patel, cell: +1-415-555-0172 - SRE oncall: Carlos Rivera — pager: @sre-oncall, slack: #sre-redwood - Runtime lead: Sophie Nguyen — slack DM or escalation channel #runtime-oncall Post-cutover stabilization (2–72 hours) - Hours 2–6: Keep traffic at production level while running heavy monitoring. Run daily regression checks against representative prompt set (n=5000). - 6–24 hours: Execute sample customer transactions end-to-end with logging enabled. Pay special attention to stateful chat sessions and embedding index builds. - 24–72 hours: Prepare a stabilization report with metrics and anomalies; schedule a review with the customer to confirm SLA satisfaction and plan any follow-up mitigations. Operational runbook snippets - Check p95 for the last 10 minutes: rcli metrics query --service inference --customer customer-123 --metric request.p95_latency_ms
title: Migration cutover stability playbook

text: satisfaction and plan any follow-up mitigations. Operational runbook snippets - Check p95 for the last 10 minutes: rcli metrics query --service inference --customer customer-123 --metric request.p95_latency_ms --range 10m - Promote fallback model (example JSON payload): { "customer_id": "customer-123", "action": "promote_model", "model_id": "model-v1-stable" } - Quick health summary (one-liner for status channel): cutover-status: green | p95=320ms | errs=0.12% | gpu=48% | qd=42 Postmortem and RCA guidance - If an incident occurs that required rollback or manual mitigation, create a postmortem within 72 hours. The postmortem should include: timeline, contributing factors, detection method, impact, remediation, and follow-up actions owners with SLAs (30 days for code fixes, 90 days for capacity changes). Appendices Appendix A — Example warmup script (bash): #!/usr/bin/env bash for i in {1..200}; do curl -s -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY" -d '{"prompt":"Summary of user event ${i}","max_tokens":64}' >/dev/null & done wait Appendix B — Useful links - Hosting status page: https://status.redwood.ai - Cutover dashboard template: /dashboards/cutover/migration-template - Incident runbook: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process/postmortem-template Notes and ownership - Owner: Customer Success (Aisha Patel). SRE and Runtime teams own automated mitigations. - Review cadence: this playbook should be reviewed quarterly or
title: Migration cutover stability playbook

text: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process/postmortem-template Notes and ownership - Owner: Customer Success (Aisha Patel). SRE and Runtime teams own automated mitigations. - Review cadence: this playbook should be reviewed quarterly or after any major cutover incident. text: Summary: This playbook defines the operational process, SLAs, communications templates, and ownership for resolving enterprise billing disputes and chargeback requests during onboarding and the first 90 days post-go-live. It is intended for CSMs, BillingOps, Finance, Support Engineering, and Legal. Purpose and scope: - Purpose: Reduce mean time to resolution (MTTR) for billing disputes, ensure consistent customer communications, protect revenue recognition, and capture audit evidence for chargebacks or credits. - Scope: Applies to all enterprise tenants (Dedicated and Private) with contract value >= $50k ARR during onboarding and the first 90 days after go-live. Does not replace Finance-led invoicing corrections for legacy invoices; it coordinates with Finance for those cases. Definitions: - Dispute: A customer-initiated claim that an invoice, usage charge, or billing calculation is incorrect. - Chargeback: A request to reverse or offset billed amount against a future invoice or issue a formal credit. - Priority levels: 1) P0 — Billing outage affecting all invoices or system-side errors that systematically overcharge customers; target acknowledgement < 1 business hour; remediation plan in 4 hours. 2) P1 — Material dispute where customer-facing usage appears incorrect (>10% deviation or > $5k discrepancy); target acknowledgement < 4 business hours; resolution in 3 business days. 3) P2 — Minor disagreement or clarification (<10% and < $5k); target acknowledgement within 1 business
title: Billing dispute and chargeback resolution playbook

text: or > $5k discrepancy); target acknowledgement < 4 business hours; resolution in 3 business days. 3) P2 — Minor disagreement or clarification (<10% and < $5k); target acknowledgement within 1 business day; resolution in 10 business days. Ownership and contacts: - First responder: Customer Success Manager (CSM) — initial intake, triage, and customer comms. - Technical validation: Support Engineering (SE) — investigates telemetry, request logs, KV/cache behavior, and model fallback records. - Billing operations: BillingOps — recalculates usage, verifies aggregation + quota logic, prepares credits. - Finance: Signs off on formal credits and updates AR records. - Legal: Engaged when contractual dispute or potential revenue recognition impact. Escalation matrix: 1) CSM escalates to SE (channel: #billing-ops-escalations, tag @oncall-se) after initial intake if P0/P1. 2) SE triages, produces technical findings and a short timeline (within 8 hours for P1). 3) If disagreement remains after technical validation, CSM opens BillingOps ticket (billing-api) and informs Finance. 4) If resolution crosses 5 business days or exceeds $50k, escalate to Director of Customer Success + Head of Billing for approval and external comms. Operational procedure (step-by-step): 1) Intake (CSM) - Capture: customer contact, invoice ID, invoice date, disputed amount, business impact, and preferred remedy (credit, refund, or adjustment). - Assign priority (P0/P1/P2) and open the internal incident in the Customer
title: Billing dispute and chargeback resolution playbook

text: customer contact, invoice ID, invoice date, disputed amount, business impact, and preferred remedy (credit, refund, or adjustment). - Assign priority (P0/P1/P2) and open the internal incident in the Customer Success ticketing queue (prefix: BILL-). Attach customer-provided artifacts (screenshots, billing CSVs). 2) Triage (CSM, within SLA for priority) - Verify customer identity and contract tier. Confirm invoice numbers and timeframe. - If P0, immediately declare incident and notify #ops-all and on-call SRE. 3) Technical investigation (SE) - Confirm request routing and model selection logs: check `billing-api` request logs, `ingress-gateway` traces, and `telemetry-gatekeeper` aggregation. - Query usage records: run query against `usage-aggregation` table (example SQL): SELECT tenant_id, sum(tokens) as total_tokens, model_name, date FROM usage_aggregation WHERE tenant_id = 'TENANT_X' AND date BETWEEN '2026-10-01' AND '2026-10-31' GROUP BY tenant_id, model_name, date; - Verify any cache-busting events, canary rollouts, or model-fanout that could double-count tokens. - Produce a short findings doc (one-page) that lists root-cause candidates and evidence links. 4) BillingOps reconciliation - Recompute the invoice using the canonical aggregation export (CSV/JSON) and the rate-card in effect for the invoice period. Maintain a checksumed export and attach to the case. - If code/aggregation bug found, create a paging ticket in engineering backlog with severity and request a hotfix/retroactive
title: Billing dispute and chargeback resolution playbook

text: for the invoice period. Maintain a checksumed export and attach to the case. - If code/aggregation bug found, create a paging ticket in engineering backlog with severity and request a hotfix/retroactive adjustment. 5) Proposal and customer communication (CSM + BillingOps) - Draft proposed remedy (credit/refund/adjustment) and the timeline for application. - Use the communication templates below. 6) Finance sign-off and execution - Finance validates credit amount, posts credit memo or refund, updates AR, and records reason codes. - BillingOps updates invoice metadata and attaches evidence to the ticket. 7) Closure and retrospective - Confirm customer acceptance, update ticket with resolution artifacts, and schedule a 1-week postmortem for P0/P1 cases. - Capture follow-up actions in the customer QBR if systemic. Evidence and artifacts to collect (minimum): - Customer-submitted dispute form or email (archive). - Relevant invoice PDF and raw aggregation export (CSV/JSON). - Query IDs and trace IDs from `ingress-gateway` and `billing-api` (trace links). - Findings doc from SE and final recalculation spreadsheet with checksum. - Finance credit memo number or refund transaction ID. Communication templates (examples) 1) Acknowledgement (send within SLA) Subject: Acknowledged — Billing enquiry for invoice INV-12345 Body: Hi [Customer Name], Thank you — we have received your billing enquiry for invoice INV-12345 (amount: $X). We have opened case BILL-6789 and assigned it priority [P1].
title: Billing dispute and chargeback resolution playbook

text: enquiry for invoice INV-12345 Body: Hi [Customer Name], Thank you — we have received your billing enquiry for invoice INV-12345 (amount: $X). We have opened case BILL-6789 and assigned it priority [P1]. Our technical and billing teams will investigate and we will update you inside the SLA timelines. If you have additional artifacts, please attach them to this thread. 2) Interim status (P1/P0) Subject: Interim update — Billing enquiry INV-12345 (case BILL-6789) Body: Hi [Customer Name], We are actively investigating. Current findings: [short summary]. Target resolution: [date/time]. If the issue is high-impact we will provide hourly updates. 3) Resolution and credit confirmation Subject: Resolved — Billing enquiry INV-12345 (case BILL-6789) Body: Hi [Customer Name], We have completed our investigation and applied the following remedy: [credit/refund/adjustment details with reference #]. The credit will appear on your next invoice (or refund dispatched on [date]). Summary of cause: [one-paragraph root cause]. Preventative steps taken: [bullet list]. Customer-facing acceptance criteria (must be documented in ticket): - The refunded or credited amount equals the agreed remedy. - Finance has produced a credit memo or refund transaction ID and AR is updated. - Customer confirms acceptance in writing. Automation and tooling recommendations: - Automate evidence collection: attach aggregated usage export and trace IDs at intake using the `billing-intake` Lambda hook. - Add a billing dispute
title: Billing dispute and chargeback resolution playbook

text: writing. Automation and tooling recommendations: - Automate evidence collection: attach aggregated usage export and trace IDs at intake using the `billing-intake` Lambda hook. - Add a billing dispute webhook to notify `#billing-ops-escalations` with structured payload: {"case_id":"BILL-XXXXX","tenant_id":"T-123","priority":"P1","invoice_id":"INV-..."} (engineering backlog item: BILLING-2381). - Maintain a shared Google Sheet (read-only for customers) for long-running reconciliations; link in the ticket. Reporting and KPIs: - SLA compliance: percent of disputes acknowledged within target SLA (goal >= 98%). - MTTR by priority: target P1 <= 3 business days (rolling 90-day median). - Error rate: percent of invoices requiring adjustment after go-live (target <= 0.5%). - Fiscal impact: mean credit amount and top contributing root causes. Playbook examples and edge cases: - Duplicate charge due to canary rollback: If SE confirms double-counting tied to canary rollback, proceed with immediate credit and create an engineering incident. - Customer claims lost feature credits (promo/discount not applied): verify contract-level entitlements and reconcile with rate card; escalate to Finance/Legal for contract amendment if needed. - Suspected fraud / abuse: pause account billing (billing-api flag: billing_hold = true), notify Legal and follow fraud handling SOP. Postmortem and continuous improvement: - For P0/P1 incidents, schedule a 1-week postmortem with CSM, SE, BillingOps, and Finance.
title: Billing dispute and chargeback resolution playbook

text: flag: billing_hold = true), notify Legal and follow fraud handling SOP. Postmortem and continuous improvement: - For P0/P1 incidents, schedule a 1-week postmortem with CSM, SE, BillingOps, and Finance. Capture root cause, corrective action, and preventive measures. - Feed recurring root causes into the onboarding playbook and update rate-card validations in the billing pipeline. Appendix A — Quick triage checklist (for CSMs) 1) Confirm invoice ID, disputed amount, and date range. 2) Confirm tenant tier and active contract adjustments. 3) Attach customer artifacts and create BILL- ticket. 4) Ping #billing-ops-escalations with case link for P0/P1. 5) Confirm SE assigned and expected update time. Appendix B — Example internal Slack alert (to #billing-ops-escalations) Payload (one-line): BILL-6789 | TENANT_T-123 | P1 | INV-12345 | $8,900 | @priya.nambiar — investigation assigned to @oncall-se. Link: <ticket_url> Appendix C — Minimal JSON export schema for reconciliation artifacts - ticket_id: string - tenant_id: string - invoice_id: string - period_start: YYYY-MM-DD - period_end: YYYY-MM-DD - raw_usage_export_url: https://... - recalculated_amount: string (decimal) - evidence_links: [comma-separated links] Notes and links: - Service names referenced: billing-api, ingestion-gateway, usage-aggregation, telemetry-gatekeeper, billing-intake lambda. - See related playbooks: tenant-finops-cutover-governance-playbook (onboarding), enterprise-cost-anomaly-detection-and-action-playbook (health

text: Overview: This playbook defines the tenant-level chaos gaming and synthetic-resilience program used during enterprise onboarding and the first 90 days post-cutover. It standardizes simulated failure scenarios, telemetry assertions, success criteria, and escalation steps to validate that a new tenant meets Redwood Inference stability, performance, and cost-safety requirements prior to and immediately after canary-to-100% rollouts. Purpose and scope: - Purpose: Reduce production surprises by exercising realistic failure modes against a customer’s dedicated or private deployment in a controlled, observable way. - Scope: Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants (VPC/on-prem) being onboarded or undergoing major model/capacity cutovers. Does not replace load testing performed by SRE for platform-wide capacity. Service-level targets (tenant SLOs): - Availability SLO: 99.95% for API success rate (5xx and client-facing errors excluded per contract). - Median latency target: <120ms for small-gen calls (<128 tokens) during canary windows. - Cost safety guardrail: 2x expected cost spike tolerance during orchestrated synthetic tests; anything beyond triggers immediate throttling. Owners and contacts: - Playbook owner: Customer Success (Aisha Patel) - Runtime escalation owner: Eng SRE (Miguel Ramos) - Dedicated/private deployment owner: Eng Private Deployments (Jordan Lee) - Customer POC: (fill per customer onboarding sheet) Prerequisites (before running a game day): 1. Tenant must be deployed
title: tenant-chaos-gaming-and-resilience-onboarding-playbook-2026

text: Ramos) - Dedicated/private deployment owner: Eng Private Deployments (Jordan Lee) - Customer POC: (fill per customer onboarding sheet) Prerequisites (before running a game day): 1. Tenant must be deployed to a non-prod or canary tenant slice with identical routing, or to a production tenant with explicit consent and an agreed blast radius. 2. Observability contract validated: metrics, traces, and logs forwarded to Redwood telemetry cluster and customer-observability link. 3. Canary token/key configured and QA test suite green. 4. Rollback and safe-failover plans approved by customer and SRE. Game day checklist (pre-execution): - Schedule and obtain approvals from customer, SRE, and account team (minimum 72 hours notice). - Configure synthetic load generator (see examples below) and ensure isolation of test traffic via label/filter. - Snapshot current tenant config and telemetry baseline (last 24h). - Confirm cost cap and automatic throttles are active. - Run a brief smoke test of observability dashboards and alerting rules. Synthetic scenarios (recommended list): 1) KV-cache corruption simulation - Intent: Ensure graceful detection and refetch behavior for prefix cache invalidation. - Method: Inject cache-miss pattern by toggling a feature flag in the canary runtime to disable cached lookup for 60s. - Success criteria: Error budget unaffected; latency increase <50% for affected requests; automated PK cache warm-up completes within 120s. 2) Model-variant fallback under load -
title: tenant-chaos-gaming-and-resilience-onboarding-playbook-2026

text: cached lookup for 60s. - Success criteria: Error budget unaffected; latency increase <50% for affected requests; automated PK cache warm-up completes within 120s. 2) Model-variant fallback under load - Intent: Validate model fallback routing if primary quantized model fails or is cyclically unavailable. - Method: Simulate model-worker OOM by marking worker pool unhealthy via the orchestration API. - Success criteria: Requests routed to fallback variant within 10s; tail latency spike limited to 2x baseline for under 5 minutes. 3) Sudden traffic surge from one tenant (ingress storm) - Intent: Confirm tenant fairshare and quota protector sidecar behavior. - Method: Generate a 10x RPS spike for 5 minutes using synthetic generator. - Success criteria: No cross-tenant degradation; affected tenant receives throttling with appropriate 429s and customer-notification webhook triggers. 4) Telemetry pipeline delay and partial loss - Intent: Ensure offline buffering and alerting are robust when telemetry forwarding lags. - Method: Introduce deliberate forwarding latency in the staging observability forwarder for 3 minutes. - Success criteria: Alerts that depend on telemetry use alternative guard signals (health check pings); post-mortem shows no blind spots longer than 5 minutes. Execution play (numbered runbook): 1. Kickoff: Confirm attendees and intent. Document start time in the incident timeline channel. 2. Baseline sampling: Start 5m metric sampling to baseline key telemetry (request_rate,
title: tenant-chaos-gaming-and-resilience-onboarding-playbook-2026

text: (numbered runbook): 1. Kickoff: Confirm attendees and intent. Document start time in the incident timeline channel. 2. Baseline sampling: Start 5m metric sampling to baseline key telemetry (request_rate, error_rate, p50/p95 latency, cost_per_minute). 3. Scenario run: Execute scenario steps as defined. One scenario per 15–30 minute window. 4. Observability watch: SRE and CS watch dashboards for SLO violations. Assign a single comms owner. 5. Containment: If a hard threshold is crossed (see thresholds table), immediately stop the synthetic generator and trigger rollback if needed. 6. Cooldown: 15–30 minutes post-scenario for caches and queues to settle. Collect logs and traces. 7. Post-checks: Run validation tests (sanity generations, embedding consistency checks). Thresholds and automated actions (table represented in plain text): - If 5xx rate > 0.5% for >2 minutes -> Automated throttle and page SRE (Pager). - If median latency > 3x baseline for >5 minutes -> Pause canary rollouts and create severity-3 incident. - If cost rate > 3x baseline sustained >10 minutes -> Trigger cost-protector webhook and notify finance-safety. Telemetry assertions (examples to add to the telemetry contract): - request.success_rate >= 99.5% (rolling 10m) - model.fallback.count <= 5 per 10m window during synthetic tests - cache.hit_ratio >= 70% post-warmup (rolling 5m) - ingress.rps <= documented expectation for the tenant + 10% burst allowance Observability queries (examples): - Error rate: sum(rate(http_requests_total{tenant=\"<TENANT>\",status=~\"5..\"}[5m]))
title: tenant-chaos-gaming-and-resilience-onboarding-playbook-2026

text: 5m) - ingress.rps <= documented expectation for the tenant + 10% burst allowance Observability queries (examples): - Error rate: sum(rate(http_requests_total{tenant=\"<TENANT>\",status=~\"5..\"}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{tenant=\"<TENANT>\"}[5m])) - Median latency: histogram_quantile(0.5, sum(rate(request_latency_bucket{tenant=\"<TENANT>\"}[5m])) by (le)) Synthetic load generator examples: - k6 (simple RPS spike) - Command: k6 run --vus 0 --iteration-duration 5m --rps 200 spike-script.js - Note: Use request headers to tag traffic: {\"X-Tenant-Test\":\"chaos-game-2026\"} - locust (ramp test): - Command: locust -f locustfile.py --headless -u 500 -r 50 --run-time 10m --host=https://tenant.example Rollback and safe-failover steps: 1. Stop synthetic generators immediately. 2. If degradation is tenant-isolated and within cost guardrails, hold and let cooldown complete. 3. If cross-tenant impact or severity thresholds exceeded, perform automatic fairshare enforcement and execute configured rollback (model pin to previous stable version). Post-game day analysis (deliverables): - Short RCAs for any threshold breach (published within 3 business days). - Telemetry snapshot and comparison to baseline (attach to the onboarding ticket). - Action items with owners and target completion dates (tracked in JIRA). QBR & onboarding handoff checklist: - Add game day results to the QBR packet: include pass/fail per scenario, regression deltas for p95, and cost delta. - Recommend final tenant
title: tenant-chaos-gaming-and-resilience-onboarding-playbook-2026

text: dates (tracked in JIRA). QBR & onboarding handoff checklist: - Add game day results to the QBR packet: include pass/fail per scenario, regression deltas for p95, and cost delta. - Recommend final tenant readiness vote (Accept / Accept with mitigations / Reject) during the commercial go/no-go. Appendix A — Example alerting rule YAML (snippet): alert: TenantHigh5xx expr: sum(rate(http_requests_total{tenant=\"<TENANT>\",status=~\"5..\"}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{tenant=\"<TENANT>\"}[5m])) > 0.005 for: 2m labels: severity: page annotations: summary: "High 5xx rate for tenant <TENANT>" runbook: "/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/incident-process" Appendix B — Tracing play snippet: - When a scenario runs, add a trace tag: chaos.game=tenant-onboard-<ID> so Perf and Trace teams can filter. Appendix C — Postmortem template fields to include: - Timeline of actions (UTC) - What failed vs expected - Root cause and contributing factors - Immediate mitigations and permanent fixes - Customer impact and communications Frequently asked questions: Q: Can we run game days against production? A: Only with explicit customer written consent and a pre-negotiated blast radius. Use a dedicated canary slice where possible. Q: Who authorizes rollbacks? A: SRE owns urgent rollbacks; CS must be informed within 15 minutes for customer-facing incidents.
