text: ## Purpose This playbook defines how Customer Success (CS) and Support partner with Sales, Solutions Engineering (SE), and Security/GRC during late-stage security reviews. It standardizes: - When and how to loop in Security/GRC - How to handle urgent deadlines and incomplete questionnaires - Escalation tiers, response SLAs, and handoff points - How we avoid unapproved claims and keep a clear audit trail This is an internal operating document. It references restricted evidence processes (e.g., SOC 2 report) but does not include restricted artifacts. --- ## When to use this playbook Use this playbook when any of the following occur: - A prospect/customer requests SOC 2 report, pen test summary, subprocessor list, or other formal evidence. - A prospect/customer sends a security questionnaire, RFP security section, or needs a security addendum. - Procurement/security introduces a hard deadline (\<= 5 business days) or threatens deal delay without answers. - The customer requires deployment-specific assurances (Dedicated vs Private vs Hosted) around data flows, retention, encryption, KMS, logging, SSO/RBAC, or incident notification. Not in scope: - Product roadmap commitments or “custom features” to satisfy a questionnaire. Use the Exceptions Policy for non-supported controls. --- ## Roles and responsibilities (who does what) ### Primary owners - Sales / AE: owns customer timeline, coordinates meetings, maintains CRM notes, ensures customer has correct links. - Solutions Engineering
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: Roles and responsibilities (who does what) ### Primary owners - Sales / AE: owns customer timeline, coordinates meetings, maintains CRM notes, ensures customer has correct links. - Solutions Engineering (SE): owns technical narrative (deployment mode, architecture diagrams, controls explanations). - Security Engineering: validates technical claims, clarifies implementation details, reviews non-standard security language. - GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance): owns audit/compliance language and evidence distribution process (SOC 2 under NDA, artifact lists, timelines). - Legal: owns contractual language (DPA, security addendum, breach notification terms). - CS / Support: owns the escalation workflow, ensures correct process is followed, prevents churn/relationship damage, and creates a clean handoff to post-close operations. ### CS/Support “single-threaded owner” (STO) For any security review that is time-bound or involves evidence requests, CS/Support assigns a single-threaded owner who: - Creates/updates the tracking ticket(s) - Confirms next steps and internal owners - Drives SLA compliance and escalations - Ensures final approved language is used --- ## Golden rules (do / don’t) ### Do - Use the published Security FAQ and standard answer blocks as the starting point. - Confirm the customer’s deployment mode (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) before answering. - Route formal evidence requests through the Evidence Request Playbook (NDA checks, controlled distribution). - Track
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: the customer’s deployment mode (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) before answering. - Route formal evidence requests through the Evidence Request Playbook (NDA checks, controlled distribution). - Track all customer asks, versions, and approvals in a ticket and in CRM. ### Don’t - Don’t claim certifications we do not have (e.g., “ISO 27001 certified”) or imply “compliant with X” unless approved. - Don’t promise data residency beyond what is available in the customer’s selected region/deployment mode. - Don’t share restricted evidence (SOC 2 report, pen test summary) over email or Slack without following the controlled process. - Don’t edit standard RFP/security language “just to make it fit.” Use the approval workflow. --- ## Intake checklist (required information before escalating) CS/Support STO should collect the following before pulling in Security/GRC: 1) Account + deal context - Customer/prospect name, segment, region - Stage (evaluation, procurement, renewal, expansion) - Business impact and date the deal will stall without response 2) Deployment mode and architecture - Hosted API / Dedicated / Private (VPC or on-prem) - Target cloud/region(s) and any data residency constraints - Any special requirements (HIPAA/BAA request, financial services controls, air-gapped constraints) 3) Customer asks (exact wording) - Questionnaire/RFP sections pasted verbatim or attached - Specific artifacts requested (SOC 2, policies, subprocessor list, pen test summary) - Required response format
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: Customer asks (exact wording) - Questionnaire/RFP sections pasted verbatim or attached - Specific artifacts requested (SOC 2, policies, subprocessor list, pen test summary) - Required response format and due date/time zone 4) Constraints - NDA status (executed? pending? required?) - Customer’s requested response channel (portal, email, GRC tool) - Any customer “red lines” (e.g., retention must be configurable to X days) 5) Internal context - Existing approved snippets used (link to FAQ section or RFP library block) - Any planned deviations from standard language (must be explicitly flagged) If any of the above is missing, proceed with escalation but mark as “incomplete intake” and request missing items in parallel. --- ## Escalation tiers and SLAs CS/Support uses the following tiers to set expectations and drive responsiveness. ### P0 — Imminent deadline / deal-stopping within 24 hours Criteria: - Customer deadline in \<= 1 business day OR live procurement call requiring answers - Customer requires evidence distribution (SOC 2) to proceed immediately SLA targets: - Acknowledge internally within 30 minutes (business hours) - First response plan within 2 hours Approvals: - Any non-standard claim requires Security + GRC approval (and Legal if contractual) ### P1 — Time-bound security review (2–5 business days) Criteria: - RFP/questionnaire due in 2–5 business days - Customer is blocked pending “security sign-off” meeting SLA targets: - Acknowledge internally within 4 business hours
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: review (2–5 business days) Criteria: - RFP/questionnaire due in 2–5 business days - Customer is blocked pending “security sign-off” meeting SLA targets: - Acknowledge internally within 4 business hours - Draft response/plan within 1 business day ### P2 — Standard review (\>= 6 business days) Criteria: - No imminent risk; customer requests information for internal review SLA targets: - Acknowledge within 1 business day - Provide materials/plan within 3 business days Business hours assumption: Mon–Fri, local team coverage. If customer is in EMEA/APAC and timing is critical, treat as one tier higher to account for time zone compression. --- ## Step-by-step workflow (CS/Support STO) ### Step 1 — Create a tracking ticket and identify the STO Create a ticket in the internal system of record (Support/Jira or the designated GTM intake queue) with: - Tier (P0/P1/P2) - Due date/time zone - Links/attachments to the questionnaire/RFP portal - Requested artifacts list - Deployment mode and region Add watchers: - AE + SE lead on the account - GRC on-call analyst (or team alias) - Security Engineering reviewer (if technical claims are involved) - Legal reviewer (if contract language requested) ### Step 2 — Send the internal “first plan” message Within the SLA window, post a clear plan (in the ticket and in the designated internal channel): - What we will answer with standard language - What requires review/approval - What evidence requires NDA + controlled process - Who owns each workstream
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: ticket and in the designated internal channel): - What we will answer with standard language - What requires review/approval - What evidence requires NDA + controlled process - Who owns each workstream and deadlines ### Step 3 — Separate “language” work from “evidence” work Run two tracks in parallel: A) Response language - Use the Security FAQ and Standard Answers as the baseline. - If the questionnaire asks for non-supported controls, use the Exceptions Policy to propose alternative language. - If the customer requires a custom architecture/security narrative, SE drafts and Security validates. B) Evidence requests - Follow the SOC 2 Customer Evidence Request Playbook. - Confirm NDA status before any restricted artifact is shared. - Log exactly which artifacts were shared, when, and via what method. ### Step 4 — Run a time-boxed review cycle For P0/P1, use a single redline pass with explicit approvers: - Security Engineering: technical controls, logging, encryption, runtime behavior - GRC: compliance posture wording, audit periods, “report under NDA” phrasing - Legal: DPA/addendum terms, breach notification, liability language If edits exceed one pass or the customer changes scope, re-baseline and reset expectations with Sales. ### Step 5 — Customer-facing delivery CS/Support STO ensures: - Final answers use approved language and correct deployment mode caveats - Restricted artifacts are delivered only via the approved method - The customer receives a concise “what we sent +
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: STO ensures: - Final answers use approved language and correct deployment mode caveats - Restricted artifacts are delivered only via the approved method - The customer receives a concise “what we sent + how to follow up” summary Recommended customer email structure: - One-paragraph overview - Bullets of attached/linked documents - Evidence request steps (if applicable) - Offer a 30-minute security review call with Security/GRC if needed ### Step 6 — Close the loop and create post-close handoff notes Once the security review is complete (or “good enough” for procurement), capture: - Final list of commitments made (e.g., log retention option selected, region selection) - Any exceptions granted and their approvers - Any open items that must be implemented/configured during onboarding Add the notes to CRM and to the account’s implementation plan. --- ## Common scenarios and how to handle them ### Scenario A: “Send us your SOC 2 report today” 1) Confirm whether NDA is executed. 2) If NDA is not executed, coordinate with Legal/Sales immediately; do not share the report. 3) Use the approved wording: “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA upon request.” 4) Open an evidence request per the playbook and track distribution. ### Scenario B: Customer asks “Do you train on our data?” - Use the exact FAQ language and definitions (training vs transient processing; prompts/logs vs model weights). - Do not introduce new qualifiers. If customer asks for contract language, route to Legal. ###
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: the exact FAQ language and definitions (training vs transient processing; prompts/logs vs model weights). - Do not introduce new qualifiers. If customer asks for contract language, route to Legal. ### Scenario C: EU data residency objection (must stay in EU) - Confirm which regions are available for the chosen deployment mode. - Use the approved residency language (no blanket promises). If constraints can only be met with Private, have SE present options. - Escalate to GRC/Security if the customer requests written guarantees beyond standard documentation. ### Scenario D: Questionnaire asks for a control we do not support (e.g., specific IdP feature, custom audit event) - Do not answer “Yes” with future intent. - Use the Exceptions Policy to provide alternative language and document compensating controls. - If customer insists on a “must-have,” flag to Product Security PM for capability alignment (without committing roadmap). --- ## Communication channels and meeting guidance ### Internal channels (recommended) - Use the designated security review intake channel and link the tracking ticket. - Keep decisions in the ticket. Slack is for coordination, not for the authoritative record. ### Customer call structure (30–45 minutes) 1) Confirm deployment mode + region + data flow expectations 2) Walk through top 5 controls customer cares about (usually: data use, encryption, access controls, logging, retention) 3) Evidence requests: explain NDA gating and timelines 4) Capture remaining
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: expectations 2) Walk through top 5 controls customer cares about (usually: data use, encryption, access controls, logging, retention) 3) Evidence requests: explain NDA gating and timelines 4) Capture remaining blockers and assign owners Avoid: - Live “off the cuff” answers to compliance/certification questions. Offer to follow up with approved language. --- ## Handback to CS/Support after close (operationalization) If any of the following were discussed, CS must validate configuration during onboarding: - Audit log export destination (SIEM/S3) and retention window - SSO/SAML and SCIM provisioning requirements - RBAC roles and least-privilege model for admins - Data retention/deletion configuration for the chosen deployment mode - Private deployment network boundaries (VPC peering, egress controls) and key management (KMS/HSM) Create an onboarding checklist item for each requirement and confirm acceptance criteria with the customer security contact. --- ## Templates ### Internal escalation summary (paste into ticket) Account: Deployment mode: Region(s): Tier (P0/P1/P2): Customer deadline (date/time zone): Top asks (verbatim): Artifacts requested: NDA status: Draft response plan (what we can answer with standard language vs needs review): Owners: ### Customer follow-up email (minimal) Subject: Redwood security review materials + next steps Hi <Name>, Sharing the requested security materials for your review: - <Doc link 1> - <Doc link 2> - <Audit logging / retention overview> For
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: (minimal) Subject: Redwood security review materials + next steps Hi <Name>, Sharing the requested security materials for your review: - <Doc link 1> - <Doc link 2> - <Audit logging / retention overview> For the SOC 2 Type II report (restricted), we can provide it under NDA via our controlled evidence request process. If you confirm NDA status, we will initiate distribution. Happy to schedule a 30-minute security review call to walk through data handling, encryption, access controls, and audit logging. Best, <Name> --- ## Metrics and continuous improvement CS/Support STO should log the following for quarterly review: - Time-to-acknowledge and time-to-first-draft by tier - # of evidence requests and average turnaround time - Top repeated questionnaire questions and where our standard language was insufficient - Any instances of “unapproved language” caught in review (and required remediation) Feedback loop: - If a question repeats \>= 3 times in a month, open a request to update the Security FAQ or RFP library rather than rewriting per deal. --- ## Ownership and review cadence Owner: Customer Success (CS sponsor), in partnership with Security/GRC. Review cadence: Quarterly, or immediately after any material change to security posture, evidence availability, or product behavior affecting common questionnaire answers. Last review highlights (2026-02-21): - Tightened P0/P1 SLA expectations and clarified NDA gating for evidence. - Standardized separation of “language” vs “evidence”
title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)

text: affecting common questionnaire answers. Last review highlights (2026-02-21): - Tightened P0/P1 SLA expectations and clarified NDA gating for evidence. - Standardized separation of “language” vs “evidence” workstreams. - Added explicit post-close operationalization checklist items (SSO/SCIM, audit logs, retention).

text: Summary This document defines the technical handback process and the operational expectations for the first 90 days after an enterprise customer goes live on Redwood Inference (hosted or Dedicated). It is intended for CSMs, Solutions Engineering (SE), Onboarding Engineers, and the L2 oncall team. Scope - Applies to enterprise customers with Dedicated or Private deployments and enterprise-level Hosted integrations that require an SE handover. - Covers: handoff checklist, SLA matrix, runbook entries for common post-go-live issues, escalation steps, monitoring baseline, and QBR readiness checklist for the first 90 days. Goals and success metrics (first 90 days) - Availability SLO: 99.95% per calendar month for production inference endpoints. - Latency SLO: P95 request-to-first-token < 300 ms for short-text generation workloads (<= 256 tokens) on Dedicated pools. - Error budget: < 0.05% failed requests (5xx) attributable to Redwood services. Customer-side errors (4xx) excluded. - Onboarding health: 80% of customer-defined success criteria met by day 30, 95% by day 90. - QBR readiness: onboarding artifacts, traffic profile, cost trends, and open action items delivered two weeks before the 90-day review. Handback checklist (must be completed before formal handoff) 1) Contractual & access items (owner: CSM) 1. Confirm customer account and billing profile in Finance (billing contact verified). 2. Verify list of whitelisted IPs, VPC peering, or VPN endpoints (for Dedicated/Private). 3.
title: Onboarding Handback and First-90-Days Operational Guide

text: access items (owner: CSM) 1. Confirm customer account and billing profile in Finance (billing contact verified). 2. Verify list of whitelisted IPs, VPC peering, or VPN endpoints (for Dedicated/Private). 3. Confirm KMS/HSM key rotation schedule and access for logs/audit. 2) Technical verification (owner: SE / Onboarding Engineer) 1. Baseline performance test completed: runbench report attached and passing acceptance criteria (attach: runbench-<customer>-v1.json). 2. Model versions pinned and documented (model-id, quantization profile if any). 3. Ingress/egress networking validated: HTTPS termination, MTU checks, and route tables. 4. Observability hooks installed: metrics, ingest for traces, and token-level cost tagging enabled. 3) Operational readiness (owner: Oncall + CSM) 1. Oncall runbooks created for the customer under team wiki (link to runbook entry). 2. Alerting thresholds verified (see SLA matrix below). 3. Access matrix for customer ops and Redwood SRE/SE is finalized (least privilege). Operational SLA matrix (reference thresholds and owners) | Area | SLO / Threshold | Primary owner | Escalation target | |---|---:|---|---:| | Endpoint availability | 99.95% monthly | Customer Success (CSM) / SE | SRE oncall within 30m | | P95 latency (<=256 tokens) | < 300 ms (Dedicated) | SE / Runtime team | Runtime oncall within 60m | | Error rate (5xx) | < 0.05% | Runtime team | SRE within 30m | | Cold-start median | < 1200 ms | Runtime team | Engineering within 2h | | Cost anomaly
title: Onboarding Handback and First-90-Days Operational Guide

text: | SE / Runtime team | Runtime oncall within 60m | | Error rate (5xx) | < 0.05% | Runtime team | SRE within 30m | | Cold-start median | < 1200 ms | Runtime team | Engineering within 2h | | Cost anomaly | < 25% deviation vs forecast | Finance + CSM | Growth/RevOps notified 1 business day | Note: thresholds above are targets agreed during onboarding and may be tuned per-contract. All alerts should map to the customer-specific alerting policy in PagerDuty and Slack channel. Escalation & handoff timeline (high level) - Day 0 (Go-live): SE oncall and CSM present. Monitor first 24 hours for errors and latency regressions. - Day 1–7: Daily sync between CSM, SE, and SRE for the first-week incidents and visibility. - Day 8–30: Bi-weekly operational check-ins, performance tuning (batching/KV cache config), and cost review. - Day 31–90: Weekly check-ins until SLA stability and customer success criteria are met; prepare QBR artifacts by day 75. Handoff procedure (detailed, numbered) 1. Pre-handoff readiness demo (SE + customer): run the baseline load test (recommended: k6 or locust harness) and demonstrate acceptance criteria. 2. Fill the Onboarding Runbook template: include endpoints, model pins, route policies, alerting, and contact matrix. 3. Create customer-specific monitoring dashboard: tokens-per-minute, p95 latency, 5xx rate, cost-by-route. Share read-only link with customer. 4. Add the customer to the SRE PagerDuty rotation as a secondary for the first 30 days. 5. Formal handoff meeting:
title: Onboarding Handback and First-90-Days Operational Guide

text: tokens-per-minute, p95 latency, 5xx rate, cost-by-route. Share read-only link with customer. 4. Add the customer to the SRE PagerDuty rotation as a secondary for the first 30 days. 5. Formal handoff meeting: CSM presents business context, SE presents technical state, SRE presents monitoring/alerts. Capture action items in Jira and assign owners. Post-handoff runbook excerpts (copy these into the customer runbook) - Immediate checks when an alert fires: - Step 1: Verify if alert is customer-generated (4xx) vs platform (5xx) by checking trace id and edge logs. - Step 2: If platform (5xx), run diagnostic command: curl -v -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' https://api.redwood.example/v1/engines/<engine-id>/health || cat /var/log/runtime/errors.log (SE only). - Step 3: Check KV cache hit rate and batching stats from /metrics endpoint (prometheus name: redwood_runtime_kv_cache_hit_ratio). Thresholds: < 60% indicates mis-priming. - Step 4: Escalate per matrix (see table above) and create a postmortem if the incident causes > 30m downtime affecting > 10% of requests. Common post-go-live issues and mitigations - High P95 latency after deployment: - Likely causes: non-optimal batching, model quantization fallback, and warmup not run. - Mitigation steps: confirm batching policy in route config, enable prefix/KV cache for repeated prompts, trigger a lightweight warmup job: POST /internal/warmup {"engine":"<engine-id>","samples":10}. - Increased token spend vs forecast: - Likely causes: prompt
title: Onboarding Handback and First-90-Days Operational Guide

text: enable prefix/KV cache for repeated prompts, trigger a lightweight warmup job: POST /internal/warmup {"engine":"<engine-id>","samples":10}. - Increased token spend vs forecast: - Likely causes: prompt inflation, unexpected retries, or high token-per-request usage. - Mitigation steps: enable token-limit policy on the route, add request logging with sampling to capture outlier prompts, review SDK integration to verify retries/backoff. - Frequent 429s (rate limit) during ramp: - Likely causes: client-side concurrency and missing client-side rate limiting. - Mitigation: apply client-side leaky-bucket throttling; coordinate with SE to provision burst capacity for first 48 hours. Monitoring & instrumentation checklist (must be visible in the first 24 hours) - Dashboards: Tokens/minute, per-route cost, p95 latency, 5xx rate, KV cache hit ratio. - Traces: sample 1% of requests with full request/response payload for the first 7 days. Ensure PII redaction enabled. - Logs: structured JSON logs with request_id, trace_id, route, model_id. Retention: 90 days by default for enterprise customers. CLI and example snippets - Fetch engine health: - Example: curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" https://api.redwood.example/v1/engines/my-prod-engine/health - Warmup job (cURL example): - curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"engine":"my-prod-engine","samples":["Hello","Summarize: short text"],"concurrency":2}' https://api.redwood.example/internal/warmup
title: Onboarding Handback and First-90-Days Operational Guide

text: "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"engine":"my-prod-engine","samples":["Hello","Summarize: short text"],"concurrency":2}' https://api.redwood.example/internal/warmup - Sample route config (JSON fragment to include in onboarding runbook): - { "route_name": "customer-a-default", "engine": "my-prod-engine", "batching": { "max_batch_tokens": 1024, "max_batch_size": 32 }, "cache": { "prefix_cache": true, "kv_profile": "high-hit" }, "slo_tags": ["enterprise-2026"], "rate_limits": { "requests_per_minute": 1200 } } QBR readiness checklist (to deliver by day 75) - Usage trends and cost breakdown (token-level) for day 0–75. - Performance baseline vs current (p50/p95/p99). - Open incidents and remediation items. - Proposed optimization roadmap (batching/cache/quantizaton) with estimated cost impact. Owner & contacts - Onboarding SE: name & email (populate in runbook). - Customer Success Manager: name & email (populate in runbook). - Runtime Oncall: rotas listed in Ops PagerDuty. Appendix: Acceptance test checklist (sample commands) 1. Latency smoke test (10 concurrent requests): run k6 script; expected p95 < configured threshold. 2. Fault injection: simulate a dropped node and verify automatic fallback to other capacity pools within 60s. 3. Cost smoke test: replay representative traffic for 1 hour and measure tokens-per-minute vs forecast. Notes and links - Attach: baseline runbench JSON, onboarding runbook template, SLA addendum (if different
title: Onboarding Handback and First-90-Days Operational Guide

text: smoke test: replay representative traffic for 1 hour and measure tokens-per-minute vs forecast. Notes and links - Attach: baseline runbench JSON, onboarding runbook template, SLA addendum (if different from platform defaults). - For security-sensitive customers, coordinate with Security & Compliance for audit logging and evidence packages. If you have questions about any step, ping #cs-onboarding or open a ticket in the Onboarding queue.
title: Onboarding Handback and First-90-Days Operational Guide

text:
Overview:

This playbook defines the end-to-end onboarding process for enterprise customers whose accounts require region-specific compliance and localized operational controls. It codifies pre-provisioning checks, region matrices, telemetry gating, localized SLA expectations, and safe rollback procedures. Use this document when the customer requests any of the following:

- Data residency guarantees for a jurisdiction (EU, UK, CA, APAC jurisdictions).
- Private networking (VPC peering, on-prem deployments, or air-gapped installs).
- Localized legal or regulatory controls (data classification, DPA/GDPR addenda, sector-specific controls such as HIPAA or FINRA).

Scope:

This playbook applies to:
- Hosted accounts with region pinning in AWS/EU/GC/East-APAC regions.
- Redwood Dedicated tenants that must be physically located in a defined region.
- Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem) with KMS/HSM and audit log routing requirements.

Not in scope: model-level tuning or evaluation procedures (see applied-ml-and-evals), or deep security architecture decisions (see security-and-compliance).

High-level objectives:
- Ensure legal/regulatory boxes are validated before data ingress.
- Minimize go-live disruption with region-aware smoke tests and telemetry gating.
- Provide a repeatable rollback path for failures during cutover.

Region requirements matrix (summary):

| Region | Residency guarantee | Default storage location | Default KMS boundary | Common customer asks
title: Regional compliance and localized onboarding playbook - 2026

text:
repeatable rollback path for failures during cutover.

Region requirements matrix (summary):

| Region | Residency guarantee | Default storage location | Default KMS boundary | Common customer asks | Recommended provisioning tier |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| EU (Frankfurt/Amsterdam) | EU-only data at rest + transit | S3 eu-central-1 / eu-west-1 | Customer-managed KMS in same region | DPA, SCCs, EU-only audit logs | Hosted pinned or Dedicated EU |
| UK (London) | UK-residency option | S3 eu-west-2 | Optional CMK in London | UK Addendum, export controls | Hosted pinned (UK) or Dedicated |
| Canada (Montreal) | Canadian residency | S3 ca-central-1 | Customer CMK recommended | PIPEDA questions, province-level data requests | Dedicated recommended |
| APAC (Singapore, Tokyo) | Region-specific residency on request | S3 ap-southeast-1 / ap-northeast-1 | Customer CMK recommended | Local security questionnaires, latency-sensitive | Hosted pinned or Dedicated APAC |

Pre-provisioning checklist (owner: Customer Success + Solutions Eng):

1. Contract gating
 - Confirm signed DPA/region addenda.
 - Verify export control and sector clauses (e.g., HIPAA, FINRA) if applicable.
2. Identity & Access
 - Confirm customer SSO/SAML attributes and expected admin list.
 - Collect service account thumbprints / SSH keys for on-prem agents.
3. Networking
 - Determine VPC
title: Regional compliance and localized onboarding playbook - 2026

text:
applicable.
2. Identity & Access
 - Confirm customer SSO/SAML attributes and expected admin list.
 - Collect service account thumbprints / SSH keys for on-prem agents.
3. Networking
 - Determine VPC peering, firewall rules, proxy requirements.
 - Confirm public egress constraints and NAT/whitelisting needs.
4. Key management
 - Confirm KMS/HSM provider, CMK ARNs, and key rotation cadence.
5. Telemetry & Logging
 - Determine what telemetry can leave the region (metrics only, no event payloads).
 - Configure telemetry gating policy (see Telemetry Gating section).
6. Cutover windows and stakeholders
 - Agree on blackout windows, contact matrix, and rollback thresholds.

Network & connectivity requirements (quick-reference table):

| Component | Direction | Ports | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|-------|-------|
| redwood-provisioner (control plane) | Outbound | 443 | TLS 1.2+, destination must be reachable from provisioning host; allow only to redwood-managed IP ranges for restricted setups. |
| tenant-data-plane | Inbound (customer) | 443/8443 | For Dedicated: customers may request private endpoints; require mutual TLS in sensitive deployments. |
| telemetry-gatekeeper | Outbound | 443 | Supports proxy (HTTP CONNECT) and syslog forwarding. |

Data residency and encryption – configuration template (use when provisioning Dedicated or Private):

| Setting | Recommended value | Rationale | Verification step |
|--------|-------------------|-----------|-------------------|
|
title: Regional compliance and localized onboarding playbook - 2026

text:
configuration template (use when provisioning Dedicated or Private):

| Setting | Recommended value | Rationale | Verification step |
|--------|-------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| object_store.region | eu-central-1 | Ensures data writes remain in EU | S3 bucket location check post-provisioning |
| kms.key_arn | arn:aws:kms:eu-central-1:123456:key/abcd | Customer CMK ensures separation of key control | Perform test envelope encryption/decryption with customer's key |
| audit_log_sink | s3://redwood-audit-eu-logs | Centralized but region-anchored logs | Verify last 24h log arrival and object ACLs |

Telemetry gating and SLO signoffs (owner: Customer Success + Observability):

Principles:
- Customers may require that request/response payloads never leave the region until a compliance gate is satisfied. Telemetry (metrics and non-sensitive traces) can be forwarded under an agreed policy.
- Gate states:
 - BLOCKED: No customer payload or PII leaves region. Provisioning proceeds using metadata-only validation steps.
 - PENDING_VERIFICATION: Non-sensitive telemetry (metrics, anonymized traces) allowed for 48 hours.
 - RELEASED: Full telemetry (payloads, logs) forwarded to central Redwood observability.

Typical gating workflow:
1. Initial provision with BLOCKED gate. Validate network, KMS, and control-plane reachability using metadata-only smoke checks.
2. Customer completes legal checklist and confirms retention policy. Move gate to PENDING_VERIFICATION.
3.
title: Regional compliance and localized onboarding playbook - 2026

text:
gate. Validate network, KMS, and control-plane reachability using metadata-only smoke checks.
2. Customer completes legal checklist and confirms retention policy. Move gate to PENDING_VERIFICATION.
3. Run 48h telemetry-only soak with synthetic traffic. Observability team validates no policy violations.
4. If clear, customer signs release confirmation and gate moves to RELEASED.

SLOs for onboarding validation tests:
- Control plane reachability: 99.9% within provisioning window (5 minute probe cadence).
- KV cache warm-up: 95% of synthetic requests served from cache within 15 minutes of steady traffic.
- API latency (p50): <= 120ms for host-region tests in Dedicated deployments.

Localized support model and SLA adjustments:

- For region-residency accounts, provide an on-call roster that includes regional support engineers during customer business hours.
- SLA ramp for first 90 days: 24x7 response within 60 minutes for Sev1, 4 hours for Sev2, 1 business day for Sev3. After QBR sign-off, revert to standard SLA in the contract.
- Translate key onboarding docs (summary, validation checklist, rollback steps) to customer's preferred language for accounts > $250k ARR.

Onboarding timeline (example for Dedicated EU tenant) – recommended milestones:

| Week | Activity | Owner | Exit criteria |
|------|----------|-------|---------------|
| -2 | Contract & legal gating complete | Legal / Sales | DPA signed, region addendum attached |
| -1 | Network & KMS details supplied
title: Regional compliance and localized onboarding playbook - 2026

text:
| Exit criteria |
|------|----------|-------|---------------|
| -2 | Contract & legal gating complete | Legal / Sales | DPA signed, region addendum attached |
| -1 | Network & KMS details supplied | Customer + Solutions Eng | VPC CIDRs, allowed egress, CMK ARNs submitted |
| Day 0 | Provision Dedicated tenant (EU) | Infra Provisioning | Tenant active, control plane reachable (BLOCKED gate) |
| Day 1-2 | Basic smoke tests + KMS verification | Customer Success + Observability | Encryption roundtrip successful; basic API responses validated |
| Day 3-4 | Telemetry PENDING soak (48h) | Observability | No policy violations; synthetic workload passes SLOs |
| Day 5 | Go/no-go meeting & release | Customer + CS + Infra | Customer signs release; gate set to RELEASED |
| Day 6-30 | Monitoring, stabilization, QBR prep | CS + Product | Performance baseline established; handback to customer ops |

Runbook: failed provisioning and rollback (immediate steps):

1. Detect: Provisioning failed alarms sent to #cs-provisioning and on-call.
2. Triage: Owner on-call confirms failure type (network/KMS/legal/infra).
3. If network or KMS misconfiguration: attempt non-invasive reconfiguration (update security groups, correct CMK ARN).
4. If stateful corruption or data residency risk: execute tenant quiesce and rollback to snapshot. Commands (examples):

- Quiesce tenant traffic: curl -X POST https://internal-api.redwood/api/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/quiesce -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'
-
title: Regional compliance and localized onboarding playbook - 2026

text:
tenant quiesce and rollback to snapshot. Commands (examples):

- Quiesce tenant traffic: curl -X POST https://internal-api.redwood/api/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/quiesce -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'
- Create daily snapshot: redwoodctl snapshot create --tenant {tenant_id} --label pre-rollout-2026-11-02
- Rollback: redwoodctl tenant rollback --tenant {tenant_id} --snapshot pre-rollout-2026-11-02 --confirm

5. Communicate: CS Lead posts incident update to customer within 30 minutes of decision. Include ETA, remediation steps, and next update window.

Escalation matrix (contacts and responsibilities):

| Priority | Who to escalate to | Contact method | Responsibility |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Tier 1 (Network/KMS) | Solutions Engineering on-call | Pager + Slack #se-oncall | Validate VPC/KMS and apply config fixes |
| Tier 2 (Provisioning infra) | Infra SRE lead (Elena Park) | Pager + PagerDuty | Investigate control-plane issues, re-run provisioner |
| Tier 3 (Legal/Compliance) | Security & Compliance (Aisha Patel) | Email + legal channel | Review legal gating items and exceptions |

Validation test-suite (run post-provisioning):

- Test 1: Control-plane API health
 curl -fsS -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' https://tenant-eu.redwood/api/v1/health && echo OK
- Test 2: KMS envelope encrypt/decrypt sample (script)
- Test 3: Data residency verification (list and verify bucket locations)
- Test 4: Telemetry gate enforcement

- Test 3: Data residency verification (list and verify bucket locations)
- Test 4: Telemetry gate enforcement (simulate payload with PII and ensure it is blocked)

Sample CLI/automation snippets (use with redwoodctl v2.1+):

# Provision EU tenant (automation pipeline example)
redwoodctl tenant create --name "acme-eu" --region eu-central-1 --kms-arn arn:aws:kms:eu-central-1:123456:key/abcd --storage-class regional --telemetry-gate BLOCKED

# Toggle telemetry gate (observability only)
redwoodctl telemetry gate update --tenant acme-eu --state PENDING_VERIFICATION --note "Customer signed retention addendum"

Handback and QBR preparation checklist (owner: Customer Success):

- Baseline metrics compiled: p50/p95 latency, token cost per request, uptime.
- Incident log for first 90 days summarized.
- Security posture & audit log shipping validated.
- Recommend deferred items list (integration work, additional tooling).

Appendix A — Regional doc templates and required artifacts:

- Data processing addendum (EU template) — attach to contract.
- Network diagram template (customer to Redwood data-plane).
- KMS attestation checklist and sample CMK policy.

Appendix B — Troubleshooting tips (common failure modes):

- Failure: CMK permission denied — check key policy allows redwood-provisioner principal and kms:Encrypt/kms:Decrypt.
- Failure: Telemetry

Appendix B — Troubleshooting tips (common failure modes):

- Failure: CMK permission denied — check key policy allows redwood-provisioner principal and kms:Encrypt/kms:Decrypt.
- Failure: Telemetry blocked after RELEASED — verify proxy ACL and allowlist for telemetry-gatekeeper certificate.
- Failure: Provisioner times out at 15 minutes — check control plane IP reachability and DNS SRV records for tenant endpoints.

Glossary and ownership:

- redwood-provisioner: internal control-plane service that orchestrates tenant creation (owner: Infra).
- telemetry-gatekeeper: regional agent that enforces telemetry gates and forwards observability events (owner: Observability).
- CS Lead: primary customer success contact for the account (owner: Customer Success).

text:
Summary:
This compact playbook is designed for first responders who need focused, action-oriented guidance for common Redwood-serving incidents and a lightweight postmortem template to accelerate learning. It favors short, repeatable actions (micro-runbooks), clear decision points, and prewritten comms to reduce cognitive load during high-severity events. Use this as the primary on-call cheat-sheet; link to deeper runbooks when needed.

Scope:
- Audience: first responders (on-call engineers), bridge operators, support leads.
- Coverage: performance SLO regressions, KV-cache/embedding cache failures, request throttling/backpressure, model quality regressions, regional connectivity blips, and noisy neighbor incidents.
- Not for: security incidents (use security incident runbook), full forensic investigations.

How to use this doc (quick):
1) Identify the incident class using the "Incident quick classifier" below.
2) Run the micro-runbook for that class (1–3 remediation steps).
3) If unresolved in 20 minutes or customer-impacting, escalate to the appropriate on-call (pager escalation ladder).
4) Publish a short customer-facing status update using the template.
5) After stabilization, fill the light postmortem scaffold within 72 hours and schedule a 30–60 minute blameless review.

Incident quick classifier (decision tree):
- Is the symptom increased latency or elevated error rate across many customers? -> Check p50/p95/p99 latency and error rate. If p99 > 2x baseline
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
review.

Incident quick classifier (decision tree):
- Is the symptom increased latency or elevated error rate across many customers? -> Check p50/p95/p99 latency and error rate. If p99 > 2x baseline or errors > 1% traffic, classify as P1/P2.
- Is the symptom isolated to a single customer or tenant? -> Treat as customer-impacting P2; open dedicated bridge with solutions/CS.
- Are model-quality signals (eval infra or user reports) showing semantic regressions? -> Model quality incident.
- Is there a rapid rise in throttled requests or 429s? -> Throttle/backpressure incident.
- Is KV/embedding cache miss rate > 25% and latency spiking? -> Cache degradation.

Micro-runbooks (one-page actionable steps):
1) Throttle/backpressure (common cause: bursty traffic, downstream saturation, autoscaler lag)
- Owner: inference-gateway on-call.
- Immediate check (COMMANDS):
 - curl -s https://metrics.internal/api/series?query=inference.gateway.throttled_count | jq .
 - kubectl -n platform get hpa inference-gateway -o yaml
- Mitigation steps (in order):
 1. Scale a fallback pool: runbook-ops-cli scale-pool --pool reserved-burst --replicas +2 (if dedicated pools exist).
 2. Activate traffic shaping policy: platformctl set-routing --policy degrade-to-fast-variant (falls back to cheaper/fast variant).
 3. Temporarily increase client-level quotas for critical tenants via admin-api update-quota --tenant X --burst 2000.
- Timebox: each change 5–10 minutes, observe metrics for 2 minutes.
-
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
variant).
 3. Temporarily increase client-level quotas for critical tenants via admin-api update-quota --tenant X --burst 2000.
- Timebox: each change 5–10 minutes, observe metrics for 2 minutes.
- Escalate if no improvement within 20 minutes.

2) KV / embedding cache degradation (misses, cold cache, or eviction storms)
- Owner: serving-runtime on-call.
- Immediate check:
 - grafana dashboard: Cache Overview -> miss_rate, hit_latency, eviction_rate
 - redis-cli -h cache.internal INFO stats | egrep "keyspace_hits|keyspace_misses|evicted_keys"
- Mitigation steps:
 1. Increase cache memory for affected cluster: infra-ops change-mem --cluster kv-cache-primary --delta +8GB (requires infra approval path if >16GB).
 2. Toggle read-through to passive mode to avoid stampeding: configctl set kv.read_through false
 3. Warm prefix caches for top tenants: cache-warm --tenant-list top-10 --prefixes recent
- Note: If warm-up would spike CPU, prefer toggle (2) and reduce client TTLs.

3) Model quality regression (nonsensical outputs, hallucination ramp)
- Owner: model-onboarding / applied-ml on-call.
- Immediate check:
 - eval-signal: compare latest evaluation job to baseline across prompt-sets (failure delta > 5% on guardrail prompts).
 - Customer reports: search 'quality regression' tag in support queue.
- Mitigation steps:
 1. Roll traffic to previous verified model revision: rolloutctl rollback --service model-serving --version v2025-12-18
 2. Enable inference-sanitizer:
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
regression' tag in support queue.
- Mitigation steps:
 1. Roll traffic to previous verified model revision: rolloutctl rollback --service model-serving --version v2025-12-18
 2. Enable inference-sanitizer: modelctl set-sanitizer --level conservative (adds n-gram heuristics and response filters).
 3. Open dedicated incident channel with model and product owners; capture failing prompts to eval harness.
- Escalation: product owner if rollout fails or regression persists > 60 minutes.

4) Regional connectivity blip / cloud region incident
- Owner: eng-sre on-call.
- Immediate check:
 - platform status map (internal): see region availability and circuit breaker metrics
 - curl -I https://inference.us-west2.internal/healthz
- Mitigation steps:
 1. Reroute traffic to healthy region: routingctl set-region-priority --service inference-gateway --from us-west2 --to us-east1
 2. Enable degraded-mode policies for stateful features (turn off long-polling, increase short timeouts)
 3. If cross-region failover will cause data residency issues, open legal/compliance advisory before broad reroute.

5) Noisy neighbor (GPU OOMs, preemption, or model eviction causing SLO spikes)
- Owner: gpu-fleet on-call.
- Immediate check:
 - ML node logs: elk query "OOM|preempted" | tail -n 200
 - scheduler events: kubectl get events -n gpu-run | grep eviction
- Mitigation steps:
 1. Drain offending node: kubectl drain node/<node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
 2. Pin high-priority
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
scheduler events: kubectl get events -n gpu-run | grep eviction
- Mitigation steps:
 1. Drain offending node: kubectl drain node/<node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
 2. Pin high-priority pools to reserved hardware: fleetctl migrate --pool high-priority --node-type reserved
 3. Adjust eviction thresholds if autoscaler misconfigured; notify capacity planning.

Incident evidence collection (minimum required):
- Attach to incident: relevant Grafana dashboards, last 30 minutes of gateway logs, sampling of 10 failed requests with IDs, stack traces if present, affected tenant list.
- Use incident-evidence-cli collect --incident-id <INC> --type all --since 00:30:00

Escalation ladder (when to escalate and to whom):
- T+0–20 min: on-call responder attempts mitigation. If consumer-visible impact or <80% SLO compliance -> escalate to service owner.
- T+20–40 min: bring in eng-sre lead and product owner.
- T+40+ min or unresolved P1: exec-notification (VP eng, support director), consider declaring customer incident and external status page entry.

Communication templates (copy/paste and edit):
- Internal Pager note (first 10 minutes):
 "[INCIDENT] <short-summary> | Impact: <customers/regions> | Owner: <team> | Initial action: <action taken> | Next update: <T+10m>"
- Customer-facing status (initial):
 Subject: "Service disruption affecting {feature} — {region/tenants}"
 Body: "We are investigating an issue causing {latency/errors} for {feature} impacting
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
<T+10m>"
- Customer-facing status (initial):
 Subject: "Service disruption affecting {feature} — {region/tenants}"
 Body: "We are investigating an issue causing {latency/errors} for {feature} impacting {scope}. Our on-call team has identified {high-level-cause-or-symptom} and is executing mitigations (e.g., traffic reroute, model rollback). We will provide an update by {time}. Contact your CS rep for urgent questions."
- Engineering bridge opener message:
 "Bridge open: {INC-ID} — symptom: {latency/error/quality} — first responder: {name} — runbook: compact-first-responder-playbook — mitigation in progress: {actions}. Artifacts: <grafana link> <logs link>"

Game-day scenarios and success criteria (15–60 minute drills):
- Scenario A: KV cache eviction storm for top-5 tenants. Objective: recover hit rate to >90% within 20 minutes while keeping p95 latency <2x baseline. Steps: follow KV micro-runbook, collect evidence, run postmortem scaffold within 48 hours.
- Scenario B: Model rollback under load. Objective: verify rollback process without request loss, rollback time <15 minutes, monitor model-quality sanity checks.
- Scenario C: Multi-region failover test (planned). Objective: routing cutover <10 minutes, no data residency violation, customer status template executed.

On-call rotation guidance (compact rules):
- Shift length: 12 hours (primary), 24 hours (backup) recommended cadence for SRE; rotate weekly for fairness.
- Handover checklist (to run at shift start/end):
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
rotation guidance (compact rules):
- Shift length: 12 hours (primary), 24 hours (backup) recommended cadence for SRE; rotate weekly for fairness.
- Handover checklist (to run at shift start/end):
 1. Review active incidents and bridges.
 2. Check on-call dashboard for alerts > 6h unresolved.
 3. Confirm contact info and escalation points.
- Shadowing: new on-call engineers must complete two monitored shifts and one game-day within the first 30 days.

Light postmortem scaffold (fill within 72 hours):
- Title: single-line summary of incident and impact.
- Incident ID: {INC-YYYYMMDD-###}
- Severity: P1 | P2 | P3
- Timeline (UTC, short bullets): immediate sequence of key events (discovery, mitigation steps with timestamps).
- Impact summary (customers, duration, SLO deviation): bullet list.
- Root cause (1–2 sentences): brief technical root cause statement.
- Contributing factors: short list with one-sentence explanations.
- Immediate remediation (what we changed): bullets.
- Long-term actions (owners + ETA): table-like bullets with owner and target date.
- Follow-up: status of verification, who will close the action, and acceptance criteria.

Light postmortem example (abridged):
- Title: KV cache eviction storm caused p95 latency spikes for embeddings API (INC-20260111-007)
- Severity: P2
- Timeline:
 - 08:03 UTC — alerts: cache_miss_rate > 30%
 - 08:08 UTC — on-call toggled read_through -> false (mitigation)
 - 08:23 UTC — hit_rate returned to 88%, p95
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
(INC-20260111-007)
- Severity: P2
- Timeline:
 - 08:03 UTC — alerts: cache_miss_rate > 30%
 - 08:08 UTC — on-call toggled read_through -> false (mitigation)
 - 08:23 UTC — hit_rate returned to 88%, p95 improved to baseline
- Impact: ~12% of API requests saw increased latency for 20 minutes affecting three large tenants.
- Root cause: misconfigured TTL and a surprise migration that increased memory pressure leading to eviction storms.
- Contributing factors: lack of pre-migration cache warm, missing autoscaler headroom, no migration checklist.
- Immediate remediation: toggled read-through, increased cache memory by 8GB.
- Long-term actions: add migration checklist (owner: infra-ops, ETA: 2026-02-20), implement cache warm-run in deploy pipeline (owner: serving-runtime, ETA: 2026-03-01).

Governance and runbook hygiene:
- Maintain micro-runbooks as <= 1 page. If a runbook requires > 6 steps or multiple teams, convert it into a full runbook and link it from this doc.
- Quarterly review: each micro-runbook owner must confirm validity or mark for rewrite.
- Runbook retirement: deprecate and archive runbooks with no updates for 18 months and no incidents referencing them.

Appendix: useful commands and links
- Incident CLI: incident-cli open --title "<title>" --sev P2 --assignee "on-call"
- Evidence collector: incident-evidence-cli collect --incident-id {INC} --targets grafana,logs,request-samples
- Grafana dashboards: Cache Overview | Gateway Latency | Model Quality
title: Compact first-responder playbook and light postmortem scaffold

text:
--assignee "on-call"
- Evidence collector: incident-evidence-cli collect --incident-id {INC} --targets grafana,logs,request-samples
- Grafana dashboards: Cache Overview | Gateway Latency | Model Quality Signals | Region Health
- Playbook owners: Maya Patel (eng-sre, playbook steward), Liam Chen (serving-runtime owner), Aria Gomez (oncall program manager)

If you discover gaps during an event: log them immediately under the incident as "runbook-gap" and add to the postmortem actions list. This document is intentionally compact — expand to a full runbook for repeat incidents or when a single mitigation requires >1 hour of coordinated work.

text: Summary This playbook defines the operational and governance steps Customer Success, SRE, and FinOps must follow when performing a tenant-level cutover that changes billing or capacity controls (examples: moving a customer from trial to committed Dedicated, enabling quota-protector webhooks, or toggling per-tenant burst limits). The goal is to ensure cost predictability, tenant safety, and clear rollback paths while minimizing customer-visible disruption. Scope and When to Use this Playbook - Applies to any tenant cutover that will alter: billing plan, reserved capacity assignment, per-tenant rate/quotas, or cost-protecting automations. - Not for routine model version upgrades or small config tweaks that are covered by our standard canary rollout playbooks. Definitions - Cutover: The event during which a tenant transitions from one financial/operational state to another (e.g., trial -> paid committed, hosted -> dedicated, or quota cap changes). - FinOps Gate: A pre-approved financial control threshold that requires Finance + CS sign-off before enabling. - Safety Net: Automated quota/fallback rules that prevent unbounded spend or dangerous latency regressions. Stakeholders and Responsibilities - Customer Success (CS): primary contact for customer comms, scheduling, and acceptance. - FinOps: validate contract terms, commit levels, and billing triggers; approve FinOps Gates. - Eng SRE / Platform: implement routing changes, capacity reservations, and alerting. - Oncall (runtime):
title: Tenant FinOps Cutover & Governance Playbook

text: - FinOps: validate contract terms, commit levels, and billing triggers; approve FinOps Gates. - Eng SRE / Platform: implement routing changes, capacity reservations, and alerting. - Oncall (runtime): 72-hour monitoring and first-responder for incidents. - Account Executive (AE): commercial ownership, billing follow-up. Prerequisites (pre-cutover checklist) 1) Contract & Billing - Verified signed MSA and SOW in Salesforce with committed throughput and billing start date. - Billing metadata (billing_contact_email, billing_entity_id, billing_region) present in tenant metadata. - FinOps has generated the pro-forma invoice and validated charge schedule. 2) Technical Readiness - Tenant tags: "tier=dedicated|hosted", "finops_commit_id=<id>", and "slo_profile=<profile>" set in tenant record. - Capacity reserved: GPU pool assignment and reservation confirmed in capacity inventory. - Quotas: quota config (requests/min, tokens/day, embedding-ops/day) created in quota service. Example JSON stored in deployment ticket. 3) Observability and Alerts - Dashboards prepared: Tenant Overview, Cost Burn Rate, Token Distribution by Route, Cache Hit Rate, p99 Latency. - Alerts provisioned (see Alert matrix section). - Billing report configured to deliver daily usage CSV to FinOps and CS (S3 or internal bucket). 4) Communication - Cutover schedule agreed with customer and internal teams (UTC timezone). - Customer-facing FAQ and rollback expectations drafted. Cutover Phases Phase A — Pre-cutover (T-minus
title: Tenant FinOps Cutover & Governance Playbook

text: bucket). 4) Communication - Cutover schedule agreed with customer and internal teams (UTC timezone). - Customer-facing FAQ and rollback expectations drafted. Cutover Phases Phase A — Pre-cutover (T-minus 72 to 2 hours) 1. Confirm tickets and approvals: ticket in triage board links to contract and capacity reservation. 2. Run validation script locally or via CI to verify tenant config: $ ./tools/validate-tenant-config --tenant-id T-12345 --assert-quotas --assert-tags Expected output: PASS for quotas, tags, billing metadata. 3. Snapshot current state: export tenant routing, quota, and billing metadata to the ticket. 4. Create safety net override set: temporary conservative quotas one tier below target for first 24 hours unless FinOps approves full limits. Phase B — Cutover execution (T0) 1. Put a short maintenance marker on the tenant dashboard (visible to customer): "Planned change in progress — effective <time>". 2. Apply billing plan change via Billing API (example): curl -X POST https://billing.internal.redwood/v1/tenants/T-12345/plan -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"plan":"dedicated-committed-v2","commit_units":1000000,"effective_date":"2026-02-01"}' 3. Assign capacity and update routing tags via Platform API: curl -X PATCH https://platform.internal.redwood/v1/tenants/T-12345/config -H "Authorization: Bearer $PLATFORM_TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"tags":{"tier":"dedicated","gpu_pool":"pool-west-3"}}' 4. Apply
title: Tenant FinOps Cutover & Governance Playbook

text: https://platform.internal.redwood/v1/tenants/T-12345/config -H "Authorization: Bearer $PLATFORM_TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"tags":{"tier":"dedicated","gpu_pool":"pool-west-3"}}' 4. Apply quota and safety-net policies: post the final quota JSON to Quota Service. Example snippet: { "tenant_id": "T-12345", "quotas": { "requests_per_minute": 1200, "tokens_per_day": 2000000, "embedding_ops_per_day": 50000 }, "safety_net_mode": "soft-guard", "soft_guard_thresholds": {"spend_usd":1000, "tokens":500000} } 5. Confirm route propagation and capacity allocation in the platform. Watch for immediate alert triggers. Phase C — Immediate validation (T0 + 0–2 hours) 1. Smoke tests from multiple regions: generation, streaming, embeddings. Use the customer test harness or synthetic runner. 2. Compare baseline metrics (pre-snapshot) to current within tolerance: token rate ±30%, p99 latency <= baseline*1.5, cache hit rate not falling >10%. 3. If safety-net thresholds hit, implement automatic throttle sequence: reduce requests_per_minute by 50% and notify CS & FinOps. Phase D — Stabilization (T0 + 2–72 hours) 1. 24h: FinOps reconciles daily usage CSV and confirms expected burn rate. 2. 48h: CS calls customer for acceptance check and to confirm no functional regressions. 3. 72h: If metrics stable and FinOps approves, remove safety-net overrides and promote quotas to final values. Otherwise, escalate per rollback decision tree. Alert Matrix (recommended thresholds) | Alert name |
title: Tenant FinOps Cutover & Governance Playbook

text: metrics stable and FinOps approves, remove safety-net overrides and promote quotas to final values. Otherwise, escalate per rollback decision tree. Alert Matrix (recommended thresholds) | Alert name | Trigger | Primary owner | Escalation window | |-----------|---------:|---------------|-----------------:| | Tenant: Cost burn spike | spend_rate > projected_rate * 1.4 for 30m | FinOps | 30m -> Pager -> Eng SRE | | Tenant: P99 latency high | p99 > SLO * 1.5 for 10m | Runtime oncall | 10m -> Pager -> Eng Platform | | Tenant: Token quota breach | tokens_today > quota * 1.05 | CS/FinOps | 5m -> Auto-throttle -> Pager | | Tenant: Cache hit drop | cache_hit_rate drop >= 15% | Runtime oncall | 30m -> Investigate | Rollback Decision Tree (high level) - If latency or error rate spikes and cannot be mitigated within 20 minutes with route/fallback changes -> rollback to previous routing tag and billing plan. - If cost spike is due to customer activity but within agreed commit -> engage AE + FinOps to absorb; if outside commit and rapidly increasing -> enable hard-guard quotas and notify customer. Rollback Procedure (commands) 1. Revert routing tag: curl -X PATCH https://platform.internal.redwood/v1/tenants/T-12345/config -H "Authorization: Bearer $PLATFORM_TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"tags":{"tier":"hosted","gpu_pool":"shared-default"}}' 2. Revert billing plan: call Billing API to set plan back to previous value (store previous in snapshot). 3. Re-apply previous quota
title: Tenant FinOps Cutover & Governance Playbook

text: application/json" -d '{"tags":{"tier":"hosted","gpu_pool":"shared-default"}}' 2. Revert billing plan: call Billing API to set plan back to previous value (store previous in snapshot). 3. Re-apply previous quota JSON from snapshot. Communication Templates - Customer notification (pre-cutover): Subject: Planned account update on <DATE> — brief downtime unlikely Hi <Customer Contact>, We will apply your requested plan changes on <DATE> at <TIME UTC>. Services will remain available; we will monitor performance closely for 72 hours and follow up with an acceptance call. If you have a specific traffic window to avoid, please let us know. - Customer notification (if safety-net throttle applied): Subject: Temporary throttle applied to protect account from unexpected spend Hi <Customer Contact>, We detected a rapid increase in usage and have temporarily limited request throughput to protect against unanticipated charges. This throttle is reversible and will be reviewed immediately by your CS and our FinOps team. Expect an update within 30 minutes. Post-cutover Tasks and Reconciliation - FinOps: reconcile daily usage vs projected commit; flag discrepancies >10% for AE follow-up. - CS: customer acceptance form filled and added to account record. - SRE: close monitoring dashboard and add any new alerts discovered to the alert library. - Billing: confirm invoice generation scheduled correctly. Quarterly Review and Handover - CS + FinOps will include the tenant in the next QBR if commit >=
title: Tenant FinOps Cutover & Governance Playbook

text: new alerts discovered to the alert library. - Billing: confirm invoice generation scheduled correctly. Quarterly Review and Handover - CS + FinOps will include the tenant in the next QBR if commit >= $50k/mo or if any cutover required manual FinOps interventions. QBR items should include: usage trend, cost-per-1k tokens, cache optimisation opportunities, and recommendations for model/quantization adjustments. Runbook Annexes - Example Grafana alert rule (pseudo): alert: TenantCostSpike expr: increase(tenant_spend_usd_total{tenant_id="T-12345"}[30m]) / (expected_rate) > 1.4 for: 30m - Quota API example (JSON) — see "quotas.json" attached to ticket. Operational Notes and Best Practices - Always snapshot before any cutover and keep the snapshot in the ticket; it is the single source for rollback. - Prefer "soft-guard" for first 24–72 hours unless customer explicitly requests full limits at T0. - Automate daily usage exports to reduce manual reconciliation errors. - Ensure AE is copied on FinOps anomalies that may affect contract renegotiation. Appendix: Acceptance Checklist (to be signed by CS & Customer) 1) Billing plan activated and verified in Billing UI (screenshot attached). 2) Quotas applied and smoke tests passing from three regions. 3) Daily usage delivery to FinOps confirmed. 4) Temporary safety-net policy in place for T0+72h. 4) Acceptance call scheduled for T0+48h. If any item is not complete, mark cutover as paused and follow the pause checklist in the ticket. Contact
title: Tenant FinOps Cutover & Governance Playbook

text: confirmed. 4) Temporary safety-net policy in place for T0+72h. 4) Acceptance call scheduled for T0+48h. If any item is not complete, mark cutover as paused and follow the pause checklist in the ticket. Contact and Escalation Roster - CS Lead: Maya Lopez (pager: #cs-lead) - FinOps: Jordan Kim (pager: #finops) - Runtime oncall: Oncall rotation (see internal roster) - Platform engineer responsible for tenant routing: Sofia Patel Document History - Created: 2026-01-10 by Maya Lopez - Reviewed: 2026-01-25 by Jordan Kim, Sofia Patel, Liam Chen - Published: 2026-02-02

text: summary: Introductory discovery between Redwood and Tethys Systems to evaluate a channel+SI partnership. text: summary: Northstar walked through current pain points upgrading their on-prem Redwood Private environment: upgrades are high-touch, require manual sequencing between control plane components and data plane, and rollback is mostly 'restore from VM snapshot and hope'. They emphasized air-gapped constraints (no outbound internet), strict change windows, and audit evidence requirements. Redwood reviewed the planned upgrade workflow (preflight checks -> plan generation -> backup -> staged rollout -> verification) and discussed which prechecks should block vs warn (storage headroom, DB connectivity, OIDC reachability). Backup approach was discussed across CSI snapshots and logical DB dumps, with Northstar noting they do not have consistent snapshot support across clusters. Key concerns were around schema migrations (lock timeouts, partial upgrades), deterministic rollback hooks, and how artifacts are verified offline (checksums/signatures). The call ended with agreement on beta prerequisites, a rollback drill, and a short list of environment details Northstar will provide. topics: Current on-prem upgrade workflow and failure modes Air-gapped artifact delivery and verification (checksums/signing) Preflight checks: cluster health, storage, RBAC/SSO, networking/DNS/TLS, DB readiness Backup/restore: CSI snapshots vs logical dumps, encryption/KMS expectations Rollback semantics: partial upgrade handling, schema migration guardrails Beta timeline, success criteria, and a rollback drill transcript:
title: Private upgrades technical deep dive (design partner) - upgrade plan, backups, rollback

text: CSI snapshots vs logical dumps, encryption/KMS expectations Rollback semantics: partial upgrade handling, schema migration guardrails Beta timeline, success criteria, and a rollback drill transcript: Meeting header Date: 2025-05-28 Time: 4:03pm - 5:00pm PT Duration: 57 minutes Call type: Technical deep dive Attendees Redwood Inference: Markus Klein, Fatima Noor, Bruno Silva, Irene Choi, Sean Gallagher Northstar Health Systems: Priya Raman, Evan Wu, Marisol Chen, Jake Miller --- [00:00] Markus Klein: Cool, I think we’re recording. Um, thanks everyone for making time. Goal today is to go deeper on the upgrade and rollback stuff for Private, especially for on-prem, air-gapped. We’ve got Fatima, Bruno, Irene, Sean here. [00:15] Priya Raman: Sounds good. [00:16] Markus Klein: Priya, do you wanna start with what you all do today for upgrades and, like, where it’s painful? [00:24] Priya Raman: Yeah. So currently our process is basically, we schedule a maintenance window, we… we download the new package from our internal mirror, we do a Helm upgrade, and then it’s like, fingers crossed. The ordering is not obvious. We’ve had one incident where control plane came up but data plane got stuck because of… I think it was a migration? And rollback was manual. [00:47] Jake Miller: The rollback is basically, we restore etcd snapshots and PV snapshots where we can, but not all clusters have the same storage. Sometimes we revert the whole node pool image. It’s… it’s messy. [01:01] Evan Wu: Yeah
title: Private upgrades technical deep dive (design partner) - upgrade plan, backups, rollback

text: is basically, we restore etcd snapshots and PV snapshots where we can, but not all clusters have the same storage. Sometimes we revert the whole node pool image. It’s… it’s messy. [01:01] Evan Wu: Yeah and the charts have hooks but they don’t always run in the order we expect. Like pre-upgrade jobs time out and then Helm rolls back but the DB already got migrated. [01:16] Sean Gallagher: Yep, that’s the exact class of issue we’re trying to kill. Partial upgrades + schema migration side effects. [01:23] Markus Klein: Great context. Fatima, do you wanna give the 2-minute overview of the new approach we’re implementing? [01:30] Fatima Noor: Sure. So the direction is: the installer generates an upgrade plan first, it’s a versioned artifact that lists stages and steps. Before doing anything, we run preflight checks—cluster health, storage headroom, DNS/TLS, RBAC, database connectivity, and also artifact availability. Then we orchestrate backups. After that we execute the plan in phases: control plane first, then data plane, with readiness gates. If something fails, we have deterministic rollback entry points that use stored state from the plan execution. [02:05] Priya Raman: When you say installer—this is the redwood-private-installer CLI, right? Not Helm itself? [02:10] Fatima Noor: Correct. Helm still does the actual apply of charts, but we wrap it with an orchestrator so we can be consistent and auditable. [02:19] Marisol Chen: Auditable meaning we can show, like, exactly what
title: Private upgrades technical deep dive (design partner) - upgrade plan, backups, rollback

text: Correct. Helm still does the actual apply of charts, but we wrap it with an orchestrator so we can be consistent and auditable. [02:19] Marisol Chen: Auditable meaning we can show, like, exactly what checks ran and what was backed up? [02:24] Fatima Noor: Exactly. Plan output persisted, precheck results, backup IDs, and upgrade start/stop events. [02:31] Irene Choi: And timestamps, plus versions: current version, target version, and what artifacts were used. [02:39] Marisol Chen: Okay. [02:41] Markus Klein: Evan, can you describe your on-prem constraints? Like how air-gapped are we talking. [02:47] Evan Wu: Fully. No outbound internet. We have an internal artifact mirror for container images and we can host a chart repo internally, but it’s manual. Also we need checksums for everything. Our security team won’t allow “curl random thing during upgrade.” [03:06] Marisol Chen: And signatures if possible. Checksums alone are… better than nothing, but ideally there’s a signing chain. [03:13] Fatima Noor: Yep. So we’re adding bundle verification: manifest, checksums, and optional signatures. The installer will fail early if the bundle doesn’t match. [03:24] Priya Raman: Can it operate with, like, a USB drop? We sometimes do physical transfer to the restricted enclave. [03:30] Fatima Noor: That’s a supported model. The bundle is basically a directory or tarball with a manifest file plus all referenced artifacts. [03:40] Bruno Silva: And on the Terraform side for VPC it’s a bit different,
title: Private upgrades technical deep dive (design partner) - upgrade plan, backups, rollback

text: That’s a supported model. The bundle is basically a directory or tarball with a manifest file plus all referenced artifacts. [03:40] Bruno Silva: And on the Terraform side for VPC it’s a bit different, but for on-prem we’ll document “offline registry endpoint” variables too, so the installer knows where to pull from without going to the public registry. [04:08] Sean Gallagher: Right. So we’re defining “rollback hooks” as part of the plan. The critical part is: schema migrations have to be guarded. We’re implementing idempotency checks and lock timeouts. Also, we’re biasing to backwards-compatible changes when possible so rollback doesn’t require “un-migrating” data. text: [09:02] Evan Wu: Helm hooks have bitten us, honestly. [09:05] Fatima Noor: Yep. We’re adding explicit timeouts and fail-fast behavior. And more importantly we persist state so if it fails, the rollback command knows what happened. [09:18] Jake Miller: What does rollback actually do? Like “helm rollback” only? [09:22] Sean Gallagher: It depends on where you are. If it’s an early failure before migrations, it’s basically revert chart versions. If migrations have run, rollback may involve restoring from backups or switching to a compatible older app version that can still read the newer schema (that’s the backwards-compat work). [09:41] Priya Raman: And if you can’t go back, you’ll tell us? [09:44] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. The plan has rollback metadata per step. Some steps are “non-reversible” and then rollback requires restore, not just “undo.” [09:55] Marisol Chen: That needs to be very clear. We can’t have ambiguity in a change window. text: If we fail during control-plane upgrade before applying schema changes, rollback is “revert charts and configs.” If we fail after schema changes, rollback might require restore of DB snapshot. So the rollback command reads the stored state: which steps completed, what backup IDs exist, and then executes rollback hooks in reverse order. [17:25] Evan Wu: Reverse order meaning last step undone first. [17:28] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. [17:29] Priya Raman: Can we choose “stop and hold” instead of rollback? text: summary: AWS Marketplace team walked Redwood through SaaS listing requirements and common rejection reasons. Focus was on security documentation expectations (SOC 2/ISO posture wording, data handling, encryption/KMS, audit logs, retention) and how to present shared responsibility in the listing + attached docs. AWS recommended tightening claims (avoid absolute compliance statements), ensuring support and refund language is explicit, and providing a clean architecture/data flow diagram. Timeline: AWS said first-pass review is typically ~5–10 business days after submission if artifacts are complete; security review can add a few days. Redwood targeted end-of-month submission to support a February launch window, pending final copy, screenshots, and attachment bundle. transcript: Meeting Header Date: 2026-01-14 Time: 10:03 AM PT Duration: 43 minutes Title: AWS Marketplace listing review sync - Redwood Inference (SaaS offer) Attendees (Redwood): Jordan Blake, Soojin Lee, Chris Osei, Naomi Feldman, Cole Summers, Alex Martinez Attendees (AWS): Megan Li, Andrew Patel, Sara Kline Auto Summary (Fireflies) - Discussed required listing fields and attachments for SaaS offers. - Reviewed security documentation expectations and compliant wording. - Covered review timelines, iteration loops, and common delays. [02:07] Andrew Patel: For SaaS, you’ll need: EULA or terms link, support information (hours, contact methods), refund policy statement, privacy policy, and then any security documentation you want to attach. Also, the “How it works” section has to be clear: subscribe -> get entitlement -> create account or link account -> start using. [02:27] Megan Li: And architecture diagram is not strictly required, but it’s one of the most common things reviewers ask for when the data handling story is unclear. It’s also

[02:45] Sara Kline: Not as a public attachment, no. We don’t want vendors uploading full audit reports publicly in the listing attachments. What we typically see is a security overview plus an attestation statement. If you have a SOC 2, you can say “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” or “available upon request” and give a process. text: with ISO wording. “ISO 27001 certified” is sometimes ok if you have the certificate and the scope covers the service, but you should specify scope and date. [04:00] Naomi Feldman: We’ll stick to “aligned” where appropriate and keep hard claims tied to evidence. [04:06] Chris Osei: Sara, what are the top security reasons a listing gets sent back? [04:10] Sara Kline: Three buckets. One: ambiguous data handling—where prompts go, whether you store inputs/outputs, and default retention. Two: encryption statements that are too vague, like “we encrypt everything” without in-transit/at-rest detail, or without key management model. Three: claims that imply AWS endorses the service or implies compliance you can’t back up (HIPAA, FedRAMP, PCI, that kind of thing). text: transcript: Fireflies.ai - Meeting Transcript Title: AWS private offer terms and process (Redwood Inference) Date: Feb 12, 2026 Start time: 4:00 PM UTC Duration: 46 minutes Recorded by: Fireflies.ai Attendees - Stephanie Nguyen (Redwood Inference) - Jordan Blake (Redwood Inference) - Laura Bennett (Redwood Inference) - Sofia Mendes (Redwood Inference) - Cole Summers (Redwood Inference) - Ryan Patel (AWS Marketplace) - Meghan O'Connor (AWS Marketplace) - Ankit Rao (AWS) --- Auto-summary (generated) - Reviewed AWS Marketplace private offer flow and typical approval timeline. - Discussed standard terms: effective date, acceptance window, refunds/credits, termination, and what can/can't be customized in Marketplace. - Confirmed required inputs: buyer AWS account ID, product/offer type, dimensions, pricing schedule, contract duration, and legal docs. - Flagged open items: discount guardrails, how to handle credits/refunds, and how Redwood should represent Dedicated/Private SKUs. text: common. [01:05] Stephanie Nguyen: So, first question: can we just walk the end-to-end? Like, what are the steps and typical timeline. [01:12] Meghan O'Connor: Sure. For a private offer, you’ll need the buyer’s AWS account ID, the product, the offer type, pricing and dimensions, and then you submit through the management portal. Deals desk review varies—anywhere from 24 hours to, say, 5 business days depending on complexity and whether there are custom EULA changes. [01:32] Stephanie Nguyen: When you say offer type, you're referring to… [01:34] Ankit Rao: It’s basically whether you are doing SaaS Contract, SaaS Contract with Consumption, or pure consumption. For most SaaS vendors that want a committed spend plus overage, SaaS Contract with Consumption is the pattern. [01:49] Cole Summers: Just to sanity check—"contract with consumption" allows a committed amount that draws down, and then metered overage billed monthly, right? [01:58] Ankit Rao: Yes. There are nuances in how you represent the commit—some folks do it as an upfront fee, some do it as a monthly schedule. But the key is: contract portion is billed per the schedule, consumption portion is metered through the Marketplace metering service. [02:14] Laura Bennett: And from a buyer standpoint, they see one line item on AWS invoice? [02:19] Meghan O'Connor: They typically see the contract charges and usage charges, but yes, on the AWS invoice. Depending on their consolidated billing, it can show per linked account. [02:30]

text: summary: Northpeak Analytics wants to purchase Redwood Inference through AWS Marketplace (preferably via private offer) so spend can count toward their AWS commitments. They asked detailed security questionnaire items (SOC 2 posture, encryption/KMS, retention, audit logs, data residency, incident response, SSO/RBAC) and clarified shared responsibility for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. Redwood outlined marketplace subscription → entitlement → Redwood org provisioning flow and noted typical entitlement propagation delays. Next steps include sending the marketplace security packet, confirming which SKU matches their target deployment (Hosted initially; Dedicated later), and starting an AWS private offer request with required buyer details. topics: Why AWS Marketplace purchase path (EDP / committed spend) and private offer vs public offer SKU fit: Hosted API now, Dedicated capacity later; what is and isn't supported via Marketplace Metering and billing dimensions: tokens/requests, reporting cadence, invoice visibility Security questionnaire: SOC 2 / ISO alignment, encryption in transit/at rest, KMS options, audit logs, retention controls, data residency Onboarding: subscription → entitlement validation → org linking → first API key → first call Support & escalation: SLA expectations for enterprise + marketplace customers Timeline: internal approvals, legal/security review, and target pilot start action_items: Redwood (Stephanie): Email AWS Marketplace private offer intake checklist
title: Northpeak Analytics - procurement via AWS Marketplace (security + next steps)
