text: summary: FinBank walked through DR requirements for a Redwood Private VPC deployment (plus a smaller, restricted environment with limited egress). Key asks: enterprise RPO/RTO targets, deterministic restore into a clean cluster, encryption with customer-managed keys, and evidence/validation of restores. Redwood described the Day-2 toolkit direction: control-plane backups (manifest + encrypted artifact), optional scheduled backups via Helm CronJob, least-privilege S3+KMS patterns, and post-restore validation/smoke tests. Discussion covered whether Kubernetes resources are included (Redwood will back up Redwood control-plane/state + critical Helm values; Kubernetes/etcd snapshots are separate and only for cluster-level recovery), handling of secrets (references vs secret material), KMS/HSM constraints, and offline/air-gapped-ish restore workflows (signed bundles, no outbound calls, local checksum verification). topics: FinBank target RPO/RTO and DR scenarios (cluster loss vs region outage) Backup scope: control plane state, config DB, object store references, routing/tenant config Kubernetes backup tools (Velero) vs Redwood snapshots; etcd snapshot caveats Encryption: envelope encryption, KMS key ownership, rotation, and KMS outage behavior Air-gapped / restricted egress operations: signed artifacts, offline docs, deterministic bundles Restore validation: smoke tests, health checks, audit log continuity, and evidence artifacts action_items: FinBank (Tom Reyes) - Send current target
title: FinBank x Redwood — Private DR + backup/restore technical deep dive

Colin and Sean are here to go into the runbook-y pieces, and Hanae for keys/secrets. [01:13] Colin O'Donnell: I can start with scope. When we say “backup/restore for Redwood Private,” we’re talking primarily about the Redwood control plane state and critical configuration state required to deterministically recreate the deployment: tenant config, routing policies, model pins, auth config, and the internal config database that tracks those. We’re not trying to replace cluster-level disaster recovery. [01:34] Tom Reyes: So not like Velero everything in the cluster. [01:37] Colin O'Donnell: Exactly. We see people reach for Velero as a blunt instrument, and it can work, but it’s also a foot-gun if you restore secrets or CRDs in the wrong order. Our approach is: Redwood produces a versioned backup artifact for Redwood-owned state. If you also
title: FinBank x Redwood — Private DR + backup/restore technical deep dive

text: instrument, and it can work, but it’s also a foot-gun if you restore secrets or CRDs in the wrong order. Our approach is: Redwood produces a versioned backup artifact for Redwood-owned state. If you also want to use Velero, we’ll provide guidance for what to include/exclude, but we don’t rely on it. [02:00] Sean Gallagher: And from an operational standpoint, our runbooks are basically split into: (1) consistent control-plane backup, (2) restore to a fresh cluster or existing cluster, and (3) DR plans by archetype, like single-region restore-in-place. [02:15] Aisha Patel: What’s “consistent” mean here? Do you stop the world? [02:19] Sean Gallagher: Not fully stop-the-world, but we do a quiesce step for writes in the control plane. Think “pause mutations” briefly so we get a coherent snapshot of the config DB plus the related manifests/values we need. It’s on the order of seconds to, worst case, a minute depending on environment. [02:40] Tom Reyes: That quiesce impacts inference? [02:42] Colin O'Donnell: Inference data plane stays serving. You might temporarily block admin operations like creating a new tenant or changing routing policy. Existing traffic continues. We’re careful about that separation. [02:55] Leo Grant: Where does the backup get stored? We’d prefer S3 in-region with KMS. [03:01] Colin O'Donnell: S3+KMS is the default reference. We also have customers who require NFS. For GCS it’s possible but usually only if the deployment is in GCP. For AWS VPC, we’d recommend

text: with KMS. [03:01] Colin O'Donnell: S3+KMS is the default reference. We also have customers who require NFS. For GCS it’s possible but usually only if the deployment is in GCP. For AWS VPC, we’d recommend S3 with bucket policies that only allow access from the backup service account role, and SSE-KMS with your CMK. [03:24] Nina Shah: Can you clarify the encryption model? Is it just SSE-KMS or do you do application-level encryption before upload? [03:32] Hanae Suzuki: Great question. We’re moving toward envelope encryption at the artifact level. So the installer/backup tool generates a data key, encrypts the archive locally (AES-GCM), and then encrypts the data key with KMS (or a KMS provider interface, potentially HSM-backed). That way if someone misconfigures the bucket encryption, the artifact is still encrypted. [03:57] Nina Shah: And the plaintext data key never leaves memory? [04:00] Hanae Suzuki: Correct. It’s generated and used in-process, and only the encrypted DEK is stored alongside the artifact metadata. [04:08] Tom Reyes: What exactly is in the archive? Like do you dump Postgres? [04:13] Colin O'Donnell: It depends on the backing store, but conceptually yes: a consistent snapshot of the config DB plus a manifest file that describes versions, checksums, and component inventory. In some installs it’s Postgres, in others we’ve seen managed equivalents. The runbook calls out the supported snapshot method per datastore. We also capture critical “installer inputs” like Helm
title: FinBank x Redwood — Private DR + backup/restore technical deep dive

text: inventory. In some installs it’s Postgres, in others we’ve seen managed equivalents. The runbook calls out the supported snapshot method per datastore. [04:45] Colin O'Donnell: We do not back up the whole cluster. We’ll capture the Helm values and Redwood-specific config objects that are necessary to reconstruct. For CRDs, the safe path is: reinstall the correct Redwood version (which includes CRDs) then restore state. If it’s “oops we deleted a namespace” or “config DB is bad,” app restore. If it’s “etcd is toast,” then cluster restore first, then app validation. Cross-region is doable if your dependencies

text: home”? [08:48] Colin O'Donnell: Yes. We have a “no external calls” posture for on-prem style installs, and we can apply that to restricted VPCs too. For example, image pulls would come from your internal registry mirror. [09:03] Tom Reyes: Let’s talk about secrets. Are secrets in the backup? Like DB passwords, OAuth client secrets. [09:10] Hanae Suzuki: By default, no. We don’t want secret material in the backup artifact. We back up references: secret names, where to find them, maybe the ARN of a secret in Secrets Manager, that sort of thing. On restore, you’re expected to have those secrets provisioned in the target environment. [09:31] Nina Shah: Good. But then restore is not fully self-contained. [09:34] Sean Gallagher: Right, but it’s deterministic if you treat secrets as inputs. Our restore runbook has a pre-flight checklist: “Confirm secrets exist,” “Confirm KMS key access,” “Confirm bucket access,” etc. In regulated environments, secret exfiltration via backup is usually a bigger problem than requiring re-provisioning. [09:59] Tom Reyes: What about rotating keys? If we rotate the KMS key, can we still restore old backups? [10:06] Hanae Suzuki: If you rotate by rewrapping within KMS (keeping old key versions available), you’re fine. If you disable or schedule deletion of old key material, you could lose ability to decrypt. We recommend a key lifecycle policy that preserves decrypt for the retention window. [10:25] Nina Shah: And if KMS is down during backup? [10:29] Hanae
title: FinBank x Redwood — Private DR + backup/restore technical deep dive

text: key material, you could lose ability to decrypt. We recommend a key lifecycle policy that preserves decrypt for the retention window. [10:25] Nina Shah: And if KMS is down during backup? [10:29] Hanae Suzuki: For backup creation, if we can’t encrypt the DEK with KMS, we fail closed. We don’t want a backup artifact produced that’s not properly encrypted. For restore, if KMS is down, you can’t decrypt the data key, so restore can’t proceed. That’s part of your dependency chain for DR. [10:52] Leo Grant: So KMS availability impacts RTO. [10:55] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. Most customers treat KMS as a tier-0 dependency. If you’re in AWS, KMS is pretty reliable, but you can also consider multi-region keys depending on your DR design. [11:10] Aisha Patel: How do you validate restore? Like, what’s the “smoke test” specifically? Then we run a few synthetic requests: create a test tenant (or check an existing tenant in staging), validate routing policy loads, and run a tiny inference request against a known model with expected output shape. [11:45] Colin O'Donnell: We also validate that model pins and fallbacks are restored. People forget that routing policies are part of “state.” [11:53] Tom Reyes: Do you validate audit logs? text: O'Donnell: Exactly. [13:19] Aisha Patel: Can you walk through a restore into a fresh cluster? Like the order. [13:25] Sean Gallagher: Sure. High-level order: 1) Provision the target cluster and baseline dependencies (ingress, storage class, cert-manager if used). 2) Install Redwood Private at the target version (same or compatible) via Helm/installer. 3) Ensure required secrets and KMS/bucket access exist. 4) Run restore job: download artifact, verify checksums, decrypt, apply DB restore, apply config objects. 5) Run post-restore validation hook (smoke tests) and produce a validation report. 6) Switch traffic: DNS/TLS, or update internal load balancer targets. [14:08] Leo Grant: DNS/TLS is a pain. We’d prefer to keep the same endpoint. [14:12] Sean Gallagher: For same-region restore, you can keep the same DNS name. If you’re restoring to a new cluster, you can reuse the certificate if you manage it centrally, or re-issue with the same SAN. The runbook includes the “don’t forget to update certs” checklist. [14:31] Nina Shah: For encryption, do you support customer-provided keys in an HSM? We sometimes use CloudHSM. [14:39] Hanae Suzuki: We’re designing a KMS provider interface. Our first-class is AWS KMS. CloudHSM tends to come in via KMS Custom Key Store or your own service. We’ll need to confirm specifics: do you require all crypto ops in HSM, or just key storage? If it’s KMS Custom Key Store, it can work. If it’s a bespoke API, we’d likely need a provider plugin. [15:10] Tom

text: summary: Redwood and the GCP Marketplace team reviewed how Redwood’s new marketplace SKUs map to GCP billing dimensions (usage-based token/embedding meters vs subscription/commit dimensions), and what identifiers must be carried end-to-end (consumerId/entitlement) to ensure correct metering. GCP emphasized keeping dimension names stable, ensuring aggregation and rounding are consistent, and making the onboarding flow resilient to entitlement propagation delays. The group aligned on a staging test plan: subscribe with a test buyer account, validate entitlement lookup, provision/link a Redwood org, generate an API key, send test inference traffic, confirm metering events appear with the expected dimensions, and verify cancellation/plan change behavior. Redwood will send a proposed dimension list and sample metering payloads for review. topics: GCP Marketplace SKU dimensions and Cloud Commerce metering Entitlement + account linking expectations Onboarding UX expectations (subscription -> entitlement -> Redwood org provisioning) Testing plan in staging before listing submission next_steps: Redwood to share final proposed GCP dimension names/IDs per SKU (Hosted/Dedicated/Private + add-ons) for partner review. GCP to confirm any naming/length constraints and whether multiple dimensions per offer are acceptable for Redwood’s packaging. Redwood to run the staging procurement test end-to-end (subscribe -> entitlement -> org link -> first API call -> metering verification) and report results

text: now have Hosted API usage-based, Dedicated capacity, and Private deployment. We want the marketplace SKUs to map cleanly to our internal billing meters. Dimension names and IDs in the offer need to match what you’re sending in metering events. And be careful if you’re changing them later—customers don’t like it and it can break reporting. [01:35] Cole Summers: That’s exactly what we’re trying to nail. Let me outline what we were thinking and you can tell us what’s realistic. For Hosted, we meter “tokens generated” and “input tokens” internally, but we can expose it as “tokens” total if that’s simpler. We also have embeddings measured as “embedding units” (basically per 1K tokens equivalent). For Dedicated, we have reserved GPU capacity, which internally is monthly commit + overage. Private is more of a platform fee plus support tier. [02:05] Miguel Alvarez: Okay. So in Cloud Marketplace, you can do usage-based dimensions and you can do subscription pricing models, and you can also do committed-use style constructs depending on the product type. Most SaaS partners keep it simple: one or a small number of usage dimensions, like “1K tokens” or “1M tokens”, and then for a base subscription you do a seat or a flat monthly fee. text: summary: Cascade walked through audit/compliance evidence requirements for Private upgrades (VPC) with an eye toward SOX-ish controls and internal auditability. Redwood reviewed planned upgrade lifecycle events (upgrade started, prechecks pass/fail, backup started/completed, migration start/end, rollback invoked/completed), and discussed required fields to make logs useful in Splunk (who, what version, where, correlation IDs). Open questions centered on retention/tamper-evidence, and how customer-managed KMS keys apply to backups and upgrade state. Redwood committed to sharing the audit event schema draft + an example Splunk mapping, and confirming support bundle contents (upgrade plan JSON, last-run logs, checksum list for artifacts). next_steps: Redwood to share draft audit event schema + sample payloads for upgrade/rollback lifecycle events. Redwood to confirm backup encryption behavior in VPC Private (EBS/CSI snapshots vs logical dumps) and what is encrypted with customer CMK. Cascade to provide their minimum audit log retention requirement and whether WORM storage is mandated internally. Redwood to send a one-pager on how to forward Private audit logs to Splunk (HTTP Event Collector) and what fields are indexed by default. text: Specifically: if you rollback, how do we prove we rolled back fully? And if there was a partial upgrade, how do we know what state we’re in. [08:29] Connor O'Brien: The plan-based approach helps. Upgrade apply writes a state machine record: stage started/completed. Rollback consumes that state and emits rollback events per stage. After rollback, we run post-rollback health checks, and we emit rollback.completed with health=pass/fail and references to checks. [08:50] Mei Tan: What are the checks? [08:52] Irene Choi: For control plane: API ready, controller reconciles, DB connectivity. For data plane: model runtime pods ready, service endpoints healthy. Plus we can run a simple synthetic request if configured. [09:06] Elena Petrova: Is synthetic request mandatory? Some environments don’t allow outbound. [09:11] Irene Choi: Not mandatory. It can be purely internal to the cluster. [09:15] Daniel Cho: And the DB migrations—this is the scary part. If the upgrade runs a migration that’s not reversible, rollback might not fully restore. [09:26] Marcus Lin: Right. We’re adding guardrails to require backward-compatible migrations for N-1 to N where possible. But we also rely on backups as the ultimate escape hatch. [09:38] Priya Nair: So for audit, you need to log “migration started”, “migration finished”, and also whether it was “forward-only” or reversible. [09:46] Connor O'Brien: We can log migration version and whether rollback would require restore-from-backup. But “reversible” is
title: Security review - Private upgrades: audit logs, backup encryption, rollback evidence (Cascade)

text: Meeting header Date: 2025-02-11 Start time: 1:00 PM ET Duration: 53 minutes Title: MedData Systems - on-prem backup/restore + audit log retention security review Organizer: Markus Klein (Redwood) Attendees (Redwood): Markus Klein, Aisha Rahman, Hanae Suzuki Attendees (MedData): Dana Wright, Priya Shah, Eli Brooks, Nina Gomez Auto-summary (Fireflies) - MedData asked detailed questions on on-prem backup encryption, retention, audit log export, and audit evidence. - Redwood clarified that backups are encrypted (envelope encryption) and can be configured for customer-managed keys; in air-gapped mode, keys can be handled via on-prem KMS/HSM depending on integration. - Redwood explained scope: control-plane state and configuration are backed up; data plane is treated as stateless; Kubernetes cluster recovery (etcd) is a separate procedure. - Discussion covered audit log retention/export format, immutability options, and what operational evidence Redwood can generate (manifests, checksums, restore validation reports). Topics 1) On-prem backup scope and retention ownership 2) Encryption at rest, KMS/HSM, key rotation, and KMS outage behavior 3) Audit log export + retention (long-term, immutable/WORM) 4) Evidence needs for regulated audits (restore drill proof, reports) 5) Operational boundaries: cluster recovery vs app-level restore Action items (recap) - Redwood to send written security note re: envelope encryption + KMS/HSM availability behavior. - Redwood to send example backup manifest
title: MedData Systems - on-prem backup/restore + audit log retention security review

title: MedData Systems - on-prem backup/restore + audit log retention security review

text: artifacts. How? [04:28] Aisha Rahman: The pattern is envelope encryption. The backup artifact is packaged — typically a tarball plus a manifest — and encrypted with a data encryption key, a DEK. That DEK is then wrapped by a key encryption key, KEK, managed by your KMS or HSM. So the backup at rest is always encrypted, and you control the KEK. [04:50] Nina Gomez: If the environment is air-gapped, how does KMS work? [04:54] Hanae Suzuki: We support a provider interface. In cloud deployments it’s like AWS KMS. For on-prem, it depends on what you have — some customers have a local KMS service, some have HSMs with an API gateway. But access is… it’s locked down. [05:22] Hanae Suzuki: That’s common. What we’d need from you is what operations are permitted and whether we can do unwrap during restore. Sometimes security teams allow wrap only in certain subnets. [05:34] Dana Wright: Key rotation is mandatory annually. What happens to old backups? [05:40] Aisha Rahman: Rotation is supported. Practically: backups created under an older KEK remain decryptable as long as you retain that KEK version or the ability to decrypt old wrapped DEKs. Many KMS/HSMs support key versioning. title: MedData Systems - on-prem backup/restore + audit log retention security review

Team identified confusion around org linking for existing Redwood orgs, unclear messaging during entitlement propagation delays (AWS especially), and missing docs for the "already subscribed" and "multiple subscriptions" edge cases. Engineering confirmed expected entitlement API error codes and noted where console should map them to actionable retry guidance. Docs team flagged needed screenshots and a clearer separation of AWS vs GCP steps, plus a single canonical "How marketplace billing maps to usage" section to reduce support tickets. topics: AWS/GCP marketplace subscription confirmation and entitlement propagation Console entrypoint for marketplace customers (org linking vs create new org) Entitlement verification API behaviors and error states First API key creation and first successful request path Docs gaps: step-by-step, troubleshooting, and where security links live Metering dimension/SKU mapping visibility and billing expectations Telemetry: funnel events and what we can measure reliably questions: Do we want to allow linking a marketplace subscription to an existing org without requiring org owner role, or keep strict owner/admin requirement? Should the console show the purchased SKU name (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) or only show entitlements/limits (tokens/min, capacity) to avoid mismatch with marketplace labels? What’s the maximum entitlement propagation

text: description: Motivation: address two customer-visible mismatches across SDKs: 1) session token grace/TTL handling caused transient 401s during small clock skew and rapid handoffs, and 2) inconsistent retry weighting for streaming vs request/response calls resulted in uneven load on connector pools. This PR implements a lightweight session-grace window, normalizes TTL refresh semantics, and introduces an adaptive retry-weight multiplier for streaming consumers so retries do not hammer the connector during backoff storms. Changes span Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs and include tests, examples, and a minor version bump for each SDK. Checklist: - [x] implement session grace window (py/ts/go) - [x] unify TTL refresh path and prioritize background refresh - [x] add adaptive retry-weight multiplier (configurable) - [x] add integration test harness for race scenario - [x] update README and changelogs - [x] bump package versions (py:1.18.0, ts:2.7.1, go:1.5.3) Related work: Linear ENG-4129, ENG-4187. This description is an overview of the intent, implementation details and rollout notes are in commits and file diffs below. review_comments: Jordan Kim: Noted a race when background refresh happens concurrent with stream resume; requested deterministic test and small refactor to token lookup to reduce lock contention. Priya Nair: added deterministic barrier in integration test and replaced coarse global lock with per-session RWLock; updated test expectations. Luis Martinez: asked for telemetry
title: session grace-period and adaptive retry-weighting across Py/TS/Go SDKs

text: reduce lock contention. Priya Nair: added deterministic barrier in integration test and replaced coarse global lock with per-session RWLock; updated test expectations. Luis Martinez: asked for telemetry points (token_refresh_start/complete, retry_weight_applied) for SLO analysis. Priya Nair: added telemetry counters and a brief note in the observability docs. Anya Petrova: minor nits on TS typings and Go docstrings; author addressed and pushed fixes. release_notes: SDKs: Introduce session grace-period to reduce transient auth failures during token handoffs; unify TTL refresh semantics across SDKs; add adaptive retry-weighting for streaming consumers to reduce connector pressure during retry storms. Breaking changes: none; configuration: new optional parameter session_grace_ms and retry_weight_multiplier available in SDK configs. notes: Rollout plan: 1) packaged releases pushed to registries; 2) feature flagged default behavior (session_grace_ms=250ms, retry_weight_multiplier=0.6) to allow customer opt-out; 3) monitor token_refresh_start/complete and stream_retry_rate dashboards for 72 hours post-release.
title: session grace-period and adaptive retry-weighting across Py/TS/Go SDKs

text: release_notes: Adds a pluggable attention adapter, runtime kernel probing, and a fused rope->rotary stitching path that yields latency improvements for mixed-length workloads. Backwards-compatible fallbacks retained. description: Motivation: runtime needs to pick the best attention kernel variant for heterogeneous workloads without a heavy rollout. This PR introduces a pluggable attention adapter interface plus a small fused operator (rope->rotary stitch) and a kernel-probing harness so the scheduler can prefer the fused path when beneficial. The goal is lower p99 latency for mixed token lengths and more predictable tail-latency by moving compatibility checks out of the hot path. What this does (high-level): - Introduce AttnAdapter interface to abstract kernel selection and probing. - Implement a CUDA probe kernel and lightweight C++ probe harness that records supported fused ops and numeric profiles. - Add a rope->rotary stitching fused op (rope_stitch) implemented with vectorized kernels for small rotary sizes. - Update the request router/scheduler to consult AttnAdapter decisions and prefer fused paths when they meet latency/cost heuristics. - Add deterministic unit and microbench tests, perf harness results, and docs for opt-in rollout. Checklist: - [x] AttnAdapter API and unit tests - [x] probe kernel + safety timeouts - [x] rope_stitch operator (+ fallback) - [x] scheduler integration + policy toggle - [x] docs and perf notes - [x] benchmarks on Ampere and Hopper instances
title: introduce-pluggable-attn-adapter-and-rope-rotary-stitching

text: tests - [x] probe kernel + safety timeouts - [x] rope_stitch operator (+ fallback) - [x] scheduler integration + policy toggle - [x] docs and perf notes - [x] benchmarks on Ampere and Hopper instances - [x] CI green after one rerun Commits (summary): - Add AttnAdapter interface and base impl (adapter.py + adapter.h) - Kernel probe probe_kern.cu and C++ harness (probe_harness.cc) - Rope->rotary stitch operator (rope_stitch.cc / rope_stitch.cu) - Scheduler changes to consult adapter decisions (routing.py / scheduler.cc) - Tests and perf harness (tests/attn/test_probe.py, perf/attn_adapter_bench.py) - Docs (docs/perf/attn_adapter.md) Files changed (high-level): redwood/serve/attn/adapter.py, redwood/serve/attn/adapter.h, redwood/serve/attn/probe_kern.cu, redwood/serve/attn/probe_harness.cc, redwood/serve/attn/rope_stitch.cc, redwood/serve/attn/rope_stitch.cu, redwood/serve/scheduler/routing.py, redwood/serve/scheduler/scheduler.cc, tests/attn/test_probe.py, perf/attn_adapter_bench.py, docs/perf/attn_adapter.md, setup/ci/attn-probe-matrix.yml Review thread (highlights): Lina Gómez (reviewer): "Nice separation of concerns. Two asks: 1) add a deterministic seed and additional assertions in test_probe to avoid flakiness we saw in canary; 2) make the adapter fallback path explicitly opt-out via feature flag so we can A/B on Dedicated." Daniel Hsu (author): "Added deterministic RNG seed and strengthened assertions (tests updated). Feature toggle added: ATTN_ADAPTER_ROLLOUT env + runtime
title: introduce-pluggable-attn-adapter-and-rope-rotary-stitching

text: opt-out via feature flag so we can A/B on Dedicated." Daniel Hsu (author): "Added deterministic RNG seed and strengthened assertions (tests updated). Feature toggle added: ATTN_ADAPTER_ROLLOUT env + runtime config; default off for Dedicated. See adapter.py lines ~120-145." Evan Park (reviewer): "Kernel probe harness looks solid; I'm concerned about the timeout semantics on extremely loaded hosts (we saw a 30ms probe stall last week). Can we bound probe to a pooled worker and fail-safe?" Daniel Hsu: "Probe now runs on a bounded worker thread with a 12ms soft timeout and a 50ms hard timeout; on timeout we log once per boot and mark fused ops as unavailable. This is documented in probe_harness.cc and in docs/perf/attn_adapter.md." Maya Singh (reviewer): "Perf numbers look promising; please add a perf dashboard link and note the GPU types where gains were measured." Daniel Hsu: "Added benchmark CSV and Grafana panel link to the docs; numbers: 6-12% p50 improvement, 8-18% p99 improvement on mixed-length chat workloads on A100/H100 with rope sizes <=64." CI summary: - build: pass - unit-tests: pass (1 flaky kernel test required rerun) - lint: pass - integration-tests: pass - perf-microbench: pass (bench harness runs; recorded improvements) Note: initial CI run failed on a CUDA 11.7 build due to an ABI test; fixed by pinning a sub-dependency and reran — subsequent runs passed. Performance notes: Benchmarks run with canonical chat workload (turn-taking messages with 8/32/512 histories).

text: description: Motivation: Long-tail cost and latency issues often cluster by high-level customer behavior but are masked by uniform sampling. This PR introduces an adaptive cohort sampling service and an interactive trace annotation layer in the Observability Console to enable fast triage of per-tenant token-cost/latency cohorts. Summary: Adds a streaming-aware cohort sampler that preserves chunk boundaries, assigns lightweight cohort labels to invocations, and exports cohorted sampled traces and aggregated micro-metrics. The console gains a Cohort Overlap Matrix panel and an interactive Trace Annotation Layer that surfaces cohort membership, sample fidelity, and heuristic attributions (kernel contention, prefetch misses, cold KV cache). Detailed docs and replaybench integration are included. Primary changes: 1) sampler service: new adaptive_cohorting pipeline with config knobs (target_fidelity, min_cohort_size, recency_weight) and stream boundary handler to avoid splitting chunked streaming responses; 2) telemetry: cohort labels are emitted as span attributes and as metric labels (cohort.id, cohort.fidelity_score); 3) console UI: Cohort Overlap Matrix (heatmap of tenant vs. cohort density), Trace Annotation Layer (token-level badges with cost delta and heuristic flags), and a per-panel fidelity toggle to switch between raw and cohorted views; 4) e2e: replaybench scenarios updated to validate sampler fidelity and backfill behavior; 5) docs and runbook: new observability guide
title: introduce-adaptive-cohort-sampler-and-interactive-trace-annotations

text: per-panel fidelity toggle to switch between raw and cohorted views; 4) e2e: replaybench scenarios updated to validate sampler fidelity and backfill behavior; 5) docs and runbook: new observability guide and oncall playbook section covering cohort investigation and sample-calibration steps. Why this is different: Existing sampling focused on priority or cost-weighted selection; this approach groups similar invocations into cohorts using a lightweight clustering (shape of prompt, average token velocity, model variant) and adapts sampling rate per-cohort based on observed variance and cost impact. The stream-aware boundary preservation prevents noisy artifacts where streaming chunks are sampled separately and misattribute cost spikes. Files of note: cohort_sampler.rs contains the adaptive scoring loop and boundary-preservation state machine; telemetry_instrumentation.rs adds cohort label enrichment for OpenTelemetry spans and Prometheus counters; CohortOverlapMatrixPanel.tsx includes an emphemeral fast-path renderer for large cardinality cohorts to avoid blocking the console thread. Testing and CI: Unit tests added for coordinator edge cases (cohort shrink/grow), property tests for boundary preservation, and an integration test that runs replaybench against a synthetic tenant mix. CI pipeline shows all checks passing (unit, integration, lint, console-build). Rollout plan: Feature-flagged behind obs.coho_sampler.enabled. Phased rollout: enable for 5% of tenants (canary) for 48h,

Docs & runbook: docs/observability/cohort-sampler.md describes sampling model, configuration examples, fidelity calibration steps, and a small oncall playbook to follow when a cohort shows a cost spike (investigate kernel queueing, check prefetch warmup meters, inspect kv cache miss tags). Backwards compatibility & performance: Cohort labels are optional OTel attributes; existing pipelines ignore them if unset. Sampler adds a lightweight fingerprint (sha1-like) and keeps CPU overhead under 2% in perf testing. We added a validation mode to run sampler in "shadow" to validate before enabling label emission. text: description: Motivation: we observed intermittent correctness regressions when speculative fast-path kernel swaps coincided with KV-cache miss storms and non-idempotent attention kernels. This PR adds a lightweight 'switchboard' abstraction that centralizes safety checks, soft-threshold gating, and staged fallback escalation so we can keep the fast path available for steady-state traffic while minimizing correctness incidents during transient stress. Design: - Switchboard maintains a small compatibility matrix keyed by (model-arch, kernel-variant, quant-mode) and a per-route risk score derived from KV miss-rate, recent kernel error rates, and latency variance. - Soft thresholds: two levels (warn, escalate). Warn logs metrics and reduces batch aggressiveness; escalate forces deterministic compatibility kernel and freezes KV-prefetch until risk subsides. - Burst-padding: short-lived padding of batch size for routes that saw a sudden drop in throughput to avoid tiny batches that exacerbate latency spikes. - Compatibility probes: speculative probes run on a sampled fraction of requests to validate parity between optimized and compatibility kernels; probe windows decay to avoid excess CPU during sustained risk. - Config: runtime config via /config/runtime.json and feature flags (SAFE_FALLBACK_PERCENT, PROBE_SAMPLING_RATE, RISK_DECAY_SEC). No schema changes to public API. Developer notes: The switchboard is implemented in src/runtime/switchboard.cc and is integrated as a pluggable
title: dynamic-fallback-switchboard-for-kernel-compatibility-and-kv-safety

text: (SAFE_FALLBACK_PERCENT, PROBE_SAMPLING_RATE, RISK_DECAY_SEC). No schema changes to public API. Developer notes: The switchboard is implemented in src/runtime/switchboard.cc and is integrated as a pluggable policy for scheduler::ContinuousBatcher and for the kernel selection path in src/runtime/kernel_selector.cc. KV safety hooks were added in src/kv/cache_manager.cc to report miss streaks and cold-start indicators. The change includes a small compatibility matrix in configs/compat-matrix.yaml and a doc summary in docs/compatibility/switchboard.md. Tests: unit tests for switchboard logic (tests/switchboard_test.cc), integration test for correctness under simultaneous KV miss and kernel swap (tests/integration/kv_kernel_stress_test.py). Benchmarks: microbench harness shows 0.8% median latency regression for p95-sensitive routes when switchboard is enabled in conservative mode, with a 12% reduction in correctness incidents on stress fixtures. Rollout: feature-flagged rollout with gradual ramp (10% -> 50% -> 100%) and canary that enables escalate path only in lower-traffic regions for first 48 hours. Related: addresses ENG-8472 (reduce correctness regressions from speculative kernels) and provides mitigations requested in SUP-4123 (customer incident). Checklist: [x] design docs updated [x] unit/integration tests [x] metrics and dashboards [x] feature-flag gating [x] release notes release_notes: Introduces a dynamic fallback switchboard to safely route requests between optimized kernels

text: description: Motivation: OpenAI introduced a Responses v6-style streaming frame model that emits richer per-frame attributes and explicit RNG/determinism hints. Customers integrating via the OpenAI compatibility bridge have asked for (1) stable streaming reassembly when frames are split differently by proxies, (2) a simple passthrough for RNG/determinism hints (seed_hint) so users can opt into reproducible generation, and (3) a flexible attribute routing mechanism so arbitrary metadata fields from the Responses frames can be surfaced to Redwood response objects and audit logs. This PR implements a compatibility layer that: 1) introduces frame-level IDs and mapping logic so the bridge can reassemble out-of-order or chunked frames without altering token order; 2) forwards `seed_hint` and `determinism` attributes from upstream frames into the internal Response attributes and the SDK-facing response metadata (preserving the original hint string); 3) provides an attribute-routing config with field mappings and allow/deny lists so teams can opt into passthrough of fields like `tool_call_context`, `response_flags`, and customer-supplied `meta.*` keys; 4) maintains legacy behavior unless the `openai_compat=v6` flag is set in the request or tenant config. Implementation notes: - Bridge: new frame-mapper module that consumes incoming frame envelopes and produces reassembly events. Reassembly is streaming-friendly and emits partial reassembled frames if a gap persists for configurable timeout.
title: Responses v6: frame-level streaming, RNG-hint forwarding, and metadata attribute routing for OpenAI compatibility

text: frame-mapper module that consumes incoming frame envelopes and produces reassembly events. Reassembly is streaming-friendly and emits partial reassembled frames if a gap persists for configurable timeout. - Attributes: `seed_hint` and `determinism` are copied into `response.metadata.seed_hint` and `response.metadata.determinism_level`. We preserve the verbatim source value and annotate the response with `compat.openai_v6=true`. - Routing: added `openai.attribute_route` config (string->string list) in the bridge config. Route examples are in docs. - Tool-calls: when a frame contains `tool_call` attributes split across several frames, the frame-mapper ensures tool-call payloads are reassembled before the SDK receives the structured tool object; partial tool-call fragments are buffered up to 5s by default. - Tests: end-to-end harness simulates chunked network boundaries, reordered frames, and mixed v5/v6 upstreams. New unit tests cover mapping edge cases and attribute mappings. Checklist: - [x] frame mapper module and unit tests - [x] attribute passthrough config and tests - [x] SDK shim updates to expose seed_hint in Python/TS SDKs - [x] e2e harness for chunk reassembly - [x] migration docs and examples - [x] performance microbenchmarks (no measurable latency regression in 95th percentile for typical small chat workloads) Related: ENG-8921 (feature work), SUP-4187 (customer ask), docs: #doc-openai-bridge-v6 in the internal docs space. Notes on backwards compatibility and opt-in:
title: Responses v6: frame-level streaming, RNG-hint forwarding, and metadata attribute routing for OpenAI compatibility

text: percentile for typical small chat workloads) Related: ENG-8921 (feature work), SUP-4187 (customer ask), docs: #doc-openai-bridge-v6 in the internal docs space. Notes on backwards compatibility and opt-in: Default behavior remains unchanged for tenants that have not enabled the compat flag. The new reassembly logic is conservative: only applies mapping for requests marked `openai_compat=v6` or for requests that contain an explicit `frame_version: "v6"` envelope. This reduces risk for older clients that rely on current streaming framing. Operational considerations: - There is a small additional memory cost for buffering partial frames during reassembly; we add a configurable cap (default 3MB per request) and an eviction policy with a diagnostic metric `bridge.reassembly.evicted_frames_count`. - Metric and tracing: added `bridge.frame_mapper.latency_ms` and span annotations for reassembly events. - Observability: Console exposes a table of routed attributes per-request for customers who enable detailed logging. Rollout plan: staged rollout by tenant flag, start with internal infra and a small canary group for Dedicated customers, then open to self-serve tenants. Monitor `bridge.frame_mapper.errors` and `sdk.openai_compat.seed_hint_usage` for the first 2 weeks. If you want to test quickly: use the `tools/resp-v6-sim` script that emits reordered frames and optionally attaches `seed_hint: "s-1234"`. Example request header: `X-Redwood-Compat: openai_v6`. release_notes: Add Responses

text: description: Motivation: pinpoint sub-kernel latency variance and per-tile occupancy skew seen during continuous batching on long sequences. This PR adds lightweight hardware-timestamp tracepoints around kernel launches, a small lock-free per-tile occupancy counter, and a deterministic synthetic-replay microbenchmark that replays recorded request traces at controlled concurrency for stable profiling. Changes are gated behind runtime flags so there is zero overhead when disabled. High-level summary: 1) runtime/tracing/hw_timestamp_trace.h/.cc implements RAII-style trace scopes that capture wallclock and GPU hw timestamps when available and annotate the internal timeline with span ids, 2) runtime/scheduler/tile_counter.h/.cc introduces a cache-friendly per-tile occupancy counter sampled by the scheduler to record occupancy distributions, 3) tools/replay_bench parses a compressed request trace and replays with configurable arrival patterns and concurrency to exercise the batching scheduler reproducibly, 4) observability/exporters/perf_dashboard_exporter.py converts trace output into a bundled JSON for the perf dashboard including per-kernel latency histograms and per-tile occupancy heatmaps, 5) added unit and microbench tests and CI job to validate reproducible runs. Checklist: - [x] add hw timestamp trace scopes - [x] implement per-tile occupancy counters - [x] implement synthetic replay bench - [x] export summarized metrics for dashboard - [x] add CI microbench job - [x] update
title: Add hardware-timestamp tracepoints and synthetic-replay microbench for kernel latency and tile occupancy

text: add hw timestamp trace scopes - [x] implement per-tile occupancy counters - [x] implement synthetic replay bench - [x] export summarized metrics for dashboard - [x] add CI microbench job - [x] update docs and example traces. Usage notes: enable tracing with REDWOOD_TRACING=hw_timestamp or use the scheduler flag --enable-tile-counters. Replay tool accepts --trace-file and --concurrency flags. Recorded traces are compressed with a simple run-length encoding to keep artifacts small. Backwards compatibility: tracing is opt-in and defaults to off so production behavior is unchanged. Performance: measured overhead with tracing on is ~2-3% for short sequences and up to 7% during heavy kernel churn; with tracing off overhead is nil. See release notes for more details. release_notes: Added optional hardware timestamp tracepoints and a synthetic replay microbenchmark to capture kernel-level latency distributions and tile occupancy. Exposed new dashboard metrics for per-kernel P50/P99 and per-tile live occupancy counters. No runtime behavior changes when tracing disabled. review_comments: Diego Marquez: Nice work; can you clarify the lifetime of span ids when the scheduler retries a kernel? text: description: Motivation: Multi-GPU tensor-parallel workloads suffer from lane contention between collective communication and compute when a single stream/priority scheme is used. This PR introduces a deterministic comm-lane allocator that assigns ordered communication lanes (high/low priority) per-layer and per-shard, enabling overlap patterns that reduce tail latency. In parallel, a lightweight 'lazy KV prefetch' optimizer is added to the KV cache path so that warmup for common prefixes is done opportunistically on stalled lanes rather than always prefetching eagerly. Finally, an adaptive continuous-batching heuristic tunes target batch size based on recent latency percentiles and observed KV cache hit rates. Changeset summary: - runtime/tp/comm_lane_allocator.cpp/.h: new allocator that maps layers -> comm lanes, exposes a compact policy DSL (per-layer override, default ring/pairwise modes). - runtime/tp/scheduler/heuristic_scheduler.py: integrates allocator decisions into the scheduling loop; allows comm/compute gating and preemption windows. - runtime/kvcache/lazy_prefetch.cc: lazy prefetch implementation; hooks to scheduler to request prefetch when comm lanes idle. - runtime/kernel_selector/per_layer_profile.cc: per-layer kernel hints (preferred kernel, workspace) emitted by model loader and consumed by scheduler. - runtime/telemetry/metrics: added per-lane latency metrics and kv_prefetch_hit/miss counters. - tests/integration/tp_comm_lane_test.py, perf/benchmarks/tp_adaptive_batch.yaml,
title: comm-lane-allocator: deterministic comm/compute lane assignment + lazy KV prefetch + adaptive batching heuristics

text: consumed by scheduler. - runtime/telemetry/metrics: added per-lane latency metrics and kv_prefetch_hit/miss counters. - tests/integration/tp_comm_lane_test.py, perf/benchmarks/tp_adaptive_batch.yaml, docs/perf/comm-lane-allocator.md. Design notes: - Deterministic allocation ensures reproducible overlap decisions across replicas; avoids nondeterministic NCCL stream races. - Lazy prefetch defers non-critical KV loads until comm lanes experience slack, preserving burst bandwidth for active tokens. - Adaptive batching keeps latency SLOs stable under load by shrinking/growing targets using EMA of p95 latency and a kv-hit-factor. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked (ENG-2317) - [x] Unit tests for allocator and prefetch logic - [x] Integration tests with TP pipeline - [x] Benchmarks (local cluster, p2/8xA100) - [x] Telemetry added for rollout gating Performance results (internal perf cluster, 8xA100, batcher=continuous): - Baseline: p95 generation latency 240ms, token throughput 1.0x - This PR: p95 190ms (-21%), token throughput 1.15x (+15%) - KV prefetch reduced cold-prefix penalty from 40ms to 8ms for common prefixes - Tail improvement concentrated on long-context workloads (>2k tokens) Rollout plan: feature-flag behind runtime.feature.experimental_comm_lanes; staged canary on Dedicated pools, increase traffic to Dedicated-only models, then enable globally. Related: touches on NCCL stream ordering but avoids driver changes; complements recent NCCL tuning PRs. review_comments: Rajiv
title: comm-lane-allocator: deterministic comm/compute lane assignment + lazy KV prefetch + adaptive batching heuristics

text: Summary: Adds per-model metadata and API filtering hints to the model catalog to help clients select models that support tool/function calling, have strong streaming stability characteristics, and expose recommended rate/price limits. Motivation: Customers building production-grade chat experiences need a single model listing that surfaces not just capabilities but operational hints (streaming robustness, function-calling fidelity, concurrency guidance, and pricing bounds) so they can safely route requests and set fallbacks without trial-and-error. This change is intended to reduce integration surprises for SDK users and make the hosted catalog more useful for Dedicated/Private routing policies. What changed (high level): 1) Catalog schema: added fields streaming_stability_score (float 0.0-1.0), streaming_profiles (list of short ids), tool_calling_compat (enum: none/partial/full), structured_output_profiles (list), recommended_concurrency (int hint), pricing_tier (string), per_token_price_usd_hint (decimal string), and region_rate_limits (map-style string encoded per-region hint). 2) List API: new query params supports_tool_calling, min_streaming_score, pricing_tier, max_per_token_price_usd and region to filter results server-side. Also added response hints in the model object to suggest default streaming configuration and recommended backoff. 3) OpenAI-compat shim: updated compatibility flags to include function_calling_policy and structured_output_profile names so the shim
title: Add model-catalog hints: tool-calling support, streaming-stability score, and rate/price limits

text: to suggest default streaming configuration and recommended backoff. 3) OpenAI-compat shim: updated compatibility flags to include function_calling_policy and structured_output_profile names so the shim can map OpenAI-style function calling to our tool_calling_compat enum. 4) Docs & examples: extended model_catalog.md with new filter examples and SDK snippets (TS + Python) showing how to pick models for stable streaming and safe function calls. 5) Tests & migrations: DB migration to add columns, unit tests for filters and serialization, e2e streaming stability check in CI. Backwards compatibility: All new catalog fields are optional and default to conservative values (tool_calling_compat=none, streaming_stability_score=0.0). Clients that ignore unknown fields will continue to work. text: description: Context: Customers expect parity with the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions surface for a range of parameters and behaviors. Motivation: enable drop-in compatibility for stop sequences, top-level 'logprobs' support for token-level scoring, clearer stop_reason handling in streaming mode, and better integration between function-calling/tooling and structured-output modes. This PR implements a compatibility layer and end-to-end tests to cover the most common breakage points. Changes: 1) API layer: expand openai_compat handler to accept and normalize 'stop' (string | list), 'logprobs' (int | null), and the official function_call hinting fields; 2) Streaming: ensure stop detection emits final 'stop_reason' metadata and drains KV cache correctly without truncating subsequent tokens; 3) Logprobs: wire token-level scoring through the response pipeline and include both 'token_logprobs' and 'top_logprobs' when requested; 4) Function/tool calling: preserve function_call hints in streaming chunks, and attach a deterministic 'invocation' object in the final message for structured consumers; 5) Structured-output: add a lightweight formatter that enforces top-level JSON when schema=structured is requested and emits an explicit 'parse_error' field instead of failing the stream; 6) Docs & examples: add compatibility docs and an example showing stop sequences with function calls and structured output. Backwards compatibility: default behavior unchanged if OpenAI fields are not present.
title: OpenAI /v1/chat.completions parity: stop sequences, logprobs, function-calls, and structured-output

Testing: added unit tests for normalization, integration tests that exercise streaming stop and function invocation boundary cases, and an e2e example against the openai-compat harness. text: description: Motivation: bring the Go SDK closer to parity with Python/TypeScript on credential ergonomics and streaming control hooks. Customers reported friction when swapping credential providers and when trying to emit custom stream-level signals (heartbeat/backpressure) from Go. This PR introduces an OpaqueCredentialProvider interface to allow pluggable credential lifecycles and a lightweight StreamSignalHook API to surface heartbeat and backpressure hints to the runtime. What this PR does (high level): - Add OpaqueCredentialProvider interface + default implementations (Static, RotatingWindow, DelegatingChain). - Introduce StreamSignalHook and two built-in hooks: HeartbeatHint and BackpressureHint. Hooks are opt-in on stream creation. - Provide an adapter for legacy credentials so existing users do not need immediate migration. - Improve error ergonomics on auth failures: typed errors and helper methods to introspect transient vs terminal auth errors. - Small surface parity changes: align method names with py/ts SDKs (StreamHandle -> Stream), add WithSignalHooks opts, and bump go module to v1.5.4. - Tests: unit tests for provider rotation, integration test that exercises hook emission while streaming tokens, and additional fuzzing for credential adapters. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed (ENG-1024) - [x] Unit + integration tests added - [x] Backwards-compatible adapter for legacy credentials - [x] Docs updated (README and migration note) - [x] Version bump and changelog
title: Introduce opaque credential providers and stream-signal hooks; Go SDK ergonomics parity

The legacy credential API remains functional via CredentialAdapter; a deprecation notice was added to the README and will be removed in two minor versions. The StreamSignalHook is additive and cannot break existing streams but can change scheduling behavior when hooks emit BackpressureHint frequently. Performance/observability: emitting BackpressureHint toggles an internal tracing tag and exposes a metric sdk.stream.backpressure_hints_emitted which is toggled in the runtime; a follow-up PR will wire this to the console dashboards. review_comments: Liam Chen: Can we make the RotatingWindow provider expose a jittered refresh to avoid thundering herd when many clients refresh at the same boundary? text: description: Motivation: production traces and microbenchmarks showed significant CPU-side tail latency attributed to expensive thread pin/unpin sequences and repeated small serializations of request headers when forming continuous batches. This PR introduces two complementary optimizations: 1) a dynamic NUMA-aware threadpool that probes topology at startup and maintains pliable worker groups to avoid frequent expensive task pinning/unpin operations and to reduce cross-node memory traffic; 2) a bulk request serializer that coalesces header/metadata encoding for small contiguous requests into a zero-copy-friendly header slab when safe, falling back to the original path for incompatible payloads. Changes are targeted at the runtime hotpath and designed to be opt-in via runtime flags to simplify rollout. Checklist: - [x] add NUMA probe and topology-aware worker groups - [x] implement dynamic worker affinity policy with low-overhead heuristics - [x] implement bulk-serializer with zero-copy slab and safe fallback - [x] add kvcache hinting to avoid serialized work for cached prefixes - [x] microbench harness + performance tests - [x] e2e smoke tests on dedicated hardware Motivation, design and tradeoffs are inlined in docs/runtime/numa-bulk-serializer.md. Rollout plan: behind feature flag 'runtime.dynamic_numa_bulk_serializer'; staged A/B on canary fleet; fall back to previous threadpool on degraded metrics. Impact summary: p50 generation latency decreased 6-12% across mid-to-long
title: Dynamic NUMA-aware threadpool and bulk request serializer to reduce CPU-side latency

text: flag 'runtime.dynamic_numa_bulk_serializer'; staged A/B on canary fleet; fall back to previous threadpool on degraded metrics. Impact summary: p50 generation latency decreased 6-12% across mid-to-long sequences in our internal bench, CPU syscalls related to sched_setaffinity reduced by ~82% on noisy NUMA nodes under bursty load, and end-to-end tail latency (p99) improved by up to 18ms in heavy continuous-batching scenarios. Compatibility: no changes to API surface. The feature is disabled by default. Related work: follows observations from ENG-14522 and complements existing batching work in ENG-14971. review_comments: Diego Ramirez (reviewer): Nice direction. Please justify why dynamic affinity is preferred over static pin maps in the design doc. Consider fallback when numa info is inconsistent across kernels. Priya Nair (author): Added section in docs/runtime/numa-bulk-serializer.md explaining decision; dynamic affinity avoids expensive remap operations at runtime and can react to hot-node pressure. Marta Kline (reviewer): Bulk serializer looks good but edgecases around encrypted payloads need explicit checks. Also, can you avoid allocations in the hotpath? Priya Nair (author): Bulk-serializer uses a preallocated slab per-worker and validates 'is_contiguous' and 'not_encrypted' flags before coalescing; added unit tests and comments. Liam O'Connor (reviewer): CI failure on macOS build (clang) — thread APIs used are Linux-specific. Can we gate the probe to only POSIX platforms

text: description: Motivation: Customers using the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions surface expect a few nuanced behaviors (stop semantics, role-preserving role mapping, streaming logprob envelopes, and deterministic function_call hints). Our compatibility layer has historically handled these piecemeal; this change bundles a deterministic, well-tested mapping layer plus a streaming envelope that reduces parser churn for downstream SDKs. What this PR does (high level): 1) Introduces a response-sculpting adapter that normalizes incoming v1 parameters (temperature/top_p/stop arrays/stop strings) into Redwood runtime configs, coalescing stop tokens with deduplication and priority ordering (explicit stop > system-supplied stop). 2) Adds a streaming logprob envelope: each delta event includes a compact logprob map when requested (logprobs mode) and a stable "terminate_reason" token on final events to remove ambiguity for partial consumers. 3) Preserves role metadata and adds role_alias resolution so tools that pass custom roles still receive canonical role labels in responses. 4) Extends function_call handling to surface both suggested and forced calls: when the model suggests a call we emit a standardized tool-call event; when callers force a function_call we validate against registered schemas and return an immediate structured output. 5) Implements conservative backpressure hooks in the stream coalescer to improve stability under bursty clients. Compatibility notes: The adapter keeps backward-compatible
title: v1 OpenAI chat: response sculpting, stop coalescing, and streaming logprob envelope

text: immediate structured output. 5) Implements conservative backpressure hooks in the stream coalescer to improve stability under bursty clients. Compatibility notes: The adapter keeps backward-compatible defaults; previous consumers that do not request logprobs or function schemas should see no change. New behavior is opt-in via the v1 parameter mapping and an additional header (X-OpenAI-Compat: true) for private deployments that want explicit parity checks. Testing and validation: Unit tests added for stop coalescing, role aliasing, function schema validation and negative cases. End-to-end tests simulate streaming clients (chunk reordering, reconnect resume) and assert final assembled events for both SDKs. Benchmarked against a production-like workload: median p99 tail latency increased by ~1ms in the worst case; CPU overhead on the agent path is <2% for common workloads. Rollout plan: Feature gated behind openai_v1_compat flag. Canary on dedicated region us-west-2 for 48 hours with traffic mirroring; if stable, promote to main with a one-week monitoring window (alert on increased stream reconnects or event schema validation failures). Related: ENG-1842 (param mapping), SUP-5523 (customer incident that exposed stop token mismatch). review_comments: Tomás Rivera: Important to ensure ordering for coalesced stops — what happens when a client supplies overlapping regex-based stops? Please add a test. Asha Kapoor: Added deterministic precedence and a unit test for overlapping regex
title: v1 OpenAI chat: response sculpting, stop coalescing, and streaming logprob envelope

Opt-in via openai_v1_compat feature flag and X-OpenAI-Compat header.

text: description: Motivation: Add first-class support for a strict JSON Schema style 'response_format' that can be validated synchronously or during streaming, and ensure OpenAI compatibility layer maps its 'response_format' and 'schema' usage into our runtime behavior. This PR introduces a 'strict' validation mode that rejects outputs that do not conform and produces clear, machine-readable error messages for callers. It also adds incremental/streaming validation so that long-running generation can be validated as tokens arrive and early failures can be surfaced to clients. What changed (summary): implemented a response_format parser that accepts 'json_schema' payloads and a 'strict' flag; added streaming validator that emits validation events on the stream (prefix event type: validation_error / validation_warning); updated the OpenAI compatibility shim to translate OpenAI-style 'response_format' and 'schema' into our internal representation; added runtime guards so fallbacks to permissive mode are available via policy for backward compatibility; extended API docs and SDK examples to show the new flags. Key behaviors: in strict mode the runtime will attempt to fully validate the completed JSON object against the schema; if validation fails the request returns a structured 400-level error with a short machine code (e.g. VALIDATION_ERROR_SCHEMA_MISMATCH) and a human message that includes the top 3 violations. When streaming, partial validation runs on complete JSON fragments; streaming
title: add strict JSON Schema response_format with streaming validation and OpenAI compat mapping

text: with a short machine code (e.g. VALIDATION_ERROR_SCHEMA_MISMATCH) and a human message that includes the top 3 violations. When streaming, partial validation runs on complete JSON fragments; streaming validation failures are emitted inline as 'validation_error' events and, if strict mode is enabled, the stream is closed with a trailing error event. The OpenAI compat layer accepts both top-level 'response_format' and the legacy 'schema' key and preserves the strictness flag (openai: "response_format": {"type":"json_schema","strict":true}). Compatibility: default behavior remains permissive unless the caller sets strict mode. For customers relying on older OpenAI-compatible behaviors we added a rollout toggle (policy: openai_compat.strict_response_format=false) that keeps the legacy permissive semantics. We added a migration note in docs for Dedicated/Private admins. Testing and rollout: unit tests cover schema parsing, validator edge cases, and error formatting. Integration tests exercise streaming validation with large token sequences and function-calling scenarios. We staged the change behind a feature flag and ran a canary on 5% of traffic for 48h before enabling broadly. Docs: API reference updated with examples for strict mode, streaming validation event types, and sample SDK usage. Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the validation engine pluggable; future work will add optional remediation suggestions (e.g., 'coerce_number') and richer error paths for tool-calling workflows.
title: add strict JSON Schema response_format with streaming validation and OpenAI compat mapping

text: release_notes: Adds a formal selection contract for OpenAI compatibility, deterministic streaming frames for tool outputs, per-request selection mode, and migration tooling. Default behavior remains backward-compatible (permissive). description: Background: The OpenAI compatibility layer must behave deterministically for customers that rely on single-tool selection and streaming reassembly. We observed multiple customer integrations where ambiguous tool_choice tokens and non-deterministic stream fragments caused incorrect dispatch and failed reassembly under packet reordering. This change introduces a portable selection contract and deterministic framing for tool messages to make downstream consumers resilient. What changed: 1) Selection contract: a compact contract formalizing how the adapter exposes model-declared tool choices to the router. Three modes are supported: permissive (legacy behavior, first-match), strict_single_choice (model must provide a single explicit tool_choice field; otherwise response is rejected), and advisory (model suggestions are annotated but do not gate dispatch). Modes can be set per-request via the header X-Redwood-OpenAI-Selection or via route config. 2) Mapping stage: explicit tool_schema_mapper converts internal tool descriptors to OpenAI-style function schemas and emits stable schema_id tags. Mapper supports schema_overrides and preserves custom vendor metadata with a vendor_meta field. 3) Deterministic frames: streaming encoder now emits small,
title: openai-compat-selection-contract-and-deterministic-tool-frames

text: function schemas and emits stable schema_id tags. Mapper supports schema_overrides and preserves custom vendor metadata with a vendor_meta field. 3) Deterministic frames: streaming encoder now emits small, self-describing JSON frames with fields: role, schema_id, seq_num, eof, and payload. Frames include a stable schema_id and incremental seq_num so consumers can reassemble even when fragments arrive out of order. The framing protocol is documented and opt-in via openai_compat.streaming_frame_mode: off|stable-json|compact. 4) Reassembly and idempotency: the framer attaches a frame_checksum and an optional message_hint to help downstream idempotent processors ignore duplicates. 5) Backpressure and pacing: when models request multiple tool invocations concurrently, the dispatch router enqueues candidacies into a soft-queue sized by openai_compat.max_concurrent_tool_invokes and applies a paced-launch strategy to avoid burst overload on dedicated pools. Rejections emit metric openai.compat.tool_enqueue_rejected and a standardized OpenAI-style error object with type rate_limit.error. 6) Migration & tooling: added CLI command redwood openai-compat migrate-tools that introspects registered handlers and writes a suggested schema_override file; added lint command redwood openai-compat lint-schemas which flags ambiguous unions and missing required fields. 7) Tests and docs: added unit and integration tests for out-of-order frame reassembly, multi-tool suggestion paths, and rejection/fallback
title: openai-compat-selection-contract-and-deterministic-tool-frames

text: lint-schemas which flags ambiguous unions and missing required fields. 7) Tests and docs: added unit and integration tests for out-of-order frame reassembly, multi-tool suggestion paths, and rejection/fallback behavior. Updated openai-compat.md with protocol examples and a migration checklist. Compatibility and rollout: default selection mode stays permissive. strict_single_choice is behind an opt-in flag openai_compat.enable_strict_single_choice and requires operator confirmation to flip in the console to reduce accidental breakage. For Dedicated clusters the selection_mode can be set via cluster config and propagated over redis. Implementation notes: key changes in src/compat/openai_mapper.py, src/streaming/framer.cc, src/dispatch/tool_router.rs, cli/compat_migrate.py, docs/openai-compat.md. This PR does not change model server kernels or KV cache layout. commits_summary: 4 commits: add selection contract RFC and tests; implement schema mapper and stable frame encoder; add paced dispatch queue and telemetry; docs and migration CLI. title: openai-compat-selection-contract-and-deterministic-tool-frames

text: description: Motivation: We need higher-fidelity, low-overhead micrometrics for kernel selection, per-kernel latency distributions, and continuous-batching behaviour without the cost/volume of NVTX+Cupti instrumentation. This PR introduces an in-process, optional sampling/tracing probe plus HdrHistogram-backed per-op histograms that are cheap to update from hot paths. The goal is to make p50/p95/p99/p999 and quantile-aware alerts practical in CI and on dedicated clusters, and to provide an export path for Grafana dashboards and the benchmark-lab ingestion service. What this change does: 1) Adds a lightweight tracer (runtime::light_probe) that can be enabled at runtime via env var or admin API. It records sparse sampled events (~1%) and tagged metadata (model_id, route, device, kernel_family). 2) Integrates HdrHistogram-based counters for critical runtime hotspots: kernel_selection.latency, kv_cache.lookup.latency, continuous_batch.worker.wait_ms, continuous_batch.coalesced_size. Histograms support aggregation windows and snapshot/export without blocking the hot path. 3) Adds a microbench harness (tools/perf/microbench) that drives focused scenarios (small sequence short-context, long-context, KV heavy) and emits both histogram snapshots and CSV summary that the benchmark-lab accepts. 4) CI: new microbench job that runs the harness on a small GPU instance and stores artifacts; CI baseline comparisons are optional and gated. 5) Observability: exporter to push snapshots to Prometheus
title: add-hdrhist-metrics-and-lightweight-tracing-probes-for-kernel-selection-and-batching

text: 4) CI: new microbench job that runs the harness on a small GPU instance and stores artifacts; CI baseline comparisons are optional and gated. 5) Observability: exporter to push snapshots to Prometheus via a pushgateway adapter and a JSON export compatible with our internal benchmark-lab ingestion. Also includes a sample Grafana dashboard JSON (dashboards/perf/histogram-kernel-selection.json) and a README describing dashboard panels. Design notes and tradeoffs: - We intentionally avoid NVTX and CUPTI for the default path because they create heavy volumes and require CUDA tools; the light_probe is lower fidelity but far cheaper and safe for production. - HdrHistogram was chosen for wide dynamic range and cheap non-blocking snapshots; histograms are kept sharded per-thread to avoid contention. - Sampling rate and histogram bucket config are tunable at runtime; defaults are conservative (1% sampling, 3s snapshot window). - Exporters batch snapshots and respect RT deadlines so we don't affect SLOs. Files changed (high level): tools/perf/microbench/bench_runner.cpp; runtime/metrics/hdr_histogram.h; runtime/metrics/hdr_histogram.cc; runtime/tracing/light_probe.h; runtime/tracing/light_probe.cc; runtime/batching/continuous_batch.cpp (added histogram hooks and sampling points); runtime/kernel/kernel_selector.cpp (additional counters around selection latency and candidate scoring); observability/exporters/pushgateway_adapter.cc; dashboards/perf/histogram-kernel-selection.json; docs/perf/light_probe.md;
title: add-hdrhist-metrics-and-lightweight-tracing-probes-for-kernel-selection-and-batching

text: runtime/kernel/kernel_selector.cpp (additional counters around selection latency and candidate scoring); observability/exporters/pushgateway_adapter.cc; dashboards/perf/histogram-kernel-selection.json; docs/perf/light_probe.md; ci/jobs/microbench.yml; plus small test additions in tests/perf and unit tests for histogram correctness. Commit summary: - Add HdrHistogram wrapper and unit tests - Introduce runtime::light_probe sampling tracer and runtime flags - Hook histograms into kernel selection, KV cache lookup, and continuous batch hotpath - Microbench harness and CI job - Exporter for pushgateway and benchmark-lab JSON format - Grafana dashboard and docs - Fix race in continuous_batch previously surfaced by microbench Testing and validation: - Unit tests for histogram correctness and concurrent updates added (tests/perf/hdr_histogram_test.cc). - Microbench runs on CI with mocked small workloads; artifacts uploaded to internal CI storage (link in job). - Manual validation on perf-canary cluster: collected histograms for 24 hours and compared p95/p99 under controlled load; results attached to this PR as artifacts (ci/artifacts/microbench-20260222.tar.gz). Rollout plan: 1) Merge to main behind feature flag disabled by default. 2) Enable light_probe on perf-canary and dedicated staging clusters for 48h, verify no SLO regressions. 3) Enable histogram snapshot export and ingest to benchmark-lab for dashboarding. 4) Ramp to more clusters if metrics show stable behaviour. text: description: Motivation: Customers integrating complex tool chains reported flaky outputs when multiple tools ran in parallel and tool results were injected back into the model stream. This PR standardizes execution semantics for multi-tool calls, adds runtime safeguards for parallelism, and harmonizes behavior with our OpenAI-compat surface. Summary of changes: 1) Executor: introduce a per-request "tool-parallelism" cap (configurable at API/route level) that bounds concurrent tool invocations and provides backpressure hooks to the scheduler; 2) Injection protocol: formalize a deterministic injection ordering when multiple tool results arrive within the same model turn and attach an idempotency token to each injected result so retries do not duplicate content; 3) Retry semantics: new retry policy enum (none, best-effort, strict-idempotent) exposed via the API and respected by the runtime and OpenAI-compat shim; 4) Streaming stability: when streaming responses include tool-calls, we now atomically flush tool-invocation markers to the stream and gracefully resume generation once injected results are committed to the KV cache; 5) OpenAI-compat updates: function-calling payloads now accept an optional "execution_hints" map with parallelism and idempotency hints that the compat layer translates into our internal routing and executor options; 6) Observability: emit new metrics (tool.invocations.active, tool.invocations.queued, tool.injection.latency) and traces annotated with execution_hints
title: tooling/execution: concurrency safeguards and tool-result-injection ergonomics

text: into our internal routing and executor options; 6) Observability: emit new metrics (tool.invocations.active, tool.invocations.queued, tool.injection.latency) and traces annotated with execution_hints for easier debugging. Implementation notes: - Executor changes are confined to runtime/executor/* and api/handlers/tool_call_handler.go; the executor preserves the previous scheduling algorithm but applies per-request parallelism caps before dispatching tool workers. - Injection order uses a deterministic comparator: (request_tool_sequence, tool_priority, start_timestamp). This avoids nondeterministic interleaving in model-visible outputs. - Idempotency tokens are short-lived UUIDs persisted in the request context and kept in the per-request token-cache; replayed tool results with matching token are ignored by the injection layer. - The OpenAI compatibility shim accepts additional fields but falls back to previous behavior when execution_hints are omitted to preserve backward compatibility. Checklist: - [x] Design doc updated with sequence diagrams and failure modes (ENG-1523) - [x] Unit tests for ordering and idempotency - [x] Integration tests simulating multi-tool fan-out and parallel retries - [x] Metrics/tracing added and tested in canary - [x] Backwards compatibility verified for existing function-calling clients - [x] Docs snippet for API surface added Notes on tradeoffs: We intentionally chose a conservative default parallelism cap of 4 to reduce burst load on downstream
title: tooling/execution: concurrency safeguards and tool-result-injection ergonomics

text: for existing function-calling clients - [x] Docs snippet for API surface added Notes on tradeoffs: We intentionally chose a conservative default parallelism cap of 4 to reduce burst load on downstream tool services; customers can raise this per-route with a warning in the console. The idempotency token approach requires minimal state but does not protect against cross-request duplicate side-effects (we call this out in the design doc). Migration: No action required from existing users; new execution_hints fields are additive. For customers wanting deterministic injection ordering across retries, they should include explicit tool priority in their tool manifest. Rollout plan: Canary on a small percentage of hosted traffic for one week with annotated traces and enhanced logging; force-enable flag included to accelerate rollouts for enterprise tenants. Backwards compatibility: tests included to ensure older SDKs (OpenAI-compat) do not break. Risk and mitigations: The biggest risk is increased latency when a tool result arrives slightly after generation — mitigated by configurable timeouts and an async-fallback policy that allows the generator to continue with a best-effort placeholder if strict-idempotent is not set. Local testing: see integration test harness in tests/integration/tool_invoke_fanout_test.go. CI: all unit/integration checks passed after two reruns (flaky test fix in metrics). Merge outcome: squash-merged to main with commit 0a9f3d2 (executor: add parallelism cap

text: description: Motivation: observed end-to-end tail latency spikes when heavy KV cache churn interacts with continuous batching and the scheduler's single evaluation path. This addresses a class of regressions where the eager fast-path evaluator would return slightly out-of-spec logits under low-precision kernel fallbacks during GC-like KV churn. Design: split the scheduler's evaluation phase into two distinct evaluators: fastpath_evaluator (aggressive kernels, lower overhead) and safe_evaluator (conservative kernels, strict correctness). The scheduler now assigns requests to the fastpath by default using a runtime gating heuristic; on repeated soft failures (accuracy-check mismatches, KV lookup misses that cause cache rebuilds, or precision toggle events) requests are demoted to the safepath and a per-session KV heuristic updates to avoid oscillation. Added a KV cache heuristic module that tracks per-key miss-rate and recent rebuild windows and suppresses aggressive cache eviction when continuous batching is high. Implemented an opt-in environment flag REDWOOD_FASTPATH_SAFEGATES with three modes: off | permissive | strict. Default is permissive so we gain perf without surprising correctness regressions. Includes metrics, tracing, and a canary rollout plan. Compatibility: preserves API and config surface; no schema changes. Tests: unit tests for evaluator selection logic, integration test that replays a recorded workload with induced KV churn and asserts token-level similarity
title: split-scheduler: introduce fastpath evaluator and KV heuristic fallback

text: preserves API and config surface; no schema changes. Tests: unit tests for evaluator selection logic, integration test that replays a recorded workload with induced KV churn and asserts token-level similarity thresholds, e2e perf test results included below. commits: b1a2d3e: scheduler: split evaluation into fastpath_evaluator and safe_evaluator c4d5e6f: kv: add heuristic tracker for per-key miss-rate and rebuild windows d7e8f9a: runtime: hook evaluator gating into scheduling decision and metrics e0f1a2b: config: add REDWOOD_FASTPATH_SAFEGATES flag and documentation f3a4b5c: tests: add recorded workload replay with KV churn and similarity assertions review_comments: Marco Chen: Great separation. Please add a comment explaining why the fastpath does not check kv->validity synchronously; I want to avoid future regressions where callers assume strong consistency. Aisha Patel: Added inline docs and a unit test that ensures the fastpath observes KV validity before returning tokens. The fastpath only skips a heavyweight KV-lock in permissive mode. Lina Ortega: Can we get perf numbers for both permissive and strict modes and a regression window? I want to see the cost of demotion under heavy load. Aisha Patel: Attached canary run stats to the PR and summarized below. Demotion costs ~6-10% P50 token latency on a pathological trace but reduces mispredictions to zero. Daniel Kim: Security note: ensure the safe_evaluator always uses deterministic kernels for audited deployments. Marking
title: split-scheduler: introduce fastpath evaluator and KV heuristic fallback

text: ~6-10% P50 token latency on a pathological trace but reduces mispredictions to zero.
