Aisha Patel: Added runtime/flags check and documented the default to be enabled only for enterprise compliance profiles. Updated release notes accordingly. Lina Ortega: Approving after the perf dashboard link and CI is green. Marco Chen: Approved. Squash merge recommended to keep history focused. perf_summary: Canary workload: real customer trace with high prefix reuse and induced KV churn Baseline (main): P50 token latency 22ms, P95 78ms, tokens/sec 540 Fastpath (permissive): P50 18ms (-18%), P95 68ms (-13%), tokens/sec 630 (+16%) Strict mode (safe-heavy): P50 20ms (-9%), P95 70ms (-10%), tokens/sec 600 (+11%) Worst-case demotion trace: demotion increased P50 by 6-10% for affected sessions but eliminated 100% of small-output-drift regressions observed previously rollout_plan: Stage 1: Feature gate off in prod, enabled in canary cluster for 48h Stage 2: Permissive mode to 10% of traffic for 72h with similarity monitoring Stage 3: Ramp to 50% if no regressions, strict mode available via config for sensitive customers Rollback: instant toggle via REDWOOD_FASTPATH_SAFEGATES=off and rollback automation that reassigns in-flight batches to safe_evaluator release_notes: Introduces scheduler split (fastpath/safe) and KV cache heuristics.

text: description: Summary: Add full parity support for several /v1/chat.completions behaviors used by OpenAI-compatible clients: 1) map and persist incoming 'session' metadata and new session.* params into request context for routing/metrics; 2) normalize stop parameter precedence with a new stop_policy (explicit arrays, string fallback, stop_priority), preserving exact semantics expected by downstream tool callers; 3) keep logprobs block as a first-class envelope field in streaming chunks (no interleaving with text tokens) to allow incremental logprob aggregation; 4) implement a stream event coalescer that reduces tiny heartbeat-only frames and groups adjacent token->toolcall transitions to improve client stability and reduce network chatter; 5) tighten the function/tool-call handshake framing so the runtime only emits a single framed TOOL_CALL_START event per logical call and guarantees TOOL_CALL_END with idempotent retry semantics. Motivation: customers integrating with OpenAI-compatible SDKs ran into corner cases where session-level metadata was lost across retries, stop semantics differed between the Redwood v1 adapter and client expectations, streaming clients had difficulty consuming micro-frames which led to flakiness for low-latency workloads, and some tool-calling sequences produced duplicate partial events under high concurrency. This PR addresses those problems and adds tests and docs. Changes in this PR: - new mapping layer: server/adapter/chat_v1_session.py that maps
title: v1/chat: session param mapping, stream coalescing and envelope-normalized stop/logprobs

text: produced duplicate partial events under high concurrency. This PR addresses those problems and adds tests and docs. Changes in this PR: - new mapping layer: server/adapter/chat_v1_session.py that maps session and session.* params to internal RequestContext and tags telemetry; ensures deterministic hashing for routing and warm-cache reuse - stop normalizer: server/adapter/stop_policy.py with unit tests that implement precedence: explicit_stop_array > stop_string_list (legacy) > model_default; also supports stop_priority: longest_first|first_match - streaming envelope: runtime/stream/envelope.proto + runtime/stream/envelope_writer.cpp implementing "logprob_envelope" blocks that can be emitted as separate events; client SDKs updated to expect envelope events during streaming - coalescer: runtime/stream/coalescer.cpp + tests that batch small events (<1ms apart) into a single websocket/frame payload when safe and within latency SLO - toolcall framing: runtime/tool_call/handshake.h/.cpp and server/adapter/toolcall_adapter.py updated to ensure idempotent start/end markers and a retry-safe call id - SDK adapters: sdk/python/openai_compat.py and sdk/typescript/openai_compat.ts updated to surface session param and envelope parsing - docs: docs/openai_compat_v1.md updated with migration notes and examples - tests: integration tests added under tests/integration/test_chat_v1_session.py and several new e2e playback fixtures. Description/Checklist: - Context and design rationale included in
title: v1/chat: session param mapping, stream coalescing and envelope-normalized stop/logprobs

text: notes and examples - tests: integration tests added under tests/integration/test_chat_v1_session.py and several new e2e playback fixtures. Description/Checklist: - Context and design rationale included in the top-level docstring of chat_v1_session.py - Backwards compatible defaults: stop_policy defaults to legacy behavior unless customer enablement flag set - Add unit and integration tests covering: stop arrays, stop strings, logprob envelope aggregation, websocket coalesce thresholds, toolcall retry scenarios - Performance: coalescer is opt-in via runtime flag and disabled for Dedicated plans by default - Docs and SDKs updated - Linear references included below Testing summary: - Local unit test suite: all green - Integration matrix (linux/gcc, macos) run in CI; initial CI run failed on a flaky websocket test and was retried; stabilized after increasing default coalesce timeout in the test harness (see CI section). title: v1/chat: session param mapping, stream coalescing and envelope-normalized stop/logprobs

text: description: Motivation: Production workloads show wide variance in tenant access patterns: steady traffic tenants get large effective cache hit rates, while bursty tenants cause cache churn and tail latency spikes. This PR introduces a lightweight "cache ecology" controller that synthesizes per-tenant eviction and residency profiles from recent access histograms and a microburst fallback that temporarily reduces batch size and switches to microbatch-optimized execution paths when burst detection and KV-cache churn would otherwise violate latency SLOs. What this changes: Adds an inference-side service and control loop that: 1) ingests tenant-level access metrics (prefix length, inter-arrival, hit/miss ratios) and produces a ranked set of eviction policies (LRU-variant, segmented-TTL, weighted-residency) and quantization rollback hints; 2) exposes a profile API used by the scheduler to apply tenant-scoped cache and batching overrides; 3) implements a fast-path microburst detector that toggles a microbatch executor and reduces prefetch aggressiveness for the affected tenant for a short cooldown window. The goal is to reduce cost from repeated cache thrashing while preserving 99.9th percentile latency by gracefully degrading to microbatches rather than dropping requests or causing large KV-cache recomputations. Design notes: The controller runs in the serving plane with bounded CPU and memory budgets and uses exponentially-weighted histograms with a sliding window to avoid storing
title: tenant-aware cache-ecology + microburst fallback profiles

text: large KV-cache recomputations. Design notes: The controller runs in the serving plane with bounded CPU and memory budgets and uses exponentially-weighted histograms with a sliding window to avoid storing per-request traces. Profiles are versioned and lightweight (<=2KB). We intentionally keep the policy space small (4 eviction families x 3 residency levels) to limit combinatorial explosion and make rollout/safety analysis tractable. The microburst guard implements conservative heuristics first and records signals for later ML-based tuning. Backward compatibility & safety: Default behavior is a no-op unless a tenant has opted into adaptive profiles. All profile changes are annotated with a safety score and a TTL; the scheduler rejects changes below a configurable safety threshold. We included a forced fallback toggle in the Console and a feature flag for private deployments. No model format changes. Testing & benchmarks: Unit tests cover eviction generator math, profile serialization, and scheduler integration. Integration tests simulate interleaved steady and burst tenants using the perf-lab harness. On internal staging workloads, we observed: - 12% average token cost reduction across mixed workloads - 18% reduction in 99.9th percentile latency on bursty tenants (microburst path engaged) - negligible impact on steady tenants (sub-1% latency delta). Rollout plan: Start with a small canary (5 tenants) + telemetry visibility (prefetch/miss-rate/latency per-tenant). If stable for
title: tenant-aware cache-ecology + microburst fallback profiles

text: engaged) - negligible impact on steady tenants (sub-1% latency delta). Rollout plan: Start with a small canary (5 tenants) + telemetry visibility (prefetch/miss-rate/latency per-tenant). If stable for 48h, extend to 20% of production traffic using a canary policy and auto-roll back on elevated P99 or miss rate regressions. Migration steps: Console toggle and API allow opt-in; no mandatory migration. For Private installs, operators can enable the controller via the helm chart values (see docs change). Related: ENG-2191 (workload telemetry), ENG-2210 (microburst execution paths). review_comments: Lena Park: Can you explain how profile churn cost is accounted for? Suggest adding a metric for profile churn and an opt-out TTL. (requested change) Author (Ravi): Added profile_churn_total and churn_ttl configuration, added to controller.go and dashboard. Please re-review. Diego Alvarez: Microburst detector thresholds look conservative; consider moving to a percentile-of-percentiles approach for noisy tenants. (comment) Author (Ravi): Agreed. Adjusted detector to use 99th-percentile over the sliding window and added a noisy-tenant suppression flag. Kept default conservative for safety. Priya Nair: Integration tests flaky on first run due to harness timing; reran CI and added small sleeps in test harness. Approving after CI green. (approved) CI Bot: initial CI run failed lint on api proto formatting; fixed in second commit. All checks passed on re-run.
title: tenant-aware cache-ecology + microburst fallback profiles

text: description: Introduce SLA-aware fleet partitioning and a proportional autoscaler that allocates reserved capacity to tenant partitions based on SLO priorities and recent utilization. This change creates a new Partition Allocator that groups capacity into small SLO domains (e.g., low-latency, standard, best-effort), extends reservation accounting with per-partition watermarks and decay logic, and plugs a proportional autoscaler which uses soft thresholds and credit buckets to drive scale-out/scale-in decisions. Motivation: customers running Dedicated pools require predictable throughput guarantees for different SLAs while still allowing safe bursting and cluster-level utilization efficiency. Key design points: 1) Partitioning is configurable via operator CRD; partitions map to tenant tags and can be hot-swapped. 2) Reservation watermarks and decay protect against noisy spikes by smoothing effective reserved capacity over a sliding window with exponential decay coefficient. 3) Proportional autoscaler computes target instances per partition as baseline_reserved + alpha * utilization_delta, bounded by min/max allocation and respecting cluster headroom. 4) Burst pacing: tenants above their partition share are served from temporary burst credits which decrement and are replenished on cooled down utilization patterns. 5) Backpressure and graceful fallback: when cluster headroom exhausted we apply model-fallback routing and elastic throttling instead of hard denial. 6) Telemetry: new
title: SLA-aware fleet partitioning and proportional autoscaler

text: cooled down utilization patterns. 5) Backpressure and graceful fallback: when cluster headroom exhausted we apply model-fallback routing and elastic throttling instead of hard denial. 6) Telemetry: new metrics partition/p95_latency, partition/burst_credit, partition/allocated_instances, and partition/reservation_watermark; added histogram bucketting for per-partition utilization for percentile reporting. Compatibility & migration: partitions default to a single legacy partition to maintain existing behavior; migration opt-in via feature flag cluster.enablePartitioning; rolling upgrade tolerated by version gates. Safety: scale decisions are rate-limited with 60s cooldown per partition; preemption is disabled by default and gated behind operator policy. Testing & rollout: unit tests, integration harness simulating multi-tenant noisy neighbors, and a canary rollout plan with gradual partition splits at 10/30/60/100% traffic. CI: unit + integration + perf; manual canary on staging. Release-note: adds SLA-aware partitioning and proportional autoscaler to Dedicated fleets; operators must opt-in to enable partitioning. Referenced Linear: ENG-2141, SRE-732. review_comments: Daniel Kim: Overall nice design. A couple of questions: how do we reason about large tenants that span partitions? Is cross-partition burst transfer allowed? Asha Patel: Answered - tenants are assigned to a single partition via tag; cross-partition transfer is not automatic to avoid SLA violations; operator can re-tag
title: SLA-aware fleet partitioning and proportional autoscaler

text: cross-partition burst transfer allowed? Asha Patel: Answered - tenants are assigned to a single partition via tag; cross-partition transfer is not automatic to avoid SLA violations; operator can re-tag tenant to migrate it. Updated docs with migration section. Sofia Martinez: Please ensure we've got observability hooks for per-partition headroom so SRE can set alerts; also add an event when a partition hits burst credit exhaustion. Asha Patel: Added partition/burst_credit and partition/allocated_instances metrics and emitted k8s events on exhaustion. Also added alert examples to docs. Liam O'Connor: Unit tests look good. Integration harness reproduced regression scenario; suggested lowering alpha in the proportional formula to reduce oscillation. Asha Patel: Tuned alpha from 0.4 to 0.25 and added a hysteresis window to reduce flip-flopping. Added stress tests in integration suite. SRE: Approved after staging canary OK. Release manager: Verified rollout playbook and upgrade compatibility. Approved to merge. release_notes: Adds optional SLA-aware fleet partitioning and a proportional autoscaler for Dedicated pools. Operators must enable cluster.enablePartitioning to opt in. New telemetry: partition/p95_latency, partition/burst_credit, partition/allocated_instances, partition/reservation_watermark. See docs/ops/partitioning-and-slos.md for migration and rollout guidance.
title: SLA-aware fleet partitioning and proportional autoscaler

text: description: Motivation: Customers expect OpenAI-style function/tool calling to 'just work' when routed through Redwood's serving runtime. However, in practice we see a set of recurring issues: heterogeneous tool descriptors from third-party models, ambiguous tool_choice semantics when multiple tools match, and stream fragmentation that splits the tool invocation payload across SSE boundaries. This PR introduces a compatibility-focused proto mapper and decision telemetry that together make tool invocation more deterministic and observable.Design summary: 1) proto_mapper: a lightweight rule engine that canonicalizes incoming OpenAI-compatible invocation envelopes into a single Redwood internal representation. It normalizes fields (name/description/schema/arguments), expands legacy aliases into canonical redwood_tool_id, and attaches a compat_hint when the mapping required heuristics. Mappings are deterministic and extensible via a small YAML ruleset shipped with the package. 2) choice_scorer: when multiple tools match a requested function name/schema, the scorer computes an ordered candidate list using schema affinity (how well the provided arguments conform to each tool schema), stream-priming context (tokens already generated that imply certain tool semantics), and operational priors (recent latency/cost observed for the customer's tenant). The winner receives an explicit confidence score and a short rationale encoded in metadata. 3) stream_coordinator: improves streaming stability
title: Invoke compatibility proto mapper and decision telemetry

text: (recent latency/cost observed for the customer's tenant). The winner receives an explicit confidence score and a short rationale encoded in metadata. 3) stream_coordinator: improves streaming stability by aligning fragments to logical tool frames: partial JSON fragments are buffered and resynchronized rather than forwarded to downstream tool adapters; we emit a fragment_resync event for observability. 4) telemetry: emit an invoke.decision event for every tool invocation that contains candidate list, scores, chosen_index, confidence, request_id, and quick pointers to the proto_mapper rule that fired. This event is low-cardinality and intended for debugging regressions and production investigations.Changes and files: See changed_files for a compact list. Key behavioral changes are limited and guarded behind a feature flag 'openai_compat_proto_mapper' that defaults to on for Hosted only and off for Dedicated/Private until customers opt in. Tests: added unit tests covering nested JSON arguments, missing schema resolution, alias expansion, and integration tests that simulate streaming SSE fragmentation across multiple boundaries to verify deterministic reassembly and correct candidate scoring. Performance: choice_scorer is designed to be cheap (fast schema affinity checks and cached token-based signals). Benchmarks on a 4k sequence workload show median decision latency ~= 0.9ms additional per-invoke at p50 and 2.4ms at p95 under load (detailed numbers in benchmark artifact attached
title: Invoke compatibility proto mapper and decision telemetry

text: token-based signals). Benchmarks on a 4k sequence workload show median decision latency ~= 0.9ms additional per-invoke at p50 and 2.4ms at p95 under load (detailed numbers in benchmark artifact attached to CI). Rollout and risk mitigation: rollout is staged. Hosted -> 10%/50%/100% over two weeks with monitoring dashboards. Dedicated/Private customers will see release notes and can enable via console or config. We included a migration script to generate alias mappings for large customers that rely on legacy tool names.Open questions and future work: - Consider surfacing the decision rationale in debug traces for customers with private deployments. - Expand rule engine to accept safe machine-suggested mappings (requires guardrails). - Add optional delta compression for repeated identical invocation envelopes to reduce egress cost.Review conversation snapshot: See review_thread field for representative comments. Merge strategy: squash; small logical commits squashed to a single logical change and merged to main.Approvals: Approved by core runtime reviewers and SRE, CI passed and manual integration harness validated in staging. release_notes: Adds an OpenAI-compatibility proto mapper that normalizes function/tool invocation envelopes to Redwood internal shapes. Introduces an explicit decision telemetry event (invoke.decision) that records tool selection candidates, normalized scores, and final chosen tool to aid post-deployment debugging. Default rollout: gradual on Hosted, opt-in
title: Invoke compatibility proto mapper and decision telemetry

text: decision telemetry event (invoke.decision) that records tool selection candidates, normalized scores, and final chosen tool to aid post-deployment debugging. Default rollout: gradual on Hosted, opt-in for Dedicated/Private until config flag 'openai_compat_proto_mapper' enabled. review_thread: Ethan Park: Nice separation of concerns. text: description: Motivation: Customers using the OpenAI Responses family expect stable streaming semantics, reliable tool/function call signal delivery, and preservation of determinism hints (seed/RNG) and user metadata when we bridge from our internal Responses v9-compatible runtime to the OpenAI API shape. This PR adds a resilience layer that: 1) performs backpressure-aware chunking and reassembly for streaming frames to avoid partial json/toolcall corruption on slow clients, 2) proxies a lightweight 'tool-signal' envelope so function/tool-calling boundaries are deterministic across retries and reconnections, and 3) forwards seed/determinism flags and a subset of user metadata transparently through the compatibility layer. What this changes (summary): consistent chunk framing and reassembly on the server and client-facing bridge; explicit tool-signal envelope that annotates tool-call start/end + unique tool invocation id; passthrough of 'rng_seed' and 'deterministic' attributes from our Responses request model into the OpenAI-compatible response attributes; metadata routing (user-provided tags/trace id) added to response attributes and to audit logs so downstream services (console, evals) see the same identifiers. Compatibility notes: We keep the OpenAI-compatible surface stable for existing callers. Default behavior is identical unless callers set determinism/seed or rely on streaming reconnects; in those cases the bridge guarantees idempotent framing (replay-safe chunk markers)
title: OpenAI-compat: resilient chunking + tool-signal proxy for Responses v9 (seed passthrough & metadata routing)

text: existing callers. Default behavior is identical unless callers set determinism/seed or rely on streaming reconnects; in those cases the bridge guarantees idempotent framing (replay-safe chunk markers) and consistent toolcall reassembly. There are small opt-ins: the 'tool-signal' envelope is only applied when callers include a 'enable_tool_signal' flag (default true for our hosted API). For private deployments customers can opt-out via config. Checklist: - [x] Server-side chunking + reassembly implementation - [x] Tool-signal envelope design + tests - [x] Seed/determinism passthrough plumbing - [x] Unit tests for corrupted-chunk scenarios - [x] Integration tests against the openai-compat test harness - [x] E2E regression test in perf-canary - [x] Docs + migration note (redwood-docs) Related: linear/ENG-5272 (streaming reliability for responses bridge), ENG-5319 (toolcall idempotency). See design doc: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-5272/openai-bridge-resilient-framing (internal). review_comments: Liam O'Connor: Overall approach looks good. Two requests: (1) add a short comment in stream_framer explaining the choice of 1KB reassembly window vs dynamic, (2) ensure the tool invocation id is cryptographically random to avoid collisions in multi-tenant setups. Maya Chen (author): Added comment and switched the id generator to use crypto/rand with fallback to xoshiro for perf-sensitive paths. See stream_framer.go: lines ~78-110. Sana Patel: The integration test is flaky on Windows
title: OpenAI-compat: resilient chunking + tool-signal proxy for Responses v9 (seed passthrough & metadata routing)

Sana Patel: The integration test is flaky on Windows CI (timeout on reconnect). Can we increase the reconnect window or make the test use the perf-canary harness? Maya Chen: Moved the test to perf-canary harness and added retry/backoff stabilization. CI run passed on the rerun: https://ci.redwood.internal/runs/7981 Diego Ruiz: Small nit: audit_mapper should not copy full user metadata into logs. Limit to allowlist [trace_id,user_tag]. Maya Chen: Adjusted audit_mapper to redact non-allowlisted fields and added unit tests to verify. release_notes: Bridge for Responses v9: improved streaming resilience with backpressure-aware framing, deterministic toolcall envelope and rng_seed passthrough. Opt-out config available for private deployments. See docs for migration steps. testing_notes: Added unit tests for chunk corruption/reassembly, tool-signal replay safety, and seed propagation. Perf-canary scenario exercised reconnects at 50ms/200ms jitter. Manual smoke test performed against hosted API (create streaming chat + forced reconnect) and verified tool call reassembly and seed parity. security_considerations: Tool invocation ids are generated with crypto/rand to avoid predictability. We redact non-allowlisted user metadata from audit logs. No new permissions surface exposed by this change.
title: OpenAI-compat: resilient chunking + tool-signal proxy for Responses v9 (seed passthrough & metadata routing)

text: description: Summary: Introduces a hybrid scheduling path that allows requests to start on the fast, lower-precision kernel chain and transparently fall back to a conservative (safe-mode) kernel when numerical consistency checks fail or when upstream correctness policies are triggered. This change targets tail-latency and cost while preserving correctness guarantees for production workloads. Motivation and context: We've seen intermittent regression cases where aggressive fast-path kernels (quantized/mixed-precision attention) provide large median improvements but occasionally diverge on corner conditions for long sequences or very large batch re-ordering. Existing fastpath gating is binary and heavily conservative, causing either too many misses (performance lost) or too few (correctness risk). This PR implements a gradual fallback strategy, per-request precision toggles, and a non-blocking correctness validator that reduces unnecessary escalations. What changed (high level): - New hybrid_scheduler module that tracks per-request health and assigns a decay score; requests with low confidence are staged onto safe-mode without global blocking. - Introduced "precision_toggles": a lightweight per-layer hint metadata carried on requests so we can switch specific kernels from int8->fp16 or fp16->fp32 at layer granularity. - Added an asynchronous correctness validator that performs lightweight checksum/consistency probes on a sampled subset of tokens; violations trigger targeted fallbacks
title: hybrid-scheduler-gradual-fallback-consistency-kernel-toggles

text: fp16->fp32 at layer granularity. - Added an asynchronous correctness validator that performs lightweight checksum/consistency probes on a sampled subset of tokens; violations trigger targeted fallbacks rather than global disable. - KV cache eviction integration: cache_manager now exposes a fast eviction hint for requests on fallback to reduce memory pressure and avoid cache thrash during degen sequences. - Continuous batcher: added an adaptive window that short-circuits batching for requests flagged with high fallback probability to maintain tail latency. - New unit and perf tests plus a small demo notebook exercising progressive fallback and toggles. Performance notes: On our TPU and A100 perf lab runs with llama-variant workloads (seq=1024, mix of short/long prompts): - Median latency (P50) improved by ~9% across the board due to opportunistic fast-path use. - P95 improvements vary: up to 12% for conversation loads with moderate concurrency. - P99 improved by 6-8% in scenarios where adaptive batching prevented harmful queueing. - Cost: token compute cost reduced by ~7% in synthetic traces with 70% of requests eligible for fast-path. Correctness/regression mitigation: - The async validator samples ~1% of requests by default; when a mismatch is seen it records a fingerprint and triggers targeted re-execution on fp32 for that request only. - Added metrics and tracing (fallback.reason, fallback.scope, fallback.count) to Redwood Console to make any degradation visible immediately.
title: hybrid-scheduler-gradual-fallback-consistency-kernel-toggles

text: triggers targeted re-execution on fp32 for that request only. - Added metrics and tracing (fallback.reason, fallback.scope, fallback.count) to Redwood Console to make any degradation visible immediately. Backwards compatibility and rollout: - Feature gated behind config runtime.hybrid_fallback.enabled (off by default). - Safe default: the scheduler starts in observation mode where it logs but does not alter routes; opt-in rollout via Dedicated flags and console toggles. review_comments: Aisha: Requested clearer invariants around when per-layer toggles are allowed; asked for explicit bounds on sampling rate and rationale. Miguel: Found a race in cache_manager eviction hints when requests downgrade mid-flight; asked for a small mutex or epoch check. Samir: Wanted a perf-run showing memory overhead for the async validator and suggested lowering default sample rate to 0.5% in prod. Author replies: Added committed fixes — replaced a naive atomic with epoch-based guard for cache hints, tightened toggle scope to attention+ffn only, and added an opt-out tag for latency-sensitive routes. Follow-ups: Add a dashboard panel for fallback fingerprints and a small incident runbook. Final: approved by all reviewers after 2 rounds of review. release_notes: Introduced Hybrid Scheduler with gradual fallback and per-layer precision toggles. Default disabled; opt-in via runtime.hybrid_fallback.enabled. Improves median latency and cost by opportunistically using lower-precision kernels while providing
title: hybrid-scheduler-gradual-fallback-consistency-kernel-toggles

text: fallback and per-layer precision toggles. Default disabled; opt-in via runtime.hybrid_fallback.enabled. Improves median latency and cost by opportunistically using lower-precision kernels while providing targeted fallbacks to maintain correctness. See docs/notes/hybrid_fallback.md for rollout steps and runbook.
title: hybrid-scheduler-gradual-fallback-consistency-kernel-toggles

text: description: Context and motivation: Several customers and internal benchmarks showed inconsistent latency and throughput for long-context models when rotary and rope positional encodings were mixed across model families. The runtime was selecting a generic FlashAttention variant that did not account for rope compensation or token-importance for KV cache eviction, which led to suboptimal batching and premature KV spills under load. This PR introduces the FlashAdaptive adapter: a small compatibility layer + kernel selection glue that enables a fused rotary/rope compensation path, integrates a priority-aware KV eviction policy, and exposes scheduling hints to continuous-batcher so the runtime can pick the optimal kernel variant based on sequence distribution and quantization profile. What this PR does (single-string summary): Adds flash_adaptive kernel adapter and fused rope/rotary compensator, implements KV Priority Evict manager (recency + token-weight heuristic), hooks into continuous-batcher for policy-driven kernel routing, includes micro-benchmarks and integration tests, and ships a docs page with guidance and rollout feature-flag. Commits (high-level): - 7a1d2f: introduce flash_adaptive adapter interface and selection table; - 9c3b8e: implement fused_rope_rotary_compensator (CUDA + C++ wrapper) with numerics safeguards; - b2f9d1: kv/cache_priority_manager: implement eviction scoring and async prefetch hooks; - d4e7c3: continuous_batcher: expose scheduling hints and add policy
title: introduce-flashadaptive-attn-adapter-with-rope-compensation-and-kv-priority-evict

Numbers measured with feature-flag on vs off, averaged across 5 runs. review_comments: Diego Ramos: Can you add a short note in the docs about numeric stability for mixed

text:
body: Context and motivation:
This PR addresses a recurring class of incidents where aggressive fast-path kernel selection and lower-precision execution (fp16 / int8) produced rare but hard-to-detect correctness deviations for edge prompts. At the same time, customers expect throughput and lower cost. We need a runtime mechanism that lets us enable high-performance paths without risking silent correctness regressions in production.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds a 'safelock' gating layer in the kernel selection path. The safelock prevents switching to a fast path unless a set of runtime validators pass (numerical consistency, kv-cache integrity checks, and recent success window).
- Introduces a precision toggle API (per-model, per-deployment) with three modes: strict (fp32 only), balanced (fp16 with strict validators), aggressive (fp16/int8 allowed once warmed).
- Adds a lightweight runtime validator harness: stochastic correctness probes run as background requests against canary inputs to compute divergence metrics (token-level mismatch rate, logit-delta distribution). Validators are cheap and attach metadata to requests that triggered path selection failures.
- Changes KV cache eviction policy to be conservative when cache-warmness is below threshold; combined with safelock it avoids invalidating cached KV frames that fast kernels rely on.
- Integrates safelock signals with the continuous-batching scheduler: until a deployment's validators reach a success threshold,
title: Introduce "safelock" fast-path gating + precision toggle and KV-eviction safety for runtime

text:
safelock it avoids invalidating cached KV frames that fast kernels rely on.
- Integrates safelock signals with the continuous-batching scheduler: until a deployment's validators reach a success threshold, scheduler prefers conservative batching and disables cross-model micro-batching that can expose interleaving bugs.
- Adds observability hooks: metrics (safelock.blocked_count, validator.mismatch_rate, precision.switch_count, kv.eviction_suppressed) and a dashboard stub.
- Adds a feature flag runtime.enable_safelock (default true for new deploys) and model config fields precision_mode and validator_policy.

Design notes and rationale:
- The safelock is intentionally conservative: it is designed to minimize customer-visible regressions at the cost of initial throughput until a model warms. We prefer false negatives (staying on slow path) over false positives (silent incorrect outputs).
- Validators are probabilistic and run only on a small fraction of traffic (configurable). We do not rely on end-user prompts alone; the system feeds a small canary corpus that includes known edge cases and adversarial tokens used in previous incidents.
- KV-cache eviction suppression is implemented as an additional guard in the eviction worker: if cache_warmness < 0.7 and safelock.enabled, eviction respects a soft TTL and only evicts when memory pressure exceeds a higher threshold. This prevents state churn that invalidates prefix caching assumptions used by some kernels.

Commits (high-level):
-
title: Introduce "safelock" fast-path gating + precision toggle and KV-eviction safety for runtime

text:
respects a soft TTL and only evicts when memory pressure exceeds a higher threshold. This prevents state churn that invalidates prefix caching assumptions used by some kernels.

Commits (high-level):
- feat(runtime): add safelock kernel gating and validator harness (A. Kumar)
- feat(config): model precision_mode and validator_policy proto + defaulting (A. Kumar)
- fix(kv): conservative eviction path and warmness estimator (R. Patel)
- perf(scheduler): integrate safelock signal into batching decisioning (L. O'Connor)
- test: end-to-end validator integration tests and canary job (E. Tan)
- docs: runtime safelock/precision-mode doc, migration notes (doc team)

Files touched (high-level):
- runtime/kernel_selector.cpp/h (safelock implementation, gating checks)
- runtime/precision_policy.proto (new model-level config)
- runtime/validator/ (background probe harness, small canary corpus)
- serving/scheduler/continuous_batcher.cpp (respect safelock signals)
- serving/kvcache/eviction_worker.cpp (suppressed eviction path)
- metrics/registry (new metrics added)
- tests/e2e/validators_integration_test.cc (new suite)
- docs/runtime/safelock.md (operator-facing guide and flags)

} else {

title: Introduce "safelock" fast-path gating + precision toggle and KV-eviction safety for runtime
Introduce "safelock" fast-path gating + precision toggle and KV-eviction safety for runtime

text:
integration, perf-smoke).

Review discussion (highlights):
- Marco: "I like the safelock abstraction; can we ensure the validator corpus is editable by SREs without code changes?"
- Aisha: "Yes — validators read a canary file from /etc/redwood/validators/corpus.json and we added operator docs. SRE can update via configmap for k8s deploys."
- Eve: "Concerned about metric cardinality when we annotate every request with validator metadata."
- Aisha: "We emit aggregated counters and histograms; only a small sampling logs request-level debug traces behind a flag."
- Liam: "Scheduler changes look sane. Need additional canary rollout plan for enterprise customers with strict SLAs."
- Aisha: "Added rollout doc and an opt-out label on deployment config; default for existing deployments is unchanged (safe-mode learned)."

Edge cases / known tradeoffs:
- There is an initial throughput hit until validators warm. We mitigate by sampling only a small portion of traffic for validators and allowing manual warm-up (canary traffic injection).
- Aggressive precision_mode is opt-in; customers should enable only after verifying on internal test prompts. We include a CLI tool for running the validator corpus locally against a deployment.
- The KV eviction suppression increases memory use under some failure modes; we documented emergency knobs and introduced an automatic disable threshold if memory pressure remains high for 10s.

Migration / operator guidance:
- New deploys:
title: Introduce "safelock" fast-path gating + precision toggle and KV-eviction safety for runtime

text:
memory use under some failure modes; we documented emergency knobs and introduced an automatic disable threshold if memory pressure remains high for 10s.

Migration / operator guidance:
- New deploys: safelock enabled by default. Operators can set runtime.enable_safelock=false to revert (not recommended).
- To opt into aggressive precision: update model config: precision_mode=aggressive and monitor validator.mismatch_rate.
- Dashboards: added a starter Grafana dashboard (docs link) and alerts for validator.mismatch_rate > 0.1% and safelock.blocked_count being unexpectedly high.

Backward compatibility:
- All config fields default to conservative behavior; no breaking changes. Models without precision_mode set continue to use fp32 until they pass validators.

Post-merge actions / follow-ups (linear):
- ENG-4983: add automated warmup job to inject synthetic canary traffic during deploy windows (follow-up).
- ENG-5122: add per-customer precision analytics and billing impact report.

Merge outcome:
- Final status: merged via squash. Branch merged to main after approvals from Marco, Eve, and Liam. CI green at merge.

If you want to test locally: run tools/runtime_cli --run-validators --config test/configs/precision_local.yaml against a dev deployment; run scripts/kv_warmup.sh to pre-fill the kvcache with the canary prefix set.

Thanks to Rohan Patel for eviction work, and the QA team for extended regression runs.

Patch size: moderate; favors safety and observability

text: description: Motivation: multi-tool patterns (parallel calls, fan-in injection) increased nondeterminism in how tool outputs were injected into the model prompt and streaming responses. This caused intermittent flakiness in structured outputs and made retries unsafe for side-effectful tools. This PR introduces a staged execution model and idempotent replay primitives to make multi-tool executions deterministic, safe to retry, and compatible with existing OpenAI-style function calling semantics. What this changes (summary): - Introduce a runtime 'staged' pipeline for tool calls: tools are scheduled into deterministic stages (prepare -> call -> collect -> inject) to guarantee a stable injection order across parallel executions. - Add a gating/semaphore layer that limits in-flight tool calls per-request and enforces backpressure to protect downstream serving resources. - Implement an idempotent replay token and deterministic result hash so retries can be replayed without duplicate side effects (requires tool adapter support). - Update the OpenAI compatibility layer so function-call responses preserve staged injection metadata; no caller-facing API breaking changes — current behavior preserved unless staged mode is enabled. - Add SDK shims (python/ts) that expose an opt-in 'staged' flag when calling tools or using run_tool_chain; adapters updated to support idempotent hooks. - Improved telemetry: new metrics for stage latencies, semaphore contention, and replay counts. Design notes:
title: Staged tool-pipeline execution, deterministic injection order, and idempotent replay for parallel calls

text: when calling tools or using run_tool_chain; adapters updated to support idempotent hooks. - Improved telemetry: new metrics for stage latencies, semaphore contention, and replay counts. Design notes: - Determinism is achieved by assigning stable sort keys to tool requests (source order + tie-breaker) before parallel dispatch. We batch by stage so KV/cache insertion and prompt injection happen in a single deterministic pass. - Replay token is generated from request id + stage metadata. Tool adapters must implement a replay check to short-circuit side effects; a fallback safe-execute path is provided for older adapters. - Backpressure gating uses a leaky-bucket semaphore per-tenant that integrates with existing autoscaling hooks to avoid queuing storms during traffic spikes. Testing and validation: - Added unit tests for stage ordering, simulated parallel tool calls, and replay idempotency. - End-to-end tests included in perf-canary harness to validate latency tail at 95/99 pctl under staged mode. - Manual regression checks against OpenAI compatibility matrix to ensure function-calling payloads remain unchanged when staged mode is off. Rollout plan: - Feature gated behind ENABLE_STAGED_TOOL_PIPELINE (runtime config). - Default: off. Rollout plan: 1) internal dogfood 0->10% for 48h, 2) 10->50% for 72h if metrics stable, 3) 100% after 7 days with opt-in telemetry retained for 30d. Backward compatibility: staged mode is opt-in; existing customers see no change unless they enable the
title: Staged tool-pipeline execution, deterministic injection order, and idempotent replay for parallel calls

text: 2) 10->50% for 72h if metrics stable, 3) 100% after 7 days with opt-in telemetry retained for 30d. Backward compatibility: staged mode is opt-in; existing customers see no change unless they enable the flag. SDKs provide transparent shims so older clients will continue to work. Related: ENG-1942, SUP-712. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for scheduler and replay primitives - [x] E2E tests in perf-canary - [x] SDK adapters updated (python/ts) review_comments: Omar Ruiz: Overall this looks good. Also added an integration test that simulates a legacy adapter to verify no double side-effects. Lucia Fernandez: CI flaky on one e2e — the perf-canary job timed out once. Reran CI and verified metrics. Approving once CI green. Lucia Fernandez: Approve — LGTM (post-approval tweak: added more assert messaging in unit tests) release_notes: Adds a staged execution

text: description: Context: we need a single, testable control plane for running short-duration experiments (canaries, A/B splits, and shadow runs) that enforces safety gates based on observed metrics and can gracefully and deterministically shut down an experiment if thresholds are breached. Motivation: prior rollouts relied on ad-hoc workflows and separate operators; incidents in Q4 showed slow rollbacks when latency regressions or hallucination rate spikes occurred. This PR introduces an Adaptive Experimentation Controller (AEC) that centralizes rollout decisions, exposes a policy-language for metric gates, and implements a soft-shutdown sequence that preserves in-flight requests and drains prefixes to avoid KV cache invalidations. Design summary: the AEC is implemented as a controller in pkg/orchestrator with three primary components: (1) policy-engine: evaluates metric windows and decides PASS/WARN/FAIL per experiment; (2) controller-loop: phases traffic (0->mirror->phased-canary->full) using token-aware sampling and deterministic hashing; (3) safe-shutdown: a soft-stop path that 1) immediately disables new canary allocations, 2) flips traffic to fallback model variant for new requests, and 3) drains existing sessions with an adjustable grace window. Key invariants: decisions are idempotent, metric windows are sampled from the centralized metrics store (observability-pack), and fallback mapping is explicit in rollout spec to avoid implicit fallthroughs. Config changes: new CRD-like
title: Adaptive experimentation controller: metrics gates, soft-shutdown and safe fallback policy

text: idempotent, metric windows are sampled from the centralized metrics store (observability-pack), and fallback mapping is explicit in rollout spec to avoid implicit fallthroughs. Config changes: new CRD-like rollout spec additions in api/v1/experiment.go: "metricsGates" (list), "graceWindowSeconds", and "fallbackPolicy" (enum: degrade|fallback-model|region-redirect). Default behavior keeps legacy rollouts unchanged unless the new fields are supplied. The CLI (rdwctl rollout apply) accepts --enable-aec flag for early rollout in staging clusters. Testing and validation: unit tests for policy evaluation and deterministic hashing (pkg/orchestrator/policy_engine_test.go) added. Integration test harness uses benchmark-lab to simulate 100k qps with mixed sequence lengths and synthetic hallucination signals to trigger gate states. A small e2e in redwood-quickstarts demonstrates staged activation with dashboards in redwood-console (observability/dashboard.json example). Performance: local bench shows <2% p95 latency overhead at 1k qps when metric evaluation is on-path; objective is to push evaluation off-path for large clusters in follow-up PR. Migration and rollout plan: opt-in feature gate (aec.alpha.redwood.io) enabled on staging. Plan: deploy controller to staging, run 2 weeks of canaries for internal traffic, then enable for early customers with explicit opt-in. If a gate FAILs, the controller performs soft-shutdown and posts a structured incident event to incident-bot with rollback
title: Adaptive experimentation controller: metrics gates, soft-shutdown and safe fallback policy

text: canaries for internal traffic, then enable for early customers with explicit opt-in. If a gate FAILs, the controller performs soft-shutdown and posts a structured incident event to incident-bot with rollback suggested. Owners must validate fallback mapping before enabling for production. Docs and observability: added docs/experiments/adaptive-controller.md and a monitoring playbook describing gate metrics (latency_p95_delta, token_error_rate, hallucination_rate_est). Console graphs for experiment state transitions and a per-experiment timeline have been added. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in Linear (ENG-7812) - [x] Unit & integration tests - [x] e2e quickstart example - [x] Docs & monitoring dashboards - [x] Feature gate behind "aec.alpha.redwood.io" Risks and mitigations: the policy engine is conservative by default (WARN -> requires manual confirm) and FAIL triggers soft-shutdown not an immediate hard rollback. The first rollout uses a short graceWindowSeconds=30 to limit long-lived sessions. Next steps: move metric evaluation to a sidecar aggregator and add automatic rollback reasons to audit logs. release_notes: Adds the Adaptive Experimentation Controller: metric-gated canaries, automatic soft-shutdown, and deterministic fallback policy. Backwards compatible with existing rollout configs. Deploy with safety gates enabled by default for new experiments. review_conversation: Ava Martinez (reviewer): Overall nice clean split. Question: how do we guarantee cache-affinity
title: Adaptive experimentation controller: metrics gates, soft-shutdown and safe fallback policy

text: existing rollout configs. Deploy with safety gates enabled by default for new experiments. review_conversation: Ava Martinez (reviewer): Overall nice clean split. Question: how do we guarantee cache-affinity when we do a fallback-model redirect? Suggest we add a KV prefix map to the fallback step. Ravi Kapoor (author): Good point — added a fallbackPrefix map and updated controller.go to preserve prefix affinity during soft-shutdown. See commit b2d8e3f. Jordan Li (reviewer): CI flaked on the integration test (benchmark scenario) — looks like a race while the metrics source is being torn down. Can you add a retry/backoff? text: body: Summary: This PR introduces a KV-cache-aware zero-copy dequantor to bridge INT8/FP8 model weights with the FP16 execution path without an explicit full-buffer dequant allocation. It also adds a per-layer scale table and runtime selection policy to reduce quantization overhead and variance for mixed INT8/FP8 model variants. Motivation: we saw two related issues in production benches: (1) dequant buffer allocations and copies dominated short inference tails for small requests under continuous batching, and (2) a single global scale for requant/dequant caused output quality variability across certain layers (particularly large-attention heads). The goal is to reduce latency/cost and reduce quality regressions when switching to INT8/FP8 hybrid paths. Design: - Zero-copy KV staging: when loading KV blocks for attention, the runtime now exposes an optional staging pointer that the dequant microkernel can write into directly while applying dequant scaling. For compatible devices/drivers this avoids a separate host/device memcpy. - Per-layer scale table: a compact table stored alongside model metadata with per-layer requant/dequant multipliers and a small 8-bit exponent adjustment. The runtime chooses the appropriate multiplier at serving time based on model variant and input distribution (simple moving avg statistics). - Fused microkernel: implemented an aligned-fallback microkernel that performs int8 -> fp16 dequant + layout transpose when needed. It uses packed vector instructions

text: description: Motivation: reduce token-cost and tail-latency for quantized INT8/FP8 inference by fusing a lane-aware hybrid dequantization pass that opportunistically keeps some heads in low-precision while dequantizing others, combined with KV-cache window hinting to avoid unnecessary KV prefetch for short context reuse. This PR implements a new compiler pass (DequantLanePass) plus a small runtime hint protocol for KV windows and a microbatch coalescer that groups short-lived requests without increasing max latency. The goals were: 1) improve throughput on mixed-length workloads, 2) avoid per-token dequant overhead for heads that can safely remain quantized, 3) reduce redundant KV prefetch I/O when working sets are small. Summary of changes: added DequantLanePass with a lane-sampling heuristic and per-head scale-tracking; added runtime KV window hints and a conservative prefetch backoff; implemented a microbatch_coalescer that targets 1-3 ms additional latency budget for tiny requests and aggregates tokens to amortize dequant and kernel launch costs; updated quant kernels to expose a light-weight vectorized requant path used when lanes are partially dequantized. Includes new unit and integration tests and benchmark harness. Checklist: [x] design doc/rationale included, [x] unit tests, [x] integration perf test, [x] docs RFC, [x] canary rollout plan. Performance: internal bench on A100x8, mixed prompt distribution (90% short <128, 10% long 2k) showed token/sec +14% and p99 latency
title: Lane-aware hybrid dequant pass with KV-cache window hints and microbatch coalescing

text: tests, [x] integration perf test, [x] docs RFC, [x] canary rollout plan. Performance: internal bench on A100x8, mixed prompt distribution (90% short <128, 10% long 2k) showed token/sec +14% and p99 latency -8% compared to baseline dequant-all approach while maintaining output parity within eval tolerances. On smaller GPUs (T4 emulation) we see +9% throughput and slightly higher variance at p99 (+3%) which we mitigate via canary traffic shaping. Backwards compatibility: runtime hinting is optional and defaults to conservative behavior when older components are present. Rollout: staged canary on Dedicated us-west-2 with circuit-breaker to main model variants. See release_notes for user-facing impact. review_comments: Asha Patel: Interesting approach. Two concerns: (1) how do we guarantee numeric parity when only a subset of heads are dequantized? Need a deterministic fallback if eval drifts. (2) microbatching could introduce head-of-line delay for single-token steady traffic. Please add metrics and a knob to bound latency. Author (Evan Park): Added per-head scale tracking and a small 'reconcile' pass that forces full dequant on detected drift windows. Microbatch_coalescer has a max-wait knob and tracks per-route latency. See new tests and metrics in latest commit h8c13d4. Liam O'Connor: KV hint API looks clean. Please ensure older coordinators ignore hints gracefully; and add a metric for hint utilization ratio. Also add a comment on why conservative backoff was chosen vs predictive

text: description: Summary: Introduce dynamic tenant weighting in the Dedicated capacity allocator and a compact utilization_delta reporter that emits per-tenant utilization change metrics to the telemetry pipeline. Motivation: current static tenant weights make burst fairness and throughput guarantees brittle when tenant workloads shift rapidly (e.g., short-lived traffic spikes or background batch imports). We need a way to adapt allocations quickly without causing oscillation or violating throughput SLAs, and to produce a lightweight delta metric stream for rolling-window reporting and chargeback. Design: 1) Dynamic weighting controller: a new controller in the dedicated allocator that computes an 'effective_weight' per tenant every control interval by combining configured reservation (static), recent short-window utilization, and an inertia term to damp oscillation. The formula used: effective_weight = max(static_reservation, clamp(static_reservation * (1 + alpha * short_util_delta), min_factor, max_factor)) where alpha defaults to 0.4 and short_util_delta is the fractional change in utilization over the last 30s window. The controller runs asynchronously and updates the tenant weight in the allocator without requiring a global restart. 2) Utilization-delta reporter: emits compact metrics: tenant.utilization.delta (percent change), tenant.effective_weight (float), tenant.burst_reconcile.count (int) with 10s granularity. The reporter intentionally avoids full histogram payloads to
title: dynamic-tenant-weighting-and-utilization-delta-reporter

text: metrics: tenant.utilization.delta (percent change), tenant.effective_weight (float), tenant.burst_reconcile.count (int) with 10s granularity. The reporter intentionally avoids full histogram payloads to reduce cardinality and egress cost. 3) Burst reconciliation loop: when short_util_delta > threshold (0.25 by default) for more than two consecutive intervals, the allocator triggers a bounded 'burst_reconcile' that makes ephemeral burst tokens available to the tenant up to a tenant-specific budget. Tokens decay over a configurable window. Implementation highlights: - New package: dedicated/control/dynamic_weighting.go (controller + integration tests) - Add a lightweight encoder for delta metrics in telemetry/compact_encoder.go - Add e2e scenario harness for short spike -> weight adjust -> throttle avoidance in test/perf/dedicated_weight_e2e.test - Feature flag: --enable-dynamic-tenant-weighting (off by default in release builds) Rollout plan: 1) Merge behind feature flag. 2) Deploy to canary pool (slo-canary-1) and enable for a small set of non-critical tenants. 3) Monitor tenant.utilization.delta and tenant.effective_weight and gate on no more than 1.5x increase in tail latency over 5m per tenant. 4) Gradually widen the cohort if metrics are stable. Backwards compatibility: The change is opt-in; if the flag is off the allocator behavior is unchanged. Metrics names are additive only. Testing: - Unit tests for controller logic with deterministic inputs and mocked clock. - Integration
title: dynamic-tenant-weighting-and-utilization-delta-reporter

text: description: Motivation: unify staged model rollouts with a deterministic simulation pass that verifies rollback behavior and SLA invariants before any traffic promotion. This PR introduces ServeGraph, a lightweight graph representation of incremental rollout stages and transitions plus a rollback simulator that can run against historical traces and live shadow traffic to surface dangerous promotion paths early. High-level goals: aggregate canaries and A/B branches into a single ServeGraph representation; provide an offline 'simulate-rollback' mode to validate that automatic rollback playbooks will terminate and re-stabilize; enforce SLA gates at each graph edge; persist annotated runbooks to the rollout metadata so operators and the console can show the exact rollback steps taken. What this changes (summary): - New data model: rollout_graph_v2 stored in the control-plane Postgres table with lightweight transition annotations (safety_gate, rollback_playbook_ref, cohort_selector). Backwards-compatible migration included. - Rollout executor: graph-walker component that advances nodes using the existing rollout engine but with deterministic replay hooks to allow simulation. - Rollback simulator: a new binary (pkg/rollout/simulator) that accepts traces or a live shadow feed and emits a finite-state trace of promotions+rollbacks plus reasons (latency breaches, error spike, quality regressions). - SLA enforcer: pluggable policy that evaluates per-node SLOs (p99 latency, error-rate,
title: ServeGraph: incremental rollout graph with rollback simulator and SLA enforcer

text: a finite-state trace of promotions+rollbacks plus reasons (latency breaches, error spike, quality regressions). - SLA enforcer: pluggable policy that evaluates per-node SLOs (p99 latency, error-rate, quality-regression) and marks transitions as blocked/unblockable. - Console + API: new endpoints (POST /rollouts/:id/simulate, GET /rollouts/:id/graph) and a small console UI panel showing simulated paths and annotated runbook steps. - Tests & docs: integration tests that run simulator against recorded traces and a new docs page describing graph semantics and operator guidance. Checklist: - [x] Data migration with reversible steps - [x] Simulator runs in CI against synthetic traces - [x] End-to-end test covering a promoted canary that triggers an automated rollback - [x] Console rendering of simulation results Compatibility and risk: designed to be opt-in. Existing linear canary flows map to a simple two-node ServeGraph and behave unchanged unless the new simulation API is called. There is a DB migration but it is non-destructive and reversible by rolling back the feature flag. The rollout executor falls back to the legacy code-path when ServeGraph is disabled. Why this is useful: teams often discover that automated rollback playbooks have non-terminating or unsafe sequences only after several partial promotions. Running a deterministic simulation against historical traces and shadow traffic reduces blast radius by uncovering promotion paths that would oscillate or violate SLAs.
title: ServeGraph: incremental rollout graph with rollback simulator and SLA enforcer

text: description: Motivation: Current observability surfaces provide detailed per-request token cost and latency, but we lack a scalable, route-oriented rollup and an efficient way to correlate sampled KV-cache traces back to route-level cost attribution. This PR implements route rollups for token-level metrics, a lightweight attribution index that ties sampled KV/tracer spans to route keys, and a set of Grafana panels and alert rule improvements to reduce noisy alerts on long-tail routes. Design summary: - Prometheus metric rollups: add prometheus metrics with suffixed _route_rollup that aggregate token_count, token_cost_usd, and tail_latency_ms by route and time window. Rollups are emitted at the collector sidecar and computed with series cardinality controls (hashed route buckets + tag cardinality caps). - Attribution index: introduce an append-only, sharded in-memory index (observability/attribution_index) that stores a compact mapping from sampled trace_id -> {route_bucket, token_window_hash}. Span enrichment adds an index lookup id to tracer spans so sampled KV-cache traces can be resolved to route rollups without scanning logs. The index is ephemeral and bounded by memory and TTL to preserve privacy and performance. - Tracing changes: add a small span attribute `obs.route_bucket` and `obs.attribution_id` for sampled KV/kv-cache spans. OTEL exporter includes these attributes and a small indexer process batches them to the attribution index. - Dashboards & alerts: new Grafana
title: introduce-route-rollups-and-attribution-indexing-for-observability

text: `obs.route_bucket` and `obs.attribution_id` for sampled KV/kv-cache spans. OTEL exporter includes these attributes and a small indexer process batches them to the attribution index. - Dashboards & alerts: new Grafana dashboard `Route Rollups` with top-N route cost/latency panels, 95/99 percentile time-series per route bucket, and a heatmap for route cost concentration. Prometheus alert rules tuned to use percentile-based thresholds per route_bucket instead of a single global SLO, and with a noise suppression window for routes under 100 requests/min. Backward compatibility and migration: instrumentation changes are additive. Rollups are opt-in via feature flag `obs.route_rollups.enabled` (off by default); tracing attributes are guarded by `obs.attribution.tracing` (default on for sampled traces). The attribution index runs in the observability sidecar by default but can be disabled for Private installs to avoid memory overhead. Performance and safety: rollups reduce cardinality by hashing route names into 256 buckets by default; this value is configurable. Attribution index uses a fixed-size ring buffer per shard with LRU eviction to prevent unbounded growth. Benchmarks included show <2% CPU overhead on a 1000rps mixed workload with sampling_rate=0.02. Files changed: Collector sidecar rollup emitter, OTEL span enricher, attribution index implementation, Grafana JSON dashboard, Prometheus alert rules, docs and migration notes, e2e tests and a small perf benchmark harness. Checklist:

text: description: Context: Customers are adopting the OpenAI Responses vNext shape which adds a deterministic 'seed' hint and richer top-level metadata annotations. Our openai-compat layer previously dropped these fields for some model adapters and did not forward annotation keys consistently into tool/function-call payloads or the streaming reassembly path. This caused non-deterministic behavior and missing metadata for downstream tooling. What this PR does: single change set that: (1) forwards the Responses vNext 'seed' hint transparently through the compat proxy when the target model adapter supports determinism hints, falling back to no-op when not supported; (2) proxies top-level metadata annotations into both the final response envelope and into tool/function-call payloads under redwood.metadata to preserve provenance; (3) fixes streaming reassembly edge-cases where interleaved tool-call tokens could arrive out-of-order and cause corrupt function-call invocations during assembly; (4) adds an opt-in feature flag (OPENAI_RESP_VNEXT_PROXY) guarded behind runtime config for gradual rollout; (5) expands integration tests to cover cross-region streaming, seed-forwarding, and metadata-preservation with token-level assertions. Checklist: - [x] Implement seed hint passthrough (compat layer + runtime adapters) - [x] Add metadata annotation proxying for tool-calls and final response - [x] Fix streaming reassembly ordering bug (race on partial-chunk boundary) - [x] Add unit and integration
title: responses-vnext: proxy seed & annotations for tool-calls + streaming reassembly fixes

text: + runtime adapters) - [x] Add metadata annotation proxying for tool-calls and final response - [x] Fix streaming reassembly ordering bug (race on partial-chunk boundary) - [x] Add unit and integration tests covering determinism and metadata - [x] Add documentation note in redwood-docs outlining compatibility behavior and opt-in flag - [x] Telemetry events for seed-forward decisions and fallback reasons Related: Linear ENG-9876 (Responses vNext compatibility), ENG-9941 (streaming reassembly), SUP-312 (customer impacted by missing metadata). See design notes: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-9876/responses-vnext-proxy-design (internal). Notes on behavior: When seed is present in incoming Requests-API shape we attach seed_hint into the internal inference request headers so model-adapters that accept deterministic hints can honor them. If the chosen model does not advertise determinism capability the proxy logs a telemetry event and preserves the original request seed in the final response.metadata.seed for auditability. Metadata keys are merged with a redwood.metadata map before tool-call invocation so downstream tool runners receive context. Streaming reassembly changes introduce a small per-request buffer and an ordering validator that rejects malformed chunk sequences with a 409-style reassembly error that triggers automatic model fallback (same-model cold-retry) to preserve availability. Observability and rollout: added tracing spans openai_compat.proxy.forward_seed and

text: release_notes: Adds KMS region affinity hints and audit-forwarding middleware. Improves compliance visibility for cross-region KMS fallbacks and enforces tenant policy checks for key locality. Backwards compatible; feature-flagged rollout. description: Motivation: customers with strict data residency requirements need deterministic behavior and better observability when KMS operations fall back to keys in other regions. This change introduces (1) a region-affinity hinting layer that prefers in-region keys where allowed and annotates requests when cross-region key usage is required, and (2) an audit-forwarding middleware that emits structured audit events including KMS key region, policy decision, and fallback reason. Design summary: implement a pluggable KMS routing helper that consults tenant attributes + RBAC v2 attributes and config flags, update the policy evaluator to add an explicit deny path when tenant policy forbids cross-region decrypts, and add an audit forwarder that tags and forwards enriched audit events to the central audit sink. Rollout strategy: feature-flagged under security.kms_region_affinity (default enabled for canary tenants), staged canary that runs on 10% of requests in dedicated clusters, fallback to existing paths on error, telemetry for policy-denies and fallback-rate. Migration: no data migration required; operators should verify tenant KMS bindings and optionally supply per-tenant region_hint annotations. Checklist: [x] implement routing helper,
title: kms-region-affinity-and-audit-forwarding-middleware

text: and fallback-rate. Migration: no data migration required; operators should verify tenant KMS bindings and optionally supply per-tenant region_hint annotations. Checklist: [x] implement routing helper, [x] add audit forwarder, [x] update policy evaluator, [x] add tests and unit coverage, [x] e2e on canary, [x] docs/ops guide. Related: ENG-4123 (policy audit), SEC-198 (compliance ask). review_thread: Ethan Kim: Please add explicit unit tests for the policy evaluator deny path when tenant has ForbidCrossRegion set. Also call out any latency regression for hot paths. Maya Patel (author): Added unit tests and microbenchmarks; latency impact <0.5% for typical token sizes. Updated tests in commit 3. Lina Rodrigues: We should make sure the audit event schema is backward compatible with the central sink. Can we include a version field? Maya Patel: Added `schema_version: v2` to the audit payload and a compatibility shim in the sink client. Samuel Ortiz: Approved pending CI green. CI bot: initial pipeline failed (policy_evaluator_test race). Maya Patel: Pushed fix for test race and updated mock to stabilize timing. Samuel Ortiz: LGTM. Merge approved.
title: kms-region-affinity-and-audit-forwarding-middleware

text: description: Motivation: Streaming tool/function-calling paths and the OpenAI compatibility layer had diverged semantics around project-scoped tokens and per-stream proof headers. This caused intermittent 401s during long-lived streaming tool calls (especially when tokens rotated) and inconsistent CORS behavior for browser-originated streaming sessions. This PR consolidates the runtime and compat layers behind an ephemeral, canonical header contract and deprecates the older project-token prefix behavior. What this change does (high level): - Introduces a transient header X-Redwood-Ephemeral-Scope that carries scoped token metadata (scope id, expiry, hmac) in a compact base64 form. This header is honored on streaming handshakes and toolcall preflight requests. - Adds X-Redwood-Stream-Proof for per-connection integrity checks (short-lived HMAC of the handshake). The runtime validates this on accept and rejects if mismatch or expired. - Updates the OpenAI-compat mapping layer (openai_compat) to translate legacy Authorization: Project <token-prefix> into the new ephemeral-scoped handshake when the gateway can mint/upgrade tokens, with a best-effort migration path. - Deprecates the behavioral reliance on prefix-based project tokens in the gateway and documents the migration. The prefix still accepts but logs a deprecation metric and a warning header to clients. - Harmonizes CORS preflight acceptance for the new headers: OPTIONS now returns Access-Control-Allow-Headers including X-Redwood-Ephemeral-Scope
title: deprecate-x-project-token-prefix-and-align-ephemeral-scope-headers-for-streaming-toolcalls

text: logs a deprecation metric and a warning header to clients. - Harmonizes CORS preflight acceptance for the new headers: OPTIONS now returns Access-Control-Allow-Headers including X-Redwood-Ephemeral-Scope and X-Redwood-Stream-Proof, and adds explanatory Access-Control-Expose-Headers for the structured-output advisory header. - Adds a small structured-output advisory header X-Redwood-Structured to signal that the model endpoint returned JSON-LD style structured sections. This helps browser clients avoid early text-only parsing heuristics. Compatibility and rollout strategy: - Backward compatible: gateways will continue to accept legacy Project-prefixed Authorization for 30 days after rollout; during that window they automatically exchange for an ephemeral scope token when possible. - Canary-first: change gated behind feature flag runtime.ephemeral_scope_enabled. We will enable on 10% of traffic for 48 hours, monitor auth error rate, stream disconnects, and latency regressions. - Migration docs and quickstart added (docs/migration/project-scoped-to-ephemeral.md) explaining SDK changes required for client-side token refresh and handshake renewal. Tests and validation: - Unit tests for header parsing and HMAC validation added (runtime/streaming/handshake_test). - Integration test for streaming toolcall lifecycle with token rotation simulated (perf-canary/streaming-auth-rotate.yml). - E2E smoke against openai_compat endpoints ensuring header translation and CORS preflight acceptance.
title: deprecate-x-project-token-prefix-and-align-ephemeral-scope-headers-for-streaming-toolcalls

text: streaming toolcall lifecycle with token rotation simulated (perf-canary/streaming-auth-rotate.yml). - E2E smoke against openai_compat endpoints ensuring header translation and CORS preflight acceptance. Rollout checklist (included in description to aid reviewers): - [x] Add header parsing and validation logic - [x] Implement compat translation path with explicit logging for legacy tokens - [x] Add CORS allowed/expose headers for streaming flows - [x] Add migration docs and SDK example update (js/python) - [x] Add metrics: auth.legacy_token_usage, auth.ephemeral_handshake_failures, stream.handshake_latency - [x] Canary rollout + monitor Why not a cookie or existing Authorization-only flow: cookies are fragile for cross-origin streaming and add CSRF surface; existing Authorization flows lacked a standard short-lived handshake that binds the connection to the token lifecycle (causing out-of-band token rotation races). The ephemeral header + stream-proof HMAC gives an accountable, per-connection contract without affecting bearer semantics for non-streaming REST calls. Notes on SDK changes: JS and Python SDKs gain a helper that performs a background handshake refresh and replaces the X-Redwood-Ephemeral-Scope header before a long stream reaches 70% of the scope expiry. Example code snippets live under redwood-sdk-typescript/examples/ephemeral-handshake. Related: ENG-9342 (stream stability), ENG-9410 (openai compat header mapping), SUP-8471 (support case: repeated 401 on long streams).
title: deprecate-x-project-token-prefix-and-align-ephemeral-scope-headers-for-streaming-toolcalls
