text: description: Background: Customers operating in regulated regions asked for two pain points to be addressed together: (1) ensuring KMS usage is region-affine to tenant data residency policies, and (2) removing sensitive tokens and PII from downstream audit sinks prior to export. Motivation: previous work added KMS region scoping checks at the API surface, but key usage still flowed to global key wrappers in some paths and audit exporters occasionally leaked request-level tokens. This change bundles a defensive, production-ready approach: (A) an ephemeral KMS affinity layer that selects tenant-scoped, region-bound keys for short-lived operations and records deterministic key-tag metadata; (B) an audit-sanitization pipeline that applies attribute-aware redaction rules before any logs or audit events are published to external sinks. What this PR does (concise): - Adds a KMS Affinity evaluator (policy-driven) that resolves key candidates based on tenant attributes, data residency tag, and RBAC v2 attribute checks. The evaluator returns a short-lived key handle (ephemeral KMS token) and emits sealed metadata to the audit subsystem. - Introduces an audit-sanitizer stage in the audit forwarder that: 1) removes bearer tokens and secrets from headers/metadata; 2) normalizes and redacts user-identifiable fields (email, phone, SSN-like patterns) using configurable regex rules; 3) annotates events with non-sensitive geotags (region-only). - Adds RBAC v2 enforcement hooks so key selection
title: Ephemeral KMS affinity and audit sanitization pipeline

text: user-identifiable fields (email, phone, SSN-like patterns) using configurable regex rules; 3) annotates events with non-sensitive geotags (region-only). - Adds RBAC v2 enforcement hooks so key selection requires the caller to possess both the key:use permission and the attribute:kms_region match for targeted tenants. - Adds unit and integration tests for the affinity evaluator and sanitizer (including regression tests for previously-leaked path). - Updates documentation (security/rbac.md and audit/exporter.md) and adds an operational guide for enabling sanitizer rules on a per-tenant basis. Rollout plan: feature gated behind "security.ephemeral_kms_affinity" and "audit.sanitizer_v2" flags. Step 1: enable in staging and run log-compatibility job against internal sinks. Step 2: enable for a small set of low-volume tenants behind canary. Step 3: full rollout once metrics (no increased error rate, <0.1% sanitizer-induced drops) are green. Checklist: - [x] Design doc: ENG-2145 linked - [x] Unit tests added - [x] Integration test: local KMS emulator - [x] Docs updated - [x] Feature flags added - [x] Backwards-compatible by default (flags off) Notes/Limitations: - The sanitizer uses heuristics for PII detection; customers with strict legal obligations should review rules and may opt to route raw audit streams to internal sinks only. - Ephemeral KMS tokens are short-lived (5m TTL) and rely on internal caching to avoid QPS spikes; autoscaling of the KMS proxy is included as an operator
title: Ephemeral KMS affinity and audit sanitization pipeline

text: route raw audit streams to internal sinks only. - Ephemeral KMS tokens are short-lived (5m TTL) and rely on internal caching to avoid QPS spikes; autoscaling of the KMS proxy is included as an operator note in the rollout guide. review_comments: Luca Patel: Can we avoid additional synchronous KMS round-trips on hot paths? Suggest returning a cached ephemeral handle when request TTL remains > 60s. Author (Maya): Implemented caching layer in ephemeral_handle_manager with a kube-configurable ttl and max-stale window. See ephemeral_handle_manager.go (cache logic) and added unit tests. Jordan Reyes: Sanitizer rules look good, but some regexes may be too aggressive — flagged potential false positives for order numbers. Please add allowlist exception for "order_id" patterns used in commerce flows. Author (Maya): Added allowlist rules and a tenant-level override. Also added a test case that simulates commerce payloads. Priya Verma: Need to ensure RBAC audit trail includes the attribute match that permitted key selection. Otherwise downstream reviewers can't verify region affinity. Author (Maya): Audit event now contains a sealed 'kms_affinity_metadata' blob (non-sensitive) with region tag and policy hash. Confirmed no secret material is leaked.
title: Ephemeral KMS affinity and audit sanitization pipeline

text: description: Motivation: CPU-side overhead on the request path has been the dominant cost for short requests in our hosted service. Profiling across multiple workloads (chat, short-completion, and embeddings) shows excessive context switches, frequent small allocations during request serialization, and lock contention in the per-GPU worker pool. This PR introduces a compact ring-buffer based serializer + a lightweight batch coalescer to: (1) avoid copying small message frames across thread boundaries, (2) reduce futex wakeups by aggregating send events, and (3) make continuous batching cheaper for high-qps short requests. The change is intentionally incremental and guarded behind a runtime flag for easy rollback. Design summary: a lock-free single-producer-multi-consumer ring buffer sits between request acceptors (IO threads) and per-worker executors. Small serialized frames are placed into the ring buffer with a fast path that avoids heap allocations and avoids taking the worker pool mutex on the hot path. Workers poll the ring buffer and coalesce adjacent frames into a single outbound batch when the combined size is under a configurable coalesce_window (default 5120 bytes / 2ms). The serializer reserves contiguous ranges in the ring to allow zero-copy writes; when a frame crosses the end, it uses a short two-fragment header to avoid an extra copy. The worker-side dequeue is wait-free for typical cases (backpressure uses a lightweight backoff) and only falls back to the main
title: Reduce CPU context switches and message copying with ringbuffer serializer and batch coalescer

text: crosses the end, it uses a short two-fragment header to avoid an extra copy. The worker-side dequeue is wait-free for typical cases (backpressure uses a lightweight backoff) and only falls back to the main executor queue when the ring is full. KV-cache and scheduling interaction: the ring buffer exposes per-request metadata flags so worker executors can prefetch KV entries before deserialization of the prompt body. We also tuned the executor scheduler to prefer local dequeue and to delay thread yields for a small adaptive window when high throughput is detected. That reduces context switching between IO threads and worker threads under steady load. Implementation highlights: runtime/transport/ring_serializer.h/.cc (new), runtime/worker_pool/batch_coalescer.cc (new), runtime/scheduler tweaks to prefer local work-stealing avoidance, runtime/tests/ring_serializer_test.cc (unit), perf/bench/ring_coalesce_bench.cc (microbench). Small API additions: TransportFrame header contains coalesce_hint and kv_prefetch_hint bits. The runtime flag `--enable-ring-serializer` defaults to off in production until canary runs complete. Benchmarks: representative end-to-end perf harness (chat-short template): p50 latency reduced from 18.4ms -> 12.2ms (-33.7%), p95 68ms -> 45ms (-33.8%), p99 156ms -> 97ms (-37.8%). CPU per-request cycles (measured via perf and taskstats) decreased ~28% on average. Tail throughput improved ~+22% in the continuous-batching scenario. Memory allocator churn (malloc/free
title: Reduce CPU context switches and message copying with ringbuffer serializer and batch coalescer

text: 97ms (-37.8%). CPU per-request cycles (measured via perf and taskstats) decreased ~28% on average. Tail throughput improved ~+22% in the continuous-batching scenario. Memory allocator churn (malloc/free counts) for the hot path dropped by ~90% in microbench runs. Testing & rollout: added unit tests and integration tests. Perf harness scripts included to reproduce numbers. Canary rollout plan: enable flag in dedicated canary pool for 48 hours; monitor p99 latency, CPU utilization, and trace sampling (we added a trace tag to indicate ring-serializer path). Early canary on staging shows stable improvements without new errors. Checklist: - [x] unit tests - [x] integration tests - [x] perf bench harness - [x] canary plan documented - [x] telemetry (metric: runtime.ring_serializer.coalesced_bytes, coalesce_batches, ring_contention) Related: ENG-1289 (request path CPU optimizations), ENG-1297 (continuous batching improvements). See design note: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-1289/ringbuffer-serializer-design for rationale. review_comments: Diego: Can you explain failure modes when ring buffer is full? Is there a fallback to existing path? Author (Priya): Yes — when ring is full we fall back to enqueueing on the existing worker mutex-protected queue. That path preserves correctness and is documented in the code. We added metrics to track fallbacks. Anya: Please reduce the default coalesce window; 10ms felt long for low-latency callers. Author (Priya): lowered default to 2ms and

text: and is documented in the code. We added metrics to track fallbacks. Anya: Please reduce the default coalesce window; 10ms felt long for low-latency callers. Author (Priya): lowered default to 2ms and added dynamic tuning based on request size histogram. Also added an env var for per-deployment override. text: description: Context: Continuous-batching latency spikes remain correlated with long-lived, low-KV-hit requests that accumulate in a single queue window and prevent small fast requests from being coalesced. Motivation: previous fixes (deadline steering, probe jitter) reduced some tail pain but left scenarios where a small number of KV-heavy requests would create a persistent backlog and cause cascading micro-timeouts in downstream queues. This PR introduces two coordinated strategies: 1) staggered window thresholds (per-priority, per-queue moving thresholds) that allow the batcher to open secondary ingestion windows when primary windows are clogged; 2) a lightweight KV-revival heuristic that performs a fast, opportunistic KV pre-warm for requests that are likely to unblock the cache and restore batching efficiency. The changes are safe-by-default, revertible, and covered by unit + perf tests. commits: 5 commits: (1) add staggered window scheduling skeleton; (2) implement per-priority moving thresholds; (3) KV-revival probe and metrics; (4) tests + docs; (5) canary rollout config review_comments: Ethan Moore: Questioned CPU impact of KV-revival during high throughput; asked for upper bound throttle and accounting in metrics. Priya Singh: Added a token-bucket throttle (configurable) and instrumentation (kv_revive_attempts, kv_revive_success). Updated PR with throttle default=2 revives/sec per worker. Sofia Chen: Requested clearer invariants for window promotion to avoid oscillation
title: Staggered window thresholds and KV-revival heuristic for continuous batcher

text: and instrumentation (kv_revive_attempts, kv_revive_success). Updated PR with throttle default=2 revives/sec per worker. Sofia Chen: Requested clearer invariants for window promotion to avoid oscillation under bursty arrival patterns. Priya Singh: Introduced hysteresis (min_promotion_interval=100ms) and a backoff multiplier. Added tests that simulate bursty arrivals to assert no oscillation observed. Miguel Alvarez: Confirmed no change to public API and asked for rollout plan and rollback safety nets. Priya Singh: Added canary.yaml to repo and rollout_plan section in PR description; tested rollback path (feature flag + immediate disable) in perf-lab. Sofia Chen: Approved after seeing perf numbers and tests. Ethan Moore: Approved. Miguel Alvarez: Merged after canary verification. bench_summary: Perf lab (mixed small/large request mix): median latency improved 5.8% overall; p99 reduced ~22% for prioritized small requests on congested queues; throughput increased ~8% under sustained mixed load. KV-hit-rate improved by ~6 percentage points in scenarios where revival successfully warmed frequently-missed keys. Observed CV of batch sizes decreased indicating more consistent batching behavior. tests_run: unit integration perf-lab: mixed-load-48h canary-rolling-2pct rollout_plan: Phased canary: 2% traffic for 6 hours, observe metrics (batch_window_utilization, queue_escape_count, p99); if stable, ramp to 10% for 12 hours, then full rollout. Immediate rollback by disabling feature flag
title: Staggered window thresholds and KV-revival heuristic for continuous batcher

text: canary: 2% traffic for 6 hours, observe metrics (batch_window_utilization, queue_escape_count, p99); if stable, ramp to 10% for 12 hours, then full rollout. Immediate rollback by disabling feature flag or reverting canary service routing. Ops runbook added in docs link. observability_changes: metrics added: batch_window_utilization, window_promotion_count, kv_revive_attempts, kv_revive_success, queue_escape_count tracing spans: window_promotion -> kv_revival probe dashboard: scheduler/windowed-batcher-overview (new) danger_signals: kv_revive_spike_cpu queue_escape_rate_increase p99_regression > 5% from baseline release_notes: Staggered window thresholds for the continuous batcher and a best-effort KV-revival probe. Improves tail latency for mixed workloads and reduces starvation risk when KV-heavy requests appear. Configurable via feature flag and runtime params. notes_for_ops: Default config is conservative: promotion hysteresis 100ms, max 2 kv-revives/sec per worker, revival cost capped by token bucket. If ops see CPU spike correlated with kv_revive_attempts, set 'kv_revival.enabled=false' and reroute canary until investigation completes.
title: Staggered window thresholds and KV-revival heuristic for continuous batcher

text: description: Motivation: Customers were seeing indeterminate behavior when clients disconnected mid-stream (mobile network flaps, proxy resets) and then reconnected: generated tokens could be duplicated or lost, function-calling side effects were inconsistently applied, and OpenAI-compat clients received confusing error payloads. This PR introduces a pragmatic, opt-in streaming lifecycle handshake and cancellation safeguards to make disconnect/reconnect/resume behavior deterministic and backwards-compatible. Summary of changes: Adds a resume handshake token, server-side grace-window and per-stream timeout semantics, improved mapping for OpenAI-compat abort/cancel errors, and safe function-calling guarantees when clients reconnect. Includes runtime changes, adapter mapping, SDK surface notes, tests, and docs. Design goals and constraints: 1) Backwards-compatible by default: existing clients continue working with current semantics; new handshake is enabled via header or query param. 2) Deterministic retries/resume: resumed streams should continue from the last acknowledged token, never re-execute completed function calls. 3) Minimal latency impact for non-resuming clients: default flow retains current fast-path. 4) Observable: metrics for resume attempts, aborted-inflight, and resume success rate. Protocol and API changes (high level): - New optional outbound header: X-Resume-Token: client-supplied token used to correlate a resumed stream with a recent disconnected stream. Servers
title: client-disconnect-reconnect-handshake-cancel-safeguards-openai-compat

text: success rate. Protocol and API changes (high level): - New optional outbound header: X-Resume-Token: client-supplied token used to correlate a resumed stream with a recent disconnected stream. Servers issue a Resume-Offer frame at disconnect that contains a server-side resume token and last-ack offset. - New request query param: resume_window_ms (default 30000) controls how long the server will honor resume attempts for a given stream id. - New server timeout param: stream_idle_timeout_ms (default 60000) to bound resource retention post-disconnect. - New SSE/Chunk frames: RESUME_OFFER, RESUME_ACCEPT, RESUME_REJECT, HEARTBEAT to coordinate state. (When using HTTP/2 gRPC-like streaming the frames are translated into control messages.) Server behavior and safety: - On client disconnect, the runtime records the last-confirmed token offset and issues a RESUME_OFFER when it is safe to resume (i.e., KV cache intact). The offer includes a server_resume_token and last_ack_position. - The server holds execution state for resume_window_ms. If a new connection presents X-Resume-Token matching a live server_resume_token and offset <= last_ack_position + hysteresis, the server responds with RESUME_ACCEPT and resumes token emission from next position. - To avoid double-execution of function/tool calls, function-call side effects are only marked committed after the client acknowledges receipt of a special CALL_CONFIRM frame. During resume, the runtime consults the call ledger and will not re-run
title: client-disconnect-reconnect-handshake-cancel-safeguards-openai-compat

text: calls, function-call side effects are only marked committed after the client acknowledges receipt of a special CALL_CONFIRM frame. During resume, the runtime consults the call ledger and will not re-run committed calls; in-flight calls that did not reach CALL_CONFIRM will be retried once with idempotency keys. - If the resume fails (mismatch token or expired window), the server returns RESUME_REJECT and the client may reopen a fresh stream; server enforces server-side backpressure and cold-start fallback to alternate model variants if capacity is constrained. OpenAI compatibility adapter changes: - Map native abort/disconnect reasons to OpenAI-compat error semantics: aborted-by-client => code: 499/cancelled with message "Client cancelled stream"; server-timeout => 503 with body matching OpenAI shape. - For streaming-chat endpoints, the adapter now honors X-Resume-Token and includes resume metadata in the chunked response for OpenAI-compat clients that opt-in via header "X-OpenAI-Resume: 1". This preserves OpenAI-compatible behavior while enabling deterministic resume. - Function-calling: adapter uses the new CALL_CONFIRM frame to report that a function invocation payload has been delivered; only then will OpenAI-compatible /responses include function result committed state. Compatibility and opt-in rollout: - Feature is guarded under feature flag streaming-lifecycle-v2. Default off for all existing accounts; enabled on canary accounts and progressively rolled to larger tenants.
title: client-disconnect-reconnect-handshake-cancel-safeguards-openai-compat

text: Compatibility and opt-in rollout: - Feature is guarded under feature flag streaming-lifecycle-v2. Default off for all existing accounts; enabled on canary accounts and progressively rolled to larger tenants. - SDKs (Python/TS) will include helpers to manage resume tokens and heartbeat handling. Docs include migration notes and an example for mobile reconnect patterns. Testing and observability: - Unit + integration tests that simulate flapping connections, mid-call disconnects, duplicate resume attempts, and expired resume windows. - Added e2e tests for OpenAI-compat streaming in CI matrix. - New metrics: streaming.resume_attempts, streaming.resume_success, streaming.resume_rejections, streaming.inflight_call_retries. Added tracing spans for handshake phases: handshake.offer, handshake.accept, handshake.reject, handshake.timeout. Migration checklist: - [x] Implement server-side resume token store and eviction. - [x] Add SDK helpers and docs. - [x] Add OpenAI-compat adapter mapping and tests. - [x] Add metrics and tracing. - [x] Canaries in prod and rollout automation. Risks and mitigations: - Risk: state retention increases memory footprint on high-concurrency workloads. Mitigation: server enforces stream_idle_timeout_ms and per-tenant limits; resume_window_ms default is conservative. - Risk: tool/function idempotency edge cases. Mitigation: explicit CALL_CONFIRM semantics and idempotency keys; we test common patterns and add telemetry for uncommitted calls. Rollout plan: Canary
title: client-disconnect-reconnect-handshake-cancel-safeguards-openai-compat

text: conservative. - Risk: tool/function idempotency edge cases. Mitigation: explicit CALL_CONFIRM semantics and idempotency keys; we test common patterns and add telemetry for uncommitted calls. Rollout plan: Canary on 3 customers and internal staging for 48 hours; measure resume_success and in-flight_call_retries; open rollout gradually, disabling on SLO breach. This description is intentionally dense; see changed files for implementation details and inline comments. review_comments: Tomás Nguyen: Overall approach looks good. A few clarifying questions: 1) How do we prevent resume tokens from being reused by malicious clients? 2) Can we tighten the default resume_window_ms for free-tier tenants? Asha Patel: Answers: 1) Resume tokens are cryptographically signed and bound to tenant+stream id; we also rotate and revoke tokens on explicit stream termination. 2) Yes, we added per-tenant overrides and free-tier defaults to 15s to limit retention (see runtime/streaming/handshake.go). Maya Reddy: We need more unit tests around function-call idempotency when network fails between CALL_CONFIRM and ACK. Also please add a metric for uncommitted_call_retries. Asha Patel: Added tests in handshake_test.go and adapter tests; added streaming.uncommitted_call_retries metric. Also added explicit lint justification for the new control frame names. Ben Carlson: CI flakes on the OpenAI-compat e2e in the first run, re-ran and it passed. Approving once CI is green. release_notes: Optional streaming resume

text: body: Problem: for certain tensor-parallel workloads we observed repeated NCCL stalls caused by misaligned collective boundaries and kernel granularity, especially when sequence length and microbatch size vary across traffic patterns. Existing heuristics waited for full collective readiness before issuing kernels which increased tail latency and reduced steady-state throughput under mixed batch conditions. Motivation: overlap comm and compute at finer grain with deterministic safety (no correctness regressions) and proactively warm KV cache entries so later steps reuse host/GPU-resident KV without blocking. Summary of changes: 1) Introduce a new CommCompute Splice Scheduler that slices layer-level comm/compute windows into lightweight 'splice' segments. The scheduler computes split points at runtime using recorded token/service time statistics and issues NCCL collectives on a lower-priority stream while launching dependent compute kernels on high-priority streams as soon as safe. 2) Add KV lookahead prefetch (kv_lookahead) configurable per-model. The runtime tracks upcoming attention key ranges and issues prefetch for those KV slabs into a pinned double-buffered slab pool to hide device-host latency. 3) Implement an interleaved host-pinned double-buffer pipeline (hostbuf_a/hostbuf_b) for cross-node reduce/gather to reduce hostcopy stalls; introduces a small host-side rendezvous for eager release. 4) Kernel chaining improvements: add a small chaining trampoline in the CUDA dispatcher
title: commcompute splice scheduler + KV lookahead prefetch to reduce TP comm stalls

text: for cross-node reduce/gather to reduce hostcopy stalls; introduces a small host-side rendezvous for eager release. 4) Kernel chaining improvements: add a small chaining trampoline in the CUDA dispatcher so dependent microkernels can be queued without extra host roundtrips; this reduces launch overhead for tiny kernels used in sliced attention. 5) Continuous batching adjustments: the batching controller now aligns batch boundaries to KV lookahead windows and splice boundaries to maximize reuse and minimize cross-splice synchronization. 6) Safety and fallbacks: when NCCL reports unexpected errors or when dynamic profiling is insufficient, scheduler falls back to original full-collective behavior to preserve correctness. Commits (high level): - b3f1a2d: initial splice scheduler core loop and scheduler interface - 7a9c0de: kv_lookahead tracking, slab allocator and double-buffer hostbufs - 2c4d9ef: cuda dispatcher trampoline and kernel-chain tests - e8f7a1b: batching-controller alignment integration and metrics - 9d2b7f3: unit tests, integration test harness updates and perf benchmarks - 44a1e6c: docs and runtime flags + CLI examples Files changed (high level): - runtime/tp/scheduler/splice_scheduler.cc/.h: core scheduler and heuristics - runtime/tp/kv/kv_lookahead.*: lookahead tracker and prefetch logic - runtime/io/hostbuf_doublebuffer.*: host double-buffer pipeline and rendezvous - runtime/cuda/dispatcher_trampoline.*: small trampoline for kernel chains - runtime/batching/continuous_batcher.*:
title: commcompute splice scheduler + KV lookahead prefetch to reduce TP comm stalls

text: logic - runtime/io/hostbuf_doublebuffer.*: host double-buffer pipeline and rendezvous - runtime/cuda/dispatcher_trampoline.*: small trampoline for kernel chains - runtime/batching/continuous_batcher.*: alignment logic and metrics hooks - tests/integration/tp_splice_perf_test.py: microbench harness and CI perf check - docs/ops/tuning_kv_lookahead.md: operator-facing tuning guide Small illustrative snippet (from splice_scheduler.cc): "// compute splice points based on per-layer stats int splice = std::max(1, layer_tokens / config.splice_granularity); // issue collective on low-prio stream while launching compute on high-prio stream ncclLaunchCollective(low_prio_stream, ...); launchCompute(high_prio_stream, ...);" Benchmarks: - Cluster: 8xA100-80GB NVLink, CUDA 14, NCCL 2.18 - Workloads: mix of chat (context 8k), long-form generation (context 32k), and reranking microbatch tests - Results: 12% median throughput uplift on mixed-chat workloads; 18% uplift on long-form generation microbenchmarks with sequence reuse; 20–45% reduction in 99th percentile token latency for heterogeneous microbatch flows. Tests & CI: - Unit tests added for scheduler heuristics and kv_lookahead eviction logic - Integration perf job added (tp_splice_perf_test.py) that runs in the perf lab; CI runs full unit suite and a short perf smoke. - Initial CI run showed intermittent NCCL flaky in perf harness; added retry + tightened hostbuf ordering; final CI: all checks passed. Rollout and flags: - Default behavior:

text: body: Motivation: Reduce duplicated auth lifecycle logic across the Python/TS/Go SDKs and make streaming consumer ergonomics simpler for common patterns. This change introduces a compact AuthFacade that centralizes fallback and token refresh semantics, a lightweight RetryProfile helper shared by all SDKs, and a small consumer-friendly StreamConsumer surface that wraps common async-iterable/pullstream patterns. Goals: 1) cut cognitive load for application engineers integrating streaming and token rotation; 2) eliminate a recurring class of races observed in long-lived streams under intermittent 401s; 3) align retry semantics so hosted and dedicated deployments observe consistent behavior. What changed: - New compact AuthFacade API added to redwood-sdk-python, redwood-sdk-typescript, and redwood-sdk-go. It exposes a minimal credential lookup + fallback chain and a single refresh hook that SDKs call when 401s occur. The facade is intentionally tiny (3 methods) to encourage composition rather than inheritance. - Shared RetryProfile presets added (fast-fail, balanced, durable) with documented defaults and tuned backoffs for streaming vs unary calls. SDKs accept RetryProfile by name or config object. - StreamConsumer utility: a thin wrapper that normalizes chunked server streaming into an ergonomic async iterator with built-in reauth-on-401 wiring and opt-in checkpointing. - Small refactor to internal session code to let SDKs plug the AuthFacade without changing public client constructors.
title: compact-auth-facade-and-stream-consumer-polish

text: ergonomic async iterator with built-in reauth-on-401 wiring and opt-in checkpointing. - Small refactor to internal session code to let SDKs plug the AuthFacade without changing public client constructors. - Migration helpers and deprecation notices: old TokenProvider.swap_to and SessionManager.startWarmup are deprecated but remain functional with console warnings; migration guide added. Backwards compatibility: No breaking behavior for existing call sites by default. New APIs are additive; a few internal helper signatures were changed but kept private. We added deprecation warnings where required and documented explicit upgrade steps for users who want to opt into the new stream reauth behavior. Testing and rollout: All unit and integration tests updated across tri-sdk matrix. We added an integration test that simulates mid-stream 401 and validates graceful reauth and resume logic. CI initially flagged a flaky streaming test on Linux; the failure was due to a timing assumption corrected by a small sleep + event ordering fix and retry on test harness. Performance: Slight reduction in allocations for streaming hot path in Python and Go (microbenchmarks show ~6-8% CPU improvement for steady-state multi-connection throughput). Also small improvement in TypeScript memory churn under high-concurrency streaming. Docs and version bumps: docs/migration/compact-auth-facade.md and examples/streaming/reauth-example added. SDK versions bumped: python 1.9.0, typescript 2.4.1, go v1.6.0. Rollout
title: compact-auth-facade-and-stream-consumer-polish

Rollback path: revert the facade registration inside SDK constructors if unexpected regressions occur. See linked Linear tasks for rollout ownership and telemetry additions. review_comments: Jared Lowe: Nice consolidation. The goal with short names was to balance discoverability and brevity; we can adjust further if you prefer more verbose labels. Marta Ivanov: Found a small race when stream resumed immediately after refresh; suggested a mutex around the session swap and an integration test. I added the mutex and integration test in the last commit. Chen Li: CI green on final run, approved. Left one stylistic nit on facade.ts (prefer explicit return types). I applied the change as part of the squash. commits: feat(auth): introduce compact AuthFacade interface and basic fallback chain feat(retry): add RetryProfile presets (fast-fail, balanced, durable) feat(stream): add StreamConsumer

text: description: Context and motivation: customer support traces and internal canaries revealed intermittent auth failures for streaming tool-calls when requests cross tenant/org boundaries (hosted -> dedicated routing, or reverse proxied plugin gateways). Failures were caused by brittle header heuristics (we previously prioritized X-Project-Token prefixes only) and a mismatch between the OpenAI-compat streaming handshake and our session-anchored signed-nonce path. This PR unifies header heuristics and introduces a streaming handshake adapter to map legacy OpenAI compat frames to Redwood's tolerant auth checks. Summary of changes: - Header normalizer: introduce x-rw-tenant and tenant discovery heuristics that try (in order) explicit x-rw-tenant, project-scoped token prefix, org-key fallback, and a safe header-mapping table populated for proxied requests. This is a best-effort discovery that does not change auth decision semantics (we still validate token scope/TTL) but reduces false negatives during routing. - Streaming handshake adapter: a small adapter layer that inspects the initial connect frame (OpenAI style "openai-session", or Redwood "rw-session-nonce") and normalizes fields into a canonical session object used by the runtime. Adapter tolerates both signed-nonce formats (v1 HMAC header and v2 structured signature) to maintain compatibility with older SDKs. - Signature parsing: make request-signature verification tolerant to 2 formats (X-RW-Signature: hmac={sig};v=1 and X-Signature:
title: Align tenant header heuristics and stream handshake adapter

text: and v2 structured signature) to maintain compatibility with older SDKs. - Signature parsing: make request-signature verification tolerant to 2 formats (X-RW-Signature: hmac={sig};v=1 and X-Signature: sig_v2={b64};alg=ed25519) and log a single metric for signature format fallbacks. - CORS: relax preflight mapping for streaming upgrade endpoints; the adapter now echoes project-origin hints in Access-Control-Allow-Headers if the request includes X-Project-Origin to avoid some browser preflight failures when proxying through third-party hubs. - Tool/function calling stability: preserve tool-call context across reconnects by applying a lightweight reconnect token derived from the original signed nonce (TTL 60s). If reconnect presents the reconnect token, adapter rehydrates the call context and hints to the tools-layer to resume safely. - Observability: add tenant_auth_attempts, tenant_auth_fallbacks, and stream_handshake_format metrics; wire them to the console dashboards. Commits (high-level): - 4c2d9a2: header_normalizer: new package and heuristics implementation - b7f1e3d: stream handshake adapter and format toleration - d5a7b99: signature parsing helpers + metric emission - a2e8f12: unit + integration tests for streaming auth + reconnects - f9c4b01: docs and upgrade notes Files changed (high-level): - internal/auth/header_normalizer.go: main heuristics + a small mapping table for proxied hosts - runtime/stream/handshake_adapter.go: adapter that canonicalizes session frames - api/openai_compat/stream_bridge.go:
title: Align tenant header heuristics and stream handshake adapter

CI summary: initial run (run #1) reported 1 failing test in tests/integration/stream-auth-suite.test.js (external-hub flake). After adding retry and handling empty auth-frame, rerun (run #2) passed all checks: unit, integration, lint, e2e-nightly. Metrics smoke test included. Backward compatibility and migration notes: - This change is backward compatible: we accept both legacy and new signature formats and both openai-style and rw-style handshake frames. - Operators upgrading Dedicated/Private should review cmd/installer/upgrade-notes.md to optionally enable extended tenant-mapping lists for proxy gateways. - No breaking changes to public SDKs; we added a reconnect token path that SDKs can opt-into to improve resume behavior. Why this is different from prior PRs: prior work focused on signed-nonce normalization and CORS mapping in isolation;
title: Align tenant header heuristics and stream handshake adapter

text: we added a reconnect token path that SDKs can opt-into to improve resume behavior. Why this is different from prior PRs: prior work focused on signed-nonce normalization and CORS mapping in isolation; this PR explicitly centralizes tenant discovery heuristics (to reduce cross-tenant false rejects) and introduces a streaming handshake adapter that acts as a single compatibility surface for both auth headers and streaming frame formats. It reduces duplication and clarifies where compatibility decisions live. Testing performed: unit tests for header heuristics, property-based token-prefix parsing tests, integration suite exercising hosted->dedicated routing via a test proxy, manual canary on perf-canary with a small traffic slice verifying no latency regression, observability smoke tests verifying metric emission. Merge details: squash merged by Maya Patel after approvals from Liam and Nora. Merge method: squash. Changelog: included in release notes for the next patch release. Follow-ups / future work: - ENG-2145: expand tenant mapping ingestion from control plane for multi-tenant proxies - ENG-2212: plumb reconnect-token usage to TS/Go SDKs as an opt-in resume API - Add tracing spans to show which heuristic matched for easier debugging in the console release_notes: Normalize tenant discovery headers across streaming handshakes; add tolerant request-signature parsing to support legacy OpenAI-compat clients; improve streaming reconnect heuristics to preserve tool-call contexts.
title: Align tenant header heuristics and stream handshake adapter

text: description: Introduce a lightweight kernel lifecycle sampler and dashboard annotation hooks to improve visibility into per-kernel latency, queueing, and KV-cache interaction in the serving runtime. Motivation: production incidents in Oct/Nov showed transient latency spikes correlated with long tail kernel scheduling and cache thrash; existing NVTX ranges were useful but lacked compact per-kernel occupancy histograms and cross-request tag continuity. This PR adds: 1) a thread-local kernel lifecycle sampler that records kernel enqueue/start/complete timestamps with a compact ring buffer; 2) per-kernel occupancy counters exposed as exponentially-bucketed HDR-style histograms; 3) a small annotation API to emit short-lived dashboard events (start/stop markers) that are mapped to Grafana variables in the observability-pack; 4) KV-cache probe points to record soft-hit vs hard-miss latencies and a per-request hit-sequence fingerprint to aid regression triage; 5) lightweight continuous-batching probes that capture batch size distributions and per-batch scheduling delays. Implementation is runtime-only and conditioned behind a runtime flag (--enable-runtime-sampler) so cost-free when disabled. The description below documents usage, compatibility, and verification steps. Usage: enable flag on dedicated/private and hosted dev clusters; sampler outputs to /var/run/redwood/sampler.sock (unix socket) and integrates with existing observability-exporter via a new microprofile exporter that ships
title: add-kernel-lifecycle-sampler-and-perf-dashboard-annotations

text: dedicated/private and hosted dev clusters; sampler outputs to /var/run/redwood/sampler.sock (unix socket) and integrates with existing observability-exporter via a new microprofile exporter that ships small JSON/line proto events. Backwards compatibility: the API is additive and feature-flag guarded; NVTX integration remains unchanged but now the sampler enriches NVTX ranges with kernel IDs for easier aggregation. Testing: unit tests for ring buffer semantics, integration test against perf-canary harness, and microbench added for 50/95/99 latency regression checks. Linked work: ENG-3284 (runtime observability), ENG-3340 (scheduling regressions). review_comments: Asha: requested lowering syscall frequency and exposing an opt-in exporter; Miguel: asked for clearer semantics around ringbuffer overflow and suggested HDR bucketing; Claire: asked for a smaller surface area for hosted runtime and added flag gating. Author replies: implemented batching of exporter writes, added overflow drop counter with sampling, and moved default to disabled. Follow-up: add rollout notes to Dedicated runbook. Final: approved after microbench shows 0.5% median wall-time overhead in non-production sample and clear improvements in 99th percentile scheduling latency on perf-canary. release_notes: Add runtime kernel lifecycle sampler and dashboard annotations (experimental, disabled by default); exposes KV-cache hit/miss probes; adds microbench for batch scheduling; configuration behind --enable-runtime-sampler
title: add-kernel-lifecycle-sampler-and-perf-dashboard-annotations

text: kernel lifecycle sampler and dashboard annotations (experimental, disabled by default); exposes KV-cache hit/miss probes; adds microbench for batch scheduling; configuration behind --enable-runtime-sampler flag; recommended for Debug/Canary deployments; benchmark_results: perf-canary baseline: 99p scheduling delay = 42ms perf-canary with sampler: 99p scheduling delay = 28ms (after tuning scheduler knobs) microbench overhead: median +0.5%, p99 +1.2% when sampler enabled testing: Unit tests: sampler ring buffer semantics and HDR helper; Integration: perf-canary runs with CI gating; E2E: deployed on internal dedicated cluster with flag enabled and exercised via continuous-batching workload; CI pipeline added to run microbench and report regression diffs. notes_for_ops: Sampler writes to unix socket and exporter batches events; when enabling at scale, set exporter batch_size to >=128 and sampling_rate to 0.01 to bound metric cardinality. Add grafana dashboard variable sampler_scope to filter annotations per deployment. Follow rollout plan in docs/observability/runtime-sampler.md.

text: description: Context: We have three SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go) drifting in ergonomics for retry presets and streaming lifecycle: consumers have inconsistent default backoff windows, token refresh races subtly leak stale bearer tokens during resume, and stream consumers have no canonical close/handoff semantics which makes deterministic cleanup tricky for upstream frameworks. Motivation: small, cross-language ergonomics wins reduce support burden and make tri-sdk examples portable. This PR introduces a compact, documented 'slim' retry presets bundle, a lightweight explicit stream-close API surfaced consistently across languages, and a defensive auth handshake change to avoid a token-swap race during stream resume. Summary of changes: 1) Add retry_presets.slim (spec) and implement bindings for py/ts/go. The presets provide three profiles (fast, balanced, conservative) with deterministic jitter math consistent across runtimes. 2) Introduce StreamHandle.close()/asyncClose() and CloseStream(ctx) to ensure the SDK flushes KV cache cursors and emits a final telemetry event; this is exposed as a simple sync/async method in each SDK. 3) Fix token-swap race: introduce a short-lived token window (50ms) while resuming streams and ensure outgoing requests observe the latest token via atomic swap. 4) Make small public API surface changes: add retry_policy=RetryPresets.SLIM on client constructors and a StreamOptions.auto_close boolean defaulting to false to avoid behavioral change for current
title: converge-slim-retries-and-explicit-stream-close-across-py-ts-go

text: swap. 4) Make small public API surface changes: add retry_policy=RetryPresets.SLIM on client constructors and a StreamOptions.auto_close boolean defaulting to false to avoid behavioral change for current users. 5) Add end-to-end tests for resume + token refresh, stream close idempotency, and cross-language parity asserts in the monorepo harness. 6) Bump versions: python -> 1.12.0, typescript -> 1.8.0, go -> 1.5.2. Migration notes: existing users are unaffected by default; to opt into the new presets pass retry_policy=RetryPresets.SLIM (or equivalent). If you rely on implicit stream finalization via GC, opt-in to StreamOptions.auto_close=true to enable explicit close hooks. Checklist: - [x] API design review with SDK leads - [x] Unit tests for each SDK - [x] E2E parity tests in harness - [x] Documentation snippets for migration - [x] Telemetry event added for stream.close - [x] Version bumps and changelogs updated Files of note changed: see changed_files list below. CI: first run failed due to a flaky ts test (race in mock timer), fixed by introducing deterministic fake timers in ts harness. All checks green on final run. Merge: squash-merged to main with canonical commit message below. Related: ENG-2048 (retry profile consolidation), ENG-2101 (stream lifecycle hygiene). review_comments: Arjun Patel: Overall design looks good. Two asks: (1) Can we document the precise jitter math here (exact formula) and add it to the spec? (2) The 50ms window feels magic — can we justify with
title: converge-slim-retries-and-explicit-stream-close-across-py-ts-go

text: description: Motivation: production regressions traced to complex interactions between kernel instruction sequences and KV-cache churn in continuous-batching workloads require finer telemetry than existing counters. This PR implements: (1) an instruction-code heatmap collector that buckets cycle counts by opcode id ranges and scheduler stage, (2) a KV-cache probe that emits sampled per-batch metadata (hit/miss ratio, top-key-size distribution, eviction events), (3) batch watermarks that tag scheduler events so we can map kernel time back to logical request boundaries under continuous batching, and (4) an NDJSON exporter (line-delimited JSON) that produces heatmap and probe artifacts compatible with our perf dashboard pipeline. Implementation details: low-overhead per-thread circular buffer guarded by relaxed atomics; sampling.rate and sampling.mode controls to trade fidelity vs overhead; CUDA-path uses device clock sample points when permitted, otherwise uses steady_high_res fallback on host. Exporter emits versioned payloads (heatmap_v1, kvcache_v1). A python microbench runner (bench/micro/instr_heatmap_runner.py) simulates prefix churn and long-lived contexts to validate correlation between opcode hotspots and p99 increases. Tests: unit tests for circular buffer safety, deterministic sampling tests, and an end-to-end perf-canary job that validates exporter schema and ingestion. Safety: feature is disabled by default, can be toggled via runtime flags and operator config; no
title: instruction-code heatmap + KV-probe + ndjson exporter for continuous-batching profiling

text: deterministic sampling tests, and an end-to-end perf-canary job that validates exporter schema and ingestion. text: release_notes: Improve installer release manifest generation: rotate artifact-level annotations for provenance, add environment-tagged manifest fragments for safer rollbacks, and bump Helm chart schema for v0.18.0. No user-facing breaking changes. description: Motivation: our private-installer release pipeline currently stamps artifact provenance in a single monolithic annotations blob which makes incremental rollforward/rollback brittle in air-gapped environments. This PR splits per-artifact annotation blobs, rotates annotation keys for KMS-backed signing metadata, and introduces a small manifest fragment assembler that produces environment-tagged release manifests (e.g. vpc-staging, onprem-aws, onprem-baremetal). Summary of changes: consolidated signing metadata serializer into scripts/signing/annotation_rotator.py; added a manifest fragment assembler at ci/release/manifest_fragments.go that merges signed shards into environment-specific release manifests; bumped helm/redwood-installer/Chart.yaml to apiVersion v2 and updated values schema for chart hooks used by upgrade/rollback; added Terraform module output to terraform/modules/installer/outputs.tf for release_manifest_ref to ease automation; added docs/docs/releases/manifest-rotation.md with operational runbook. Why this is different: instead of rotating a single index, we now maintain per-artifact annotation files alongside the release index so the installer can apply or rollback individual components without re-signing
title: Rotate artifact annotations and enhance release manifest assembly for private installer CI

text: different: instead of rotating a single index, we now maintain per-artifact annotation files alongside the release index so the installer can apply or rollback individual components without re-signing unrelated artifacts. The annotation_rotator supports retention windows and key version tags so KMS/COSIGN rotations are decoupled from manifest assembly. Testing: unit tests for annotation serializer and assembler integrated into CI; end-to-end pipeline run in staging created env-specific manifests and performed a dry-run install using the private-installer image. Verified artifact apply/rollback by toggling a single service manifest. See CI logs linked below. Rollout plan: merge to main; the release job will publish a v0.18.0-installer candidate to the internal bucket and publish environment-tagged manifests. Staged rollout to vpc-staging for 48h, then to onprem-aws. If anything fails, the manifest assembler supports a --rebuild=false mode to re-use last consistent fragments and we can perform immediate rollback by selecting the previous fragment tag. Notes: this PR touches signing utilities and release assembly; ops must have KMS key version available in staging before the next roll. Linear ENG-1972 contains the automated optional fallback to previous-key when cosign verification fails. Small illustrative snippet (annotation_rotator usage): $ python3 scripts/signing/annotation_rotator.py --key-version v2 --source-bucket releases/staging/2025-11-03 --out-prefix fragments/vpc-staging
title: Rotate artifact annotations and enhance release manifest assembly for private installer CI

text: description: Context: Multiple SDKs implemented similar ad-hoc auth handoffs for long-lived streams which caused duplication and subtle bugs around token refresh timing and callback ordering. Motivation: provide a small, explicit abstraction (session-stitcher) that centralizes token handoff and a companion compact stream facade that gives ergonomic async iterables/pullstreams across languages while keeping per-call surface minimal. This PR implements the stitcher in Python/TS/Go, wires it into existing streaming clients, harmonizes retry preset names across SDKs, and performs a coordinated minor version bump for each SDK. What changed (high-level): commits: 1) introduce session-stitcher abstraction and core interfaces (py/ts/go) 2) implement compact stream facade backed by existing transport layers 3) update retry presets and documentation 4) add cross-sdk integration tests and e2e CI pipeline job 5) version bumps and changelog entries. Files touched: key files listed above include small, targeted changes in each SDK and docs; most SDK changes are adapter layers and isolated helpers, not public surface breakages. Compatibility notes: default runtime behavior is unchanged. The stitcher is opt-in via a new client option (session_stitcher: true by default in next minor), and the compact stream facade is a convenience wrapper that delegates to the existing streaming transport. Existing call sites continue to work. If teams rely on custom auth callback ordering, they should validate
title: Introduce session-stitcher and compact stream facade across SDKs

text: compact stream facade is a convenience wrapper that delegates to the existing streaming transport. Existing call sites continue to work. Testing and CI: added unit and integration tests in each SDK and a dedicated e2e job that runs Python/TS/Go flow end-to-end against the test harness. Initial CI run failed on a flaky TS test (stream timeout) — fixed by adding a small prefetch/backoff in the facade and rerunning. Final CI status: pass. Actions: added deterministic token-race test in Python, expanded the docs with a migration note and examples, and applied consolidated names in presets.yaml. text: description: Motivation: On multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP) inference we observe intermittent comm stalls when large KV cache synchronization coincides with heavyweight collective traffic. These stalls inflate p95 latency and reduce sustained throughput for long-sequence workloads. This PR implements two coordinated runtime improvements: 1) an adaptive NCCL throttler that dynamically paces collective progress across TP ranks based on recent stream congestion metrics and bandwidth headroom estimates; 2) a KV-cache slicer that splits large KV synchronizations into size-aware shards and schedules them colocated with low-priority collectives so they can be interleaved with compute. Design: The NCCL throttler monitors per-stream counters (bytes_in_flight, op_latency_histogram) and computes a throttle_factor per-node which is translated into a lightweight MPI-like backoff token that delays lower-priority collectives for up to a configurable window. The KV-cache slicer computes shard boundaries keyed to tensor-parallel shard layout and sequence-length distribution, emitting incremental allgather fragments that respect ordering constraints. Both subsystems expose runtime feature flags and thresholds via the existing config layer so customer deployments can toggle aggressive vs conservative modes. Implementation: Changes are split across runtime/collectives (src/runtime/collectives/nccl_throttle.cc, nccl_throttle.h, test harnesses), runtime/kv_cache (src/runtime/kv_cache/slicer.{cc,h}),
title: adaptive-nccl-throttler-and-kvcache-slicing-to-reduce-comm-stalls-in-tp

text: modes. Implementation: Changes are split across runtime/collectives (src/runtime/collectives/nccl_throttle.cc, nccl_throttle.h, test harnesses), runtime/kv_cache (src/runtime/kv_cache/slicer.{cc,h}), and the interleaved dispatcher scheduler (src/runtime/scheduler/interleaved_dispatcher.cpp) which integrates token-based pacing. Added unit and integration tests (tests/unit/nccl_throttle_test.cc, tests/integration/tp_kvcache_slice.py) and a benchmark harness (benchmarks/tp_throughput/bench_throttle.py). Performance: Microbenchmarks on a 4xA100 TPU-equivalent cluster show 18–22% p95 latency reduction for opt-13b-like models at 2k sequence lengths under mixed request sizes; stress testing at 16 GPUs shows up to 32% throughput improvement in sustained QPS with long-context workloads. Rollout plan: behind feature flag 'adaptive_nccl_throttle' in main config, rollout to Dedicated clusters as an A/B experiment then opt-in for Hosted after 2 weeks of stability metrics. Rollback: feature flag off and fallback to existing static-priority collectives. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked and reviewed (ENG-4117) - [x] unit tests for throttle heuristics - [x] integration tests across 2/4/8 GPU configs - [x] perf harness and reproducible benchmark runbooks - [x] observability: expose throttle_factor and shard_progress metrics - [x] docs: short guide and recommended knobs (docs/perf/nccl_tuning.md) Related work: complements earlier NCCL stream coalescing and priority-stream experiments (see ENG-3782).
title: adaptive-nccl-throttler-and-kvcache-slicing-to-reduce-comm-stalls-in-tp

This PR is intentionally conservative about default thresholds to avoid unexpected perf regressions in heterogenous clouds. release_notes: Introduce an adaptive NCCL throttler and KV-cache slice manager to improve overlap of communication and compute on tensor-parallel workloads; exposes runtime knobs for aggressive/ conservative throttling; improves p95 latency and throughput on multi-GPU jobs; configurable rollback via feature flag. notes_for_operators: Feature is behind 'adaptive_nccl_throttle' flag and 'kvcache_slicing' flag. Recommended default: adaptive_nccl_throttle=false on heterogeneous clusters until validated; kvcache_slicing=true safe for most long-context workloads. Metrics introduced: runtime.throttle_factor, runtime.kvcache_shard_progress, runtime.bytes_in_flight_histogram. If regressions observed set both flags false and notify infra@redwood. bench_summary: 4xA100: p95 down 18–22% on long-context; 16-GPU sustained throughput +32% on targeted TP configs. Memory overhead: <1.5% for slice metadata; CPU scheduler overhead: ~3–6% at 1k qps which is offset by reduced stall time. manual_test_plan: 1) Enable feature flags in staging with small Dedicated cluster (4 GPUs). 2) Run bench_throttle on opt-13b-like model at 512/1024/2048 seq lengths. 3) Validate metrics for throttle_factor
title: adaptive-nccl-throttler-and-kvcache-slicing-to-reduce-comm-stalls-in-tp

Ivy Chen: Added retry with exponential backoff in the integration harness and tuned timeouts; re-ran CI and green. Security review (automated): no new secrets or network exposure. Approving for merge.

text: description: Motivation: We observed several regressions in production and in perf canaries where CUDA-graph capture/replay would cause large warmup token penalties when requests arrived with partially-populated KV caches or when continuous-batching created fragmented shapes. This PR introduces a conservative, deferred-capture strategy plus KV-cache alignment heuristics and scheduler adjustments to avoid capturing graphs too early and to stitch kernels deterministically when replay shape variance is detected. What this PR does (single-line description): Implement deferred graph capture, liveness-aware shape relaxation, and KV-cache alignment heuristics to reduce warmup regressions and stabilize replay in continuous-batching scenarios. Key changes (high-level): - runtime/cudagraphs/deferred_capture.cc: new coordinator that postpones final capture until a stable probe window has validated KV-cache priming and stable sequence-length distribution. - runtime/scheduler/token_priority.h/.cc: add token-aware scheduling to prefer priming requests for probes and ensure priming work doesn't evict hot entries. - runtime/kvcache/alignment.h/.cc: heuristics to align batch KV cache offsets across requests and to optionally inject short-priming micro-requests before capture. - runtime/cudagraphs/kernel_stitcher.cc: kernel stitching path to merge a deterministic set of kernels when replay shapes differ slightly (relaxed shape guards). - tests/ci/deterministic_replay_test.cpp: new deterministic
title: deferred-capture-liveness-and-kv-alignment-to-reduce-cudagraph-warmup-regressions

text: runtime/cudagraphs/kernel_stitcher.cc: kernel stitching path to merge a deterministic set of kernels when replay shapes differ slightly (relaxed shape guards). - tests/ci/deterministic_replay_test.cpp: new deterministic replay tests and a CI-mode warmup benchmark that runs in the matrix. - docs/observability/cudagraphs.md: documentation on telemetry emitted for deferred-capture decisions and warmup counters. Design notes: - Deferred capture: Instead of capturing as soon as a request first traverses the runtime, we now start in PROBE mode. PROBE gathers a small window of live requests (configurable, default 8 contiguous priming tokens) to validate the KV-cache priming state and sequence-length distribution. Only when the coordinator sees stable metrics (low shape variance, >75% requests with aligned KV offsets) does it perform final capture. This reduces captures that only see cold KV caches or fragmented batches. - KV alignment: For workloads that routinely put different KV offsets into the same batch (e.g., mixing chat turns with and without prior context), we introduce alignment: small, cheap prefix priming (a short run that populates KV entries) before capture or replay. The alignment is conservative (default off for small models) and can be toggled by model profile. - Liveness-based shape relaxation: Instead of strict shape guards that force fallback for any mismatch, we compute a liveness score and allow relaxed stitching for low-risk differences (e.g., a few tokens difference
title: deferred-capture-liveness-and-kv-alignment-to-reduce-cudagraph-warmup-regressions

text: Liveness-based shape relaxation: Instead of strict shape guards that force fallback for any mismatch, we compute a liveness score and allow relaxed stitching for low-risk differences (e.g., a few tokens difference at tail). This is implemented as a deterministic kernel-stitch path that merges replayed kernels to match the observed shape while preserving replay safety. - Scheduling: Token-priority scheduler ensures priming/probe requests are scheduled with slight priority so they can complete and stabilize the capture coordinator without causing large throughput regressions. Benchmarks: - Mixed-chat benchmark (50% cold-contex, 50% warm-context), p50 latency improved 8-12% post-merge across GPUs (A100, H100) at 32 concurrent streams. Warmup token count reduced from median 48->10 for probe-enabled captures. - Continuous-batching stress: 99th percentile stable; failure/replay incidences decreased from 4.7% of captures to 0.6% over 24h of synthetic traffic. Testing and CI: - Added deterministic replay tests to ensure reproducible capture/replay on CI GPUs; these run in the night matrix and are fast-mode for PRs. - Added local perf harness scripts used for the benchmark numbers. - CI initially failed on an unrelated linter (long line in docs) — fixed in follow-up commit. Risk and rollout: - Default config is conservative and off for small models; rollout plan: enable PROBE mode by default for models listed in model-profiles/perf.json and monitor telemetry (deferred_capture.* metrics).
title: deferred-capture-liveness-and-kv-alignment-to-reduce-cudagraph-warmup-regressions

text: - Default config is conservative and off for small models; rollout plan: enable PROBE mode by default for models listed in model-profiles/perf.json and monitor telemetry (deferred_capture.* metrics). - Rollback: feature gated via runtime flag (--cudagraph-deferred-capture=false) and model profile entries; metrics emit a non-impacting trace to trace capture decisions. Follow-ups: - Integrate with autoscaler to consider priming load when scaling up dedicated pools. - Telemetry-driven automatic tuning (future PR) to adjust probe window size per model. Related work: references to prior PLs and tickets included in commits and Linear tasks ENG-1842 (capture regressions) and ENG-1908 (kv cache stability). Commits summary: This is a squash PR that landed the following work in pieces: initial coordinator prototype, kernel-stitcher, KV alignment heuristics, scheduler changes, tests, and docs. Files changed (high-level): - runtime/cudagraphs/* (coordinator, stitcher, probes): ~450 additions, ~110 deletions - runtime/kvcache/* (alignment): ~220 additions, ~45 deletions - runtime/scheduler/* (priority logic): ~120 additions, ~30 deletions - tests/ci/* (deterministic replay + perf harness): ~90 additions - docs/observability/cudagraphs.md: ~60 additions Review conversation (highlights): - Miguel Santos (reviewer): "Great approach. My primary concern is ensuring we don't introduce head-of-line blocking due to probe priming. Can we cap the priming runtime and ensure probes are opportunistic?"
title: deferred-capture-liveness-and-kv-alignment-to-reduce-cudagraph-warmup-regressions

text: Miguel Santos (reviewer): "Great approach. My primary concern is ensuring we don't introduce head-of-line blocking due to probe priming. Can we cap the priming runtime and ensure probes are opportunistic?" - Asha Iyer (author): "Answered: probes are capped to 8 priming tokens by default and the scheduler will make priming requests low-impact (token-priority with backoff). Added tests to simulate backpressure." - Priya Kapoor (reviewer): "Please add a telemetry counter for 'probe_rejections' so we can see when alignment is skipped due to low coverage." - Asha Iyer: "Added probe_rejections counter and dashboard snippet in docs. Also added an e2e test that asserts counter increments on synthetic misalignment." - Evan Liu (approver): "Verified perf numbers in local harness on H100; approving pending CI green." - CI: initial build failed on clang-format; rerun fixed by formatting commit. Final CI matrix: linux/cpp: pass, linux/python: pass, night/perf: pass. Merge outcome: Squash-merged to main after approvals and green CI. Merged by Asha Iyer. Notes for on-call / SRE: If you see increased priming load causing capacity pressure, the temporary mitigation is to toggle --cudagraph-deferred-capture=false for the affected pool or reduce model-profile probe window to 2. Telemetry keys: deferred_capture.probes, deferred_capture.captures, deferred_capture.probe_rejections, kv_alignment.injected_priming. release_notes: Reduced CUDA-graph warmup token regression by introducing deferred capture
title: deferred-capture-liveness-and-kv-alignment-to-reduce-cudagraph-warmup-regressions

Improves p50 tail during steady-state by up to 12% in mixed batch workloads. No API changes.
title: deferred-capture-liveness-and-kv-alignment-to-reduce-cudagraph-warmup-regressions

text: description: Motivation: The OpenAI-compat layer has historically accepted a mix of Authorization header shapes (Bearer <key>, Redwood-Key: <key>, and project-scoped headers) which produced inconsistent behavior for scoped tokens and streaming websocket upgrades. This PR standardizes header semantics across the API surface (REST, streaming, and websocket upgrades), introduces scoped tokens for project/org scoping, and implements lightweight request signing with nonce headers to mitigate replay attacks for sensitive operations. Summary of changes: consolidated auth parsing into packages/openai-compat/src/auth.ts; introduce scoped-token parsing and enforcement; add request-signing middleware that validates X-Request-Nonce + X-Signature HMAC-SHA256 for high-risk endpoints (tool/function-calling, model upload, admin routes); update CORS preflight to expose new headers (X-Request-Nonce, X-Signature, X-Project-ID) and increase preflight cache for common clients; wiring for streaming endpoints and websocket upgrader to accept ephemeral scoped tokens and validate signatures on initial handshake; test and docs updates. Behavioral contract (externally visible): - Authorization header still accepts Bearer and Redwood-Key for backward compatibility. - New header X-Project-ID may be sent to route requests within org-scoped tokens; if absent and token is project-scoped the project is inferred from token payload. - Scoped tokens carry claims of the form: scope=project:<project-id>:roles=<comma-separated-roles>;
title: unify auth header semantics, add scoped tokens and request-signing for openai-compat

text: org-scoped tokens; if absent and token is project-scoped the project is inferred from token payload. - Scoped tokens carry claims of the form: scope=project:<project-id>:roles=<comma-separated-roles>; enforcement is checked per-route (serve/generate, tools.invoke, admin). - Streaming and websocket upgrade flows require the same signature checks as REST for operations that change state (tool calls, uploads). Non-state streaming (pure text generation) skips signature requirement but still supports scoped tokens. - CORS policy now exposes X-Request-Nonce, X-Signature, X-Project-ID and caches OPTIONS for 600s. Checklist: - [x] unify header parsing and normalization - [x] implement scoped-token format and enforcement stubs - [x] add request-signing middleware (nonce validation + HMAC check) - [x] extend streaming/ws auth paths to use new middleware - [x] update openai-compat shim to translate existing Authorization shapes to normalized internal auth context - [x] tests for walker (unit) and an integration test that covers streaming + tool-call handshake - [x] update docs and changelog snippets Testing and CI: initial push failed lint (missing docstring in auth util) and a flaky streaming test. Fixed by adding a clarifying doc, bumping a 200ms timeout in the integration harness, and pinning a test seed. CI then passed all suites including e2e-streaming. Rollout / compatibility: This change is backwards-compatible for existing API keys and most clients. Scoped tokens and signature enforcement
title: unify auth header semantics, add scoped tokens and request-signing for openai-compat

text: test seed. CI then passed all suites including e2e-streaming. Rollout / compatibility: This change is backwards-compatible for existing API keys and most clients. Scoped tokens and signature enforcement are opt-in per-route via feature flags controlled in the config (default: signatures off for non-admin routes). The openai-compat layer will log a deprecation notice when clients use legacy Redwood-Key without project scoping to encourage migration. Notes for reviewers: focus on the auth parsing normalization and websocket handshake validation; verify we don't accidentally require signatures for every streaming generate call (only stateful flows). The scoped-token parser is intentionally conservative; we only validate route-level permissions and leave further claim checks to downstream RBAC middleware. Related: ENG-9382 (design notes in Linear), SEC-127 (threat modeling), small follow-up will add UI for token minting and rotate nonces in the dedicated proxy. review_comments: Liam O'Connor: Please confirm the websocket upgrade path still accepts query-string tokens for legacy clients. If not, add a compatibility shim. Maya Patel: Added compatibility shim that translates query-token to Authorization header on initial handshake. Documented in docs/openai_compat_auth.md. Diego Ramirez: The nonce store needs a TTL / eviction policy to avoid unbounded growth. Can we use the existing redis rate-limiter bucket? Maya Patel: Wired nonce store to redis with 5 minute TTL; fallback to in-memory
title: unify auth header semantics, add scoped tokens and request-signing for openai-compat

text: description: Summary: This PR collapses multiple serializing codepaths into a compact zero-copy serializer and replaces static pinned worker pools with an adaptive eventloop pool that dynamically balances affinity and batching pressure. Motivation: we observed two CPU-side bottlenecks under mixed workloads: excessive memcpy/encoding churn in the request serializer and coarse-grained thread pinning that amplified latencies when concurrent request shapes changed. Those interactions prevented the KV cache prefetch hints and continuous batching heuristic from realizing throughput gains. Goal: reduce per-request CPU overhead, preserve cache locality where helpful, and allow batching heuristics to scale without manual thread pin fiddling. Changes made: 1) Introduced compact_serializer: a unified serializer layer that emits zero-copy message frames for common request shapes and falls back to full encoding for edge cases. The serializer integrates with the KV cache layer to annotate frames with prefetch keys so the runtime can start KV lookups while the request is still being assembled. 2) Replaced the fixed-size pinned worker pools with adaptive_eventloop_pool: each event loop now starts with a light-weight io/compute split and can grow/shrink workers per numa-domain based on observed queue depth, latency SLO pressure, and batch formation rate. The pool avoids premature pinning; threads are opportunistically pinned only when sustained CPU affinity advantages are measured. 3) Decoupled
title: Collapse serialization path and introduce adaptive eventloop pool

text: queue depth, latency SLO pressure, and batch formation rate. The pool avoids premature pinning; threads are opportunistically pinned only when sustained CPU affinity advantages are measured. 3) Decoupled serialization from response assembly: serialization tasks are now scheduled onto a small, low-latency serializer ring and can be executed inline for tiny requests to avoid enqueue/dequeue overhead. Larger requests use the ring to amortize copies. 4) KV cache hints and prefetch integration: serializer emits prefetch hints and an opt-in fast-path that requests KV lookups at frame creation time. We added a prefetch budget to avoid thundering when many frames are created. 5) Continuous batching improvements: made batch formation aware of the adaptive pool so that in high-concurrency scenarios batch timers are relaxed when threads are saturated, and in low-concurrency scenarios timers are tightened to reduce tail latency. 6) Kernel selection advisory tweak: the runtime now feeds short-lived request size histograms into the kernel selector to prefer lower-overhead kernels for sub-256 token runs. Testing and validation: microbenchmarks across representative workloads (token lengths 8, 64, 512; batch sizes 1..64) show median CPU-per-request reduction of 22% and end-to-end throughput improvements of 12-18% depending on model and hardware. p99 latency improved by 10-30% in mixed traffic scenarios that previously showed high variance due to thread migration. Load tests on dedicated pools
title: Collapse serialization path and introduce adaptive eventloop pool

text: improvements of 12-18% depending on model and hardware. p99 latency improved by 10-30% in mixed traffic scenarios that previously showed high variance due to thread migration. Load tests on dedicated pools with NUMA enabled confirm no regressions in long-running stability. Rollout plan: feature gated by opt-in config runtime.flags.compact_serializer and runtime.flags.adaptive_eventloop. We will enable behind a canary on 10% of inbound traffic for 48 hours and monitor p50/p95/p99 latency, kernel selection changes, batch size distribution, KV hit/prefetch ratios, and CPU usage per-core. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. The serializer keeps the wire format identical; fallbacks ensure edge cases behave as before. Notes about safety: added safeguards to avoid unbounded prefetching and to revert thread pinning when it increases tail latency. Observability: new metrics added - serializer.frames_zero_copy, serializer.inline_hits, eventloop.pool_size, eventloop.pin_rate, kv.prefetch_attempts, kv.prefetch_evictions. Follow-ups: plan to wire these into the Console rollout charts and add a regression eval for small-request regressions in the eval-harness. release_notes: Reduced CPU path overhead for small/medium requests: compact serializer + adaptive eventloop pool yield improved throughput and lower p99 latency. No API changes. review_comments: Alejandro: Can we avoid allocating the frame metadata on the hot path? Maya: Yes, replaced it with an inline tag for the common case; fallback
title: Collapse serialization path and introduce adaptive eventloop pool

text: throughput and lower p99 latency. No API changes. review_comments: Alejandro: Can we avoid allocating the frame metadata on the hot path? Maya: Yes, replaced it with an inline tag for the common case; fallback uses heap metadata. Priya: The adaptive pool seems to oscillate on the second load pattern. Please add hysteresis. Maya: Added 250ms stabilization window and unit tests to prove no oscillation. Samir: Request to add a safety cap to KV prefetch budget to avoid thundering during spikes. Implemented with per-node budget and integrated into metrics. Alejandro: Approved after CI green and perf numbers verified on benchmark-lab.
title: Collapse serialization path and introduce adaptive eventloop pool

text: description: Motivation: Small-batch, low-concurrency inference workloads (PLG webhooks, customer assistants, SDK local tests) spend disproportionate time in QK preparation and rotary normalization. Existing FlashAttention variants provide excellent throughput at high batch sizes but miss a low-latency fast-path for eager QK fusion + device-side rotary normalization while streaming KV micro-batches. This PR introduces an alternative fast-path designed to reduce per-request token latency and cost for 1-8 request micro-batches while preserving compatibility with our continuous batching and KV caching stack. What this PR does (high-level): Adds an "eager QK fuse" kernel that fuses pre-softmax QK matmuls, rotary application, and small-size softmax into a single GPU kernel for short sequences; introduces a device-side rotary-normalization helper to eliminate a host-side normalizer roundtrip; implements a streaming-KV micro-scheduler that pipeline-evicts and re-inserts small KV chunks to keep GPU memory pressure low while preserving prefix cache hits; and exposes a runtime flag to enable the small-batch fast-path (off by default). Design notes: The eager QK kernel is optimized for typical interactive sequence lengths (<=128) and request micro-batch sizes (1..8). It avoids large temporary allocations by writing QK and rotary outputs directly into shared/global memory pages suitable for the fused softmax. The micro-scheduler allows the runtime to (a) detect small-batch latency-sensitive
title: Eager-QK fuse + rotary normalization fast-path and streaming-KV micro-scheduler

text: allocations by writing QK and rotary outputs directly into shared/global memory pages suitable for the fused softmax. The micro-scheduler allows the runtime to (a) detect small-batch latency-sensitive flows, (b) allocate a limited number of fast-path slots per GPU, and (c) progressively fall back to the standard FlashAttention path when contention rises. The implementation is backward-compatible: the canonical KV cache layout and the public inference API remain unchanged; the kernel is selected at scheduling time. Compatibility and safety: Feature gated behind runtime config `enable_eager_qk_fastpath` (cluster and per-model override). We add conservative checks for sequence length, dtype (fp16/nbits8 supported), and KV layout. The code contains a disabled-but-available emulation path for CI that runs on CPU to validate numerical equivalence on small examples. Performance summary (microbenchmarks, internal): For a 1-4 request micro-batch with seq_len=64 on A100-80GB: median tail latency reduced by 18% (single-token step) and 22% p50 for generation with small prompt prefixes; aggregate token throughput improves 7-12% on these small-batch regimes. For larger batches (>=32) there is no regression — scheduler prefers FlashAttention path. End-to-end e2e tests showed negligible output diffs (max rel error < 1e-4 for fp16 baseline). Files changed (high-level): runtime/attention/eager_qk_kernel.cu, runtime/attention/kernels.h, runtime/scheduler/micro_scheduler.cc, runtime/kv/streaming_eviction.cc,
title: Eager-QK fuse + rotary normalization fast-path and streaming-KV micro-scheduler

text: From: Alyssa Chen <alyssa.chen@cascadefg.com> To: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@cascadefg.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 3, 2025 at 9:12 AM Subject: Escalation: rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage (Redwood Private) Markus, We’re preparing our next Redwood Private upgrade in our VPC environment and need written clarity on two items before we’ll schedule the maintenance window: 1) Rollback guarantees: if the upgrade fails (including DB migration issues), can we roll back to the prior version and what is the expected time to restore service? We need something more concrete than “best effort.” 2) Support coverage: we will be doing this in our standard maintenance window (Saturday 1:00–4:00am ET). We need to know if Redwood will have engineering/SRE coverage during the window, and what escalation path exists if rollback is required. We’ve had issues with other vendors where “rollback” meant “rebuild the cluster,” which is not acceptable for us. Please respond by EOD Thursday so we can confirm the window with our change advisory board. Thanks, Alyssa — Alyssa Chen Director, Platform Operations Cascade Financial Group From: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com> To: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>,
title: Escalation: Rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage for Redwood Private (Cascade Financial Group)

title: Escalation: Rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage for Redwood Private (Cascade Financial Group)

text: <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 3, 2025 at 10:47 AM Subject: Re: Fwd: Escalation: rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage (Cascade) For support coverage: - We can offer “upgrade window coverage” as a planned event: open an internal incident bridge at start of window, have SRE on-call (Rafael) + one release eng point (Connor or Devon) on standby. - For a Sat 1–4am ET window, we can commit to: ack within 15 minutes on the bridge + continuous coverage until upgrade completes or rollback is confirmed. - If they want this as a contractual commitment, it needs to be captured as a paid premium support addendum / named escalation path, but for this upcoming window we can do it as a one-off (assuming 48h notice and precheck outputs shared beforehand). I’d avoid using the word “guarantee” on RTO/RPO. We can give typical timings and what determines rollback time (snapshot restore vs logical restore).
