title: Escalation: Rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage for Redwood Private (Cascade Financial Group)

text: Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 3, 2025 at 11:26 AM Subject: Re: Fwd: Escalation: rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage (Cascade) On rollback semantics (what we can say today): - Supported upgrade path is N-1 → N for Redwood Private. We do not currently support skipping major versions without a review. - Rollback is supported to the previously running version provided: (a) the upgrade hasn’t crossed a non-reversible schema boundary, and (b) the recommended backups/snapshots were taken before applying migrations. - Our current guidance: take a pre-upgrade backup of the control-plane DB + config state; if on EBS/CSI, take a volume snapshot; if managed DB, take a point-in-time snapshot. - For migration failures: we run migrations with guardrails (lock timeouts, idempotent steps), but in the event of a failure we can roll back application components immediately and then restore DB to the pre-upgrade snapshot. What I’d like to avoid: “guaranteeing” a fixed rollback time without knowing their storage/backend. We can give a typical range and define prerequisites (snapshot ready, prechecks clean, artifacts available). If helpful, we can offer a rollback drill in staging prior to prod. Vivek — Vivek Kulkarni Product, Private Deployments Redwood Inference From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com> To: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>,
title: Escalation: Rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage for Redwood Private (Cascade Financial Group)

text: <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com> To: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 3, 2025 at 12:05 PM Subject: Re: Fwd: Escalation: rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage (Cascade) Two notes for the written response: 1) Please avoid hard “guarantees” unless it’s in their contract. Use “supported rollback procedure” and “expected restoration time is typically X–Y given prerequisites.” 2) Since they’re regulated, mention auditability: we can provide an upgrade report (precheck results, timestamps for backup start/complete, and change summary). If they require SIEM evidence, we can provide audit log entries for upgrade start/stop + rollback invocation in our newer Private releases (verify what version they are on / targeting). text: <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com> Date: Wed, Jun 4, 2025 at 9:18 AM Subject: Draft response to Cascade (rollback + support coverage) All — draft below. Please reply with edits/approvals. If no objections by 2pm PT I’ll send. --- Alyssa, Thanks — understood on the need for operational clarity. Rollback (supported procedure) - For Redwood Private, we support in-place upgrades on the supported path (N-1 → N). As part of the upgrade workflow, Redwood requires a pre-upgrade backup/snapshot of the control-plane database and configuration state. - If an upgrade fails (including during database migrations), the supported remediation is: 1) stop/roll back the application components to the prior running version, and 2) restore the control-plane database from the pre-upgrade snapshot (if migrations were applied and need to be reverted). - In environments with pre-upgrade snapshots in place, service restoration is typically on the order of tens of minutes. Exact timing depends on the snapshot mechanism (managed DB PITR vs volume snapshot) and the size of the database. Upgrade window support coverage - We can staff your planned maintenance window (Sat 1:00–4:00am ET) with Redwood support + SRE escalation. - Proposed plan: you share precheck outputs and the target version 48 hours prior; we open
title: Escalation: Rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage for Redwood Private (Cascade Financial Group)

text: coverage - We can staff your planned maintenance window (Sat 1:00–4:00am ET) with Redwood support + SRE escalation. - Proposed plan: you share precheck outputs and the target version 48 hours prior; we open a live bridge at the start of the window; Redwood SRE/release escalation is available throughout until completion or rollback confirmation. Auditability - We can provide an upgrade report containing precheck results and timestamps for backup/upgrade steps. If required, we can also provide audit log entries that reflect upgrade start/stop and rollback invocation (version dependent). If you’d like, we can also run a rollback drill in a non-production environment prior to the production change window. --- Markus From: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com> To: Alyssa Chen <alyssa.chen@cascadefg.com> Cc: Tom Becker <tom.becker@cascadefg.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com> Bcc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com> Date: Wed, Jun 4, 2025 at 4:38 PM Subject: Re: Escalation: rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage (Redwood Private) Attachments: Redwood-Private-Upgrade-Rollback-Guidance-DRAFT-2025-06-04.pdf (application/pdf) Alyssa, Thanks — understood on the need for operational clarity before you take this to CAB. text: From: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwoodinference.com> To: Kevin Osei <kevin_osei@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Allison Grant <allison_grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 10, 2025 at 9:12 AM Subject: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention Attachments: redwood-private-upgrades-audit-events-v0.3.xlsx Team, Looping you in on auditability requirements coming in from Cascade Financial (regulated VPC private). They’re explicitly tying upgrade/rollback audit logs to their change-management controls. What they’re asking for (summary): - Upgrade/rollback audit logs must be tamper-evident (immutability expectation). For Private they’ll accept “immutable in customer-controlled SIEM” as long as Redwood components can reliably forward/export. - Retention expectation: 13 months minimum for audit events (their policy is 400 days). They want confirmation on default retention and whether it’s configurable. - Event detail level: they want more than “upgrade started/stopped.” They want step-level outcomes for prechecks, backups, migrations, and rollback invocation. - Correlation: must correlate to change tickets + an operator identity (SSO) and the target environment/cluster. I attached our current proposed event list + fields (v0.3). Can you review and confirm what we can commit to for
title: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention

Open questions I need answers on by EOW for the customer: 1) Can we support 400-day retention in the Private control plane audit store? If not, what’s our supported export story + how quickly after event creation do we deliver? 2) Which identity fields are available in Private upgrades (SSO user id vs service account)? 3) Can we guarantee we emit the same event schema across VPC + on-prem (air-gapped) with a stable versioned schema? Two clarifications from our side: 1) We need audit logs for upgrade lifecycle events retained for 400 days. If Redwood doesn’t retain that long, we need a supported mechanism to forward the logs to Splunk (HEC) and/or syslog in near-real time. 2) For each upgrade attempt, our auditors typically ask for: - who initiated it (SSO identity) - what was changed (from version/to version, artifacts)
title: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention

text: to Splunk (HEC) and/or syslog in near-real time. 2) For each upgrade attempt, our auditors typically ask for: - who initiated it (SSO identity) - what was changed (from version/to version, artifacts) - when it happened - whether the backup completed successfully before changes were applied - whether rollback was invoked, and why We can be flexible on the transport (Splunk HEC vs syslog), but we can’t accept “logs only visible in UI for 30/90 days.” Regards, Alicia Morgan Director, Security & Risk Cascade Financial Group From: Kevin Osei <kevin_osei@redwoodinference.com> To: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison_grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 10, 2025 at 10:41 AM Subject: Re: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention Irene — makes sense and matches what we’ve been hearing. Security/compliance POV: - Retention: For Private, we should not position the Redwood control plane as the system-of-record for 400-day retention unless we’re 100% confident in sizing + backup/restore of the audit store. I recommend: (a) default retention (e.g., 30/90 days) in-product for operator convenience, AND (b) a supported export/forwarding mechanism to customer SIEM with guidance that customer SIEM satisfies 400-day requirement. - Immutability: We
title: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention

text: (e.g., 30/90 days) in-product for operator convenience, AND (b) a supported export/forwarding mechanism to customer SIEM with guidance that customer SIEM satisfies 400-day requirement. - Immutability: We can say Redwood emits append-only audit events and supports export; tamper-evidence is achieved by forwarding to an immutable destination under customer control (Splunk index with WORM policy, etc.). - Schema: Strong preference for a versioned schema and explicit event_version field. Field-level requirements (minimum set): - event_id (UUID) - event_type (enum) - event_version - occurred_at (RFC3339) - environment_id / cluster_id - upgrade_id (stable across an attempt) - initiated_by (subject id) + initiated_by_type (oidc_user | service_account) - request_source (cli | ui | api) and client_ip (if available) - target_version + source_version - result (success | failure) and error_code / error_summary Nice-to-have (but probably required for regulated accounts): - artifact_digests (sha256 for bundle/chart/images) - backup_id + backup_result + backup_location_ref (not actual secrets) - rollback_reason + rollback_target_version I’ll defer to Allison/Connor on where/how we emit/retain today. text: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Kevin Osei <kevin_osei@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 10, 2025 at 12:09 PM Subject: Re: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention Adding observability specifics + what’s realistic for GA. Where events should be emitted: 1) Control plane API: authoritative events for “upgrade attempt created,” “precheck summary,” “backup completed,” “rollout started/completed,” “rollback invoked/completed.” 2) Installer (on-prem and VPC operator CLI): should emit locally too (stdout + local file) and optionally forward to the control plane when connected. Export story (recommendation): - Provide an “audit sink” configuration for Private: - sink_type: splunk_hec | syslog_tls | otlp_http (if we want future-proof) - endpoint, tls config, auth token ref (in secret), batching + retry - Sinks run inside the customer’s environment, so this works for VPC and for on-prem (including air-gapped if they have internal Splunk/syslog). For fully disconnected on-prem, we at least have deterministic local file logs that can be rotated + handed to auditors. Retention: - I agree with Kevin: don’t promise 400 days in Redwood storage. We can specify: - Default in-product retention: 30 days (subject to change) - Optional extended retention add-on for hosted
title: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention

text: Retention: - I agree with Kevin: don’t promise 400 days in Redwood storage. We can specify: - Default in-product retention: 30 days (subject to change) - Optional extended retention add-on for hosted offerings — but for Private, make it clear the customer SIEM is the long-term store. Event granularity: - Cascade is going to want step-level outcomes. That implies we need events for: - precheck.<check_name>.pass/fail (or a single event with an array, but arrays complicate schema; better emit per-check w/ correlation_id) - backup.start/complete - migration.start/complete (duration_ms) - helm_phase.control_plane.start/complete and helm_phase.data_plane.start/complete Note: Your attached spreadsheet (v0.3) includes “steps” as a list. That’s clean conceptually, but it’s a nested payload and some SIEMs are annoying with parsing. If we keep both (structured JSON + flattened key fields) we’ll reduce pain. Allison — Allison Grant Observability & Audit Signals Redwood Inference From: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwoodinference.com> To: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwoodinference.com> Cc: Allison Grant <allison_grant@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin_osei@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi_feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com> Date: Tue, Jun 10, 2025 at 2:22 PM Subject: Re: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention +1 to sink-based export. title: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention

text: Date: Tue, Jun 10, 2025 at 2:22 PM Subject: Re: Private Upgrades: audit log requirements (enterprise stakeholders) — event fields + retention +1 to sink-based export. Release Eng constraints / commitments I’m comfortable with for GA: - Stable event schema with event_version and a published schema doc (even if minimal) and changelog. No breaking changes without version bump. - Correlation IDs: - upgrade_id: generated at plan execution start - plan_id: deterministic hash of the plan artifact (so support can tie back to exact steps) - release_id: Redwood Private release train identifier (maps installer + charts + control plane build) - Artifact integrity: - We can include sha256 digests for the upgrade bundle (on-prem) and chart/image digests where available. (On VPC, image digests are easiest; on-prem bundles: digest is mandatory.) - Retention statement: - “Control plane retains audit events for operational troubleshooting (30 days default). Customers must configure an export sink for long-term retention.” If Cascade pushes back on 30 days: we can consider making it configurable up to N days based on disk sizing, but I’d rather not commit before we measure impact. ["From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>

Subject: Private upgrades: beta plan w/ design partners (VPC + on-prem) — approvals + timeline
Attachments: Private_Upgrades_Beta_Plan_v0.3.pdf

Connor / Irene / Markus —

I’m proposing we start the beta for the new Private upgrade + rollback workflow with 2 design partners (1 VPC, 1 on-prem/air-gapped-ish) and I need alignment + explicit approvals on the gating/support plan.

Design partner candidates (already engaged):
- Cascade Financial Group (VPC Private) — regulated, cares about maintenance windows + rollback guarantees
- Northstar Health Systems (on-prem) — strong audit requirements, offline artifact mirror constraints

Beta scope (what we will support):
- N-1 → N upgrades only
- Installer-generated upgrade plan + preflight checks (blocking + warning)
- Automated backup orchestration (snapshot where possible + logical backup fallback)
- Deterministic rollback entry points (control-plane first; data-plane gated)
- Audit log events for upgrade lifecycle + support bundle inclusion

Proposed timeline (aggressive but doable if we keep scope tight):
- Week of May 19: internal dogfood in staging + rollback drill (one “planned failure”)
- Week of May 27: design partner dry-run (prechecks + backup verification)
title: Private upgrades: beta plan w/ design partners (VPC + on-prem) — approvals + timeline

text:
but doable if we keep scope tight):
- Week of May 19: internal dogfood in staging + rollback drill (one “planned failure”)
- Week of May 27: design partner dry-run (prechecks + backup verification) + schedule windows
- Week of June 3: beta “go” for Cascade (VPC)
- Week of June 10: beta “go” for Northstar (on-prem)
- Late June: GA readiness review (PM-812 checklist)

Key risks I want to call out up front:
1) Precheck strictness: storage headroom + DB connectivity checks will block some upgrades. We need crisp messaging and an explicit “override policy” decision.
2) Backup backend coverage: EBS snapshots are straightforward; CSI/on-prem varies. We need a clear supported matrix and how we degrade gracefully.
3) Version compatibility enforcement: we need a single enforcement point (installer vs control plane) and an escape hatch only via explicit approval.
4) Support coverage during beta: who is on point during the upgrade window (and what the escalation path is)?

Approvals/asks:
- Connor: OK to proceed with beta contingent on Release Eng gating (what are the minimum test signals you require before we ship the beta bits)?
- Irene: OK on infra/backups readiness + any non-negotiable prerequisites for Northstar’s on-prem environment.
- Markus: Confirm both accounts can commit to maintenance windows + agree to a rollback drill.

If we agree on the above, I’ll schedule a 30-min go/no-go next week and lock the beta dates with the customers.

Thanks,
Vivek

Vivek

Vivek —

I’m supportive, but I want to be very explicit about what “go” means from Release Eng.

Minimum gating signals for beta bits (not GA):
1) Upgrade E2E (N-1→N) green in CI for: one VPC scenario + one on-prem-ish scenario (offline bundle path). I’m fine with a reduced matrix for beta as long as it’s *repeatable*.
2) Version pinning is enforced (Terraform var + installer validation). No “floating latest” for beta customers.
3) Rollback drill: we need to demonstrate we can rollback from a partial failure (e.g., control plane upgrade succeeded but a data plane stage is intentionally failed).
4) Clear release artifact mapping: installer version ↔ platform version. No ambiguity for Support.

On the “escape hatch”: I do not want a general override flag exposed to customers. If we absolutely need an override, it should require a signed-off approval chain

To: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>

Date: Wed, May 14, 2025 at 2:26 PM PT
Subject: Re: Private upgrades: beta plan w/ design partners (VPC + on-prem) — approvals + timeline

+1 to running the beta, but we should set expectations on the backup story for on-prem.

Infra/backups notes:
- VPC (Cascade): We can use EBS snapshots for the stateful bits (where we control the storage class) + keep the logical DB dump option as a fallback.
-
title: Private upgrades: beta plan w/ design partners (VPC + on-prem) — approvals + timeline

text:
backup story for on-prem.

Infra/backups notes:
- VPC (Cascade): We can use EBS snapshots for the stateful bits (where we control the storage class) + keep the logical DB dump option as a fallback.
- On-prem (Northstar): we *cannot* assume snapshots. Some clusters will have CSI snapshot support, some won’t. We need to be comfortable with “logical backup only” as the supported baseline for beta.

I strongly recommend we make “backup verification” a first-class step before attempting the upgrade:
- Take backup
- Restore into a scratch namespace / validate checksums + DB connectivity
- Only then proceed with upgrade

Risks / prerequisites for Northstar specifically:
- Storage headroom precheck will likely be the #1 blocker. We should provide remediation steps in plain language and *not* let folks override without talking to us.
- Offline artifact supply chain: we need checksums/signatures verified and a clear error message when the bundle is wrong/missing. If that’s not fully baked, I’d rather not start on-prem beta.
- Security: they will ask about encryption at rest and key ownership for backups. We should align with Naomi/Marcus on what we can claim in beta vs GA.

From my side: OK to proceed if we treat the on-prem track as “beta with known constraints” and Markus is confident we can get the environment requirements nailed down early (K8s version, storage, registry/mirror details).

Irene

Redwood Inference

Thanks — this is helpful. I updated the beta plan with the constraints + gating callouts (v0.4 attached).

Proposed decisions (unless someone strongly disagrees):
1) No customer-facing override flag in beta. Any override (if we end up needing it) requires explicit internal approval + is logged.
2) Beta “minimum viable” test signals = 1 VPC E2E + 1 offline-bundle path E2E. Reduced matrix is acceptable for beta; expand for GA.
3) On-prem baseline backup = logical backup + verification step. Snapshots supported opportunistically where CSI supports it.

Blocking vs warning prechecks (beta proposal):
BLOCKING
- K8s cluster health (nodes ready, kube-system critical
title: Private upgrades: beta plan w/ design partners (VPC + on-prem) — approvals + timeline

text:
backup + verification step. Snapshots supported opportunistically where CSI supports it.

Blocking vs warning prechecks (beta proposal):
BLOCKING
- K8s cluster health (nodes ready, kube-system critical pods)
- DB connectivity + migrations lock timeout sanity check
- Storage headroom below threshold (configurable threshold; default 20% free)
- Artifact availability + checksum verification (incl. offline bundles)
- RBAC/service account permissions for installer operations

WARNING
- OIDC reachability (warn + link remediation)
- DNS/TLS validations (warn if non-fatal; block only if control plane endpoints fail)
- Region connectivity drift (warn)

Next steps / asks:
- Connor: can you confirm by Tue (May 20) that you’re comfortable cutting beta artifacts the week of May 27 assuming the May 19 drill is successful? If not, what’s the hard block.
- Irene: can you sanity-check the on-prem prerequisites section and tell me what we should make “must have before scheduling” vs “nice to have”? (I pulled in your notes.)
- Markus: can you confirm both Cascade + Northstar can commit to (a) 2-hour maintenance window, (b) named on-call contact, and (c) rollback drill during beta?

I’ll put a 30-min go/no-go on calendars for Wed May 21 AM PT.

Vivek

['From: infra-team@orpheus-corp.com
To: jonas_weber@redwood.com, lauren_bishop@redwood.com
Cc: sre@orpheus-corp.com
Date: 2026-09-18T08:14:00-07:00
Subject: Post-cutover: large file uploads failing -> 502s (initial alert)

Hi team,

We flipped the private-connect cutover at 04:30 PDT this morning. Since then our clients report intermittent 502s and stalled multipart uploads to our onprem service. The issue started immediately after we updated the route to hairpin traffic through the provider transit (temporary design decision).

Attached is a tiny pcap extract showing a client TCP retransmit followed by RSTs. We also see a pattern of many simultaneous connections from the same NATted IP and a spike in ephemeral port reuse.

Quick facts:
- Affected path: client -> provider transit -> our VPC edge -> back to provider (hairpin)
- Symptoms: stalled uploads, 502s from edge LB, increased client timeouts
- Logs: 502s with upstream timeout & short-lived backend connections

Attachments: orpheus_upload_failures.pcap.txt (pcap stub)

Can Redwood help triage? We can provide full dumps if needed.

--
J. Miller (Orpheus infra)
infra-team@orpheus-corp.com

To: lauren_bishop@redwood.com, karthik_iyer@redwood.com, vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com

Subject: Re: Post-cutover: large file uploads failing -> 502s (initial alert)

Team — putting this in inbox, we should treat as high-impact customer outage (Orpheus).
title: Orpheus cutover - hairpin SNAT + MTU mismatch causing upload stalls

- Upstream LB timeouts / short-lived backend dial failures

Quick findings from the edge we control (collected 08:50-09:00):
- conntrack count jumped from ~12k -> 94k for src 10.40.0.0/22 (Orpheus NAT pool). Many entries show same src/dst with different
title: Orpheus cutover - hairpin SNAT + MTU mismatch causing upload stalls

text:
alert)

Quick findings from the edge we control (collected 08:50-09:00):
- conntrack count jumped from ~12k -> 94k for src 10.40.0.0/22 (Orpheus NAT pool). Many entries show same src/dst with different sport but sport cycling back to same ephemeral values.
- netstat shows ephemeral ports hitting upper bound: /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range = 32768 60999 (current ephemeral usage near 60k)
- tcpdump shows multiple fragments with DF=0, and occasional DSACKs on retransmit followed by upstream RSTs. Notably we see MSS clamped to 1460 on initial SYN (no provider clamp).

Commands/output (trimmed):
# conntrack -L | grep 10.40.
... many entries ...
# ss -s
TCP: inuse 98, orphan 0, tw 12
# cat /proc/net/nf_conntrack | wc -l
94521

Interpretation: hairpin path causes all client flows to be SNATed at our edge; with high concurrency we hit ephemeral port pressure and port reuse leads to mid-session collisions. Fragmentation on the route (likely lower MTU through provider transit) increases retransmits.

Immediate mitigations I can apply (30m risk window):
1) Temporarily increase ip_local_port_range to 10240 65535 (low risk for short window).
2) Apply MSS clamp to 1420 on the hairpin interface (iptables --clamp-mss-to-pmtu).
3) Add connection tracking timeout adjustments for ESTABLISHED flows if we see long tails.

Longer term: request provider to add direct non-hairpin route for Orpheus heavy upload CIDRs or use source-based routing to avoid double-NAT.

I'll
title: Orpheus cutover - hairpin SNAT + MTU mismatch causing upload stalls

text:
adjustments for ESTABLISHED flows if we see long tails.

Longer term: request provider to add direct non-hairpin route for Orpheus heavy upload CIDRs or use source-based routing to avoid double-NAT.

I'll hold off on changing ephemeral range until we coordinate with Orpheus SRE (they may need to avoid conflicting NAT windows).

-karthik
Senior Network Engineer

Subject: Re: Post-cutover: large file uploads failing -> 502s (initial alert)

J. / Orpheus SRE — quick update and a playbook proposal.

We’ve seen two contributing factors: 1) SNAT port pressure at our edge due to hairpinning, and 2) an MTU/fragmentation mismatch on the transit leg where DF=0 fragmented frames are being dropped by a provider firewall.

Recommended immediate plan (canary then full):
- Step 0 (canary): Apply MSS-clamp 1420 on the VPC edge for Orpheus CIDR and increase ephemeral range on the edge hosts behind a maintenance window (coordinate time).
- Step 1 (if canary mitigates): Request provider firewall team to allow fragmented frames OR ensure PMTU discovery on that path (they asked us to avoid fragments historically).
- Step 2 (if intermittent persists): Provision a temporary static route at provider to steer Orpheus upload CIDRs directly to our VPC (bypass hairpin) — provider must confirm route acceptance.

Owners:
title: Orpheus cutover - hairpin SNAT + MTU mismatch causing upload stalls

text:
Step 2 (if intermittent persists): Provision a temporary static route at provider to steer Orpheus upload CIDRs directly to our VPC (bypass hairpin) — provider must confirm route acceptance.

Owners: Redwood network (karthik) to implement MSS + ephemeral change; Orpheus infra to coordinate provider routing change; I’ll own rollout and canary monitoring.

We need packet captures from both sides: client-to-provider and edge ingress. If Orpheus can share the pcap (they attached a small extract), please send the full capture (pcap) to us.

-Lauren
Head of Deployments, Redwood Inference

Subject: Post-discovery: cross-region language risk assessment & next steps

Thanks again for your time today — useful call. Quick recap oriented around the specific language risks you flagged and our suggested next steps so we can scope a small pilot.

Key takeaways from the call:
- Priority locales: EU (IT, FR, DE) and APAC (JA, KO).
- Primary concerns: correctness for finance-specific terms, hallucination on regulatory language, and latency targets for JA/KR flows.
- Data residency & PII handling will be a gating requirement for procurement.

Our immediate recommendations for an evaluation pilot:
1) Two model variants to compare in a 2-week pilot:
 - Variant A: redwood/l10n-small-v2 (optimized for locale prompts; 8k context)
 - Variant B: redwood/large-general-quant-8bit (higher-capacity quantized variant for throughput)
2) Targeted prompt sets: we attached finserv_eval_promptset_v1.csv with 120 locale-specific prompts (IT/FR/DE/JA/KO). Please review and flag any customer-owned phrases to redact.
3) Benchmarks: initial topline summary attached (benchmarks_eu_apac_summary.pdf). We ran small dev runs and saw elevated hallucination rates in JA for currency/IBAN examples versus FR/DE.
4) Mitigations to test in pilot: prefix KV
title: Post-discovery: cross-region language risk assessment & next steps

text:
summary attached (benchmarks_eu_apac_summary.pdf). We ran small dev runs and saw elevated hallucination rates in JA for currency/IBAN examples versus FR/DE.
4) Mitigations to test in pilot: prefix KV caching for repeated templates, localized few-shot exemplars, and soft-constrained decoding for numeric tokens.

Questions for you to confirm so we can scope pricing and schedule:
- Do you require EU-only hosting for the pilot (data residency)?
- Do you have a PO window or procurement lead we should loop in?
- Any additional locales to include?

Proposed next steps (if you approve):
- We kick off a 2-week canary (instrumented telemetry + eval harness) next Monday.
- Deliver interim results after 5 business days and a short readout at the end.

Tonight we'll produce: 1) a one-page annotated dataflow (PDF), 2) a short matrix on token persistence, and 3) KMS custody language.\\n\\nQuick expectations:\\n- The dataflow will show ingestion -> transient-tokenizer -> isolated inference enclave -> ephemeral KV cache -> result egress.\\n- Transient tokens are held in-memory by the runtime and flushed to ephemeral encrypted caches per node; durable persistence is opt-in and documented.\\n- Cross-border routing is region-policy driven; customers can pin processing to specific regions or block egress.\\n\\nWe'll attach the first-pass to a follow-up email once Rishi confirms the technical bits.\\n\\nBest,\\nGrace O'Connor\\nHead of Customer Privacy (Redwood Inference)\\n+1 415-555-0132\\n", 'From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>\\nTo: Grace O\'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>\\nDate:

text: Privacy (Redwood Inference)\\n+1 415-555-0132\\n", 'From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>\\nTo: Grace O\'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>\\nDate: 2027-05-01T08:22:00-07:00\\nSubject: Internal: technical details for Meridian SSQ (PII enclave)\\n\\nGrace / Kim — notes for the packet: please fold these into the matrix and diagram.\\n\\n- Tokenization: We perform deterministic tokenization only when a customer enables the pseudonymization feature. Tokens are generated on ingest, stored in an in-memory KV and optionally backed up to a customer-scoped encrypted store for replay (explicit opt-in). File: transient-token-matrix.csv shows the opt-in vs default behavior.\\n\\n- KV cache behavior: KV caches (per-GPU process) are encrypted with process-local keys and are not written to multi-tenant durable storage. We do rotate process keys on restart; crash-dumps do not contain raw tokens.\\n\\n- Enclave boundaries: The \\"inference enclave\\" is a tenant-isolated compute pool with network egress controls and blocked admin access. We\'ll annotate the diagram to show: ingress load balancer (TLS), front-end validators (scrubbing), tokenizer (optional), isolated GPU pool (no shared filesystem), egress gateway (policy-enforced).\\n\\n- Cross-border: Smart routing policy allows region pinning. If a customer pins to eu-west-1, requests are routed to eu capacity; fallback is documented and disabled by default. Audit logs include region, node-id,
title: SSQ: PII enclave routing, transient tokens & transfer handbook

text: Cross-border: Smart routing policy allows region pinning. If a customer pins to eu-west-1, requests are routed to eu capacity; fallback is documented and disabled by default. Audit logs include region, node-id, and returned model id for every request.\\n\\n- KMS: For customer-managed keys we integrate with the customer\'s KMS (Azure Key Vault / AWS KMS / GCP KMS). We support HSM-backed key stores and can provide a custody statement and key rotation logs on request. We do not hold private key material when a customer uses CMK (bring-your-own-key).\\n\\n- Evidence: We\'ll attach an anonymized trace snippet and redact node IDs before sharing (sample-trace.json).\\n\\nI\'ll produce the annotated dataflow PDF and the short answers doc by EOD May 3.\\n\\nRishi\\nPrincipal Security Engineer\\n', "From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.ai>\\nTo: Claire Patel <claire.patel@meridianhealth.com>\\nCc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>\\nDate: 2027-05-03T17:22:00-07:00\\nSubject: RE: SSQ: PII enclave routing, transient tokens & transfer handbook\\n\\nHi Claire — attaching our package (attached: redwood_enclave_dataflow.pdf, transient-token-matrix.csv, ssq-answers-summary.docx). Summary below to help your reviewers skim:\\n\\n- Ingest and tokenization: Raw PII may be received over TLS at the customer-dedicated endpoint. Tokenization is performed in an optional preprocessing stage; tokens are generated deterministically only when enabled
title: SSQ: PII enclave routing, transient tokens & transfer handbook

text: tokenization: Raw PII may be received over TLS at the customer-dedicated endpoint. Tokenization is performed in an optional preprocessing stage; tokens are generated deterministically only when enabled by the customer. Default (no-token) path does not persist identifiers.\\n\\n- KV / cache persistence: Runtime caches are in-memory and encrypted; they are not persisted to shared durable storage. Durable persistence is only used when customers opt into archive/replay and is encrypted with the customer's CMK when provided.\\n\\n- Enclave and egress controls: Inference runs in tenant-isolated pools. Customers can pin regions and block failover egress. Audit logs record region, node, and model id per call.\\n\\n- KMS custody: We support customer-managed keys (BYOK) with HSM-backed KMS providers; Redwood does not retain private key material for CMK customers. Rotation and custody logs can be exported for your audit.\\n\\nRelated links: Meridian review folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-redwood-meridian-ssq, HubSpot: HBSP-84721\\n\\nIf you want a short walkthrough for the review team, Rishi and I are available for a 30-minute call next week — propose Thu 2027-05-06 09:00-10:00 PT and we'll send an invite.\\n\\nThanks,\\nGrace O'Connor\\nHead of Customer Privacy\\nRedwood Inference\\ngrace_oconnor@redwood.ai\\n\\nAttachments: redwood_enclave_dataflow.pdf (annotated diagram), transient-token-matrix.csv (options & persistence), ssq-answers-summary.docx (Q&A compact)\\n"]

That would help narrow tenant_tag attribution.\\n\\nAppreciate the patience — we'll treat this as high priority.\\n\\nRegards,\\nGrace O'Connor\\nRevenue Operations — Redwood Inference\\ngrace.oconnor@redwood.com\\n+1 (415) 555-0123", 'From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>\\nTo: Grace O\'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>\\nDate: 2026-05-21T09:05:00-07:00\\nSubject: Internal: initial forensic notes for EVK spike\\n\\nGrace / Jordan — I pulled the per-request export and a few quick observations:\\n\\n1) Volume breakdown (May 18-21) — total billed tokens ~3.15M.\\n - ~1.9M tokens originate from requests with a common user_agent \\"evoke-backfill/1.2\\" and request_ids that share the prefix \\"evk-bf-\\".\\n - ~850k tokens from normal interactive sessions (unique session ids).\\n - ~400k tokens flagged as \\"aux-model-reroute\\"
title: Evoke invoice: unexpected metering surge on May statement

text: \\"evoke-backfill/1.2\\" and request_ids that share the prefix \\"evk-bf-\\".\\n - ~850k tokens from normal interactive sessions (unique session ids).\\n - ~400k tokens flagged as \\"aux-model-reroute\\" where our routing system fell back to a compatibility model.\\n\\n2) Tenant attribution — ~1.85M of the backfill-prefixed requests have tenant_tag unset (NULL) in the metadata. Those requests still carried Evoke\'s customer_id in the header, but because tenant_tag was missing they were attributed to the default production owner for billing. It appears a missing client-side header in an older SDK version caused tenant_tag to be dropped during the batch job.\\n\\n3) Duplicate/Retry patterns — about 7% of the backfill request_ids show retry suffixes (\\"-r1\\", \\"-r2\\") within short windows; some retries seem to have been replayed by an ingestion orchestrator.\\n\\n4) Recommended interim remediation\\n - Offer a provisional credit for the portion clearly attributable to a backfill + tenant_tag bug (we estimate 60% of the spike), pending Evoke confirming the job.\\n - Provide the full request-trace bundle and a CSV export for their auditors.\\n\\nI\'ve packaged the evidence files and a trimmed trace list in the shared drive link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcdExampleEvidence (files: EVK_usage_export_20260520.csv, request-traces-evoke-20260520.log, supporting-telemetry-snippets.txt).\\n\\nIf you want a draft credit calculation, I can run numbers now.\\n\\nBen\\nBilling

Apologies for the disruption.\\n\\nSummary: Ben's analysis indicates ~1.9M billed tokens were generated by an Evoke backfill job that omitted tenant_tag in ~98% of requests. Those requests were still authenticated with Evoke's customer_id but were billed to production because tenant_tag metadata was empty. There were also some retries (~7% of that set) that increased token counts further.\\n\\nProposal:\\n- Redwood will issue a one-time credit of $8,740 to offset the portion of May charges attributable to the tenant_tag/backfill bug. Credit will appear on the June invoice (reference: credit EVK-CR-2026-05).\\n- We will provide the full export and traces (attached) for Evoke's

text:
Fri, 19 Jul 2028 11:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Coupa enrollment + Order Form questions

Grace / Kimberly — thanks for the heads up and Sanjay for the comments. Quick legal take:

1) PO acknowledgement SLA — we can offer a 48-hour electronic PO acknowledgement for customers that meet one of the following: a) pre-paid reserved capacity (minimum 3-month commit) or b) settlement of payment terms via a P-card or ACH on file. We should avoid changing core MSA service levels; instead, add a short Order Form addendum that states: "For the term of this Order Form, Redwood will electronically acknowledge POs submitted by Customer within 48 hours provided Customer maintains payment arrangement X or committed capacity Y."

2) Tax ID / Remit mapping — ops needs to ensure the Order Form explicitly exposes a "remit_tax_id" header. That mapping can be captured as an Order Form field and referenced in the Coupa enrollment checklist. No MSA change required; a single-line Order Form field and an Appendix describing field usage is sufficient.

I drafted a suggested amendment and a proposed one-paragraph Order Form appendix (attached). Grace, can you send the draft back to Sanjay with the proposed language and an ask for DocuSign-signable acceptance?

Attachments: MSA-Amendment-proposed.docx

– Markus Klein

["From: Paul Hart <paul.hart@zenithone.com>
To: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Cc: Ravi Kapoor <ravi.kapoor@zenithone.com>
Date: Wed, 05 May 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Enterprise controls for SSO/SCIM cutover

Hi Grace,

Thanks for the run-through on the call earlier. Before we lock dates for our SSO/SCIM cutover I need to surface a few firm constraints from our security and infra teams so Redwood can account for them in the implementation plan:

- ZenithOne requires IP allowlist enforcement for production SAML endpoints (we'll provide CIDR blocks).
- SCIM needs to map groups with external ID persistence (we cannot re-hash group IDs during cutover).
- Service accounts used by the SCIM bridge must be non-interactive accounts with MFA-exempt tokens and a naming scheme.
- We need audit-level logs for provisioning events retained for 13 months.
- Can you share an ops runbook and a short blackout/rollback window suggestion?

Attached is our internal questionnaire (ZenithOne_SSO_Questions.xlsx) with answers that will help but we expect Redwood to confirm which items are gating vs advisory.

Would like to get this cutover scheduled the week of 5/24 if possible — suggest we sync with your security lead.

Thanks,
Paul
Paul Hart
Head of Identity & Platform, ZenithOne
paul.hart@zenithone.com

To: Paul Hart <paul.hart@zenithone.com>
Cc:
title: SSO/SCIM cutover: IP-fencing & service-account gating for ZenithOne

ZenithOne
paul.hart@zenithone.com

--- Attachment: ZenithOne_SSO_Questions.xlsx (spreadsheet, 24KB)", "From: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Paul Hart <paul.hart@zenithone.com>
Cc: Ravi Kapoor <ravi.kapoor@zenithone.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 05 May 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Enterprise controls for SSO/SCIM cutover

Paul — thanks, this is very clear.

Summary of our proposed approach and immediate asks so we have a single source of truth:

1) Gating items (must be satisfied before production cutover):
 - IP allowlist applied to Redwood-hosted SAML endpoints (we'll need the CIDRs by 5/14).
 - SCIM token with a known rotation schedule and a service-account naming convention (see Neha's security items below).
 - Audit export configuration enabled and retention policy set to 13 months.

2) Implementation pattern:
 - Canaries: initial SCIM sync to a test tenant -> dry run provisioning -> canary group cutover -> full cutover.
 - Rollback: we will pin previous model/route and revoke SCIM sync if mapping errors detected.

3) Proposed blackout window for minimal impact: 02:00–04:00 UTC (suggested) on the cutover date. Please confirm any global maintenance windows you have.

I attached our internal SSO checklist (sso-checklist-v2.pdf) that maps each gating item to an owner and step. Neha, Ben — would you confirm the security validation steps and an EIC for the canary?

Thanks,
Grace

On Wed, May 5, 2027 at 11:03 AM Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com> wrote:
> I attached our internal SSO checklist (sso-checklist-v2.pdf) that maps each gating item to an owner and step. Neha, Ben — would you confirm the security validation steps and an EIC for the canary?

Quick security validation and comments below (I've kept to gating items):

- IP allowlist: confirm that the CIDRs provided by ZenithOne will be added to an allowlist applied to the tenant's SAML endpoint only. No broad network ACL changes.
- Service account pattern: use this naming convention: sa/zenithone/scim/<env>/<purpose>. All tokens created must be short-lived; we prefer rotation via our KMS-backed automation.
- SCIM schema: require externalId preserved; do not auto-generate new group IDs on sync. See attached csv for required attributes (scim-schema-recommendations.csv).
-
title: SSO/SCIM cutover: IP-fencing & service-account gating for ZenithOne

text:
via our KMS-backed automation.
- SCIM schema: require externalId preserved; do not auto-generate new group IDs on sync. See attached csv for required attributes (scim-schema-recommendations.csv).
- Audit & retention: enable provisioning audit events to the central logging bucket with 13-month lifecycle; validate access is read-only for ZenithOne security when providing audit exports.
- Approval gates: security sign-off after canary provisioning and after the first 24-hour reconciliation window.

I'll be EIC for the security gating steps during the cutover and will join the call. If Jonas can confirm that our runtime can be pinned and rolled back via routing policy, we can include that in the rollback play.

Neha Kapoor
Security Program Manager, Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.com

title: MAP: BridgeCare POC environment - access & peering

text:
Alice — great to hear. Quick responses and next steps below. We also uploaded a single MAP workbook so both teams can track ownership and deadlines. Attaching: MAP_BridgeCare_Master.xlsx.

High-level plan (targeting 6/9 POC start):
1) Org and API keys (owner: grace_oconnor) — create org "bridgecare-prod-eval" and issue two keys: one for ingestion/testing and one for production-simulated traffic. ETA: 6/4 EOD for key + initial restrictions.
2) IP allowlist (owner: BridgeCare / network ops) — we will add your staging CIDR to our allowlist once you confirm the exact /24 range and test IP.
3) VPC peering (owner: marcus_lin) — Marcus will run the peering setup. We need your VPC CIDR, account id, and preferred peering window. We typically do peering in a 30–60 minute maintenance window and validate routing with a short iperf test.
4) Test schedule & acceptance criteria (owner: neha_kapoor) — we'll run 48 hours of throughput tests and a 4-hour latency campaign; acceptance metrics live in the MAP sheet.

Action items:
- Alice: confirm staging CIDR + preferred maintenance window for peering by 6/3.
- BridgeCare NetOps: sign the attached MAP sheet to confirm ownership rows (column D).
- Redwood (Grace): will create the org and return key material via secure link by 6/4.

Will follow up to propose two peering slots.

text: Type II report + targeted evidence request — extraction plan Grace — we can deliver everything requested with the following caveats and controls: 1) Authn log slice (2027-05-01 -> 2027-06-30): - Default slice will include: timestamp, event_type (login/sso_failure/token_refresh), user_id_hash, client_app, source_ip (first 3 octets), device_type, region, and session_duration_ms. - We will redact full_email and full_username fields unless ClearSight signs a minimal data-sharing amendment. - Format: CSV with sha256 checksums for each file and an extraction manifest. 2) MFA enforcement logs: - We can provide enforcement events and policy version referenced during the window. No plaintext secrets or OTP values will be shared. 3) Privileged access review exports: - We'll provide attestation records (who attested, timestamp, scope) and a redacted list of privileged IDs. If the auditor needs the mapping to actual usernames we require a data-use agreement and secure handoff. 4) Bridge letter: - I can draft a bridge letter that asserts control coverage for the requested window and references the Type II report. 5) Timeline and delivery method: - Bridge letter + SOC2 summary: within 3 business days. - Extracts and attestation exports: within 7 business days after scope confirmation. - Delivery via secure S3 presigned link or encrypted Drive folder. technical notes for Compliance: 
- Our KV cache is per-region and is not cross-replicated by default for the NordicBank tenant; during warm-standby we only replicate model weights/artifacts, not tenant data.
- For the warm-standby failover model, we keep a synchronized metadata index that allows redirecting traffic, but session-level ephemeral caches are rebuilt in the target region — which drives the RTO.

I will follow up with specific traceroute timestamps and an expanded sample audit log entry in the morning.

title: EU/APAC deployment boundary — proof artifacts & cutover tasks

text:
(attached). Key operational points below: 

- RTO/RPO: We set RTO = 15 minutes (for control plane failover only) and RPO = 5 minutes for metadata index sync. Application-level session recovery depends on client retry windows; we include guidance in the playbook.
- Failover mechanics: primary (eu) stays read-only for the customer namespace until traffic is cut. During warm-standby we run warm instances in apac with model artifact sync but not tenant cache replication.
- Cutover checklist (short): 
 * Confirm inbound egress ACLs and BGP advertisements in target region (ops run).
 * Confirm KMS key accessibility and that HSM policy allows key usage from standby region under the emergency role.
 * Run synthetic traffic from EU clients to verify egress localization after route pinning is applied.

Hi Grace,

We're kicking off an internal review for a pilot using Redwood to generate clinical summaries. Before we set up an eval, our security and legal teams asked whether Redwood will sign a BAA and whether the hosted API can be used with PHI.

High-level: pilot volume ~5k patient messages/month, data will contain limited identifiers (name, MRN) and clinical notes.

Can you confirm:
- Do you sign BAAs for hosted customers?
- If hosted is not eligible, do you offer Dedicated or private options that support PHI?
- Any constraints on retention, logging, or subprocessors we should know about?

Thanks — happy to jump on a quick call.

Elena

Cascade Health

BAAs, but eligibility depends on deployment mode and a few controls. Looping in Monica (legal) and Vivek (security) to confirm the exact scope and required evidence.

Immediate notes:
- Hosted API: supported with a BAA in our Dedicated/Private offerings — hosted (multi-tenant) generally not recommended for PHI unless customer has an approved data flow; we'll need to review the exact integration pattern.
- Dedicated: recommended for predictable throughput and isolation; can be provisioned in your preferred region.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): available for highly regulated setups if required.

I'll ask legal for the BAA template and security for SOC2/architecture artifacts. If you can share the expected integration (API direct vs. proxying through your VPC) that will speed this up.

1) Which Redwood service do you plan to use: Hosted API (redwood-hosted), Dedicated (customer-dedicated pool), or Private (VPC/on-prem)?
2) Do you intend to send raw identifiers (SSNs, full DOB) or only name/MRN + clinical text?
3) Expected retention/archival needs (e.g., retain logs for X days, or require deletion on request)?
4) Is Cascade using a proxy in your VPC or calling Redwood directly from your application?

For Hosted (multi-tenant): our standard position is to support non-PHI use cases. We will consider PHI on a case-by-case basis with compensating controls (TLS+mutual auth, dedicated tenancy, contractual commitments). For Dedicated/Private: we can include PHI in scope and execute a BAA.

I'll attach a redlineable BAA template and our SOC 2 summary so you can share with your legal/security reviewers. Once we confirm deployment mode and retention, we'll prepare a final BAA and a DocuSign send.

Best,
Monica
Senior Counsel, Privacy & Security
Redwood Inference

Hi Elena — Vivek from Security. Thank you for the clear answers. Technical summary & next steps: 

1) Hosted eligibility:
- Multi-tenant hosted APIs are not our default for PHI unless we agree to dedicated tenancy and specific contractual limits. Given Cascade's plan to call from app servers (no VPC), we can offer a logically isolated tenancy layer (customer-dedicated model instances + strict routing) but require Dedicated for guaranteed isolation and SLA.

2) Dedicated option:
- We can provision a Dedicated GPU pool in us-east-1. That gives separate tenant allocation, configurable
title: Clarifying PHI handling & BAA for Cascade Health integration

text:
strict routing) but require Dedicated for guaranteed isolation and SLA.

2) Dedicated option:
- We can provision a Dedicated GPU pool in us-east-1. That gives separate tenant allocation, configurable retention, and no shared KV caches across tenants.
- Network: we support secure egress + optional customer-managed VPN or VPC peering if you want to avoid public internet.

3) Controls we provide and evidence:
- Encryption in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at-rest with KMS-managed keys.
- Audit logs (access and admin) retained configurable up to 365 days — default 90 days. We can set log retention to 90 days to match your requirement.
- PHI handling: we do not use customer data for model training by default; we can contractually prohibit it and document the data flow.
- Subprocessors: list available; we'll provide current subprocessors and SOC 2 summary.

I've attached: redwood_SOC2_type2_summary.pdf, BAA_template_redwood.docx, network_diagram_v1.pdf (attachment stubs in this thread). Also including a short checklist for provisioning Dedicated.

Next steps:
- If Dedicated is acceptable, confirm commitment window so we can provision capacity (typically 3–5 business days for a standard pool).
- Legal to exchange and finalize the BAA.

Happy to do a technical call to walk through the network diagram.

Thanks,
Vivek

Redwood Inference

['From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Dec 2026 08:12:00 -0800
Subject: Coverage Redraw: compact pod design & manager splits

Team —

We need to finalize a narrow, low-disruption coverage redraw for early Q1 that reduces cross-team handoffs and clarifies manager spans for the serving/runtime org. This is a focused operational change not a headcount move — the intent is to improve coverage and accelerate incident ownership while keeping people in current pods where possible.

Summary of the proposal (attached: org-chart-proposal.pdf, role-impact-risks.xlsx):
- Compress three adjacent pods into two "compact pods" aligned by model surface (text-gen vs. embeddings/utility).
- Shift ownership of runtime infra ops from Platform Pod C to Pod A lead (one dotted-line report).
- Split Manager Band: two managers (senior ICs promoted to manager shadow for 6 months) for 12-person spans to reduce escalation tail.
- Create a lightweight on-call pairing rotation across the two compact pods to reduce primary responder load.

Primary asks:
1) Review the high-level org chart and call out any immediate conflicts with ongoing Q1 projects.
2) Flag people who must not be moved for product or compliance reasons.
3) If this looks directionally ok, I want to route to Aditya for exec sign-off and
title: Coverage Redraw: compact pod design & manager splits

text:
immediate conflicts with ongoing Q1 projects.
2) Flag people who must not be moved for product or compliance reasons.
3) If this looks directionally ok, I want to route to Aditya for exec sign-off and Monica for HR coordination by EOD Thursday.

Risks called out in the spreadsheet: ramp cost for shadow managers, temporary slowdown in cross-functional projects, and potential PM/engineer misalignment in the short window. I've added mitigations in the doc (overlap weeks, dedicated rollout comms).

I plan to run a quick 30-minute sync tomorrow to walk the chart and answer concerns if people want.

Thanks —
Grace O'Connor
Head of Product — Inference
Redwood Inference

especially for hotfix windows.
- Pod composition: Please ensure the embeddings utility owners are not split across two pods — that kills velocity. In my redline I suggested keeping the embeddings SRE + infra engineer together and moving the database reliability piece to Pod B.

Looping in my concerns from the perspective of operational load and escalation windows.

"On Mon, Dec 28, 2026 at 08:12, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com> wrote:
> We need to finalize a narrow, low-disruption coverage redraw for early Q1 that reduces cross-team handoffs and clarifies manager spans for the serving/runtime org.

Key points:
- Manager span: reducing to ~12 is good, but the proposal relies on two newly promoted managers taking on active incident ownership during ramp. We should publish an explicit escalation flow that covers a 2–3 week overlap where both the old and new escalations are live.
- On-call pairing: rotating responders across compact pods is sensible, but we must keep at least one responder with deep model-kv-cache knowledge on cycle to avoid P1 regressions. That knowledge is concentrated in two engineers; moving them would be high risk.
- Metrics: let's add concrete SLO targets for the first 60 days post-change (median p95 latency, deploy rollback frequency, mean time to acknowledge). That will make the rollout measurable and reversible.

If we can address the two high-risk people and add the SLOs to the rollout doc, I can support the change.

Jonas
VP, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

Eliot —

Appreciate the clear ask. Draft MAP attached (MAP-final-draft.docx) — below is the condensed scorecard and the acceptance tests we propose for the 6-week pilot. If this looks right, we’ll update the spreadsheet and DocuSign the mutual action plan so procurement has a single source of truth.

Pilot readiness KPI scorecard (pass thresholds):
- Latency: p95 generation latency <= 400ms on production-simulated traffic (sample trace attached)
- Throughput: sustained 60 requests/min with 95% success under dedicated instance profile
- Accuracy / quality: automated eval on 1,000 labeled prompts: F1 >= 0.86 OR human review pass rate >= 92% across a 200-case random audit
- Safety/regulatory: zero high-severity PII leakage incidents in 6-week window; logging retained for 90 days and accessible via customer audit logs

Exit gates (stop or re-scope if any of these occur):
- Reproducible P95 latency > 700ms after tuning and one autoscaling cycle
- Human review pass rate < 80% after two remediation sprints
- Evidence of data exfiltration or inability to meet KMS/HSM key controls

Acceptance test suite (end of
title: Pilot readiness KPI scorecard + Close playbook

text:
tuning and one autoscaling cycle
- Human review pass rate < 80% after two remediation sprints
- Evidence of data exfiltration or inability to meet KMS/HSM key controls

Acceptance test suite (end of pilot):
1) Performance smoke: run 30-minute burst test using Clearwell traffic trace; verify p95 and error rate
2) Quality eval: run the 1,000-sample automated eval; sample 200 cases for human audit. Report confusion clusters and top 3 failure modes.
3) Integration resilience: execute failover injection (region switch) and confirm request routing and caching behavior preserved
4) Security evidence: provide SOC2 extract + audit log export (redacted) and confirm retention policy via console

Timeline / close path:
- Week 0: Kickoff + data exchange (sample set + access)
- Week 1–2: Integration and baseline runs; initial tuning
- Week 3–4: Full evaluation runs (automated + manual audits)
- Week 5: Remediation sprint if needed; compile findings
- Week 6: Acceptance run + mutual signoff; procurement handoff

Next steps I propose:
- Marissa: will lead the evaluation runs and quality analysis (see tasks below)
- Ben: will schedule the baseline perf runs in Dedicated env and prepare the 30-min burst test config

I’ll push the MAP to the Google Sheet and link it to the HubSpot deal (RDW-4521). If you confirm the thresholds above I’ll convert to a DocuSign-ready MAP.

Best,
Grace O'Connor
Enterprise Account Executive, Redwood Inference
grace_oconnor@redwood.com

Subject: Eval guardrail + ops handshake for prompt-set rollout

Team —

Quick sync request as we prepare the prompt-set A/B rollout for Acme next week. Product asks we lock down the acceptance criteria and the ops handoff so we can sign the canary and schedule the dedicated capacity. Key asks below: 

1) Acceptance thresholds (prompt-set A vs B): suggested draft in attached workbook. I want concrete pass/fail rules for the AB job so SRE/ops can automate rollback.
2) Regression signals to monitor: functional accuracy delta, P95 latency, hallucination rate (human-rated), and support ticket spike.
3) Rollback policy: who triggers (ops vs product), quick rollback playbook, and fallback model variant.
4) Canary timeline & ops checklist (traffic ramp, monitoring runbooks, alerting thresholds).

Attachments: eval_acceptance_draft.xlsx (contains proposed numeric thresholds) and ab-plan-v1.md (AB plan draft).

Can each of you reply with a single line for: (a) comfortable thresholds or objections, (b) any ops constraints (capacity/quantization variants), and (c) owner for the on-call rollback runbook? I'd like to capture decisions and then fold into the runbook by EOD tomorrow.

Thanks

To: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-04-18T11:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Eval guardrail + ops handshake for prompt-set rollout

Grace — thanks for the clear list. Quick replies inline: 

(a) Acceptance thresholds (I'm suggesting we land on these now):
- Human-rated coherence: mean >= 4.1 (5-point scale) for B vs A
- Accuracy-based metrics: absolute drop <= 1.5 percentage points for task-specific F1
- Hallucination: manual samples <= 2% flagged in two random buckets of 500 prompts
- Latency: P95 increase <= 10% vs baseline

(b) Ops constraints: we have a scheduled GPU maintenance window Thu night (4/22) — avoid heavy canary ramp during that window. Also, the quantized 4-bit variant shows ~6% latency improvement but we must validate numeric fidelity; recommend initially using float16 variant for canary.

(c) Runbook owner: I can own the acceptance checklist and coordinate the human-rater sampling. Ben/ops should own rollback automation.

I attached brief comments to eval_acceptance_draft.xlsx.

—Marissa
Marissa
title: Eval guardrail + ops handshake for prompt-set rollout

text: Purpose This doc is a combined architecture + operational runbook for our routing policy engine and control-plane components across multi-region deployments. It is intentionally practical: short diagnostics, commands, and mitigations embedded in architecture context so on-call engineers can both reason about root cause and act quickly. Scope - Components: routing-policy-service (RPS), control-plane-api, policy-store (etcd/consul), route-agent (edge sidecar), regional health aggregator, and the global policy router. - Cross-region concerns: affinity, KV replication lag, split-brain on leader election, and fallback model variant routing. - Not included: serving-runtime GPU node-level debugging (see serving-runtime/sre-runbooks). Architecture notes (brief) - Control plane (control-plane-api + policy-store) is the single source of truth for routing policies, rollout stages, and region weightings. It exposes a versioned API used by route-agents to fetch policies on startup and at policy-change webhooks. - Route-agents run in each region and maintain an in-memory policy cache and local evaluation engine for low-latency routing decisions. Agents watch the policy-store via streaming endpoints and apply delta updates. - Policy evaluation order: explicit-route (customer pin) -> canary -> weighted-routing -> geo-affinity -> fallback. Policies are deterministic and evaluated top-to-bottom. - Rollouts: canaries are enabled by rollout rules stored in the policy-store. Each rollout has a name,
title: routing-and-controlplane-runbook-architecture-notes

text: weighted-routing -> geo-affinity -> fallback. Policies are deterministic and evaluated top-to-bottom. - Rollouts: canaries are enabled by rollout rules stored in the policy-store. Each rollout has a name, window, and metric criteria (latency and error thresholds). - Health checks: agents report region-level health via health-aggregator which updates a region-status index used by global router. Common incident scenarios + runbooks 1) Control plane API unresponsive (HTTP 5xx or timeouts) - Symptom: config change fails; route-agents show repeated fetch retries; recent deploy of control-plane-api; alerts: PLAT-CONTROL-API-ERR. - Quick checks: - Check SLOs/dashboard: control_plane_api_request_latency_p50/p95, control_plane_api_error_rate (Dash: Platform / Control Plane / API) - kubectl -n platform get pods -l app=control-plane-api; kubectl logs -f deployment/control-plane-api -c api --tail 200 - curl -v https://control-plane-api.internal/healthz || echo "down" - Quick mitigations: - If pods are CrashLoopBackOff: kubectl describe pod <pod> to see OOM or config errors. Revert recent config change (kubectl rollout undo deployment/control-plane-api) if deploy timestamp aligns with incident. - If high CPU due to policy-store scan: scale up replicas temporarily: kubectl scale deploy/control-plane-api --replicas=4 (notify infra). - If external dependency (auth/KMS) failing: flip maintained feature-flag to allow unauthenticated local reads for emergency: POST to admin toggle endpoint (requires
title: routing-and-controlplane-runbook-architecture-notes

text: deploy/control-plane-api --replicas=4 (notify infra). - If external dependency (auth/KMS) failing: flip maintained feature-flag to allow unauthenticated local reads for emergency: POST to admin toggle endpoint (requires infra approval). - Recovery steps: - Redeploy last known-good image: kubectl set image deployment/control-plane-api control-plane-api=registry.redwood/internal/control-plane-api:stable-20260201 - Validate route-agents reconnected: check route-agent logs and policy version: kubectl logs -l app=route-agent -c agent | grep "policy_version=" - Post-incident: - Open PLAT JIRA ticket, attach timeline and last deploy id (PLAT-CONTROL-API-ERR-YYYYMMDD), run postmortem template, update changelog. 2) Policy drift or incorrect evaluation order deployed (bad rollout rules) - Symptom: certain traffic unexpectedly hits a fallback model; regression alerts on quality (Rerank/quality evals) or sudden cost increase. - Quick checks: - Check policy versions: curl https://control-plane-api.internal/v1/policies/versions last_3 - Search for recent policy changes: git log --since="48 hours" -- platform-configs/policies - Query route-agent logs for "policy-eval" traces and decision path: kubectl logs -l app=route-agent | egrep "policy_eval|decision_path" - Quick mitigations: - Push emergency policy patch that restores previous rule ordering. Use the policy-store admin UI to revert version or use API: POST /v1/policies/revert {"version":"v123"} - If automatic rollouts continue to apply
title: routing-and-controlplane-runbook-architecture-notes

text: emergency policy patch that restores previous rule ordering. Use the policy-store admin UI to revert version or use API: POST /v1/policies/revert {"version":"v123"} - If automatic rollouts continue to apply bad policy, disable rollout engine: PATCH /v1/admin/settings {"rollout_enabled":false} (only for <30m). - Recovery steps: - Validate behavior in canary region and run synthetic tests (see linked_artifacts). Promote fix to all regions after 30 minutes of stable metrics. 3) Regional split-brain / KV replication lag causing inconsistent policies across regions - Symptom: region A has new policies, region B still uses older policies -> inconsistent routing; alerts: PLAT-POLICY-REPL-LAG. - Quick checks: - Check policy-store replication status: kubectl -n platform exec -it statefulset/policy-store -- /bin/psql -c "SELECT * FROM replication_status;" - Check agent-sync metrics: route_agent_policy_sync_lag_seconds{region="us-east-1"} - Look for leader-election warnings in logs: kubectl logs -l app=policy-store | egrep "leader|raft|election" - Quick mitigations: - If leader elected incorrectly, force leader stepdown to trigger re-election: kubectl exec -it pod/policy-store-0 -- /bin/consul operator raft remove-peer <peer-id> (follow infra runbook) or use etcdctl to promote leader. - Temporarily set global routing to prefer region with majority policy consistency: POST /v1/admin/routing/override {"global_prefer":"us-west-2"} - Recovery steps: - Reconcile KV store: run the replication
title: routing-and-controlplane-runbook-architecture-notes

text: Temporarily set global routing to prefer region with majority policy consistency: POST /v1/admin/routing/override {"global_prefer":"us-west-2"} - Recovery steps: - Reconcile KV store: run the replication repair script from git (scripts/repair-kv-replication.sh) with oncall approval. text: TL;DR: We ran a small set of controlled playback tests to compare dispatch semantics across edge-forward dispatch (short hops to nearby edge cells with small prefetch), core-tiling multiplexing (centralized GPU tiles with larger batching windows), and a hybrid with KV-prefetch evictions. Results show: edge-dispatch-prefetch gives best p50 and p95 for short responses (<=128 tokens) at a modest cost premium; core-tiling-multiplex achieves far better $/1k tokens for long outputs (>=512 tokens) but suffers p99 regressions under bursty arrival; hybrid-evict trades in slightly higher median latency for better p99 and solid $/1k for mid-length responses. Why we ran this (context): - Product asks for a short, shareable comparison to inform routing policy defaults for chat-lite vs doc-embed routes. - We need simple knobs that map to developer-facing toggles: prefer-edge, prefer-core, hybrid-prefetch. - This is intentionally lightweight — not exhaustive — but focused on practical, explainable deltas to communicate with PM and Console teams. Experiment setup (short): - Date: 2026-05-15 to 2026-05-17. - Hardware: G-core (A100x2) for core tiles; Edge cells running T4-equivalent virtual nodes (quantized runtime). - Model: open-llm v2-3B (float16 in core, int8 quantized at edge). - Workloads: 3 synthetic mixes: * Short-burst: 80% <= 64 tok, 20% 128 tok (chat-lite mimic) * Mid-thread: 60% 128-256 tok, 40% 256-512 tok (assistant flows) * Long-pour: 100% >=512 tok (document summarization) - Traffic
title: Dispatch surface playback: edge vs core econ memo

text: synthetic mixes: * Short-burst: 80% <= 64 tok, 20% 128 tok (chat-lite mimic) * Mid-thread: 60% 128-256 tok, 40% 256-512 tok (assistant flows) * Long-pour: 100% >=512 tok (document summarization) - Traffic shape: poisson baseline + injected microbursts (10s, 100req/s spikes) to exercise tail behavior. - Measurement: p50, p95, p99 latency on response-ready (end-of-stream), tokens/sec observed, and computed $/1k tokens using current infra cost model (GPU-hour amortized + infra egress). Configs tested (names used below): - edge-dispatch-prefetch: nearest edge cell, small prefetch window (prefetch 16 tokens, KV cache enabled), quantized runtime. - core-tiling-multiplex: central core fleet, tiling multiplex scheduling, larger batching window (50ms), float16 kernels. - hybrid-evict: route initial tokens to edge (prefetch 32), then stitch/resume on core if session length > 256 or predicted tail; KV eviction policy to avoid cache thrash. Raw (annotated) numbers (mid-thread workload, 10k requests each): Config | p50 ms | p95 ms | p99 ms | tokens/sec | $/1k tokens --------------------------|--------|--------|--------|------------|------------- edge-dispatch-prefetch | 72 | 180 | 420 | 1,350 | $0.145 core-tiling-multiplex | 120 | 350 | 1,150 | 3,800 | $0.062 hybrid-evict | 95 | 240 | 470 | 2,200 | $0.085 Notes on numbers: p99 for core spikes when bursts coincide with long outputs — batching helps throughput but hurts tail. Edge has lower medians due to proximity + quantized runtime but unit
title: Dispatch surface playback: edge vs core econ memo

text: 2,200 | $0.085 Notes on numbers: p99 for core spikes when bursts coincide with long outputs — batching helps throughput but hurts tail. Edge has lower medians due to proximity + quantized runtime but unit cost is higher because T4 nodes have lower utilization and int8 inefficiencies on our current kernels. Hybrid avoids the worst tail by stitching early tokens locally and moving heavy tails to the core when predicted, which balances p99 and $/1k. Short-burst workload summary (best config per metric): - p50: edge-dispatch-prefetch (42 ms) - p95: edge-dispatch-prefetch (112 ms) - $/1k: core-tiling-multiplex ($0.058) but p95/p99 worse Long-pour workload summary: - Core-tiling-multiplex dominates in $/1k and tokens/sec (as expected). p99 improves if burst smoothing is applied (admission control). Edge becomes prohibitively expensive for >512 token outputs. ASCII latency profile (mid-thread, approximated CDF buckets): - edge-dispatch-prefetch: [<=100ms: 64%] [<=300ms: 90%] [<=500ms: 98%] - core-tiling-multiplex: [<=100ms: 28%] [<=300ms: 76%] [<=1000ms: 96%] - hybrid-evict: [<=100ms: 48%] [<=300ms: 86%] [<=500ms: 97%] Operational implications and trade-offs: - Developer UX (chat-lite): prefer-edge gives snappier interactive feel. Cost delta is modest at low scale but compounds at volume. Recommend UI default "prefer-edge" for chat-lite small volumes with an opt-out in Console for cost-conscious teams. - Enterprise/default: prefer-core or hybrid for high-throughput/long-inference pipelines.
title: Dispatch surface playback: edge vs core econ memo

text: Recommend UI default "prefer-edge" for chat-lite small volumes with an opt-out in Console for cost-conscious teams. - Enterprise/default: prefer-core or hybrid for high-throughput/long-inference pipelines. Core routing improves unit economics and should be promoted for summarization, batch embeddings, and long-chats. - SLO design: if customers require p95 < 200ms for chat, edge or hybrid is necessary for short responses; but p99 SLOs need admission control and burst smoothing in core deployment to be viable. Caveats & open questions (things to follow up): - Kernel inefficiencies: our quantized int8 kernel on edge shows a per-token penalty for certain attention shapes; rebenchmark after kernel patch (PR #342). - Network variability: tests used emulated edge latencies; run a small customer-realistic replay to confirm. - Cost model sensitivity: $/1k uses current amortization assumptions; re-run with alternate GPU pricing (spot vs reserved) for a sensitivity table. - Stitching overhead: hybrid transition points used a simple heuristic; potential for ML-based tail prediction to reduce unnecessary core handoffs. Recommended next steps (practical): 1) Share this note with Console + Router owners and ask for A/B of default route for new chat-lite workspaces (2-week experiment). 2) Re-run mid-thread tests after quant kernel patch and with spot pricing assumptions (owner: Evan). 3) Build small visual one-pager for PD and Sales that explains the three knobs (prefer-edge, prefer-core, hybrid-prefetch)
title: Dispatch surface playback: edge vs core econ memo

text: tests after quant kernel patch and with spot pricing assumptions (owner: Evan). 3) Build small visual one-pager for PD and Sales that explains the three knobs (prefer-edge, prefer-core, hybrid-prefetch) with the 3-line summary and a simple cost/latency matrix. 4) Add hybrid handoff telemetry in the onboarding trace (small schema change) to measure handoff rate and end-to-end join latency in real traffic. Appendix: quick checklist for replicability - seed traffic generator: scripts/bench/playback_v2 with config: mid-thread.json - model commit: open-llm/v2-3b@sha:f3a2c4 - runtime flags used: --batch-window-ms=50 (core), --edge-prefetch=16 (edge), --kv-evict=adaptive (hybrid) - cost calc script: pricing/calc_unit_cost.py --amort=on-demand --include-egress Notes-to-self (raw): - push a short slide deck to /product/presentations after Evan's kernel results. - add a small experiment to try prefetch=64 on edge to see if p99 improves (may hurt p50). - consider adding 'prefer-core-for-long' automated route: client-side hint + server-side enforcement. End of memo. -- Maya (rough draft; will tidy into one-pager once follow-ups done)
