text: Purpose: This is a working notebook for a compact, operational-first SLO cadence bootcamp aimed at product, infra, platform, and support partners. The goal is not to write another policy doc — its to rehearse precise handoffs, validate signals, and train responders on the SLO rhythm we expect teams to run for model-serving features (hosted API, dedicated, private). Objective (measurable): - Run a 10-person cross-team rehearsal and complete 3 tabletop-to-live escalation handoffs within 2 weeks. - Validate the SLO signal chain end-to-end (client metric -> aggregators -> alert -> incident bridge -> postmortem starter). - Produce 5 small, actionable changes to the SLO observability checklist and 2 quick configuration changes (alert thresholds, runbook links). Audience: - SREs, Feature Engineers owning model endpoints, Platform Engineers, Customer Success on-call leads, Product PMs for priority features. High-level bootcamp plan (2 weeks): Week 0 (prep) - Day -7: Invite list, pre-reading, attach current SLOs + incident runbooks, mapping doc. - Day -5: Data sanity check run: make sure metrics exporter, rollups, and alerting queries are returning expected values. - Day -3: Quick sync with observability infra to enable synthetic tests and a short-lived alert channel for the rehearsal. Week 1 (rehearsals) - Day 1: Orientation (1 hour): cadence overview, roles, how to read SLO dashboards. - Day 2-3: Tabletop sessions (2x 90m) focused on: slow tail latency (P95->P99 divergence) and token-cost
title: SLO Cadence Bootcamp & Cross-functional Rehearsal Notebook

text: (rehearsals) - Day 1: Orientation (1 hour): cadence overview, roles, how to read SLO dashboards. - Day 2-3: Tabletop sessions (2x 90m) focused on: slow tail latency (P95->P99 divergence) and token-cost regression. Use incident-sculpting templates (see exercises section). - Day 4: Live mini-sim (4 hours): inject synthetic latency to a staging model pool and exercise automated fallback routing. Observe system kinetics, KV cache warm-up effects, request batching shifts. - Day 5: Debrief and quick fixes (2 hours): triage suggested changes and apply 2 configuration items in staging. Week 2 (handoff & measurement) - Day 8: Canary failure escalation (full replay): simulate a sustained GPU pool failure in a single region and route traffic to fallback variant. Walk the oncall rota through the escalation. - Day 10: Customer-impact tabletop (90m): product PM and CS run prioritization decisions (who to call, customer comms). - Day 12: Postmortem starter & metrics audit (2 hours): ensure the postmortem stub has required SLO evidence and signal artifacts. Session roles and responsibilities (short form): - Incident Commander (IC): SRE oncall (primary). Starts bridge, drives tl;dr, ensures runbook steps executed. - Observability Lead: platform-observability engineer. Owns dashboards, ensures alerts fired and screenshots are captured. - Feature Owner: product/feature engineer for the affected endpoint. Executes rollback/fallback if required, decision authority for product trade-offs. - Routing/Fallback
title: SLO Cadence Bootcamp & Cross-functional Rehearsal Notebook

text: and screenshots are captured. - Feature Owner: product/feature engineer for the affected endpoint. Executes rollback/fallback if required, decision authority for product trade-offs. - Routing/Fallback Engineer: serving-runtime engineer. Validates model variant/fallback routing and capacity changes. - CS Rep: customer-success oncall lead. Prepares communication template and customer impact assessment. - Recorder/PM: takes notes, timestamps key signal artifacts for postmortem. Exercises (unique, repeatable): 1) Incident Sculpting: P95->P99 Divergence - Scenario: After a model update, P95 stays stable but P99 latency spikes for long-tail queries. No immediate request drop, but error budgets are bleeding. - Steps: - Observability Lead runs the "tail-sampler" query (provided snippet) to collect full-request histograms. - IC declares SLO burn threshold; Feature Owner decides between throttling long queries or rolling back. - Routing Engineer toggles prefix-KV caching to test mitigation; measure 15-minute impact. - Learning targets: how quickly can teams identify root-cause (model complexity vs batching behavior), and can fallback routing prevent escalation within 20 minutes. 2) Model Serving Cost Spike (token-cost regression) - Scenario: A change increases token churn for a set of prompts causing 2x cost on a mid-tier route and a steady SLO burn on latency under load. - Steps: - Use recorded traffic replay in a staging pool for 30 minutes. - Observability Lead shows token-cost attribution
title: SLO Cadence Bootcamp & Cross-functional Rehearsal Notebook

text: causing 2x cost on a mid-tier route and a steady SLO burn on latency under load. - Steps: - Use recorded traffic replay in a staging pool for 30 minutes. - Observability Lead shows token-cost attribution per route; CS flags customers at risk. - Quick mitigations: reduce max_tokens, enable quantized variant for that route, or apply rate limiting. - Learning targets: map cost signal to SLO choices and runbook entries. 3) Partial-region GPU Failure + Fallback Validation - Scenario: One region's dedicated pool loses 60% capacity over 10 minutes (simulated), autoscaler triggers but not fast enough. - Steps: - Routing/Fallback Engineer force-routes 20% traffic to secondary region/model variant. - IC checks latency and errors on radar; if customer-impacting, CS drafts comms. - Learning targets: time-to-fallback, warm-up cost, and customer impact communication template timing. Rehearsal scripts (short snippets you can read out): - "Bridge open. We see P99 at 2.4s for route X, error rate stable. Token cost has increased 2.1x for the last 10m. IC asks: Feature owner, do you own rollback or do we throttle?" - "Observability: attaching dashboard snapshot and flame graph link. Routing, can you confirm fallback candidate is ready?" Artifacts to capture during runs (must be saved to the incident folder): - Dashboard screenshots with timestamped queries. - Alert firing history (raw JSON). - Sampling of raw traces and the input prompts that caused the tail. - Command history for any toggles (rolling
title: SLO Cadence Bootcamp & Cross-functional Rehearsal Notebook

text: folder): - Dashboard screenshots with timestamped queries. - Alert firing history (raw JSON). - Sampling of raw traces and the input prompts that caused the tail. - Command history for any toggles (rolling back, routing changes). Success metrics and acceptance criteria: - All alert-to-bridge times <= 5 minutes for synthetic tests. - At least 1 successful automated fallback during live mini-sim with < 15% customer-impact traffic diverted. - Post-bootcamp: 80% of participants can list the SLO signal chain in a 3-point checklist test. Quick runbook checklist (bite-sized items): - Is the correct SLO policy attached to the route? (yes/no) - Can synthetic tests be triggered in < 10m? (who owns it) - Alert severity mapping set: P99 latency -> page; error-rate -> page; cost-spike -> slack only. - Postmortem stub created with links to dashboards, alert JSONs, and timeline. Open questions / TODOs (notes to self): - TODO: Get explicit buy-in from Product for automated rollback on canary model updates (Marco). - TODO: Validate synthetic load harness for token-cost tests (Lina). - Question: should cost-spike alerts page SRE or only alert CS+product first? (discuss in debrief). - TODO: Add a short "customer-impact rubric" to runbooks so CS can rapidly classify impacted tenants. Debrief template (use after every rehearsal): - What worked? (top 3) - What blocked the run? (top 3) - Quick fixes applied during the run (list + owner) - Follow-ups and owners (JIRA tickets recommended) Appendix: handy

text: Purpose --- This is a lightweight PRD-style playbook for a focused playtest: exercise approval gates and audit-log retention semantics in Private deployments using synthetic (fabricated) change events. The goal is to validate operational assumptions, measure evidence size/provenance, and exercise customer-managed-network handshakes without touching real customer configs. Why this helps --- - Low-risk way to verify that approval sampling, compact evidence bundles, and rollback tokens behave end-to-end in on-prem environments. - Lets us measure network cost, archive size, and query latency for common forensic queries before committing to a retention/ingest model. - Provides concrete UX/automation gaps for operator-side agents and control-plane attestation. Scope (what we will test) --- 1) Approval gate flow: generate a sequence of synthetic VPC-change requests that exercise each approval tier (operator, delegated approver, emergency bypass). 2) Evidence packaging: create compact evidence bundles (metadata + signed delta + optional snapshot). Measure bundle size across varying payload heuristics. 3) Audit log ingestion paths: on-prem forwarder -> customer-edge aggregator -> control-plane ingest. Validate backpressure and retry semantics. 4) Query and replay: run a sample forensic query (time-window, actor, changed-cidrs) against the stored evidence set and a replay harness. 5) Fallbacks: simulate control-plane unavailability and ensure graceful local audit retention + later reconciliation.
title: Approval playtest: synthetic evidence protocol

text: (time-window, actor, changed-cidrs) against the stored evidence set and a replay harness. 5) Fallbacks: simulate control-plane unavailability and ensure graceful local audit retention + later reconciliation. Non-goals --- - This playtest will not attempt live customer traffic or real credential rotation. No production customer state will be modified. High-level design / components --- - Synthetic change generator (local): emits deterministic change events (JSON) with controllable branching, sizes, and frequency. - Edge forwarder (on-prem agent): collects events, signs them with ephemeral operator key, and forwards to customer-edge aggregator. - Attestation layer: compact witness token creator that includes: event-hash, operator-sig, control-plane-ack (if available). - Evidence packager: converts event stream into compact bundles (delta only / delta+snapshot) with an index for fast query. - Control-plane ingest + indexer (staging): receives bundles, stores in object store, updates index for tfast queries. - Forensic query harness: runs a set of canonical queries and measures latency, false positives, and data completeness. Synthetic evidence structure (prototype) --- - event_id: stable string - timestamp: ISO string - actor: synthetic principal name (operator-e2e-1) - change_summary: short text (e.g., "add-prefix 10.40.0.0/16 to tenant-abc") - delta_blob: base64-encoded minimal patch (optional snapshot flag) - operator_sig: base64 signature of (event_id + delta_blob) - witness_token:
title: Approval playtest: synthetic evidence protocol

text: short text (e.g., "add-prefix 10.40.0.0/16 to tenant-abc") - delta_blob: base64-encoded minimal patch (optional snapshot flag) - operator_sig: base64 signature of (event_id + delta_blob) - witness_token: optional base64 token returned by control-plane after ack Note: we'll keep event payloads intentionally small (128B / 1KB / 10KB sizes) to observe compression/archival effects. Playtest scenarios (initial set) --- A. Normal approval path (canary): 1) operator submits change, 2) delegated approver approves, 3) control-plane returns witness token. B. Tiered approval (long path): change escalates through 3 approvers; simulate 5s, 30s, 2m delays to test queuing/backpressure. C. Emergency bypass: operator marks emergency flag; change applies with emergency token; verify audit marks and extra retention. D. Control-plane interruption: forwarder continues collecting locally for 24h and reconciles later; verify no evidence lost and dedup works. E. Bulk churn test: burst 10k synthetic changes over 10 minutes to stress packaging and indexing. Acceptance criteria (measurable) --- - End-to-end: For 1000 normal-path synthetic changes, forensic queries by event_id return correct bundle 100% of the time after eventual consistency window (<= 60s). - Bundle size: 95th percentile evidence bundle (delta-only) should be <= 2.5x of raw change payload after compression. - Query latency: simple predicate queries on index (actor, prefix) should return results in <= 250ms P95 on staging hardware. - Local
title: Approval playtest: synthetic evidence protocol

text: (delta-only) should be <= 2.5x of raw change payload after compression. - Query latency: simple predicate queries on index (actor, prefix) should return results in <= 250ms P95 on staging hardware. - Local retention: during control-plane down test, on-prem agent must hold 24h worth of events without dropping, and reconcile within 30m of control-plane recovery. - UX: operator-side agent CLI/console shows clear failure/retry state and exposes a quick-export for legal/compliance. Metrics to collect --- - events emitted / s (by size class) - forwarder throughput (events/s, bytes/s) and backlog depth - bundle generation latency and bundle sizes (min/median/p95) - control-plane ack latency for witness tokens - query latency distribution for canonical queries - reconciliation success rate and time-to-consistency after outage Operator UX experiments (quick ideas) --- - Show a compact "evidence card" in Console that summarizes an approval: event_id, actor, time, size, snapshot? yes/no, proof-hash. Click to download bundle. - One-click legal export: compile events+signatures for time range and tenant, zipped with human-readable changelog and a signature manifest. - Local CLI: redwood-evidence verify <bundle> to validate signatures and token consistency. Risks and unknowns --- - Key management: ephemeral operator keys vs long-lived keys. How does rotation affect evidence validation? - Proof size with real network configs: synthetic payloads might underrepresent real-world diff sizes when
title: Approval playtest: synthetic evidence protocol

text: management: ephemeral operator keys vs long-lived keys. How does rotation affect evidence validation? - Proof size with real network configs: synthetic payloads might underrepresent real-world diff sizes when customers have large ACL lists or BGP policies. - Privacy: even synthetic events could leak shape of network tests to infosec. Need guardrails. - Cross-tenant indexing costs if index includes many attributes. Runbook / steps for this playtest --- 1) Prep: staging cluster, object-store bucket, indexer instance, and on-prem agent docker image. 2) Generate baseline synthetic corpus (500 events across sizes). 3) Exercise scenarios A..E, capture logs and metrics. 4) Run forensic queries and full replay for a random 10% subset and compare results vs local generator ground truth. 5) Document divergences, enlarge corpus if needed, and file follow-ups. Deliverables from playtest --- - Metrics report (CSV + short writeup) with key acceptance criteria results. - Sample evidence bundles (3 sizes) and a validation script. - UX notes: operator CLI/console mockups and friction points. - Follow-up tickets: KM rotation impact, index sizing changes, retention policy proposals. Open questions (to resolve in next week) --- - Is the witness_token model required for all flows? (tradeoffs: immediate control-plane ack vs offline-signed receipts) - What default retention class should we recommend for emergency-change bundles? - How do we signal that a bundle contains PII-like network metadata that
title: Approval playtest: synthetic evidence protocol

text: control-plane ack vs offline-signed receipts) - What default retention class should we recommend for emergency-change bundles? - How do we signal that a bundle contains PII-like network metadata that requires tighter access? Next steps --- - Schedule a 2h playtest kickoff with eng-sre, eng-platform, and product-private-deployments (owner: Priya). - Assign Marco to build the synthetic generator; Sofia to wire the forwarder; Jordan to prepare indexer staging. - Run initial baseline by 2025-12-10 and produce first metrics deliverable by 2025-12-17. Notes to reviewers --- This is intentionally concise and operational — treat as experiment plan not final spec. I expect a few iterations, especially around proof format and retention classes. Add comments inline or drop test-requests into #prod-private-playtests on Slack.
title: Approval playtest: synthetic evidence protocol

text:
DAYLOG: quick brain dump and run notes (informal)

Date: 2026-03-05 (last update)
Context: working on 4-bit/3-bit quant profiles for gptx-3b variants; focus on latency SLOs for low-latency chat and keeping quality regression under 2% (measured on conversational-cases-v2 eval set).

Top-of-mind TODOs:
- finish bullseye run comparing 4-bit q5_0 vs q4_0 (small sample) across 3 prompt families
- measure kv-cache warmup penalty with prefix-caching enabled (cold vs warm)
- try per-layer quant scale on attention matrices (idea from convo w/ Evelyn)
- add simple eval wrapper to post metrics to /optimize/rollups (script)

Quick hypotheses:
- 4-bit q4_0 with per-head scale will keep BLEU/ROUGE-like scores within 1.5% of fp16 for short-turn dialog, but will reduce peak memory by ~35%.

quant-test:latest

Experimental notes (raw):
- run q4_0, batch1, kv-cache enabled
 * avg latency (single-turn): 72ms
 * p95: 130ms (bad)
 * tokens/sec: 340
 * GPU mem peak: 5.1GB
 * eval-acc (conv-cases v2): 0.882 vs fp16 baseline 0.895 -> -1.4%
 * weird: occasional token hangs at step ~120 (trace shows lock contention in batching module)

- run q5_0, batch1, kv-cache enabled
 * avg latency: 95ms
 * p95: 180ms
 * tokens/sec: 280
 * mem: 6.6GB
 * eval-acc: 0.890 -> -0.6%

Observations:
- q4_0 is promising for mem + throughput, but p95 latency needs attention. Suspect batching and attention kernel selection for short sequences.
- KV-cache warmup: first request after model load is ~2.3x slower; prefix caching reduces that to ~1.15x. Need to instrument the router to pre-warm common prefixes for chat templates.

text: Context / why this exists - We’re upgrading regional failover automation as part of Reliability Program (Q1–Q2 2026). Today, routing policy evaluation has a few gaps: - Non-deterministic behavior in edge cases (e.g., multiple signals firing, partial signal availability, time-window ambiguity). - Hard to reason about priority across trigger signals (reachability, 5xx burn, tail latency burn, capacity exhaustion). - No first-class “hold-down” / dampening in evaluation; logic is split between policy rules and external controller. - No consistent mechanism to enforce safety constraints per tenant/tier (max flips, blast radius caps, required approval). Goals - Add policy engine primitives so failover decisioning is: - Deterministic and auditable (same inputs => same decision; explainability built-in). - Compatible with ADR-042 (signal priority + corroboration) and ADR-043 (rollback + anti-flap). - Safe by default (rate limits, required corroboration, blast radius constraints). - Support both: - “Recommend” (dry-run) mode: compute candidate flips + evidence. - “Enforce” mode: same evaluation but returns executable actions. Non-goals - Replace the automation controller. The controller still orchestrates execution, approvals, and cross-system workflow. - Re-architect signal ingestion. We consume normalized, time-bucketed signals from Region Health + SLO toolkit. - Build customer-facing UI here. Terminology - Policy: configuration describing routing behavior for a route/service/model/tier.
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: consume normalized, time-bucketed signals from Region Health + SLO toolkit. - Build customer-facing UI here. Terminology - Policy: configuration describing routing behavior for a route/service/model/tier. - Region set: primary and fallback regions eligible for this route. - Trigger signal: normalized boolean or scalar indicating regional impairment or risk. - Decision: computed outcome (no-op, failover, partial shift, rollback, hold). - Action: executable change (policy flip, weight adjustment, circuit breaker mode) emitted by controller. Inputs / data model assumptions - Policy engine receives inputs as a single evaluation payload, including: - route_id (e.g., hosted.chat.completions, dedicated.pool.<pool_id>) - tenant_id (optional for shared Hosted; required for Dedicated) - tenant_tier (free/pro/enterprise/dedicated) - current_routing_state (primary region, weights, last_flip_at) - signals (per region, per signal type): - reachability: ok|degraded|down + confidence + last_updated - errors_5xx_burn: burn_rate + window + confidence - tail_latency_burn: burn_rate + p95/p99 deltas + confidence - capacity_headroom: percent + “hard gate” boolean - control_plane_health (optional): ok|degraded|down - evaluation_time (UTC epoch + monotonic sequence) - constraints (policy-level + tenant-level): max_flips_per_hour, min_hold_down_seconds, require_approval, blast_radius_limit Determinism requirements (core) 1) Stable ordering - Evaluation must not depend on map iteration order. - Rule
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: tenant-level): max_flips_per_hour, min_hold_down_seconds, require_approval, blast_radius_limit Determinism requirements (core) 1) Stable ordering - Evaluation must not depend on map iteration order. - Rule matching must define explicit ordering: by priority, then by tie-breakers. 2) Stable time semantics - All windowed signals must be anchored to evaluation_time and include: - effective_start, effective_end, and a “freshness” timestamp. - Engine must treat stale signals consistently: - If authoritative signals are stale beyond threshold, decision = HOLD (do not fail over) unless reachability is explicitly DOWN with high confidence. 3) Single decision per evaluation - For a given (route_id, tenant_id, evaluation_time), engine emits exactly one decision object, even if multiple actions are possible. - Multi-step actions (e.g., drain -> shift weights -> finalize) must be represented as a sequence recommendation but still within a single decision record. 4) Explainability - Decision must include: - decision_type (NO_OP | FAILOVER | PARTIAL_SHIFT | ROLLBACK | HOLD) - chosen_reason (canonical enum) - evidence: ordered list of signals used, with values, freshness, and thresholds. - rejected_candidates: (optional) top 1–3 alternatives and why they were not selected. Proposed changes to policy engine A) New predicates / functions 1) region_health(region, signal_type) -> {status, value, confidence, freshness_seconds} - Wrapper that pulls normalized data from payload and enforces freshness
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: policy engine A) New predicates / functions 1) region_health(region, signal_type) -> {status, value, confidence, freshness_seconds} - Wrapper that pulls normalized data from payload and enforces freshness calculations. 2) is_authoritative_reachability_down(region) -> bool - True only if: - reachability.status == down - confidence >= configured_min_confidence (default 0.9) - freshness_seconds <= reachability_max_staleness (default 30s) 3) burn_rate_exceeded(region, metric, window, threshold) -> bool - Works for errors_5xx_burn and tail_latency_burn. - Window must be one of approved windows (e.g., 1m/5m/30m) to avoid configuration drift. 4) capacity_hard_gate(region) -> bool - True if headroom below threshold AND capacity signal confidence is high. - Intended to prevent shifting into an already overloaded region. 5) corroborated(primary_signal, secondary_signal) -> bool - Enforces ADR-042 “required corroboration signals” when configured. - Example: if tail latency burn triggers, require either 5xx burn OR reachability degraded/down OR control plane degraded. 6) hold_down_active(last_flip_at, min_hold_down_seconds) -> bool - If true, policy engine returns HOLD unless reachability down is authoritative. 7) flip_budget_ok(tenant_id, route_id, max_flips_per_hour) -> bool - Input includes a precomputed counter from controller/state store (policy engine does not store state). 8) blast_radius_ok(tenant_tier, requested_action) -> bool - Enforces “can’t shift more than X% traffic for tier
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: a precomputed counter from controller/state store (policy engine does not store state). 8) blast_radius_ok(tenant_tier, requested_action) -> bool - Enforces “can’t shift more than X% traffic for tier Y without approval”. - For Hosted, blast radius is route-scoped; for Dedicated, pool-scoped. B) Deterministic evaluation mode (required) - Add an “evaluation_mode” field: - recommend: compute decision + evidence; do not mark as executable - enforce: compute decision; include explicit requested_action(s) - Engine must produce identical decision_type and evidence in both modes (action packaging differs). C) Signal hierarchy evaluation (ADR-042) Priority order (proposed canonical order for v1): 1) Reachability DOWN (authoritative) => FAILOVER (unless no healthy fallback) 2) Errors (5xx) fast burn (high severity) => FAILOVER or PARTIAL_SHIFT depending on blast radius and capacity 3) Tail latency burn (p99/p95) => PARTIAL_SHIFT first; escalate to FAILOVER if corroborated and sustained 4) Capacity exhaustion / hard gate => HOLD + load shed (controller action), avoid shifting into constrained region 5) Control plane degradation => HOLD (do not flip rapidly; prevent oscillation) Notes: - “No healthy fallback” means every eligible fallback region fails capacity_hard_gate OR reachability is down OR stale beyond threshold. - If no healthy fallback, decision should be HOLD and include “degrade_allowed=true” hint (controller can activate load shedding / retry-after). D) Policy language / config
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: OR stale beyond threshold. - If no healthy fallback, decision should be HOLD and include “degrade_allowed=true” hint (controller can activate load shedding / retry-after). D) Policy language / config schema additions Add new fields (names tentative): - failover: - enabled: boolean - eligible_regions: [us-east, eu-west, ...] - signal_requirements: - primary_trigger: enum - corroboration: [enum] - min_confidence: float - max_staleness_seconds: int - dampening: - min_hold_down_seconds: int - require_sustain_seconds: int (for non-reachability triggers) - safety: - require_approval: boolean (default true for prod initially) - max_flips_per_hour: int - max_traffic_shift_percent: int - rollback: - auto_rollback_enabled: boolean - rollback_after_seconds: int - rollback_require_healthy_primary_seconds: int E) Output schema Decision output (single object, JSON): - decision_id (stable hash of route_id + tenant_id + evaluation_time + policy_version) - policy_version - evaluation_time - decision_type - chosen_reason - target_state (string encoding; owned by controller) - requested_actions (string encoding; empty in recommend mode) - evidence (string encoding; ordered) - annotations (string encoding; includes thresholds, staleness, hold-down state) Note: In our implementation we’ll likely use structured objects, but for logging/audit we must generate a stable, versioned string representation. Safety constraints (ADR-043 alignment) - Anti-flap: - Enforce hold-down between flips for non-reachability
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: structured objects, but for logging/audit we must generate a stable, versioned string representation. Safety constraints (ADR-043 alignment) - Anti-flap: - Enforce hold-down between flips for non-reachability triggers. - Any rollback decision must respect a separate rollback hold-down (avoid ping-pong between primary and fallback). - Rate limiting: - flip_budget_ok must be true, else HOLD. - Approval hooks: - If require_approval is true, decision_type can still be FAILOVER but requested_actions must be empty and “approval_required=true” annotation set. - Tenant/tier gates: - Enterprise/Dedicated can be configured with stricter “do not cross data residency boundary” constraint (policy engine should surface “constraint_violation:data_residency” rather than attempt partial shifts). Edge cases and expected behavior 1) Partial outage in primary region - Reachability degraded (not down), 5xx burn elevated, capacity OK in fallback. - Expected: PARTIAL_SHIFT (e.g., shift 25–50% depending on max_traffic_shift_percent) if corroborated. If sustained and escalates to reachability down or error burn becomes severe => FAILOVER. 2) Signal missing / ingestion delayed - Any authoritative signal is stale beyond max_staleness_seconds. - Expected: HOLD. Evidence must explicitly call out staleness and which signal blocked action. 3) Fallback region constrained - Primary is failing (5xx burn), fallback capacity_hard_gate true. - Expected: HOLD + annotate “fallback_unhealthy_due_to_capacity”. Controller
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: which signal blocked action. 3) Fallback region constrained - Primary is failing (5xx burn), fallback capacity_hard_gate true. - Expected: HOLD + annotate “fallback_unhealthy_due_to_capacity”. Controller should invoke load shedding and retry-after rather than shifting load. 4) Control plane degraded - Control plane health degraded; data plane signals ambiguous. - Expected: HOLD unless reachability down is authoritative. (Avoid flipping policies when control plane is suspect.) 5) Multi-fallback selection - If multiple eligible fallback regions are healthy, selection tie-breakers: 1) policy-specified order 2) lowest latency probe (if available in inputs) 3) highest capacity headroom 4) stable sort by region name Testing plan (draft) Unit tests - Determinism: - Same payload with randomized key order => same decision_id, decision_type, evidence ordering. - Staleness gating: - Stale error burn + fresh reachability OK => HOLD. - Priority: - Reachability DOWN overrides hold-down. - 5xx burn overrides latency burn when both exceed thresholds. Integration tests - Replay recorded signal snapshots from staging incidents + controlled game day traces. - Validate output is compatible with controller dry-run UX (must contain reasons + evidence). Performance considerations - Evaluation must be cheap; do not add per-eval remote calls. - Evidence strings must be bounded in size. Cap rejected_candidates and evidence list. Operational / observability hooks - Emit a single log line per decision with:
title: Draft Spec: Policy Engine Extensions for Regional Failover Automation

text: cheap; do not add per-eval remote calls. - Evidence strings must be bounded in size. Cap rejected_candidates and evidence list. Operational / observability hooks - Emit a single log line per decision with: - decision_id, route_id, tenant_id (hashed in shared Hosted contexts), decision_type, chosen_reason - key thresholds and freshness - Export metrics: - policy_engine_decisions_total{decision_type, chosen_reason, tier} - policy_engine_hold_reasons_total{reason} - policy_engine_eval_latency_ms Open questions - Exact shape of “target_state” string: should it be a controller-owned serialization of desired weights, or should the policy engine output high-level intent only? - Do we want PARTIAL_SHIFT as a first-class decision type (recommended), or represent it as FAILOVER with weight < 100%? - How strict should corroboration be for tail latency burn? (ADR-042 indicates corroboration required; we need the exact default set.) - Where should data residency constraints live? - Option A: policy engine validates “region set allowed” and blocks action. - Option B: upstream policy compilation prevents invalid eligible_regions. Near-term next steps - Align with Miles/Ravi on canonical signal enum + reason enum. - Draft schema PR for policy config additions (behind feature flag). - Implement deterministic ordering + evidence output in policy engine. - Wire to dry-run mode (ENG-2415) so the controller can display “recommended action + confidence + evidence” on game day. Appendix: candidate

text: Purpose This is a rough exploratory sketch for a hybrid continuous-batching scheduler weall Slope Window Confluence (SWC). The goal is to reconcile token-level merging benefits (higher throughput, better kernel utilization) with request-level latency SLOs and per-customer fairness. Trying to find a simple, low-operator-friction design that plays nicely with KV cache affinity and kernel selection heuristics. Motivation / observed pain points - Pure token-level coalescers push throughput but occasionally catastrophically increase p99 for small, latency-sensitive requests (chatty agents, textbox autocompete). - Request-level batching preserves request isolation but underutilizes GPUs when many short requests are arriving and have small token counts. - KV cache hotness matters: merging tokens from requests that don't share KV state yields more memory pressure and more KV misses on kernel paths. - Static fairness (round-robin or fixed weights) does not adapt to workload dynamics (bursts, multi-tenant hot spots). Key ideas (high level) 1) Slope window: for each incoming request we maintain a lightweight per-request latency slope estimate: the recent trend of waiting time vs tokens emitted. This estimates how sensitive the request is to waiting. Fast-rising slope -> low tolerance for batching. Flatter slope -> good candidate for merge. 2) Confluence windows: continuous overlapping windows (not fixed micro-batches) that admit token fragments. Each window has a dynamic merge threshold
title: Slope Window Confluence Scheduler (exploratory)

text: batching. Flatter slope -> good candidate for merge. 2) Confluence windows: continuous overlapping windows (not fixed micro-batches) that admit token fragments. Each window has a dynamic merge threshold driven by expected kernel efficiency delta and the current KV affinity score. 3) Two-tier fairness: per-tenant deficit counters at the request level (ensures long-term share) + token-granular fairness credits for short-term burst smoothing. 4) KV-aware admission: window merge score penalizes mixing requests with orthogonal KV prefixes by an affinity multiplier; concurrently, we allow speculative KV-prefetch for high-value merges (configurable per-tenant). 5) Kernel steering signals: windows attach a preferred kernel hint (fp16/quantized/fast-attention) based on token length distribution and merged batch size; scheduler avoids merges that would cause a costly kernel fallback. Detailed sketch - Request arrival flow: - On accept, timestamp + initial estimate: tokens_remaining (from client metadata or tokenizer prediction) and client_tier weight. Initialize request_slope = 0. - Request enters an "open" slot. Open slots are grouped into active slope-buckets (quantized slope bands: e.g., 0-2ms/token, 2-8ms/token, >8ms/token). - Each slope-bucket maintains a candidate queue prioritized by earliest-deadline (estimated client-level p95 target) adjusted by deficit credits. - Window formation: - Every 1ms we attempt to form a confluence window from candidate queues. The window has a soft
title: Slope Window Confluence Scheduler (exploratory)

text: by earliest-deadline (estimated client-level p95 target) adjusted by deficit credits. - Window formation: - Every 1ms we attempt to form a confluence window from candidate queues. The window has a soft size target (tokens_target) computed as tokens_target = base_target * kernel_efficiency_multiplier * kv_affinity_boost, where base_target is a function of hardware and current occupancy. - Candidates are selected greedily from the flattest slope-bucket downward (favor coalescing tolerant requests) but respecting per-tenant deficit ceilings. - Each candidate adds a marginal utility value: delta_throughput / delta_latency_risk. The window accepts candidates until marginal utility drops below a threshold. - Execution and backpressure: - When a window fires, build the merged input (concatenate token streams, maintain per-request end markers and KV affinity metadata). Choose kernel according to the merged distribution; if kernel fallback is needed mid-exec, abort merge for next window and increment a kernel-fallback penalty. - On completion, update per-request slope via exponential moving average of observed waiting time vs tokens served. Update deficit counters (debit for tokens served, credit on long idle periods). Fairness model - Long-term fairness: per-tenant token credits accumulate proportionally to committed weight; tokens served decrement credits. If credits negative, tenant is rate-limited from contributing to candidate windows until credits replenish. - Short-term smoothing:
title: Slope Window Confluence Scheduler (exploratory)

text: proportionally to committed weight; tokens served decrement credits. If credits negative, tenant is rate-limited from contributing to candidate windows until credits replenish. - Short-term smoothing: small per-request tokens-credits allow temporary bursts even if tenant credits are low (keeps UX snappy for short chat bursts). - Latency safety: any request with slope above a safety threshold or explicit latency tag from client is protected: it can skip merges or be limited to windows under a stricter latency cap. KV cache integration - Affinity score: for each request we compute a KV fingerprint vector (coarse): namespace, top-N keys expected, model-route. Similar fingerprints have high affinity. When merging, we compute window affinity = mean pairwise affinity. Windows with high affinity get a reduced merge penalty. - Speculative prefetching: for high-value merges (high throughput gain, strong tenant weight), we can prefetch likely KV pages to reduce miss penalty. This is controlled with a budget per-host and per-tenant. - Eviction interplay: merged windows that raise KV working set must be charged against an eviction budget and can be deprioritized if host memory pressure is high. Kernel steering and execution hints - Each window computes: expected seq_len distribution, token_count, and kv_miss_risk. If quantized kernel benefits are >X% and kv_miss_risk low -> prefer low-precision kernel. Otherwise choose fallback. - Windows failing to meet kernel preferences incur a penalty
title: Slope Window Confluence Scheduler (exploratory)

text: token_count, and kv_miss_risk. If quantized kernel benefits are >X% and kv_miss_risk low -> prefer low-precision kernel. Otherwise choose fallback. - Windows failing to meet kernel preferences incur a penalty that reduces their marginal utility for subsequent scheduling rounds. Metrics and experiments - Core metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency per-tenant, tokens/sec per-GPU, kernel utilization (SM occupancy proxy), kv-miss-rate, merged_request_fraction, fairness_error (deviation from ideal share over 1h window). - Experiments to run: 1) Synthetic mixed workload: 70% short low-latency requests (<=16 tokens), 30% long LLM completions (>=256 tokens), compare SWC vs token-only coalescer vs request-isolated scheduler. 2) KV-affinity study: vary fraction of requests sharing KV prefixes and measure miss rates and end-to-end latency. 3) Kernel-fallback sensitivity: force mismatched kernel conditions and measure rollback and penalty calibration. Operational concerns / open questions - Complexity: does slope estimation and marginal utility computation add too much CPU overhead? Can it be approximated with cheap counters? - Predicting tokens_remaining: many clients don't supply reliable estimates; we need fallbacks (recent-history, model-side estimator). Mispredictions change slope bands — how sensitive is system? - Stability under adversarial tenants: tenants could spoof low tokens_remaining to game priority. Need server-side sanity checks and penalties. - Rollout path: start with host-level
title: Slope Window Confluence Scheduler (exploratory)

text: sensitive is system? - Stability under adversarial tenants: tenants could spoof low tokens_remaining to game priority. Need server-side sanity checks and penalties. - Rollout path: start with host-level feature gate + per-tenant opt-in. Run in observation-only mode collecting utility and fairness_error signals for 2 weeks. Then flip to soft-enforcement with conservative thresholds. Implementation sketch / TODOs - [ ] Proto: lightweight per-request proto (id, arrival_ts, tokens_hint, slope_est, kv_fingerprint, tenant_id, client_latency_tag) - [ ] Simulator: event-driven sim to validate merge heuristics and measure metrics under varied workloads (use existing microbench harness). - [ ] KV fingerprint design: choose fields and approximate similarity metric (Jaccard on top-keys or namespace-hash). - [ ] CPU budget target: ensure scheduler loop is <= 1% host CPU at 100k req/s (profile early). - [ ] Telemetry: add instrumentation points for marginal utility, window composition, kernel hint selection, and deficit counters. Questions for the team - Is per-request slope estimator acceptable complexity or prefer a token-waiting percentile per-tenant? - How aggressive should speculative prefetch budgets be, especially for multi-tenant hosts? - Are we comfortable exposing a client-side latency tag (trusted) or must server trust only observed behavior? Notes-to-self - Keep this doc deliberately high-level; implement sim + small PoC in the continuous coalescer module and iterate. This hybrid
title: Slope Window Confluence Scheduler (exploratory)

text: tag (trusted) or must server trust only observed behavior? Notes-to-self - Keep this doc deliberately high-level; implement sim + small PoC in the continuous coalescer module and iterate. This hybrid is intended to reduce tail impact from pure token batching while reclaiming most throughput benefits. References / inspiration - Prior internal: continuous-batcher-design-notes-token-level-vs-request-level (for baseline comparisons) - External literature: micro-batching heuristics from video transcoding schedulers, deficit round robin variants, and KV-prefetch papers. End of sketch.

text: Purpose: This doc captures notes and a pragmatic design direction for multi-region traffic steering across Redwood’s control plane and edge: GeoDNS, Anycast, region-preference policies, and failover behavior. The intent is to surface trade-offs, give concrete parameter recommendations, and outline an operational rollout path. Scope: - Ingress routing for customer-facing inference endpoints (API and websocket streams). - Control-plane APIs and distribution for routing policies and real-time regional weights. - Failure/fallback behavior and observability required for SLOs. Non-goals: - Detailed BGP peering configs or vendor-specific router commands. - Deep dive into egress/data-plane security (TLS/key mgmt handled elsewhere). Background / constraints: - Regions: NA-east, NA-west, EU, APAC. Each region runs edge proxies + regional pools for serving. - Customers can pin to Dedicated or Private regions; hosted customers default to global endpoint. - We must balance: latency (user proximity), cost (region price differences), capacity (regional saturation), and regulatory constraints (data locality). - Two main technical primitives available: GeoDNS (region-weighted answers with TTL control) and Anycast (single IP announced from multiple PoPs). Both can be used in combination. Design goals and acceptance criteria: - Route most traffic to the closest healthy region with p95 added latency within 20% of local baseline. - No customer-visible outage on a single-region failure if any region
title: Geo routing & control-plane design notes

text: goals and acceptance criteria: - Route most traffic to the closest healthy region with p95 added latency within 20% of local baseline. - No customer-visible outage on a single-region failure if any region has spare capacity (failover target within 60s-3min). - Allow operator-defined preferences (cost-sensitive, regulatory, capacity-aware) per-customer or per-route. - Predictable, observable behavior during rollouts and incidents. Decision matrix: GeoDNS vs Anycast (short): - GeoDNS: Pros: fine-grained control per-FQDN, weightable, easier to reason about failover ordering, works with existing CDN/DNS providers. Cons: DNS caching causes slower convergence (TTL tradeoffs), more brittle for clients behind aggressive DNS caches. - Anycast: Pros: fast failover for BGP withdraws, simple single-IP customer endpoint, client-less reliance on DNS. Cons: less control by region (routing depends on network topology / upstream ISPs), harder to steer by policy, can send traffic to undesired region during route flaps, requires BGP and announced IP space. Recommendation: hybrid approach by route class: - Public API endpoints: primary = GeoDNS with low TTL (20–60s) and per-region weighted answers; secondary = Anycast for global fallback (announce reserved IP only from healthy regions). - Low-latency streaming (websockets): favor Anycast within a region footprint and GeoDNS for cross-region steering because persistent TCP state and NAT behavior favor stable 5-tuple routing. Control-plane model:
title: Geo routing & control-plane design notes

text: Low-latency streaming (websockets): favor Anycast within a region footprint and GeoDNS for cross-region steering because persistent TCP state and NAT behavior favor stable 5-tuple routing. Control-plane model: - Central policy service (single source of truth) where operators and automated systems express routing policies in a small declarative language. - Policy components: region preference list, capacity thresholds, cost weight, regulatory flags, health thresholds, and temporal rules (canary windows). - Policies are compiled to two artifacts: DNS weight table (per-FQDN per-region weights + TTL) and edge-proxy directives (local weight adjustments, ejection). - Distribution: use a streaming config bus (gRPC pub/sub) to push updates to edge control-plane agents. DNS provider updates happen via an orchestration plugin that applies weight diffs and validates propagation. - Versioning: each policy publish creates an immutable version id. Rollbacks use previous version id with an automated canary check. Policy example (conceptual): - customer A: prefer NA-east -> NA-west -> EU, reject APAC (data residency). fallback_policy = sequential; capacity_threshold = 70% (don’t send more if >= threshold). Failover & ejection behavior: - Active health detection is multi-layer: DNS provider health, edge-proxy health, and regional capacity signals from serving runtime. - When a region is unhealthy (high error rate or CPU>90%): control plane marks region degraded and applies steering rules: 1)
title: Geo routing & control-plane design notes

text: health, edge-proxy health, and regional capacity signals from serving runtime. - When a region is unhealthy (high error rate or CPU>90%): control plane marks region degraded and applies steering rules: 1) Reduce DNS weight gradually (from 100% -> 50% -> 10%) over 30–90s to give room to in-flight requests. 2) Instruct edge proxies to evade local region routing for new sessions (immediate). 3) If region remains degraded beyond a threshold (configurable e.g., 120s), remove region from DNS answers and trigger Anycast withdrawal from that region for global fallback. - This staged approach helps avoid flapping and makes failover observable and reversible. DNS TTLs and weight strategy: - Default TTL: 30s for hosted API endpoints. Rationale: balance convergence speed and DNS query volume. - Canary TTL: 20s for canary subdomains during rollouts. - Weight change cadence: control plane should throttle weight deltas to avoid sudden global shifts (max 30% delta per 30s). Health checks: - Multi-probe: TCP connect, application-level (HTTP /healthz returning schema), and synthetic inference sample for end-to-end validation. - Health aggregation window: sliding 30s with p95 latency and 5xx error rate. - Use both active probes (from multiple vantage points) and passive metrics from runtime (error counts, queue depth). Affinity & stickiness: - For session-based workloads (streaming), edge proxies track session affinity and use source-ip/cookie session keys. - When steering away from a region, control
title: Geo routing & control-plane design notes

text: counts, queue depth). Affinity & stickiness: - For session-based workloads (streaming), edge proxies track session affinity and use source-ip/cookie session keys. - When steering away from a region, control plane signals proxies to avoid redirecting existing sticky sessions; only new sessions get steered. Observability & SLOs to track: - Regional p50/p95 latency, regional request success rate, DNS answer distribution, time to failover (time from health event to >90% traffic shift), control-plane distribution lag (published -> agent ack), DNS provider propagation time. - Dashboards: per-route traffic map (by region), automated anomaly alerts for unexpected cross-region traffic shifts. Test plan and canary rollout: - Unit tests for policy compiler. Integration tests that simulate regional failure and validate traffic shifts in staging with DNS TTL=5s. - Pre-roll: run synthetic traffic from multiple continents to verify steering preferences. - Rollout: start with internal-only endpoints (100% internal traffic) -> limited customers -> global. Operational runbooks (short): - Regional outage detected: 1) tag region degraded in control plane -> triggers weight-shed. 2) confirm via edge logs and synthetic checks. 3) if persistent, remove from DNS answers and withdraw Anycast announcement. 4) communicate via incident channel and customer notifications if SLA impacted. - Emergency rollback: publish previous policy version id to control plane which will restore weights and proxies within
title: Geo routing & control-plane design notes

text: 4) communicate via incident channel and customer notifications if SLA impacted. - Emergency rollback: publish previous policy version id to control plane which will restore weights and proxies within versioned release windows. Open questions / risks: - DNS caching behaviour of large CDNs and mobile carriers can hold answers longer than TTL. How often do we need to fall back to connection-level redirect strategies? - Anycast blackholing risk during BGP updates and how operators will coordinate with backbone partners. - Cost model: automated region-preference to cheaper regions may increase tail latency — how to expose this tradeoff clearly to customers? Next steps / action items: - Validate DNS provider supports per-record weight diffs via API with <30s apply time. (Owner: Ethan) - Prototype control-plane policy compiler and streaming distribution in staging; include simulated failures. (Owner: Priya) - Create synthetic test harness for multi-region failover with configurable TTLs. (Owner: Sofia) Notes: this is a working doc; expect follow-ups with more formal policy schema and sample config snippets for the SDK.
title: Geo routing & control-plane design notes

text: Purpose This PRD proposes a set of mechanisms collectively called the Graceful Deescalation Protocols (GDP) and a complementary Credit Smoothener service for Redwood Dedicated pools. Goal: reduce disruptive preemptions, smooth tenant experience during sudden demand drops and spikes, and provide predictable, auditable capacity handoffs while preserving SLOs for latency-sensitive lanes. Problem statement Customers buying Dedicated capacity expect near-deterministic performance. However, real traffic exhibits sharp microbursts and abrupt drops (e.g., queue drains after batch jobs finish, traffic fan-ins), which lead to: - abrupt eviction or hard throttles when we reclaim or reassign GPUs, causing user errors and bad tail latency spikes - excessive oscillation of reserved capacity (spin-up/down churn) increasing cost - brittle tenant isolation when simple hard cutoffs are used (no graceful transition) This doc outlines an approach that combines: a credit-based smoothing bank, epochal handshakes for reserve decommit, and a deescalation window that steps fidelity down before hard eviction. The goal is to make degradation graceful and observable while keeping operator complexity bounded. Design principles - Latency-first: avoid tail-latency regressions for high-SLO lanes even during rebalancing. - Predictable economics: provide clear accounting for credits and burst usage so customers understand cost tradeoffs. - Operator-sane: mechanisms should integrate with existing autoscaler and
title: Graceful Deescalation Protocols and Credit Smoothener - PRD Draft

text: rebalancing. - Predictable economics: provide clear accounting for credits and burst usage so customers understand cost tradeoffs. - Operator-sane: mechanisms should integrate with existing autoscaler and reservation ledger with minimal new operational surface area. - Observable and reversible: every state transition emits structured telemetry and can be rolled back or accelerated. Key components 1) Credit Smoothener (service) - Purpose: decouple instantaneous demand from immediate compute reassignments by issuing time-bound credits that represent transient capacity allowances. - Credits: small, ephemeral tokens (seconds-to-minutes lifetime) that allow a tenant to exceed their committed concurrency/reserved slots without immediate preemption. Credits are charged to a per-tenant credit balance. - Credit policies: configurable at customer or pool level: burst_quota_per_minute, refill_rate, max_outstanding_credits. Different policies for fidelity lanes: high-SLO lanes get conservative refill; best-effort lanes get aggressive refill. - Accounting: credits are visible in Console (tokenized ledger) and are billed/discounted per contract. Credits decay with a configurable curve (linear by default) to encourage efficiency. 2) Deescalation Window (grace sequence) - When we need to reclaim or reduce capacity for a tenant, the system enters a Deescalation Window instead of immediate eviction. This window is staged: - Notice phase (T_notice): client gets structured header indicating impending
title: Graceful Deescalation Protocols and Credit Smoothener - PRD Draft

text: reduce capacity for a tenant, the system enters a Deescalation Window instead of immediate eviction. This window is staged: - Notice phase (T_notice): client gets structured header indicating impending scale-down and remaining time. - Soft-fidelity step (T_soft): runtime switches to degraded compute path (e.g., smaller batch sizes, quantized variant, reduced KV cache), preserving correctness but reducing throughput. - Prefetch drain (T_drain): KV/prefix caches are checkpointed to warm pools to minimize coldstart on re-grant. - Hard reclaim (T_reclaim): actual resources reclaimed if demand persists. - Each phase emits events and metrics. The default Deescalation Window is 90–180s but is configurable by pool. 3) Epochal Handoff and Rescind Handshake - Capacity moves happen in epochs (short-lived leases). The autoscaler negotiates lease end via a Rescind Handshake: propose -> ack -> final. If a tenant has credits, the handshake can be extended automatically by the Credit Smoothener. - Handoff includes a fidelity descriptor: what runtime fallback to apply if capacity not returned (model variant, beam size, temperature restrictions). 4) Isolation Safety Leash - A soft leash per-tenant that prevents a single tenant from causing more than X% of a pool’s transient cold starts during a configured window. The leash uses historical cold-start incidence to limit burst acceptance if it would hurt overall pool health. Observability and SLO mapping We will add the following metrics so product
title: Graceful Deescalation Protocols and Credit Smoothener - PRD Draft

text: configured window. The leash uses historical cold-start incidence to limit burst acceptance if it would hurt overall pool health. Observability and SLO mapping We will add the following metrics so product and SRE can reason about GDP effects: - credits_issued_total, credits_consumed_rate, credits_balance_by_tenant - deescalation_phase_duration{notice,soft,drain,reclaim} - soft-fidelity_switch_count, fidelity_revert_time - tenant_cold_start_rate, pool_cold_start_tail_p99 - reclaim_success_rate, reclaim_rollback_count - billing_credits_adjustments_total Dashboards and alerts - Per-tenant dashboard showing credit balance, recent deescalations, and fidelity transitions. - Pool-level heatmap of deescalation events and cold-start spikes. - Alerts: reclaim_failure_rate > 1% / 10m, credits_balance_negative for > 5m, deescalation_phase_long_tail (any phase > configured percentiles). Failure modes and mitigations - Credit exhaustion: if a tenant runs out mid-burst we fall back to immediate soft-fidelity step (no sudden hard kill). Mitigation: recommend conservative credit buffer for high-SLO customers. - Lost handshake messages: lease remains in limbo — autoscaler will escalate to hard reclaim after epoch timeout and emit for operator attention. - Abuse/race where tenants repeatedly game credit refill: throttle refill rate and enforce heat penalty in leash. Operational runbook (quick) - Operator sees recurring deescalation for a tenant: check credits_balance; inspect workload fingerprint
title: Graceful Deescalation Protocols and Credit Smoothener - PRD Draft

text: credit refill: throttle refill rate and enforce heat penalty in leash. Operational runbook (quick) - Operator sees recurring deescalation for a tenant: check credits_balance; inspect workload fingerprint and advise model/quantization configuration; consider increasing reserved pool for that tenant. - If pool-wide cold starts spike during rollout: reduce leash strictness temporarily and enable prewarm probes; consider widening Deescalation Window. - Emergency rollback: manual flag to disable GDP for pool, causing immediate hard-reclaims (used only in severe incidents). API and Console changes - New API: /v1/dedicated/credits.getBalance, .requestTopUp, .policy.update (REST + SDK). - Rollout controls in Console: toggle GDP per-pool, configure Deescalation Window durations, set credit pricing and refill rules. Success criteria and guardrails Primary success metrics (measured in A/B pilot): - Reduce eviction-induced request errors by >= 70% in pilot customers during peak microburst events. - Decrease p99 tail latency regression during pool rebalances by >= 40% for high-SLO lanes. - Keep additional infra cost from Credit Smoothener < 12% of baseline during pilot (measured as net GPU-hours from extensions). Rollout plan Phase 0 (internal QA): implement simulator to replay historical Dedicated traffic and validate Deescalation Window parameters and credit curves. Phase 1 (pilot): enable GDP in one non-critical region for 3 enterprise customers (opt-in) across high-SLO and best-effort
title: Graceful Deescalation Protocols and Credit Smoothener - PRD Draft

text: Dedicated traffic and validate Deescalation Window parameters and credit curves. Phase 1 (pilot): enable GDP in one non-critical region for 3 enterprise customers (opt-in) across high-SLO and best-effort lanes, 6 weeks. Phase 2 (scale): after monitoring and tuning, enable for all Dedicated pools with conservative defaults and expose policies in Console. Phase 3 (GA): formalize billing and SLA implications; include on-boarding docs and runbooks. Open questions and tradeoffs - Pricing model: should credits be billed at peak or discounted? We need sales/legal guidance. - Deescalation window length: user tolerance vs reclaim velocity — empirical calibration required. - Complexity vs benefit: how many customers need this to justify operational cost? Pilot will inform. Appendix: simulator notes - Simulator should replay tenant-level concurrency traces and support toggling credit refill curves (linear, exponential decay, step). - Can simulate adversarial tenants to test leash and heat penalties. Next steps (next 2 weeks) - Engineering spike: prototype Credit Smoothener API and a minimal Deescalation Window orchestration (2 sprints). - Product: finalize default policy values and pilot customer list. - SRE: design telemetry schema and SLI targets for reclaim success and cold-starts. Contacts Aisha Patel (product lead) — aisha@redwood.example.com Marco Ruiz (eng lead — autoscaler) — marco@redwood.example.com Lena Huang (SRE) — lena@redwood.example.com
title: Graceful Deescalation Protocols and Credit Smoothener - PRD Draft

text:
Context: weekly export of the dashboard that breaks GPU utilization out by cluster and pool, then groups by priority class. Goal: surface skew between priority pools (critical/standard/batch/burst), identify emergent saturation, and recommend short actions for the 24–72h runway.

Date range: 2026-10-05 -> 2026-10-11 (7 full days)
Generated by: infra-metrics-job v2.8 (bigquery export -> sheet)
Query used (simplified):
 SELECT cluster, pool, priority, region, DATE(window) as day, avg_util_pct, p95_latency_ms, queued_slots, preemptions
 FROM infra.gpu_utilization_daily
 WHERE day BETWEEN '2026-10-05' AND '2026-10-11'
 GROUP BY cluster, pool, priority, region, day

Executive summary:
- Fleet-average utilization (7d weighted by GPU-hours): 64.1% (target operating band: 55-70%).
- Critical pools are at 79.4% avg (above preferred cap of 75%) with a rising 3-day trend.
- Standard pools: 62.0% avg with pronounced weekday peaks and nightly troughing.
- Batch/burst pools: 35.8% avg but high variance (range: 8% -> 87%) — several pools show underutilized pockets alongside microbursts.
- Top 3 congested clusters (by 95th p95 latency and queued slots): cl-us-west-3, cl-eu-central-2, cl-apac-sg-1. Total queued_slots across fleet peaking 2.3k on 2026-10-08.

text: Context and intent: This is a working note to consolidate an operationally practical approach for control-plane async workflows across regions. The focus: how the router (control-plane request router + policy engine) should pick paths, when to fall back to lower tiers, how to coordinate distributed sagas across regions, and pragmatic idempotency/retry semantics that avoid thrash and double-side effects. Goals: - Provide a concise decision matrix for routing + fallback behavior (fast path -> degraded path -> queueing -> fallback responses). - Standardize retry and idempotency token semantics for control-plane commands (e.g., model deploy, scale, pin/version changes). - Surface tradeoffs for orchestrator vs choreography in multi-region sagas and recommend a hybrid pattern. - List actionable TODOs, instrumentation hooks, and a small message-format sketch. High-level assumptions: - Control-plane operations are mostly eventually consistent and can be long-running (>30s to minutes). - We run active-active regions; control-plane metadata is replicated with asynchronous replication and causal ordering guarantees from the metadata service. - Workers processing commands are region-local by default (for data locality and IAM), but can failover to other regions if explicitly routed. - We need predictable retry/backoff to avoid thundering herd on downstream infra (KMS, infra APIs, cloud provider quotas). Router / policy engine responsibilities (summary): - Evaluate request.priority, request.tenant_tier,
title: Saga router fallback matrix & async orchestration notes

text: retry/backoff to avoid thundering herd on downstream infra (KMS, infra APIs, cloud provider quotas). Router / policy engine responsibilities (summary): - Evaluate request.priority, request.tenant_tier, region_affinity, SLA (sync/async), and idempotency metadata. - Deterministically decide: execute-in-region, enqueue-in-region, proxy-to-dedicated-control-plane (dedicated capacity), or return a deferred-accept (202 + job-id). - Apply per-tenant fallback policy: e.g., for enterprise tier, prefer synchronous success with cross-region failover; for free-tier, prefer queueing and eventual completion. Routing + fallback matrix (textual): Columns: request_tier | synchronous_capable? | preferred_execution | fallback_sequence - enterprise | yes | execute-in-region-primary -> failover-to-region-secondary -> proxy-to-dedicated -> enqueue -> alert + 500 if impossible - standard | partial | execute-in-region (with relaxed timeouts) -> enqueue-in-region -> return 202 + polling-hints - free | no | enqueue-low-priority -> best-effort-execute-in-batch -> return 202 + long-poll suggestion Notes on 'enqueue' vs 'proxy-to-dedicated': - enqueue-in-region: create a durable work-item in the region-local queue (SQS / PubSub / Kafka). Preserves region locality and respects quotas. - proxy-to-dedicated: if tenant has Dedicated capacity, route to that control-plane; expect lower latency and stronger SLOs. This uses different auth path and tracking. Queue topology options (pros/cons): 1) Per-tenant queue
title: Saga router fallback matrix & async orchestration notes

text: tenant has Dedicated capacity, route to that control-plane; expect lower latency and stronger SLOs. This uses different auth path and tracking. Queue topology options (pros/cons): 1) Per-tenant queue (single-tenant queues) - Pros: easy tenant isolation, per-tenant retries, assertion of order. - Cons: heavy if many tenants; ops overhead. 2) Per-region shared queue with tenant sharding key - Pros: fewer queues to manage, cheaper. - Cons: requires worker-level tenant-id isolation and backpressure mechanisms. 3) Global queue with region-aware worker assignment - Pros: centralized scheduling, easier global rate-limits. - Cons: cross-region latency; requires strong anti-duplication. Recommendation: Use per-region shared queue with strong tenant sharding key and rate-limit headers; create ephemeral per-tenant FIFO lanes for mission-critical tenants (enterprise). Retry semantics and idempotency: - All control-plane commands must accept an idempotency_key (UUID v4 or user-supplied stable token). - System idempotency window: configurable per command type; default 24 hours for deploy-like commands, 7 days for long-running lifecycle operations. - Retry policy (default): immediate fast retry 1x, exponential backoff starting at 500ms, max 6 attempts, then escalate to queueing/fallback. For network errors -> retry. For 409/optimistic-conflict -> do not retry automatically, return to caller with conflict info unless caller opts for retry. - Workers must persist attempt_count and last_error in
title: Saga router fallback matrix & async orchestration notes

text: network errors -> retry. For 409/optimistic-conflict -> do not retry automatically, return to caller with conflict info unless caller opts for retry. - Workers must persist attempt_count and last_error in the work-item metadata. On worker crash, when work-item becomes available again, broker should apply jittered visibility timeout and increase backoff. Idempotency storage and garbage collection: - Store idempotency entries in the control-plane metadata DB with keys: (tenant_id, command_type, idempotency_key) -> {result_ref, created_at, status, result_shard}. - GC policy: keep for idempotency_window + 1 day. For very high QPS tenants, use a bounded LRU cache with persistence to avoid replay storms. Saga coordination patterns (orchestrator vs choreography): - Choreography (event-driven): Each service emits events for its local state change; compensating actions are owned by the emitting service. Low coupling, easy to scale, but hard to reason globally and to observe for long sagas across regions. - Orchestration (central orchestrator): Central saga coordinator drives steps and keeps state. Easier to reason (single source of truth for saga), easier rollback/compensation coordination, but becomes a central dependency and scaling point. Hybrid recommendation (pragmatic): Use an orchestrator for multi-step tenant-facing control-plane flows (e.g., create-dedicated-cluster, attach-key, commit-policy) and choreography for intra-region post-processing tasks (metrics cleanup, audit logs).
title: Saga router fallback matrix & async orchestration notes

text: orchestrator for multi-step tenant-facing control-plane flows (e.g., create-dedicated-cluster, attach-key, commit-policy) and choreography for intra-region post-processing tasks (metrics cleanup, audit logs). Orchestrator runs in the region of origin and persists saga state in the metadata service with replication metadata; orchestrator can spawn local choreographer events for each step. Multi-region considerations for sagas: - Region-of-origin anchor: the request defines an origin region that is the primary orchestrator. If origin region is degraded, we can failover orchestrator to a standby in another region, but only after acquiring a durable lease to avoid split-brain. - Lease semantics: Use short (e.g., 30s) leader leases with renewals and transaction logs. On failover, candidate region must verify last-committed step idempotently before resume. - Cross-region I/O: avoid synchronous cross-region calls in hot paths. Prefer eventual propagation via event streams. If synchronous cross-region is required, put a strict timeout and fallback path (enqueue or compensation). Failure modes and example handling: 1) Worker processes step but fails to ack due to crash -> broker visibility timeout expires, step redelivered -> worker must detect prior completion via idempotency_key/result_ref. 2) Network partition: metadata writes may be delayed; orchestrator should write 'pending' step record and publish step-event locally; workers should be tolerant to missing global metadata when possible;
title: Saga router fallback matrix & async orchestration notes

text: Network partition: metadata writes may be delayed; orchestrator should write 'pending' step record and publish step-event locally; workers should be tolerant to missing global metadata when possible; use causal id fields in messages. 3) Double-commit risk during failover -> require last-committed-step id check and idempotent apply operations on resources (cloud API often allows idempotent create with client token). Instrumentation & observability hooks (must-have): - Saga trace id on every log/event. Correlate across regions. - Per-step timing and retry counts exposed on dashboards. - Work-item visibility timeline (queued -> dequeued -> processing -> retry -> done/failed). - Alerting rules: step retry_count > threshold in last X minutes; queue growth rate > N per minute; replication lag > Y ms Message format sketch (JSON text): { \"job_id\": \"uuid\", \"tenant_id\": \"t-123\", \"origin_region\": \"us-west1\", \"command\": \"apply-model-pin\", \"idempotency_key\": \"client-supplied-or-generated\", \"payload\": { /* small canonical payload */ }, \"attempts\": 0, \"created_at\": \"2026-02-15T14:22:00Z\" } (Keep payload small; put large artifacts in blob store and reference by URL + signed token.) Open questions / tradeoffs we still need to decide: - Should the router return a 202 with polling hints by default for long-running control-plane ops, or try to keep callers connected with streaming updates? (Implications for API surface.) - GC window defaults: will 24h idempotency be sufficient
title: Saga router fallback matrix & async orchestration notes

text: polling hints by default for long-running control-plane ops, or try to keep callers connected with streaming updates? (Implications for API surface.) - GC window defaults: will 24h idempotency be sufficient for enterprise flows that can take days? Offer per-tenant override? - Exactly which operations are allowed to cross-region synchronously under strong consistency? (KMS transactions are tricky.) Immediate action items (next steps): - (Priya) Draft a small RFC enumerating the router decision matrix and API changes for idempotency_key acceptance (due: 2026-02-28). - (Ethan) Prototype per-region shared queue with tenant sharding and test backpressure behavior under 2x production traffic (due: 2026-03-14). - (Samir) Add saga trace ids to existing control-plane logs and add dashboard: "saga step latency" (beta) (due: 2026-03-07). Notes to self / rough todos on UX: - Provide a lightweight client helper that auto-generates idempotency_key and retries with our policy. Expose environment variable hooks for tests to disable retries. - Add clear API docs for which endpoints are synchronous vs asynchronous and provide sample polling code. References and related tickets: JIRA-PLAT-483, PR #214 on serving-runtime, confluence:platform-sagas-v2. Unresolved TODOs in this doc: - finalize the lease duration and exact failover handshake protocol (open: Samir to propose). - decide GC policy for idempotency per tenant tier. End of note. -- Priya (draft)
title: Saga router fallback matrix & async orchestration notes

text: PURPOSE This is a working draft that introduces the Axis Cascade method for stitching quarterly initiatives to a single executive narrative and the all-company comms for the next 90→180 day window. High-level goal: Rapidly align the leadership cohort on three cross-cutting initiatives that materially change our inference unit economics and customer reliability story. Use this doc as a lightweight decision matrix plus the exec talking points for the leadership update. PRINCIPLES (quick) - Single narrative: choose one coherent frame per quarter rather than many unconnected asks. - Outcome-first OKRs: start from measurable customer/monetary outcomes, then map initiatives. - Risk-prioritize: surface failure modes early, set contingency triggers. - Lightweight comms: single page for execs + 3 talking bullets for each manager. AXIS CASCADE OVERVIEW (how it works) 1) Axis = primary company objective (North Star for the cycle). 2) Cascade = three initiative lanes (Infra, Product, Go-to-Market) that each get 1–2 focused KR candidates. 3) Decision matrix: trade-offs and fallback policies are recorded against each KR (cost impact, latency delta, rollout complexity). Current candidate Axis (for Q2→Q3 cycle): "Make inference 3x cheaper at p95 latency target for hosted API customers." Why: clear unit-economics lift, aligns product demand and infra work, sells well to customers at enterprise renewal time. CANDIDATE OKRs (raw, for debate) O: Improve hosted inference economics while preserving
title: Axis Cascade initiative matrix and exec update (v0.1)

text: clear unit-economics lift, aligns product demand and infra work, sells well to customers at enterprise renewal time. CANDIDATE OKRs (raw, for debate) O: Improve hosted inference economics while preserving p95 latency SLOs KR1: Reduce per-token GPU seconds by 60% for median request through batching + KV prefix caching improvements (measure: GPU-seconds/1k tokens). KR2: Deliver two quantized model profiles validated for parity on priority prompt-suite (quality delta < 3% on regression metric). KR3: Achieve 99.9% uptime for hosted-model endpoints under 24/7 traffic pattern for a 30-day window. Cascade lanes (initiative mapping): - Infra lane (owner: Ravi Singh): Continuous batching engine (v2), auto-bump grouping, cold-start KV cache prewarm. Linked ticket: ENG-7362. - Key actions: prototype grouping heuristic (2 sprints), telemetry gates, rollout canary to 5% traffic. - Risk: regression in tail latency if batch windows misestimate short requests. Contingency: dynamic cutoff and immediate rollback to v1. - Product lane (owner: Eleni Park): Prompt-set driven quantization profiles + catalog flags (2 profiles: quality-first, cost-first). - Key actions: eval harness, integration tests with streaming. GH PR: redwood-inference/platform#1824. - Risk: customer-visible hallucination increase. Contingency: phased opt-in + default fallback to FP16. - GTM + CS lane (owner: Tomás Rivera): Customer pilots for pricing experiments and renewal playbook. - Key actions: 6 pilot customers for early
title: Axis Cascade initiative matrix and exec update (v0.1)

Contingency: phased opt-in + default fallback to FP16. - GTM + CS lane (owner: Tomás Rivera): Customer pilots for pricing experiments and renewal playbook. - Key actions: 6 pilot customers for early adopter program, playbook for solutions engineers. - Risk: slow uptake. Contingency: expand incentives, shorten pilot. COMMUNICATIONS AND TIMELINE (exec update + company touch) - Week 0 (leadership sync): present Axis + decision matrix, ask execs to commit owners and accept tradeoffs. - Week 1–4 (build): infra & product sprint zero; GTM lines prepping pilot cohort. - Week 5 (mid-sprint review): technical demo + canary plan for infra. - Week 8 (leadership checkpoint): go/no-go on broad rollout, prepare exec update for the company. - Week 9 (company-wide comms): single-slide narrative + three manager bullets + FAQ doc. Exec update talking points (3 mins) - What: We chose an "economics-first" axis to improve hosted API unit costs while keeping latency and quality stable. - So what: If successful, this lowers hosting price pressure and shortens time-to-value for customers; enables competitive pricing. - Ask: Approve the Axis, the three lane owners, and the pre-approved rollback triggers.
