text:
Purpose:
This is a compact, field-friendly brief for platform architects and SRE buyers that converts technical tradeoffs (cost, p99 latency, fallback complexity, observability) into actionable heuristics and sample decision trees. Use this in technical discovery calls, architecture review sessions, and pre-demo prep to quickly map buyer constraints to the ideal Redwood deployment mode and demo configuration.

Executive summary (TL;DR):
- When buyers trade cost vs latency with strict SLA windows, pick Redwood Dedicated or Private; use hosted only for early prototyping or non-critical internal tools.
- If the core ask is “control + auditability” (regulation, data residency), default to Private (VPC) and highlight KMS/HSM integrations and local audit logs.
- For teams that care mostly about predictable unit economics at scale, show our batching + caching configuration and run a cost-sensitivity calc vs hyperscaler managed endpoints (breakpoints included below).
- For ops/infra-first buyers, emphasize rollback/rollout controls (canary, A/B, automatic fallback to lower-capacity models) and tracing hooks as operational risk mitigators.

Buyer personas and one-liners: 
- Platform Architect (SRE-adjacent): "I need predictable p99 and low-blind-spot for failures." -> Lead with Dedicated, autoscaling policies, SLOs, and fallback routing.
- Security/Compliance Lead: "We need keys in our control and auditable transforms." -> Lead with Private + KMS/HSM + audit log retention
title: Cost + Latency Decision Heuristics for Platform Architects

text:
Dedicated, autoscaling policies, SLOs, and fallback routing.
- Security/Compliance Lead: "We need keys in our control and auditable transforms." -> Lead with Private + KMS/HSM + audit log retention options.
- Financial/Cost Owner: "Show me where we save vs managed endpoints as volume grows." -> Run token-level cost comparison with recommended batching/quantization profile.
- Product/ML Engineer: "Can we iterate quickly and test multiple models?" -> Start hosted for rapid iteration; provide clear upgrade path to Dedicated/Private without code changes.

Decision heuristics (rapid checklist for AE/SE):
1) Hard regulation or data residency requirement -> Private.
2) < 200 qps, exploratory, no SLA -> Hosted API is fastest path.
3) Sustained 200–2k qps or heavy burstiness and need for predictable p99 -> Dedicated with burst capacity and reserved pools.
4) When p99 must be < 200ms for 512-token responses, prefer Dedicated on curated kernels or Private on tuned infra; quantify with sample latency curves.
5) If budget owner demands <X cost per 1M tokens (customer-specific threshold), simulate batching + KV cache at target scale and show cost delta vs managed endpoints.

Cost breakpoints and how to model them (quick script for SEs):
- Inputs: avg tokens per request, qps distribution (median and 95th), % prefix reuse, target p99, tolerance for occasional higher tail.
- Key knobs: batching window, KV cache hit rate, quantization level, model family.
- Breakpoint example
title: Cost + Latency Decision Heuristics for Platform Architects

text:
distribution (median and 95th), % prefix reuse, target p99, tolerance for occasional higher tail.
- Key knobs: batching window, KV cache hit rate, quantization level, model family.
- Breakpoint example (illustrative): with 1.2k qps, avg 150 tokens, 30% prefix reuse -> Dedicated + autoscaling becomes cheaper than hyperscaler managed endpoints at ~6M tokens/day when batching window = 20ms and cache hit = 30%. Replace these numbers with actual benchmarks during deal prep.

Technical objection handling (rapid rebuttals):
- "Hyperscaler gives us one bill and native infra integrations": Acknowledge convenience, then show predictable unit economics and incident isolation (our Dedicated pools avoid noisy-neighbor interference and give deterministic latency SLOs).
- "We cannot manage more infra": Offer Private with managed control-plane option or dedicated-managed bundle; highlight our console/operator UX and optional professional services.
- "What about fallbacks and availability?": Explain smart routing (policy-based fallback to compatible model variant, multi-region routing) and demo a canary failure with automatic rollout to a smaller variant.

Demo checklist (for a 20–30 minute technical demo tailored to infra teams):
1) Show latency profile for a realistic workload (prefer customer-supplied trace).
2) Flip routing policy: hosted -> dedicated -> private to show identical API and SDK behavior.
3) Induce model failure and show automatic fallback + alerting in Console.
4)
title: Cost + Latency Decision Heuristics for Platform Architects

text:
customer-supplied trace).
2) Flip routing policy: hosted -> dedicated -> private to show identical API and SDK behavior.
3) Induce model failure and show automatic fallback + alerting in Console.
4) Walk through billing breakdown per token and per route, adjust batching live to show cost delta.
5) Show KV prefix cache effectiveness using a sample conversation.

Sales hooks & win themes (concise):
- Predictable economics: clear knobs to trade cost vs latency with measurable ROI.
- Operational simplicity + control: same API across hosted/dedicated/private reduces migration risk.
- Reliability engineered for production: deterministic p99 guarantees and graceful degradation.
- Enterprise-ready security: bring-your-own-key, audit trail, and private networking.

Competitive signal map (how to emphasize differences with hyperscaler managed endpoints):
- Pricing transparency: show per-token + batching math vs opaque instance+license + egress models.
- Avoiding noisy neighbors: emphasize scheduling and dedicated pools vs multi-tenant spot-pricing variability.
- Faster remediation and custom kernels: highlight our runtime kernel selection for short-tail latency profiles.

Suggested play for a typical mid-market deal (step-by-step):
1) Discovery: collect tokens/request, expected qps, tail-latency targets, data residency needs.
2) Rapid PoC on hosted with customer dataset (2–7 days).
3) Cost + latency simulation (SE runs batch+cache sim and quantization recommendation).
4)
title: Cost + Latency Decision Heuristics for Platform Architects

text:
qps, tail-latency targets, data residency needs.
2) Rapid PoC on hosted with customer dataset (2–7 days).
3) Cost + latency simulation (SE runs batch+cache sim and quantization recommendation).
4) If sustained load or compliance needs present: propose Dedicated initial commit with 90-day canary and SLOs.
5) Offer Private as an upgrade path for regulated workloads; include professional services estimate for on-prem integration.

Appendix: useful artifacts for deal teams (links and quick notes)
- Cost sensitivity spreadsheet template (copy this into drive and fill inputs)
- Kernel/latency benchmark deck (internal bench notes)
- Quick demo script (playbook for inducing cascade and showing fallback)

Open questions to surface during technical discovery:
- What does your 95th/99th token-length distribution look like?
- How much prefix reuse do you expect across sessions?
- Any regulatory constraints on key management or retention windows?

Notes to SEs: keep the first 5 minutes of technical calls to mapping constraints to our decision heuristics. Avoid long product slides up front. Use the cost-breakpoint calc early to align on financial decision makers.

Draft history: first draft 2025-11-02 by Aisha Patel; updated 2025-11-26 after bench sync and SE feedback.

Action items (next steps for GTM):
- Convert cost-sensitivity example into a one-click spreadsheet. Owner: Liam Chen, due 2025-12-05.
- Create a canned demo recording that walks the routing flip and fallback.

text: Purpose and scope: This runbook captures the operational design and step-by-step procedures for key lifecycle management (cloud KMS, HSM-backed keys, and customer BYOK), rotation patterns, and formatted audit export to the SIEM. It is intended for security engineers, platform engineers (private/dedicated), and on-call SREs who will perform rotations, evidence collection, and incident responses related to encryption-at-rest. Non-goals: - Specifying vendor-specific API docs (those are linked in the artifacts section). - Replacing the Verified KMS onboarding checklist — this complements it with operational playbook material. Overview of crypto hierarchy (recommended model): - Root/Signer (offline or HSM-protected root key): only used to sign intermediate keys and for bootstrapping. Extremely high entropy, stored in FIPS-validated HSM or offline guard. - Master Key (MK): KMS-managed single-purpose key used to encrypt/unwrap key-encryption-keys (KEKs). Rotated infrequently; stored in cloud KMS or HSM with strict RBAC. - Key-Encryption Keys (KEKs): Per- tenancy (customer or environment) keys used to wrap Data Encryption Keys (DEKs). KEKs live in KMS/HSM and are subject to rotation policies independent of DEKs. - Data Encryption Keys (DEKs): Short-lived keys used for envelope encryption of object data (S3, blockstore, DB column encryption). DEKs are generated by the service, encrypted with KEK, and stored alongside ciphertext. Why envelope encryption: improved rotation and small blast
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

text: of object data (S3, blockstore, DB column encryption). DEKs are generated by the service, encrypted with KEK, and stored alongside ciphertext. Why envelope encryption: improved rotation and small blast radius — DEKs are cheap to rotate and do not require re-encrypting blobs when KEK rotates (only rewrap operation). RBAC v2 for keys (mapping to roles): - key-admin: create/delete/grant/revoke KEKs and manage key policy metadata. Should be restricted to Security Eng and Platform Admins. (Granting on a per-key resource basis.) - key-operator: initiate rotate/rewrap operations, request key usage metrics, and run emergency revocations. Intended for SRE on-call + rotation automation service accounts. - kms-viewer: read-only access to key metadata, audit entries, and rotation history; used by auditors and compliance reviewers. - crypto-service (machine/account): service principal used by runtime to perform encrypt/decrypt operations — scoped to specific KEKs via least privilege. Access controls and constraints: - No human accounts with direct encrypt/decrypt rights on production KEKs unless explicitly approved and logged. - All changes to key policy must require multi-approver workflow via ticket (JIRA-SEC or ServiceNow) with a signed justification field. Rotation policy matrix (recommended default): - Root/Signer: rotate only on explicit key compromise or re-keying cycle every 5+ years (with offline ceremony). - Master Key (MK): 18–36 months, or immediately on suspected compromise.
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

text: (recommended default): - Root/Signer: rotate only on explicit key compromise or re-keying cycle every 5+ years (with offline ceremony). - Master Key (MK): 18–36 months, or immediately on suspected compromise. - KEK (per-tenant): 6–12 months; automated rewrap of DEKs recommended during low-traffic windows. - DEK: TTL 24 hours to 90 days based on workload sensitivity; for high-volume object stores, prefer short TTL (7–30 days) and frequent rewraps. Rotation step-by-step (KEK rotation, automated): 1) Pre-checks: - Confirm current KEK metadata and last_rotation in KMS. - Verify successful snapshot/backup of key metadata snapshot (S3 location: s3://redwood-sec-key-metadata/yyyymmdd). - Check outstanding re-wrap tasks and in-flight writes; consult per-route write-latency dashboard. - Ensure automation runbook is in a healthy state (CI job green). 2) Create new KEK version in KMS/HSM using secure API. Tag version with rotation-correlation-id and timestamp. 3) Update service principal grants for the new key version (if using explicit version grants). 4) Enqueue rewrap job that will read ciphertext metadata, call KMS unwrap with old KEK -> DEK, then wrap DEK with new KEK, and write back wrapped value. Jobs are sharded and rate-limited to preserve latency SLOs. 5) Monitor rewrap progress, look for rewrap-failure counts and backoffs. Failures trigger automatic re-try with exponential backoff and eventual pager escalation to on-call. 6) After >= 95% rewrap completion, update key policy primary-version
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

text: rewrap-failure counts and backoffs. Failures trigger automatic re-try with exponential backoff and eventual pager escalation to on-call. 6) After >= 95% rewrap completion, update key policy primary-version pointer to new KEK version. Keep old version alive for 30–90 days for rollback and emergency decrypt. 7) Post-ops: generate evidence bundle (audit logs, job run IDs, manifest of rewrapped object IDs) and export to secure evidence store. Manual emergency rotation (compromise suspected): - Immediate steps: set KEK to disabled state for new wraps (maintain unwrap to allow decrypts if needed), create incident ticket, notify CS/Legal if customer keys involved. For BYOK, escalate to customer contact according to contract. BYOK specific flows and customer-supplied HSMs: - Onboarding requires: customer intent form, key rollover policy acceptance, and KMS import key ceremony recorded in Confluence page. - BYOK keys are treated as external-key IDs — Redwood stores only wrapped DEKs. Key operations with external HSM require dedicated connector host in customer VPC or HSM proxy with mutual-auth TLS. - Rotation for BYOK: coordinate schedule with customer; do not perform unilateral rotation of a customer-supplied root. Documented SLA window must be observed. Audit log schema (key events) — canonical fields (JSON log entry): - timestamp: ISO8601 string - event_type: one of [KEY_CREATE, KEY_ROTATE, KEY_VERSION_CREATE, KEY_DISABLE, KEY_ENABLE, KEY_DELETE, KEY_UNWRAP, KEY_WRAP, KEY_POLICY_CHANGE]
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

text: canonical fields (JSON log entry): - timestamp: ISO8601 string - event_type: one of [KEY_CREATE, KEY_ROTATE, KEY_VERSION_CREATE, KEY_DISABLE, KEY_ENABLE, KEY_DELETE, KEY_UNWRAP, KEY_WRAP, KEY_POLICY_CHANGE] - resource_id: kms://<region>/<key-id> - actor: <principal-id or service-account> - actor_type: human|service|external-customer - action_id: UUID (correlation across services) - request_id: cloud-kms-request-id (if available) - outcome: success|failure|partial - reason: free text (failure reason / policy change note) - correlation_tags: list of tags (rotation-correlation-id, ticket-id) - old_version: version-id (if rotation) - new_version: version-id (if rotation) - rewrap_manifest_loc: s3://... or null - sampled_trace: diagnostic trace id or null - raw_event: raw provider event (if available) Example log (single-line JSON): { "timestamp": "2024-11-05T22:14:31Z", "event_type": "KEY_ROTATE", "resource_id": "kms://us-east1/ke-abc123", "actor": "svc-key-rotation@redwood-infra", "actor_type": "service", "action_id": "b3a85d6f-4c2b-4a9d-8e1c-3d9f7a9d2fbb", "request_id": "cloudreq-9922", "outcome": "success", "reason": "scheduled-rotation", "correlation_tags": ["rotation-20241105", "JIRA-SEC-1290"], "old_version": "v1", "new_version": "v2", "rewrap_manifest_loc": "s3://redwood-sec-key-manifests/rewrap-20241105.json", "sampled_trace": "trace-7a9e", "raw_event": null } SIEM export mapping guidance: - Ingest each canonical audit log into the SIEM as a single event with the following
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

text: "s3://redwood-sec-key-manifests/rewrap-20241105.json", "sampled_trace": "trace-7a9e", "raw_event": null } SIEM export mapping guidance: - Ingest each canonical audit log into the SIEM as a single event with the following mapped fields: time, src_principal (actor), event_type, resource, correlation_id (action_id), outcome, tags, message. - Enrich events with identity attributes from IAM (team, manager, risk_score) and with customer ids for tenant-scoped keys. - Create SIEM detection rules for abnormal activity patterns: - Multiple KEY_UNWRAP calls from different service principals for the same resource within a short window. - New key-version creation without a preceding JIRA ticket tag. - KEK rotates outside maintenance window. - Unexpected external-customer key access from internal VMs. Evidence automation (post-rotation evidence bundle): - Components to collect automatically after a rotation or emergency action: - Audit log slice covering action_id and +/- 24 hours. - Rewrap manifest (list of objects rewrapped and shard ids). - KMS provider raw response payloads and request IDs. - CI/CD job run ids and artifacts (automation run logs). - Signed PDF of rotation approvers (if manual approvals present). - Automation job will package the above into a signed tarball, compute a SHA256, upload to s3://redwood-sec-evidence/<action_id>/evidence.tar.gz and emit an evidence-created audit event. Testing, validation and rollback: - Canary run: pick a low-traffic tenant and perform a KEK
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

text: SHA256, upload to s3://redwood-sec-evidence/<action_id>/evidence.tar.gz and emit an evidence-created audit event. Testing, validation and rollback: - Canary run: pick a low-traffic tenant and perform a KEK rotate + rewrap. Validate decrypt success on sampled objects for 48 hours. Use automated smoke tests (github.com/redwood-infra/tests/key-rotate-canary). - Rollback: if rewrap fails en masse, revert primary-version pointer to previous version and abort further wrap jobs. Keep failure manifest for investigation. Monitoring and observability: - Key metrics to publish: rewrap_progress_percent, rewrap_error_rate, key_usage_per_minute, key_latency_p50/p90/p99 for wrap/unwrap calls, key_policy_change_count. - Dashboards: per-region key counters, tenant-impact view, and a security incidents dashboard that surfaces any disabled keys or rapid version churn. Operational playbook checklist (pre-rotation): - [ ] Confirm maintenance window and customer notifications (if tenant scoped). - [ ] Snapshot key metadata. - [ ] Ensure rewrap jobs have capacity and throttles configured. - [ ] Backup evidence store writable. Post-rotation checklist: - [ ] Rewrap >= 95% completed or documented exception list. - [ ] Evidence tarball produced and verified. - [ ] Post-rotation smoke tests green. - [ ] Update key inventory and rotation calendar. Compromise handling (short runbook): 1) Immediately mark key as disabled for new wraps and log action. 2) Create incident ticket with correlation_id and contact
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

text: Update key inventory and rotation calendar. Compromise handling (short runbook): 1) Immediately mark key as disabled for new wraps and log action. 2) Create incident ticket with correlation_id and contact list. 3) If customer key (BYOK), notify customer per contract and follow agreed escalation. 4) Start forensic capture: full audit slice, host traces, and rewrap manifest. 5) If necessary, perform emergency rewrap to new KEK and reject old version; expedite evidence export for compliance. Open questions / decisions needed: - Do we require HSM for all MKs by default or only high-sensitivity tenants? (propose: high-sensitivity and enterprise with SLA >$Xk/month) - Retention policy for old key versions beyond rollback window (30/90/365 days?) - Integration point for RBAC v2 authorization checks in the KMS proxy — pre-check or delegated to provider? Next steps (action items): - Lina: draft BYOK onboarding form for customer agreements (due 2024-11-20). - Diego: implement SIEM enrichment pipeline prototype (P0) and push PR. - Samir: add rotation canary to infra tests and schedule monthly runs. References and links: - KMS provider docs (internal vault): CONFLUENCE-SEC/KMS-Provider-APIs - Rewrap job implementation: github.com/redwood-infra/rewrap-service (PR-487 linked above) - Evidence automation template: CONFLUENCE-SEC/Evidence-Automation-Template - Incident escalation contact list: CONFLUENCE-SEC/Incident-Contacts Notes and contextual snippets (working): - Consider adding rate-limit
title: Crypto hierarchy, rotation, and SIEM export — runbook

Document history: - 2024-11-08 created (initial draft) — Asha - 2024-12-02 major edits: added audit-schema, SIEM mapping — Diego - 2025-01-14: added BYOK coordination language and evidence automation details — Lina

text:
Purpose and scope:
This runbook captures the model selection, quantization choices, and prompt/pipeline tuning we used during the Q4 retail POC (customer: mid-market eCommerce search and chat augmentation). Goal: produce a reproducible set of recommendations and an ops playbook that balances predicted unit cost and latency constraints while preserving required quality for customer queries. This doc is intentionally practical — bench numbers, tradeoffs, and step-by-step tests to run in followup POCs.

Customer constraints and profile:
- SLA targets provided: p50 latency < 120ms, p95 < 350ms for short queries (< 256 tokens).
- Throughput target: sustained 120 requests/minute with occasional bursts to 500 RPM for promotional events.
- Quality bar: pass retrieval-augmented answer correctness > 92% on the provided evaluation set (1.2k queries).
- Security: private deployment preferred (VPC), no external prompt logging for sensitive inputs.
- Budget: per-response cost target equivalent to running an MPT-7B int8 profile at ~0.8x the current hosted cost baseline.

Testing harness summary (how we measured):
- Synthetic load generator: 3 profile types (short chat, medium prompt 128 tokens, long generation 512 tokens).
- Metrics captured: avg latency, p50/p95/p99, throughput, GPU utilization, token/sec, memory headroom, model output quality (accuracy on eval set), and hallucination rate (manual checks on a 150-item subset).
- Repeated runs at cold start and warm cache to observe
title: poc-runbook-model-quant-pipeline-lab-report

text:
utilization, token/sec, memory headroom, model output quality (accuracy on eval set), and hallucination rate (manual checks on a 150-item subset).
- Repeated runs at cold start and warm cache to observe KV/prefix caching effects.
- Cost estimator: tokenized input+output token counts * per-token inference cost (profiled per model/quant).

Model family candidates and rationale (shortlist):
- Mistral-7B + 8-bit (nf4/int8) — high quality on instruction-following, good latency when quantized; generally lower memory footprint with NF4; good baseline for cost-sensitive customers.
- Llama-2-13B (quantized to int8/GPTQ4) — higher quality in long-form reasoning, but higher token costs and larger KV cache; useful as a fallback for complex queries.
- Falcon-7B Instruct (int8) — fast on A10G/T4; slightly lower instruction baseline but very predictable latency; toyed with as cost-first variant.
- Redpajama/Platform-open weights (7B/3B) — useful when customer requires open-weights; quantization maturity varied across toolchains.

Quantization profiles and quick guidance:
Profiles tested:
- nf4 (GGML-style) at 8-bit equivalent: best quality/size ratio in many of our tests for 7B-class models; preserves numeric dynamics of attention and softmax to an extent; recommended default for 7B families when using our private runtime that supports nf4 kernels.
- int8 (bitsandbytes 8-bit) with 16/32-bit residuals: very fast and stable on GPUs with bfloat16 or fp16 kernels; lower memory than
title: poc-runbook-model-quant-pipeline-lab-report

text:
families when using our private runtime that supports nf4 kernels.
- int8 (bitsandbytes 8-bit) with 16/32-bit residuals: very fast and stable on GPUs with bfloat16 or fp16 kernels; lower memory than fp16 but occasionally shows tokenization edge-case score drops (see tokenizer section).
- GPTQ4 / int4 (4-bit) aggressive: biggest cost wins (2-3x over int8) but brittle — observed a 4–7% drop in answer accuracy on constrained retrieval tasks. Use only for low-complexity classification or when latency/cost is the uppermost priority and you can add fallback to a higher-quality model for NLU failures.

Bench summary (representative numbers from lab runs):
- Mistral-7B nf4 on A10G: p50=85ms, p95=210ms, tokens/sec=320, memory headroom=3.4GB, eval accuracy=93.8%, cost per 256-token response ~= $0.0018.
- Llama-2-13B GPTQ4 on H100-80GB: p50=165ms, p95=420ms, tokens/sec=450, memory headroom=8GB, eval accuracy=96.1%, cost per 256-token response ~= $0.0043.
- Falcon-7B int8 on T4: p50=110ms, p95=260ms, tokens/sec=300, eval accuracy=91.5%, cost per 256-token response ~= $0.0015.
- Mistral-7B int4 (GPTQ) on A10G: p50=70ms, p95=195ms, eval accuracy=88.6% (regression vs nf4).

Interpretation and recommended default for this customer:
- Default deploy: Mistral-7B with nf4 profile on dedicated A10G pool behind Redwood Dedicated. Reason: meets latency and accuracy targets with acceptable cost.
- Canary/High-quality fallback: Llama-2-13B GPTQ4 pinned on H100 for queries flagged as high-complexity
title: poc-runbook-model-quant-pipeline-lab-report

text:
pool behind Redwood Dedicated. Reason: meets latency and accuracy targets with acceptable cost.
- Canary/High-quality fallback: Llama-2-13B GPTQ4 pinned on H100 for queries flagged as high-complexity by the routing classifier (see pipeline below).
- Aggressive low-cost mode: Falcon-7B int8 for non-critical batching or async summarization pipelines where slight quality drops are acceptable.

Prompting & pipeline tuning (practical recipes):
1) System + context framing: short system message tuned to reduce verbosity. E.g., system: "You are a concise assistant for product search. Provide factual answers, one paragraph max unless asked for detailed steps. Cite product IDs when relevant."
2) Retrieval augmentation: top-k idx=5; include source metadata (title, price) in a structured JSON block inside context; use a fixed-length context window (truncate older docs) to avoid variable latency.
3) Safety/guardrails: add explicit stop sequences and a short post-filter for PII. Offload PII redaction to a lightweight regex microservice before sending to the model if privacy required.
4) Temperature/tuning: default temp=0.0–0.15 for deterministic answers; use 0.6 for creative suggestion flows. Top-p=0.9, top-k=40 for general answers; reduce top-k/top-p for shorter completions to guarantee stability.
5) Few-shot pattern: for structured output (JSON with fields), prefer 2–3 examples. Keep examples short and use the same tokenization footprint as expected inputs.

Prompt failure detection
title: poc-runbook-model-quant-pipeline-lab-report

text:
stability.
5) Few-shot pattern: for structured output (JSON with fields), prefer 2–3 examples. Keep examples short and use the same tokenization footprint as expected inputs.

Prompt failure detection and routing:
- Heuristic triggers (post-response): length mismatch, low-confidence token signature (many repeated <unk> tokens), or verification failure vs retrieval.
- Classifier-based detection: small binary classifier (distilBERT) to check whether response needs higher-quality rerun. If flagged, synchronous retry to Llama-2-13B with same prompt + expanded context. Always cache first run results for audit.

KV/prefix cache and batching notes: 
- Prefix caching for common product search prefixes gave 30–45% per-token latency improvements with warm KV cache on Mistral-7B (our bench). Warm-cache tests were key: cold-start p95 spikes were 2–3x warm p95.
- Continuous batching: use small max_batch_size=8 and max_wait_ms=15 for interactive flows. For async summarization, increase batch size to 32–64 and allow up to 80–120ms wait to amortize overheads.
- Tokenization alignment: canonicalize input whitespace & punctuation before tokenization to avoid token count variability across runs.

Operational rollout and canary plan: 
1) Build quantized artifacts and validate in our staging cluster with the same hardware family planned for production.
2) Canary 5% traffic for 24h (capture all metrics and run automated correctness checks on sampled requests).
3) Expand to 25% if no
title: poc-runbook-model-quant-pipeline-lab-report

text:
staging cluster with the same hardware family planned for production.
2) Canary 5% traffic for 24h (capture all metrics and run automated correctness checks on sampled requests).
3) Expand to 25% if no regressions, then 100% over 48–72h.
4) Canary fallback logic: if p95 latency degrades >20% vs baseline or accuracy drops >2% on sample eval, auto-failover to previous model/quant profile and page on-call.

Monitoring signals and alert thresholds (suggested):
- Latency: p95 increase > 20% -> page SRE. p99 increase > 40% -> page.
- Error rate: 5xx > 0.5% for 10m -> page.
- Quality regressions: daily eval accuracy drop > 1.5% -> alert to SE and Product.
- Cost drift: 7-day rolling cost >10% above forecast -> alert to Finance/SE.

Runbook for common incidents (quick actions):
- High p95 after deploy: 1) Check GPU utilization and queue depth in console. 2) Rollback canary by switching route to previous model. 3) If rollout must remain, increase dedicated pool capacity (burst) or reduce batch windows temporarily.
- Unexpected accuracy regression: 1) Re-run 200-sample eval against the exact quant artifact (not the source fp16). 2) Try a deterministic run (temp=0.0) to see if sampling caused divergence. 3) If confirmed, rollback and open an incident for quantization investigation.

Tooling and reproducibility checklist (to include with each POC):
- Include exact model hash/Git ref for quant artifacts.
- Record tokenizer version and vocabulary hash.
- Persist a 1k-sample
title: poc-runbook-model-quant-pipeline-lab-report

text:
investigation.

- If customers require stricter compliance (no external model weights), push for private deployment and replicated benchmarking on on-prem

text:
Purpose and scope:
- Quick reference for people-stewardship during small-batch canaries and A/B experiments for console/rollouts features.
- Focus: who to call, what telemetry to triage first, comms templates, immediate safety gates, and short decision ladder.
- Intended audience: release stewards, product owners, SRE oncall, experiment owners, PMs.

High-level principles (tl;dr):
- Decide who is on stewardship before exposure. No surprises.
- Small batch -> aggressive short windows for observation (5–15m early, 1h mid, 24h long tail).
- If latency or error SLOs are violated, pause traffic first, investigate second.
- Prefer rollback/hold over complex mitigation at high user exposure.

Phone tree (roles and primary contact) - use Slack/PD first, call within 10m if escalation needed:
- Release steward (owner of rollout): Riley Chen (Slack: @riley, PagerDuty: PD:release-steward)
- Experiment owner / PM: Maya Gupta (Slack: @maya, Email: maya@redwood.ai)
- SRE oncall (infra & perf): Jonah Alvarez (Slack: @jonah-oncall, PD:infra)
- Serving runtime expert (latency, KV cache): Sabrina Lee (Slack: @sabrina, PD:serving-runtime)
- Observability lead (dashboards, alert config): Priya Narang (Slack: @priya-metrics)
- Customer comms lead (if user-facing impact): Daniel Park (Slack: @daniel-custcomms)
- Legal/Compliance (if data or privacy exposure): Emma Torres (Slack: @emma-legal)

Quick pre-launch checklist (to be confirmed at steward handoff meeting):
- [ ] Canary

Quick pre-launch checklist (to be confirmed at steward handoff meeting):
- [ ] Canary hypothesis documented (business metric + safety signals). Link: Confluence page.
- [ ] Traffic slices defined (percent, cohorts, geos).
- [ ] Observability kit created: dashboard link, query IDs, alert thresholds, example traces.

Initial monitoring checklist (0–30 minutes after exposure starts):
- Health signals (first line):
 - 5xx rate by route (threshold: >2x baseline or >0.5% absolute)
 - p95 latency for route (threshold: +200ms over baseline or exceeds SLO)
 - Queue/backpressure and GPU utilization for Dedicated pools
- Model-level signals:
 - Token generation stalls / truncated responses metric
 - KV cache hit ratio drop > 10%
- Cost/throughput signals:
 - Requests per second across serving lanes (unexpected spikes)
 - Billing burn rate vs expected profile (early check)
- Behavioral signals (LLM-specific):
 - Safety classifier triggers per 1k requests (sudden spike)
 - NER/QA fallback rate for structured outputs

Escalation

text:
rate vs expected profile (early check)
- Behavioral signals (LLM-specific):
 - Safety classifier triggers per 1k requests (sudden spike)
 - NER/QA fallback rate for structured outputs

Escalation ladder (fast):
1) Steward determines pause/hold if any primary health signal crosses threshold OR any safety classifier spike. Notify channel.
2) SRE oncall investigates infra metrics and applies short-term circuit (rate limit or route kill).
3) Serving runtime expert examines KV cache, batching metrics, model variant selection.
4) Experiment owner + product PM decide to rollback vs adjust slice.
5) If rollback, steward triggers automated rollback (link to playbook).

Rollback decision matrix (quick heuristic):
- Severe infra errors (5xx > 1% or system OOMs): immediate rollback.
- Latency regressions that persist after a short throttle: rollback if user-facing SLA violated > 2m.
- Behavioral safety failures (sustained classifier alerts or customer complaints): rollback and open high-sev.
- Cost anomalies (unexpected spend > 2x forecast for >30m): pause and investigate; escalate to finance if persists.

On-call run steps (first 15 minutes):
1) Open release channel and post initial observations (use template below).
2) Verify synthetic smoke pass/fail.
3) Triage 5xx traces and map to code path/route.
4) Check model selection routing (is traffic hitting new variant?).
5) If unclear, increase logging sampling for affected routes (short window).
6) Decide pause/rollback

and map to code path/route.
4) Check model selection routing (is traffic hitting new variant?).
5) If unclear, increase logging sampling for affected routes (short window).
6) Decide pause/rollback or let run with mitigations.

Slack post template (first notification):
- Channel: #release-<feature>-stewardship
- Message: "Heads up: starting canary for <feature> (slice X%). Steward: @riley. Watch p95 latency, 5xx, model-safety. Dashboard: <link>. Will report at :10, :30, :60. PDs tagged."

Escalation phone script (if calling SRE):
- "Hey <name>, we just observed X (5xx/p95/safety). Canary slice at Y% for <feature>. Can you check infra queues, GPU memory, and any autoscaler events? We're considering a pause if issue persists."

Comms templates (customer-facing, light):
- Short incident note: "We observed intermittent latency for [feature] affecting a small set of users. We paused the rollout while investigating. No data exposure. We'll post updates in 60 minutes."
- Internal update cadence: :10, :30, :60 then hourly until resolution.

Observability playbook (queries / quick links):
- p95 latency by route: query id [LAT-420]
- 5xx by route: query id [ERR-901]
- Token stall traces: query id [TRACE-73]
- Safety classifier rate: query id [SAF-11]
- Cost burn profiler: query id [COST-3]
(Note: replace ids with dashboard links in final steward handoff)

Post-mortem checklist (if rollback or incident):
- [ ] Short timeline created within 3 hours.
- [ ] Root cause

Post-mortem checklist (if rollback or incident):
- [ ] Short timeline created within 3 hours.
- [ ] Root cause hypothesis within 24h.
- [ ] Regression tests added (if code/serving change).
- [ ] Eval set replayed against rolled-back variant to check for behavioral drift.

Open questions / heuristics we should decide:
- How long is the "early window" for all rollouts? Current thought: 15m, but some models need 30m due to cold KV warming.
- Default traffic slice for first exposure for backend-affecting changes: 0.5% vs 1%?
- Runbook automation maturity: can we automate pause on p95 breach without human confirmation? (pros/cons below)

Notes to self / TODOs:
- Add phone numbers & PD escalation IDs here once we confirm PD rotations (TODO: reach out to SRE manager).
- Build a tiny Slack bot that posts :10/:30/:60 checks automatically and aggregates meta (p95, 5xx, safety counts).
- Validate that cost profiler has a quick-view for new model variants (ask optimize team).
- Draft a canned apology + status page blurb for customer-comms lead to adapt.

Mini case study (recent small incident, learning):
- What happened: canary for reranker tweak increased p95 by 180ms after 10m; KV cache hit ratio dropped due to key-shift.
- Immediate action: steward paused rollout at 12% exposure; SRE flushed a stale cache config and restored
title: Stewardship phone tree and ops checklist (canary & A/B) - WIP

reranker tweak increased p95 by 180ms after 10m; KV cache hit ratio dropped due to key-shift.
- Immediate action: steward paused rollout at 12% exposure; SRE flushed a stale cache config and restored baseline.
- Result: quick rollback avoided customer impact; added a preflight cache-warm script to canary checklist.

Appendix: short glossary
- Steward: person accountable for rollout decisions and communications.
- Canary: small traffic exposure to validate behavior in prod.
- Slice: cohort of users (by id hash, geo, or experiment assigner) receiving new variant.
- Pause vs rollback: pause = stop increasing exposure; rollback = revert to previous variant.

References and links to flesh out before finalizing:
- Confluence: Release Stewardship Playbook (link pending)
- JIRA: ROLL-812 (canary automation workstream)
- GH PR: platform/pull/2384 (auto-rollback hooks)

End (working doc) - feedback welcome.
- Outstanding: decide early-window length; confirm PD rotos; wire in dashboard links; add phone numbers.

text:
Executive summary:

This export captures a novel lens — 'entropy' — to quantify short-timescale variability across GPU slot occupancy by cluster/pool and by logical queue. The goal is to highlight where headroom is brittle (low sustained free capacity but high churn) versus where there is truly steady free capacity we can reassign. Entropy here is a heuristic measure (see definition below) that helps prioritize where to place reserved capacity or throttle burstable workloads.

Key takeaways (high level):
- APAC-eu1-pool-A shows low avg utilization (47%) but high entropy (0.72) indicating frequent slot churn due to ephemeral developer jobs; headroom is unreliable during 9:00–13:00 UTC.
- na-west-cluster-3 has consistently high p95 (>92%) and low entropy (0.18) — saturated and stable, needs either scale-up or stronger routing to dedicated capacity.
- Pools with entropy >0.6 tend to have short-lived queue spikes that inflate p95 but leave temporary windows of free slots; consider opportunistic batch placement (+cache) instead of reserved commitments.

Entropy metric (operational definition):
- For each slot we consider occupancy time-series over 1-minute bins for the export window (7 days). Convert to a binary occupied/unoccupied per bin. Entropy per slot = -p*log2(p)-(1-p)*log2(1-p) where p = fraction of bins occupied. Cluster/pool entropy = mean(slot_entropy) normalized by max entropy (1.0).
- Interpretation: 0 => perfectly stable (always busy or always idle), higher
title: GPU entropy + headroom export (slice & queue attribution) 2026-06-09

text:
-p*log2(p)-(1-p)*log2(1-p) where p = fraction of bins occupied. Cluster/pool entropy = mean(slot_entropy) normalized by max entropy (1.0).
- Interpretation: 0 => perfectly stable (always busy or always idle), higher values => more unpredictable churn. We care about high entropy when avg_util > 40% (means churn reduces reliability of headroom).

Data snapshot (CSV excerpt, fields: cluster,pool,logical_queue,avg_util,p95_util,entropy_norm,estimated_headroom_hours_at_peak,notes):

cluster-01,core-pool-1,default,0.47,0.83,0.72,3.2,"dev ephemeral jobs causing short spikes 08-14UTC"
na-west-cluster-3,compute-pool-x,ml-train,0.94,0.98,0.18,0.0,"steady saturation; many inference lanes pinned"
apac-eu1-2,burst-pool-b,experiments,0.61,0.89,0.65,1.1,"high p95 but available short windows, consider slot-level preemption"
eu-central-5,reservoir-1,highprio,0.36,0.57,0.12,8.5,"clean headroom for reservation; low churn"
na-east-cluster-2,spillover-A,background,0.55,0.88,0.69,0.9,"large entropy and short headroom; throttle candidate"

Notes on numbers:
- avg_util and p95_util are token-weighted occupancy (slot-time weighted by GPU core percentage).
- estimated_headroom_hours_at_peak = (free_slots_during_global_peak / slots_consumed_per_hour_by_typical_request) converted to hours; rough estimator used for quick ops decisions.

Queries & extraction notes (working):
- Slot occupancy collected from fleet telemetry (shipper v2) with 1-minute resolution; missing samples forward-filled
title: GPU entropy + headroom export (slice & queue attribution) 2026-06-09

text:
estimator used for quick ops decisions.

Queries & extraction notes (working):
- Slot occupancy collected from fleet telemetry (shipper v2) with 1-minute resolution; missing samples forward-filled up to 10m then considered unknown.
- SQL-ish pseudocode used to compute entropy: SELECT cluster, pool, slot_id, SUM(occupied_bins)/count(bins) as p FROM slot_timebins WHERE ts BETWEEN $start AND $end GROUP BY 1,2,3; then compute per-slot entropy and aggregate.
- PromQL used to cross-check p95 util: max_over_time(avg_over_time(gpu_utilization[1m])[7d:1m]) and p95() on the resulting series for the cluster scope.

Observed anomalies and hypotheses (work-in-progress):
1) cluster-01 dev spikes line up with scheduled CI window in the APAC morning — hypothesis: CI runners are not being routed to dedicated background pools after 2026-05-25 config change. Action: check PR #3417 (runner routing) and re-route CI to spillover pools.
2) na-west-cluster-3 sustained saturation appears correlated with rollout of model-x v2 (in hosted inference); peak usage coincides with new customer on-boarding on 2026-06-03. Action: engage solutions-eng for throttle policy and consider bumping dedicated capacity for that tenant.
3) burst-pool-b has high entropy but short headroom windows that are inconsistent with our batching heuristic — suspect short TTL jobs from experiments service with aggressive backoffs. Task: instrument job durations in experiments to confirm.

Operational recommendations (short
title: GPU entropy + headroom export (slice & queue attribution) 2026-06-09

text:
with our batching heuristic — suspect short TTL jobs from experiments service with aggressive backoffs. Task: instrument job durations in experiments to confirm.

Operational recommendations (short term):
- For pools with entropy > 0.6 and avg_util between 40-70%: enable opportunistic eviction + batch placement and add a 10% conservative buffer to routing headroom estimates.
- For clusters with p95 > 90% and entropy < 0.25: prioritize capacity growth (either add nodes or shift production routes to dedicated capacity) — these are stable hot spots.
- Add a daily alert for "entropy drift": if entropy increases by >0.15 over a rolling 3-day window for a cluster that has avg_util > 45%, create an ops ticket.

Dashboard links used for export: /d/fleet/gpu-entropy-heatmap, /d/fleet/cluster-headroom, /d/fleet/queue-attribution-sankey (internal).

Action items & owners:

- Arjun: check CI runner routing PR and propose a rollback or reroute for APAC jobs (JIRA-OPS-934) (due 2026-06-12).
- Sofia: instrument experiments service job TTLs and post a follow-up with duration distribution (due 2026-06-16).

Open questions / discussion points:
- Sensitivity: is 1-minute binning the right fidelity for entropy detection or should we move to 30s for low-latency churn? Tradeoff: telemetry cost vs signal quality.
- Policy: what is the acceptable entropy threshold for
title: GPU entropy + headroom export (slice & queue attribution) 2026-06-09

text:
1-minute binning the right fidelity for entropy detection or should we move to 30s for low-latency churn? Tradeoff: telemetry cost vs signal quality.
- Policy: what is the acceptable entropy threshold for committing reserved capacity? Current thumbs: <0.25 safe, 0.25–0.5 borderline, >0.5 not safe — but this needs gating from product finance.

text: Purpose and scope: This is a portable playbook-style doc for planning the span timeline (waterfall) surface in the Console trace viewer. Focus: practical interaction patterns for exploring long requests, span grouping, quick filters, and debug-friendly annotations. Intended audience: product, frontend, SRE, and UX who will ship the first iteration (MVP) for Q1 roadmap. High-level goals (why): - Help engineers quickly pinpoint latency hotspots in multi-model requests (KV cache misses, routing fallbacks, model switch latencies). - Make it cheap to triage production incidents without switching to raw logs (link out when needed). - Provide a compact summary view plus progressively revealed detail (progressive disclosure). Constraints and assumptions: - Must work for traces up to 10kms (long traces) and 200 spans without freezing the UI. - First pass will support server-side timestamps + durations; no distributed tracing correlation yet (future). - Prefers purely client-side rendering with virtualization for rows. MVP surface (what to build): 1) Compact timeline (collapsed spans by default): - Row per span group (service.action) with count and aggregated duration range (p50/p95). - Tap/expand to animate open slot into full span rows beneath. - Visual cue for long-tail spans: thin red overlay on portion > threshold. 2) Span detail flyout: - Header: span name, service, duration, start offset, tags (key/value), request id. - Tabs: Metadata (headers/attrs), Logs (linked), Events (KV/cache
title: span-timeline-playbook-grooming-notes

text: overlay on portion > threshold. 2) Span detail flyout: - Header: span name, service, duration, start offset, tags (key/value), request id. - Tabs: Metadata (headers/attrs), Logs (linked), Events (KV/cache hits), Raw JSON (copy). - Quick actions: "Copy timing", "Open in logs", "Create ticket" (prefill fields). 3) Filters & quick probes bar (persistent top strip): - Text search over span names + attributes (syntax: name:token, tag:key=value). - Prebuilt quick filters: long > 500ms, error spans, model-fallback, cache-miss. - Toggle: group-by-service / group-by-operation / show-only-errors. 4) Time-slice scrubber and zoom controls: - Mini-waterfall overview (Sparkline) with a brush to zoom time ranges. - Keyboard shortcuts: shift + drag to select, +/- to zoom, space to play animation. Interaction notes and micro-flows: - Expand flow: click on row -> row animates open -> fetch per-span details (lazy) -> arrow to first child span -> keyboard focus goes into expanded region. - Inspect-range: user highlights a time window in the scrubber -> timeline reflows showing only spans intersecting window; counts of hidden spans shown as collapsed badge. - Multi-select: shift+click selects multiple spans -> aggregate action bar appears with suggested next steps (trace compare, export, make ticket). Wireframe description (ASCII sketch for devs): [Header bar: trace id | duration | region | download] [Quick probes bar: (search) | [long>500ms] [errors] [cache-miss] [group:service]] [Mini-sparkline

(Option A: stacked lanes per service, Option B: layered single lane with translucency). Decision needed by next sync. - Should group-by default to service or operation? text: Purpose: This doc captures experiment notes and profiling observations from targeted benchmarks comparing dense and MoE variants across Llama, Mistral, and Qwen families, with an emphasis on long-context scaling (8k, 32k, 64k tokens) and MoE routing overhead under production serving conditions. Goal: actionable mitigations for the runtime (kernel selection, batching heuristics, routing optimizations) and a short recommended next-step plan. Summary of main findings (TL;DR): - MoE models show attractive FLOP efficiency but exhibit 20–70% throughput variability due to routing hotspotting and memory fragmentation on single-GPU serving. - Long-context dense models (32k+) are dominated by KV-cache memory bandwidth and attention kernel scaling; throughput drops non-linearly beyond ~16k in our current Triton-backed attention implementation. - Qwen-like architectures (wider FF layers, shallower attn heads) trade latency for lower per-token memory pressure; in some configs they beat Llama at 8k but lose at 64k due to kv-layout inefficiencies. - Fast wins: reduce padding and prefer per-request prefix caching; Medium-term wins: routing-load-aware batching and locality-aware expert placement; Long-term: fusion of routing + expert kernels to reduce kernel launch overhead. Testbed and methodology: - Hardware: A100 80GB (persistence mode), 2x A100 for multi-GPU runs, occasional H100 spot checks. - Runtime build: serving-runtime @ commit c3f7b4 (custom Triton kernels enabled), CUDA 12.2, cuBLASLt
title: moe-routing-long-context-benchmarks

text: - Hardware: A100 80GB (persistence mode), 2x A100 for multi-GPU runs, occasional H100 spot checks. - Runtime build: serving-runtime @ commit c3f7b4 (custom Triton kernels enabled), CUDA 12.2, cuBLASLt for matmuls, PyTorch 2.2.0 for model loading. - Profilers used: nsight-system (aggregated traces), torch.profiler (CPU/CUDA op times), nvtx markers in runtime, and occasional nvprof runs for kernel-level stats. - Load generator: synthetic streaming traffic via our streaming client (p50 interarrival), mixes: chat-like (prompt 1–3k tokens + 512 gen) and doc-completion (8k–64k prompt + short gen). - Metrics captured: tokens/sec (steady-state), p50/p95/p99 generation latency, GPU mem peak, number of kernel launches per token, expert dispatch imbalance (stddev of tokens/expert), and device-side host-to-device memcpy time per request. Experiment matrix (short): - Models: Llama-2-like 13B dense, hypothetical Llama-MoE-30B (8 experts), Mistral-in-13B-dense, Mistral-MoE-Variant-20B (16 experts), Qwen-14B-dense. - Contexts: 2k, 8k, 32k, 64k. - Batch sizes: 1, 4, 16 (where memory permits). - Quantization: fp16 baseline; 4-bit QAT-lite on a subset. Key profiling observations by topic: 1) Kernel launch overhead and tiny-kernel fragmentation - For MoE inference, each token generates: gating kernel (dispatch indices), scatter/gather to experts (small GEMMs), expert local FFN kernels, and a gather. On our build this leads to 6–12 separate CUDA kernel launches per active expert per token. - When
title: moe-routing-long-context-benchmarks

text: kernel (dispatch indices), scatter/gather to experts (small GEMMs), expert local FFN kernels, and a gather. On our build this leads to 6–12 separate CUDA kernel launches per active expert per token. - When many experts are lightly loaded, the number of launches explodes and per-launch latency dominates, especially at batch size 1. For 8-expert model at bs=1, kernel-launch latency accounted for ~35% of token latency (nsight timelines show many <50us kernels). - Recommendation: prioritize merging small expert kernels into a fused dispatch+FFN kernel (engineering spike: Triton fused expert kernels). 2) Routing imbalance and hot experts - Gating softmax tends to select a small subset of experts for short prompts and skewed inputs (we recorded top-2 experts getting ~60% of tokens on average for the Mistral-MoE variant). - Hotspotting increases memory contention (multiple streams touching same expert memory) and causes stalls due to serialization at mutexes in our current expert queue. - Short-term mitigation: apply a random jitter to gating logits at serving time (small temperature anneal) which redistributed load and improved tokens/sec by ~8% in a microbench. 3) KV cache memory bandwidth and layout for long-context - Dense long-context cases are constrained by KV cache reads/writes; our current layout is per-layer contiguous with stride-by-batch, which is suboptimal for long sequences since it leads to scattered accesses during attention. - At 32k tokens, KV read latency and PCIe/NVLink
title: moe-routing-long-context-benchmarks

text: current layout is per-layer contiguous with stride-by-batch, which is suboptimal for long sequences since it leads to scattered accesses during attention. - At 32k tokens, KV read latency and PCIe/NVLink transfers (for multi-GPU sharded KV) accounted for >40% of p95 latency. - A layout change (interleaved-by-seq-chunk with blocking) reduced memory traffic and improved throughput by ~18% on 32k benchmarks (engineering note: requires reindexing support in runtime and careful alignment). 4) Attention kernel scaling ceiling - Our Triton attention kernel scales well to ~8k tokens, but beyond that the memory working set exceeds L2 cache assumptions and kernel runtime increases superlinearly. - For Llama-like models at 64k we observed 2.8x increase in per-token time vs 8k, whereas theoretical FLOP scaling would suggest ~8x — so memory system is the bottleneck, not compute. - Consider implementing blocked attention streaming (chunked attention + local context) for very long contexts when exact global attention is not required. 5) Quantization interactions - 4-bit QAT reduces memory pressure, but MoE expert kernels suffer from lower arithmetic intensity on int4 GEMMs and experience lower throughput than expected because our int4 kernels are not yet well-optimized for small expert sizes. - For dense long contexts, int4 helped: memory-bound attention kernels saw ~30% mem bandwidth reduction and ~20% throughput gain. For MoE, net effect was model and expert-size dependent. Representative
title: moe-routing-long-context-benchmarks

text: For dense long contexts, int4 helped: memory-bound attention kernels saw ~30% mem bandwidth reduction and ~20% throughput gain. For MoE, net effect was model and expert-size dependent. Representative numbers (A100 80GB, p50 steady-state, bs=1 unless noted): - Llama-13B dense, 8k prompt: 550 tokens/sec, p95 120ms, GPU mem 34GB. - Llama-13B dense, 32k prompt: 210 tokens/sec, p95 430ms, GPU mem 46GB. - Mistral-13B dense, 8k: 630 tokens/sec (tighter heads => slightly better throughput), 32k: 260 tokens/sec. - Llama-MoE-30B (8 experts), 8k: 720 tokens/sec but with 40% variance over runs; hot expert stalls visible. - Mistral-MoE-20B (16 experts), 8k: 680 tokens/sec, dispatch imbalance stddev 0.48 (tokens/expert normalized), bs=4 reduces variance. - Qwen-14B dense, 8k: 760 tokens/sec, 32k: 290 tokens/sec; Qwen shows better 8k perf due to FF layout but loses at 64k. Operational impacts and notes: - Serving SLOs: for bs=1 low-latency endpoints, MoE introduces jitter that may violate strict p95 targets unless we implement routing-tail mitigation. - Cost vs throughput: MoE models can reduce inference cost at similar average throughput but increase tail latency and operational complexity. For synchronous APIs, this is a tradeoff — for async/batched workloads MoE is more attractive. - Monitoring: need to surface expert-load metrics in Console (per-expert token counts, stddev, recent gating entropy) and an alert when any expert > 3x mean load for >30s. Immediate recommendations (next 6 weeks):
title: moe-routing-long-context-benchmarks

text: need to surface expert-load metrics in Console (per-expert token counts, stddev, recent gating entropy) and an alert when any expert > 3x mean load for >30s. Immediate recommendations (next 6 weeks): - Engineering spike to prototype fused expert kernels in Triton (estimated 2–3 engineer-weeks). - Change KV layout to blocked/interleaved as an opt-in config for long-context routes; run regression tests across 8k/32k/64k. - Add small-gating-jitter as a configurable runtime flag to spread expert load when tail latency alarms fire. - Add per-expert telemetry and a dashboard in Console; include gating entropy and expert imbalance charts. Medium-term (quarter roadmap): - Routing-aware batching: group requests that route to similar expert sets to reduce memory churn and improve cache locality. - Explore hybrid attention: local-window + periodic global for ultra-long contexts to reduce KV working set. - Optimize int4 kernels for small-expert FFNs or fallback logic to fp16 for small experts. Open questions (for follow-up experiments): - How does H100 change the kernel-launch vs compute balance for MoE at bs=1 (we only ran a handful of H100 checks)? - Can model-side gating regularization (during fine-tuning) reduce runtime hotspotting without hurting quality? - Is there a practical consistent hashing placement for experts across multi-GPU that minimizes cross-device memcpy? Action items (owners): - Aisha: prototype gating jitter flag + run 2wk stability test (due 2026-03-20). - Evan: spike

Action items (owners): - Aisha: prototype gating jitter flag + run 2wk stability test (due 2026-03-20). - Evan: spike fused Triton expert kernel (pair with Marina) (due 2026-04-10). - Marina: implement KV blocked layout prototype and perf regression suite for 32k/64k (due 2026-04-01). text: PRD draft — Approval lifecycle chronicle and edge handoff playbook Purpose - Provide a single pragmatic playbook for how customer-managed network changes (VPCs, peering, NATs, firewall rules, edge gateways) are proposed, approved, recorded, and retained for Private deployments. - Ensure auditability for regulatory customers while minimizing friction for operators. - Define minimal evidence schema, retention SLAs, and escalation paths that integrate with Redwood control plane and customer SIEM. Background and motivation Customers running Redwood Private require clear, verifiable trails for network changes that affect inference connectivity and data flows. Existing drafts focus heavily on attestation and immutable logs; we need a compact operational playbook targeted at day-to-day edge/network handoffs (operator-initiated or customer-initiated) and how those flows surface evidence into the audit store without excessive retention costs. High-level goals 1) Make every significant VPC/network change produce a compact evidence bundle that proves who approved it, when, and what the before/after state was. 2) Automate low-risk changes where possible (policy-driven auto-approval) while routing higher-risk changes to human approvers. 3) Retain searchable audit records for default 1 year (configurable per-customer) with compact retention tiers for long-tail compliance. 4) Provide standard ingestion hooks for customer SIEMs, plus a Redwood query API for ad-hoc investigations. Scope (in/out)
title: Approval lifecycle chronicle: edge handoff playbook

text: (configurable per-customer) with compact retention tiers for long-tail compliance. 4) Provide standard ingestion hooks for customer SIEMs, plus a Redwood query API for ad-hoc investigations. Scope (in/out) In-scope: - Customer-managed VPC changes surfaced via the Private control plane (peering, BGP sessions, route table changes, firewall/NACL rule changes, gateway updates). - Approval gate definitions, evidence schema, minimal snapshot diffs, and retention tiers. - Integrations with Redwood Console, RBAC, and SIEM/forwarder endpoints. Out-of-scope: - Deep packet-level forensic logs (left to customer's own networking stack). - Internal infra-only changes not visible to customer via control plane. Key stakeholders - Product: private-deployments (Aisha K. Patel) - Eng: private-control-plane, network-operator UX, observability (Ravi Menon) - SRE & Security: audit retention policy and long-term storage (Carmen Ortiz) - Solutions/PE: customer handoff and runbook validation (Noah Hsu) User stories - As a network operator, I want to propose a route table modification and get an automated approval if it matches policy so I can move quickly. - As a security reviewer, I want evidence of who allowed an edge gateway open to the internet and an attached diff so I can triage incidents promptly. - As a compliance officer, I want to query all approval decisions affecting firewall rules for the last 12 months and export a compact evidence bundle. Functional requirements 1) Approval gates - Policy
title: Approval lifecycle chronicle: edge handoff playbook

text: As a compliance officer, I want to query all approval decisions affecting firewall rules for the last 12 months and export a compact evidence bundle. Functional requirements 1) Approval gates - Policy engine evaluates each change against risk factors: scope (customer-breadth), exposure (public IPs), blast radius (number of subnets), prior incidents, and on-call windows. - Gates: automatic-approve, soft-approval (notify reviewers, change is staged for 15m), manual-approval (must be explicitly approved by named approvers). - Approval records must include approver id, method (UI, API, delegated-role), timestamp, reason (optional), and linked ticket id if present. 2) Evidence bundle schema (compact) - id: unique change id (UUID) - actor: string (email or service account) - change_type: one of [route-mod, firewall-rule, peering, nat-gw, gateway-config, bgp-session] - before_snapshot: base64-encoded delta-friendly snapshot (only fields that changed) - after_snapshot: base64-encoded delta-friendly snapshot - diff_summary: short human text summary (<= 280 chars) - approval: {approver: string, method: string, timestamp: string, gate: string} <-- store as compact JSON string in the evidence bundle field when persisted - signatures: list of cryptographic assertions (optional) - related_ticket: string - retention_tier: one of [standard-1y, compact-3y, minimal-7y] (decided by policy or contract) Note: Physical storage in Redwood audit store should keep evidence bundles compressed and indexed
title: Approval lifecycle chronicle: edge handoff playbook

text: string - retention_tier: one of [standard-1y, compact-3y, minimal-7y] (decided by policy or contract) Note: Physical storage in Redwood audit store should keep evidence bundles compressed and indexed by id, actor, and change_type; the "approval" sub-object will be stored as a single escaped string value to simplify our metadata model. 3) Retention and access controls - Default retention: 1 year full evidence, 3 years compact index (metadata only), 7 years minimal index (change id + hash) as optional paid add-on. - Customers can request longer retention in contract; retention_tier is set at change time according to policy + customer override. - Access: RBAC enforced. Only roles with 'audit:read' can retrieve full evidence bundles. Retrieval must generate an audit retrieval record. 4) Ingestion and SIEM forwarding - Audit forwarder endpoint: HTTPS multipart with JSON metadata + gz compressed evidence bundle. - Support for S3 dump + signed manifest for customers preferring batched exports. - Real-time webhook to customer-specified URL for manual-approval events and failures. 5) Console UX / operator flows - Propose change: inline diff editor, risk score indicator, suggested retention tier, optional ticket link. - Approval modal: show minimal before/after, list of required approvers, escalate button. - Evidence view: retrieve and download full evidence bundle (signed) with export provenance. Non-functional requirements - Audit writes: < 200ms additional latency on change commit (async
title: Approval lifecycle chronicle: edge handoff playbook

text: escalate button. - Evidence view: retrieve and download full evidence bundle (signed) with export provenance. Non-functional requirements - Audit writes: < 200ms additional latency on change commit (async write to audit store allowed but must be acked to operator within 2s). - Query API: support range queries up to 12 months with pagination and filters by actor/change_type. - Storage: use compressed protobuf-like structure for evidence bundles to reduce footprint by ~5x vs raw JSON. - Tamper evidence: every stored bundle must include a server-side HMAC and optional customer-signed digest. Operational playbook (edge handoff example) 1) Customer submits an edge gateway change via API. 2) Policy engine classifies change -> risk score 4/10 -> soft-approval. 3) Change is staged; system posts an approval notification to reviewers and creates an evidence bundle with retention_tier standard-1y. 4) If reviewers take no action within 15 minutes, the change auto-promotes to active (soft-approval policy). 5) Evidence bundle is forwarded to customer SIEM endpoint and persisted in Redwood audit store. 6) Any retrieval of the bundle requires audit:read and creates a retrieval audit record. Sample compact evidence payload (human-readable form) - id: "d6b9e8c2-8b4b-4a20-a9c6-1e2f4d1c7b9a" - actor: "svc:onboarding@customer.com" - change_type: "peering" - diff_summary: "add peering between vpc-02 and vpc-08 for inference traffic" - approval: "approver=ops-lead@customer.com;method=ui;ts=2024-11-12T15:02:22Z;gate=manual-approval"
title: Approval lifecycle chronicle: edge handoff playbook

text: change_type: "peering" - diff_summary: "add peering between vpc-02 and vpc-08 for inference traffic" - approval: "approver=ops-lead@customer.com;method=ui;ts=2024-11-12T15:02:22Z;gate=manual-approval" - retention_tier: "standard-1y" Privacy and minimization guidance - Evidence bundles should include only the minimal configuration fields necessary to reconstruct intent. Avoid including customer PII (usernames/emails may be present in approver fields; retention policy should mask or redact if required by contract). Migration/compatibility notes - Existing internal attestation logs will be batched to the new evidence bundle format via a one-time migration tool. - Customers currently using the SIEM-forwarder will be given a compatibility shim for 6 months. Metrics & success criteria - Time-to-approve for low-risk changes: median <= 2 minutes for auto-approval, <= 20 minutes for soft-approval flows. - Evidence bundle size: median < 8 KB per change. - Audit query latency: 95th percentile < 350ms for 12-month queries. - Customer satisfaction: > 85% of pilot customers report handoff process is satisfactory in first 30 days. Open questions / decisions needed - Do we require customer-signed signatures by default or make it opt-in? (security team leaning opt-in due to UX). - Retention tiers pricing model and default contract mapping (legal + finance). - How to treat third-party integrator service accounts that initiate changes on behalf of customers? Next steps - Host a cross-functional
title: Approval lifecycle chronicle: edge handoff playbook

text: pricing model and default contract mapping (legal + finance). - How to treat third-party integrator service accounts that initiate changes on behalf of customers? Next steps - Host a cross-functional review (eng/product/security) to finalize approval gate definitions and minimal schema (target week of 2024-12-09). - Implement a small prototype: control plane change -> evidence bundle -> SIEM-forwarder (2 sprint spike). - Run a 4-customer pilot with diverse network topologies to validate retention sizing and retrieval UX. Appendix: quick checklist for operator handoff - [ ] Ensure proposed change has a ticket reference. - [ ] Validate policy recommendations and suggested retention tier. - [ ] If manual approval required, document approver and justification. - [ ] Confirm evidence bundle delivery (console + SIEM). - [ ] Mark change and bundle as retrievable and note retention tier in customer contract. Draft author notes - This draft aims to strike a balance between compliance needs and operator velocity. It intentionally favors compact evidence over full-state retention unless contractually required. Feedback requested on the evidence schema encoding and default retention mapping.
title: Approval lifecycle chronicle: edge handoff playbook

text: Purpose This is a short, practical operator-facing cheatsheet and running notes for on-call engineers and runtime devs. Focus: when to use TensorRT engine paths, when to go through Triton (server-managed), and when to fall back to our custom-kernel pipeline. Document collects tradeoffs, gotchas, quick checks, and lightweight experiments that surfaced surprising KV-cache and scheduling interactions. TL;DR (operator summary) - TensorRT engine path: best for high-throughput stable models with consistent sequence lengths and quantized/FP16 builds. Expect fastest token throughput and smallest per-token GPU compute, but non-trivial cold-start + engine build/deserialize overhead and limited flexibility for dynamic shapes unless we prepare multiple engines. - Triton server path: good for multi-model host consolidation, easier remote autoscaling, and when you want built-in model control (versions, metrics). Slightly higher per-request overhead vs bare TRT but easier operational controls and fast reloads in many cases. - Custom-kernel path: best when model needs ops not supported by TRT, for experimental attention changes, or when we need fine-grained control over KV layout and memory. Higher dev/maintenance cost and usually slower than TRT but gives predictable behavior for odd sequence profiles. Background/context We run three main inference adapter types in production: 1) TRT-direct: our runtime loads a serialized TRT engine and executes decoding kernels directly through our engine-adapter
title: Runtime operator cheatsheet: TRT / Triton / custom adapter tradeoffs

text: profiles. Background/context We run three main inference adapter types in production: 1) TRT-direct: our runtime loads a serialized TRT engine and executes decoding kernels directly through our engine-adapter shim. 2) Triton-hosted: Triton manages the model instance(s), provides health/metrics, and we route requests over gRPC/HTTP. Our adapter wraps the client and maps sessions to Triton model instances. 3) Custom-kernel: our own fused kernels (cuda graphs + custom attention) executed by the runtime without TRT/Triton indirection. These interact with the KV cache subsystem and the micro-scheduler in different ways — this doc records observed interactions and quick mitigation patterns. Observed tradeoffs and concrete behaviors (from recent experiments) - Cold-starts and engine loads - TRT engine deserialize + CUDA context warmup cost: 60-120ms on an A100 (depends on engine size). If we need to create a new TRT engine for a model variant (different max_seq or repacked layers) expect a 100-300ms stall first request. - Triton model loading is more predictable (containerized lifecycle hooks), typically 80-150ms for model warmup using our warmed snapshot flow. But Triton has additional RPC overhead for each request (~2-4ms). - Custom-kernel warmup is fast for small kernels (20-50ms) but may require kernel JIT/graph warm-up for larger fused graphs (70-160ms). - Runtime memory and fragmentation - TRT engines allocate large contiguous GPU memory regions. If host nodes are memory-fragmented
title: Runtime operator cheatsheet: TRT / Triton / custom adapter tradeoffs

text: require kernel JIT/graph warm-up for larger fused graphs (70-160ms). - Runtime memory and fragmentation - TRT engines allocate large contiguous GPU memory regions. If host nodes are memory-fragmented due to other processes, TRT will fail to load or OOM (observed on nodes with 5–10% fragmentation). Mitigation: prefer nodes with eviction headroom, or proactively pre-allocate and hold a small pool of engines for popular models. - Custom kernels can use smaller, fragmented buffers via our slab allocator but then pay higher bookkeeping overhead. - KV cache behavior and cross-adapter session stickiness - KV cache layout is adapter-specific (different packing/alignment). When a session switches adapter (e.g., fallback from TRT to custom), the KV cache cannot be trivially reused; we either translate (expensive) or invalidate. Observed p99 tail increase when sessions flip adapters mid-conversation. - Heuristic that worked: make routing sticky per-session: choose adapter at session start based on predicted sequence-length distribution and model variant, avoid switching unless a clear failure occurs. - KV shadowing: keep a lightweight mirrored cache of first N tokens in both layouts so short adapter switches can be warmed without full migration. This is a moderate memory tradeoff but reduces tail latency spikes in practice. - Batching and micro-scheduler impacts - TRT benefits more from large batch sizes and long sequences (amortizes kernel-launchs). For small short-query workloads, Triton
title: Runtime operator cheatsheet: TRT / Triton / custom adapter tradeoffs

text: tail latency spikes in practice. - Batching and micro-scheduler impacts - TRT benefits more from large batch sizes and long sequences (amortizes kernel-launchs). For small short-query workloads, Triton + custom path with continuous batching often outperforms TRT due to lower per-request overhead. - Scheduler should consider adapter-specific throughput curves. Example: TRT throughput grows superlinearly with batch up to GPU saturation; custom-kernel scales more linearly. A naive global batching policy caused TRT engines to starve custom-kernel tasks in earlier experiments. - Suggested heuristic: maintain per-adapter token-per-second (TPS) models and run a weighted fair scheduler for batching windows. For latency-SLO routes, cap batch wait to 10ms and prefer custom/Triton pipelines for sub-8-token requests. - Quantization and numeric degredation - TRT FP16/INT8 builds gave 20-35% per-token speedups in experiments but introduced occasional hallucination noise at extreme tokens for some LLM variants. Always run a guardrail eval set for each quantized engine before rolling. - Custom kernels allow mixed-precision control per-op; safer but more engineering cost. - Fallback patterns and failure modes - Common failures: TRT engine load OOMs, Triton model unavailable (OOM or controller restart), custom-kernel kernel timeout/launch failure. - Operator-friendly fallback order that balances latency and correctness: 1) TRT (if loaded) -> 2) Triton (warm model) -> 3) Custom (last resort). This
title: Runtime operator cheatsheet: TRT / Triton / custom adapter tradeoffs

text: restart), custom-kernel kernel timeout/launch failure. - Operator-friendly fallback order that balances latency and correctness: 1) TRT (if loaded) -> 2) Triton (warm model) -> 3) Custom (last resort). This keeps fastest path prioritized while preserving continuity. - Important: fallback must be session-aware to avoid KV cache thrash. When falling back, do not immediately evict the TRT cache; mark session as in-“fallback” and progressively warm the new adapter. Mini experiments (how we measured) - Microbench harness used simple single-token loop timed across 10k iterations, warm/warm+cold tests. Metrics: median single-token latency, p95/p99, GPU utilization, and KV cache hit-rate. All runs on A100-40GB with isolated instance. - Another test used 500 synthetic sessions with Zipf-length distribution to simulate chat workloads and measured adapter-switch tail behaviour. Quick operator checklist (when seeing rising p99 or errors) - Check engine load errors in /var/log/runtime/trt-engine.log and Triton logs. Look for CUDA_ERROR_OUT_OF_MEMORY or engine deserialization exceptions. - Inspect node fragmentation: nvidia-smi topo & free/available GPU memory. If fragmentation > 10%, consider migrating low-priority jobs or restarting node to defragment. - Confirm session routing: is there an adapter-switch event? Query internal key session-adapter map: /api/sessions/{id}. If switching frequently, enable sticky routing or raise route-level stickiness TTL. - If p99 spikes with small token lengths,
title: Runtime operator cheatsheet: TRT / Triton / custom adapter tradeoffs

text: adapter-switch event? Query internal key session-adapter map: /api/sessions/{id}. If switching frequently, enable sticky routing or raise route-level stickiness TTL. - If p99 spikes with small token lengths, throttle batch-window for TRT pipelines (reduce to 5–8ms). - Run the KV cache analyzer script (internal) to spot high invalidation rates. High invalidation -> consider KV shadowing for short contexts or pre-warm translation for longer ones. Open questions / experiments to run next - Automated engine packing: build engine variants for common max_seq lengths and pick automatically based on observed per-session sequence histograms. Need to measure memory cost of retaining multiple engines vs load latency. - Adaptive sticky routing: dynamic policy that allows switching only if predicted cost-benefit > threshold (consider KV migration cost). - Lightweight on-GPU translation for KV cache between TRT and custom layouts: is a GPU memcpy+repack cheaper than full decode fallback for 1–4 tokens? Run microbench. - Test Triton vs TRT RPC overhead on high QPS bursts with 256-token sequences: does Triton add more than 10% latency at scale? Notes to self - Keep this doc short; create a follow-up with quantitative tables once we finish the engine-packing experiment next sprint. - Add quick reference logs/grep commands here later. Next steps - Run the KV shadowing memory-vs-tail-latency sweep (JIRA:RINF-723). - Prototype small GPU translator for first-8 tokens between layouts. Add perf hooks.
title: Runtime operator cheatsheet: TRT / Triton / custom adapter tradeoffs

text: reference logs/grep commands here later. Next steps - Run the KV shadowing memory-vs-tail-latency sweep (JIRA:RINF-723). - Prototype small GPU translator for first-8 tokens between layouts. Add perf hooks. - Add a route-level toggle for sticky-routing TTL in the control plane so product can tune per-customer.
