text: Purpose: This draft defines the design and staged rollout plan for an "attestation-aware" secrets gateway for private (VPC / on-prem) deployments. The gateway centralizes secret distribution and enforces hardware-backed key operations (HSM/KMS) combined with node attestation. The focus is operational hardening: key management patterns, secrets lifecycle, terraform module recommendations, upgrade and rollback procedures, and audit/logging requirements for compliance. Goals: - Provide a single control plane for secrets that can interoperate with customer-managed HSMs (on-prem or cloud) and our KMS adapter. - Enforce attestation-based release of DEKs to runtime nodes (VMs / containers) to ensure only verified hosts can unwrap keys. - Minimize blast radius when migrating keys or upgrading modules; provide clear terraform migration steps and rollbacks. - Produce tamper-evident audit trails and integrate with existing observability pipelines. Non-goals: - Implementing full PKI for customer environments (we rely on existing attestation providers: TPM, Nitro Enclaves, or GCP/AWS attestation services). - Replacing customer HSM vendors; focus on interop and fallback. High-level architecture (textual): - Secrets Gateway (SG): stateless control plane deployed in customer VPC; authenticates operator and clusters, stores metadata only. - KEK Manager: interacts with customer KMS/HSM via configured connectors (cloud KMS, network HSM, HSM appliance over PKCS#11). Holds only KEK references, not
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: operator and clusters, stores metadata only. - KEK Manager: interacts with customer KMS/HSM via configured connectors (cloud KMS, network HSM, HSM appliance over PKCS#11). Holds only KEK references, not plaintext DEKs. - DEK Store (encrypted): encrypted blob store (object store or vault) in customer environment; encrypted under KEK. - Attestation Service: verifies node identity and health (TPM quote, cloud attestation token) and issues a short-lived attestation credential used to request DEK unwrapping. - Secrets Agent (on node): requests DEKs from SG after presenting attestation token and mTLS identity; caches DEKs in memory with TTL + automatic wipe on signals. Key flows: 1) Provisioning: Operator registers KEK connector in SG with PKCS#11 URI, connector credentials, key ID. Terraform-managed resource: redwood_secrets_kek_connector. 2) Secret write: Operator uploads secret to SG API; SG generates DEK (random), encrypts secret with DEK, calls KEK Manager to wrap DEK -> stores wrapped DEK + ciphertext in DEK store. 3) Node fetch: Node performs attestation -> obtains attestation token -> mTLS to SG -> SG validates attestation / policy -> asks KEK Manager to unwrap DEK (transient) -> returns plaintext DEK to SG just long enough to encrypt transport to node (or uses ECDH established session) -> node receives and decrypts secret locally. 4) Rotation: Operator triggers key rotation; new DEK generation and rewrap. Support rolling rewrap without downtime via dual-KEK acceptance window.
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: session) -> node receives and decrypts secret locally. 4) Rotation: Operator triggers key rotation; new DEK generation and rewrap. Support rolling rewrap without downtime via dual-KEK acceptance window. Operational hardening patterns: - Always prefer HSM for KEK operations; configure offline HSM policies for high-sensitivity KEKs. - Use split knowledge / dual-operator approval for KEK rotations on production class secrets. - Enforce short attestation token lifetimes (<= 30s) and single-use tokens where backend supports it. - Secrets Agent must zero memory on SIGTERM and provide explicit API to purge memory on SIGKILL tolerant platforms. - Use envelope encryption: SG never persists plaintext DEKs to disk. Logs must redact all DEK plaintext. Terraform module notes (recommendations): - Module name: redwood/secrets-gateway/aws (or /gcp /onprem depending on provider). Keep provider-specific pieces behind small wrapper modules. - Versioning: Semver for modules. Breaking changes only on major bump. - Resources to expose: - variables: keystore_connector (object/string ref), deks_bucket (string), attestation_provider (enum), node_agent_config (map), audit_sink (string), admin_principals (list). - outputs: gateway_url, sg_service_account, kek_connector_id, audit_sink_id. - Example minimal variables block for terraform uses (text snippet): variable \"keystore_connector\" { type = string } variable \"deks_bucket\" { type = string } variable \"attestation_provider\" { type = string } - Important:
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: variables block for terraform uses (text snippet): variable \"keystore_connector\" { type = string } variable \"deks_bucket\" { type = string } variable \"attestation_provider\" { type = string } - Important: implement "import" helpers (terraform import) for existing KEKs and DEK blobs. Provide a migration script that imports existing SecretsGateway state into the module state: scripts/terraform/import_existing_keys.sh (see migration steps below). - Lock provider versions and add explicit lifecycle rules to prevent accidental destroy: lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } on critical resources when env == "prod". Upgrade and migration plan (high level): - Preconditions: backups of wrapped-DEK blobs, KEK connector configuration exported, snapshot of SG configuration, full test suite passing in staging. - Stage 1 (canary): Deploy new module to staging namespace; run end-to-end test where node attestation -> fetch -> decrypt cycle is exercised with staged HSM connector. Verify audit entries and metrics. - Stage 2 (pilot): Deploy to one customer tenant (non-critical) or to one AZ within customer VPC. Use traffic shaping to limit live traffic and increase observability. - Stage 3 (gradual rollout): For each customer region, perform a rolling gateway update with the following steps per region: 1) terraform plan and review. 2) terraform apply in canary subnet (1 instance). Verify attestation flow. 3) Run rewrap test: generate test secret, rotate DEK, verify both old and new DEKs valid
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: region: 1) terraform plan and review. 2) terraform apply in canary subnet (1 instance). Verify attestation flow. 3) Run rewrap test: generate test secret, rotate DEK, verify both old and new DEKs valid within acceptance window. 4) Increase SG replicas and switch traffic. 5) Remove old connectors after confirmation window. - Rollback plan: keep previous module version in a branch that can be applied quickly. If unwrapping starts failing, immediately reverse to previous version and run a reconciliation script that rewraps any DEKs that were rekeyed under new KEK back to previous KEK, using backed-up plaintext OR via HSM facilitate multi-wrap (if available). Terraform-specific migration steps (detailed): - Step 0: Export current state: aws s3 cp s3://<deks-bucket>/metadata/backup-YYYYMMDD.json ./backup.json - Step 1: Map existing KEKs to new terraform resource ids: create a mapping file (keystone-map.csv) with columns: existing_key_id, provider, suggested_tf_id. - Step 2: Use terraform import for each KEK connector: terraform import module.sg.redwood_kek_connector["<tf_id>"] <existing_key_id> - Step 3: For DEK blobs: if module maintains list objects, import via terraform import aws_s3_object.dek["<blob_name>"] <bucket>/<path> - Step 4: Apply plan in dry-run mode: terraform plan -var-file=pilot.tfvars -out=pilot.plan - Step 5: Apply: terraform apply pilot.plan - Step 6: Run smoke tests and audit checks. Secrets lifecycle acceptance tests (smoke checklist): - Write secret -> read
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: -var-file=pilot.tfvars -out=pilot.plan - Step 5: Apply: terraform apply pilot.plan - Step 6: Run smoke tests and audit checks. Secrets lifecycle acceptance tests (smoke checklist): - Write secret -> read from node agent -> verify plaintext matches. - Force attestation failure (tamper test) -> ensure secrets not returned. - Rotate KEK -> ensure old DEKs still decrypt during acceptance window. - Simulate HSM unavailability -> verify fallback policy: either deny unwrapping or use secondary KEK only if explicitly permitted. - Verify audit entry emitted for every wrap/unwrap with fields: operation, subject (node id or operator id), timestamp, kek_id, secret_id, attestation_status. Audit & logging requirements: - Structured audit events (JSON): include event_id, operation, actor, subject_id, kek_id, attestation_result, outcome, request_id. Do not include plaintext or raw DEKs. - Tamper-evident ledger: support streaming of audit events to a write-once object store or append-only service (configurable by customer). Provide optional signed checkpoint metadata for periodic snapshots. - Retention: default 90 days hot, 1 year cold; customer config override supported via module variable. - Integrations: ELK (Logstash -> ES), Splunk HEC, and syslog. Provide example fluent-bit config blocks in the module docs. - Alerts: alert if unwrap failure rate > 0.5% in 5m window or if abnormal spike in rotation operations. HSM/KMS interop considerations and edge cases: - PKCS#11 vs Cloud KMS APIs: provide
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: module docs. - Alerts: alert if unwrap failure rate > 0.5% in 5m window or if abnormal spike in rotation operations. HSM/KMS interop considerations and edge cases: - PKCS#11 vs Cloud KMS APIs: provide adapter layer. PKCS#11 connectors must run in a hardened subnet and use mTLS to SG. - Network HSM latency: add retry/backoff in KEK manager and avoid blocking attestation path for more than 10s. - Multi-KMS setups: support multi-wrap (wrap DEK under multiple KEKs) to allow migration with zero-downtime fallback. Document the increased storage and complexity costs. - Attestation dropout: if attestation provider temporarily fails, do not fallback to unconditional unwrapping. Use configurable emergency procedure requiring operator approval recorded in the audit log. Observability metrics (suggested): - secrets_gateway.requests.total, secrets_gateway.unwrap.success, secrets_gateway.wrap.success, secrets_gateway.attestation.failures, secrets_gateway.kms.latency_seconds (p95,p99), secrets_gateway.memory_dek_cache_size. - Tag metrics by customer_tenant, region, kek_id. Export to Prometheus and set SLOs: unwrap latency p95 < 200ms (local HSM), p95 < 1.5s (network HSM). Security checklist (pre-deploy): - [ ] KEK connectors validated with test keys in staging HSM. - [ ] Terraform module with prevent_destroy in prod-critical resources. - [ ] Audit sink configured and smoke-tested. - [ ] Attestation provider integration verified (TPM quotes validated in staging nodes). - [ ] Secrets Agent signed
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: prevent_destroy in prod-critical resources. - [ ] Audit sink configured and smoke-tested. - [ ] Attestation provider integration verified (TPM quotes validated in staging nodes). - [ ] Secrets Agent signed binaries and reproducible build provenance available. Open questions / risks: - Can all customer HSMs support transient unwrap operations without exposing DEK? (some older appliances may require adaptations). - How to handle air-gapped customers who cannot reach our attestation CA? Proposed: provide local CA distribution and offline attestation mode but requires operator approval and manual audit steps. - Customer regulatory needs for longer audit retention or different storage location. Module needs retention config per-tenant. Next actions / owners: - Prototype adapter for PKCS#11 node -> Diego (due 2024-12-10). - Create terraform import helper scripts and docs -> Linnea (due 2024-12-05). - Build staged canary runbook with exact tests -> Marcus (due 2024-12-08). - Run tabletop for rollback scenarios with SRE -> Asha (due 2024-12-01). Appendix: quick command snippets - Example import command for KEK connector: terraform import module.sg.redwood_kek_connector["cust-onprem-hsm-01"] pkcs11://token-label/key-id-0 - Example smoke curl (node requesting secret): curl -v -k --cert node-agent.crt --key node-agent.key \ -H "Authorization: Bearer <attestation-token>" \ https://secrets-gateway.internal/v1/secrets/read?name=service/a/postgres - Minimal fluent-bit output config for audit
title: Attestation-aware Secrets Gateway roadmap and terraform notes

text: Purpose: A compact, pragmatic bootstrap plan for telemetry that teams can copy when they roll a new feature or model variant. Goal is to get a minimal set of reliable metrics + traces + dashboard widgets live within one sprint so product/SEs have immediate visibility and we can iterate. Scope and constraints: - Applies to new serving routes, rollout toggles, and model swaps across hosted/dedicated/private. - Not a comprehensive instrumentation spec; this is the minimum observability surface for production readiness. - We prioritize signals that help: (1) detect degraded latency/cost, (2) catch correctness regressions tied to model changes, (3) enable quick RCA within 30m. Quick checklist (bootstrap template): 1) Define 6 core metrics to emit (see definitions below). 2) Add a lightweight trace span for request -> router -> model-executor -> cache-check. 3) Create 3 dashboard widgets: latency quantiles, error-rate by upstream, and anomalous token-cost heatmap. 4) Wire alerting on SLI thresholds (P95 latency, error rate > X, token-cost delta > Y%). 5) Add rollout tags to traces and metrics (rollout_id, model_version, route_tag). Core metric definitions (emit at request granularity unless noted): - request_count: counter of incoming requests to the route. Tag by route, customer_tier, rollout_id. (unit: count) - request_success: counter where response HTTP/streaming completed with non-error closure. useful for percent-success. (unit: count) - latency_ms: histogram of end-to-end wall
title: Telemetry bootstrap plan for feature rollouts

text: rollout_id. (unit: count) - request_success: counter where response HTTP/streaming completed with non-error closure. useful for percent-success. (unit: count) - latency_ms: histogram of end-to-end wall clock from request arrival to response last-byte. Buckets: 0-10,10-25,25-50,50-100,100-250,250-500,500-1000,>1000. Tag by route, model_variant. (unit: ms) - model_infer_ms: histogram of time spent in model execution (excludes router, network, and kv-cache hits). Tag by instance_type, quantization_profile. - kv_cache_hit: counter with labels {hit|miss} and kv_key_prefix. Important for cost SLOs and to tune TTL. - token_cost_units: gauge that estimates billed token units per request (use same calc as billing pipeline). Emit per-request and aggregate daily. Tracing basics (span plan): - trace root: request-received (tags: request_id, user_id?, route, rollout_id) - span: router-dispatch (time in routing layer & policy eval) - span: prefix-cache-lookup (indicate hit/miss; attach cached_bytes) - span: model-proxy (network costs, queue wait) - span: model-executor (embed model_variant, instance_id, sequence_length) - Attach lightweight annotations: model_variant_rollout (boolean), fallback_reason (if any), token_estimate. - Sampling: for initial rollout, keep traces for 10% of successful requests + 100% of errored requests. Increase sampling to 50% during active canary windows. Dashboard widget suggestions (compact page layout): Top row (overview): - [W1] Rolling key numbers: request_count
title: Telemetry bootstrap plan for feature rollouts

text: requests + 100% of errored requests. Increase sampling to 50% during active canary windows. Dashboard widget suggestions (compact page layout): Top row (overview): - [W1] Rolling key numbers: request_count (1d), request_success%, P95 latency, avg token_cost_units. - [W2] Latency heatmap split by model_variant / rollout_id (bucketed by latency_ms). Second row (investigation): - [W3] Error waterfall: error_type vs route vs recent traces (links into trace UI). - [W4] KV cache hit ratio by prefix + trend (7d). Bottom row (cost & regression): - [W5] Token-cost delta: compare current model_variant to previous baseline (7d median). - [W6] Request-level scatter: model_infer_ms vs token_cost_units colored by instance_type. Notes: each widget must be filterable by rollout_id and environment (prod/staging). Keep panel query windows short (5m/1h/24h) as appropriate. Alerting and SLOs (starter): - SLO1 latency_p95 < 400ms for small routes; separate buckets for long-running batched routes. Alert if breached for 5m with support paging. - SLO2 error_rate < 0.5% (5m). Alert on sudden step-ups > 3x baseline. - SLO3 token_cost anomaly: trigger if median token_cost_units for variant deviates by > 20% vs baseline for >= 30m. - Pager escalation: alerts on SLO1/SLO2 are paged; cost anomalies create tickets (ops + product). Investigation playbook (first 20 minutes): 1) Check W1 for global context (requests per min, P95). 2) Narrow by rollout_id and correlate with W2/W4; if cache_hit dropped, suspect
title: Telemetry bootstrap plan for feature rollouts

text: tickets (ops + product). Investigation playbook (first 20 minutes): 1) Check W1 for global context (requests per min, P95). 2) Narrow by rollout_id and correlate with W2/W4; if cache_hit dropped, suspect TTL/invalidations. 3) If latency spike, open trace for recent errored and slow traces; look at model-executor span durations and queue wait. 4) If token-cost delta high, compare prompts/inputs in sampled traces and check inference config (temperature, stop tokens, max_tokens param). 5) Triage to model, infra, or routing. Create RCA ticket and attach trace links and a pinboard screenshot. Sampling and cost tradeoffs (practical rules): - Keep base trace sampling low (10%) to limit storage, escalate during canaries to 50-100% for a short window (2–24h). - For high-volume routes, use head-sampling for errors: keep 100% of non-200 responses. - Lease metrics gateway quotas per team: metrics with cardinality > 1k per minute need design review. Rollout instrumentation timeline (example sprint): Day 0: instrumentation PR merged (metrics + span annotations + rollout tags). Day 1: smoke deploy to staging, sanity dashboards for W1/W2; team signoff. Day 2–3: begin canary (5% traffic), set canary-level alert thresholds, increase trace sampling. Day 4–7: gradual ramp to 100% if SLOs hold. Keep enhanced telemetry for 72h after 100% before scaling down traces. Open questions / tradeoffs (to resolve in review): - token_cost_units calc parity: should we emit billing-estimate or raw token-count?
title: Telemetry bootstrap plan for feature rollouts

text: enhanced telemetry for 72h after 100% before scaling down traces. Open questions / tradeoffs (to resolve in review): - token_cost_units calc parity: should we emit billing-estimate or raw token-count? Which makes downstream alerts simpler? - Label cardinality: how many model_variant labels are reasonable? prefer version aliasing to long sha strings. - KV cache instrumentation detail level: prefix vs prompt-fingerprint. Storing fingerprint may be sensitive. To-dos (owners and next actions): - Ethan: add rollout_id tag to router and ensure it surfaces to traces (due: 2025-11-21). - Maya: PR for metric names & histogram buckets in runtime (due: 2025-11-18). - Jamal: dashboard skeleton + widget queries (due: 2025-11-22). - Sofia: write alert runbook snippets and oncall escalation (due: 2025-11-25). Notes to self: keep this doc small and reusable. Copy into feature repo README as OBS-bootstrapping.md when feature is ready. References & links: - Telemetry playbook index (Confluence) — see linked artifacts. - Example PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/512 - Canaries: see OBS-421 for previous canary thresholds and dataset. Version history: - 2025-11-14 created (initial checklist + metrics). - 2025-12-02 updated sampling guidance after infra review. - 2026-02-20 added rollout timeline and to-dos after kickoff.
title: Telemetry bootstrap plan for feature rollouts

text: TAB: Summary Last updated: 2025-03-14 (Nikhil) Goal Define a KPI + telemetry spec to measure whether Quickstart Templates (RAG + Agents) improve Hosted API activation, conversion, and reduce support load, without collecting sensitive content. Scope - Hosted API self-serve + sales-assisted (but metrics primarily PLG). - Template-originated traffic only (tagged / identifiable). - Initial templates: rag-agent-python, rag-agent-typescript. North Star (v1) - Time-to-First-Success (TTFS): from first API key created to first successful template-run request (200 + stream completed OR non-stream response completed) within 60 minutes. Primary KPIs 1) Activation - TTFS (p50/p90) for template users vs non-template new users - Activation rate: % of new accounts reaching “First Success” within D0 (same day) / D7 2) Conversion - % of activated template users converting to paid within 14/30 days - $: first 30-day revenue per activated template account (median) 3) Retention / quality of adoption - D7/D30 retention: % of activated template accounts with >= X tokens / >= Y requests - Share of traffic tagged as template-originated for first 7 days (proxy for “stuck with it”) 4) Support deflection - Support tickets per 1k new accounts (template cohort vs baseline) - Template troubleshooting page engagement vs ticket creation (proxy) Guardrails - Opt-in telemetry by default for non-essential headers; templates must still function if telemetry is disabled. - No prompts, completions, retrieved documents,
title: Quickstarts Adoption KPIs (Hosted API) — draft sheet

text: engagement vs ticket creation (proxy) Guardrails - Opt-in telemetry by default for non-essential headers; templates must still function if telemetry is disabled. - No prompts, completions, retrieved documents, or tool outputs in analytics events. - Avoid high-cardinality dimensions (don’t log raw request IDs, customer IDs in metrics tags; use internal joins). Owners - KPI definitions + targets: Nikhil (Hosted API PM), Henry (Hosted API PM) - Telemetry platform + analytics plumbing: Logan Wright - Template implementation: Monica Patel (DevEx) - Docs funnel + troubleshooting instrumentation: Elliot Price (Docs), Wei Chen (Tech writer) - Support deflection measurement: Priya Shah (Support feedback loop), Dev Patel (Support ops) TAB: KPI Definitions KPI 1 — Time-to-First-Success (TTFS) Definition - Start: timestamp of first API key created OR first successful auth handshake (whichever is available reliably in data warehouse). - End: timestamp of first “template_success” event OR first “api_request_success” with template tag + completion/stream end. Inclusion criteria - Accounts created after template launch date. - Template cohort identified by either: (a) CLI init event (qs.cli.init) (b) template app event (qs.app.start) with template metadata (c) API traffic containing template tag header (see telemetry section) Success condition - Any generation/embedding request that returns 200 AND is not immediately followed by a client-abort flagged error. For streaming: - Success = stream
title: Quickstarts Adoption KPIs (Hosted API) — draft sheet

text: header (see telemetry section) Success condition - Any generation/embedding request that returns 200 AND is not immediately followed by a client-abort flagged error. For streaming: - Success = stream completed (client received end-of-stream) OR server sent a final chunk. Reporting - p50/p90 TTFS by: template_name, language, sdk (py/ts), region (bucketed), and plan type (free/paid) KPI 2 — Activation rate Definition - Activation event: first_success - Rate D0: % of new accounts that reach first_success within 24 hours of signup. - Rate D7: % within 7 days. Activation funnel stages (see “Funnel Events” tab) - signup_created - api_key_created - template_scaffolded - local_run_started - first_success - deployed_success (optional, later) KPI 3 — Conversion to paid Definition - % of accounts in template cohort converting to paid plan within 14 and 30 days of signup. - Also track: time from first_success -> paid conversion (p50/p90). Notes - Separate self-serve paid vs sales-assisted contract conversions. - For sales-assisted, treat “opportunity created” as intermediate metric. KPI 4 — Retention / sustained usage Definition - D7 retention: activated accounts with >= 10 successful requests OR >= 200k tokens by day 7. - D30 retention: activated accounts with >= 50 successful requests OR >= 1M tokens by day 30. Rationale - We want to avoid counting “one-and-done” template runs as success. - Thresholds should be revisited after internal beta baselines. KPI 5 — Support deflection Definition
title: Quickstarts Adoption KPIs (Hosted API) — draft sheet

text: 1M tokens by day 30. Rationale - We want to avoid counting “one-and-done” template runs as success. - Thresholds should be revisited after internal beta baselines. KPI 5 — Support deflection Definition - Ticket rate: (# support tickets created by accounts in cohort within first 14 days) / (# accounts in cohort) * 1000 - Compare to baseline cohort (new accounts same time window, non-template identified). Ticket taxonomy (initial) - auth (401) - quota/rate limit (429) - networking/timeout - streaming disconnect - otel exporter/config - tool schema validation - retrieval empty/poor relevance Success metric - Reduce ticket rate by 15–25% for template cohort vs baseline within 60 days of public launch. TAB: Funnel Events (Event dictionary) Conventions - Prefix “qs.” = template/quickstart specific events - Prefix “api.” = already-existing API traffic events, with added template tags/dimensions Required fields for qs.* events - template_name (enum; e.g., rag-agent) - template_language (python|typescript) - template_version (semver string) - distribution (cli|github) - telemetry_opt_in (true|false) - environment (local|ci|prod) — keep as small enum Event table | Event name | When fired | Source | Required properties | Optional properties (bounded) | Owner | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | qs.cli.install | CLI installed | CLI | os (mac|linux|windows), arch (x64|arm64) | shell (zsh|bash|fish) | Monica | If we can’t reliably capture, drop. Useful to debug install friction. | | qs.cli.init
title: Quickstarts Adoption KPIs (Hosted API) — draft sheet

title: Quickstarts Adoption KPIs (Hosted API) — draft sheet

text: are production-minded; primary value is reducing onboarding entropy and failure loops. Targets (first 60–90 days post public launch) - TTFS p50 <= 20 minutes for template cohort (from api_key_created) - Activation D0: +10–15pp vs baseline - Activation D7: +5–10pp vs baseline - Conversion to paid (30d): +2–4pp vs baseline (small early expectation) - Support tickets per 1k new accounts: -15% vs baseline What “baseline” means - New accounts in same time window that do not have template tags or qs.* events. TAB: Implementation Notes / Constraints Privacy - Do not log prompts, retrieved text, tool inputs/outputs, file paths. - For error events, include category + short stable error_code only (no full stack traces in analytics). Cardinality constraints - Allowed dimensions for dashboards: template_name, template_version (top N only), language, environment, status code, error_category. - Not allowed as metric labels: account_id, request_id, raw model name if too granular (use “model_family” bucket where needed). Data quality concerns (known) - Some users scaffold via GitHub template without CLI; we need API-level attribution via headers. - Some proxies block headers; telemetry should be resilient/optional. - Streaming “success” is tricky; we need consistent detection (SDK improvements in flight). Dependencies - CLI emits qs.cli.init / qs.cli.doctor (DevEx) - SDK/gateway header allowlist + safe handling (Platform) - Console filter for template-tagged traffic (Console/Analytics) - Support
title: Quickstarts Adoption KPIs (Hosted API) — draft sheet

text: flight). Dependencies - CLI emits qs.cli.init / qs.cli.doctor (DevEx) - SDK/gateway header allowlist + safe handling (Platform) - Console filter for template-tagged traffic (Console/Analytics) - Support ticket tagging alignment with taxonomy (Support Ops) TAB: Open Questions 1) TTFS start timestamp source - API key created is best but not always tied to “first attempt”. Alternative: first 401/200 request timestamp. 2) Opt-in defaults - Do we default telemetry_opt_in=true for template events but make header tagging opt-in? (current leaning: yes; reduce proxy issues) 3) GitHub template attribution - If user clones repo, we can still set X-Redwood-Template by default from app config. But we need an obvious “turn off telemetry” switch. 4) Defining “paid conversion” for sales-assisted - Use opportunity created + stage progression? Or strict paid contract signature only? 5) Support deflection attribution window - 14 days vs 30 days. Support suggests 14 days captures most onboarding tickets; keep both in reporting. 6) Regions / deployment modes - For hosted API metrics, we can bucket by region. For Dedicated/Private, separate dashboards later. Change log - 2025-02-07: Added support deflection definitions + ticket taxonomy mapping. - 2025-02-21: Updated template header proposal to be optional due to proxy/header issues found in early trials. - 2025-03-14: Added funnel step definitions (qs.app.first_success preferred over api.request.success for streaming correctness).
title: Quickstarts Adoption KPIs (Hosted API) — draft sheet

text: Purpose and scope: This is a working heuristics doc for teams doing a greenfield or migration deployment of Kubernetes clusters across two common on-prem footprints: dedicated bare-metal racks and VMware vSphere pools. Focus: pragmatic preflight/postflight checks, Terraform module notes, and an operational upgrade strategy that minimizes downtime for inference serving workloads. Audience: - SREs and infra engineers running private deployments - Solutions engineers validating customer on-prem requirements - Platform engineers adapting Terraform modules for mixed fleets Assumptions (explicit): - Clusters are upstream Kubernetes (1.28+ recommended) with kubeadm or cluster-api-based lifecycle. - Providers: metal nodes with iPXE/bootstrap or VMware vSphere (vCenter 7.0+). - Networking: Calico or Cilium; BGP optional for bare-metal. - Storage: local PVs for cache, CSI for persistent workloads (Longhorn or vSphere CSI). - Customer can provide an out-of-band bastion and DNS control. High-level phases (quick): 1) Preflight discovery and inventory 2) Provisioning (Terraform + bootstrap scripts) 3) Initial validation and smoke tests 4) Workload onboarding and soak 5) Controlled upgrades and rollback plan Inventory & discovery checklist (pre-install, items to collect): - vCenter FQDN/IP, datacenter, datastore names, cluster name, resource pool path, template/OVF names - Bare-metal: BMC IPs, MAC addresses, iPXE config, remote console access, serial-over-LAN details - Network: VLAN IDs, MTU,
title: K8s deploy heuristics: baremetal-to-vSphere transition

text: datacenter, datastore names, cluster name, resource pool path, template/OVF names - Bare-metal: BMC IPs, MAC addresses, iPXE config, remote console access, serial-over-LAN details - Network: VLAN IDs, MTU, L3 gateway, BGP ASN (if used), egress NAT info for private registries - Storage: list of SAN/NFS endpoints or vSphere datastore policies, CSI compatibility matrix - Security: KMS/HSM endpoints, customer K8s RBAC policy constraints, allowed Egress URL list for images - Operational: NTP servers, corporate CA certs, monitoring endpoints (Prometheus pushgateway or pull), log collectors Preflight automated checks (scripts to run from an operations laptop or bastion): - SSH and BMC access test: ssh root@<ip>; ipmitool -I lanplus -H <bmc> -U <user> sol info - DNS resolution: dig +short <vcenter>, <registry>, <k8s-api-lb> - Template/OS image checksum: sha256sum vsphere-template.ova - Network MTU probe: ping -M do -s 8972 <target> - Time sync: chronyc tracking or timedatectl status - Disk health: smartctl -H /dev/sdX (bare-metal) Terraform module notes (patterns & pitfalls): - Keep provider blocks in a top-level module that chooses between vsphere and metal providers via a "provider_mode" variable. - Module naming: module "compute_pool" should accept a "mode" var with values ["baremetal","vsphere"] and produce a unified output set: node_ips, node_names, ssh_user, provisioner_hooks - Avoid encoding bootstrap scripts inline in Terraform resources. Instead, write bootstrap scripts to an
title: K8s deploy heuristics: baremetal-to-vSphere transition

text: ["baremetal","vsphere"] and produce a unified output set: node_ips, node_names, ssh_user, provisioner_hooks - Avoid encoding bootstrap scripts inline in Terraform resources. Instead, write bootstrap scripts to an artifact bucket or a file asset, reference via templatefile(). This makes diffs tractable and avoids huge plan outputs. - Sensitive outputs: do NOT output private keys or kubeconfigs. If an operator needs kubeconfig, write to an S3/MinIO object with short TTL and record an object id as output. - Recommended variables (minimal): cluster_name, region_tag, provider_mode, node_counts (controlplane, worker), vsphere_datastore, baremetal_profile_id, registry_mirror - Example variable naming convention: "vm_template_name", "metal_profile_ilo_user". Keep provider-specific vars grouped under a map for clarity. - State management: for mixed fleets, separate Terraform state files per provider to reduce blast radius (e.g., state-baremetal.tfstate, state-vsphere.tfstate). - Idempotency: ensure that external actions (like OS image import) are guarded with data resources and null_resource triggers, not recreated every apply. Bootstrap & provisioning heuristics: - Bare-metal: prefer PXE+iPXE flows for automated installs. Keep a fallback manual : serial console and a preseed/cloudinit fallback image for emergency provisioning. - vSphere: use resource pools + templates; use cloud-init or sysprep-equivalent. Avoid powering on directly into automatic kubeadm join steps until node registration
title: K8s deploy heuristics: baremetal-to-vSphere transition

text: image for emergency provisioning. - vSphere: use resource pools + templates; use cloud-init or sysprep-equivalent. Avoid powering on directly into automatic kubeadm join steps until node registration is predictable. - Image hygiene: build immutable golden images with required kernel tunables for hugepages, device-mapper, and iommu settings baked in. Use a nightly CI job for image builds and artifact promotion. Initial validation & smoke tests (post-provision): - Node readiness: kubectl get nodes --no-headers | awk '{print $2}' should show "Ready" - CNI checks: verify pod-to-pod across nodes, run netshoot traceroutes between nodes, validate serviceClusterIP connectivity - CSI: create a PVC, bind, write a file, and verify reading from another pod on a different node - GPU checks (if applicable): nvidia-smi on host and inside container with --gpus=all; check driver-container compatibility - KV cache and inference-specific checks: run a small inference job against a canary model; measure p95 latency and KV cache hit metrics Upgrade strategy (control plane + node upgrades): - Principles: one thing at a time, measurable, reversible. Aim for planned node-by-node upgrades with canary control plane when possible. - Control plane: prefer rolling controller-manager/kube-apiserver upgrades using static pods or kubeadm upgrade plan. If using stacked etcd on controlplane nodes, upgrade etcd sequentially with snapshot and sanity checks after each node. - Worker nodes: drain/workers one at a
title: K8s deploy heuristics: baremetal-to-vSphere transition

text: using static pods or kubeadm upgrade plan. If using stacked etcd on controlplane nodes, upgrade etcd sequentially with snapshot and sanity checks after each node. - Worker nodes: drain/workers one at a time with PodDisruptionBudgets respected for inference stateful workloads. Use rollout strategy: cordon->drain->update image->reboot->uncordon. - Canary workloads: maintain a small dedicated worker pool for canaries; send 5–10% of traffic and validate latency/correctness before broader rollouts. Rollback plan (quick bullets): - Always take etcd snapshot before control-plane change: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /tmp/etcd-snap.db --endpoints=<ep> - If control plane fails, restore etcd snapshot into isolated restore cluster to validate snapshot integrity before full restore. - For node upgrade failures, reprovision with previous golden image and rejoin cluster using token or kubeadm join --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash. Post-upgrade validations (smoke): - Run suite: node readiness, core DNS resolution for pods, basic inference canary, storage mount validation, cluster autoscaler sanity if used - Compare perf baselines: p50/p95 token decode and model latency compared to pre-upgrade baseline; tag metrics with "upgrade_id" for correlation Operational runbook snippets (copy-paste friendly): - Drain & update node (example): kubectl cordon node-42 kubectl drain node-42 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data --force ssh admin@node-42 'sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y
title: K8s deploy heuristics: baremetal-to-vSphere transition

text: friendly): - Drain & update node (example): kubectl cordon node-42 kubectl drain node-42 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data --force ssh admin@node-42 'sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y linux-image-... && sudo reboot' # wait for node to return, then kubectl uncordon node-42 - Snapshot etcd (example): export ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/peer.key snapshot save /var/backups/etcd-$(date +%F).db Testing & canary plan for inference services: - Traffic split by routing proxy (NGINX/Envoy): add header X-Upgrade-Canary: true for 5% of requests - Run A/B evaluation harness for 24 hours: compare token generation quality, latency, failure rate; auto-abort if error rate > 0.5% or p95 latency regresses > 30% Notes on air-gapped environments: - Mirror registry images into local registry and pin image digests in manifests. - Preload CRI-O/containerd images on nodes or use an init job to pull images from local registry. - Keep a small HTTP artifact store for Terraform templates and bootstrap scripts reachable via corporate proxy. Open questions / action items (work in progress): - Decide preferred control-plane blueprint: kubeadm stacked vs. external etcd vs. CAPI (tradeoffs: operational complexity vs. provider compatibility). - Standardize image signing and attestation for golden images. - Define a lightweight rollback automation to restore etcd and reapply last-known-good manifests
title: K8s deploy heuristics: baremetal-to-vSphere transition

text: complexity vs. provider compatibility). - Standardize image signing and attestation for golden images. - Define a lightweight rollback automation to restore etcd and reapply last-known-good manifests within SLA window. Appendix: useful commands & references - kubeadm upgrade plan - kubectl get cs / kubectl get componentstatuses (deprecated but sometimes used for quick checks) - ipmitool and racadm command snippets for BMC ops - link to internal policy: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Private+Deploy+SLA Quick TODOs before this doc is final: - Add explicit Terraform module examples (small code snippets) and a sample vars.tfvars for a mixed fleet - Add a small CI test harness that validates the bootstrap flow end-to-end in a lab environment - Author a postmortem template for failed upgrades to capture metrics and root cause quickly Notes to self: keep this doc short and pragmatic. If something feels like "too much theory", try it in the lab and record the sequence that worked (commands, timings, race conditions). Change log (high level): - 2025-11-08: initial draft (S.P.) - 2026-01-17: added Terraform state recommendations and preflight checks (J.L.) - 2026-03-10: upgraded upgrade strategy with etcd snapshot examples and canary thresholds (P.N.)

text:
Purpose:
This is a working guide for the company-wide OKR sprint bootcamp (3-week cadence) and the parallel executive intake template. The doc is intentionally tactical — step-by-step checklists, facilitator script snippets, intake form for execs/product leaders, and a lightweight rubric to triage asks.

Why this exists (short):
- Reduce back-and-forth during OKR submission week.
- Give execs and managers a predictable intake flow so we can evaluate tradeoffs quickly.
- Provide a repeatable all-hands + townline agenda that surfaces cross-team dependencies and blocking decisions.

High-level timeline (Q+3 sprint model):
Week 0 (prep) — communications, submitter training, intake open
Week 1 (bootcamp) — focused drafting workshops, review clinics, peer feedback
Week 2 (triage) — exec intake reviews, dependency mapping, narrow to final slate
Week 3 (finalize & announce) — final edits, leadership approvals, all-hands rollout

Owners and roles: (quick reference)
- Program lead: Priya Shah — overall orchestration, comms ownership
- Intake ops: Jamal Rivera — form owner, collates submissions, runs triage board
- Facilitation lead: Elena Gomez — all-hands + bootcamp day room lead
- Product liaison: Marcus Li — coordinates product rubric and model impact estimates
- Legal/Compliance reviewer: Abby Chen — reviews any OKRs touching customer data, private deployments

Submission rules (must-haves for an OKR to be accepted into triage):
1) Goal statement (1 line): what
title: OKR sprint bootcamp guide and exec intake

text:
Legal/Compliance reviewer: Abby Chen — reviews any OKRs touching customer data, private deployments

Submission rules (must-haves for an OKR to be accepted into triage):
1) Goal statement (1 line): what success looks like by quarter end.
2) 3 measurable signals: primary metric + two supporting indicators (baseline and target).
3) Owner(s): name, manager, and cross-team partners.
4) Impact estimate: qualitative + ballpark engineering cost (person-weeks) and infra impact (GPU-hours/week).
5) Risks & mitigations: short list (3 max).
6) Dependencies and blockers: explicit team + ticket refs (Jira preferred).
7) Rollout constraints: any compliance/regulatory gating or region-specific limits.

Intake form template (copy/paste for Confluence/Jira):
- Title:
- Owner (name + email):
- Manager approver:
- One-line goal:
- Why this matters (2–3 sentences):
- Success metrics (primary + baseline + target):
- Estimated engineering effort (pw) and infra footprint (GPU hrs/wk):
- Customer/stakeholder impact and use cases:
- Dependencies (team + ticket links):
- Compliance notes (if any):
- Fall-back plan if the primary approach stalls:
- Preferred prioritization level (critical / high / medium / low):

Triage rubric (0–5 scoring; cut to accept >8 combined):
- Alignment to company north star (0–3)
- Economic/ROI potential (0–3)
- Execution feasibility (0–3) — realistic estimate considered
- Compliance or legal blockers (-3 to 0)
- Cross-team dependency risk (-2 to

text:
to company north star (0–3)
- Economic/ROI potential (0–3)
- Execution feasibility (0–3) — realistic estimate considered
- Compliance or legal blockers (-3 to 0)
- Cross-team dependency risk (-2 to +1)
Notes: use scores to bucket into: Accept (9+), Iterate (6–8), Defer (<6).

Bootcamp day agenda (4 half-days or 2 full days):
Day 1 AM — kickoff, primer on good outcome statements, short examples from last quarter
Day 1 PM — breakout: drafting groups by theme (hosted-api, dedicated, private, optimize, console)
Day 2 AM — peer reviews, rubric calibration with product liaisons
Day 2 PM — intake clinic: intake ops reviews submissions and flags missing data

Facilitator script snippets (short, copyable):
- Kickoff: "We have 45 minutes to make progress on one OKR. Focus on metrics — if you can’t measure it, we can’t iterate. Keep scope small."
- Review: "Reviewer, point out one strength and one risk. Owner, note action items and commit to an update in 48 hours."
- Closing: "If you’re blocked by a dependency, add a ticket to the triage board and assign a primary; we’ll escalate in the next 24 hours."

All-hands / Townline plan (30–40 minutes slot):
- 5 min: brief context + cadence reminder
- 10 min: leadership headlines (CEO + Head of Product — 2 slides each)
- 10 min: 3 lightning OKR pitches (1 min each + 2 min clarifying Qs)
- 5 min: cross-team blockers + call-to-action
- 5 min: closing + next steps (where to submit feedback)

Decision escalation and fallbacks:
-

Decision escalation and fallbacks:
- If an OKR requires cross-org funding (>$250k infra or >8 eng pw), route to Exec Funding Review (ops JIRA OPS-452).
- If a compliance hold exists, Legal must sign off within 3 business days or the OKR is deferred.
- Fallback policy: limited runway for experiments — if not ≥50% progress on metric midpoint at 6 weeks, automatically re-evaluate.

Communication templates (short):
- Comm: "OKR intake is open through DATE. Submit via the intake form linked on Confluence. Workshops all next week — calendar invites posted."
- Reminder to execs: "Please use the exec intake template (one-pager) so the triage board can evaluate consistently"

Operational checklist (bootcamp week):
- [ ] Intake form live and access permissions verified
- [ ] Bootcamp rooms created (Zoom + Miro) + facilitators assigned
- [ ] Triage board skeleton (Jira) with scoring fields ready
- [ ] Exec reviewers roster and review windows scheduled
- [ ] All-hands slides slot reserved and lightning presenters confirmed
- [ ] Communications schedule: announcement, 72h reminder, 24h reminder, day-of

- Should infra costs be surfaced as a currency ($) or

to self / open questions (working):
- Do we need a standardized infra-estimate template for GPU-hours? Marcus to prototype a one-row estimator.
- Should infra costs be surfaced as a currency ($) or relative band (low/med/high)? Ops finance input requested.
- How to handle evergreen cross-team OKRs that roll across quarters (e.g., platform reliability)? Keep a separate backlog lane.

Examples from last quarter (brief):
- Example A: "Reduce tail latency for streaming responses by 30%" — primary metric p95 latency, supporting metrics token cost per req; outcome accepted; delivered via batching and kernel upgrade.
- Example B: "Lower embedding unit cost by 20% for search pipeline" — failed to land due to infra funding shortfall (escalated and deferred).

Follow-ups assigned (immediate):
- Jamal: finalize intake form and link in Confluence by 2025-11-14.
- Marcus: prototype GPU-hours estimator by 2025-11-10 (embed in intake form).
- Elena: finalize bootcamp facilitator notes and quick-start slide deck by 2025-11-13.
- Priya: finalize comms schedule and exec ask email by 2025-11-12.

Version notes: v0-1 = working draft; expect edits after first pilot run. Keep this doc as the canonical bootcamp checklist in the company/misc drive and then copy the finalized template to Confluence when stable.

End of doc — quick sign-off line:

Priya Shah (company-ops) — comments welcome.

text: Purpose: This sheet is an exploratory playground for building stochastic cost/latency/quality curves across quantization options (FP16 baseline, FP8 variants, INT8 dynamic/static, and 4-bit post-training / GPTQ). The goal is a compact calculator teams can use to estimate per-token unit economics and recommended quant choices for different SLOs and workload shapes. Context / why this exists: - We have many one-off spreadsheets for quantization impact; this is trying to centralize a heuristics-driven scenario explorer that trades TTL (tail latency), throughput (tokens/sec), unit cost ("+$ per M tokens"), and expected quality delta (negative log-likelihood or eval delta). - Not a canonical pricing model; meant for internal product/solutions use in sizing Dedicated and Private proposals and for recommending quant profiles in Redwood Optimize. High-level assumptions (editable fields): - Baseline model: name + FP16 baseline perf (tokens/sec, p95 latency at batch=1) - HW profile: gpu type (A100-40, H100, T4), price/hr (on-prem equivalent), inference concurrency factors - Batch size distribution: p50/p90 batch sizes; average effective batch = weighted avg - Requests per minute (rpm) and token distribution: average seq length, prefix reuse rate (cache hit %), kv-cache savings exponent - Costing: GPU hr cost -> cost per second -> cost per token = (1 / tokens_per_second) * cost_per_second, adjusted for batching and concurrency - Quality model: expected BLEU/ROUGE/EM/LM delta per quant choice
title: Stochastic Quant Cost Curve Explorer

text: Costing: GPU hr cost -> cost per second -> cost per token = (1 / tokens_per_second) * cost_per_second, adjusted for batching and concurrency - Quality model: expected BLEU/ROUGE/EM/LM delta per quant choice (user-provided or default heuristics derived from offline evals) Input columns (sheet layout suggestion): - scenario_id (free text) - model_family (e.g., Llama-2-13B, Red-Base-7B) - baseline_fp (FP16|BF16) tokens_per_sec_baseline (numeric) - hw_type (A100/H100/T4) - gpu_cost_per_hour_usd - avg_request_tokens - avg_response_tokens - avg_batch_size - cache_hit_pct - quant_profile (none|int8-dyn|int8-static|fp8-e5m2|4bit-gptq) - throughput_multiplier_vs_baseline (auto-calc or override) - p95_latency_multiplier_vs_baseline (auto-calc or override) - quality_delta_pct (negative = worse, positive = better) Auto-calculated columns (formulas described): - tokens_per_second_effective = tokens_per_sec_baseline * throughput_multiplier_vs_baseline * (1 + cache_hit_pct * cache_speedup_factor) - cost_per_token_usd = (gpu_cost_per_hour_usd / 3600) / tokens_per_second_effective / effective_concurrency - cost_per_request_usd = cost_per_token_usd * (avg_request_tokens + avg_response_tokens) - annualized_cost_estimate = cost_per_token_usd * total_tokens_per_year (user input) - latency_p95_ms = baseline_p95_ms * p95_latency_multiplier_vs_baseline * (1 - batching_latency_reduction_function(avg_batch_size)) Heuristic defaults (editable): - throughput multipliers: INT8-dyn = 1.6x, INT8-static = 1.4x,
title: Stochastic Quant Cost Curve Explorer

text: baseline_p95_ms * p95_latency_multiplier_vs_baseline * (1 - batching_latency_reduction_function(avg_batch_size)) Heuristic defaults (editable): - throughput multipliers: INT8-dyn = 1.6x, INT8-static = 1.4x, FP8-e5m2 = 1.35x, 4bit-gptq = 2.4x (best-case) - p95 latency multipliers: INT8-dyn = 0.95x, INT8-static = 0.9x, FP8-e5m2 = 0.95x, 4bit-gptq = 0.75x (note: quant noise may increase variance -> watch tails) - quality delta guidance (avg across open models): INT8-dyn = -0.3% eval, INT8-static = -0.8%, FP8 = -0.4%, 4bit-gptq = -1.6% (high variance by model/task) - cache_speedup_factor: 0.25 (i.e., cache hits reduce work by 25% on average) - effective_concurrency default: 1.0 (teams should change for multi-tenant or higher qps) Scenario examples (rows to try): - Low-latency chat (batch=1, avg tokens 32 in/out, p95 target < 150ms) -> favors FP8 or FP16 if quality critical; 4-bit often fails tail SLO without increased concurrency or dedicated HW tuning. - High-throughput embedding-generation (batch=64, avg tokens 1) -> 4-bit or INT8-static may be best; cost per embedding drops sharply with batch. - Regulated enterprise LLM (on-prem H100, must maintain <0.5% quality delta) -> prefer INT8-dyn with conservative heuristics; include a quality safety margin column. Quality vs Cost frontier pane (how to visualize in the sheet): - X axis = cost per 1M tokens (USD), Y axis = expected quality delta (relative to baseline) -> plot points per quant_profile per HW type. Color by p95_latency_MS
title: Stochastic Quant Cost Curve Explorer

text: pane (how to visualize in the sheet): - X axis = cost per 1M tokens (USD), Y axis = expected quality delta (relative to baseline) -> plot points per quant_profile per HW type. Color by p95_latency_MS bucket. Add hover text for batch size and cache hit. - Also a stacked bar view of cost components: GPU time, host overhead, memory swap penalties (rare), and amortized pre/post processing. Recommended decision rules (rule-of-thumb outputs): - If p95_target_ms <= baseline_p95_ms * 0.85 -> avoid 4-bit unless proven with latency tail testing. - If quality_delta_pct < -1.0% and task is generation-sensitive (summaries, code) -> prefer INT8-static or FP8 with eval set check. - If avg_batch_size >= 16 and seq_len <= 8 -> 4-bit is recommended candidate (run offline eval + calibration). - If on-prem with low GPU cost/hr and high qps -> prioritize throughput gains; cost per token less decisive. Open questions / TODOs (short): - Get updated real-world throughput multipliers for Red-Base and Red-Large series on H100 (OPT-1287). - Add per-layer GPTQ sensitivity map (not just global 4-bit) -> need bench harness output format. - Bake in Redwood Optimize suggestions: map workload to a recommended quant_profile + confidence score. - Add variance propagation: show p95 tail increase probability with 4-bit for small batch sizes. Quick example CSV snippet for copy/paste into a visualization sheet: scenario_id,model_family,baseline_tps,hw_type,gpu_usd_hr,avg_batch,cache_hit,quant_profile,throughput_mult,p95_mult,quality_delta
title: Stochastic Quant Cost Curve Explorer

Quick example CSV snippet for copy/paste into a visualization sheet: scenario_id,model_family,baseline_tps,hw_type,gpu_usd_hr,avg_batch,cache_hit,quant_profile,throughput_mult,p95_mult,quality_delta chat-lowlat,Red-Base-7B,120,A100-40,6.5,1,0.1,fp8-e5m2,1.35,0.95,-0.4 embed-bulk,Red-Base-7B,800,H100-80,12.0,64,0.02,4bit-gptq,2.4,0.75,-1.6 enterprise-secure,Red-Large-13B,60,H100-80,12.0,2,0.2,int8-dyn,1.6,0.95,-0.3 Notes on validation: - Cross-check against microbench results (GH: redwood-inference/benchmarks#342) and the confluence quant-benchmarks page. Use at least 3 seed runs for noisy 4-bit numbers. - Where possible, use real downstream task evals (QA, summarization, code) rather than LLM perplexity alone. Suggested next steps for product/solutions engineers: - Convert this sheet into a small Redwood Optimize widget prototype (inputs -> compute -> frontier plot) and validate with 5 pilot customers. - Run the 4-bit GPTQ sensitivity harness on Red-Base & Red-Large on H100 (ticket OPT-1287) and pull realistic throughput multipliers into the heuristics tab. - Draft short runbook for "safe quant rollout" that pairs recommended quant profile with a canary eval prompt set and automatic fallback policy. Appendix: shorthand formulas to paste into sheet cells (example Excel/Sheets friendly) - tokens_per_second_effective = =C2*D2*(1+E2*0.25) (C2=tokens/sec baseline, D2=throughput multiplier, E2=cache_hit_pct) - cost_per_token_usd = =(gpu_usd_hr/3600)/tokens_per_second_effective/F2
title: Stochastic Quant Cost Curve Explorer

text: friendly) - tokens_per_second_effective = =C2*D2*(1+E2*0.25) (C2=tokens/sec baseline, D2=throughput multiplier, E2=cache_hit_pct) - cost_per_token_usd = =(gpu_usd_hr/3600)/tokens_per_second_effective/F2 (F2=effective_concurrency) - latency_p95_ms = =baseline_p95_ms * p95_mult * (1-0.12*LN(1+avg_batch)) Contact / owner notes: Aisha Kapoor (product-optimize) — use this as a working sheet, keep heuristics conservative until we have H100 datapoints. Leave comments inline, flag any numbers copied from other teams. Change log (recent): - 2026-03-10: moved default 4-bit throughput multiplier from 2.0x -> 2.4x after internal bench run; added variance note. - 2026-01-12: initial draft and heuristics table created.
title: Stochastic Quant Cost Curve Explorer

text:
Purpose:
This doc captures a slice-first approach for designing dashboards, metric definitions, and alert tuning for tokenized LLM inference flows. It prioritizes operator workflows (fast triage, low-noise alerts) and investigative artifacts (trace anchors, prompt snapshots) so oncall can go from alert -> hypothesis -> remediation in under 15 minutes.

Scope and constraints:
- Applies to hosted and dedicated inference paths (same signal set, different thresholds).
- Focus on runtime surface: request ingress, routing, KV cache effectiveness, model execution, and response emission.
- Not covering long-term cost chargeback or offline eval pipelines (those are linked).

Principles (short):
1) Slice-first: dashboards and alerts should be built around a small set of high-value slices (customer-tier, route, model-variant, cold-cache) rather than global aggregates.
2) Alert finessing: prefer graded alerts + investigation scorecards vs single hard thresholds.
3) Observation as product: include example queries, a minimal replay harness, and a teardown checklist on every dashboard.

Important slices to onboard (initial):
- customer_tier=enterprise (high priority SLA)
- route=/chat/stream vs /chat/batch
- model_variant={gptx-8b, gptx-4b-quantized}
- kv_cache_warmth={hot,warm,cold} (defined below)
- tenant_concurrency_bucket={low,med,high}

Metrics definitions (canonical list, naming and intent):
- inference.request.count: counter. incremented on request ingress. tags:
title: Observability slice storyboard and alert finessing notes

text:
kv_cache_warmth={hot,warm,cold} (defined below)
- tenant_concurrency_bucket={low,med,high}

Metrics definitions (canonical list, naming and intent):
- inference.request.count: counter. incremented on request ingress. tags: route, model_variant, customer_tier, tenant_id
- inference.tff.first_token_ms: gauge/histogram. time from request start to first token emitted. buckets: [50,100,250,500,1000,2500]
- inference.tff.nth_token_ms: histogram for downstream per-token latency (use for tail analysis)
- inference.model.exec.gpu_ms: histogram. GPU execution time broken out per-pass (include kv-hit flag)
- inference.kv.cache.hit_rate: ratio (0-1) evaluated per tenant/model_variant over sliding 5m
- inference.kv.cache.warmth_index: integer 0..100; computed as weighted recent hit_rate * request_frequency (heuristic described in appendix)
- inference.queue.wait_ms: histogram for time spent in scheduler queue
- inference.errors.rate: counter with tags error_type={timeout,oom,model-mismatch,invocation-error}
- inference.output.quality.regressions: export from quality-eval hook (occurs when eval set fails)
- observability.trace.sample_rate: exported for visibility into sampling controls

KV warmth heuristics (practical):
- warm: warmth_index >= 70 and hit_rate >= 0.8 over 5m
- warm-borderline: 50..69 or hit_rate 0.6..0.8
- cold: warmth_index < 50 or hit_rate < 0.6
Rationale: cache warmth correlates with TTF and per-token GPU time; use for adaptive batching and prefetch triggers.

Tracing
title: Observability slice storyboard and alert finessing notes

text:
50..69 or hit_rate 0.6..0.8
- cold: warmth_index < 50 or hit_rate < 0.6
Rationale: cache warmth correlates with TTF and per-token GPU time; use for adaptive batching and prefetch triggers.

Tracing annotations and span contract (lightweight):
Span names: ingress.request -> scheduler.queue -> model.exec -> emitter.stream -> exporter.kv_lookup
Required span tags: request_id, tenant_id, route, model_variant, kv_hit (true/false), batch_id (if batched), prompt_sha (hash of prompt prefix), token_count
Trace size policy: prefer compact spans; only include prompt_sha, not raw prompt. Prompt snapshots linked as external secure artifact when sample flagged.
Sampling policy prototype:
- Error traces: 100% capture for error_type != success
- High-latency: capture 50% where first_token_ms > p99 of slice
- Regular: 1% uniform sampling, with bias to enterprise tenants to 5%
Retention: full traces kept 30d, compact breadcrumb trace (no attachments) 90d

Dashboard composition (slice-first wireframe):
Top row (operational anchors): request rate (per-slice), p50/p90/p99 first_token_ms, error rate.
Second row (causality suspects): kv_cache.hit_rate, queue.wait_ms distribution, GPU exec_ms distribution.
Third row (investigation hooks): sample trace list (linked), recent prompt snapshot sample ids, active long-lived batches.
Fourth row (alert health): recent alerts fired, noise score, MTTR trend.
Widget best practices: 1) default to slice dropdown at top-left, 2) show both absolute
title: Observability slice storyboard and alert finessing notes

text:
sample ids, active long-lived batches.
Fourth row (alert health): recent alerts fired, noise score, MTTR trend.
Widget best practices: 1) default to slice dropdown at top-left, 2) show both absolute and normalized (per-1000 reqs) views, 3) annotate with typical baseline ranges for each model_variant.

Alerting and finessing strategy (concrete):
- Strategy: avoid single global hard alert. For each slice, implement 3-level alerting:
 - P2 warning (informational): rolling 10m first_token_ms p95 > baseline*1.5 for 15m; notify #ops as low-urgency.
 - P1 degradation: p95 > baseline*2 for 10m OR error_rate increased by 5x sustained for 5m; page on-call.
 - P0 incident: service-level SLA breach (e.g., enterprise p99 > SLA), or sustained 50% request loss.
- Noise controls: require at least N tenants affected (default N=3) OR sustained absolute growth in requests in slice to avoid single-tenant flakiness spamming ops.
- Alert enrichment payload: include top 3 tenants by error rate, top 3 routes, last 10 sample prompt_shas, and a link to a pre-run investigation query.

Investigation scorecard (what oncall should collect quickly):
1) Snapshot: time window + tenant(s) involved + slice dropdown state
2) Quick metrics: p50/p95/p99 first_token_ms, kv_hit_rate, gpu_ms, queue_wait_ms, errors by type
3) Trace anchor: open a sampled trace with spans annotated by kv_hit, batch_id, prompt_sha
4) Reproduce: run replay harness (if safe) in staging with same prompt_sha and model_variant;
title: Observability slice storyboard and alert finessing notes

3) Trace anchor: open a sampled trace with spans annotated by kv_hit, batch_id, prompt_sha
4) Reproduce: run replay harness (if safe) in staging with same prompt_sha and model_variant; check model.exec.gpu_ms delta
5) Rollback plan: if rollout suspected, failover route to known-good model_variant (automated rollback playbook link)

Instrumentation checklist (developer handoff):
- Emit request_id and prompt_sha on ingress span
- Increment inference.request.count with all defined tags
- Report kv_hit boolean on both span and metrics pipeline (so hit-rate can be computed without tracing)
- Export histogram buckets for first_token_ms and queue.wait_ms (use documented bucket boundaries)
- Add feature flag hooks to increase trace sampling for experiments

Operational runbooks to author next (owners):
- rollout-drift-investigation-runbook (owner: Sara Patel)
- kv-prefetch-triggering-playbook (owner: Diego Ramos)
- alert-noise-tuning-playbook (owner: Liam O'Connor)

Open questions / decisions to make:
- Should enterprise tenants have dedicated higher-sampling traces or just higher retention? (ops cost vs signal)
- Where to store prompt snapshots for privacy; do we always store prompt_sha only and require JIT retrieval?
- How conservative should noise de-dup be for low-volume tenants?

Appendix: quick SQL snippets (for dashboard queries)
- recent noisy tenants by error rate:
 SELECT tenant_id, sum(errors) as errs, sum(requests) as reqs FROM inference_metrics
title: Observability slice storyboard and alert finessing notes

text:
low-volume tenants?

Appendix: quick SQL snippets (for dashboard queries)
- recent noisy tenants by error rate:
 SELECT tenant_id, sum(errors) as errs, sum(requests) as reqs FROM inference_metrics WHERE ts > now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY errs DESC LIMIT 10;
- kv warmth index (pseudo):
 warmth_index = round( 100 * (0.7 * hit_rate_5m + 0.3 * min(1.0, requests_5m / 100)) )

Next steps (this doc):
- Review with ops and SRE (schedule 30m sync)
- Create dashboard template from wireframe and push to staging (owner: Maya Chen)
- Prototype enriched alert payload with top-tenants and prompt_shas attached (owner: eng-observability)

Notes to self / scratchpad:
- Keep prompt snapshots out of traces by default; use an encrypted artifact store referenced by prompt_sha.
- Consider tie-breaker metric: p95 first_token_ms normalized by kv_hit_rate to quickly surface cache regressions.

text: use_case_summary: Customer support SaaS that will embed an assistant in-app (chat) and run background reranking + embeddings for search. Primary goals: fast response for conversational flows (p50 < 350ms), reasonable token cost for heavy daily active users, and predictable monthly spend. Want hosted API for speed of integration and self-serve before committing to Dedicated. notes: Lead profile: product-led SMB, mature engineering (React + Node), 2 ML engineers. Quote from CTO: 'We need something that beats our current open-source stack on latency but doesn't blow the budget.' Key theme: model-selection questions. Specific asks: - How do Llama 2/3 variants compare to Mistral/Qwen for short conversational latency vs output quality? - Any recommended cost-effective flavors (quantized or smaller context length) for high-concurrency chat? - Can we route cheap requests to smaller models and fall back to higher-quality for escalation? - Any streaming token/pricing caveats we should budget for? SE notes: provided suggested A/B matrix: Llama 2-13B (fast, low cost) vs Mistral-7B-Instruct (good quality sweet spot) vs Qwen-7k (large context) for long-form transcripts. Customer keen to run 3-day micro-benchmarks on hosted_api using our sample prompt set. Security: needs SOC2 + SSO, plus retention controls; wants drive link to security FAQ. Next step: issue trial keys and shared benchmark harness, set POC start date (target 2026-03-16). recent_activities: 2026-03-02 - Inbound signup: initial

text:
notes: Account background:
- Mid-sized radiology network with 45 hospitals and 120 outpatient clinics. Heavy regulatory constraints (HIPAA plus regional data residency).
- Building a diagnostic-assist platform: image embeddings for search (primary), automated report summarization, and a real-time triage chat for clinicians.

Value drivers / why Redwood: 
- Need predictable unit economics for inference; current cloud spend on ad-hoc endpoints is spiking.
- Primary ask: Dedicated reserved GPU capacity with throughput guarantees and clear latency SLOs; fallback behavior during capacity saturation is critical.

Technical requirements discussed on calls: 
- Throughput/latency targets: baseline 100 concurrent image inferences (1 study ~ 50–200 tokens for report gen) with p95 infer latency < 250ms and p99 < 800ms for primary model; nightly batch embedding jobs up to 8k studies/hour.
- Reserved GPU planning: expect H100-class GPUs for initial rollout. Proposed config: 4 reserved GPUs in primary region (na-east) + hot-standby in a secondary region (na-west). Need guidance on sizing: Redwood asked for workload traces; Orion provided anonymized QPS + token length histograms.
- Capacity buffer: Orion wants guaranteed headroom for +30% burst for scheduled peak windows (morning rounds).
- Model variant fallback: must be able to degrade to smaller/quantized model variants with graceful latency increase but bounded quality loss. Default fallback chain requested: full fp16 model ->
title: Orion Medical Imaging AI

text:
rounds).
- Model variant fallback: must be able to degrade to smaller/quantized model variants with graceful latency increase but bounded quality loss. Default fallback chain requested: full fp16 model -> int8 quantized variant -> condensed anatomical model for non-critical triage tasks.
- Cross-region failover: regulatory limitation on PHI movement; primary failover plan: cross-region only for metadata and inference routing; image payloads must remain in-region unless explicit patient consent. Need to define what "failover" means operationally (read-only vs. full active-active).

Security / compliance points: 
- KMS/HSM integration mandatory. Key rotation policy: monthly.
- Audit logs retention: minimum 7 years; export capability to customer SIEM.
- SSO/SAML + SCIM for provisioning; RBAC controls for model rollout and key ops.

SLAs discussed / negotiation points: 
- Redwood proposed: 99.9% uptime for Dedicated cluster; p95 latency SLOs per model profile.
- Orion counter: require financial credits for p99 misses during scheduled hours; require documented runbook and support escalation matrix (on-call 24/7).
- Desired contractual item: explicit behavior under saturation (how routing/fallback will occur, preemption policies, how long failover can last before data residency concerns).

POC plan & success criteria: 
- 4-week POC in dedicated tenant. Weeks 1–2: baseline performance validation (latency, throughput, token-cost metrics). Week 3: stress to 2x expected
title: Orion Medical Imaging AI

residency concerns).

- Success metrics: sustained 100 concurrent inferences with p95 < 250ms, model-fallback triggers within 250ms, no PHI leaves primary region during failover test, and cost-per-inference within 20% of forecast.

text: Mid-market B2B SaaS (collaboration/productivity) exploring migration from current LLM vendor due to repeated latency regressions over last two quarters. Primary driver: chat/agent user experience — streaming + tool calling for real-time assistant inside product. Context: they run a multi-agent orchestration layer that issues tool calls (search, calendar, ticket API). Incumbent started showing P50/P95 spikes after model rollout in Dec. Users report "stutters" in streaming assistant, hurting adoption. Team sized at ~350 engineers and product folks, cost-conscious but will pay for predictable UX. POC notes: 2-week Hosted API POC completed. Benchmarks (client-reported, infra validated): incumbent p50 ~230ms, p95 ~560ms for streaming-first flows; Redwood Hosted p50 ~58ms, p95 ~180ms under same request mix (streaming enabled, small tool-call fanout). Tool-calling latency when orchestration waits for function output improved by ~2-3x on Redwood in measurements. Quotes: "We saw the streaming dropouts disappear on Redwood — real-time feel is back." — Lead PM (call). Requirements: must support streaming tokens + function/tool calling with end-to-end latency SLOs (p95 target <250ms for assistant response with single external tool call), support SSO/SAML for internal users, SOC2-level assurances, audit logs, and optional EU data residency for customer data. text: notes: Quick summary: Axion Guard is a payment+health fintech (card processing + claims billing) with strict PCI + PHI needs. Wants agent-assist integrated into Salesforce and internal desk ops, automatic ticket summarization, and safe tool-calling (CRM writebacks, refunds). Primary constraint = VPC deployment + customer-managed KMS + immutable audit trail for 7 years. Performance: p50 token latency target <= 120ms, p95 under 300ms during normal load; must sustain bursty spikes up to ~10k concurrent agent events with graceful degradation (fallthrough to cached responses). Pricing sensitivity: moderate — will trade some cost for strong compliance and predictable latency. text: notes: Deal overview: Hightide runs low-latency personalized creative selection for ad auctions. Interested in Dedicated capacity + VPC control. Primary ask: firm p95 and p99 SLOs for 1k RPS sustained traffic and spike handling up to 3k RPS. Cost sensitive; wants clear unit economics for reserved GPUs vs burst on shared. Key quotes / call snippets: - "We need guarantees at peak -- p95 under 120ms for non-streaming generate calls; anything worse loses auctions." (CTO) - "Streaming vs batch counts differently for our SDKs; client sees extra 30–40ms because of first-byte framing." (Platform lead) Measurement alignment concerns: - Customer measures end-to-end on client: DNS + TLS handshake + app routing + Redwood call time. Redwood measures server-side token emission. Need a common definition: agree to report both client-side E2E and server-side server-emit latency. - Want p50/p95/p99 on both streaming and non-streaming paths. For streaming, p95 should be defined on time-to-first-token and time-to-last-token for controlled-length responses. - Request: include percentiles from both Redwood nodes and client SDK (sampled). Add synthetic runner that mirrors their SDK to avoid sigma differences. Throughput & reserved GPU planning notes: - Target sustained 1k RPS mixed payloads (avg tokens per request ~120). Peak planning to 3k RPS with 2-minute sustained bursts. - Ask for Redwood to map model variants + quantization to GPU pool sizes; want a recommended reserved GPU count + burst allowance

text: notes: Profile: mid-to-large legal eDiscovery vendor. Heavy search + rerank workload; 180M doc corpus (emails, contracts, filings). Current stack: BM25 + SBERT (sentence-transformers) reranker for top-50 hits. They pay external embedding vendor today; cost per query ~ $0.035 at 95th. Want to get under $0.02. Primary drivers: reduce per-query cost, tighten p95 latency (<150ms end-to-end for search), and ensure no relevance regressions for Triage & Privilege review flows. POC ask: run embeddings + new learned reranker (distilled cross-encoder) vs current SBERT reranker on 20k labeled holdout with DCG/NDCG, recall@k, and precision@k, plus human eval on 1k critical queries. Evals plan: SE to provide harness that computes NDCG@10, pairwise win-rate, and failure cases. Lacuna insists on canary A/B with rollback automatically triggered if NDCG drops > 1.5 points or customer-reported misses > 0.5%. Latency/infra: Target 120 qps sustained for top-k retrieval + rerank path during peak (legal big batch jobs). P95 overall response <= 150ms for interactive UI. Need quant and batching guidance from Redwood Optimize. Indexing constraints: 180M docs -> average doc length 1.2k tokens. Concern about shard sizing and memory for KV caches. Asking about prefix caching + partial reranker offload to CPU for cost saving. Security: must run in VPC/on-prem for regulated clients; KMS + audit logs mandatory. They will not accept hosted API for certain clients. Air-gapped option may be requested later. Quote

text: notes: Account overview: Pelagia is a national maritime intelligence agency building a consolidated RAG search across ship logs, AIS feeds, classified incident reports, and analyst notes. Primary constraint = must operate in air-gapped datacenters (no egress). Procurement-heavy; long procurement lead time (estimated Q1 FY25 close). Key technical requirements (from SE calls): - Indexing scale: target ~120M documents, 3-5B tokens; incremental ingestion at ~200k docs/day from port systems. - Embedding strategy: high-dimensional embeddings preferred for precision; want on-prem vector store with multi-shard nearest neighbor and deterministic reranking step. - Reranking: neural reranker after ANN recall step to improve precision on low-recall queries (high consequence). Need configurable re-rank budget per query. - Latency/throughput: 95th p90 < 250ms for short fact lookups; batched analytics queries allowed higher latency. Expected steady-state QPS for live search ~80 qps, spike to 600 qps during incident surges. - Cost sensitivity: tight OPEX constraints for cloud; prefer predictable reserved pricing or fixed-capacity model. Security/compliance notes: - CISO requires SSO/SAML integration for analysts, KMS/HSM for key management, full audit logs for query and model access, and offline-installable artifacts with SBOM. - Data residency: all data must remain inside org network; optional replication to DR facility in same gov cloud region. - They asked for a transcript-proof POC that
title: Pelagia Maritime Intelligence

text: offline-installable artifacts with SBOM. - Data residency: all data must remain inside org network; optional replication to DR facility in same gov cloud region. - They asked for a transcript-proof POC that can run without outbound network (we proposed a USB-distro style installer plus signed container images). Commercial/procurement notes: - They will evaluate Redwood Private (on-prem) with Dedicated capacity for the inference tier and Optimize for tuning cost/performance. - PV: Interested in an annual committed capacity contract; RA will prepare draft SOW and IL4 packaging. Finance wants clear unit economics: cost per query for 1M queries/month with 120M-index baseline. - Comp concerns: "We like Palantir for end-to-end but expensive; Elastic is familiar but lacks secure NN reranker as a managed offering." POC plan agreed (high-level): - Phase 1 (2 weeks): Security & packaging review, finalize SBOM and installer checklist. - Phase 2 (3 weeks on-prem POC): Import 5M representative docs, measure embed throughput, ANN recall latency, and reranker accuracy on Redwood models. - Phase 3 (1 month): Scale tests to 50M docs, report cost/Q, propose production topology and reserved capacity commit. Quotes & direct items from meetings: - CISO: "No external egress; we need a box we can ship to the datacenter and install behind the firewall." - Head of Search: "Precision at the top is everything — false positives in incident triage cost lives." text: notes: Summary: enterprise fintech platform (risk analytics + customer conversations). Strong preference for dedicated GPU pools and on-prem-like VPC isolation. They run both synchronous chat assistants and high-throughput embedding pipelines for search/rerank. Key requirements (shorthand): - Baseline steady traffic for chat ~120 QPS across 12k daily concurrent users; embeddings batch jobs overnight. - Latency targets: p95 <= 120ms for short prompts (<=256 tokens), p95 <= 350ms for multistep flows. - Throughput need: steady-state token/sec ~8k aggregate; peak bursts up to 420 QPS (3–4x baseline) during market open. - Security: SSO (SAML), SOC2, KMS/HSM + customer-managed keys, audit logging, EU data residency for certain customers. - Ops: want per-token cost breakdown, token-level tracing, rollout canary and rollback policies. Quote from CTO: "We can't accept unpredictable throttling during market spikes — need a capacity guarantee and a clear escalation SLA." POC/Workshop notes: - 02/02 sizing workshop ran collection of synthetic and production traces. Confirmed baseline QPS and token distribution. - Team validated that prefix caching and KV eviction policies materially reduce token egress; asked for batching recommendations. - Agreed to follow-up on reserved GPU mix (80GB class) + burst pool sizing. Procurement/Legal: discussed 12–24 month reserved commitment. Legal asking for explicit availability SLA + credits table. Sales play: propose 8xA100-80GB reserved w/ 4x burst pool,
title: Peregrine Capacity Works
