text: notes: Summary: Marlow Edge evaluated Redwood for low-latency edge inference across robotics fleets. Strong initial interest from product & platform teams. Primary drivers: strict data residency, deterministic latency for edge controllers, cost control at scale. Decision: closed-lost — internal infra team will self-host using vLLM/TGI and TensorRT-LLM optimizations. Key quotes: "We need kernel-level control and hardware-direct deployment — hosted model won't fly for our safety team." Security: Security and compliance ran a deep checklist; SSO + audit logging OK but residency and 3rd-party managed KMS flagged. They asked for on-prem air-gapped option; our private offering answered some items but internal procurement pushed for owning everything. Commercial: initial pricing looked competitive, but after procurement's TCO model (3yr) the self-host option wins on per-token cost with batching/quantization. Also employee_count and fleet scale tipped economics. Internal dynamics: after a VP-level org change the internal champion (Lead ML Infra) moved to a different role, and the new team prefers in-house ops. There is curiosity about Redwood Optimize recommendations, but no timeline. Next actions captured: 1) Mark closed-lost; 2) Add to watchlist for infra pain signals (latency spikes, model ops cost); 3) Offer a short post-mortem doc & migration checklist for when they consider hybrid. Stated blockers on the final call: budget freeze, internal champion left, and strategic mandate to
title: Marlow Edge Systems

text: notes: Company: Lodestar CodeFlow — JetBrains plugin vendor (IntelliJ/PyCharm) focused on low-latency completions and local code indexing. Primary ask: integrate Redwood inference to power streaming completions with model/version pinning and an offline fallback running lightweight local model or cached completions. User base: 15k MAU across OSS + paid tiers; enterprise trial group ~120 dev seats. Architecture note: plugin runs local indexed AST + repo-level embeddings; policy: local index stays on-device; inference can be hosted or run in customer's VPC for enterprise. POC goals (agreed 2026-02-25): - End-to-end completion latency (IDE -> Redwood -> token stream) median <50ms at 95th percentile for single-line completions - Streaming reliability >99.5% over 1k concurrent devs in demo harness - Model pinning support: must freeze model_id per release channel and support automatic fallback to quantized variant when Dedicated capacity constrained. text: behavior next_step: Finalize POC SLOs and reserved GPU count; legal to review SLA draft; confirm VPC peering details. blockers: disagreement on reserved GPU count vs autoscale budget legal SLA uptime credit language network egress limit for VPC peering quantization accuracy tradeoffs on 4-bit profiles poc_results_summary: Throughput: steady-state validated at ~550 rps for short rerank requests with 6x GPUs under continuous load Latency: p95 ~180ms at steady state, p99 spikes to 420ms during cold-starts without warm pool Warm pool impact: pre-warmed nodes reduced cold-start p99 from 420ms to 120ms (90% reduction on test cases) Autoscale: default cooldown (30s) led to thrashing under bursty traffic; 45-60s cooldown recommended Queueing: unbounded queueing caused queue times to exceed 8s in synthetic bursts — prefer bounded queue + early reject or prioritized tails Model fallback: automatic fallback to 13B variant keeps latency within targets but increases token-level error on some rerank cases Network: VPC peering egress caps caused ~10% throughput degradation during large batch prep; needs infra tweak desired_slos: Reranking: steady throughput >= 500 rps, p95 latency <= 200ms, p99 <= 350ms Doc-search embedding: p95 <= 150ms for vector upsert and query pipelines Availability: 99.95% monthly uptime target for dedicated pool (SLA credit required) Queue wait time: median queue < 200ms, 99th < 2s for prioritized traffic proposed_reserved_capacity: Initial commit: 8x A100-equivalent
title: Proxima Throughput Works

text: 99.95% monthly uptime target for dedicated pool (SLA credit required) Queue wait time: median queue < 200ms, 99th < 2s for prioritized traffic proposed_reserved_capacity: Initial commit: 8x A100-equivalent GPUs (region spread across 2 AZs) Buffer plan: 2x GPU warm pool reserved per AZ to reduce cold starts Burst capacity: allow surge to additional 4 GPUs for peak market hours on pre-approved billing Auto-fallback: route excess to hosted API fallback with throttling and degraded SLA for non-critical routes autoscaling_policy_summary: Scale-up trigger: add 1 GPU node when queue length > 40 for 30s or CPU/GPU busy > 85% for 20s Scale-down trigger: remove 1 GPU node after 300s of node idle time, but maintain warm-pool floor (min 2 per AZ) Cooldowns: recommended scale-up cooldown 60s, scale-down cooldown 300s to avoid oscillation Batching: leverage continuous batching where applicable with max batch latency 50ms to trade throughput vs latency Canary: canary 10% traffic to new model variant for 10 mins then ramp if metric delta <2% queueing_and_warm_pool_requirements: Warm pool: maintain 2 hot nodes/GPUs per AZ for sub-second cold-start behavior Queueing strategy: hybrid bounded queue with priority lanes (re-rank high priority, background indexing low priority) Backpressure: apply early reject for non-priority requests when queue depth > 200 to protect SLOs Observability: require token-level latency histograms, per-route queue depth alerts, and tracing for queued requests SLA expectations:

text: notes: - Mid-market product org (PM + infra) evaluating Hosted first, then Dedicated for predictable unit economics. - Primary workload: embeddings for "recommendation" index + chat assistant for power users (support + in-product help). - Latency targets: interactive chat p95 < 500ms (server-side), embedding throughput ~200 qps during cron windows; cost sensitivity high — wants to reduce token costs by >=2x compared to hosted baseline. - On 2026-03-02 workshop we walked through practical levers: continuous batching, prefix/KV cache for repeated prompts, and quantization profile tradeoffs (fp16 -> 8/4-bit). Customer: "If we can get 2.5x cost improvement without <10% quality drop, we can justify Dedicated commit." - SE notes (Priya): initial quantized run shows 2.1x inference cost reduction at ~5% drop on abstractive summarization eval set; team wants a deeper eval on in-domain prompts. - Security: must integrate SSO (SAML) and sign DPA + require audit_logging + KMS for envelope keys. Data residency: primary in US-East; CISO flagged long-term retention policy. - Procurement: asked for committed capacity pricing tiers and burst policy SLA. Also requested a written fallback policy if Dedicated capacity degraded. - POC scope agreed: 3-week Dedicated trial with 2 model variants, cost dashboard + unit economics report, and one on-site (virtual) cost-optimization session. - Quote action: AE to deliver Dedicated pricing draft by 2026-03-15; follow-up workshop to finalize quant settings

text: deal_notes_snippet: AE note: "Customer is high strategic value — strong chance to move to Dedicated + Private in 2026 H1 if POC shows cost parity and latency SLOs. Priority blocker is SOC2 + NDA." notes: Summary: Enterprise RAG + code search POC. Customer builds internal developer tools for a large engineering org; primary product = cross-repo code search + authoring assistant. They have ~120M code + doc artifacts, plan to store dense embeddings and run reranking on top-k. Context / pain: - Current infra: self-hosted Elasticsearch + keyword search; brittle for semantic queries, high maintenance. - Trying to move to vector-first pipeline: approx 120M vectors (1.5k dim candidate), expect 300-400k daily updates (CI-driven re-indexes). - Sensitive IP: cannot send raw source code to third-party hosted endpoints without VPC/on-prem controls + SAML + enterprise KMS. Technical requirements (customer language): - "Need sub-100ms 95p for retrieval + rerank in interactive dev UI (aim 50-80ms typical)." - Support for frequent incremental re-indexing (nightly full + continuous small deltas). Reindex window must not block queries. - Reranker: cross-encoder rerank for top-50 candidates; can run on smaller GPU pool but must finish <250ms in aggregate. - Cost target: ~$0.002-0.005 per query (retrieval + rerank) at scale. Need cost model for 120M corpus and 300k q/day. - Indexing: want sharded embedding store with fast warmup and ability to snapshot/restore within 30m. - Model policy: prefer open
title: Pelorus EmbedSearch Labs

text: Prism Devtools Collective: builds IDE-integrated copilots and hosted code-assist services. Primary ask: agents can call functions to run tests, lint, and builds in a hermetic sandbox and stream logs back to editor with very low latency. Requirements: 1) sandboxed test runner: ephemeral containers/chroot, no outbound network by default, syscall whitelist, cgroups limits, artifacts signed and returned. Time budget per run 30s-2m. 2) deterministic lint runs that return structured patch suggestions via function-call API; cache results for unchanged commits. 3) build log ingestion from GH Actions/GitLab; retention configurable (30/90/365d), EU-only buckets for some customers. 4) function-calling API must provide ephemeral creds, per-call audit trail, and integration with KMS/HSM for signing. 5) model pinning: pin major.minor model versions and quantized variants; prefer <200ms cold start for pinned variants; editor median token latency <=50ms. 6) routing: route private repos to dedicated pools; automatic fallback to cheaper variants on capacity. Security: SSO/SAML + SCIM, SOC2/ISO27001 references, full audit logs, retention controls. Performance SLOs and burst capacity expectations included. Timeline: 2025-10-05 inbound from OSS meetup (AE assigned). 2025-11-12 kickoff call: "need safe exec of tests inside repo". 2026-01-19 initial security questionnaire completed; asked SAML and residency options. 2026-02-18 POC scoping deep-dive (ff_20260218_prism_poc). 2026-03-05 security review
title: Prism Devtools Collective

text: sales_notes_short: Procurement (enterprise, EU). BYOK + PrivateLink required. Legal/DPA and PKI verification are blockers. POC positive — next: finalize network diagram + KMS workshop. notes: Summary: Enterprise finance customer with strict EU residency and network isolation requirements. Only private VPC deployments accepted — no public managed endpoints. Primary concerns: control-plane exposure, audit log retention, BYOK KMS, and egress control. Key asks / constraints: - Data residency: all customer payloads and model outputs must remain in EU regions; logs with PII must be retained per their policy (7 years) and stored in a customer-managed S3 bucket. - KMS: BYOK required. Customer will provision a CMK in their AWS account and grant Redwood a narrowly scoped IAM role and key policy permissions. HSM-backed key requested for high-risk models. - Audit logging: CloudTrail + application audit events forwarded to customer's SIEM (Splunk). Need sample log schema and example event types. - Network security: networking team prefers AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints) over VPC peering due to centralized routing/NAC controls; also allowed: cross-account VPC peering only with strict route table controls. - Firewall rules & egress controls: customer requires deny-all egress at the subnet level with explicit allowlist for Redwood control-plane IP ranges and Dynamo/S3 endpoints. Management plane limited to HTTPS (443) to specific IPs/hostnames; no SSH. - IP allowlists: customer will provide
title: Saltmarsh EdgeGuard Technologies

text: level with explicit allowlist for Redwood control-plane IP ranges and Dynamo/S3 endpoints. Management plane limited to HTTPS (443) to specific IPs/hostnames; no SSH. - IP allowlists: customer will provide three CIDR blocks for management; Redwood asked for a short-lived test-range for POC. - Observability: metrics and traces must flow over the established PrivateLink; token rotation and trace redaction proofs requested. POC notes: - POC ran in a staging VPC using an Interface Endpoint to Redwood control plane. Basic chat flow validated (p95 latency 80–110ms for 256 token conversations). - KMS proof-of-concept: Redwood demonstrated envelope encryption with a customer CMK; key policy required updating to allow Encrypt/Decrypt from the Redwood role. - Audit log export configured to a test S3 bucket (eu-west-1) and validated ingestion into customer's Splunk instance. Security review status: - VRM initial assessment complete. Waiting on legal for DPA/data residency clause tweaks. Infosec has an outstanding ask for PKI-level verification of Redwood PrivateLink certificates and a network diagram that shows route tables, NACLs, and NAT gateway boundaries. Quotes / customer color: - "We cannot allow any management plane egress over the public internet — every control connection must be via PrivateLink or peered VPCs with explicit routes." — Head of Cloud Networking - "BYOK is non-negotiable for production models that touch cardholder or transaction metadata." — CISO Recommended approach
title: Saltmarsh EdgeGuard Technologies

text: PrivateLink or peered VPCs with explicit routes." — Head of Cloud Networking - "BYOK is non-negotiable for production models that touch cardholder or transaction metadata." — CISO Recommended approach (summary): 1) Use PrivateLink (interface endpoints) for the Redwood control plane to minimize routing changes and centralize security policy. 2) Deploy Redwood data plane components into a dedicated customer VPC subnet(s) with host- and subnet-level deny-all egress; explicitly allow endpoints for S3 (logs), KMS, and Redwood control-plane IPs. 3) BYOK: accept CMK in customer account; provide Terraform snippets and an IAM role template for the required key policy statements. 4) Audit logging: configure CloudTrail + application audit events to a customer-managed S3 bucket with lifecycle/retention rules; provide log schema and a Splunk ingestion example. 5) Provide a PKI bundle and PrivateLink hostname cert chain for infosec; include CRL/OCSP details. Open items / next steps: - Customer to send VPC CIDRs, route table layout, and desired subnet for data plane placement. - AE/SE to deliver final network diagram (target 2026-03-18). - Legal to finalize DPA and data residency wording (customer legal expects redlines by 2026-03-20). - Schedule KMS policy workshop (SE + Customer Infra) week of 2026-03-21. text: notes: Lead background: small marketplace for niche outdoor gear. Heavy catalog + product Q&A. Self-serve signup, fast iteration cycle. Initial ask (paraphrase): 'We like Redwood API — simple. But token costs spiking on chat and embedding lookups. How quickly can we get practical savings?' Technical details from calls: - Primary workloads: customer chat bot (FAQ + product lookup) and embeddings-based search for product catalog (~200k vectors prototype). - Current integration: single-hosted-api key tied to staging; calls done per-session (no client-side prompt compression). - Latency target: 150-300ms for chat first token average; throughput modest (peaks ~20 rps during promos). - Models: prefer smaller high-quality open models for cost-sensitive paths; willing to route to larger models for complex queries. Cost optimization asks (explicit): - Batching: how to batch multi-message sessions + best-practice concurrency settings for hosted_api to reduce per-token overhead. - Caching: recommend prefix/KV caching strategy for product descriptions and static prompts; examples of TTLs and invalidation patterns. - Prompt compression: concrete examples converting verbose prompts into compact templates without losing intent; request code snippets. recent_activity: 2025-11-12: inbound self-serve signup; used free credits to test chat API 2025-11-20: quick dev sync — integrated basic chat flow (server-side) using hosted API keys 2025-11-29: 45m discovery call (recorded) — main ask: reduce

text: notes: Summary: enterprise evaluation for Reserved GPU pools across NA + EU with warm-standby headroom. - Business need: low-latency reranking + per-user personalization for ad auctions; strict cost control. - Traffic profile: bursty bid windows (10–60s), steady background personalization queries, peak concurrency during campaign launches (x10 baseline). - Latency SLOs requested: p50 < 35ms, p95 < 120ms for 512-token generation paths; p95 < 40ms for emb/rerank endpoints. - Throughput goals: sustained 800k tokens/sec global (mixed traffic) with regional peak 300k tokens/sec NA, 200k tokens/sec EU. - GPU preferences: H100-preferred for long-context chat/rerank; supports A100 fallback for cost-sensitive routes. - Routing/fallback: want policy-based routing by tenant region + automatic fallback to quantized variants when latency > SLO or capacity constraints. - Reserve planning ask: dedicated reserved pools in NA + EU, multi-AZ distribution, warm-standby equal to 20% peak for immediate failover, plus 10% cross-region burst allowance. - Failover model: active-active with local primary; passive warm-standby in second region (GPU pre-warmed with model weights, prefix cache priming during low-traffic hours). - Acceptance criteria for POC: 1) sustain 80% of target throughput for 30m without QoS degradation, 2) failover cutover under 45s with <5% error increase, 3) token-cost per 1M under quoted threshold. - Security/compliance: require SOC2 attestation, KMS integration, SSO for engineer
title: Nyx Vector Core

text: notes: Summary: Mid-market product analytics SaaS evaluating Redwood as primary inference provider. Initial appetite for Hosted API to validate fidelity and infra integration; if POC meets latency and unit-cost thresholds they want Dedicated for predictable throughput and data isolation. POC scope: two-week run using hosted_api with traffic routing to a 13B open model for embeddings and a 7B chat model for in-app summarization. Test vectors: support transcripts, product docs + 20k QPS token-limited runs. Primary eval metrics: p95 latency, tokens/sec cost, and embedding recall for search. Cost-optimization workshop goals (Mar18): - Walkthrough batching strategies: request-level batching vs continuous batching; expected latency impact and throughput gains. - KV/prefix caching review: cache hit-rate targets (aim 30-45% for support flows) and eviction policy suggestions. - Quantization profiles: test int8 vs int4 on specific 7B/13B variants; tradeoffs on quality vs unit cost. - Unit economics modeling: build tokens-per-session, avg tokens/respond, hit-rate sensitivity; show Dedicated committed vs hosted on-demand break-even for their projected volume. Key quotes/asks: "We need to know whether switching to Dedicated will cut our per-message cost by at least 40% while keeping p95 <200ms" — Head of Product. Security notes: Require SSO/SAML + KMS integration; retention controls for support transcripts (90 days default, longer for enterprise customers). Data residency request for EU customers

text: notes: Summary: High-potential logistics customer evaluating Redwood Dedicated/Private for low-latency routing and high-burst chat support. Timeline snapshots: - 2024-06-18: Intro call (AE Maya) - high-level product fit, want to replace slow internal API. - 2024-07-10: Scoping workshop with ML infra + ops (SE Elliot) - workload: spikes during morning dispatch windows, peaks ~20k concurrent QPS across routing microservices. - 2024-08-19: POC kickoff (fireflies ff-2024-08-19-9832) - 2 x Dedicated node baseline, synthetic burst generator. - 2024-09-05: Mid-POC sync - observed tail latency and growing request queueing under 20% higher burst than baseline; Redwood team recommended larger burst pool + autoscale policy tweak. - 2024-09-18: Security review submitted (drive doc) - asking for KMS integration + residency confirmation; legal reviewing. - 2024-10-02: Emergency incident during simulated BlackFriday workload; queue depth exceeded SLO, fallbacks not exercised as planned (fireflies ff-2024-10-02-1145). - 2024-10-15: Customer paused procurement while evaluating in-house rewrite and cloud-managed endpoints; internal Head of ML left company. - 2024-11-20: Competitor (in-house) demo accepted by ops; budget freeze communicated by CFO pending exec review. text: notes: Topline: Apex Lane is an ad-tech DSP evaluating Redwood Dedicated for real-time model inference (reranking + bidding decisions). They need guaranteed sustained throughput with predictable burst behavior for major campaigns. Throughput/Capacity asks (from AE/SE convo): - Baseline guaranteed tokens/sec: 25,000 tokens/sec sustained across EU+NA (business asks baseline SLA). - Burst policy: allow up to 100,000 tokens/sec for 10 minutes per campaign window (x3 per day) with explicit warm-up plan (pre-warm KV cache). - Concurrency caps: prefer soft concurrency cap at 800 concurrent calls, hard cap 1200 (policy for throttling). - Reserved GPU plan: initial commit 8x A100/H100-class GPUs (multi-node), ability to burst to 12 GPUs during campaign windows; want reserved slots for model variants (quantized vs fp16). - Latency SLOs: 95th percentile request latency < 200ms for short requests (<512 tokens); 99th < 500ms with fallback rules. - Fallback routing: must support automatic fallback to smaller variant (e.g., 2x quantized model) with <50ms added latency and explicit routing policy. - Billing: committed monthly baseline + overage per-token beyond baseline; legal wants an overage cap and clear credit formula for SLA misses. Security/Compliance: SOC2 required, SSO/SAML, KMS integration for model keys, audit logs for inference calls. EU data residency for cached prefixes is a hard ask (some clients are EU-only). POC plan agreed: 2-week synthetic replay + 1-week live traffic ramp.

text: notes: - Lead came from our marketing webinar (content ops for e-commerce). - Primary workload: generate 8-25k short-to-mid length landing pages + thousands of social posts/month. text: notes_brief: Evaluating hosted -> dedicated. Cost-optimization workshop on 2025-02-15 to prove unit economics (batching, caching, quant profiles). Legal DPA + budget are main blockers. notes: CRM notes (shorthand, from AE/SE): - Company: mid-market CX platform, 450 employees, heavy chat + search use. - Primary ask: move quickly from hosted api to dedicated for predictability once perf locked. want a clear cost model. - Workload: chat agents for customer support (p95 latency target ~ <200ms), batch embedding jobs for nightly search index, reranking for CSAT follow-ups. - Throughput target: steady-state 60-100 reqs/s for chat at peak; embeddings batch ~25k docs/night. - Cost sensitivity: high — procurement pushed for < $X per 1M tokens (internal goal: reduce token unit cost by 40% vs current managed provider). - Model preferences: prefer open/Redwood-verified models (13B quantized variants), fallback to 7B for spikes. Interested in quantization profiles (INT8 for cheap inference, FP16 for quality-sensitive flows). - Routing: want policy-based routing (latency-first for live chat, cost-first for background jobs). request ability to pin versions and automatic fallback. - Security: must have SSO/SAML, SOC2 evidence, KMS integration, audit logs, and EU data residency for EMEA customers. VPC required for dedicated deployment. - Recent asks: sample cost model spreadsheets, sample quantization/latency benchmarks for 13B int8, example batching configs (max_batch_size vs latency tradeoffs).

Baseline sizing assumptions (workshop pre-read): - Average chat request ~140 tokens => steady-state token/sec ~= 18 rps * 140 = 2520 tok/s. Peak sustained ~= 110 * 140 = 15400 tok/s. Burst peak brief ~= 220 * 140 = 30800 tok/s. - Reranker steady tokens/sec (during window) ~= 3000 * 60 tokens / 900s = ~200 tok/s sustained across the window; but concurrency requires handling 3k parallel lightweight inferences. - Reserve planning: recommend a mix of reserved H100-equivalent slots for chat (low-latency) + dedicated A100 pool for throughput-heavy reranking/embeddings. - Suggested redundancy: N+1 across two AZs to meet 99.95% target and permit rolling upgrades. text: notes: Summary: Avalon is evaluating Redwood Dedicated for distributed inference across customer-facing chat and high-throughput reranking pipelines. Primary objective is predictable tail latency under reserved GPU capacity and clear, enforceable SLOs in the contract. They care about measurement alignment (client vs server timing), streaming vs non-streaming semantics, and how p50/p95/p99 are computed in practice. Key requirements discussed (shorthand): - Workloads: agent chat (avg payload 80-120 tokens), reranking (batch queries, up to 500 qps), personalization scoring (low-latency inference). - Targets proposed by Avalon: non-streaming generation p50 < 150ms, p95 < 300ms, p99 < 700ms for single-response short outputs; streaming initial-token p50 < 120ms, subsequent-token p95 < 80ms (they want smooth tail for streaming). - Throughput: baseline 2 x A100-equivalent reserved GPU nodes for 200 qps sustained; burst to 500 qps for 2 hours/day. Must support autoscale within reserved burst policy. - Measurement alignment concerns: client-side timing (end-to-end) includes: client serialization + network RTT + ingress queuing + server decode + model-inference + streaming chunking + client assembly. Avalon insists contract SLOs be measured at two surfaces: (1) server-side egress time (server_sent_first_byte to server_sent_last_byte) for internal enforcement and (2) client-observed first-byte and completion times for external SLA and penalties. They want mapping rules between them. Proposed
title: Avalon Latency Consulting

text: (server_sent_first_byte to server_sent_last_byte) for internal enforcement and (2) client-observed first-byte and completion times for external SLA and penalties. They want mapping rules between them. Proposed measurement spec (agreed for POC): - Define timing events: client_send_ts, server_ingest_ts, server_first_byte_ts, server_last_byte_ts, client_receive_first_byte_ts, client_receive_complete_ts. All timestamps must be in UTC and use monotonic clocks. - Metrics to publish: * server_processing_latency_ms: server_last_byte_ts - server_ingest_ts (server internal). * server_time_to_first_byte_ms: server_first_byte_ts - server_ingest_ts (important for streaming warm-up). * client_e2e_latency_first_byte_ms: client_receive_first_byte_ts - client_send_ts (external SLA for initial response). * client_e2e_latency_complete_ms: client_receive_complete_ts - client_send_ts (external SLA for full response). * token_emit_interval_ms distribution for streaming (p50/p95/p99). - SLO reporting windows: 5m rolling and 30d trailing for contractual review. P95 used for operational dashboards; P99 used as contractual tail for incident classification and credits. P50 used for performance baselining only. - Sampling and attribution: require 100% capture of latencies for sampled endpoints during POC; production may use 10% sampling with bias-free hash (request id) for long-term telemetry. Streaming vs non-streaming semantics discussion notes (bulleted): - Streaming initial latency is critical for UX;

text: production may use 10% sampling with bias-free hash (request id) for long-term telemetry. Streaming vs non-streaming semantics discussion notes (bulleted): - Streaming initial latency is critical for UX; Avalon will treat first-byte e2e as the primary SLA for chat features and full-completion for deterministic batch jobs. - For streaming, server_time_to_first_byte must exclude queueing that occurred before model assignment if requests are queued due to concurrency limits; Redwood to provide queue length and wait time metrics to correlate. - Chunked streaming: measure token emit interval; if interval spikes beyond configured threshold (e.g., >500ms) more than 1% of tokens, this triggers incident in Avalon ops. SLA language requests (for negotiations): - Define SLOs both in server-internal terms (what Redwood guarantees per reserved node) and customer-observed terms (what Avalon will include in SLAs with their clients). Redwood to provide translation metrics and a clear credit formula tying server-side breaches to external credits (accounting for customer network). - Latency credits: only apply when internal server_processing_latency_ms p99 > contractual threshold AND packet traces show <100ms symmetric network RTT for the sample; otherwise, network issues carve-out. POC plan (next steps): 1) Redwood to deliver test harness + sample client (node+python) that stamps timing events and uploads traces to shared drive. 2) Run 48h synthetic replay (mix of chat and reranking) during business
title: Avalon Latency Consulting

text: notes: Topline: Product analytics SaaS, mid-market (200-400 eng + product), evaluating Redwood for search & support workflows. Started on Hosted API POC (chat + embeddings) in Jan; results promising on latency and cost per query. They want to move to Dedicated/VPC for data isolation and to meet compliance. POC summary: hosted api POC ran for 3 weeks (Jan 15 - Feb 5). Used redwood-hosted model rtx-compatible for embeddings + chat routing. Observations: - Avg chat p95 latency 280ms (acceptable), embedding throughput ~600 qps with batching enabled. - Some hallucination on long product docs, team using reranking+structured prompts to improve. - Cost envelope under estimates on hosted during POC; expecting higher base cost under Dedicated but want predictable committed throughput. Security & procurement notes: - Internal security asked for SOC2 Type II report (recent), SSO/SAML integration guide, audit log retention options, and KMS integration pattern for Dedicated (customer-managed keys). - "We can't use hosted for sensitive customer data; need VPC with private endpoints" — security director (A. Whitman). - Data residency: US only for now; asked about multi-region fallbacks. Eng/Infra concerns: - Network topology: want dedicated subnet + strict egress rules; ask: can Redwood Dedicated be deployed into customer VPC or peered with AWS Transit Gateway? - KMS: need option for BYOK + rotation policy. Commercial: - Requested Dedicated pricing worksheet (committed nodes vs burst) 2025-02-14.

text: notes: Account summary: mid-market B2B SaaS (product analytics dashboards) looking to move from Hosted API trial -> Dedicated for production helpdesk/chat + document search. Highly product-minded team; CTO (quote): "We need predictable latency and unit economics — if we can hit ~150ms p95 and stable costs we buy." Key ask: POC must validate canary + fallback routing across model variants and region failover. They want explicit rollback steps and monitoring hooks (per-model latency and token cost). Technical details / preferences: - Primary workload: real-time chat (support agent) with embeddings-backed doc search and occasional reranking for top results. - Latency target: p95 <= 200ms for chat in NA region; throughput burst ~200 qps during peak. - Cost sensitivity: mid-market budget, expects Optimize recommendations (quantization + batching) to reduce token spend ~30% without quality regression. - Model preference: open models with possible Redwood-curated variants; willing to test 8-bit/quantized variants if fidelity checks pass. - Routing plan desired: canary to model-variant A (high quality) on 5% traffic, fallback to cheaper variant B on overload or region outage, automatic region failover NA->EU for continuity, and manual rollback path. Operational / security notes: - Need SSO/SAML for users, audit logging retention 1 year, and integration with customer KMS for key wrapping. - Data residency: primary NA, legal asks for contract language on ephemeral data retention. text: Summary: Mid-market B2B SaaS (product analytics platform) evaluating Redwood Hosted API first; planned migration to Dedicated if performance/cost targets met. Trial completed 2-week hosted proof-of-concept — now sizing for dedicated. Key contacts: - VP Product: Lila Moreno (decision maker for product & roadmap) - Head of Platform: Arjun Rao (technical lead, cares about latency/throughput) - Procurement: Mark Chen (contract/commit negotiations) Timeline / recent activity: - 2026-02-10: Intro call (AE Sofia) — high-level use cases, asked for latency SLOs and pricing bands. - 2026-02-11: Deep dive demo + security checklist (SE Ethan) — provided SOC2 docs. (fireflies ff-20260211-0192) - 2026-02-12: Hosted API trial kickoff — 2-week trial, integration with staging using SDKs. - 2026-02-25: Mid-trial checkpoint — reviewed logs, saw higher than expected long-prompt usage (ff-20260225-0341). - 2026-03-03: Trial wrap sync — product team asks for Dedicated capacity estimate for production rollout. - 2026-03-04: Pricing call w/ finance & procurement; asked for committed vs burst pricing options. Hosted trial observed metrics (representative): - Duration: 2 weeks of traffic from staging + small beta cohort. - Average QPS (all endpoints): ~12 req/s (avg) with spikes to 52 req/s during load tests. - Average concurrency (in-flight requests): 6 typical, peaks 28. - Token mix: 68% short prompts (avg 70 tokens), 22% mid-length (avg 450 tokens), 10% long context (avg 1,600 tokens) used for multi-doc
title: Pegasus Bridge Systems

text: - Average concurrency (in-flight requests): 6 typical, peaks 28. - Token mix: 68% short prompts (avg 70 tokens), 22% mid-length (avg 450 tokens), 10% long context (avg 1,600 tokens) used for multi-doc summarization. - Embeddings usage: 18% of calls, mostly small-batch (up to 256 vectors) for semantic search. - Latency targets: p95 <= 350ms for short prompts; p95 <= 1.2s for mid; tolerates up to 3s for long-context ops. - Cost sensitivity: mid-high — product KPIs depend on predictable per-token cost; procurement requires committed discount at ~6-12 month ARR thresholds. Dedicated sizing ask / initial recommendation (SE notes): - Expected baseline steady-state: 15 QPS (mix weighted to text gen + embeddings). - Target peak planning: design for sustained 60 QPS for 10m windows (headroom for 95th percentile bursts). - Concurrency target: reserve for 40 concurrent gen requests to keep latency SLAs. - Token throughput estimate (monthly): ~600M tokens/month (based on projected 150k users x 50 tokens/session avg and embeddings usage). - Hardware guidance: 3x H100-class nodes with autoscaling + 1 dedicated embeddings node (multi-GPU) OR 5x A100-80GB depending on quantization and model choice. - Model strategy: prefer an open Llama-family v2-13B quantized for cost-sensitive routes; fallback to a higher-quality 34B variant for long-context summarization. - Routing policy: latency-first for support-agent endpoints (hosted -> local Dedicated primary), cost-first for low-priority batch summarization
title: Pegasus Bridge Systems

text: notes: -- Context: Peregrine builds near-real-time route optimization for enterprise shippers. Primary pain: unpredictable inference cost and tail latency causing missed SLAs with customers. -- They are evaluating Redwood Dedicated to secure reserved GPU capacity + strict 99.9% p95 latency SLOs for their chat+reranking flows. -- Quote from VP Eng: "We need guaranteed throughput during peak windows (06:00-10:00 local) and predictable burst behavior for seasonal spikes." -- Interest areas: warm-pool strategies, scale-up latency (cold start to steady-state), controller queueing behavior under burst, and pre-warming for large KV-cache models. -- Current infra: hybrid clusters (GCP + on-prem for sensitive PII), prefer VPC peering and KMS-managed keys for logs. SOC2 in progress; security wants audit log retention >= 1 year. -- Performance targets discussed: sustained 2000 tokens/sec p50 across 8 concurrent streams, p95 < 120ms for chat generation for 512-token context, peak throughput headroom for 2.5x traffic spike. -- POC plan: 4-week dedicated eval on a reserved pool (8xA100 equiv) with synthetic replay and two customer traffic profiles; run three autoscaling policies (reactive CPU/GPU utilization, request-queue-length, and predictive schedule-based warm pools). -- SE notes: look at batching heuristics + KV cache sharing to reduce per-token GPU-ms; plan to test partial-quantized variants for cost tradeoffs. -- SLA negotiation items: formalize credit for missed p95 windows, define

text: notes: - Inbound from a mid-sized support automation vendor building a lightweight AI layer on top of Zendesk. - Primary ask: Hosted API (self-serve) for rapid proof-of-value; likely to onboard via sandbox keys and upgrade if latency/cost OK. - Workload: high-volume ticket summarization + auto-fill (subject, category, triage tags) and agent-assist chat snippets. Emphasis on structured JSON output to push back into Zendesk fields via webhooks. - Tool calling: want ability to call internal triage microservice (ticket-enrich) to attach metadata (customer_tier, recent_purchases). Demoed Redwood tool-calling flow; customer liked the idea of deterministic function outputs. - Latency / throughput targets: p95 <= 200ms for short summaries (<=200 tokens), bursty spikes up to ~60 requests/sec during morning ticket surge. - Cost sensitivity: high — SMB budget. Asking for guidance on batching, KV caching, and token-level cost optimizations. - Model preference: prefer smaller open models or Redwood-curated lightweight variants; want fallbacks for cheaper routing during non-business hours. - Security: must support SSO (SAML) for console, audit logs for API usage, and 30-day retention controls for transcripts. No current need for on-prem or VPC; public cloud acceptable. - Quote from CTO on call: "We need consistent JSON outputs we can rely on to auto-populate Zendesk macros — if parsing fails we create more work than value." - Integration notes: will likely wire into existing Zendesk workflows;

text: description: Issue summary: Customer deployed two optimizer changes in prod earlier today: a new quantization profile (profile id qp-2026-03-13-v3) and enabled kv_prefetch=true at the serving-group level. Within ~30 minutes they observed a sharp increase in P95 token latency from ~120ms to ~620ms and intermittent request timeouts for long streaming requests. The issue impacts multiple routes and is causing user-facing latency spikes for LexiHealth's triage assistant flows. Impact: - Increased P95 token latency across affected model variants. - Streaming sessions hitting websocket disconnects after 10–20s in ~5% of requests. - Business impact: slowdown of patient triage flows; customer marked as Priority-1. Environment: - Redwood Dedicated pool: rwd-dedicated-lexi-01 (prod us-east) - Serving groups: sg-lexi-triage-a, sg-lexi-triage-b - Model variants: redwood-open-7b-quant-8bit (pinned), gptx-13b-qat (fallback) Recent changes (per customer): 1) Applied quant profile: qp-2026-03-13-v3 via Console rollout at 2026-03-13T13:22:00Z. 2) Enabled kv_prefetch=true on both serving groups at 2026-03-13T13:47:00Z. Steps to reproduce (customer): 1) Send long-context streaming prompt (~1.5k tokens) to route /v1/generate/triage with concurrency 8. 2) Observe slow first-token times in traces and increased P95 across the Service Route dashboard. Observed behavior: - P50 stable, P95 and P99 jumped significantly after changes. - Kernel-level slowdowns seen in runtime metrics: token processing time
title: High P95 tail latency after enabling new quantization profile + KV prefetch — request assisted safe-toggle checklist

text: increased P95 across the Service Route dashboard. Observed behavior: - P50 stable, P95 and P99 jumped significantly after changes. - Kernel-level slowdowns seen in runtime metrics: token processing time per-step increased. - KV prefetch metrics show spike in disk IO wait and prefetch read latencies. Expected behavior: - No material change in P95 after applying quant/kv adjustments; throughput should be preserved or improved. Attachments: - Snippets of service traces and perf histograms provided by customer (attached in ticket). - Sample logs: "kernel: op_conv_slow_path: avg 3.2ms -> 8.9ms", "kv_prefetch: read_latency_ms p95 145 -> 980". Immediate mitigation attempted by customer: - Rolled forward traffic to fallback variant (gptx-13b-qat) with partial success but still high P95 on that variant. - Reduced concurrency on client side to 4 which reduced error rate but not P95. Customer request: - Assistance with a safe sequence to toggle/undo the optimizer changes with minimal customer downtime and guidance on verification before and after each step. investigation: Summary of findings: 1) Timeline correlation: quant profile rollout preceded KV prefetch enable. Both changes overlap with latency spike. 2) Kernel compatibility: qp-2026-03-13-v3 appears to select an experimental GEMM kernel for certain sequence lengths. That kernel has higher per-token latency at 2048+ context in this runtime build. 3) KV prefetch interaction: kv_prefetch=true causes aggressive upstream block prefetch
title: High P95 tail latency after enabling new quantization profile + KV prefetch — request assisted safe-toggle checklist

text: kernel for certain sequence lengths. That kernel has higher per-token latency at 2048+ context in this runtime build. 3) KV prefetch interaction: kv_prefetch=true causes aggressive upstream block prefetch which increases IO contention on node-local SSDs for this workload pattern (many long contexts, high KV hit rate). 4) Autoscaling and cgroup traces show GPU utilization remained below capacity; CPU and IO were saturated during spikes. Hypothesis: The quant profile selection resulted in higher per-step CPU overhead for kernel marshalling on long sequences; combined with aggressive KV prefetch this pushed the host IO/CPU path into queuing, increasing token P95 and causing stream disconnects. Evidence: correlated timestamps across Console rollout audit, runtime logs, and node metrics (attached links). Noted CPU/io pressure on nodes. Asked SRE to check node-local SSD health and IO queue depth on rwd-dedicated-lexi-01 cluster.

Expect drop within 1–5 minutes. - If canary OK, apply to sg-lexi-triage-a. 2) Revert quant profile via model registry rollback (do not restart pods yet): - Console: Models -> redwood-open-7b-quant-8bit -> Rollback profile -> select qp-2026-03-09-v2. - Or CLI: redwoodctl model set-profile redwood-open-7b-quant-8bit --profile qp-2026-03-09-v2 --force --project lexihealth - Verify: runtime logs show model profile change acknowledged; samples route to previous kernel selection. 3) Rolling restart of serving group instances with golden check: - Restart one instance at a time using redwoodctl serving-group restart-instance --id <instance-id>. text: description: Customers on Dedicated pools are seeing intermittent 504s and large p99 generation latencies for repeated prompts that should hit the prefix/KV cache. Symptoms started ~2026-03-09 after a runtime kernel selection change. Initial hypothesis: certain prefix keys are being misclassified as stale and routed to cold model path, causing heavy synchronous KV lookups and request timeouts. impact: LexaHealth experiences elevated user-visible latency and sporadic failed responses for repeated-chat workflows. Affects a subset of traffic on their dedicated pool (estimated 5-8% of requests) and results in degraded experience for end-users; potential revenue impact for high-volume features. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use LexaHealth dedicated API key against us-east endpoint in prod 2) Send a chat sequence with a stable 120-token prefix (same prompt repeated in subsequent requests) 3) Observe initial successful warm responses, then intermittent 504s or p99 spikes on subsequent identical-prompts 4) Reproduce reliably by replaying captured traffic from 2026-03-09 14:00-16:00 UTC (recorded session ID in logs) 5) Capture request/response traces from tracing ID header x-rdwd-trace for affected requests logs: 2026-03-09T14:12:03Z request_id=02c9... status=504 backend_error=kv_lookup_timeout shard=kv-shard-7 2026-03-09T14:12:03Z trace_id=tr-9f21: prefix_key=msg-prefix-0a3c lookup_path=cold_lookup duration_ms=3204 2026-03-09T14:12:03Z serving-runtime: kernel_selector switched to int8-path
title: Prefix lookup fallback to cold path causing repeat-prompt 504s and p99 latency spikes

text: shard=kv-shard-7 2026-03-09T14:12:03Z trace_id=tr-9f21: prefix_key=msg-prefix-0a3c lookup_path=cold_lookup duration_ms=3204 2026-03-09T14:12:03Z serving-runtime: kernel_selector switched to int8-path for seq_len=120 during request 2026-03-09T14:12:03Z cache: prefix_entry=missing -> enqueued cold fetch -> concurrent fetches=12 investigation_notes: 2026-03-10 09:15 - Support (Aisha): Confirmed LexaHealth dedicated pool shows p99 jumps correlated to prefix keys recorded in their replay logs. 2026-03-10 10:02 - SRE (Derek): Observed pattern where prefix entries are reported as expired by cache coordinator while downstream KV still serving shards; spikes follow an increase in concurrent warm starts after a kernel-selection deploy. 2026-03-10 12:30 - Eng (Mariana): Reproduced locally by toggling kernel selector flag; when kernel path changes, prefix cache TTL validation yields false-negative and enqueues cold fetches from persistent KV which are serialized, causing head-of-line blocking. Hypothesis: Kernel selector change (opt-in int8 path) altered the cache key normalization (sequence-length bucket) leading to mismatch between in-memory prefix index and persisted KV keys. Result: cache coordinator marks prefix as stale, triggering concurrent cold fetches and long synchronous waits -> 504s. Telemetry: Concurrent cold fetches per key observed up to 30; KV read p99 increased from 45ms to 2.9s for affected keys during incidents. Error budget breach risk for customer SLA. workaround: 1)
title: Prefix lookup fallback to cold path causing repeat-prompt 504s and p99 latency spikes

text: 504s. Telemetry: Concurrent cold fetches per key observed up to 30; KV read p99 increased from 45ms to 2.9s for affected keys during incidents. Error budget breach risk for customer SLA. workaround: 1) Roll back kernel selector change for LexaHealth dedicated pool (actioned 2026-03-10 15:00 UTC) to restore key normalization parity. 2) Temporarily increase cache coordinator dedup window to 10s to prevent multiple simultaneous cold fetches while full fix is developed. 3) Advise customer to throttle bursty automated replay workflows until we confirm stable behavior (applied rate-limit on route for 6 hours). resolution_plan: Short-term (P0): Keep rollback in place and monitor p99 for 24h; verify no further 504s for repeated prompts. Medium-term: Patch cache coordinator to use explicit key normalization that includes kernel-path metadata so kernel-selector changes do not flip cache identity. Long-term: Add unit and e2e tests to validate cache key stability across kernel selector and quantization flags; add canary gating for kernel selector feature toggles. Owners: Derek (SRE) -> rollback & monitoring; Mariana (Serving Eng) -> patch; QE -> test coverage; ETA for patch: 2026-03-18 resolution: Rollback applied 2026-03-10 and temporary dedup window increased. Post-rollback monitoring shows p99 for affected routes returned to baseline within 3 hours. Patch in review: github.com/redwood/inference-runtime/pull/2798 addresses key normalization and dedup behavior. follow_up_actions: 1) Run

text: description: Issue summary: Transient contention on the prefetch/kv-cache lock in the continuous batcher is causing periodic p99 latency spikes (and occasional request timeouts) for short streaming connections. This manifests as ~1–2 minute windows where many short requests (single-turn streaming with small prompt length) see p95/p99 climb from baseline ~80ms/220ms to 700ms–3s and some 504/timeout errors. Impact: - Customer AppScribe (dedicated) experiencing user-visible slowdowns in transcription preview and occasional failed streaming responses. - Roughly 2–4% of requests during affected windows hit >2s latency, causing user retries. Environment: - prod-us-east, Dedicated pool charmed-redwood-03 - Affected model variants: redwood-optimized-7b (default), fallback llama2-13b-quant Steps to reproduce (observed pattern): 1) Idle period of service for ~5–20 minutes (low QPS). 2) Traffic resumes with many short streaming requests (avg prompt tokens < 32). 3) P99 climbs in waves for 60–120s, then decays to baseline. 4) Repeatable when we replay a short-request trace at ~120–200 rps for 30s after an idle window. Observed behavior / evidence: - Metric correlation: p99 spikes align with elevated "prefetch-lock-wait-ms" histogram on batcher host nodes. - Logs (samples): [2026-03-01T14:12:07.342Z] request_id=aa3f7 prefetch_wait_ms=512 batch_flush_id=9882 stream_conn=stream-71 status=queued [2026-03-01T14:12:07.856Z] request_id=aa3f7 kv_prefill_status=delayed flush_duration_ms=740 status=flushed
title: Transient prefetch lock contention causing periodic p99 latency spikes for short streaming requests

text: request_id=aa3f7 prefetch_wait_ms=512 batch_flush_id=9882 stream_conn=stream-71 status=queued [2026-03-01T14:12:07.856Z] request_id=aa3f7 kv_prefill_status=delayed flush_duration_ms=740 status=flushed [2026-03-01T14:12:08.013Z] request_id=bb9c1 error=504 deadline_exceeded - Sampling of stack traces during spike shows contention on PrefetchManager::acquire_prefill_lock and subsequent wait on condition variable. - We see prefetched KV misses more often during spikes (cache_warm_ratio drops to ~30% from baseline ~85%). Hypothesis: - When the continuous batcher resumes after idle, a small subset of requests trigger prefetch routines concurrently. A separated codepath uses a coarse-grained prefill lock that serializes kv-cache prefill across in-flight streams. Under certain timing, this serial prefill blocks the batcher flush path, increasing end-to-end latency and creating a positive feedback loop as delayed flushes queue more short requests. Temporary workaround provided to customer: - Reduce per-connection streaming concurrency (client-side) and enable a short keepalive/ping to avoid long idle windows (>5m) while we investigate. Applied on 2026-03-01 16:40 UTC. This reduced spike amplitude but did not fully eliminate windows. Next steps planned: 1) Instrumentation: add per-request prefill lock wait metric and trace tag (short-term PR). 2) Code change: refactor PrefetchManager to use sharded locks or optimistic prefill to avoid global serialization. Target PR: runtime/prefetch-shard
title: Transient prefetch lock contention causing periodic p99 latency spikes for short streaming requests

This changes prefetch to use 16-way sharded locks and adds metrics. Making canary plan. 2026-03-03 11:11 - Maya Patel (Support): Customer confirmed workaround lowered error rate; scheduled replay for 2026-03-04 02:00 UTC per low-traffic window. investigation_notes: Replayed production
title: Transient prefetch lock contention causing periodic p99 latency spikes for short streaming requests

text: plan. 2026-03-03 11:11 - Maya Patel (Support): Customer confirmed workaround lowered error rate; scheduled replay for 2026-03-04 02:00 UTC per low-traffic window. investigation_notes: Replayed production short-request trace at various QPS; contention appears at >120 rps after idle windows. text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports a large jump in end-to-end latency and intermittent request timeouts after enabling function/tool-calling with schema validation turned on. Problem began at ~2026-03-10T09:02:00Z and affects a subset of production traffic on dedicated capacity in us-west-2. Impact: - ~15% of CodexSoft production requests that use function/tool-calling now hit 5xx/timeouts or experience p95 latency increase from ~120ms to ~1.2s. - User-facing timeouts cause failed workflows and support escalations. Environment: - Redwood Dedicated pool: codex-dedicated-usw2 - Model variants: redwood-7b-instruct (default), stable-compat-13b (fallback) - Feature flags: function_calling=enabled, schema_validation=sync (customer default) Steps to reproduce (customer provided): 1) POST /v1/chat with function_calling enabled and schema validation included in request body. 2) Use prompt that triggers 2-3 function calls (e.g., calendar.lookup -> contact.resolve -> confirm). 3) Observe E2E latency in client and server traces; many requests exceed client timeout (10s) or return 502/504. Observed behavior: - p50 is mildly elevated, but p95/p99 spikes dramatically. - Traces show repeated short RPCs between serving runtime and schema-validator service for each function call; each validation leg adds ~120-300ms. - When multiple function calls happen in a single session there appear to be additional serial round trips rather than parallelized validation. Expected behavior: - Schema
title: Significant end-to-end latency & request timeouts when function/tool-calling enabled — schema validation extra round-trips

text: validation leg adds ~120-300ms. - When multiple function calls happen in a single session there appear to be additional serial round trips rather than parallelized validation. Expected behavior: - Schema validation should not add serial blocking round-trips per function call that multiply with number of calls; validation should be performed locally or batched to keep E2E latency within SLOs. Relevant logs / traces (examples): - trace id: tr-7f4a2c45 (user session) — observed three validation RPCs: 180ms, 210ms, 240ms. Total validation time contribution ~630ms. - s3 logs: s3://codex-logs/2026-03-10/trace-tr-7f4a2c45.json - console dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/projects/codex-dedicated-usw2/latency?from=2026-03-10T08:45Z&to=2026-03-10T10:15Z Notes on scope: - Issue reproduces for multiple customers that enable sync schema validation with function calls, but CodexSoft has high-volume chains of function calls which make the impact visible. - No evidence of GPU saturation; CPU and IO metrics for the serving nodes are within expected ranges. comments: 2026-03-10 09:15 — Aisha Khan (Support): Acknowledged report, capturing initial traces and escalated to SRE. ETA for staged rollout: 2026-03-18 (can be accelerated on customer dedicated pool if agreed). 2026-03-12 16:10 — Aisha Khan (Support): Confirmed next steps with customer and opened procurement discussion if customer wants expedited dedicated pool patching. investigation_notes: Metric delta: p95 increased from ~120ms to ~1.2s for affected endpoints between 2026-03-10T08:55 and 2026-03-10T09:05. Tracing shows schema-validator RPCs are synchronous calls from serving runtime; each function call triggers its own validation call rather than batching or local in-process validation. Hypothesis A: Schema-validation service single-call latency ≈ 100-250ms; serial calls across 3 functions accumulate and push requests to timeout thresholds. Hypothesis B: Routing accidentally sends validation RPCs across AZ boundaries for some requests, adding network hop latency — observed in a subset of traces (investigating routing policy). Load testing: Reproduced on staging with 20% function-calling-heavy traffic; enabling sync validation raised p99 by ~900ms. No evidence of model runtime slowdown (GPU/CPU normal); root cause isolated to validation RPC path and function-calling orchestration logic. Related work: runtime PR #2198 proposes batching validation calls per request and adding in-process validation fallback; openshift patch to pin validation service to same AZ to avoid

Related work: runtime PR #2198 proposes batching validation calls per request and adding in-process validation fallback; openshift patch to pin validation service to same AZ to avoid cross-AZ hops. resolution_notes: Immediate workaround provided to CodexSoft: set schema_validation=async or disable schema validation for high-throughput endpoints; increase client timeout to 30s while patching. Engineering change in progress: PR #2198 implements batched validation and optional in-process validation path (reduction of ~500-800ms p95 in tests). Plan: staged rollout to dedicated pools starting 2026-03-18, monitor for regression. Follow-ups: INT-4567 for runtime fix, add telemetry to detect cross-AZ validation RPCs and fail fast to local fallback, product decision to expose 'validation_mode' with explicit async/local/remote options. Customer will be offered expedited patching on their dedicated pool for acceptance testing. next_steps: Ship and stage runtime PR #2198 to canary (target date 2026-03-18). SRE to add alert for validation_rpc_latency and cross-az_validation_count (by 2026-03-15). Product to create customer-facing doc outlining validation_mode options and tradeoffs (linked in Confluence). Support to confirm workaround coverage and track customer acceptance testing for expedited patching.

text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent 403 Forbidden errors when Redwood Private serving nodes attempt to download model artifacts via presigned S3 URLs. Failures are sporadic (roughly 1 in 20 attempts) and impact model startup on a subset of serving nodes. Impact: Several model pulls during scale-ups fail leading to increased cold-start latencies and transient 5xx errors observed by customer application. Customer has dedicated Private deployment in us-west-2 and is on a timetable for a feature launch. Environment: Private deploy behind customer-managed egress proxy (corporate forward proxy) that performs TLS session pooling/offloading for outbound HTTPS connections. Serving nodes run on k8s nodes in the customer's VPC with normal egress via the proxy path. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Trigger serving node scale-up to pull artifact: kubectl scale deployment model-worker --replicas=10 2) Observe artifact download attempts in worker logs (artifact fetcher uses presigned S3 URL returned by control plane) 3) Some workers report HTTP 403 on GET to presigned URL; retry succeeds for same URL in many cases 4) Reproduce is more likely under concurrency (20+ parallel pulls) and when proxy TLS session pool is active logs: [2026-03-10T09:12:15Z] artifact-fetcher INFO downloading https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/acme-models/prefix/model.tar.gz?X-Amz-Signature=... [2026-03-10T09:12:15Z] artifact-fetcher ERROR http status=403 content="AccessDenied: SignatureDoesNotMatch" attempt=1
title: Intermittent 403s on presigned S3 artifact downloads caused by TLS session pool sharing at customer egress proxy

text: https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/acme-models/prefix/model.tar.gz?X-Amz-Signature=... [2026-03-10T09:12:15Z] artifact-fetcher ERROR http status=403 content="AccessDenied: SignatureDoesNotMatch" attempt=1 [2026-03-10T09:12:16Z] artifact-fetcher INFO retrying download (attempt=2) [2026-03-10T09:12:16Z] artifact-fetcher INFO download succeeded Proxy tcpdump excerpt (customer supplied): client-src=10.10.5.12:45678 -> proxy:443 TLSv1.2 Handshake (SNI: s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com) client-src=10.10.5.13:56789 -> proxy:443 TLS session reused (no SNI) -> backend connection reused to s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: presigned URL signature mismatch indicates Host header / TLS SNI seen by S3 did not match the host used when signing or when creating the canonical request. Collected packet capture from customer egress proxy and compared TLS server_name indications to the Host header in proxied requests. Findings: - The customer egress proxy performs TLS session pooling and will resume sessions across connections regardless of the requested SNI if the backend certificate is valid. - In pooled/resumed sessions the proxy sometimes forwards requests on a backend connection that was originally established to a different virtual host, causing mismatch between TLS server_name and Host header seen by AWS S3. - AWS S3 validates the presigned URL signature against expected host/canonical request; when SNI/host mismatch occurs S3 responds with SignatureDoesNotMatch
title: Intermittent 403s on presigned S3 artifact downloads caused by TLS session pool sharing at customer egress proxy

text: server_name and Host header seen by AWS S3. - AWS S3 validates the presigned URL signature against expected host/canonical request; when SNI/host mismatch occurs S3 responds with SignatureDoesNotMatch -> 403. - Replays show retries often succeed because a new non-pooled connection is created or the session used happens to match the expected SNI. Reproduction done with cURL through customer's proxy under high concurrency mirrored the behavior (enabled with --verbose and reuse enabled in the proxy). comments: 2026-03-10 13:22 - Maya Singh (Support): Initial customer report received; requested packet capture and proxy config details. Customer provided proxy vendor (BlueWave Proxy) and config snippet showing TLS session reuse = enabled. 2026-03-10 16:08 - Evan Patel (SRE): Confirmed 403s correlate with proxy connection reuse. Attachments: pcap excerpt, worker logs. Asked customer to run a controlled test disabling session reuse for s3 hostnames. 2026-03-11 09:37 - Acme Eng (customer): Disabled session pooling for domains matching '*.s3.amazonaws.com' as a test. Error rate dropped to ~0.1% from ~5% during concurrent pulls. Customer will test for 24 hours. 2026-03-11 18:05 - Evan Patel (SRE): Suggested long-term fixes: (1) configure proxy to preserve SNI and avoid reusing backend TLS sessions across different hostnames; (2) pin S3 egress to a dedicated NAT without proxy; (3) update artifact fetcher to retry with new connection on SignatureDoesNotMatch (short-term). Logged follow-up
title: Intermittent 403s on presigned S3 artifact downloads caused by TLS session pool sharing at customer egress proxy

text: sessions across different hostnames; (2) pin S3 egress to a dedicated NAT without proxy; (3) update artifact fetcher to retry with new connection on SignatureDoesNotMatch (short-term). Logged follow-up task INT-44512 to track artifact-fetcher retry hardening. 2026-03-12 08:12 - Maya Singh (Support): Customer confirmed workaround in place; monitoring continues. Preparing postmortem template and suggested doc for private deploy runbook. resolution_notes: Workaround applied by customer: disabled TLS session pooling for S3 hostname patterns on their corporate proxy and configured a bypass rule for artifact fetch traffic. Immediate impact: 95% reduction in presigned URL 403s during concurrent pulls. Planned next steps: - Customer: keep bypass for S3 for 7 days and report any regressions. - Redwood: add a short-term artifact-fetcher mitigation to force new TLS connections when SignatureDoesNotMatch on presigned URLs (tracked in INT-44512). - Joint: create guidance in private deployment runbook describing proxy configuration recommendations (preserve SNI, avoid cross-host session reuse, or whitelist S3 endpoints). Root cause: customer egress proxy TLS session pool reused backend TLS sessions across different hostnames, leading to mismatched TLS SNI vs HTTP Host for presigned S3 requests which in turn produced SignatureDoesNotMatch / 403 responses from S3. Status: Waiting on customer validation window (24-72h). Once stable we will move ticket to Resolved and record runbook addition.

text: description: Issue summary: - Customer reports streaming chat responses intermittently stop mid-generation. Client keeps the HTTP connection open; server stops emitting SSE tokens and never sends [DONE]. Impact: - Production incident for Arcadia Health’s support-agent. End users see partial completions and stuck spinners. Environment: - Dedicated pool in us-east. - Client uses OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint. Steps to reproduce (customer-provided): 1) Start streaming completion with max_tokens ~800. 2) After ~10–40 seconds, stream stalls mid-sentence. 3) No further bytes, no close, no error. Investigation notes: - Correlates with longer generations + high concurrent streams. - Gateway shows upstream still open; runtime logs show request context cancelled late or not at all. - Suspected backpressure interaction between token streamer + gzip + chunked transfer. Workaround: - Customer can set client-side read timeout and retry with idempotency key. Resolution: - Rolled out gateway change: disable gzip for SSE responses and flush after each token chunk. - Added runtime guard: heartbeat/flush every 2s while streaming. References: - Slack: #support thread_ts 1769084201 - Customer emails in Ava Chen mailbox (thread_id gth_9a3f1b0a) comments: Ava Chen: Customer escalation via email + screenshot of stalled curl. They see it ~1 in 20 requests; worse at peak. Alex Martinez: Seeing similar symptoms in perf-canary when gzip enabled. I can reproduce in staging with 200 concurrent streams.
title: Streaming responses freeze mid-generation for Arcadia Health (SSE stalls, request never completes)

text: screenshot of stalled curl. They see it ~1 in 20 requests; worse at peak. Alex Martinez: Seeing similar symptoms in perf-canary when gzip enabled. I can reproduce in staging with 200 concurrent streams. Dylan Brooks: Gateway fix in progress; will ship a config toggle for SSE gzip off by default. Ava Chen: Confirmed with customer: after change, no stalls observed over 2 hours of load test. Closing with workaround note still recommended on client side.
title: Streaming responses freeze mid-generation for Arcadia Health (SSE stalls, request never completes)

text: description: Customer requests a certified delivery pattern for audit events used in regulatory forensics. They want incremental (delta) batches that are strictly ordered per-tenant with a manifest that proves sequence continuity, gap detection metadata, and TTL provenance for legal hold and retention verification. The deliverable should include a sample delivery to Azure Event Hubs and an S3 fallback with signed manifests (RFC 3161 timestamping or equivalent) and an explanation of how KMS-wrapped signing keys are rotated. impact: Customer's compliance team is unable to complete their inbound SOC or audit because they cannot demonstrate chain-of-custody and ordered delivery guarantees. They have paused a scheduled audit until we supply an attested sample. Business impact: potential delay in contract renewal and elevated legal scrutiny. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer configured tenant-level webhook to our audit export endpoint. 2) Expected strictly ordered event batches with sequence_number per tenant. 3) Observed reordering and occasional missed sequence numbers across two business days. 4) Customer attempted replays and saw duplicate offsets from our S3 fallback manifest. 5) Customer requests a reproducible sample and artifact demonstrating gap detection and reconciliation behavior. sample_payload_request: Customer requests (a) 10-minute sample of delta bundles for tenant 'finovia-prod' in prod.us-east, (b) JSON manifest format with sequence_number, prev_hash, batch_hash, rfc3161_tstamp,
title: Sequenced delta audit delivery with gap-detection, per-tenant manifest, and TTL provenance proof

text: sample_payload_request: Customer requests (a) 10-minute sample of delta bundles for tenant 'finovia-prod' in prod.us-east, (b) JSON manifest format with sequence_number, prev_hash, batch_hash, rfc3161_tstamp, and kms_key_id, (c) a redaction flag for PII fields and sample mapping for the customer's QRadar ingestion (field mappings and overflow policy). Provide the sample both as Azure Event Hubs push and as signed S3 objects. logs: Provided upload from customer (truncated): - webhook_delivery.log: 2025-02-16T09:14:21Z tenant=finovia-prod seq=12534 status=200 latency_ms=48 - webhook_delivery.log: 2025-02-16T09:14:27Z tenant=finovia-prod seq=12536 status=200 latency_ms=62 - s3_fallback.log: 2025-02-16T09:18:04Z tenant=finovia-prod manifest_id=mf-20250216-0918 uploaded=true size=2.1MB Note: customer attached CSV of observed sequence gaps (12535 missing) and Event Hubs consumer offsets for the same window. investigation_notes: - Initial triage: observed intermittent delivery backpressure in the webhook router during the time window; two 429 spikes recorded in the router metrics. - Correlated with autoscaling events: dedicated delivery worker pool scaled from 4->2 briefly due to throttling logic threshold. - Manifest generation job uses best-effort accumulation window; when delivery worker retries out-of-order, manifest prev_hash may not reflect final persisted order. - Identified missing invariant: manifest creation currently assumes idempotent append but does not enforce strict sequence-lock
title: Sequenced delta audit delivery with gap-detection, per-tenant manifest, and TTL provenance proof

text: out-of-order, manifest prev_hash may not reflect final persisted order. - Identified missing invariant: manifest creation currently assumes idempotent append but does not enforce strict sequence-lock during concurrent batch flushes. - Reproduced locally by simulating concurrent batch flush with artificially delayed KVS persistence; observed the same gap pattern and duplicate offset in fallback manifest. proposed_workaround: 1) Temporarily enable per-tenant sequence-lock mode for finovia-prod to force a single writer path (console toggle). 2) Advise customer to hold audit replay until we provide attested sample. 3) Provide manual reconciliation manifest covering the requested window with signed timestamps and explicit gap markers. resolution: (pending) Engineering has a prioritized patch to add a lightweight sequence-lock for manifest flushes and to include explicit gap-detection entries in manifests. SRE will generate the requested attested sample for 2025-02-16 09:10-09:20 window and deliver via secure S3 presigned link plus Event Hubs test push. Will include RFC-3161 style timestamping via our timestamping proxy and a key-rotation note. Target delivery: 2025-02-20. comments: 2025-02-18 11:02 Maria Chen: Opened with customer after they reported missing sequence 12535 and mismatched offsets between Event Hubs and S3 fallback. 2025-02-18 12:18 Ethan Patel: Acknowledged; asked for raw logs and the customer's consumer offsets (received). Escalated to SRE for manifest behavior review.
title: Sequenced delta audit delivery with gap-detection, per-tenant manifest, and TTL provenance proof

text: offsets between Event Hubs and S3 fallback. 2025-02-18 12:18 Ethan Patel: Acknowledged; asked for raw logs and the customer's consumer offsets (received). Escalated to SRE for manifest behavior review. 2025-02-18 15:40 SRE-James: Found two 429 spikes in router during window; reproduced ordering issue via concurrency test harness. Assigned to eng for sequence-lock patch. 2025-02-19 09:05 Customer-Compliance (Finovia): Provided CSV of observed gaps and confirmed audit is on hold pending sample. Asked for signed artifact and confirmation on KMS rotation frequency. 2025-02-20 08:30 Ethan Patel: Uploaded reconciliation manifest (pending signature) to secure workspace and requested timestamping team to apply RFC-style proof. text: description: Issue summary: Around 18:20 UTC we observed a rapid elevation in 5xx responses for requests routed to the us-east serving pool for a single dedicated tenant (LexiSearch). Impact: Customer saw ~60% error rate on chat/streaming requests and ~30% on embedding uploads for 18:20-18:55 UTC. Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated capacity pool pinned to customer. Steps to reproduce: 1) Send a mixed workload from LexiSearch that includes long-chat generation (>2k tokens) and bulk embedding upload concurrently. 2) Observe runtime worker thread crashes and subsequent 502/503 responses from API gateway. Logs & traces: Grafana: https://grafana.redwood.example/dashboard/s/serve/tenant-us-east Lexi thread panic trace: logs-redwood://prod/us-east/runtime/worker-7/2026-03-10T18:22:31Z (see stack frames w/ tokenizer::segmenter::process). Slack thread: #support-lexisearch (link: https://redwood.slack.com/archives/C12345/p164) steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use LexiSearch account to send a simultaneous streaming chat request (long prompt + function calls) and a bulk embedding upload of ~12k items. 2) Observe increased prefetch activity and repeated KV cache invalidations in serving-runtime logs. 3) Monitor API Gateway 5xx rate and runtime worker restarts during the burst. investigation_notes: Memory and GPU driver checks: NVML logs show transient GPU driver reset coincident with worker SIGABRT; GPU memory usage not at peak but post-reset fragmentation observed. Stack traces point to tokenizer::segmenter::process
title: Evening thread panic in runtime causes cascading API 5xx surge for one tenant

text: checks: NVML logs show transient GPU driver reset coincident with worker SIGABRT; GPU memory usage not at peak but post-reset fragmentation observed. Stack traces point to tokenizer::segmenter::process -> tokenize_utf8_sequence -> std::vector resize in patched tokenizer library (new behavior introduced in runtime-hotpatch). Prefetch/KV cache behaviour: Long streaming sessions caused frequent KV writes; combined with bulk embeddings, this created lock contention on kvcache shards causing coroutine queue growth and increased thread concurrency in runtime worker. Amplification path: runtime thread panic leads to worker process crash -> Kubernetes restarts worker -> API gateway keeps retrying connections which temporarily increases connection churn and accept queue backlog -> additional 5xx from api-gateway while runtime pool recovers. Correlation: error spike window matches customer activity (confirmed by customer logs and request-ids). No similar signals for other tenants in the same pool. root_cause: A recent runtime hotpatch updated the tokenizer library with an aggressive internal buffer resize which can trigger a stack corruption when combined with high concurrency on kvcache prefetch locks. The stack corruption produced thread-level panics and worker process SIGABRTs. Worker crash/restart churn amplified load at the API gateway (retries and accept queue saturation), producing the observed 5xx surge for the affected tenant. resolution: 1) Rolled back the runtime hotpatch to
title: Evening thread panic in runtime causes cascading API 5xx surge for one tenant

text: crash/restart churn amplified load at the API gateway (retries and accept queue saturation), producing the observed 5xx surge for the affected tenant. resolution: 1) Rolled back the runtime hotpatch to the previous stable tokenizer build (deployment rollback PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/6789). 2) Applied temporary per-tenant ingress throttle for LexiSearch to prevent immediate re-flood during restarts. 3) Increased api-gateway accept_queue and provisional connection backoff to smooth retry amplification. 4) Performed rolling restarts of affected runtime shards and monitored NVML/GPU health. Result: error rate returned to baseline by 19:22 UTC. comments: 2026-03-10 18:26 - Aisha Nair (Support): Customer reports heavy error rates, provided request IDs and sample payloads. Initial triage: spikes correlate to one tenant's traffic pattern. 2026-03-10 18:34 - Diego Martinez (SRE): Observed repeated SIGABRT from runtime worker process; thread panic originates in tokenizer segmentation code after recent runtime hotpatch.
