text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports that when a client reconnects a websocket mid-generation, the new socket receives only the trailing segment of the response (typically the last 20-40% of tokens) and no assistant.metadata/finalization flags. Impact: Customer's UI shows incomplete transcript and no closing metadata, causing the client to retry and double-submit prompts. Environment: Dedicated deployment (reserved pool) in us-west, customers using long-response generation (~12k tokens) over websockets with client-side reconnect strategy. Observed behavior: After a short network blip and websocket reconnect, the new connection gets a partial tail of the original stream; the original connection's socket (recently closed) appears to receive the earlier tokens in internal logs but those were not forwarded to the new socket. Expected behavior: New websocket should resume or receive the entire message (either resume stream from the last ack offset or receive full finalization metadata). Logs/Artifacts: customer supplied 3 client logs, two wire captures, and server-side request_ids: req-845b and req-845b-replay. Attached: client_logs_2024-10-04.zip, wirecap_req-845b.pcap steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use a websocket client subscribed to streaming chat on dedicated deployment (long prompt that produces ~10k-15k tokens). 2) Begin generation and allow first few hundred tokens to stream to client. 3) Force a short network disconnect (client reconnects within 4-8s with same client-side
title: Streaming reconnect leads to partial transcript: idempotency-mapping race leaves new WS with only trailing tokens

text: prompt that produces ~10k-15k tokens). 2) Begin generation and allow first few hundred tokens to stream to client. 3) Force a short network disconnect (client reconnects within 4-8s with same client-side reconnect token but a fresh websocket connection). 4) Observe that the new websocket receives only the later tokens and no "final" metadata. 5) Reproducible in customer environment 6/10 times under bursty mobile conditions; lab repro requires forced close of transport while runtime idempotency mapping still points at old socket. investigation_notes: Initial triage by Support: validated customer logs, matched server request_id req-845b. SRE pulled internal streaming router logs and found an idempotency mapping table (X-Idempotency-Key -> active_socket_id) that had a short TTL. Timeline reconstruction: original socket (socket-A) closed cleanly at 12:03:21Z, new socket (socket-B) connected at 12:03:23Z with same idempotency key. Due to a race, the router forwarded the next token batches to socket-A's write buffer (still referenced in the map) while socket-B received only the final KV-cache-driven tail. Router metrics show a race window of ~2s where mapping cleanup lags behind socket close. Hypothesis: when socket-A is closed but mapping not yet recycled, runtime continues to write into socket-A's buffer (which is drained by internal cleanup job but not re-associated to socket-B). This results in missing early tokens on socket-B and missing finalization metadata because the finalizer
title: Streaming reconnect leads to partial transcript: idempotency-mapping race leaves new WS with only trailing tokens

text: socket-A's buffer (which is drained by internal cleanup job but not re-associated to socket-B). This results in missing early tokens on socket-B and missing finalization metadata because the finalizer routine saw the stream as already completed on socket-A. We reproduced in staging by artificially delaying socket close propagation and observed identical partial-tail behavior. Additional observation: dedicated pool autoscaler and a routing edge cache produce slight ordering shifts that increase race probability under burst reconnects. SRE also noted a gap in the idempotency table metrics and missing instrumentation for socket handover events. comments: Priya Patel (Support) 2024-10-05: Customer reported intermittent but impactful partial transcripts; supplied logs and timeline. Diego Ramirez (SRE) 2024-10-05: Reproduced in staging by delaying close propagation. Suspect idempotency mapping race; creating short-lived mitigation. Markus Vogel (Runtime) 2024-10-06: Confirmed router writes can target stale socket id if cleanup delayed. Preparing PR to atomically rebind idempotency keys on new connections and to add metric counters for handover misses. Priya Patel (Support) 2024-10-07: Shared temporary customer workaround: randomize reconnect id per reconnect attempt (client sends X-Reconnect-Attempt header) to force new stream instead of handoff. Customer accepted as short-term. Diego Ramirez (SRE) 2024-10-08: Deployed TTL reduction and handover-metrics to staging; metrics look stable.
title: Streaming reconnect leads to partial transcript: idempotency-mapping race leaves new WS with only trailing tokens

text: X-Reconnect-Attempt header) to force new stream instead of handoff. Customer accepted as short-term. Diego Ramirez (SRE) 2024-10-08: Deployed TTL reduction and handover-metrics to staging; metrics look stable. Preparing canary to prod next window. resolution: Workaround: Instructed customer to include a short reconnect nonce header (X-Reconnect-Attempt) for mobile clients to avoid relying on server handover until patch deployed. Short-term platform mitigation: reduced idempotency mapping TTL from 10s to 500ms and added an atomic rebind path for new websocket connections. Long-term fix: merged runtime PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/7421 which adds atomic rebind semantics for idempotency keys, explicit handover metrics, and a unit test covering the close/rebind race. Rollout plan: canary to 10% traffic in us-west on 2024-10-09, full prod by 2024-10-11 if no regressions. Postmortem/next steps: add a Confluence runbook for websocket handover, add alert on handover-miss-rate > 0.1%.
title: Streaming reconnect leads to partial transcript: idempotency-mapping race leaves new WS with only trailing tokens

text: description: Issue summary: FinAssist reported intermittent 403 AuthorizationDenied errors for several users after SSO login when calling admin APIs and the customer console. Impact: Since 2026-02-24 08:30 UTC approximately 12% of auth attempts for FinAssist prod (eu-west) returned 403s for otherwise-valid users. Affects console and admin API flows; regular inference requests authenticated as service accounts were unaffected. Environment: Private deployment (FinAssist Inc.), control plane in eu-west, identity-proxy v1.7.3, auth-service v2.1.0, RBAC tenant-index. Customer IdP: third-party OIDC/SAML gateway with locale-specific transform rules. Observed behavior: The identity-proxy receives the principal header (X-Remote-User / preferred_username / sub) URL-encoded for certain users (e.g., 'alice%40finassist.com' instead of 'alice@finassist.com'). RBAC matching is strict and attempts a direct lookup against the tenant user index; encoded forms do not match canonical entries, resulting in 'rbac: no matching principal' and a 403 response. Steps to reproduce: 1) Configure an IdP to send the subject/preferred_username URL-encoded (simulate locale transform that encodes '@'->'%40'). 2) Perform a standard SSO login and call an admin API or open the console. 3) Inspect headers at the identity-proxy and auth-service. 4) Observe 403 and 'Principal not found' RBAC entries in logs. Logs / examples: - identity-proxy: "Incoming principal: alice%40finassist.com" - identity-proxy: "Principal
title: Locale-encoded principal (%40) in IdP assertion leads to RBAC mismatch and 403s in Private deployment

text: identity-proxy and auth-service. 4) Observe 403 and 'Principal not found' RBAC entries in logs. Logs / examples: - identity-proxy: "Incoming principal: alice%40finassist.com" - identity-proxy: "Principal decode check: no match for tenant user index" - auth-service: "rbac: no matching principal for tenant finassist (principal=alice%40finassist.com) -> 403" Hypothesis: Some IdP configurations (regional/localization transforms or older gateway proxies) URL-encode special characters in the principal claim. identity-proxy does not reliably URL-decode or canonicalize these values before RBAC lookup, and RBAC matching is implemented as an exact match against the canonical (unencoded) username/email stored in the tenant index. This mismatch yields authorization failures for legitimate users. Workarounds applied: Support added a temporary ingress rewrite rule on the customer's edge to URL-decode X-Remote-User for affected tenants. We also created a tenant-level mapping entry in FinAssist's RBAC config to accept the encoded principal form while a fix is prepared. These mitigations restored console/admin access for impacted users. Recommended next steps: - Engineering: implement a safe URL-decode pass in identity-proxy normalization pipeline (idempotent; should tolerate already-decoded values and avoid decoding harmful sequences), add unit+integration tests covering single and double-encoded forms. - SRE: patch and rollout identity-proxy v1.7.4 to private control plane and coordinate with
title: Locale-encoded principal (%40) in IdP assertion leads to RBAC mismatch and 403s in Private deployment

text: and avoid decoding harmful sequences), add unit+integration tests covering single and double-encoded forms. - SRE: patch and rollout identity-proxy v1.7.4 to private control plane and coordinate with FinAssist for a controlled restart of services if required. - Support / Docs: update private-deploy SSO troubleshooting docs and runbook to include instructions for detecting encoded principals and the temporary ingress rewrite pattern. Cross-refs: Slack #support (thread FinAssist-encoding-2026-02-24), related PR and runbook links above. investigation_notes: 2026-02-25 09:10 (Aisha): Customer supplied raw proxy logs. Noticed X-Remote-User shows percent-encoding. 2026-02-25 10:02 (Carlos): Reproduced locally by configuring a test IdP to emit 'bob%40example.com' as preferred_username. identity-proxy forwarded header unchanged. 2026-02-25 13:20 (Eng): RBAC lookup code uses exact string key against tenant_user_index; no decode/normalize step present. Potential safe place to add decode is immediately after header ingestion but before tenant resolution. 2026-02-26 08:05 (SRE): Customer ingress rule added to decode header as stopgap. Verified 3 affected users regained access. 2026-02-27 11:30 (QA): Tested double-encoded input (alice%2540finassist.com -> alice%40finassist.com -> alice@finassist.com) and verified proposed normalization logic must be idempotent and avoid over-decoding arbitrary %-sequences. comments: 2026-02-24 09:05 - FinAssist (support contact): 'Several admins can't access
title: Locale-encoded principal (%40) in IdP assertion leads to RBAC mismatch and 403s in Private deployment

text: alice@finassist.com) and verified proposed normalization logic must be idempotent and avoid over-decoding arbitrary %-sequences. comments: 2026-02-24 09:05 - FinAssist (support contact): 'Several admins can't access console after SSO, getting 403. Works for some users. Please advise.' 2026-02-24 09:22 - Aisha: 'Thanks — collecting headers and logs. Can you confirm IdP platform and any recent config changes? Any ingress proxies in front?' 2026-02-24 10:01 - FinAssist: 'We're using LocaleGate 2.3.1 (regional transformer) + on-prem SAML gateway. We did a change earlier this week.' 2026-02-25 10:10 - Carlos: 'We've reproduced an encoded principal in our lab and confirmed RBAC fails on exact-match lookup. Applying a temporary ingress rewrite recommended.' 2026-02-26 09:00 - FinAssist: 'Ingress rewrite applied and verifies users can access console again — thanks. Please provide permanent fix ETA.' 2026-02-27 15:40 - Eng (Carlos): 'Patch for identity-proxy normalization in review: https://github.com/redwood-inference/identity-proxy/pull/842. Targeting v1.7.4 rollout next week with tests for idempotent decoding.' 2026-02-28 12:30 - Aisha: 'Coordinating schedule with FinAssist for a Friday low-traffic rollout. Updating runbook and marking ticket In Progress.' resolution_notes: Temporary workaround: ingress header URL-decode rule and tenant mapping applied. Permanent fix: identity-proxy patch merged (PR #842) that adds safe URL-decoding + canonicalization step before RBAC lookup; backported
title: Locale-encoded principal (%40) in IdP assertion leads to RBAC mismatch and 403s in Private deployment

text: workaround: ingress header URL-decode rule and tenant mapping applied. Permanent fix: identity-proxy patch merged (PR #842) that adds safe URL-decoding + canonicalization step before RBAC lookup; backported to 1.7.x and released as v1.7.4. Deployed to FinAssist control plane 2026-03-05 and validated. Runbook updated with detection and workaround steps. Follow-up: monitor for similar behavior from other IdP vendors and add an onboarding checklist item to validate principal encoding behavior during Private deployments.

description: Issue summary:
Customer reports a mid-window billing discrepancy where reserved-capacity credits (enterprise committed hours) were not applied for approximately a 40-minute window during a regional failover to hosted capacity. The invoice line for 2026-03-09 includes a hosted-rate charge for tokens that should have been billed at reserved capacity (credit-eligible), resulting in an overage of ~$3,800.

Impact:
- BrightCart's production checkout workflow experienced increased invoice line items for 2026-03-09.
- Customer is requesting provisional credit and root cause; they flagged potential SLA breach for billing accuracy.
- Affects finance reconciliation for enterprise reserved commitments.

Environment:
- Customer org: BrightCart Inc. (enterprise tenant)
- Deployment: Dedicated pool (us-west-2) with fallback policy to hosted when dedicated saturation detected
- Time window: 2026-03-09T18:12:00Z -> 2026-03-09T18:52:00Z (approx)
- Models used: redwood-2.1 for chat, gptq-3.1 for embeddings batch during overlap windows

Steps to reproduce / observed sequence:
1) Dedicated autoscaler reported GPU saturation and triggered canary overflow route at 18:10Z to hosted API.
2) Traffic routed to hosted pool for two subnets; token-level metering reported at hosted-rate.
3) At ~18:50Z traffic returned to dedicated pool.
4) Billing pipeline later attributed token usage to hosted-rate and did not apply reserved-credit bucket for the afflicted window.

Logs /
title: Missing reserved-capacity credits for dedicated->hosted fallback window causing overcharge

text:
hosted-rate.
3) At ~18:50Z traffic returned to dedicated pool.
4) Billing pipeline later attributed token usage to hosted-rate and did not apply reserved-credit bucket for the afflicted window.

Logs / evidence provided by customer:
- Customer provided a CSV of per-minute token counts and request IDs covering 18:00-19:00Z (attached to ticket).
- Invoice PDF line: "Hosted compute - transient usage - 2026-03-09 18:12-18:52" ($3,800).
- Customer timestamps and request_ids: rc-ck-43f7* set.

Investigation notes so far:
- Support ingested customer's CSV and matched request_ids against routing logs in us-west-2. Early match shows requests were routed to hosted endpoints (confirmed by routing logs) due to a transient autoscaler spike.
- Billing pipeline: hosted-metering service produced postback entries for that window. However, reserved-credit reconciliation should detect enterprise committed hours and offset hosted charges for requests that matched the reserved-subnet/tenant mapping. That reconciliation step did not mark the postbacks as credit-eligible.
- Initial trace shows a missing tenant tag in the hosted-metering postbacks between 18:12 and 18:20Z. The tag reappears after 18:20Z — correlates with one of the router agents (router-az2) that had an upgraded image rolled out earlier that day.
- INT-4455 (linked) is an internal maintenance job that rotated routing agent image in us-west-2 at 17:59Z. Hypothesis: router image change temporarily emitted postbacks without
title: Missing reserved-capacity credits for dedicated->hosted fallback window causing overcharge

text:
out earlier that day.
- INT-4455 (linked) is an internal maintenance job that rotated routing agent image in us-west-2 at 17:59Z. Hypothesis: router image change temporarily emitted postbacks without tenant metadata which bypassed reserved-credit matching.

Next steps (in progress):
- SRE to replay postback logs for 18:00-19:00Z and reconstruct untagged entries (assigned to Diego).
- Billing to hold provisional charge and create a provisional credit line pending root cause (Finance notified).
- Engineering to validate router image change that might drop tenant tag and produce follow-up PR to add guarded metadata emission (linked PR provided).

Customer requested outcome:
- Provisional credit to remove the $3,800 line pending confirmation.
- Root cause and mitigation plan to avoid recurrence.
- Confirmation of any SLO/SLA implications for enterprise reconciliation. comments: 2026-03-10 — Aisha Patel (Support): Opened ticket after initial customer escalation. Collected CSV and invoice PDF. Notified finance and SRE. SLA first response ETA: 24h. 2026-03-10 — Diego Ramos (SRE): Beginning log replay for us-west-2 router logs. Found spike at 18:10Z and image rotation events at 17:59Z in router-az2. Correlating tenant tag loss with router agent version v2.8.1-alpha. 2026-03-11 — Priya Nair (Billing): Placed provisional hold on invoicing and opened an internal billing investigation. Will not finalize invoice until reconciliation concludes. Finance alerted to possible customer
title: Missing reserved-capacity credits for dedicated->hosted fallback window causing overcharge

description: Issue summary:
Customer reported that a set of requests generated by their CI/testing pipeline were billed at production token rates and consumed the customer's free hourly buffer. The customer believes these requests should have been classified as 'staging/test' and either excluded from billing or applied to a separate free buffer pool.

Impact:
- Narrative Labs saw a ~3.2k token charge spike on their daily invoice for 2026-03-04 (billing window us-west-2).
- Free hourly buffer for their account did not reset for the impacted hour, causing subsequent small requests to be charged.
- Customer is a Dedicated tier account with pinned capacity; they consider these charges unexpected and request reversal + postmortem.

Environment:
- Dedicated deployment (org: narrative-labs) in us-west-2
- Edge proxy layer with request tagging (header X-RD-ENV: staging/test/prod) is used by customer CI to mark traffic.
- Requests originated from a known CI IP range (provided by customer).

Steps to reproduce (reported by customer):
1) Run CI job that sends 200 small chat completions over 10 minutes using test API key and X-RD-ENV: staging header.
2) Observe meter entries and invoice line for that hour.
3) Confirm token usage attributed to prod rate and free buffer not applied.

Observed behavior:
- Metering records show the header X-RD-ENV was present on some requests but absent on many others in the same CI run.
- Upstream access logs at the edge show intermittent
title: Unlabeled staging/test requests billed at production rate after edge proxy tag failure; free buffer not applied

text:
applied.

Observed behavior:
- Metering records show the header X-RD-ENV was present on some requests but absent on many others in the same CI run.
- Upstream access logs at the edge show intermittent 504s from internal tagger service during the window 2026-03-04T18:12:00Z–18:22:00Z.
- Billing system applied production tariff for the entire hour; free-buffer counter was decremented.

Expected behavior:
- Traffic with X-RD-ENV: staging or tagged via org's CI IP should be classified into the 'test' namespace and either exempt from billing or deducted from a dedicated staging buffer.

Attachments / logs provided by customer:
- sample_request_ids: [req_9f3b, req_9f3c, req_9f4a] (they included full request payloads)
- customer CI IP range: 52.34.120.0/24
- screenshot of daily invoice lines for 2026-03-04

Preliminary investigation (Redwood):
- Edge proxy logs indicate a routing step to the internal "env-tagger" service that annotates requests. That service returned 502/504 intermittently between 18:11 and 18:23 UTC.
- Fallback behavior for requests without a tag currently defaults to production classification (this is documented but may not be expected by customers using CI-based tagging).
- Metering service accepted events with no env tag and attributed them to the org's default billing namespace.
- A correlating deploy to the env-tagger component was recorded at 2026-03-04T18:05Z (see PR referenced).

Workaround suggested to customer:
- Re-run CI using an explicit
title: Unlabeled staging/test requests billed at production rate after edge proxy tag failure; free buffer not applied

text:
default billing namespace.
- A correlating deploy to the env-tagger component was recorded at 2026-03-04T18:05Z (see PR referenced).

Workaround suggested to customer:
- Re-run CI using an explicit API key scoped to a staging billing namespace (scoped keys prevent fallback to prod).
- Add retry/backoff in CI to avoid hammering the tagger during transient deploys.

Immediate ask from customer:
- Reverse the specific invoice lines for the hour 2026-03-04 18:00–19:00 UTC or issue a provisional credit while investigation completes.
- Provide timeline and mitigation to prevent misclassification during tagger outages.

Next steps (internal):
- SRE to review deploy logs for env-tagger and rollback window 18:05–18:25 UTC (assigned).
- Billing team to prepare provisional credit and evaluate automated reversal if classification error is confirmed.
- Product to add a safety policy: when env-tagger rejects, default classification should be 'staging-unknown' for orgs with explicit staging IP ranges or explicitly registered non-prod keys.

Notes:
- Customer is sensitive because Dedicated customers expect separation of prod vs test billing. They included contract clause referencing separation of test traffic.
- This ticket should remain P1 until credit/rollback is agreed with customer. comments: 2026-03-05 10:12 Priya Desai (Support): Acknowledged the report and requested CI run artifacts and example request IDs. Asked for invoice screenshot and CI IP range. SLA first response

text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports repeated client-side timeouts when running extended completion requests (max_tokens >= 150k) with long prompt history. Impact: customer-facing feature degrades (session resets) during multi-minute generations; spikes observed between 2026-03-09T04:00Z and 2026-03-10T06:00Z. Observed behavior: generation starts and streams initial chunks, then stalls with a server-side idle gap (~6-12s) before the next chunk; client eventually closes with timeout and records a 504. This is intermittent (approx 10-15% of long runs) and correlates with requests that cross batching thresholds and kernel-swap events. Customer workaround: reduce max_tokens or split into smaller generation segments. Business impact: enterprise customer has production workflows for medical summarization; repeated timeouts block nightly batch jobs. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use RW API with model rw-7b-instruct; 2) Provide ~120k token prompt (long history + system notes) and set max_tokens=160000; 3) Enable streaming; 4) Observe output streaming then pause for ~6-12s before next chunk; 5) Repeat ~10-20 times to reproduce intermittent stalls. Note: reproduction more likely when system load is moderate and batching_enabled=true. investigation_notes: Hypothesis 1: Batching policy uses a min wait window that, combined with kernel swap, produces an idle gap large enough for client timeouts when streaming small chunks. Hypothesis 2: Streaming proxy closes idle connections prematurely
title: Intermittent idle gaps during extended completions cause client-side timeouts for long max_tokens requests

text: a min wait window that, combined with kernel swap, produces an idle gap large enough for client timeouts when streaming small chunks. Hypothesis 2: Streaming proxy closes idle connections prematurely when there is a kernel swap or KV cache reload between chunks. Steps performed: pulled request traces, matched request IDs, replayed a subset in staging under load, toggled batching min_duration and kernel prewarm flags. Findings: reproduced idle gap when batch_size=1 and min_batch_duration=50ms under kernel swap conditions; kernel_swap added 40-200ms depending on model variant, but when combined with prefetch/backpressure and prefix reassembly it occasionally reached >6s in production due to queuing behind large batched jobs. Related signals: small spike in streaming-proxy connection resets (metric: stream_conn_reset) correlated to queued kernel swaps; KV cache evictions were not significant during failure windows. Impact scope: affects long max_tokens requests that produce many small streamed chunks and that hit kernel selection paths that are not prewarmed. Linked telemetry: trace links and dashboard IDs provided to infra team (dashboard: /confluence/dashboards/streaming-idle-gaps). resolution_notes: Temporary mitigation deployed 2026-03-12: reduced min_batch_duration from 50ms to 10ms for long-session route and enabled kernel prewarm for rw-7b-instruct in us-west-2 canary (10% traffic). Observed effect: reproduction rate dropped from ~12% to ~2-3% on canary. No client-visible
title: Intermittent idle gaps during extended completions cause client-side timeouts for long max_tokens requests

text: 10ms for long-session route and enabled kernel prewarm for rw-7b-instruct in us-west-2 canary (10% traffic). Observed effect: reproduction rate dropped from ~12% to ~2-3% on canary. No client-visible errors during 24h canary window for non-customer traffic. Planned permanent fix: (1) implement adaptive batching that detects long-session streaming and prioritizes continuity over throughput; (2) ensure kernel prewarm on route creation; (3) add streaming-proxy keepalive/test-chunk to prevent idle close during kernel swap. Target ETA: 2026-03-19 for rollout to all us-west-2, then eu-central-1 by 2026-03-22. Follow-ups: add synthetic tests for long-session streaming to CI, update runbook for support to suggest temporary route override (min_batch_duration) as immediate workaround. text: description: Issue summary: FinGrid Capital (enterprise) has requested an on-demand quarterly audit bundle with the following constraints: 1) Tenant identifiers must be delivered as irreversible SHA-256 salted hashes with a per-tenant salt provided by FinGrid (mapping file must be delivered separately and encrypted). 2) All event timestamps must be normalized to the customer's preferred timezone (America/New_York) and include original UTC timestamp as an additional field. 3) The entire bundle must be cryptographically signed using an X.509 certificate chain issued by Redwood's signing service; customer requires the full chain plus OCSP stapling proof where possible. 4) PIi redaction: username/email fields should be pseudonymized according to agreed field map; redaction must be reversible only via the encrypted mapping file delivered to the customer via SFTP. 5) Delivery format: compressed Parquet files partitioned by date and event_type, with a manifest CSV that includes checksums (sha256) for each file. Impact: FinGrid needs this bundle to complete SOC2 quarterly evidence collection; delay blocks their compliance attestation. Customer flagged potential contract penalty if not delivered within contract window (end of quarter). Requested deliverables (customer-provided): - Per-tenant salt file (FinGrid will upload to our secure intake bucket). - Desired timezone string (America/New_York). - Pseudonymization field map (CSV). Environment: - Production audit stream for FinGrid workspace
title: Customer requests tenant-id hash mapping + timezone-normalized audit bundle with X.509 signing chain for quarterly SOC2 evidence

text: salt file (FinGrid will upload to our secure intake bucket). - Desired timezone string (America/New_York). - Pseudonymization field map (CSV). Open questions / blockers: - Does FinGrid require HSM-backed signing for the delivered bundle, or will Redwood-signed chain be sufficient? (Customer later clarified Redwood-signed is acceptable if chain and OCSP proof are provided.) - Is the per-tenant salt rotation acceptable if we store the salt for future replays? Customer requested we do not retain the plaintext mapping beyond delivery window. Acceptance criteria: - Audit bundle SFTP delivery completed to FinGrid staging box with manifest and checksums. - Files signed with X.509 chain and OCSP responses attached; signature verification steps documented in delivery note. - Hash mapping file encrypted with customer's public key and uploaded separately; Redwood does not retain plaintext mapping beyond delivery confirmation. - Timestamps normalized to America/New_York with original_utc_ts included for every event. - Customer confirms

text: description: Issue summary: Customer runs recurring off-peak batch embedding jobs in eu-west-1 on a Dedicated pool. During the nightly window (23:30-02:00 UTC) a subset of jobs that start in EU are observed to make requests routed to our us-east-1 dedicated pool, introducing ~600-900ms extra p95 latency per request and extra egress/egress-costs. Impact: customer reported increased overall job completion time (up to 30%) and billing anomalies due to cross-region egress. Environment: Dedicated capacity, tenant-specific reserved pool, batch job runner using SDK v1.4.2. Expected behavior: jobs pinned to EU dedicated capacity for entire run when customer uses residency=dedicated and pool pinning. Observed behavior: routing drifts to US mid-run for some long-running batches. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer schedules a long-running batch embedding job (500k embeddings) targeting eu-west-1 reserved pool with residency=pinned and pool_id provided. 2) Job launches at 23:45 UTC and begins processing; initial requests served from eu-west-1 nodes (confirmed by IP and edge headers). 3) After ~18-30 minutes into the job, subset of worker requests show backend-edge headers with us-east-1 edge and serving IPs. 4) Customer observes p95 latency climb and notices additional egress entries in billing report crossing region boundary. logs: 2026-03-13T23:46:12Z DEBUG router: token=acct-9a8b pool=eu-west-1 assigned_node=node-eu-13 2026-03-13T23:47:01Z INFO serving: embed_request id=req-0034 src=edge-eu-1
title: Off-peak EU batch embeddings occasionally routed to US dedicated pool causing large p95 latency regressions

text: report crossing region boundary. logs: 2026-03-13T23:46:12Z DEBUG router: token=acct-9a8b pool=eu-west-1 assigned_node=node-eu-13 2026-03-13T23:47:01Z INFO serving: embed_request id=req-0034 src=edge-eu-1 dst=node-eu-13 latency=180ms 2026-03-13T24:05:22Z WARN control-plane: pool eu-west-1 under temporary eviction due to node-drain; fallback_eval started 2026-03-13T24:05:25Z INFO router: token=acct-9a8b pool=eu-west-1 fallback_pool=us-east-1 route_decision=force_fallback reason=insufficient_capacity 2026-03-13T24:05:27Z INFO serving: embed_request id=req-241 src=edge-us-1 dst=node-us-07 latency=760ms egress=true investigation_notes: 2026-03-13 02:00 - Triage: Correlated customer report with autoscaler activity on eu-west-1 dedicated pool during nightly maintenance window. Observed control-plane decision logs indicating a fallback to us-east-1 with reason 'insufficient_capacity' and 'drain' flags on eu-west nodes. Autoscaler traces show spike in 'scale_down' activities between 23:50-00:10 UTC followed by rapid 'scale_up' attempts; cooldown policies allowed transient period where available capacity for pinned pools dipped below request demand. Router policy for pinned pools has a soft-fallback path that can route to compatible dedicated pools when pinned pool cannot satisfy request and 'strict_residency' is not enforced; customer believed pool pinning would be strict by default for Dedicated tenants. No evidence of SDK misconfiguration on customer side; their pool_id was set in
title: Off-peak EU batch embeddings occasionally routed to US dedicated pool causing large p95 latency regressions

text: and 'strict_residency' is not enforced; customer believed pool pinning would be strict by default for Dedicated tenants. No evidence of SDK misconfiguration on customer side; their pool_id was set in job config and authenticated token scoped correctly. comments: Support (Maya Chen) 2026-03-13: Thanks for the report — I can see the routing lines that moved to us-east. Please advise. SRE (Liam O'Connor) 2026-03-14: Assigning to routing/autoscaler teams. Initial hypothesis: dedicated autoscaler cooldown allowed transient capacity dip; control-plane allowed fallback to preserve throughput. Need to confirm policy defaults for 'pinned' jobs on Dedicated contracts. Customer testing now. Alternatively schedule batch to avoid autoscaler churn window (start before

description: Issue summary:
Customer reports a ~50-250ms increase in end-to-end latency for requests using function/tool calling after a runtime change that introduced a preflight schema serialization/validation hop. This appears to affect only requests that include tool schemas and function signatures.

Impact:
- End-user API latency increased (median +60ms, 95th +180ms) for function-enabled routes for the GloboCommerce account (dedicated pool).
- Customer-facing pages load slower for chatbot flows that call external tools; some timed out after 1.5s.
- Regression began after a canary rollout of schema-validator v1.4.0 on 2026-03-09.

Environment:
- Prod, us-east, Dedicated pool #GCU-42
- API: POST /v1/chat/completions with "functions" schema present
- SDK: redwood-js v0.9.16 (customer-integrated)

Steps to reproduce:
1) Send a chat completion request that includes a tool definition and function schema.
2) Observe higher request latency compared to identical request without functions.
3) Capture trace (X-Trace-Id header) and inspect spans between api-gateway -> schema-validator -> function-proxy -> serving-runtime.

Observed logs / traces (examples):
- Trace ID: trc_20260310_7f9a3b; total server time 410ms; span breakdown: api-gateway 18ms, schema-validator RPC 130ms, function-proxy 9ms, serving-runtime 240ms. Schema-validator shows multiple blocking calls to serialization routine and an auth handshake to function-proxy.
- Datadog traces show schema-validator
title: Preflight schema serialization hop adding extra RPC and increasing E2E latency for function calls

text:
130ms, function-proxy 9ms, serving-runtime 240ms. Schema-validator shows multiple blocking calls to serialization routine and an auth handshake to function-proxy.
- Datadog traces show schema-validator had a 2-hop RPC: -> schema-serializer -> signature-normalizer -> back. Each hop ~40-70ms.

Initial hypothesis:
- v1.4.0 of schema-validator introduced synchronous schema normalization and a cross-service handshake to verify tool signatures which causes additional RPC hops on the hot path.
- No batching/caching for repeated schema hashes; every request with a schema triggers the full synchronous path.

Investigation notes (timeline):
- 2026-03-10 09:12 UTC: Customer opened ticket reporting latency spikes after deployment.
- 2026-03-10 09:30 UTC: Support captured sample traces and escalated to SRE/Runtime team (Slack #support -> #sre-runtime thread).
- 2026-03-10 11:00 UTC: SRE confirmed elevated RPC counts from api-gateway to schema-validator for function-enabled requests (metrics: rpc_per_request increased from 1.02 to 1.98).
- 2026-03-10 14:25 UTC: Runtime engineer reproduced locally by deploying schema-validator v1.4.0 and measured avg extra latency ~85ms per request.
- 2026-03-11 02:10 UTC: Temporary mitigation tested: enable local LRU cache keyed by schema hash in api-gateway, avoiding round trip for repeated schemas. Measured reduction in median extra-latency to ~18ms for cached flows.

Root cause (current understanding):
- v1.4.0 implements a synchronous, canonical
title: Preflight schema serialization hop adding extra RPC and increasing E2E latency for function calls

text:
api-gateway, avoiding round trip for repeated schemas. Measured reduction in median extra-latency to ~18ms for cached flows.

Root cause (current understanding):
- v1.4.0 implements a synchronous, canonical serialization + signature verification step that lives in the schema-validator service. It performs a call to a normalization microservice for backward-compat checks. That normalization call is on the hot path and is not cached. For customers with repetitive tool schemas (common for SDK-driven product integrations), this adds an entire RPC hop per request.

Workaround / customer guidance:
- For affected customers on Dedicated: set request field "tool_validation": "off" (temporary flag supported by gateway) to bypass preflight validation for known-good schemas. Note: this reduces safety checks; use only with trusted schemas.
- For immediate improvement without turning off validation: request that the customer upgrade to runtime client that caches schema hashes locally (redwood-js >= 0.9.18 suggested).

Planned remediation and timeline:
- Short-term (2026-03-12): deploy LRU cache in api-gateway for schema hashes across prod us-east (mitigation rollout completed 2026-03-12 03:20 UTC).
- Medium-term (2026-03-18): change schema-validator to async normalization path (fire-and-forget for known schemas) and introduce per-instance in-memory cache. PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4271 (merged, staged).
- Long-term: add integration test for schema-validator
title: Preflight schema serialization hop adding extra RPC and increasing E2E latency for function calls

text:
(fire-and-forget for known schemas) and introduce per-instance in-memory cache. PR: github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4271 (merged, staged).
- Long-term: add integration test for schema-validator that asserts no more than one outbound RPC for canonical schemas and instrument a dashboard alert for rpc_per_request regression.

Follow-ups / action items:
- SRE: add Datadog monitor for api-gateway -> schema-validator RPC count per request (alert if >1.2 baseline).
- Runtime: ship async normalization and add strict cold-start caching behavior.
- Support/CS: notify GloboCommerce of mitigation and recommend SDK upgrade; schedule a postmortem with customer on 2026-03-20.

Investigating service changes; rolling back to v1.3.8 reduces extra hop and latency in canary. Logging more traces. 2026-03-10 14:40 - Daniel Morris (Runtime

text: increased RPCs to schema-validator in us-east. Investigating service changes; rolling back to v1.3.8 reduces extra hop and latency in canary. Logging more traces. 2026-03-10 14:40 - Daniel Morris (Runtime Eng): Reproduced locally. v1.4.0 introduced sync normalization that calls normalizer svc for each request. PR for async normalization merged (runtime/pull/4271); full rollout planned and monitored. Follow-up: monitor rpc_per_request, add integration test, schedule postmortem with customer.

text: description: Issue summary: Customer requests a one-off delta export of their tenant activity as a CSV with strict UTC timestamp normalization, a rotating MD5 manifest for each batch file, and a machine-verifiable 'proof-of-exclusion' artifact listing records deleted since the last snapshot. They need the package for a regulatory evidence submission and require deterministic ordering and replayability. Requirements: (1) CSV delta with canonical UTC timestamps and ISO8601 formatting; (2) each batch file accompanied by an MD5 manifest entry and a signed manifest file (HMAC-SHA256 with their provided per-tenant key); (3) a separate "proof-of-exclusion" file: a compact list of deleted record IDs with per-ID deletion-tombstone hashes and a top-level Merkle-root signed by Redwood to prove non-inclusion in prior snapshots; (4) resume tokens and offsets for replay; (5) timezone normalization mapping notes and a field-translation glossary in the bundle. Why unique: Customer explicitly needs evidence that certain records were omitted (deleted) and wants cryptographic proof to present to compliance that deletions are auditable and non-reversible in the delivered export. impact: Customer cannot complete regulatory submission without per-record deletion attestation. Blocking legal review and audit deadline for FinCloud Bank. High urgency because compliance audit window closes in 10 calendar days. steps_to_reproduce: N/A - this is a data/evidence export request rather than a bug. Repro steps
title: On-demand delta CSV export with UTC normalization, rotating MD5 manifest, and proof-of-exclusion for deleted records

text: description: Issue summary: Customer FinOptima reports intermittent 200–800ms additional tail latency on requests from eu-west that should be served by their dedicated eu pool. They observe spikes approximately 1–3 times per 1k requests and see traces indicating a short-lived cross-region egress to us-east for some requests despite region affinity. Impact: - Sporadic increased tail latency (affects user-facing search/assist features). - No obvious 5xxs; throughput unaffected. - Customer worried about data egress policy compliance and cost implications of cross-region routing. Environment: - Customer tier: dedicated reserved capacity - Primary region: eu-west (eu-west-1) steps_to_reproduce: 1) Send a steady stream of generation requests pinned to model redwood/open-llama-7b against customer's dedicated eu endpoint (500 rps, typical prompt length ~120 tokens). 2) Monitor per-request region tag in trace and routing logs for a 24h window. 3) Correlate latency spikes to any route changes or capacity events in the serving logs. observed_behavior: Small subset (~0.1%-0.3%) of requests were routed out of eu-west to us-east briefly and returned results; traces show an extra ~200-800ms attributable to cross-region network hop and additional TLS handshakes. No model fallback to smaller-quantized variants observed in the traces. expected_behavior: All requests from customer's eu-resident endpoint should be served from the dedicated eu-west pool (no cross-region egress) unless explicit failover
title: Cross-border egress and session-sticky routing causing sporadic latency spikes — clarification & mitigation

text: description: Issue summary: Customer (BlueLeaf Analytics, dedicated) requested guidance and an in-platform dry-run for a tenant-scoped optimizer change that toggles KV prefix caching and applies a more aggressive mixed quantization profile for low-memory GPUs. They want a safe preflight simulation, targeted A/B rollout steps, and a conservative surgical rollback sequence if any downstream latency or quality regressions appear. Impact: - Customer reports intermittent 95p tail latency spikes in their production traffic after a recent internal experiment. They have paused global rollout but want a guided re-test with our simulator before reapplying changes. - Business impact: data-processing pipelines (internal analytics) are sensitive to 95p latency; sustained regressions could force job retries and revenue-impacting SLA breaches. Environment: - Dedicated pool: dlx-dedicated-us-east-01 (tenant: blueleaf-prod) - Model variants: mistral-7b-epoch (cpu/gpu mixed), llama-2-13b-chat for fallback path - Current optimizer profile: default-stable + tenant overrides (prefetch disabled, conservative quant profile) Requested outcome: 1) Run a non-disruptive preflight simulation that mirrors prod request mix (short/long sequences) and estimates latency/cost delta for proposed config. 2) Provide an explicit, minimal-impact A/B rollout plan (traffic split, guardrail metrics). 3) Deliver a surgical rollback playbook to revert only the changed optimizer flags without touching unrelated tenant settings.
title: Tenant preflight simulator and surgical rollback plan for mixed optimizer profile changes

text: explicit, minimal-impact A/B rollout plan (traffic split, guardrail metrics). 3) Deliver a surgical rollback playbook to revert only the changed optimizer flags without touching unrelated tenant settings. Attachments: customer sent sample traffic traces and a small prompt set for quality checks. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Apply proposed optimizer profile: enable kv_prefix_caching=enabled (tenant-level), batch_policy=aggressive-2, quant_profile=mix-q3b 2) Route 10% of traffic to the modified profile (traffic-split in console) 3) Observe latency, error rate, and token generation quality for 60 minutes 4) Gradually increase traffic to 50% if metrics stable Note: Customer requested we run a simulated dry-run using the preflight simulator before any real-world traffic split. logs: sample-trace-ids: trace-20260312-9a1b, trace-20260312-9a2c Recent metrics pulled (2026-03-11 -> 2026-03-12): p50=85ms, p95=420ms (spike observed to 1.8s during 03-11 14:20 UTC), error rate=0.12% (mostly 504 timeouts during long-sequence jobs) console-sync: snapshot taken: optimizer-snapshot-20260312-15:02 investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: aggressive batching + mixed quant profile increased KV cache pressure and caused longer serialize/deserial latency for large prompts. Another possible contributor: tenant-specific request distribution has a higher proportion of long-sequence requests than the global average, which wasn't accounted for in the profile. Actions taken so far: - Collected request corpus from

text: summary_detail: Customer runs a single chat turn that contains a batch of intended tool invocations (a multi-action response). They observe duplicate side-effects when the client retries at the request level. They want explicit guidance and a recommended pattern to achieve per-tool at-most-once semantics. description: Issue summary: SonderAI calls Redwood with a single chat turn that asks the model to return an ordered list of separate actions (e.g., "[{call: \"create_ticket\", payload: {...}}, {call: \"send_email\", payload: {...}}]"). The model returns a multi-action response and the customer's runtime executes each action sequentially. When the initial request hits a transient network error, the customer's client library retries the whole request which causes the same set of tool calls to be executed again, producing duplicate side-effects. Impact: - Duplicate external side-effects (duplicate tickets, duplicate emails). - Hard to dedupe because the actions are generated inside the model response (no built-in per-action idempotency token currently). - Customer is using Dedicated tier and is sensitive to side-effect correctness. Environment: - Dedicated deployment (prod-us-east) - SDKs: Python 1.8.2 and Node TS 2.0.1 in separate services - Server runtime executes tool calls as HTTP webhook POSTs to internal services Steps to reproduce (high level): 1) Send a chat request that instructs the model to produce multiple actions in a single structured response (JSON list). 2) Client
title: Per-action idempotency guidance for batched / multi-call function-calling requests

text: HTTP webhook POSTs to internal services Steps to reproduce (high level): 1) Send a chat request that instructs the model to produce multiple actions in a single structured response (JSON list). 2) Client receives response but the network connection fails before client ack; SDK retries the request at the HTTP level. 3) Backend returns same model response and the runtime executes the same sequence of tool calls again. Observed behavior: - Two tickets created in downstream system with different global IDs but same payload content. - No per-action idempotency token present in the function_call objects returned by the model. Expected behavior / ask: - Guidance on recommended patterns (client + server + SDK) to ensure per-action at-most-once semantics when the model returns multiple actions in a single response. - Suggested SDK changes or patterns to carry idempotency through retries without changing model generation prompts significantly. steps_to_reproduce: Example prompt: 'Return a JSON array of actions to take. Each action is {name:string, args:object}.' Run Python SDK call: client.chat.create(...) with prompt above and target model redwood-instruct-v1 Cut network immediately after first HTTP response to trigger a retry in the SDK (or simulate transient 5xx on first attempt). Observe downstream actions executed twice by the service that consumes the model output. logs_and_errors: Request ID: req-6a9f2b (first attempt) - HTTP 200 body: {actions: [{name:"create_ticket", args:{title:"..."}},
title: Per-action idempotency guidance for batched / multi-call function-calling requests

text: downstream actions executed twice by the service that consumes the model output. logs_and_errors: Request ID: req-6a9f2b (first attempt) - HTTP 200 body: {actions: [{name:"create_ticket", args:{title:"..."}}, {name:"send_email", args:{...}}]} Request ID: req-6a9f2b-retry (second attempt) - HTTP 200 body: identical actions array Downstream system logs show two POSTs to /tickets separated by 3s with different downstream ids: ticket_1832, ticket_1840 investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: SDK-level retry replays whole model response which contains the action list. Customer does not currently supply per-action idempotency tokens in the generation prompt or in the action metadata. Checked SDK behavior: both Python and TS SDKs retry transparently on idempotent HTTP errors; retries are at the request granularity and replay the same request body unless the application injects unique tokens. Serving runtime: does not currently synthesize per-action dedupe tokens for list responses. Runtime exposes function_call payloads as-is to the customer's action execution layer. Reproduced locally with a minimal script and confirmed duplicate side effects when retry occurs. findings: No built-in per-action idempotency tokens are produced by the model by default when returning arrays of actions. Customer replay occurs because SDK retries re-send the same request body and server responds identically; without unique per-action ids downstream cannot dedupe reliably. Two practical mitigation strategies
title: Per-action idempotency guidance for batched / multi-call function-calling requests

text: Customer replay occurs because SDK retries re-send the same request body and server responds identically; without unique per-action ids downstream cannot dedupe reliably. Two practical mitigation strategies are available: 1) application-level per-action ids injected by the client at request time; 2) runtime/SDK-assisted tokenization where runtime returns stable call IDs alongside function_call objects. recommended_solution: Short answer: adopt per-action idempotency tokens scoped to the action executor and generated by the client or SDK. Long answer and recommended pattern below: recommended_steps: 1) Client-side: Before sending the chat request, generate deterministic per-action tokens. If you expect N actions, pre-create N stable uuids (call_uuid_1, call_uuid_2, ...). Include these tokens in the prompt so the model returns them embedded in each action object, e.g. {name:'create_ticket', token:'call_uuid_1', args:{...}}. This keeps tokens stable across retries. 2) Executor-side: When executing an action, persist an execution record keyed by (customer_id, action_name, token). If an execution with that token already exists, skip re-execution and return the stored result. This ensures idempotency even when the same model response is re-run. 3) SDK helper: Short-term you can implement a small helper that generates tokens and injects them into the prompt or into the top-level metadata field of the request. We'll follow up with an SDK feature suggestion below. 4) Alternative (runtime-assisted):
title: Per-action idempotency guidance for batched / multi-call function-calling requests

text: helper that generates tokens and injects them into the prompt or into the top-level metadata field of the request. We'll follow up with an SDK feature suggestion below. 4) Alternative (runtime-assisted): For customers that cannot modify prompts, we recommend a small change: ask the runtime to return a per-action call_id alongside function_call objects. We've logged an internal feature request (INT-4021) to evaluate exposing stable call IDs from the serving runtime; this would let executors dedupe without client prompt changes. 5) Edge cases: If the model may reorder or merge actions, include both token + intended position index to make matching robust. If actions contain non-deterministic generated content, store a hash of the payload in the executor record as additional validation. code_examples: Python (client-side token injection): from uuid import uuid4 call_tokens = [str(uuid4()) for _ in range(3)] prompt = f'Return an array of actions. For each action include a token field that must match one of {call_tokens}.' resp = client.chat.create(model='redwood-instruct-v1', messages=[{'role':'user','content':prompt}]) # executor can now use resp.actions[i]['token'] as dedupe key TS (executor-side dedupe sketch): async function executeAction(action, customerId) { const key = `${customerId}:${action.name}:${action.token}`; const existing = await dedupeStore.get(key); if (existing) return existing.result; const result = await callDownstream(action.args); await dedupeStore.put(key,
title: Per-action idempotency guidance for batched / multi-call function-calling requests

text: `${customerId}:${action.name}:${action.token}`; const existing = await dedupeStore.get(key); if (existing) return existing.result; const result = await callDownstream(action.args); await dedupeStore.put(key, {result, ts: Date.now()}); return result; } Note: If the model doesn't include token field, use client-generated tokens and include them in the prompt in a predictable sequence so the model can echo them back. risks_and_tradeoffs: Embedding tokens in prompts slightly changes model conditioning and could affect output format if not constrained; use explicit formatting instructions (e.g., JSON schema) to avoid drift. If you pre-generate tokens but the model returns fewer actions than tokens provided, map tokens by position rather than name to avoid mismatches. Runtime-assisted approach requires an internal change and rollout on Dedicated/private deployments — this is a longer-term fix. next_steps: Customer to try client-side token injection pattern and confirm behavior: due by 2026-03-16. Support to provide a small SDK snippet helper package for token generation and injection (internal ticket INT-4067 opened). Product/Serving to review INT-4021 (runtime per-action call_id) and schedule feasibility assessment. If customer confirms fix, update public docs with a new section: 'Per-action idempotency for multi-action responses'. resolution_notes: 2026-03-14 - Provided explicit pattern and code samples to customer. Recommended immediate adoption of client-generated per-action tokens
title: Per-action idempotency guidance for batched / multi-call function-calling requests

text: 'Per-action idempotency for multi-action responses'. resolution_notes: 2026-03-14 - Provided explicit pattern and code samples to customer. Recommended immediate adoption of client-generated per-action tokens plus executor-side dedupe store. Customer acknowledged they will test token-injection pattern in staging. If successful, they will roll to prod and close this ticket. Support created internal follow-ups (INT-4021, INT-4067) to evaluate runtime/SDK improvements for longer-term ergonomics. customer_communication: 2026-03-12 10:15 - Customer (SonderAI) description: 'We need a pattern that stops duplicate emails/tickets when the SDK retries. Is there a way to get per-action ids from the model or a change in SDK behavior?', 2026-03-12 12:01 - Support (Aisha): 'Thanks — we're investigating. Can you share a minimal request/response (sanitized) and whether you control the action-executor? Also what SDK versions are in use?', 2026-03-13 09:40 - Customer: 'Sent sanitized payloads and examples. We control the executor and can persist dedupe metadata if we receive any unique token per action.' 2026-03-14 08:22 - Support (Marco): 'We've got a recommended pattern below. We can also open an internal follow-up to add SDK helper primitives.'
title: Per-action idempotency guidance for batched / multi-call function-calling requests

text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports that long-form streamed responses (SSE) from the hosted API consistently drop the final epilogue — typically the closing paragraph and 'source' metadata — when their client performs a short socket swap (mobile network change) behind our edge. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer opens SSE streaming chat to redwood-curated-7b in prod/us-west with X-Stream-Meta header set as two identical headers (client library adds duplicate header). 2) After ~18s of tokens, client triggers an IP/mobility handoff (WiFi -> cellular) causing a short TCP close/rebind and upstream proxy swap. 3) Client reconnects and requests resume; the session returns prior tokens but the final epilogue and trailing metadata never arrive (stream ends mid-sentence). 4) Repeatable in ~4/10 attempts when duplicate X-Stream-Meta headers present; not reproducible when header is a single consolidated value. logs: 2026-03-10T14:03:22Z proxy-ingress[edge-03] INFO: incoming SSE start session_id=sn-9f2a X-Stream-Meta=[a,b] 2026-03-10T14:03:40Z serving-runtime[worker-12] WARN: client-socket-flip session=sn-9f2a reconnect detected, resume-request=true 2026-03-10T14:03:41Z proxy-ingress[edge-03] DEBUG: sanitized headers for session sn-9f2a; X-Stream-Meta consolidated="a" 2026-03-10T14:03:41Z serving-runtime[worker-12] ERROR: sse-stream-halfclose session=sn-9f2a tail-buffer-not-flushed; bytes-dropped=512 comments: Customer (Sara @ NoteSpring): We're seeing the assistant cut off mid-paragraph
title: SSE stream epilogue dropped after ingress multi-header sanitization; final paragraphs missing

text: serving-runtime[worker-12] ERROR: sse-stream-halfclose session=sn-9f2a tail-buffer-not-flushed; bytes-dropped=512 comments: Customer (Sara @ NoteSpring): We're seeing the assistant cut off mid-paragraph after our users switch networks. It looks like the session resumes but the final section (citation block + closing paragraph) is missing ~40% of the time. Support (Maya): Thanks — confirmed we can see traces for the sample request you provided. I've escalated to SRE for deeper ingestion/proxy checks and requested pcap and tcpdump from the customer. SRE (Oliver): Observed edge sanitization logs where multi-valued X-Stream-Meta became single-valued during header normalization. Need packet captures and proxy config to validate whether the final event flush path was suppressed by header state change. Customer: Attached pcap + curl reproducible steps. We can toggle the client to send a single header as a workaround for now. Support: Temporary workaround noted; SRE to test hotfix in staging and confirm. investigation_notes: 1) Telemetry: grafana link shows increased stream-halfclose count for NoteSpring sessions around 2026-03-10 14:00 UTC (https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/streams/stream-health?var-customer=NoteSpring). 2) Hypothesis: Edge proxy header sanitizer collapses multi-header X-Stream-Meta values to the first value, which causes the serving runtime to treat the resumed connection as lacking the 'finalize' marker. The runtime then half-closes without flushing trailing buffer to
title: SSE stream epilogue dropped after ingress multi-header sanitization; final paragraphs missing

text: X-Stream-Meta values to the first value, which causes the serving runtime to treat the resumed connection as lacking the 'finalize' marker. The runtime then half-closes without flushing trailing buffer to client. 3) Evidence: proxy-ingress debug line 'X-Stream-Meta consolidated="a"' present in failure cases; tcpdump shows original request carried two identical X-Stream-Meta headers before ingress rewrite. 4) Related behavior: Not seen when client sends a single header or when using websocket fallback — implies issue is tied to header parsing path in ingress -> sse adapter. 5) Repro rate: ~40% on NoteSpring reproduction; higher when handoff occurs within 10–30s of stream close. resolution: Workaround: Instructed NoteSpring to consolidate their client SDK to send a single X-Stream-Meta header (server will accept comma-separated values). Implemented customer-side change and verified missing epilogue no longer reproduces in their test harness. Fix: Rollout PR to ingress-proxy that preserves multiple X-Stream-Meta values (avoids collapsing to first value) merged to staging (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ingress-proxy/compare/fix-multiheader-sanitize). Production rollout plan: canary 10% edge nodes 2026-03-12 02:00 UTC, monitor sse-halfclose metric for 2 hours, then full rollout. Follow-ups: add unit tests for header normalization, add metric alert for 'sse-tail-drop' and update runbook to include header-multi value check.
title: SSE stream epilogue dropped after ingress multi-header sanitization; final paragraphs missing

text: Issue Summary - During a runtime-1.22 canary expansion in prod (hosted, us-east-1), we observed a statistically significant p99 latency regression on decode-heavy traffic, most visible for mixed sequence-length distributions. - Regression correlated with elevated KV compaction work (bytes moved + compaction time) and increased allocator fragmentation ratio oscillation. - Canary was rolled back (feature flag disabled + cohort drained) per guardrails. Customer Impact - Customer: Northwind Analytics (enterprise) reported increased p99 latency and intermittent request timeouts during bursty chat traffic. - Symptoms: p95 largely stable; p99 degraded (notable tail spikes) during bursts and after churn events (rapid session start/stop). - Scope: Only canary cohort in us-east-1. Control region (eu-west-1) did not show comparable change. Detection - 2026-01-29 09:12 PT: SRE alert fired: hosted decode p99 exceeded threshold for canary cohort for 10m. - 2026-01-29 09:18 PT: Support received escalation via CSM thread referencing prior tail-latency concerns (see SUP-28411). Environment - Runtime: runtime-1.22 canary (hosted) - Canary cohort: ~5% of us-east-1 hosted capacity (dedicated pools not included) - Traffic pattern: mixed prompt lengths, bursty chat sessions, high cancellation/churn Evidence / Observations - Dashboard signals (high level): - p99 decode latency: +18–35% vs baseline during burst windows - Compaction bytes moved: spiky (peaks aligned with p99 spikes) - Compaction time
title: Latency regression after runtime-1.22 canary: elevated p99 decode latency correlated with KV compaction activity

text: Evidence / Observations - Dashboard signals (high level): - p99 decode latency: +18–35% vs baseline during burst windows - Compaction bytes moved: spiky (peaks aligned with p99 spikes) - Compaction time per step: higher variance than staging - Eviction counters: low/near-zero (so tail impact likely compaction rather than eviction) - Fragmentation ratio: saw sawtooth behavior (trigger threshold repeatedly crossed) - Trace exemplars (from compaction spans): decode loop shows intermittent stalls where compaction step executed near decode boundary. Timeline / Comments - 2026-01-29 09:22 PT (Sean Gallagher, SRE): Confirmed alert corresponds to canary nodes only. Requesting runtime team to validate compaction budget enforcement and whether compaction can run during decode. - 2026-01-29 09:31 PT (Rafael Mendes, Oncall): Opened incident bridge in #incidents; linked thread: sources/slack/incidents/1767735109.json. Requested latest canary diff + feature flag defaults. - 2026-01-29 09:40 PT (Sofia Ivanova, Runtime): Suspected scheduler boundary issue: compaction budget being applied but step can still run in an unfavorable phase when queues are shallow (burst recovery) and decode becomes latency-sensitive. - 2026-01-29 09:55 PT (Allison Grant, Observability): Noted instrumentation overhead increase in the same canary build (compaction tracing spans). Suggested testing with spans disabled to isolate. - 2026-01-29 10:05 PT (Decision): Roll back canary per hosted tail-latency guardrails. Disable
title: Latency regression after runtime-1.22 canary: elevated p99 decode latency correlated with KV compaction activity

text: increase in the same canary build (compaction tracing spans). Suggested testing with spans disabled to isolate. - 2026-01-29 10:05 PT (Decision): Roll back canary per hosted tail-latency guardrails. Disable KV compaction feature flag in hosted us-east-1 canary cohort; drain cohort to baseline build. - 2026-01-29 10:11 PT (Rafael Mendes): Executed kill-switch via config propagation (validated propagation <2m). p99 began recovering within 15m as cohort drained. - 2026-01-29 10:38 PT (Support): Informed Northwind Analytics we rolled back the canary change and are investigating; requested a 30-minute window of request IDs for trace correlation. - 2026-01-29 12:10 PT (Samir Qureshi, Runtime): Preliminary hypothesis: compaction relocation step sometimes exceeds time budget due to larger-than-expected copy size when fragmentation is high; budget accounting is per-step but actual move cost depends on page locality. title: Latency regression after runtime-1.22 canary: elevated p99 decode latency correlated with KV compaction activity

text: normalized behavior. Closing support incident; tracking engineering fixes under runtime epic/tickets. Rollback / Mitigation Actions Taken - Rolled back canary cohort in us-east-1 hosted (KV compaction kill-switch OFF). - Left fragmentation metrics ON for continued monitoring. - Paused further canary expansion pending fixes + perf canary pass. Root Cause (Provisional) - KV compaction work occasionally executed at an unfavorable time relative to decode scheduling, causing transient stalls that manifest as p99 spikes under bursty mixed-length workloads. - Contributing factors: - Compaction trigger oscillation (threshold crossed repeatedly) causing repeated compaction attempts. - Increased tracing span overhead in the same build (amplifying tail effects). Resolution - Customer-facing: Regression resolved by rolling back canary (feature flag disabled; nodes drained to baseline). - Internal: Incident closed with action items assigned; canary expansion blocked until updated perf canary scenario and revised compaction budgeting/hysteresis are validated. Follow-up Tasks / Next Steps - Runtime (owner: Sofia Ivanova / Samir Qureshi): - Tighten scheduler phase gating: ensure compaction steps never run on latency-critical decode path when queue depth is below threshold. - Add hysteresis + cooldown to fragmentation trigger to prevent sawtooth compaction behavior. - Verify budget enforcement uses wall-clock + bytes moved with hard stop when approaching decode deadlines. - Observability (owner:
title: Latency regression after runtime-1.22 canary: elevated p99 decode latency correlated with KV compaction activity

text: description: Issue summary: During a sustained traffic ramp for a single dedicated tenant (NovaChat Inc.) we observed a correlated increase in 5xx responses originating at the API gateway and upstream runtime restarts. The signal appears tied to aggressive prefetch behavior on the serving runtime where prefetch lock contention leads to a backpressure cascade: queued requests block on prefetch locks, API gateway queue growth triggers hedged retries, and runtimes hit watchdog timeouts and restart. Impact: - ~18% elevated 5xx rate for the tenant across us-east over a 30 minute window (affecting production traffic). - Streaming sessions experienced intermittent resets; short requests returned 502/503. - Customer reports increased error rates and a temporary degradation of SLAs for some user flows. Environment: - Production (us-east), Dedicated pool assigned to NovaChat. - Serving runtime version: 2026.02.11-rc3 - API gateway version: 2026.03.01 Observed behavior: - Prefetch workers spike CPU and hold kvcache locks for longer than expected (seen in runtime metrics). - API gateway accept queue depth climbs, then hedged retries increase outbound volume. - Runtimes emit watchdog timeout and restart; following restarts, prefetched KV warms cause transient additional load and more contention. Expected behavior: - Prefetch should back off under contention, API gateway should apply backpressure without creating hedged retry amplification, and runtimes should avoid watchdog restarts by failing
title: Backpressure cascade from prefetch lock contention causing elevated 5xx error rate for dedicated tenant

text: Expected behavior: - Prefetch should back off under contention, API gateway should apply backpressure without creating hedged retry amplification, and runtimes should avoid watchdog restarts by failing fast or shedding load. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use dedicated tenant with prefetch enabled and kvcache sharding (matching NovaChat config). 2) Ramp concurrent short text generation requests to ~4x normal baseline with a mix of streaming and non-streaming calls. 3) Observe prefetch worker CPU, kvcache lock wait times, and API gateway accept queue depth in prod metrics dashboards. 4) Watch for hedged retry growth and runtime watchdog restarts once queueing exceeds configured thresholds. logs: 2026-03-12T21:03:12Z api-gw us-east accept_queue=132 hedged_retries=18 status=502 tenant=novachat 2026-03-12T21:04:01Z runtime-03 watchdog_timeout pid=4213 prefetch_lock_wait_ms=1207 model=llama-3-7b 2026-03-12T21:04:03Z runtime-05 OOM_predicted=true prefetch_worker_cpu=92% kvcache_locked=true 2026-03-12T21:05:20Z api-gw us-east accept_queue=10 hedged_retries=3 status=200 tenant=novachat investigation_notes: Initial triage: alerting flagged a sudden spike in 5xx for tenant Novachat around 21:00 UTC. Pagerduty routed to SRE on-call. Metric correlation: high prefetch_lock_wait_ms correlated tightly with API gateway accept_queue growth. CPU profile shows prefetch workers doing long synchronous IO during kvcache fetch on cold keys. Hedged retry policy amplified load: gateway sent duplicate requests
title: Backpressure cascade from prefetch lock contention causing elevated 5xx error rate for dedicated tenant

text: with API gateway accept_queue growth. CPU profile shows prefetch workers doing long synchronous IO during kvcache fetch on cold keys. Hedged retry policy amplified load: gateway sent duplicate requests when upstream latency crossed hedged threshold, increasing concurrency into already-congested runtimes. Hypothesis: a particular key distribution for the tenant leads to frequent cold-prefetch across a small set of KV shards causing lock hotspots. Under burst, backpressure loops into hedged retries and watchdog restarts. comments: Support (Aisha Patel) - 2026-03-12: Customer reported 15-20% error increase starting ~20:58 UTC. Collecting logs and route-level metrics per runbook. SRE (Marco Ruiz) - 2026-03-12: Confirmed accept_queue climb and multiple runtime watchdog restarts. Temporarily increased API gateway hedged-retry threshold for the tenant as a mitigation (reduced duplicate load). Eng (Lena Cho) - 2026-03-13: Reproduced lock hotspot locally when replaying tenant request key pattern. Adding exponential backoff to prefetch lock acquisition as a candidate fix. Support (Aisha Patel) - 2026-03-13: Customer notified of temporary hedged-retry threshold increase and asked to pause heavy batch jobs. They agreed to throttle custom load for 1 hour. SRE (Marco Ruiz) - 2026-03-14: Deployed runtime backoff patch to canary pool for affected dedicated hosts (PR linked). Monitoring for error rate reduction. resolution_notes: Workaround applied: increased hedged-retry threshold in API gateway
title: Backpressure cascade from prefetch lock contention causing elevated 5xx error rate for dedicated tenant

text: runtime backoff patch to canary pool for affected dedicated hosts (PR linked). Monitoring for error rate reduction. resolution_notes: Workaround applied: increased hedged-retry threshold in API gateway for NovaChat and paused aggressive background uploads per customer request. This immediately reduced duplicate fanout and lowered 5xx rate. Temporary mitigation: throttled prefetch concurrency for the tenant and enabled a short lock-acquisition backoff in the runtime (canary rollout). Fix planned: implement exponential backoff with jitter for prefetch lock acquisition and add kvcache hot-key backpressure metric to triggering autopolicy to shed prefetch for hot shards. PR created: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/4821 Post-incident follow-up: add a dashboard and an automated runbook step to increase hedged-retry thresholds per-tenant when kvcache lock contention is detected. Schedule: engineer-led rollout next week and customer postmortem with NovaChat.
title: Backpressure cascade from prefetch lock contention causing elevated 5xx error rate for dedicated tenant

text: description: Issue summary: After a customer-directed route weight adjustment this morning, Atlas reported a sustained rise in 5xx responses from the API (primarily 502/503). Impact: ~18% of requests for the tenant returned 5xx over a 22-minute window, causing user-facing failures in chat and embedding uploads. Environment: Dedicated tenant in prod (us-east) using pinned instances and prefetch enabled. Steps to reproduce: 1) Apply route weight change (route weight shifted from default pool -> new pool) 2) Resume normal traffic ramp for tenant 3) Observe elevated 5xxs and increased runtime restart counts Observed behavior: API gateway returns 502/503; serving runtimes show repeated model-prefetch evictions and KV cache miss storms. Runtime logs contain frequent "prefetch-lock-timeout" and "connection lease expired" messages. Expected behavior: Route weight change should redirect traffic without increasing 5xx floor; prefetch cache eviction should be graceful without triggering runtime crash/restarts. Initial hypothesis: Misrouted traffic landed on a small set of warm runtimes while a prefetch-eviction race left those runtimes with tainted kvcache states; combined with ingress prioritizer dropping lower-priority requests, this led to priority-queue starvation and upstream connection churn causing 5xx amplification. investigation_notes: 13:02 UTC - Alert: tenant Atlas saw error-rate jump from baseline 0.6% -> 18%. Notified via #support and #incidents. 13:05 UTC - api-gateway metrics
title: Sustained 5xxs after route weight change: tainted prefetch cache + priority-queue starvation

text: churn causing 5xx amplification. investigation_notes: 13:02 UTC - Alert: tenant Atlas saw error-rate jump from baseline 0.6% -> 18%. Notified via #support and #incidents. 13:05 UTC - api-gateway metrics show surge in upstream 502s; 95th latency for successful requests increased from 220ms -> 1.4s. 13:08 UTC - serving-runtime team observed repeated "prefetch-lock-timeout" and kvcache eviction spikes on three nodes (node ids: s-ea-12, s-ea-15, s-ea-23). 13:12 UTC - ingress prioritizer logs show several priority-shed events for tenant traffic coincident with route weight change (link: slack://channel#incidents/clip-xyz). 13:18 UTC - Identified that connection leases to the runtime pool expired aggressively due to a stale client keepalive policy during the traffic handoff. 13:24 UTC - Correlated runtime restarts with a prefetch eviction bug where tainted entries triggered heavy KV lock contention (see runtime PR attempt: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4582). comments: Support (Ethan Myers) 13:10: Thanks for reporting, Atlas. We're triaging — can you confirm if you changed route weights or did this via the Console rollouts? Customer (Atlas SRE - Maria Lopez) 13:11: We applied a 60/40 weight shift to a new pool at 12:58 UTC as part of a planned capacity migration. We saw errors within ~4 minutes. Support (Priya Shah) 13:16: Noted. Engineering is looking at prefetch/kvcache metrics and ingress prioritizer traces. Posting initial investigation in #incidents: https://slack.redwood.ai/archives/CINC12345/p164...
title: Sustained 5xxs after route weight change: tainted prefetch cache + priority-queue starvation

text: Shah) 13:16: Noted. Engineering is looking at prefetch/kvcache metrics and ingress prioritizer traces. Posting initial investigation in #incidents: https://slack.redwood.ai/archives/CINC12345/p164... Eng (Jin Park) 13:22: Found repeated "kvcache:taint_detected" events on s-ea-15; runtime restarted after OOM fragmentation on driver. Hypothesis: eviction race left kvcache locks held causing downstream timeouts. Eng (Priya Shah) 13:30: Applied short-term mitigation: temporarily pinned Atlas route to previous pool, increased connection lease TTL on the client side, and invalidated prefetch index for the tenant. Error rate dropped to 1.7% within 6 minutes. Customer (Maria Lopez) 13:37: Confirmed traffic recovery and reduced user errors. Requesting postmortem and long-term fixes. Support (Priya Shah) 13:55: Creating follow-ups: (1) fix eviction race in runtime (PR linked), (2) add graceful prefetch invalidation on route changes, (3) add ingress prioritizer backoff on rapid weight changes. resolution: Workaround applied: rolled back route weight to original pool, increased client connection lease TTL, and issued a tenant-specific prefetch invalidation which reduced the 5xx rate to baseline. Permanent remediation planned: runtime patch to prevent tainted-kv eviction races (PR opened), change Console rollout to perform staged weight shifts with ingress backoff, and add a runtime-side guard to refuse prefetch invalidation storms. Postmortem scheduled and runbook updated. resolution_notes:
title: Sustained 5xxs after route weight change: tainted prefetch cache + priority-queue starvation

text: change Console rollout to perform staged weight shifts with ingress backoff, and add a runtime-side guard to refuse prefetch invalidation storms. Postmortem scheduled and runbook updated. resolution_notes: Short-term: rollback weight + prefetch invalidate (13:30 UTC) — immediate decrease in 5xxs. Medium-term: runtime PR to serialize prefetch evictions and avoid taint (https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4582) — targeted deploy 2026-03-18. Long-term: Console change to staged rollouts with minimum grace window and ingress prioritizer smoothing to prevent priority starvation during pool transitions. Follow-up action: update dedicated-customer runbook for weight shifts and add automated tenant prefetch drain on route changes.
