text: description: Issue summary: - Since ~2026-03-13T07:30UTC several reserved GPU pools serving Nimbus Analytics have shown a gradual p99/p95 throughput degradation over a 2–3 hour window. The symptom set is a slowly rising request queue, batch slot allocation increases despite constant request rate, and a corresponding step-up in tail latency. Impact: - Nimbus (dedicated tenant) reports degraded batch throughput and higher p99 latency; customer reports timeouts for multi-token streams and intermittent throttling. Business affected: nightly ingestion pipelines and interactive inference endpoints. Environment: - Dedicated pool: us-east reserved cluster (pool-id: rp-us-east-12). - Runtime version: serving-runtime v3.12.7. - Scheduler: gang-scheduler v1.4. Steps to reproduce (observed pattern): 1. Reproduce workload shape: many long-lived streaming requests (60–120s) that do heavy decode and minimal CPU preprocessing. 2. Start 200 concurrent small-batch streams (1–4 tokens per iteration) pinned to rwkv-13b-quant. 3. Observe scheduler's slot accounting and batcher metrics over 30–90 minutes. Observed behavior: - Certain streams keep GPU slot assignments for entire stream lifetime; slot release doesn't correlate to actual GPU utilization. - Autoscaler does not trigger additional capacity because slot occupancy appears steady (but effective usable throughput is lower). - KV-cache entries for these streams grow and cause collisions with small short-lived requests, increasing serialization
title: Long-lived low-CPU / high-GPU streams holding slots and causing reserved-pool throughput erosion

text: because slot occupancy appears steady (but effective usable throughput is lower). - KV-cache entries for these streams grow and cause collisions with small short-lived requests, increasing serialization in the batcher. Expected behavior: - Long-lived streams should let batching multiplex other requests without holding slots in a way that blocks normal batching. Autoscaler should detect real utilization drop/pressure and scale accordingly. Temporary workaround applied: - Support configured an eviction soft-threshold (5m) for long-held soft-locked slots for this pool and applied a conservative slot reclaim policy. This immediately recovered ~30% of throughput for the customer's critical route. comments: 2026-03-13T08:40Z - Aisha Kumar (Support): Customer opened with alerting showing p99 increase on two interactive routes. Not seeing obvious GPU saturation; per-GPU util at 45–60% while queue grows. Suspect slot lock behavior from long-lived streams. 2026-03-13T10:12Z - Nimbus Analytics (Customer - Rachel Li): We started a new streaming job yesterday evening that keeps an open connection while polling for outputs. This roughly matches when the degradation began. 2026-03-13T11:00Z - Benjamin Ortiz (SRE): Applied temporary slot-reclaim soft-threshold (5m). Early effect: pending_queue reduced ~27% and p99 for customer's critical

Continuing investigation into root cause and autoscaler signals. 2026-03-14T09:20Z - Margo Chen (Infra): Found that scheduler heartbeat coalescing and slot bookkeeping path does not decrement 'logical_hold' when stream-only activity enters a steady-state decode loop. Raising a runtime bug: GH-2145. investigation_notes: Hypothesis 1: Long-lived streams set a logical slot hold that doesn't get decremented unless explicit release path runs; decode-only steps don't trigger slot-update callbacks. Hypothesis 2: KV-cache growth causes increased serialization inside batcher; small streams wait behind large held slots leading to effective throughput drop despite available GPU cycles. Metric evidence: per-GPU util at <70% while assigned_slots >> active_batching_slots. This indicates slots are 'owned' but not enabling multiplexing. Correlated config: rwkv-13b-quant runtime path uses a different kernel lifecycle and may bypass the usual slot-heartbeat. Reproducer confirms kernel path correlates to the issue. Related prior tickets: linked SUP-271903 shows similar pocket of slot-lock but with ephemeral streams causing starvation; this incident differs because streams are long-lived and low-CPU. text: description: Customer reports that streaming responses intermittently stop sending chunks mid-generation. They see partial output and then no more bytes; client times out at ~75s. They describe it as: "chunked transfer stalls" / "stream stops sending". Notes: - Dedicated cluster in eu-west - Happens more often during peak hours Repro (customer): - /v1/chat/completions stream=true - Long prompts (>12k tokens) + high concurrency Cross-refs: - Slack thread in #support about 'streaming freeze' (timestamp 1770582660) comments: Aisha Rahman (2026-02-28): Customer call follow-up. They have evidence at the load balancer showing the upstream connection remains open but no more body bytes. Ethan Park (2026-03-01): Looking for correlation with gateway backpressure and flush intervals. Might be hitting per-conn write buffer limits when token rate drops. Connor O'Brien (2026-03-02): We should confirm whether keepalive comments are enabled on this dedicated environment; fix from SUP-1842 may not be deployed there. Ethan Park (2026-03-04): Found dedicated gateway is one minor release behind. Planning patch rollout + add metric for 'seconds since last stream chunk'.
title: Rivermark: streamed completions stop delivering chunks (chunked transfer stalls)

text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports that when their client reconnects to an in-progress SSE stream after a short network blip, the first emitted tokens are truncated or garbled. The remainder of the stream resumes normally, but the partial/gibberish prefix corrupts downstream parsers in their product. This is intermittent (~1-2% of reconnects) and correlates with our auth token rotation window in the prod-us-east serving fleet. Impact: - NovusRetail reports failed order-summary displays in their storefront for ~2% of streaming requests after reconnects. - Client-side parsers report JSON parse errors when the stream's initial tokens are corrupted. - Business impact: some users see blank widgets; revenue impact estimated small but customer wants SLA-level mitigation. Environment: - Customer on Dedicated cluster in us-east (dedicated pool id: dr-prod-7). - Client uses SSE via the public Redwood API with short-lived bearer tokens (rotation TTL 30s at their integration). Why this is different from other streaming reconnect cases: - Not a pure proxy fragmentation or keepalive issue — traces show a valid SSE chunk arrives but first few characters are mangled, not missing. - Occurs only when the client's reconnect happens within ~2-5s of our auth token rotation point. - Neither client nor edge shows TLS/TCP resets at time of corruption. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer has an active SSE session from the Redwood hosted API producing a long running generative response. 2) Induce
title: Auth token rotation race causes truncated prefix on SSE resume leading to partial/garbled first tokens

text: client nor edge shows TLS/TCP resets at time of corruption. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer has an active SSE session from the Redwood hosted API producing a long running generative response. 2) Induce a short network blip (client-initiated disconnect) and immediately reconnect the SSE stream within 2-5 seconds. 3) Observe that the resumed SSE stream emits a first data event where the first 2-8 characters of the tokenized text are replaced with non-UTF8 or garbled bytes, causing client JSON parsing to fail. 4) Repeat ~50-100 times to reproduce the ~1-2% occurrence window; easiest to reproduce when client performs token refresh right around server-side rotation window. logs: Client-side captured fragment (base64): H4sIAAAAAAAA/ytJLS4BAAx+f9gEAAAA (corrupt leading bytes) followed by valid UTF-8 tokens. Edge proxy (nginx) access log at 2026-03-09T14:22:08Z: 200 GET /v1/streams?id=abc123 - - 0.083 - upstream_response_time:0.080 Serving runtime trace (2026-03-09T14:22:08.042Z): conn=dr-prod-7/slot-12 auth_check ok (token_id: rtk-245) rotated at 14:22:06.867Z; resume_stream resumed_kv_cache=true; emitted first_chunk size=128 bytes Auth service audit (2026-03-09T14:22:06.860Z): rotated token rtk-246 issued; old token rtk-245 moved to grace state expires_in=30s Hex dump of received SSE chunk: 7b 22 74 6f 6b 65 6e 22 3a 22 e2 28 9f 20 2f ... (notice e2 28 9f inserted at offset 12) No TCP retransmit events in connection logs; no TLS renegotiate entries. investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis:
title: Auth token rotation race causes truncated prefix on SSE resume leading to partial/garbled first tokens

text: 22 74 6f 6b 65 6e 22 3a 22 e2 28 9f 20 2f ... (notice e2 28 9f inserted at offset 12) No TCP retransmit events in connection logs; no TLS renegotiate entries. investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: proxy chunk coalescing or MTU split — discarded after confirming chunk size variance and presence of non-UTF8 bytes that match neither nginx nor customer client encodings. Observed tight correlation with auth token rotation operations on the serving node (rotation grace period and immediate reauth paths). Repro attempt with synthetic client toggling token refresh window reproduced corrupted prefix in ~3/120 trials — not deterministic but statistically significant versus baseline 0/120 when token refresh disabled. Trace-level capture shows a goroutine in the serving-runtime that swaps auth context during resume; swapping code path copies the buffer into a reusable slab while simultaneously appending the prefix from the KV cache — race suspected between buffer zeroing and write path leading to leftover bytes in front of new data. Memory-debugging with sanitizers on a dev replica reproduces out-of-order memory copy when auth rotation coincides with resume flow (local heap reuse observed). Edge-level mitigation (disable immediate token rotation while resumes in-flight) removed occurrences in a small canary (dr-canary-2) for 48 hours. root_cause: A race condition in the serving-runtime resume path where an auth rotation path reuses/re-initializes a write buffer concurrently with resume
title: Auth token rotation race causes truncated prefix on SSE resume leading to partial/garbled first tokens

text: in a small canary (dr-canary-2) for 48 hours. root_cause: A race condition in the serving-runtime resume path where an auth rotation path reuses/re-initializes a write buffer concurrently with resume write. The concurrent memory copy left stale bytes at the buffer head which are emitted as the first bytes of the resumed SSE data, producing garbled/truncated prefixes on the client. workaround: Customer can increase bearer token TTL to >90s or defer token refresh until after stream finalization. Temporary server-side mitigation deployed to canary: hold token rotation for 10s after any resume event on that slot. resolution_summary: Patch merged to fix buffer reuse race (runtime PR #4123). Hotfix rolled to canary (dr-canary-*) observed zero reproductions in 72h. Rollout plan: staged deploy to Dedicated clusters in us-east over 48 hours with telemetry threshold checks. Post-deploy, recommend customer revert token TTL change or leave as-is; confirmed with NovusRetail that they will keep token TTL at 90s until full rollout completes. next_steps: 1) Merge and monitor runtime fix across dedicated pools (owner: serving-runtime team). 2) Post-deploy metrics: track percentage of reconnects with non-UTF8 prefix payloads; target <0.01% over 24h windows. 3) Add a defensive unit test and fuzzing harness for resume+auth rotation interleavings (owner: runtime QA). 4) Follow up with NovusRetail after full rollout and confirm SLA closure; schedule a short postmortem with SRE and runtime engineers.
title: Auth token rotation race causes truncated prefix on SSE resume leading to partial/garbled first tokens

text: description: Issue summary: During a daytime traffic burst for customer BeaconApps we observed a rapid uptick in 5xx responses from the hosted API (primarily 502/504). Initial spike started 2026-03-14T10:12Z and peaked for ~8 minutes. Errors surfaced from API Gateway fronting fleet and correlated with increased connection errors to upstream runtimes. Impact: - BeaconApps reported increased failed generation requests (25–40% error rate for a 10-minute window). - Other enterprise tenants on the same ingress pool saw minor elevated error rates (~3–5%). - No data loss; requests returned 5xx and were retried by client causing additional load. Environment: - prod | us-east-1; ingress pool apigw-canary-12; serving-runtime pool rw-runtime-prod-7. - Affected model types: open-llama-13b-v2, gptx-lite-7b. Steps to reproduce (approx): 1. Replay BeaconApps tenant burst trace (provided in attached trace file). 2. Simulate many keepalive-heavy short-lived connections to /v1/streams endpoint with varied prompt lengths. 3. Observe accept queue growth on apigw-canary-12 and backlog in proxy->runtime TCP connections. Observed behavior: - apigw returned spikes of 502/504 along with occasional 503s. - Upstream runtime containers reported long GC pauses (~700–1200ms) interleaved with thread scheduling spikes. - TCP sockets at proxy layer entered CLOSE_WAIT/lingering states causing file descriptor pressure and accept-queue pileup. Expected behavior: - Small transient bursts should be absorbed by autoscaler
title: Surge of 5xx errors traced to ingress socket linger pileup and runtime GC latency during tenant burst

text: - TCP sockets at proxy layer entered CLOSE_WAIT/lingering states causing file descriptor pressure and accept-queue pileup. Expected behavior: - Small transient bursts should be absorbed by autoscaler and connection pooling without a high 5xx floor. Workaround applied: - Ingress team increased accept queue and ephemeral socket timeout for the affected pool (temp change) at 2026-03-14T10:30Z. - Runtime team applied conservative GC tuning on one canary host (reduced pause variance) and restarted two noisy containers at 10:35Z. - Support instructed BeaconApps to apply client-side exponential backoff and avoid aggressive short-lived keepalives while we stabilise. Next steps/Action items: - Engineering to roll permanent ingress proxy patch to ensure earlier socket reclamation and limit CLOSE_WAIT growth. - Runtime team to validate GC settings across the shard and add target-level telemetry for GC pause histograms. - Observability to add an alert for socket linger fraction > 3% and correlate with runtime GC p50/p95. - Postmortem and changelog entry scheduled after full resolution. Additional notes: - This ticket references a similar historical event (SUP-421999) where keepalive pressure contributed to 5xx, but current root cause analysis shows GC pause amplification played a central role this time (different mitigations required). comments: 2026-03-14 10:22 - Priya Singh (Support): Customer BeaconApps reporting ~30% of generation requests failing with 502/504 during spike. Collecting
title: Surge of 5xx errors traced to ingress socket linger pileup and runtime GC latency during tenant burst

text: this time (different mitigations required). comments: 2026-03-14 10:22 - Priya Singh (Support): Customer BeaconApps reporting ~30% of generation requests failing with 502/504 during spike. Collecting traces and ask for tenant id and timestamps. 2026-03-14 10:28 - Observability (Amir Patel): Grafana: apigw-canary-12 accept queue length climbed from 120 -> 820; socket CLOSE_WAIT fraction rose to 6%. Forwarding APM flame for runtime-7 showing GC tail latencies. 2026-03-14 10:33 - Liam O'Connor (SRE, assignee): Applied ingress temp tunables (accept queue + net.ipv4.tcp_fin_timeout reduction) to apigw-canary-12. Restarted two runtime containers with GC config patch on node rw-runtime-prod-7a. Monitoring. investigation_notes: Correlation: timeline aligns: upstream runtime GC spikes (p99 GC pause ~950ms) co-incident with apigw accept-queue saturation. Hypothesis: frequent short-lived connections prevent TCP keepalive reuse causing large number of sockets in TIME_WAIT/CLOSE_WAIT while runtime threads stall on GC, reducing socket servicing and causing apigw to return 502s/504s. Evidence: strace on one proxy process shows >1200 fds in CLOSE_WAIT; netstat snapshot attached. Runtime logs show repeated Full GC cycles in host rw-runtime-prod-7a around 10:15–10:25Z. Heap usage modest but fragmentation + JIT thread scheduling increased pause durations. Temporary mitigation proved effective: after tunables and restarts error rate dropped to <1% within 6 minutes. Permanent fix candidates: backpressure
title: Surge of 5xx errors traced to ingress socket linger pileup and runtime GC latency during tenant burst

text: + JIT thread scheduling increased pause durations. Temporary mitigation proved effective: after tunables and restarts error rate dropped to <1% within 6 minutes. Permanent fix candidates: backpressure at ingress (limit short-lived conn rate per tenant), GC ergonomics for runtime container image, and earlier socket reclamation logic in proxy. logs: apigw-canary-12 | 2026-03-14T10:18:12Z | ACCEPT_QUEUE=812 | CLOSE_WAIT=6.2% | 502-rate=28% rw-runtime-prod-7a | 2026-03-14T10:19:01Z | GC pause=980ms | thread=main | stacktrace=attached-gc-flame.svg customer-trace | BeaconApps | 2026-03-14T10:12:40Z - 10:22:00Z | tenant=beacon-44 | repeated short-lived connections to /v1/streams resolution: Mitigated - temporary ingress tunables and runtime restart/GC tuning applied; awaiting permanent fix rollouts and postmortem. follow_up: Schedule ingress proxy patch release: ENG-PR-9276 Add GC pause telemetry dashboard and runbook update Customer follow-up: email BeaconApps summary and recommended client backoff settings
title: Surge of 5xx errors traced to ingress socket linger pileup and runtime GC latency during tenant burst

text: description: Issue summary: Customer NoteWave reports repeated downstream tool/function invocations when their orchestration service (service A) calls our API and then fan-outs to two worker microservices (workers B and C). Both the orchestration layer and the workers implement retry logic. The customer is using a mixture of Redwood SDK versions: Python SDK v1.4.2 in the orchestrator and Typescript SDK v1.2.0 in worker B. Impact: Duplicate side-effects in customer systems (invoice generation, external webhooks) resulting in billing and operational noise. Affects production traffic in us-east, estimated ~0.2% of requests from the customer. Observed behavior: On transient 502/timeout from Redwood, orchestrator retries the same request (idempotency key set), but worker B (older TS SDK) replays the function/tool call after reconnect and produces a duplicate side-effect. Worker C (Python SDK v1.4.2) correctly de-dupes using confirmation tokens. Expected behavior: With a client-supplied idempotency key and Redwood SDK semantics, a single logical call should not cause multiple side-effects even when there are retries at multiple layers. Notes: Customer uses a custom orchestration pattern where the orchestrator forwards the same request payload to workers and attaches a shared idempotency key header named X-Redwood-Idempotency. Workers may also attach internal request IDs. The duplication only occurs for streaming/long-running function calls (>3s generation time). steps_to_reproduce:
title: Cross-service idempotency mismatch when retry logic exists in both orchestration layer and SDK (mixed SDK versions)

text: idempotency key header named X-Redwood-Idempotency. Workers may also attach internal request IDs. The duplication only occurs for streaming/long-running function calls (>3s generation time). steps_to_reproduce: 1) Deploy a three-tier setup: orchestrator (Python SDK v1.4.2), worker A (TS SDK v1.2.0), worker B (Python SDK v1.4.2). 2) Orchestrator sends a single API request to Redwood with X-Redwood-Idempotency header set to a UUID and instructs streaming function/tool call. 3) Orchestrator fans out to worker A and B; worker A uses TS SDK v1.2.0 and immediately begins a streaming consume of tokens and triggers a side-effect when function return arrives. 4) Simulate a transient 502 from serving runtime (inject via fault-injection in prod staging or throttle the connection). 5) Observe orchestrator retry logic kicking in after client timeout and sending same idempotency key; worker A reconnects and replays the streaming session causing duplicate function execution. investigation: Observed that the TS SDK v1.2.0 lacks the confirmation-token check introduced in sdk v1.3 which prevents replay after streaming reconnects. API request logs show the same X-Redwood-Idempotency value used across retries, but worker A establishes a new streaming session (different connection id) and SDK v1.2.0 issues the tool call again on reconnect path. Serving runtime accepted both invocations because the upstream idempotency key is checked at the HTTP request level; streaming session reconnections currently
title: Cross-service idempotency mismatch when retry logic exists in both orchestration layer and SDK (mixed SDK versions)

text: issues the tool call again on reconnect path. Serving runtime accepted both invocations because the upstream idempotency key is checked at the HTTP request level; streaming session reconnections currently bypass server-side de-dup for in-progress stream-based function execution (design gap). Hypothesis: combination of (a) client retry at orchestrator level, (b) older SDK reconnect semantics, and (c) server-side per-stream execution isolation leads to duplicate side-effects. workaround: Temporary mitigation: Orchestrator should add additional application-level dedupe before issuing side-effects (e.g., store idempotency token in shared DB and workers check prior to executing side-effect). Also suggest adding jittered exponential backoff at orchestrator-level to reduce client-side aggressive retries. Customer can also upgrade TS SDK to v1.3+ to get confirmation-token semantics. resolution: In progress: short-term mitigation recommended to customer (application-level dedupe + orchestrator backoff). Engineering has opened INT-842 to implement server-side enforcement for stream-reconnect de-duplication and a patch to TS SDK to backport confirmation-token logic. Once INT-842 merges, we'll coordinate a minor SDK release and a patch rollout. Assign follow-up owner in eng: Priya Shah. next_steps: 1) Engineering to prioritize INT-842 (runtime and API layer change) - owner Priya Shah. 2) SDK team to prepare a TS SDK v1.3.1 backport with confirmation-token behavior and clear upgrade notes
title: Cross-service idempotency mismatch when retry logic exists in both orchestration layer and SDK (mixed SDK versions)

text: next_steps: 1) Engineering to prioritize INT-842 (runtime and API layer change) - owner Priya Shah. 2) SDK team to prepare a TS SDK v1.3.1 backport with confirmation-token behavior and clear upgrade notes - target ETA 2026-03-10. 3) Support to provide the customer a concise runbook for application-level dedupe and sample SQL/Redis checks. 4) After runtime fix, run canary with NoteWave in us-east and monitor duplicates for 48h. comments: Support (Aisha): Customer reports duplicates only when worker uses TS SDK <=1.2.0. Python SDK v1.4.2 workers do not show duplicates in the same test harness. SRE (Luis): Reproduced in internal staging with fault injection; created a short runbook to avoid duplicate webhooks while we investigate. See #support/duplicate-fanout thread. SRE (Luis): Filed INT-842 to track runtime change to enforce idempotency across stream reconnections during in-flight function executions. Customer (NoteWave engineer): Can we provide a mitigation we can apply today without patching all worker images? Current patch window is 3 weeks. logs: 2026-03-01T14:22:58Z orchestrator INFO outbound request id=9f8b... idempotency=XRD-12345 status=timeout 2026-03-01T14:22:59Z orchestrator INFO retrying request id=9f8b... attempt=2 2026-03-01T14:23:01Z worker-A WARN reconnect detected session=svc-77 cause=stream-disconnect sdk=ts/1.2.0 2026-03-01T14:23:02Z worker-A INFO function.invoke invoice_id=inv-990 created=true 2026-03-01T14:23:03Z worker-B INFO function.invoke invoice_id=inv-990

text: description: Issue summary: Customer requests a time-windowed, Parquet-native SIEM export that delivers delta files partitioned by hour and tenant, with tenant-level pseudonymization and cryptographic rotation proof so FinEdge can satisfy audit requirements. Impact: FinEdge needs deterministic daily reconcilation and tamper-evidence for ingestion into their SIEM lake (Splunk/Databricks). Current CSV/flat exports are causing performance problems and force them to run expensive ETL. Environment: production us-east-1, enterprise tenant FinEdge (multi-org). Requested deliverables: Parquet schema, partitioning strategy, record-level pseudonymization design (per-tenant reversible tokenization under customer-managed KEK), rotation-proof manifest (signed checkpoints), and sample export artifacts for one 24-hour window. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer configures 'scheduled audit export' to S3 path s3://finedge-audit-exports/prod/ 2) In Console, selects daily window and tenant scope 3) Customer observes generated files in legacy CSV format and a single monolithic manifest.json 4) Customer attempts to run ingestion job into Databricks and hits performance/timeouts logs: 2026-03-13T09:12:04Z exporter[info] Created export bundle: s3://finedge-audit-exports/prod/2026-03-12/manifest.json 2026-03-13T09:12:07Z exporter[warning] export_format=csv shard_count=1 duration_ms=120321 Customer ingestion error (Databricks): parquet read error: inconsistent schema: field 'request_headers' expected string
title: Parquet-partitioned delta feed for SIEM with tenant pseudonymization and key-rotation proof

text: 2026-03-13T09:12:07Z exporter[warning] export_format=csv shard_count=1 duration_ms=120321 Customer ingestion error (Databricks): parquet read error: inconsistent schema: field 'request_headers' expected string got map S3 ACL check: PutObject succeeded, ListBucket returned 403 for prefix 'prod/' expected_behavior: Platform should provide a Parquet-native delta export with: hourly-partitioned _date/hour_ folders, per-tenant pseudonymized identifiers (tokenization using customer KEK wrap), a compact manifest that includes file checksums and a signed rotation-proof checkpoint, and a sample ingestion pipeline config for Databricks/Splunk HEC. investigation_notes: Initial triage: exporter currently has two formats: csv and newline-delimited-json. Parquet export is not enabled for scheduled exports for enterprise tenants (missing code path). S3 ACL 403 for listing was due to bucket policy denying ListBucket on prefix; PutObject succeeded because exporter used PutObjectAcl with explicit prefix grant. Hypothesis: Parquet path exists in internal prototype branch but not wired into scheduled job runner. Also need KMS integration for KEK wrapping of pseudonyms; current tokenization service stores reversible tokens in plaintext in exporter metadata (not acceptable). Confirmed related PR in platform repo enabling parquet write (PR 1123) but missing manifest signer and KEK-rotation hooks. comments: 2026-03-13 11:02 - Aisha Patel (Support): Opened with customer request and attached sample ingestion

text: description: Issue summary: Beginning 2026-03-12 14:10 UTC BrightDesk reported a sustained elevation in 5xx responses for inference requests routed through us-east. Error rate spiked from ~0.2% to ~7% over 30 minutes. The pattern correlates with per-tenant rate adaptation behavior and a recent throttle configuration push to reduce noisy-tenant impact. Impact: BrightDesk (dedicated) saw degraded throughput for chat and embedding routes; customer-reported elevated latency, partial responses, and spikes of 502/503/504. Customer-facing SLOs impacted; requests dropped and retries amplified error volume. Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated pool for BrightDesk, models: gpt-open-13B and gpt-open-7B. Autoscaler group: ded-pool-3. Steps to reproduce (observed in-prod): 1. Run sustained mixed chat+embedding synthetic load at BrightDesk steady-state QPS (~120 qps) while toggling the rate-adaptation window from 60s->15s (as pushed in config). 2. Observe gateway start returning 502s and increased hedged retries from clients at t+2m. 3. Serving runtimes begin OOM-like memory pressure logs and eviction traces at t+5m. Relevant logs / metrics snippets: - apigw: "priority_shim: shed_rate increased to 0.45 for tenant=brightdesk" - apigw: "circuit: oscillation detected, trip_count=3, reopen_after=12s" - runtime: "gpu_mem_pressure: free<reserved_threshold, attempt_prefetch=false" - runtime: "kernel_watchdog: killing worker pid=23112 (oom suspected)" - metrics: p50 latency drifted from 120ms->450ms,
title: Rate adaptation misconfiguration causes cascading 5xx, circuit oscillation, and runtime memory pressure

text: - runtime: "gpu_mem_pressure: free<reserved_threshold, attempt_prefetch=false" - runtime: "kernel_watchdog: killing worker pid=23112 (oom suspected)" - metrics: p50 latency drifted from 120ms->450ms, 5xx rate to 7.1% over 30m Observed behavior: The shortened rate-adaptation window caused the API gateway to more aggressively shed connections for transient spikes. The shedding triggered hedged retries and client-side backoffs which, combined with aggressive prefetch cancellation, caused rapid fan-in to the serving runtime. Runtime KV cache thrashing and larger-than-expected per-request memory residency caused runtime workers to evict/panic, which surfaced as upstream 5xx. Circuit breakers on the gateway oscillated (tripped->reopened) repeatedly, amplifying error churn. Immediate customer impact: BrightDesk reported multiple timeouts and incomplete chat completions between 14:15-15:10 UTC. Customer operations flagged incident to support; they paused background bulk embedding jobs as a workaround. comments: 2026-03-12T14:25Z - Maya Chen (Support): Acknowledged. Escalating to SRE and Eng. Collected initial request IDs and metrics dashboard link. 2026-03-12T14:40Z - Ravi Patel (SRE): Noted sudden rise in shed_rate after config sweep. Reproduced gateway circuit oscillation in staging with shortened rate window. Adding to investigation notes. 2026-03-12T15:05Z - Elena Voss (Oncall Runtime): Observed increased kernel watchdog restarts and OOM-like traces on ded-pool-3. Temporarily disabled
title: Rate adaptation misconfiguration causes cascading 5xx, circuit oscillation, and runtime memory pressure

text: shortened rate window. Adding to investigation notes. 2026-03-12T15:05Z - Elena Voss (Oncall Runtime): Observed increased kernel watchdog restarts and OOM-like traces on ded-pool-3. Temporarily disabled the rate-adapt aggressive thresholds for BrightDesk which reduced error rate. 2026-03-12T15:25Z - Maya Chen (Support): Customer confirmed partial recovery after temporary config rollback. Continued monitoring. 2026-03-13T09:12Z - Product Ops: Opening follow-up ticket to review default rate-adaptation rollout gating and canary size. Assigned to product infra. 2026-03-14T11:00Z - Ravi Patel (SRE): Draft mitigation applied: increased gate reopen_after to 30s, restored adaptive window to 60s, and pinned prefetch cancellation backoff. Monitoring shows 5xx back to baseline. investigation_notes: Hypothesis A: Shorter rate-adapt window caused over-eager shedding, creating retry amplification from clients and hedged retry fan-in. Hypothesis B: Prefetch cancellation combined with shed traffic increased runtime KV cache churn and memory residency per request, leading to worker evictions. Repro steps validated in staging by toggling rate-adapt window and firing mixed chat+embed workload. Circuit breaker metrics matched production pattern. Found related config rollout PR earlier in the day: infra/pull/2401 which modified adaptive window default from 60->15s for aggressive tenants. Rollout was global by mistake; intended canary size was 10% but target selector omitted. Telemetry: top noisy
title: Rate adaptation misconfiguration causes cascading 5xx, circuit oscillation, and runtime memory pressure

text: infra/pull/2401 which modified adaptive window default from 60->15s for aggressive tenants. Rollout was global by mistake; intended canary size was 10% but target selector omitted. Telemetry: top noisy endpoints were chat.stream and embed.bulk_upload. Prefetch latency increased concurrently which matches cache invalidation traces. Mitigation tested: rollback of adaptive window + gentle reopen_after increase reduced circuit oscillation and downstream OOMs in canary cluster. resolution: Mitigated: rollback of the aggressive rate-adaptation change for BrightDesk and global pause of the rollout. Gateway thresholds adjusted (reopen_after 30s, min_window 45s). Runtime prefetch backoffs tuned and autoscaler warmup increased to reduce hot-spotting. Follow-ups: 1) revert PR infra/pull/2401 and reintroduce with strict canary gating; 2) add dashboard alarm for priority_shim shed_rate > 0.3; 3) postmortem doc and updated runbook entry for rate-adaptation rollouts. next_steps: INT-4012: Postmortem and action items (assigned to Product Ops) INT-4099: Add alerting for tenant-level shed_rate and circuit oscillation (assigned to SRE) SUP-330002: Customer follow-up to BrightDesk to share RC and timeline
title: Rate adaptation misconfiguration causes cascading 5xx, circuit oscillation, and runtime memory pressure

text: description: Issue summary: Customer BrightChat reported a negative invoice line item on the March 1-10 provisional invoice. The negative line is labeled 'reserve-credit-adjustment' and appears after a Dedicated -> Hosted fallback event on 2026-03-07 02:17 UTC. Customer expects reserved hourly credits to have applied during the fallback window; instead we see a late, negative adjustment that increased the net charge for the mid-cycle period. Impact: - Customer sees a $2,318 negative adjustment (reduces credit) on provisional invoice; customer concerned they'll be overcharged when invoice finalizes. - Affects production traffic for BrightChat; they had autoscaling fallbacks to Hosted for approx 3h on 2026-03-07. Environment: - Account: brightchat-prod (org id: oc-98f7b3) - Tier: Dedicated with reserved hours (mixed-hosted fallback enabled) - Region: us-east-1 - Timezone/DST: Customer operates in America/New_York (DST change occurred last week) Steps to reproduce / observed behavior: 1) Confirm dedicated pool saturation and automatic fallback event recorded at 2026-03-07T02:17:12Z (scheduler logs). 2) Validate daily/hourly accumulator shows reserved-hour credit not applied for 02:00-05:00 window on 2026-03-07; postback reconciler later inserted a negative adjustment on 2026-03-08 03:05:42Z labeled reserve-credit-reconciliation. 3) Provisional invoice generated 2026-03-10 shows negative reserve-credit-adjustment line (-$2,318). Expected behavior: - Reserved hourly credits should
title: Unexpected negative line on March invoice after reserved capacity failover and late credit application

text: 03:05:42Z labeled reserve-credit-reconciliation. 3) Provisional invoice generated 2026-03-10 shows negative reserve-credit-adjustment line (-$2,318). Expected behavior: - Reserved hourly credits should have been prorated and applied to hosted-fallback usage at the reserved rate or neutralized via fallback billing policy. Any adjustments should be idempotent and appear as positive credits for the customer, not negative offsets that increase the charge. Attachments/Logs: - scheduler/fallback-events lines 2026-03-07 02:17 UTC (attached) - hourly-accumulator dump for brightchat-prod 2026-03-07 (attached) - billing postback logs 2026-03-08 (attached) - provisional invoice (PDF) 2026-03-10 (attached) comments: 2026-03-10 10:12 - Maria (BrightChat Support Lead): We noticed the provisional invoice had a -$2,318 line called reserve-credit-adjustment. We observed clock drift warnings between aggregator nodes in us-east at 02:20 UTC; nodes were behind by ~2 minutes. I'm attaching aggregator node health and ntp sync events. No evidence of double-billing at the serving layer (no duplicate req ids). 2026-03-11 15:08 - Billing (Marcus Chen): Found two reconciler runs: one at 2026-03-07T05:05:10Z (initial, applied +reserved-credit tags) and a later compensating run at 2026-03-08T03:05:42Z that issued a negative adjustment. The compensating run was triggered by a late postback from an ingestion worker that had an older timestamp due to DST-related ingestion windowing. text: before final invoice generation. 2026-03-12 12:05 - Maria (BrightChat): Thank you. Please proceed with manual credit and keep us posted on the code fix timeline. We need this cleared before 2026-03-15. 2026-03-12 14:18 - Marcus Chen (Billing): Manual credit of $2,318 applied to provisional account balance (internal adjustment id: ADJ-88122). This will reflect in the preview immediately; follow-up: schedule a hotfix to prevent out-of-order reconciler compensations across DST and regional ingestion windows. investigation_notes: Timestamps: Several ingestion workers in us-east were using local time windows that did not account for recent DST change; late postbacks were ingested with timestamps placing them before reconciler window leading to compensating runs. Accumulator behavior: hourly-accumulator uses a sliding-window key by hour-start in UTC. When late postbacks arrive with epoch timestamps that map to prior hour boundaries, reconciler may issue corrections; race conditions with initial postback created a negative adjustment later. Edge-case observed: dedicated->hosted fallback currently tags usage as 'fallback:hosted' and expects reserved-credit reconcile to neutralize cost. Reconcile logic erroneously inverts sign if reconciler run identifies duplicate tag sets and a late postback removes an earlier credit entry rather than consolidating. Related code: billing/reconciler/service (PR 2491) contains logic for idempotency key generation. Current generation uses hour_start +
title: Unexpected negative line on March invoice after reserved capacity failover and late credit application

text: postback removes an earlier credit entry rather than consolidating. Related code: billing/reconciler/service (PR 2491) contains logic for idempotency key generation. Current generation uses hour_start + org_id + node_id; node_id variance caused duplicate keys in this incident. SRE note: aggregator node ntp drift amplified the effect; we should add stricter ntp alerts during DST windows and ensure ingestion workers use monotonic event time where possible. resolution_notes: Workaround: Manual credit ADJ-88122 issued to BrightChat for $2,318 on 2026-03-12 14:18. Customer confirmed receipt in portal. (Pending final invoice reconciliation.) Short-term fix: Rollback of reconciler idempotency change that allowed node_id to affect key generation. Rollback scheduled to staging 2026-03-13; hotfix to production pending post-staging validation. Long-term mitigation: Update reconciler to compute idempotency key using deterministic hour_start + org_id + reserved_pool_id and ignore node_id; add explicit handling for late postbacks arriving outside a 24-hour grace window. Add unit + integration tests for DST edge cases. Follow-ups assigned: BILL-52 (implement key-change and tests), SRE-78 (improve ntp monitoring), OPS-134 (backfill re-run for accounts affected by late-postbacks between 2026-03-01 and 2026-03-14).
title: Unexpected negative line on March invoice after reserved capacity failover and late credit application

text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports a reproducible, low-amplitude drift in rerank order for an enterprise production workflow after a bulk re-ingest. Impact: Top-5 ordering for rerank lists changes subtly across >1% of queries, causing downstream UX tests to fail non-deterministically. Degradation is not a full outage but causes incorrect search ranking for high-precision use cases (medical records). steps_to_reproduce: Customer uploads a batch of embeddings produced by a local numpy pipeline (float64) followed by a smaller batch produced by a PyTorch pipeline (float32) using the same namespace/namespace-version. Both batches are uploaded to the embeddings API via multipart parallel workers that use the standard SDK with automatic dtype inference. After ingestion completes, customer runs their rerank job that queries top-k similarity against the combined shard set. Observe top-5 differences between runs when the two batch orders are flipped (float64-first vs float32-first). logs: ingest-service.INFO: chunk_upload completed batch_id=bb3f2 size=4800 dtype=float64 namespace=meditech-v1 ingest-service.INFO: chunk_upload completed batch_id=c9a7a size=720 dtype=float32 namespace=meditech-v1 reranker.DEBUG: similarity_matrix computed using dtype=float32 (downcast applied at write path) shard=shard-13 kv-cache.WARNING: shard-merge detected mismatched 'dtype' header; coerced entries=720 downcast_path=fp32 at 2026-03-10T14:22:03Z comments: Priya Sharma (Support): Customer reports
title: Subtle top-k drift after mixed float64->fp32 coercion in embedding ingestion pipeline

text: path) shard=shard-13 kv-cache.WARNING: shard-merge detected mismatched 'dtype' header; coerced entries=720 downcast_path=fp32 at 2026-03-10T14:22:03Z comments: Priya Sharma (Support): Customer reports 1-2% top-5 reordering after a scheduled re-ingest yesterday. They provided sample query IDs and a small payload reproducer. Asked for ingestion metadata and SDK versions. Diego Morales (Eng): Confirmed logs show automated downcast to fp32 during write. Need to determine whether the coercion is performed deterministically and whether it changes vector rounding behavior. Assigning to embeddings infra to verify storage serialization path. SRE (Investigation): Found that the ingest pipeline has a heuristics branch that coerces float64->float32 when batch_size > 1024 to save space. This was introduced in commit github.com/redwood-inference/ingest-service@b4a9c3 (storage-precision-heuristic). Customer (MediTech): Provided two sample query IDs (Q-1001, Q-1002) and said the ordering flip affects triage for priority cases. Asked if a rollback or re-ingest is possible and how to avoid the issue in the future. Diego Morales (Eng): Suggested a targeted re-ingest of the smaller float32 batch into a new namespace to compare behavior. Also requested embedded sample vectors from customer to run local similarity checks. investigation_notes: Hypothesis: Non-deterministic rounding introduced by float64->float32 coercion at the ingest path changes the last 1-2 decimal places of certain vector components,
title: Subtle top-k drift after mixed float64->fp32 coercion in embedding ingestion pipeline

text: similarity checks. investigation_notes: Hypothesis: Non-deterministic rounding introduced by float64->float32 coercion at the ingest path changes the last 1-2 decimal places of certain vector components, enough to change tie-breakers in dense similarity top-k for near neighbors. Reproduction: Using customer's sample payloads, a local reranker run shows top-5 differences when coercion is applied vs. when original float64 vectors are preserved and compared using float64 similarity computation. The magnitude of cosine delta is ~1.2e-6 on average for affected pairs but sufficient to flip ordering in tightly clustered results. Root cause candidate: storage-precision-heuristic in ingest-service downcasts float64 inputs to fp32 without preserving a header indicating original dtype; downstream reranker always treats stored vectors as fp32 which silently changes similarity calculations, especially for near-duplicate items. Related: No explicit dtype header persisted with batch metadata in v1 of the namespace. There is an open PR to add dtype metadata (see related_github_prs). Risk assessment: High for high-precision customers (medical/legal). Low for standard consumer workloads. Non-urgent for availability but urgent for correctness for this customer. resolution: Pending: mitigation path identified. Short-term workaround is to re-ingest float64-produced batches into a separate namespace that preserves float64 or to enable 'preserve_dtype' flag in a forthcoming ingest-service patch. Engineering
title: Subtle top-k drift after mixed float64->fp32 coercion in embedding ingestion pipeline

text: identified. Short-term workaround is to re-ingest float64-produced batches into a separate namespace that preserves float64 or to enable 'preserve_dtype' flag in a forthcoming ingest-service patch. Engineering has a PR to add explicit dtype metadata and to disable the heuristic when customer_tier=enterprise (PR referenced). SRE will schedule a targeted re-ingest support window with the customer after approval. next_steps: Eng to merge PR that adds explicit dtype metadata and an opt-out for the auto-downcast heuristic (target: 2026-03-18). Support to coordinate a controlled re-ingest with MediTech for a 1-hour maintenance window and validate rerank ordering post-reingest. Add a telemetry alert for 'dtype_coercion_count' > 0 for enterprise namespaces to surface to Console and support channels. Document guidance in KB: 'Avoid mixed-dtype bulk-ingests in same namespace' and add recommended CLI flags.
title: Subtle top-k drift after mixed float64->fp32 coercion in embedding ingestion pipeline

text: description: Issue summary: During a sustained traffic burst from OgleMark (enterprise tenant with heavy streaming usage) the session-coordinator entered a failover amplification loop. The coordinator retried session handoffs aggressively while downstream autoscaler and serving pools were already saturated. Symptoms included elevated 5xx rate (peaked at ~18% for the tenant), duplicate acknowledgement messages seen by the customer (duplicate completions for the same prompt), and streaming connections that briefly stalled then reconnected, producing overlapping responses. Impact: - Customer-visible: duplicate completions for chat sessions, delayed first-byte for streaming responses, occasional client disconnects. - Business: SLA exposure for OgleMark (enterprise), customer escalation to account team, preliminary billing discrepancy for retried tokens. Timeline (high level): - 2026-02-18 02:13 UTC: sudden traffic spike from OgleMark (large campaign). - 02:15 UTC: session-coordinator observed increased handoff rate; autoscaler began scaling but lagged due to GPU provisioning delay. - 02:18-02:32 UTC: retries amplified; duplicate ack events observed in logs. 5xx rate for the tenant peaked at ~18%; global p99 tail latency rose from 300ms baseline to 2.4s on affected routes. - 02:35 UTC: Support engaged SRE; temporary mitigation applied (throttle window + session-affinity enforcement). - 03:02 UTC: mitigation rolled out; duplicate completions dropped to baseline; customer connections
title: OgleMark: session-coordinator failover amplification causing retries, duplicate acknowledgements, and elevated 5xx rate — after-action

text: Support engaged SRE; temporary mitigation applied (throttle window + session-affinity enforcement). - 03:02 UTC: mitigation rolled out; duplicate completions dropped to baseline; customer connections stabilized. Environment: - prod cluster us-east-1; dedicated placement group for enterprise tenants; kernel selection: quantized-ff16. Steps to reproduce (dev-repro guidance): 1) Simulate OgleMark traffic pattern: burst of ~2.5k concurrent streaming sessions over 2 minutes with frequent reconnects and several long-running completions (>2k tokens). 2) Force KV cache flush between handoffs to simulate coordination mismatch. 3) Observe session-coordinator logs for repeated handoff attempts and note overlapping ACK messages to client websocket. Logs / traces / dashboards: - Kibana session-coordinator logs: https://kibana.internal/redwood/session-coord/_search?q=oglemark+2026-02-18 - Grafana: tenant OgleMark dashboard (5xx rate, tail latency): https://grafana.internal/d/oglemark/tenant-health - Jaeger trace sample: https://jaeger.internal/trace/0a1b2c3d Temporary mitigation applied: - Throttle backlog for session handoffs to 30 reqs/s for the tenant for 20 minutes. - Forced session-affinity header enforcement to avoid cross-pool handoffs while autoscaler completed scale-up. - Issued a one-time billing credit hold for retried tokens pending audit. Root cause hypothesis (summary): - Session-coordinator aggressively retried in-flight handoffs when it received partial ACK timeouts, without
title: OgleMark: session-coordinator failover amplification causing retries, duplicate acknowledgements, and elevated 5xx rate — after-action

text: one-time billing credit hold for retried tokens pending audit. Root cause hypothesis (summary): - Session-coordinator aggressively retried in-flight handoffs when it received partial ACK timeouts, without respecting downstream autoscaler backpressure signals. The combination of aggressive retries plus a temporary delay in GPU pod readiness led to a classic amplification (retries -> more load -> more retries). Additionally, KV cache invalidations during the spike caused duplicate session state writes, which the client observed as duplicate acknowledgements/completions. Permanent fixes / recommended changes (short list): - Add a coordinated backoff layer: session-coordinator must respect serving-runtime backpressure and circuit-breaker states before retrying handoffs. - Ensure KV cache write idempotency: include session-version tokens to avoid duplicate ACKs on replays. - Autoscaler tuning: reduce minimum pod init latency by pre-warming GPU pool for known enterprise tenants during campaign windows. - Post-incident: run a chaos test that simulates KV flush + heavy reconnects to validate the fix. Customer communication (summary sent to account team): We acknowledged the customer within 10 minutes. We implemented a throttle-and-affinity mitigation and returned to normal within ~47 minutes. Engineering root cause analysis is in progress; we committed to a mitigation plan and a timeline for a permanent fix. Billing team flagged retried tokens for credit review. investigation_notes:
title: OgleMark: session-coordinator failover amplification causing retries, duplicate acknowledgements, and elevated 5xx rate — after-action

text: minutes. Engineering root cause analysis is in progress; we committed to a mitigation plan and a timeline for a permanent fix. Billing team flagged retried tokens for credit review. investigation_notes: - SRE note: coordinator metrics showed handoff retry rate increased 12x relative to baseline. - Trace analysis: several traces show the coordinator re-emitting ACK messages on state refresh; duplicates had identical session_id but different sequence numbers. - Hypothesis validated in staging repro: when KV flush and pod provisioning delay are combined, the coordinator path produces duplicate writes. - Cross-team sync: scheduler and autoscaler confirmed a 90s median pod readiness increase correlating with the window. - Security check: no indication of malicious clients or unauthorized replays. Links referenced during investigation: - Slack thread: #support-oglemark (https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C08XYZ) - Runbook: Session Coordinator Circuit Breaker (https://confluence.redwood-inference.com/display/OPS/Session+Coordinator+Circuit+Breaker) resolution: - Deployed a hotfix to session-coordinator that adds progressive retry backoff and checks autoscaler circuit state before retrying handoffs (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/session-coordinator/pull/614). - Added session-version token to KV writes to make them idempotent; duplicate ACKs no longer issued. - Adjusted autoscaler prewarm policy for enterprise tenants to reduce pod readiness latency during known campaign windows.

text: description: Issue summary: Scanly Labs reports a sharp, sustained drop in throughput from their dedicated GPU pool in us-west starting 2026-03-11 09:12 UTC. Latency for streaming generation requests increases and many requests time out. The Redwood autoscaler began oscillating (rapid scale-up then scale-down) around the same time, making the throughput degradation persistent. Impact: - Production traffic for Scanly Labs' inference endpoints degraded: 60-80% drop in token throughput on pinned routes. - Errors observed: tail latency spikes >10s, client-side timeouts, and a small increase in 5xx from serving-runtime. - Customer-facing feature (real-time report generation) impacted for ~80% of requests routed to dedicated pool. Environment: - Dedicated pool: scanly-prod-pool-usw (reserved 12 GPUs across 3 nodes) - Region: us-west - Models: gptj-compat-13b (primary), redwood-rlhf-7b (fallback) Observed behaviour / logs summary: - Prometheus graphs show GPU utilization uneven: some GPUs pegged near 98% memory usage, others at 5-10% idle. - Node exporter shows frequent GPU ECC/corrected error increases on node gpu-02-usw. - Autoscaler logs: frequent scale events every 3-7 minutes triggered by queue growth, followed by immediate scale-down when some requests fail with GPU allocation error. - Kubelet logs on affected nodes contain repeated container OOM and GPU allocation timeout messages. Steps to reproduce (customer pattern): 1) Send steady production traffic to pinned model route
title: Dedicated cluster: GPU contention causing sustained throughput collapse and autoscale oscillation

text: error. - Kubelet logs on affected nodes contain repeated container OOM and GPU allocation timeout messages. Steps to reproduce (customer pattern): 1) Send steady production traffic to pinned model route for 30+ minutes (Scanly load test reproduced issue). 2) Observe queue length and latency grow, autoscaler adds capacity. 3) Some newly added nodes immediately show GPU allocation failures; scheduler reschedules pods causing node churn and eviction spikes. Initial hypothesis: - A single tenant process on one node is causing GPU memory fragmentation or a driver-level fault that prevents the scheduler from placing pods evenly, leading to practical GPU starvation on some nodes (noisy-tenant-like behaviour). - Autoscaler is reacting to queue length but not accounting for allocation failures, so it oscillates and amplifies instability. - Potential hardware/driver issue on gpu-02-usw (ECC counters, thermal events) contributing to allocation timeouts. Customer's immediate ask: - Restore stable throughput and stop autoscale oscillation. - Short-term mitigation while root cause is identified. text: description: Issue summary: Customer BrightCart started a tenant/org switchover (subaccount migration) on 2026-03-09. After cutover we observe recurring 'soft' 429 responses for a subset of interactive chat and short-lived embedding requests. Errors present as 429 with Retry-After 1-3s and sporadic failures concentrated in short windows (30s-2m) roughly every 5–10 minutes. Impact: - ~4–8% of interactive chat messages and ~6% of small embedding batches receive 429s during the pattern window, causing user retries and visible errors in the customer UI. - Affects production us-east BrightCart dedicated pool only. Observed behavior: - Pattern started immediately after BrightCart's subaccount routing change (org switchover) on 2026-03-09 22:00 UTC. - 429s correlate with routing hits to a new burst pool backend and simultaneous health-check bursts from the migration coordinator. - Logs show token-window depletion events and 'fallback to burst-pool' trace messages. Expected behavior: - During migration the platform should honor per-route/tenant burst credits and not produce repeated soft 429s when brief health-checks or ephemeral short-lived sessions occur. Fallback behavior should gracefully queue or route to compatible capacity without double-counting tokens. Customer request: - Clarify how split burst-pools and fallback routing count tokens during a tenant migration, and advise configuration or immediate mitigations the customer can apply to eliminate the recurring soft 429s. steps_to_reproduce:
title: Clarify split burst-pool fallback behavior and recurring soft 429s after org switchover

text: description: Issue summary: Customer reports inflated billed token counts for streaming generation calls after a retention backfill ran overnight. Totals in the Console usage ledger show nearly 2x the tokens compared to raw request logs for affected API keys. Impact: Customer is facing a billing discrepancy for the last 24 hours; they are an enterprise account with a committed contract and will escalate to finance if not resolved. Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated routing to hosted inference; tracehook configured on dashboards for request attribution. Observed behavior: For streaming generation requests that include very large tracehook headers (customer uses a 5kB provenance header with session metadata), the Console usage export splits a single streaming session into multiple usage rows and counts tokens twice when the tracehook header value exceeds the internal batching threshold. Expected behavior: Streaming session should attribute all tokens to one usage row per request and not double-count across the same API key + request-id. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer config: enable tracehook for dashboard linking with a large custom header (~4–6KB). 2) Make a streaming generate call (client-side chunked) to redwood-small-chat-1 with a single request and a long conversation. 3) Wait for the usage-analytics pipeline to process the session (post-ingest backfill or live). 4) Compare Console -> Usage ledger / ad-hoc export vs raw request logs (request_id). Observed: ledger shows
title: Console billing shows duplicated token usage for streaming calls when large tracehook headers present

Observed: ledger shows two or more rows with token counts summing to ~2x the raw log tokens for same request_id. Repro script created by SE to generate large tracehook headers and streaming requests; reproduces consistently in staging. Instrumented the ingestion connector and confirmed that when header length > 32768
title: Console billing shows duplicated token usage for streaming calls when large tracehook headers present

text: script created by SE to generate large tracehook headers and streaming requests; reproduces consistently in staging. Instrumented the ingestion connector and confirmed that when header length > 32768 bits (~4KB) the connector emits an additional 'header-shard' attribute. Billing aggregation groups by (api_key, session_hash, header_shard) but later compaction merges on (api_key, session_hash) — race between compaction flush and late-arriving shard fragments causes duplicated final rows. Related recent deployment: ingestion service change that introduced header-shard to improve routing (PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/ingest/pull/762). workaround: Ask customer to temporarily reduce tracehook header size under ~3KB for affected requests until a fix is deployed. Alternatively, disable tracehook enrichment for the org for the short window. For customers on Dedicated, we can run a targeted backfill script to deduplicate by request_id and coalesce shards (manual finance adjustment may be necessary). next_steps: 1) Patch ingestion to emit stable shard key derived deterministically that avoids creating new shards for large headers (eng: Marco). 2) Add dedupe pass in billing aggregation to coalesce rows with identical request_id within a 1s window (eng: Billing). 3) Run a one-off dedupe backfill for Acme Retail (ops). 4) Communicate interim workaround and timeline to customer and Finance (CS). 5) Add alert in observability when shard-count per-request > 1 (SRE). comments: 2026-03-10T09:12Z

text: description: Issue summary: Customers (notably Nightingale AI on Dedicated) observed intermittent 502/503 responses over a 6-hour window. Errors cluster when long-lived streaming chat sessions overlap with scheduled bulk embedding uploads. Impact: customer requests return 5xx, elevated error rates for tenant, and degraded p99 latency. Impact: - Nightingale reported 15-25% request error rate during peak overlap windows. - Affected endpoints: /v1/chat/stream, /v1/embeddings/bulk-upload - Secondary effects: autoscaler churn on dedicated pool, increased GPU prefetch evictions. Timeline (high level): - 2026-03-11 03:35 UTC: Nightingale begins scheduled embedding batch (large batches of 10k vectors) while several streaming sessions remain active for long-lived conversations. - 2026-03-11 03:43 UTC: Support alerting and metrics show upstream 502/503s spike and kvcache write latencies climbing. - 2026-03-11 04:10 UTC: Initial mitigation (reduce embedding batch concurrency) applied by CS; errors drop but do not fully resolve. Steps to reproduce (internal): 1) Simulate 100 concurrent long-lived streaming sessions with keepalive interval 45s. 2) Submit a bulk embedding job (10k items) from the same tenant with 8 concurrent worker uploads. 3) Observe keepalive backlog, kvcache write queue metric spike, followed by API 502/503s. Observed logs/metrics (examples): - api-gateway: 502 upstream reset logs correlated with backend write-timeouts. - serving-runtime: repeated "kvcache_write_backpressure"
title: Intermittent 502/503s traced to KV write-backlog when long-lived streams coincide with large embedding batches

text: spike, followed by API 502/503s. Observed logs/metrics (examples): - api-gateway: 502 upstream reset logs correlated with backend write-timeouts. - serving-runtime: repeated "kvcache_write_backpressure" warning and "prefetch_eviction" entries. - infra metrics: kvcache_write_queue_depth peaked at 18k, kvcache_write_latency p95 > 2s. - GPU: brief OOM-suppress events tied to prefetch spillover. investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: large embedding batch saturated the kvcache writer pool, and long-lived streams kept sockets/worker slots pinned, amplifying backpressure to api-gateway. Traces show the api-gateway request to runtime stalls waiting on kvcache write RPCs for >1.5s before upstream reset, causing 502s. Observed that keepalive sessions are not being recycled quickly on Dedicated nodes; accept-queue shows many "HEARTBEAT" connections in CLOSE_WAIT leading to FD pressure. SRE reproduced locally by launching streaming load + bulk embedding; tuning embedding concurrency mitigated reproduction severity. Noted an interaction with TLS session reuse on the dedicated pool; old sessions delaying worker socket reuse increased effective concurrency beyond quota. Related infra PR (https://github.com/redwood/infra/pull/2241) contains a diagnostic toggle for kvcache writer pool sizing which was exercised during testing. comments: 2026-03-11 05:02 - Support (Maya Chen): Customer reports 20% 5xx over 30m window. Collected request IDs and initial logs; escalated to SRE. Asked customer

text: description: Issue summary: After an automated region failover triggered by a noisy-neighbor GPUS pool remediation in us-west, Nimbus Retail observed truncated streaming completions and a simultaneous unexpected billable token spike for multiple routes. Impact: several retail checkout flows received partial responses or abrupt stream termination; billing dashboard shows a 3x token consumption increase for Nimbus' primary API key during the window. Environment: production (us-west) routing through streaming-proxy v2.7.2 and serving-runtime build 5.12.2. impact: Customer-facing timeouts and truncated streaming responses affecting checkout and price-lookup endpoints. Approximately 62% of streaming sessions for the customer were truncated mid-stream between 2026-03-10T02:14Z and 2026-03-10T03:03Z. Billing shows an anomalous increase in billable tokens tied to repeated re-requests and resumed sessions, resulting in an estimated invoice delta of +$18k for the affected window. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use Nimbus Retail API key in us-west region with streaming=true and max_tokens=1024. 2) Simulate concurrent checkout flows (10k RPS ramp to 25 RPS concurrently for test tenant). 3) Trigger upstream failover simulation: disable primary GPU pool nodes (simulated via admin toggle) and force traffic to fallback pool. 4) Observe streaming client receives partial events with final 'stream_end' missing; clients re-try and create duplicate token accounting. Note: reproduction requires multi-connection
title: Nimbus Retail — post-failover streaming truncation and unexpected invoice spike

text: to fallback pool. 4) Observe streaming client receives partial events with final 'stream_end' missing; clients re-try and create duplicate token accounting. Note: reproduction requires multi-connection prefix cache warmup to replicate KV cache mismatch behavior seen in production. logs: Selected log excerpts: - 2026-03-10T02:14:07Z streaming-proxy[host-17] WARN connection-reset by worker during KV sync - 2026-03-10T02:14:09Z serving-runtime[worker-3] ERROR failed to apply prefix-cache for session id=sess-8b3c: mismatch-length expected=1024 actual=512 - 2026-03-10T02:14:12Z billing-agent INFO token-accounting: key=nimbus-prod-01 request_id=req-9ff3 consumed_tokens=1187 - 2026-03-10T02:16:41Z api-gateway WARN 502 upstream reset during streaming chunk - 2026-03-10T02:45:02Z streaming-proxy INFO reconnect policy fired for session sess-8b3c, retry_count=3 Full logs and trace links attached in Confluence page and internal S3 bucket (s3://redwood-incident-archive/2026-03-10/nimbus/). timeline: 2026-03-10T02:12Z — Noisy-neighbor remediation kicked (autoscaler drained pool-A) 2026-03-10T02:14Z — Customers begin reporting truncated streams (first PagerDuty alert) 2026-03-10T02:18Z — On-call SRE Diego triaged: observed KV cache mismatches in serving-runtime 2026-03-10T02:27Z — Automated failover completed; traffic routed to cold pool-B with different kernel selection 2026-03-10T02:30Z — Billing spikes flagged by monitoring; audit shows repeated replays from client retries 2026-03-10T02:47Z
title: Nimbus Retail — post-failover streaming truncation and unexpected invoice spike

text: failover completed; traffic routed to cold pool-B with different kernel selection 2026-03-10T02:30Z — Billing spikes flagged by monitoring; audit shows repeated replays from client retries 2026-03-10T02:47Z — Temporary mitigation applied: disable prefix-cache invalidation during pool transition (hotfix rollback) 2026-03-10T03:03Z — Streaming error rate returns to baseline; billing anomaly window closed 2026-03-10T05:00Z — Follow-up: started a canary for a kernel / cache-aligning build in us-west investigation_notes: Hypotheses tested: 1) KV prefix-cache deserialization mismatch after pool migration caused serving-runtime to truncate outgoing streams; 2) Streaming-proxy's connection handoff sometimes dropped final chunk+EOS during hot-pool to cold-pool switch; 3) Clients aggressively retrying due to missing EOS resulted in duplicate token accounting in billing pipeline. Findings: 1) Reproduced mismatch by bringing worker containers with different runtime kernel flags into the same pool; prefix length metadata was not normalized across kernel variants. 2) Streaming-proxy handshake had a race where the proxy would acknowledge upstream ready while downstream socket still flushing; this produced truncated client-side streams. 3) Billing pipeline counted resumed sessions as new sessions in a narrow window because session_id rewrite occurred during proxy handoff. Related PRs and configuration diffs captured in linked GitHub PRs. actions_taken: - Immediate: Rolled back prefix-cache invalidation
title: Nimbus Retail — post-failover streaming truncation and unexpected invoice spike

Streaming resumed to baseline and the customer was provided with a billing suppression for the anomalous window. Root cause: a combination of prefix-cache metadata mismatch across kernel variants + a proxy handoff race that caused missing EOS leading to client retries and duplicate billing. text: description: Issue summary: A recurring microburst of internal probe traffic (origin: sidecar health prober) was observed in LexaHealth's dedicated pool in us-west-2. The bursts correlate with a sustained p50 latency ceiling and signs of soft GPU depletion (increased attach backoffs and transient allocation failures), while raw customer traffic appears within normal bounds. Impact: - Customer reports sustained p50 increase (~1.6x) for generation routes pinned to dedicated pool during burst windows. - Throughput drop on batch-heavy routes (observed local p50 ceiling and reduced concurrent slots). - Intermittent 5xx/attach timeouts observed by customer (rate ~0.4% -> 1.6%) during windows. Environment: - Dedicated pool: LexaHealth (dedicated, reserved cluster in prod:us-west-2) - Models affected: redwood-13b-8k (pinned) Steps to reproduce / observed pattern: 1) Steady production traffic with routine background jobs. 2) At ~09:20 UTC recurring internal probe sweep from sidecar fleet begins (short, high-QPS bursts lasting ~20-40s). 3) Autoscaler telemetry shows rapid small spikes; however scaling decisions do not trigger fast enough because the probe traffic is tagged as 'health' and filtered by the weighting logic (so it doesn't count towards queue-backed scaling thresholds). 4) Despite not contributing to scaled capacity, the probe bursts still consume scheduler/attach slots and create socket churn, leading to attach latency increases and transient GPU soft depletion. Observed vs
title: Hidden prober burst causing autoscaler underreaction, soft GPU depletion and sustained p50 ceiling

text: Despite not contributing to scaled capacity, the probe bursts still consume scheduler/attach slots and create socket churn, leading to attach latency increases and transient GPU soft depletion. Observed vs expected behavior: - Observed: p50 latency plateaued at ~120ms (baseline ~75ms) for ~3 hours correlated with repeated probe bursts. Attach latency increased from median 80ms -> 240ms in affected window. - Expected: Prober traffic should be either rate-limited or accounted-for in autoscaler/slot logic to avoid causing attach/slot contention for tenant workloads. Logs/metrics/dashboards (examples): - Grafana: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/dedicated/lexahealth-pool?from=2026-03-13T08:00:00Z&to=2026-03-13T12:00:00Z - Attach traces (sample): https://sentry.redwood.ai/issue/lexa-attach/2026-03-13 - Autoscaler event log: https://logs.redwood.ai/query?job=autoscaler&pool=lexahealth&time=2026-03-13T09:00 Initial workaround applied: - Temporarily rate-limited sidecar prober traffic at the edge for LexaHealth's pool (applied a 5x reduction to prober QPS). - Manually evicted two low-priority background replicas to free GPU slots while we investigate. Requester note: - LexaHealth engineering flagged this at 2026-03-13 10:05 UTC after noticing throughput ceiling during a scheduled analytics job. They requested blocking the probe bursts and a short-term capacity bump. comments: 2026-03-13 10:12 - Support (Maya Chen): Acknowledged. Gathering initial metrics and checking autoscaler logs. Added
title: Hidden prober burst causing autoscaler underreaction, soft GPU depletion and sustained p50 ceiling

text: job. They requested blocking the probe bursts and a short-term capacity bump. comments: 2026-03-13 10:12 - Support (Maya Chen): Acknowledged. Gathering initial metrics and checking autoscaler logs. Added priority P1 and assigned to infra oncall. 2026-03-13 11:00 - Oncall Eng (Rafael Ortega): Confirmed correlation between probe bursts and attach latency. Temporarily throttled prober at edge. Observed immediate partial improvement in p50. Continuing investigation. 2026-03-13 13:30 - Customer (LexaHealth - Priya Rao): Thanks — seeing p50 drop but still intermittent timeouts during the hourly analytics sweep. Can you confirm whether this will reoccur at the next sweep? 2026-03-14 09:15 - Eng (Rafael Ortega): Deployed autoscaler filter update in staging to exclude tagged 'probe' traffic from scheduling backoff heuristics and started a gradual rollout. Scheduling a follow-up hotfix for sidecar to add jitter and built-in rate-limit. investigation_notes: Summary of findings: - Timeline: probe burst windows line up with the customer's observed p50 plateau and attach backoff spikes. - Metrics: attach success rate dipped from 99.2% -> 96.4% during bursts; attach latency median 80ms -> 240ms; small but sustained increase in gpu_allocation_backoff_count (0.8% -> 3.6%). - Autoscaler logic: weighting/filtering treated probes as "ignored" for scale-up thresholds which meant the autoscaler did not react to the sudden ephemeral load at the queue layer, but probes still consumed scheduling and
title: Hidden prober burst causing autoscaler underreaction, soft GPU depletion and sustained p50 ceiling

text: weighting/filtering treated probes as "ignored" for scale-up thresholds which meant the autoscaler did not react to the sudden ephemeral load at the queue layer, but probes still consumed scheduling and attach resources (socket+CPU). - Root cause hypothesis: unthrottled sidecar prober fanout across many small worker replicas generates short-lived connection spikes. Those spikes cause scheduler attach contention and socket churn which in turn increases per-tenant attach latency and leads to soft GPU depletion (slots busy while autoscaler remains under-reactive). - Supporting evidence: captured tcp socket churn in node-exporter logs, spikes in sidecar connection counts, and matching timestamps between probe bursts and attach error spikes. - Repro: synthetic prober flood reproduced similar attach-latency pattern in a staging copy of the LexaHealth pool when simulated at production scale. Next steps taken during investigation: - Throttled prober at edge for LexaHealth (temporary mitigation). - Evicted two background replicas to relieve slot pressure immediately. - Created a staging rollout for autoscaler filter change (exclude 'probe' tag from backoff heuristics and ensure scaling decisions consider resource usage vs. queue-backed traffic separately). - Opened infra PR to add jitter and per-pool rate-limits to the sidecar prober (see related_github_prs). resolution_notes: Immediate remediation (completed 2026-03-14): - Edge throttling applied to LexaHealth pool (reduced probe QPS
title: Hidden prober burst causing autoscaler underreaction, soft GPU depletion and sustained p50 ceiling

text: and per-pool rate-limits to the sidecar prober (see related_github_prs). resolution_notes: Immediate remediation (completed 2026-03-14): - Edge throttling applied to LexaHealth pool (reduced probe QPS by 80%) — p50 returned within 10-20% of baseline after throttling. - Autoscaler filter change merged to staging to ensure probe traffic does not suppress necessary scale-up signals while still being accounted for in slot contention analysis. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/4821 - Applied a short-term allocation of +2 GPUs to LexaHealth's dedicated pool (temporary bump) to prevent recurrence during scheduled analytics runs. Planned follow-ups (post-incident): - Permanent sidecar change to add randomized jitter and per-pool QPS caps for health probes (owner: Sidecar team). ETA: 2026-03-21. - Extend autoscaler heuristics to account for tagged probe traffic in scheduler-slot accounting (owner: Autoscaler team). ETA: 2026-03-18. - Update Dedicated Runbook to include "probe flood" scenario and specific mitigation steps; add runbook playbook entry to Confluence. - Postmortem and RCA to be published to Confluence and linked from this ticket (scheduled meeting 2026-03-18 16:00 UTC). Workaround notes for CS: - If customer reports recurrence: escalate to infra oncall and enable per-pool prober throttle (edge switch) while we enact the permanent fixes. Status: mitigated; root-cause work scheduled and tracking in linked PRs.
title: Hidden prober burst causing autoscaler underreaction, soft GPU depletion and sustained p50 ceiling

text: description: Issue summary: During a staged promote of redwood-rw-13b-v1 to 40% canary weight for ArcLens dedicated cluster, a subset of long-lived sessions lost expected version affinity and were routed to fallback model variants, causing degraded responses and increased latency. Impact: ~6% of user sessions for ArcLens produced responses from a fallback quantized variant or returned transient 502s for 3 minutes. Customers reported content mismatches and slower response times. Timeline: 2026-02-25 14:12 UTC - Canary weight change pushed from 10% to 40%. 14:15 UTC - First customer report to #support. 14:20 UTC - Automated alert: spike in 5xxs and tail latency on us-east serving fleet. 14:22 UTC - Support created ticket and engaged SRE. Environment: ArcLens dedicated pool (customer-owned VPC peering), serving-runtime v3.8.1, routing-reconciler v2.14. Observed behavior: A portion of sessions that had previously been pinned to the stable model lost their session affinity when requests hit edge proxies that had stale session-token maps. The routing layer then applied fallback policies (model-family fallback and cost-tier fallback) and routed requests to the quantized variant; some requests hit a transient 502 from a GPU node being drained. Expected behavior: Session affinity should persist for pinned sessions during staged promotes; fallbacks should not be invoked for pinned sessions unless explicit health checks fail. Customer impact: ArcLens reported incorrect predictive scores
title: Ephemeral session-affinity mismatch triggers fallback routing during staged canary promote

text: persist for pinned sessions during staged promotes; fallbacks should not be invoked for pinned sessions unless explicit health checks fail. Customer impact: ArcLens reported incorrect predictive scores for some cohorts and elevated error rates affecting visible dashboards. Temporary workaround: Rolled back canary weight to 10% and forced session-affinity reconciliation for ArcLens tenant via admin API to rebind sessions to pinned version. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Have a dedicated customer pool with session pinning enabled and long-lived conversations (session TTL > 10m). 2) Start traffic that establishes session-affinity tokens and pins to redwood-rw-13b-v1 stable variant. 3) Promote a canary by increasing canary weight from 10% to 40% during normal traffic. 4) Observe routing logs and traces for session-token lookup misses at the edge and subsequent fallback routing decisions. logs: 2026-02-25T14:16:12Z router-edge-01 WARN session_lookup miss tenant=arclens session_token=tok_3f2a 2026-02-25T14:16:12Z reconciler INFO routing_decision tenant=arclens request_id=rid_9b2 model=redwood-rw-13b-quantized-v1 reason=fallback_cost_tier 2026-02-25T14:17:03Z gpu-node-22 ERROR request_id=rid_9b2 502 upstream_read_timeout draining=true Trace link: https://observability.redwood.ai/trace/trace-abcdef123456 comments: Support (Maya Chen) 2026-02-25T14:20: We received a customer case reporting mismatch between expected stable model outputs and what their app returned after the canary increase. Escalating
title: Ephemeral session-affinity mismatch triggers fallback routing during staged canary promote
