Escalating to SRE and routing owners. SRE (Diego Ramos) 2026-02-25T14:28: Observed session_lookup misses on a subset of edge proxies. Hypothesis: session-token map TTL and concurrent routing snapshot update caused transient map divergence during rollout. Rolling canary back to 10% and triggering manual session rebind now. Customer (ArcLens, Emily Park) 2026-02-25T14:35: Thanks — we saw errors clear up after rollback. Please advise on root cause and prevention. Routing eng (Liam O'Connor) 2026-02-25T15:04: Reproducer identified: concurrent reconcile job bumping routing snapshot while edge cache invalidation still in-flight. Added short-term fix to serialize reconcile commits per-tenant and pushed hotfix to reconciler (PR #1284). SRE (Diego Ramos) 2026-02-27T09:12: Post-fix monitoring shows no recurrence on us-east. Will run a focused chaos test for edge cache eviction patterns overnight. investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: model pin eviction due to canary promotion race with snapshot commit. Evidence: router-edge logs show session_lookup misses correlated with reconcile commit timestamps. Detailed trace: requests with session_token present at client arrived at edge-02 which had not yet applied the new routing snapshot but had a cleared
title: Ephemeral session-affinity mismatch triggers fallback routing during staged canary promote

text: misses correlated with reconcile commit timestamps. Detailed trace: requests with session_token present at client arrived at edge-02 which had not yet applied the new routing snapshot but had a cleared session map; reconciler commit created a short window where tokens were considered stale by the edge cache eviction logic. Reconciler change: prior behavior allowed concurrent snapshot writes for the same tenant leading to brief inconsistent state; hotfix ensures reconcile commits are serialized and includes an atomic session-map swap to avoid token TTL races. Additional contributing factor: a GPU drain initiated nearby caused a handful of 502s which triggered fallback policy; if not for the affinity miss, those requests would have retried to pinned nodes instead of fallback variant. resolution: Applied immediate mitigations: 1) Rolled canary back to 10% for ArcLens at 14:26 UTC; 2) Triggered manual session-affinity reconciliation for the tenant; 3) Deployed reconciler hotfix (PR #1284) which serializes per-tenant snapshot commits and performs atomic session-map swaps; 4) Added short-term safeguard to defer fallback routing for requests that include a valid session-token within a 30s reconciliation window. Post-deploy monitoring for 48h showed no reappearance of the issue. Follow-ups and action items: Create an automated regression test that simulates concurrent reconcile + edge eviction; add telemetry to alert when session_lookup miss rate spikes above 0.5% during rollouts; update

text: description: Issue summary: After rotating the client mTLS certificate used for IdP token binding, FinWave reports that all admin-console and admin API calls return 401/403. Impact: Admin operators cannot manage role bindings or start rollouts. No user-facing generation impact reported yet, but administrative operations are blocked which prevents remediation of other issues. steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer rotates client certificate used for device-bound access tokens in their IdP (Azure AD Enterprise App configured for certificate-bound access). 2) IdP issues access tokens containing 'cnf' claim with x5t#S256 thumbprint referencing the new cert, but some tokens in the customer's environment continue to present the new cert while the auth-proxy validates against an old thumbprint cached locally. 3) Attempts to call the admin-console or admin API return 401 (Unauthorized) or 403 (Forbidden) with token-binding mismatch messages in auth logs. 4) Restarting the customer's admin clients does not reliably fix; a restart of the auth-proxy pod temporarily restores admin access until next cert rotation or cache refresh window. 5) Customer attempted to re-run cert rotation twice and now cannot complete automation because the token validation step fails early in the pipeline. logs: 2026-03-10T09:41:12Z auth-proxy[auth-1] WARN token_validation: token binding mismatch: presented_cert_thumbprint=3f:ab:2c..., token_cnf_claim=9e:dd:45... 2026-03-10T09:42:05Z auth-proxy[auth-1] INFO jwks_fetch:
title: Certificate-bound access token thumbprint mismatch after client cert rotation blocks admin API in private deployment

text: 2026-03-10T09:41:12Z auth-proxy[auth-1] WARN token_validation: token binding mismatch: presented_cert_thumbprint=3f:ab:2c..., token_cnf_claim=9e:dd:45... 2026-03-10T09:42:05Z auth-proxy[auth-1] INFO jwks_fetch: jwks last refreshed 2026-03-09T02:00:00Z 2026-03-10T09:42:07Z console[api] ERROR rbac: admin-lookup failed for subject=service-admin@finwave.example, reason=token-binding-verification-failed Customer-provided IdP token (redacted): {..., "cnf": {"x5t#S256": "9e:dd:45..."}, ...} comments: 2026-03-10 10:02 - Support (Asha): Thanks for the report. Can you confirm whether the IdP rotation changed only the certificate or also the client_id/secret? Also please provide a sample redacted token and the thumbprint of the new cert. 2026-03-10 11:12 - Customer (FinWave, J. Morales): We rotated only the cert. New thumbprint is 9e:dd:45:11:bf:2c. Attached a stripped token and the registration metadata from the enterprise app. 2026-03-10 14:25 - Support (Marcus): We can reproduce token-binding mismatch locally when auth-proxy has stale cert-thumbprint in cache. Tentative workaround: restart auth-proxy pod to force JWKS/cert-binding cache refresh. Will escalate to infra to investigate cache invalidation timing. 2026-03-11 09:03 - Engineering (Diego Ramos): Investigating auth-proxy cache codepath. Found that cert-thumbprint-to-client mapping is read from a long-lived cache (TTL 24h) and is not invalidated on JWKS refresh. Creating a patch to eagerly re-validate 'cnf' thumbprint on JWKS
title: Certificate-bound access token thumbprint mismatch after client cert rotation blocks admin API in private deployment

text: codepath. Found that cert-thumbprint-to-client mapping is read from a long-lived cache (TTL 24h) and is not invalidated on JWKS refresh. Creating a patch to eagerly re-validate 'cnf' thumbprint on JWKS rotation. investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: auth-proxy compares presented cert thumbprint against a cached 'known' thumbprint derived from a previous token exchange and doesn't pick up IdP-side rotation immediately. Reproduction steps executed in staging with FinWave token: reproduced 401 with mismatched thumbprints when cache had stale value. Observations: jwks fetch is occurring independently, but cert-thumbprint mapping is stored in an LRU cache keyed by client_id with TTL 24h. When IdP rotates certs, some callers present certs signed by the new key while cache still holds the old mapping for a day. Auth-proxy log lines show explicit mismatch rather than missing claim, confirming binding validation code is running but against an outdated reference. Workaround validated: restarting auth-proxy forces fresh mapping lookup and resolves the mismatch until TTL would otherwise refresh. resolution: Workaround: Recommend customer restart auth-proxy pod(s) in their private deployment to force immediate refresh of certificate-thumbprint mapping (validated). Short-term fix deployed to customer's staging: reduce cache TTL to 5m pending permanent fix. next_steps: 1) Engineering PR to change auth-proxy cache behavior: on JWKS rotation or explicit JWKS refresh, invalidate cert-thumbprint
title: Certificate-bound access token thumbprint mismatch after client cert rotation blocks admin API in private deployment

text: customer's staging: reduce cache TTL to 5m pending permanent fix. next_steps: 1) Engineering PR to change auth-proxy cache behavior: on JWKS rotation or explicit JWKS refresh, invalidate cert-thumbprint entries for matched client_ids (INT-3912). 2) Add monitoring alert for repeated token-binding-mismatch counts > 5/min (observable rule). 3) Release patch to all private customers in maintenance window; notify affected customers with remediation steps and recommended pre-rotation checks. 4) Follow-up runbook update: include explicit post-cert-rotation validation step and temporary pod restart guidance.
title: Certificate-bound access token thumbprint mismatch after client cert rotation blocks admin API in private deployment

text: description: Issue summary: Customer runs nightly scheduled model warmup jobs + hourly bulk embedding writes to their dedicated pool. Over the last 48 hours they started seeing intermittent 429s during the overlap window where warmups and large uploads run concurrently. Impact: - ~15% of warmup requests receive 429s, resulting in incomplete warmup and elevated latency for first user requests. - A portion of bulk embeddings fail and are retried from the customer side, increasing downstream backlogs and cost. Environment: - Dedicated pool (customer-managed reservation) in prod:us-east-1, pool id dpool-04f2 - Warmup jobs: scheduled cron job, hits /v1/serving/generate with short sequences (max_tokens=16) and concurrency 20 - Bulk writes: parallel uploader with 40 workers doing batching (batch_size=64) against embeddings endpoint /v1/embeddings Steps to reproduce (customer supplied): 1) Trigger warmup job immediately after scheduled bulk run start. 2) Observe 429s in warmup job logs for ~10-20 minutes. 3) If bulk finishes first, warmups usually succeed. Observed behavior: - 429s return "rate_limit_exceeded" and headers show X-RateLimit-Policy: "tenant-token-window-v2" - Throttling appears more common when bulk workers use short lived collateral API keys (rotated per worker) and uploader spikes concurrency. Expected behavior: - Warmups (short low-cost traces) should be able to proceed even during bulk embedding windows, or at a minimum there should be clear documentation and a recommended
title: Rate-limit tuning for scheduled model warmups and bulk writes causing intermittent 429s

text: spikes concurrency. Expected behavior: - Warmups (short low-cost traces) should be able to proceed even during bulk embedding windows, or at a minimum there should be clear documentation and a recommended configuration to avoid this impact. Customer ask: - Guidance on rate-limit settings and recommended separation of traffic classes for warmup vs bulk uploads. - If we can increase the short-window per-key concurrency for warmup endpoints or suggest a config that isolates warmup calls from bulk writes in their dedicated pool. comments: 2026-03-12 10:32 - Riley Nguyen (customer): Submitted logs and a timeline of scheduled jobs. Marking as Triage while we validate risk and schedule a controlled rollout. investigation_notes: Log correlation shows tenant token-window exhaustion: many distinct API keys increment per-key counters but tenant-level window is aggregated, causing earlier-than-expected throttles. Rate limiter behavior: per-key short-window and tenant aggregated window both enforced. High cardinality of keys with concurrent small requests
title: Rate-limit tuning for scheduled model warmups and bulk writes causing intermittent 429s

text: is aggregated, causing earlier-than-expected throttles. Rate limiter behavior: per-key short-window and tenant aggregated window both enforced. High cardinality of keys with concurrent small requests magnifies token consumption due to per-request overhead tracking. Serving runtime metrics indicate warmup requests are low-cost but are counted against the same token budget as embeddings which are heavier and issued in larger batches. Confirmed that the dedicated pool uses default short-window sizing. No custom per-route soft-allowances currently configured for this customer. Risk analysis: increasing tenant-wide window may affect isolation guarantees between customers on shared control plane components; per-route soft-allowance is lower risk for dedicated pool since it can be scoped to the pool id. resolution_notes: Short-term workaround provided: 1) recommend uploader reuse a smaller pool of long-lived keys (reduces cardinality), 2) stagger warmup start by 10 minutes after bulk start, 3) temporarily lower uploader concurrency to 24. Customer reports partial improvement. Medium-term action: engineering to add per-route soft-allowance config for dedicated pools to allow low-cost warmup endpoints a protected token band during scheduled windows. PR https://github.com/redwood-inference/rate-limiter/pull/142 created and under review. Follow-up: SRE to run a canary for the soft-allowance on dpool-04f2 during a maintenance window next week (INT-4021). If canary is green, we will offer
title: Rate-limit tuning for scheduled model warmups and bulk writes causing intermittent 429s

text: description: Issue summary: Northfield Bank (enterprise customer) requests an on-demand evidence bundle suitable for legal escrow that includes: 1) chronologically ordered audit events for a specified user cohort (2026-02-20 to 2026-02-28) with per-record HMAC manifest; 2) a sample OIDC session reconstruction for three anonymized session IDs to support SAR/forensic requests; and 3) a SIEM field glossary mapping (Redwood event fields -> customer SIEM fields) and sample Splunk HEC payload. Impact: Customer requires the artifacts for an imminent regulatory inspection. They will treat missing or incorrectly-mapped fields as non-compliant for the audit window. Business impact: potential delay to inspection and legal escalations. Environment: Production (us-east) tenant: northfield-bank-prod. Export should include audit events from API gateway, auth service, and the serving-runtime correlation IDs. Request details / Acceptance criteria: - Deliver a compressed evidence bundle (tar.gz) containing: ordered JSONL audit events (UTC), an index manifest (per-batch HMAC), and a resume token for each day. - Provide 3 sample OIDC session reconstructions: input events, derived session timeline, and heuristics used to attribute device/User-Agent and IP geolocation. - Provide a SIEM field map spreadsheet (CSV) mapping Redwood fields to target Splunk HEC keys and example HEC JSON event payloads (with pseudonymized identifiers). - Include a short writeup describing redaction rules applied and cryptographic
title: Ad-hoc escrow evidence bundle, OIDC session reconstruction sample and SIEM field glossary for legal review

text: (CSV) mapping Redwood fields to target Splunk HEC keys and example HEC JSON event payloads (with pseudonymized identifiers). - Include a short writeup describing redaction rules applied and cryptographic verification steps. Steps to reproduce (how we will validate): 1) Generate a 48-hour shard for tenant northfield-bank-prod for dates requested; verify chronological ordering and HMAC manifest. 2) Recreate session timelines for three anonymized session IDs provided by the customer. 3) Validate a sample Splunk HEC POST against the customer's ingestion mapping and confirm no PII is exposed beyond allowed pseudonymization. Logs / artifacts requested from the customer: - The three session IDs (customer supplied; already received in secure email). - Confirmation of target SIEM ingestion endpoint and sample index names. Notes: Customer explicitly requested the bundle be suitable for third-party legal review and for the files to include deterministically reproducible checksums. They also asked for the glossary in both CSV and human-readable Markdown. comments: 2026-03-01 10:12 - Aisha Patel: Initial intake - customer emailed session IDs and requested delivery by COB 2026-03-05. text: description: Design and produce a compact set of interactive example cards and annotated diff overlays that will be used in the Embeddings & Rerank quickstart docs. The goal is a digestible, mobile-friendly visual module that demonstrates: 1) building an embedding index, 2) running a naive similarity retrieval, 3) applying rerank to reorder results, and 4) visualizing the rerank score delta (before/after) with callouts for why items moved. Must be small enough for hero placement on the quickstart page and also export as separate assets for the docs and Console walkthrough. tasks: Design 4 compact card templates: Query, Raw Hits (unranked), Rerank Hits (post-rerank), Score Diff overlay Create annotated overlay system that highlights token-level reasons (e.g., 'topic match', 'entity overlap', 'context window') Add interactive slider prototype to simulate rerank weight from 0.0 -> 1.0 (Figma prototype) Produce responsive exports: 1200x600 hero PNG, 600x320 card PNG, SVG for docs with accessible text Coordinate with content writer for copy and microcopy for tooltip/help text Record two short demo GIFs (desktop + mobile) showing rerank transition and score deltas Run accessibility review (color contrast, keyboard nav for prototype) and iterate based on findings Package final assets in Drive and include one-line usage snippets for docs and Console component library decisions: Use a 3-stage visual flow (embed -> retrieve -> rerank) in a single horizontal card to conserve vertical real
title: Compact interactive example cards and annotated diff overlays for embeddings + rerank quickstart

text: description: Problem: High-frequency burst traffic from a small set of tenants occasionally exhausts local quota tokens and triggers hard rejects or cross-region failovers. When the central limiter or its lease-store experiences transient outages, we currently fall back to a conservative deny-all behavior that leads to large SLO violations for affected customers. Goal: implement a resilient multi-tenant burst allocator that gracefully degrades under limiter outages and sustained overloads: - allow short, proportionally-weighted burst absorption using a token escrow backed by per-tenant standing reservations - introduce a soft-eviction policy to preempt low-priority work rather than hard reject high-priority requests - integrate with circuit-breaker signals so allocator behavior automatically widens or tightens depending on downstream health - provide deterministic admission behavior during global limiter outages by supporting a read-only fallback allocator with configurable safety caps - surface telemetry and alerts tied to SLOs and fairness regressions Proposed solution summary: build an in-path Allocator service implemented as a stateless admission layer that consults a fast local lease cache and an eventual central bank for long-term accounting. Key mechanisms: 1) Escrow buffer: small, per-tenant short-term credits (milliseconds-to-seconds) that absorb microbursts. Credits decay and are replenished from the central bank at a bounded rate. 2) Weighted soft-eviction: when capacity
title: Multi-tenant burst allocator with soft eviction playbook

text: small, per-tenant short-term credits (milliseconds-to-seconds) that absorb microbursts. Credits decay and are replenished from the central bank at a bounded rate. 2) Weighted soft-eviction: when capacity is exhausted, the allocator preempts lower-priority requests (background embeddings, low-QoS retries) and returns a 202-driven defer response with retry hints instead of a 429 hard reject. 3) Emergency reserve and preemption order: reserve a configurable emergency pool for tenants tagged as SLA-critical; preemption order derived from tenant weight, recent usage, and emergency flag. 4) Circuit integration: allocator subscribes to regional circuit-breaker state; when downstream latency or error budget breaches occur, allocator shifts to aggressive preemption and tighter caps. 5) Read-only outage fallback: if central bank is unavailable, allocator uses a deterministic, conservative reservation algorithm (last-known quotas with decay) to avoid wide open fail-open. 6) Observability: fine-grained telemetry for per-tenant unfairness score, escrow consumption, preemption events, and fallback activation. 7) Chaos & sim-first rollout: validate in a simulator and run progressive chaos experiments before cluster rollout. acceptance_criteria: Simulator shows <5% increase in total 5xx SLO violations and >40% reduction in hard-429s during modeled microbursts for top-50 tenants Allocator implements escrow buffer with tunable decay and replenishment; end-to-end latency impact <2ms median on admission
title: Multi-tenant burst allocator with soft eviction playbook

text: and >40% reduction in hard-429s during modeled microbursts for top-50 tenants Allocator implements escrow buffer with tunable decay and replenishment; end-to-end latency impact <2ms median on admission path Soft-eviction path returns structured defer responses (202 + Retry-After + advisory token id) and client-visible retry hooks documented Circuit signal integration tested: when regional circuit trips, allocator tightens caps within 5s and metrics reflect transition Fallback read-only behavior activated successfully in a chaos test where central bank is partitioned for 30 minutes; no global hard-fail-open observed Dashboard surfaces per-tenant unfairness score and an alert triggers when score > 0.3 for >10m for top-100 tenants Rollout strategy (canary -> regional ramp -> global) executed with post-rollout SLO checks and automatic rollback on regression progress_notes: 2026-02-10: Scoped initial design with product; prioritized as P0 for Q1 due to repeated customer incidents 2026-02-18: Simulation harness implemented (python) and baseline microburst scenarios added; early runs show current system emits hard-429s in 30-60s windows 2026-02-24: Design review with Eng + SRE + Product. Decision: prefer escrow + soft-eviction over permissive fail-open. Requested clear telemetry contract and backwards-compatible response format 2026-02-28: Prototype Allocator service (Go) merged to feature branch. Local lease cache using mmap-backed store. Added basic weighted preemption ordering 2026-03-01:
title: Multi-tenant burst allocator with soft eviction playbook

Current plan: local with conservative cross-region reconciliation to avoid split-brain credit expansion Testing ask: Product to provide 3 customer traffic traces for realistic burst replay in simulator
title: Multi-tenant burst allocator with soft eviction playbook

text: description: Design a small set of high-contrast, accessibility-first onboarding visual assets for the API/SDK quickstart docs. Deliverables: a modular set of code-card components (light/dark, high-contrast) that clearly show auth flows (API key, OAuth device flow, and service account), a streaming response tile with keyboard-friendly controls and focus states, and a compact tool-calling sequence that can be embedded as three progressive screenshots or an animated GIF. These assets will be used across the quickstart pages and the console first-run experience. Aim for minimal copy, large monospaced code areas, visible success/failure states, and explicit aria/keyboard interaction notes for engineers implementing the UI. Keep file sizes optimized for docs and create PNG/JPEG screenshots plus one optimized animated WebP for the toolcall flow. acceptance_criteria: Three code-card variants produced: API key, OAuth device flow, service account; each in light, dark, and high-contrast palettes Streaming tile with clear play/pause, live token indicator, and keyboard focus outlines; includes 3 annotated states (idle, streaming, error) Tool-calling sequence exported as three static PNGs and one animated WebP under 1MB total Figma file with componentized tokens and exported slices; handoff notes include aria attributes and copy for accessibility Assets uploaded to Drive and referenced in quickstart docs PR; design sign-off from PM and accessibility review completed comments: 2026-03-10 Maya

text: description: Context: We need a unified approach for showing short-term deltas and anomalies on dense time-series charts that is accessible, inexpensive to render on GPU-accelerated canvases, and compatible with Redwood's token system for color/contrast. The current mini-series legends and tooltips struggle with: overlapping change markers, noisy SR output when many annotations exist, and high CPU on mobile when downsampling and computing diffs in JS. Goals: - Design a visual language for "layered differential markers" that conveys per-series and cross-series deltas without adding heavy DOM nodes. - Provide an ARIA-first audible summary pattern (single-line SR brief + optional detailed panel) to avoid verbose SR readouts for each marker. - Define token-compliant color/contrast variants and a compact contrast-pair for delta markers (increase/decrease/steady). - Specify a render-friendly decimation strategy (GPU-friendly series reduction) that offloads as much work as possible to the renderer and avoids per-point JS allocations. Constraints and trade-offs considered: - Must not change existing chart API surface for customers; these are opt-in enhancements on the component side. - Audible summaries should be short (<=2 phrases) with an option to expand into a detailed SR list for power users. - Decimation must preserve visual anomalies (spikes) even when sampling aggressively. We prefer a prioritized-preservation decimator rather than naive averaging. Design deliverables & acceptance
title: Layered differential markers, audible summaries, and GPU-friendly series reduction

text: users. - Decimation must preserve visual anomalies (spikes) even when sampling aggressively. We prefer a prioritized-preservation decimator rather than naive averaging. Design deliverables & acceptance criteria: 1) Visual spec: SVG/Canvas sketches for layered markers at 3 sizes (compact, regular, zoomed). Acceptance: markers are visibly distinct from series strokes at 4:1 contrast and pass AA contrast for both light/dark tokens. 2) Accessibility pattern: ARIA snippet + audible summary copy templates and expanded SR flow. Acceptance: SR summary length <= 200 chars; on test pages, NVDA/VoiceOver read the summary and allow expansion with a single keystroke. 3) Performance guide: recommended decimation algorithm, GPU-friendly geometry batching notes, and expected CPU/token reduction metrics. Acceptance: internal perf harness shows >=30% CPU reduction on 10-series, 60k-point timeline when decimation enabled. 4) Legend/tooltip behavior: hover/focus interaction map that prefers single shared tooltip for dense points and announces marker context succinctly. Acceptance: no more than 1 new DOM node per chart for markers; tooltips must show delta summaries without listing every marker. Non-goals: - Rewriting the renderer. We'll provide patterns for engine teams rather than implementing low-level kernels here. - Changing public API for advanced customers unless explicitly discussed with Platform API team. Artifacts and links: - High-fidelity mockups: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1qExampleLayeredMarkers
title: Layered differential markers, audible summaries, and GPU-friendly series reduction

text: Changing public API for advanced customers unless explicitly discussed with Platform API team. Agreed this will be implemented as an opt-in layer property; no API change required. 2026-02-25: First sketches delivered to Figma; engineering reviewed and requested explicit preservation rules for extreme spikes. 2026-03-02: Accessibility review with SRE/Accessibility team. Recommended an abbreviated summary template and keyboard shortcut to expand details. 2026-03-08: Prototype tested with NVDA and VoiceOver; summary reads correctly but expansion
title: Layered differential markers, audible summaries, and GPU-friendly series reduction

text: description: Summary: Design and prototype a new 'Deployment Arc' storyboard that unifies staged traffic, temporal phasing, and micro-rollback gestures into a single interactive surface inside Redwood Console. The goal is to help operators compose and rehearse complex canaries and A/B exposures (fractional splits, cohort phasing, shadowing) while keeping rollback fast and explainable. Problem: Current rollout UIs are fragmentary: different pages for traffic split, canary steps, and emergency rollback. Operators repeatedly open dashboards, run external sims, and then return to the console to patch traffic rules. This creates friction during incidents and increases time-to-rollback. Goals: - Provide a single visual surface (storyboard) where users can author staged exposures across time and cohorts, preview traffic allocation curves, and attach safety assertions. - Make emergency rollback a first-class, single-click action from any storyboard state with clear rationale and telemetry links. - Surface token-cost implications of exposure trajectories so PMs and finance can decide tradeoffs during rollouts. - Enable compact gestures for on-the-fly micro-adjustments (drag handles, nudge keys) and an undo/redo timeline. Non-goals: - Not shipping full multi-tenant RBAC or billing export in this ticket. Those are dependent features handled in separate ENG tickets. Key UX concepts in prototype: - Arc canvas: time on X, audience fraction on Y, with layered cohort lanes. - Snapshots & playhead:
title: Deployment Arc choreographer: interactive storyboard for staged traffic & micro-rollback

text: ticket. Those are dependent features handled in separate ENG tickets. Key UX concepts in prototype: - Arc canvas: time on X, audience fraction on Y, with layered cohort lanes. - Snapshots & playhead: capture named snapshots; scrub to see system state and expected traffic distribution at a point in time. - Safety assertions: attach checks (error rate, latency, cost per token) with pass/fail thresholds that can pause a rollout. - Emergency snapback: a modal with three rollback options (soft-fade, hard-snap, route-to-fallback-model) and an explainability pane summarizing metric deltas and recent traces. - Cost overlay: live projected token spend per hour visualized on the arc. Toggle by model variant and quantization profile. - Micro-gesture palette: drag handles for cohort curves; alt-drag for fine-grain nudges; keyboard shortcuts for ±1% and ±5% increments. - Runbook binding: attach a short runbook or link a playbook for each snapshot so operators have immediate remediation steps. Design trade-offs considered: - Visual density vs. clarity: we chose a layered lane approach (less compact than stacked small-multiples) to keep rollback reasoning obvious. - Cost inside the same surface vs. separate panel: integrated cost overlay (muted) to reduce context switches, with an expand-to-panel affordance for deeper inspection. - Safety automation vs. manual control: prototype defaults to manual progression with optional auto-pause when an assertion fails (opt-in). Acceptance criteria: -

text: description: Goal: introduce a small, pragmatic token update that 1) rebalances semantic color anchors using perceptual contrast-sculpting (improve legibility across dense UIs), 2) replace fixed spacing tokens with a spacing-proportion system (ratio-based spacing tokens to reduce duplication), 3) provide a compact radius scale tuned for gesture targets, and 4) unify elevation presets with clearer penumbra/ambient separation for charts and tables. This work must preserve existing component behavior where possible, give clear migration paths, and include accessibility regression checks (WCAG AA for text, CCC where applicable). acceptance_criteria: Design tokens exported to tokens/redwood-v2.json and tokens/redwood-v2-compact.json and checked into the design-tokens repo with a changelog All primary button variants render with updated semantic colors and pass 4.5:1 contrast at selected sizes; secondary and tertiary variants have documented fallbacks Table row density presets updated to use spacing-proportion tokens; charts use elevation presets with new ambient/penumbra values applied to legends and overlays Figma components and token library updated; figma file link present in attachments; dev token mapping document created Migration guide created with automated codemods (where feasible) and an engineer-verified rollout checklist No production regressions reported on canary routes after one week of monitoring; metrics dashboard created to track token-related visual regressions comments:

text: description: Goal: Design a compact, scannable trace drilldown component (Sequence Detective Lens) that surfaces the most relevant spans, correlation IDs, and anomalous timing without forcing users into a full-page waterfall. This lens will be used inside the Console request list, the per-route dashboard, and a compact floating inspector during rollouts. It must be readable at a glance, support fast filtering by correlation ID and span attributes, and allow lightweight context expansion (peek into full span details). Motivation: Platform customers need a faster way to triage noisy services and flaky requests. Full waterfall views are powerful but expensive to load. The lens should keep latency low, reveal cost/latency hotspots, and provide direct links to full trace playback or the request forensics workspace. acceptance_criteria: Lens renders within 200ms when embedded in a route dashboard (mock data acceptable for prototype) Shows top 6 spans by self-time with visual relative width bars and explicit duration values Supports search by correlation ID and quick filter chips (error, cold-start, cache-miss) Clicking a span opens a flyout with full metadata (keys: span_id, parent_id, service, op, start, duration, tags) Provides an action row: jump-to-full-trace, open-request, create-issue (pre-populated with trace link) Compact mode collapses details to a single-line summary per request for dense lists design_links: https://www.figma.com/file/xxxxx/sequence-detective-lens?node-id=0%3A1
title: Sequence Detective Lens — compact trace sifting UI for request drilldowns

text: description: Problem: The current Console navigation mixes global product surfaces (dashboards, rollouts, billing) with workspace-level routes, causing cognitive load for multi-workspace users and hiding cost signals that are important during rollouts. Goal: Propose a new information architecture (IA) and sidebar pattern called the "Beacon Switchboard" that: 1) surfaces workspace context clearly, 2) exposes lightweight cost lenses and rollout controls without modal hopping, and 3) preserves fast access to per-route observability (latency/cost/error) for owners. Scope: IA + sidebar composition, primary/secondary routing rules, workspace switcher microflow, compact cost lens UI (token and embedding breakdown), rollout quick-actions (canary, promote, rollback), accessibility guidelines, and a mid-fidelity prototype for validation. Not in scope: backend permission model changes (tracked separately in ENG-4210) and Dedicated/private deployment network settings. acceptance_criteria: 1) Mid-fidelity prototype clickable for the primary flows: switch workspace, open cost lens, open rollout quick-actions, and drill into per-route metrics. 2) Usability benchmark: median success task completion >= 90% and median time-on-task reduced by 30% vs baseline for switching and locating cost info. 3) Engineering checklist with known constraints and fallback behaviors documented (token refresh, websocket handoff, route aliasing). 4) Accessibility pass: keyboard navigation + screen reader labels for
title: Console Beacon Switchboard — information architecture proposal for nav, workspace switching, and cost lenses

text: Engineering checklist with known constraints and fallback behaviors documented (token refresh, websocket handoff, route aliasing). 4) Accessibility pass: keyboard navigation + screen reader labels for workspace list and cost lenses. design_notes: Core concepts: Beacon (persistent contextual indicator at top-left showing workspace + quick cost snapshot), Switchboard (collapsible rail with primary surfaces and contextual plugs for rollouts/dashboards), and Lenses (compact overlays for cost/latency that persist across routes). - Beacon: shows workspace name, role badge, and two micro-metrics (30d cost est, 1h P90 latency) with tooltips. Tapping expands workspace actions. - Switchboard: prioritized items (Overview, Models, Routes, Rollouts, Metrics, Settings) with contextual badges (e.g., active canary). Secondary items collapse under a "More" affordance. - Workspace switcher: combined search + recent + pinned; switching uses a soft-handoff to avoid losing open editors (prompt if unsaved changes). We propose a deferred switch (switch preview) so users can open another workspace in a new tab. - Cost Lenses: quick token/embedding cost breakdown, estimated per-route spend, and a suggested optimization tip (e.g., enable prefix caching, reduce max_tokens) with an action to create an Optimize experiment. comments: 2026-02-23 — Sofie Nguyen: Initial IA sketch completed. Shared with PM and Eng for feasibility call. Focused on minimizing clicks for rollout operations. 2026-02-26 — Miguel
title: Console Beacon Switchboard — information architecture proposal for nav, workspace switching, and cost lenses

text: description: Summary: After the 2026-02-11 capacity incident we observed recurring evictions and elevated P99 latencies caused by tenants that rapidly consumed burst tokens and drained node reservations. This ticket implements a lightweight "reservation deficit" detector that continuously estimates when a tenant's effective usage exceeds its reservation over a short rolling window and applies a graded throttling ramp instead of immediate hard ejection. Goals: reduce noisy-neighbor-driven evictions, preserve tail latency for stable tenants, and provide transparent signal for operators to decide reservation reconciliation or customer outreach. background: Postmortem link: https://confluence.redwood.ai/incidents/dedicated-2026-02-11. Observed patterns: (1) tenants that do many short high-token bursts cause reservation sinks when KV cache growth + long sequences combine with batching spikes; (2) hard ejection causes cascading rebalancing and higher churn; (3) the current burst tokenbucket is binary for soft vs hard limits and lacks progressive backpressure. Similar mitigations in prior tickets reduced frequency but not variance. This detector is intended to be minimally invasive and reversible. acceptance_criteria: Detector emits a time-series metric 'tenant_reservation_deficit' with clear threshold labels (ok/warning/critical) Throttle ramp applies per-tenant rate limits in 3 grades: warning (small slowdown), restrict (50% token allowance), hard-throttle (queue/deny) and logs rationale
title: Reservation deficit detector and graded throttling ramp for noisy tenants

title: Reservation deficit detector and graded throttling ramp for noisy tenants

text: deficit counter (tokens_used - nominal_reservation) clipped to recent max Hysteresis: require deficit above warning_threshold for 2 consecutive windows before escalating; require 4 consecutive windows to reach hard-throttle to avoid oscillation Interaction with autoscaler: detector should not trigger during controlled autoscale events; mark autoscale windows as exempt KV cache interactions: we add a KV cache growth factor to the reservation baseline for tenants with known long-seq workloads to reduce false positives Operational visibility: every throttle event must include tenant_id, reason, deficit_value, window_seconds, and active_reservation tradeoffs_and_risks: False positives: overly aggressive thresholds could throttle healthy tenants. Mitigation: staged rollout with increased logging and honey-tenant tests Complexity vs benefit: keeps allocator simpler by not reallocating reservations automatically, requiring follow-up tickets for auto-reconciliation Customer experience: graded throttling introduces some 429s which may surface to clients; we will coordinate with account teams for high-value customers Performance: detector adds small CPU/ memory overhead in allocator; limit sampling to tenants with >X qps to reduce cost rollout_plan: Feature gated behind runtime flag 'reserv_deficit_detector' Canary: enable on 2 staging dedicated clusters with synthetic traffic and one small production shard (low-risk customers) Calibration: run 72h soak, adjust thresholds to maintain <1%
title: Reservation deficit detector and graded throttling ramp for noisy tenants

text: description: Objective: Rework the Console information architecture to make key flows—dashboards, rollouts, and cost views—discoverable within two clicks while preserving low-latency access to workspace context. Proposal: a cardinal navigation grid that groups primary surfaces into four cardinal zones (Overview, Workspaces, Models, Operations) with a lightweight contextual micro-rail for in-place workspace switching. This ticket covers high-fidelity prototypes, heuristic reviews, and a handoff package for engineering to implement the grid and micro-rail transitions. acceptance_criteria: Primary surfaces reachable within two interactions from any Console entry point Workspace switch completes in <150ms perceived transition (prototype target) and preserves route-scoped filters Cost and rollout panels surface top-line metrics in the Overview quadrant with direct deep-links to per-route breakdowns Design handoff includes responsive states for collapsed/expanded rails and accessible keyboard navigation (tab order documented) Stakeholder sign-off from PM, Eng (runtime), and Accessibility review recorded in comments decisions: Adopt quadrant-first layout rather than a single monolithic left rail to surface cross-cutting metrics Keep workspace switcher as context-sensitive micro-rail (flyout on hover/click) instead of full-screen modal to preserve continuity Expose route-level deep-links in the Overview quadrant to enable direct sharing of rollout and cost views Defer multi-tenant workspace
title: Console cardinal navigation grid: reduce surface friction for dashboards & workspace switching

text: description: Design an alternative navigation pattern ("Workspace Conductor") that decouples global workspace switching from context rail state and introduces a lateral ‘crosswalk’ for fast hops between workspaces, plus a compact railmap overlay for quick route preview. Outcome should improve discoverability of rollouts and per-workspace cost lenses while preserving quick access to compute region and model routing controls. acceptance_criteria: Prototype demonstrates workspace-to-workspace switch in <= 500ms perceptual flow Crosswalk supports pinning two workspaces for one-click toggle Railmap overlay surfaces per-route cost delta (relative %) and recent latency SLOs Interactions documented with motion specs and accessibility keyboard flow Handoff tokens (components, spacing, colors) exported for implementation and engineering review accepted design_prototypes: Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD/Workspace-Conductor-Crosswalk?node-id=0%3A1 InVision flow: https://invis.io/REDWOOD-WORKSPACE-CONDUCTOR-2025 Clickable prototype (device agnostic): https://redwood-protos.company/works/conductor/crosswalk links: PRD: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/CONS/Workspace+Conductor+PRD Engineering RFC: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rfcs/pull/489 Research notes: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1workspace-conductor-research comments: 2025-01-23 - Priya Nair: Initial draft sketches uploaded. text: description: Context: Platform teams want a low-friction way to split traffic across multiple model variants while keeping a tight safety envelope. Current Canvas-style rollout UIs at Redwood are powerful but assume linear staged ramps. This ticket explores a cohort-centric composer that treats audiences as independent slices (geo, customer-tier, header, percentage) and enables rapid quarantine and rollback of individual cohorts without impacting others. Problem: Engineers and PMs need to perform targeted experiments (A/B and multi-variant) with the option to rapidly isolate problematic cohorts. Existing flows force either global rollback or complex manual edits. We need a visual composer that: 1) makes cohort definitions clear, 2) shows per-cohort health signals and relative cost impact, 3) supports quick quarantine/undo, and 4) provides predictable guardrails for automated ramps. Design goals: - Make cohort creation fast: create, preview, and save in under 90s for a common use case. - Surfaced observability: per-cohort latency, error-rate, token-cost-per-request, and sample outputs. - Fail-safe actions: quarantine variant, swap to baseline, and time-limited holds with reason capture. - Support multi-variant splits (up to 4 variants) and mixed routing (percent + attribute filters). - Handoff-ready assets: component spec, interaction doc, and Figma prototype links. Constraints & assumptions: - Backend will provide per-cohort metrics and an emergency toggle API (spiked with ENG-7428).
title: Cohort Slicer: Guided Multi-Variant Split Composer and Emergency Quarantine Prototype

text: Handoff-ready assets: component spec, interaction doc, and Figma prototype links. Constraints & assumptions: - Backend will provide per-cohort metrics and an emergency toggle API (spiked with ENG-7428). - Designer to assume a max of 4 cohorts visible at once; overflow uses accordion. - Accessibility: color-independent status indicators required. Deliverables: - Low-fidelity flow diagram and interaction map. - Hi-fidelity Figma prototype with 3 key screens: composer, cohort detail flyout, and emergency quarantine modal. - Component inventory (split slider, cohort chips, signal badges, quick-rollback CTA). acceptance_criteria: Hi-fidelity Figma prototype completed and linked Designer notes include component props and keyboard interactions Usability test: 5 internal engineers complete 'create split and quarantine' task with success rate >= 80% Design review sign-off from PM, Eng Lead, and Product Research Hand-off package uploaded to Drive including annotated flows and exportable tokens decision_log: 2026-02-14 | Decided to surface three signal tiers: green/yellow/red with numeric badges, and include textual status for color-blind accessibility. 2026-02-19 | Opted for cohort-first composer instead of timeline-first to simplify multi-variant experiments. 2026-03-01 | Chose 'swap to baseline' as the emergency default behavior to minimize customer-visible regressions. 2026-03-05 | Bounded visible cohorts to 4; extra cohorts collapse into an accordion to reduce visual complexity. usability_notes:

Background: current elevation matrix is coarse and doesn't provide subtle layering for inner table rows, dropdowns, and floating chart tooltips. text: description: Problem statement: Requests to Redwood-hosted models experience uneven latency and tail spikes when traffic shifts between regions or when an edge region temporarily loses capacity. Existing solutions either perform hard failover (fast but disruptive) or simple latency hedging (less disruptive but expensive). We need a policy that preserves locality and SLOs while providing graceful degradation when capacity constraints occur, minimizing user-visible errors and token-cost blowups. goals: 1) Keep >=99.9% p95 latency SLO for high-tier customers by preferring local regions when healthy. 2) Limit cross-region egress and cold-starts to avoid cost/latency regressions. 3) Provide deterministic, observable behavior when regions become constrained: soft throttling, credit-driven pin eviction, and controlled failover. 4) Integrate with circuit breakers/capacity signals and Console dashboards. success_criteria: 1) New policy maintains p95 latency <= SLO in chaos test across 3 regions for 95% of runs. 2) Cross-region egress increase <= 10% under typical load bursts. 3) No customer-facing 5xx increases beyond 0.05% during failover windows. 4) On-call runbook reduces mean restore time by enabling automated credit-based reroute decisions. proposal: Introduce a Capacity-Affinity Credits policy: each region allocates per-customer affinity credits based on historical locality and current capacity. Credits represent short-term routing preference. When a request arrives, the router
title: Capacity-affinity credits and graceful cross-region throttling

text: Credits policy: each region allocates per-customer affinity credits based on historical locality and current capacity. Credits represent short-term routing preference. When a request arrives, the router computes a score = latency_score * affinity_credit_multiplier * capacity_factor. The router prefers endpoints with highest score. If capacity signals dip below thresholds, we trigger soft circuit breaker modes: (a) Slow-path degrade: reduce max-concurrency and enable prefix caching for that customer. (b) Credit drain: begin consuming affinity credits to steer traffic away gradually. (c) Controlled failover: once credits exhausted and capacity still low, incrementally shift a window of requests to secondary regions using hedged requests with progressively larger timeouts. design_decisions: 1) Use existing region capacity probes (Agent-probe) as capacity_factor input; smooth with exponential moving average over 30s. 2) Affinity credits are per-customer, bucketed by route and model family. Credits refill at a tunable rate (default 5 credits/min). 3) Latency_score uses normalized recent p95 latency (lower latency => higher score). 4) Circuit breaker has three states: CLOSED (normal), SOFT_OPEN (rate-limited, prioritizes cached or low-cost variants), OPEN (reject or fast-fail with fallback model). 5) All decisions are deterministic and logged with decision_reason tags for traceability. rollout_plan: Phase 0: Simulation using traffic replay + offline scoring (2 weeks). Phase 1: Canary
title: Capacity-affinity credits and graceful cross-region throttling

text: fallback model). 5) All decisions are deterministic and logged with decision_reason tags for traceability. rollout_plan: Phase 0: Simulation using traffic replay + offline scoring (2 weeks). Phase 1: Canary in us-west-2 for internal tenants (1 week). Phase 2: Limited customer rollout (5% of production) with observability flags (2 weeks). Phase 3: Gradual ramp to global by region pairs (4 weeks). Each phase gated by SLO metrics and credit burn rate limits. monitoring: Dashboards to add: per-customer affinity credit balance over time, per-region capacity factor, routing decision distribution, cross-region egress delta, soft-circuit-breaker activation rate, and customer p95 before/after reroute. Alerting: high credit burn rate (>50% of budget/min sustained 2 min), sustained capacity_factor <0.4 for >30s, cross-region egress increase >15% above baseline. risks: Mis-tuned credit refill leads to oscillation between regions. Affordability risk: too many hedged requests increasing token costs. Edge-case: small customers with limited historical locality may be over-evicted. Observability gaps could make debugging routing-driven regressions slow. mitigations: Start with conservative credit drain rates, require manual approval to increase hedging window, implement emergency kill-switch per customer/region in Console, and add synthetic tests exercising credit exhaustion scenarios. testing_plan: 1) Unit tests for scoring and state transitions. 2) Integration test: two-region cluster simulation
title: Capacity-affinity credits and graceful cross-region throttling

text: customer/region in Console, and add synthetic tests exercising credit exhaustion scenarios. testing_plan: 1) Unit tests for scoring and state transitions. 2) Integration test: two-region cluster simulation with controllable capacity injection and traffic replay. 3) Chaos test: induce capacity drops and verify soft-open behavior. 4) Cost analysis: simulate hedging rates and project token egress delta. notes: This design tries to trade small, predictable cross-region traffic for stable latency SLOs instead of large-scale bulk failovers. It also gives product teams knobs to tune locality vs availability per customer. comments: 2026-02-11 - Aisha Kapoor: Initial problem writeup and proposed credit model. Asked infra for capacity probe stability metrics. 2026-02-15 - Marco Ruiz: Added deterministic scoring formula and state machine for soft circuit breaker. Split implementation into router-core and controller components. 2026-02-20 - Priya Shah (SRE): Reviewed monitoring and requested per-customer cooldown metrics. Flagged need for emergency kill-switch. 2026-02-28 - Marco Ruiz: Simulation results attached showing p95 improvement in 86/100 replays, cross-region egress increased 6% median. Uploaded sim-results CSV. 2026-03-03 - Helena Cho (Product): Approved canary plan for internal tenants; requested stricter cost cap for hedging. 2026-03-05 - Marco Ruiz: Opened PR #2217 for router changes; waiting on infra approval for capacity probe smoothing parameter bump.
title: Capacity-affinity credits and graceful cross-region throttling

text: description: Background: Product teams need a lightweight, exportable ledger that surfaces per-message and per-token cost attribution for mixed workloads (chat, batch generation, embeddings). Existing cost views are high-fidelity dashboards but too heavy for quick audits and downstream chargeback flows. Goal: design a compact ledger view and CSV/JSON export format that makes allocation, attribution, and pricing assumptions explicit, while supporting role-aware exports and sampling to limit data size. Scope: deliver a compact table UI for Console -> Cost -> Microledger; an export button with configurable column presets and privacy-safe sampling; a small inline allocator panel that shows cost splits (prompt/system/function/completion + model/quantization tier). Work includes prototype screens, copy for pricing-preview, export schema, and usability test notes. Users: finance engineer reconciling billing, platform engineer validating rollout impact, customer success doing diagnostics, and internal billing integrations that consume CSVs. Non-goals: not building the backend export pipeline here (engineering will define storage/retention). Not replacing the detailed per-route cost dashboards — this is complementary compact export UX. acceptance_criteria: Compact ledger UI shows collapsible rows with message-level granularity and per-token cost columns (prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, system_tokens, function_tokens) Export flow allows selecting preset columns (compact, full, audit)
title: Compact export + allocator: micro-consumption ledger UX for token-level attribution

text: description: Goal: Reduce oscillatory kernel switching and avoid tail-latency spikes when model execution path should transition between low-latency and high-throughput kernels. We will implement a lightweight online decision smoother that ingests short-window signals (seqlen histogram, KV cache hit ratio, GPU occupancy estimate, sustained SM throughput and device thermal headroom) and produces a stabilized kernel-selection decision with configurable hysteresis and failover actions. Scope and motivation: - Problem: rapid changes in incoming request characteristics (burst of long prefill requests, followed by decode-heavy short steps) combine with device-level perturbations (thermal throttling, transient memory pressure) to produce frequent kernel flips that increase overall tail latency and reduce effective throughput. Existing autotuners explore and then flip; we need operational smoothing and thermal fallbacks to keep SLAs. - We are NOT replacing the kernel-cost model; instead we add an execution-time controller that (a) smooths noisy signals before committing a kernel swap, (b) enforces minimum dwell times and soft rollbacks, and (c) can trigger a safe fallback kernel when device telemetry indicates reduced headroom. Design summary: 1) Inputs (1s sliding windows): local seqlen histogram (buckets), prefill ratio, KV cache hit rate, per-GPU sm_occupancy proxy, mem_bandwidth_saturation proxy, device_temp_delta (recent rise), and recent kernel switch count. 2) Signal normalizers:
title: Runtime kernel handoff smoother and thermal-aware fallback policy

text: histogram (buckets), prefill ratio, KV cache hit rate, per-GPU sm_occupancy proxy, mem_bandwidth_saturation proxy, device_temp_delta (recent rise), and recent kernel switch count. 2) Signal normalizers: convert inputs to unitless scores in [0,1] using running percentiles and short EWMA baselines. 3) Decision function: weighted linear score combining latency-favoring and throughput-favoring components plus a thermal penalty. Tunable weights live in config (canary). 4) Smoothing layer: two-stage filter: (a) EWMA on the decision score with tau configurable per deployment tier, (b) minimum dwell time (configurable, default 1s) plus a hysteresis buffer to prevent flip-flops (require score delta > 0.15 to change kernel). 5) Thermal fallback: if device_temp_delta exceeds threshold OR mem_bandwidth_saturation > 0.9 for >500ms, immediately route new requests to a conservative fallback kernel implementation (lower occupancy but more robust memory usage) and flag for operator alert. 6) Prefetch probe: on suspected workload shifts (prefill ratio rise), schedule a micro-probe (one long-prefill request mirrored to probe path) to validate decision without impacting primary path. Probe results feed the EWMA. Acceptance criteria (measurable): - Canary perf: reduce kernel switches by >= 70% compared to baseline over a 24h mixed workload trace. - Tail latency: p99 latency for decode requests under mixed load improves by >= 15% or stays within 5% of baseline if throughput improves. - Safety: fallback
title: Runtime kernel handoff smoother and thermal-aware fallback policy

text: baseline over a 24h mixed workload trace. - Tail latency: p99 latency for decode requests under mixed load improves by >= 15% or stays within 5% of baseline if throughput improves. - Safety: fallback path triggers must have <0.1% false positives in steady-state traffic. - No user-facing errors introduced; any fallback must preserve correctness of outputs. Instrumentation and dashboards: - Add metrics: decision_score, smoothed_decision_score, current_kernel, kernel_switch_count, device_temp_delta, mem_bandwidth_saturation, probe_success_rate. - Dashboard: runtime/kernel-handoff/v1 Canary dashboard (placeholder: https://perf.redwood.internal/dashboards/kernel-handoff-canary). Rollout plan: 1) Unit tests + microbench harness that replays production request traces (tooling in infra/kernel-probe-harness). 2) Internal canary on staging pool with synthetic mixed workload for 48h. 3) Gradual canary in hosted (5% -> 20% -> 100%) with per-minute health checks and automated rollback on regression. 4) Add to Dedicated safe-mode toggle for customer operators. Tasks (owner): - Design doc: finalize scoring function and default weights (Aisha). Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.internal/pages/kernel-handoff-smoother (draft). - Microbench harness: implement replay + probe injection (Liam). GitHub PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/XXXX - Implement controller + metrics + config flags (Liam). - E2E test: create mixed workload trace and canary script (SRE). - Oncall playbook:
title: Runtime kernel handoff smoother and thermal-aware fallback policy

text: GitHub PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/XXXX - Implement controller + metrics + config flags (Liam). - E2E test: create mixed workload trace and canary script (SRE). - Oncall playbook: list rollback triggers and incident runbook (Ops). Trade-offs and risks: - Smoothing increases inertia; if too aggressive it could delay beneficial kernel swaps and cost throughput. We'll expose tau and hysteresis thresholds to tune per deployment tier. - Additional metrics add cardinality; we'll sample device telemetry at 250ms and only if running in canary mode. - Probe mirroring incurs small extra cost; probes are rate-limited and capped (default 0.5% of requests). Open questions: - Optimal default dwell time per GPU class (A100 vs H100) — need microbench. - Whether to integrate thermal-aware weight auto-tuning into Redwood Optimize later. Notes: This ticket intentionally focuses on runtime control and safety hysteresis rather than long-running offline autotune experiments. It should integrate with existing kernel-cost-model outputs and warm-start policy from the autotuner (no duplication). comments: 2025-02-12 - Aisha: Initial design sketched; will prototype EWMA + min-dwell first. text: description: Several customers (hosted + Dedicated tenants) reported recurring long-context tail latency spikes coincident with multi-hour ingress patterns. Investigation shows scheduler tick timestamps on a subset of nodes drifted relative to the central admission clock, causing periodic backlog accumulation and delayed batch formation for long-context requests. This ticket tracks the analysis, hotfix, rollout, and validation for a portable fix that covers Hosted, Dedicated pools, and Private deployments. incident_summary: Starting 2026-03-02 customers observed 95th/99th token latency increases by 2-5x for runs that included long-context (>=16k) requests interleaved with small short requests. Symptoms: growing queue depth, delayed admission of latency-sensitive small requests once tick alignment slipped. The issue was reproducible in staging by introducing small clock skew on a subset of scheduler nodes. root_cause_analysis: Root cause: A timer drift in the per-instance admission tick (caused by a missed NTP sync during a maintenance window) allowed some nodes to under-report their contribution to the admission reservoir. The admission controller uses tick intervals to age tokens and refill admission budgets; drifted nodes would not release admission fast enough, causing neighboring healthy nodes to absorb unexpected load and creating backlog amplification for long-context requests. Secondary cause: a recent change to the admission smoothing heuristic reduced hysteresis, making
title: Fix scheduler tick drift causing queue accumulation & long-context tail latency

text: neighboring healthy nodes to absorb unexpected load and creating backlog amplification for long-context requests. Secondary cause: a recent change to the admission smoothing heuristic reduced hysteresis, making the system less tolerant to transient tick jitter. mitigation_notes: Short-term mitigations applied: (1) Emergency config rollback of admission smoothing change (PR #14822) to restore hysteresis; (2) forced NTP resync and drift monitoring script deployed to affected pools; (3) temporary routing rule to prefer nodes with healthy tick metrics for latency-sensitive routes. These reduced tail spikes within 30 minutes for the impacted tenants. updates: 2026-03-05 - Maya Patel: Escalation opened after PagerDuty alerts and customer Slack threads. Collected stack traces, admission metrics, and confirmed correlation with tick skew reports from node telemetry. 2026-03-06 - Lucas Rivera: Reproduced behavior in staging by injecting a 120-400ms tick delay on 20% of scheduler replicas. Captured queue depth, admission budget per-node, and per-route latency traces. Created perf profile showing backlog growth at 60s horizon. 2026-03-07 - Lucas Rivera: Drafted hotfix: add tick-drift-detection and local compensated-ageing (PR runtime-14845). Hotfix increases local ageing when drift detected and adds a metric 'tick_drift_seconds' with alert threshold. Requesting review from runtime leads. 2026-03-09 - Runtime team review: Feedback from Priya Shah to tighten unit tests for admission window
title: Fix scheduler tick drift causing queue accumulation & long-context tail latency

text: and adds a metric 'tick_drift_seconds' with alert threshold. Also asked to include config gating and feature-flag for private deployments. 2026-03-11 - Lucas Rivera: Addressed reviews, added e2e simulation harness (github.com/redwood/runtime/sim) inputs to reproduce mixed-length arrival patterns. Updated PR to include opt-in flag 'enable_tick_compensation' default off for Private customers until validated. 2026-03-12 - Ops: Deployed hotfix to canary pool in Hosted with feature flag enabled and routed two escalated customers for validation. Monitored 1h of production traffic: 95th/99th token latency dropped 40% for previously impacted windows. No regressions observed in throughput. 2026-03-14 - Lucas Rivera: Opening this Linear ticket for rollout planning to Dedicated and Private customers. Remaining tasks: finalize telemetry dashboards, add automated repair (auto-resync) for NTP drift, and produce communication notes for affected customers. acceptance_criteria: Hotfix merged and smoke-tested in staging with feature flagged rollout Canary deployment in Hosted shows >=30% reduction in 95/99 token latency for previous incident windows Dedicated and Private rollout plan documented with opt-in gating for Private clusters Monitoring: new dashboards for tick_drift_seconds, per-node admission
title: Fix scheduler tick drift causing queue accumulation & long-context tail latency

text: description: Build a unified Console surface — the Rollout Signal Panel — that lets PMs/Engineers quickly understand the behavioral delta between variant populations during canary and A/B experiments. This work creates an interactive Variant Delta Explorer that shows per-route metric diffs, percentile latency band comparisons, traffic-weighted effect-size estimates, and one-click trace linking into the distributed trace system. The panel should support shadow-traffic overlays and programmatic auto-rollback triggers based on composite signals. acceptance_criteria: Panel exposes per-route metrics for selected rollout: request rate, 50/90/99 latency, error rate, token-consumption rate, and generation quality score Delta view shows side-by-side percentile histograms and a statistically-informed effect-size with confidence intervals Trace linker: user can click any anomalous bucket to open a filtered trace query in the Trace UI with the cohort filter pre-applied Supports shadow traffic overlay so users can compare live production vs shadowed responses on the same chart A/B weight timeline lets users select two snapshots and view drift across chosen metrics with exportable CSV Auto-rollback rule editor in panel allows creating simple policies (thresholds, sustained window) and preview of impacted traffic routes Telemetry is instrumented: all panel queries are logged, and user selections generate reproducible query IDs for incident triage E2E tests validate UI rendering, latency of
title: Rollout Signal Panel & Variant Delta Explorer

text: Context We have multiple Q1 pricing/packaging experiments running and we need consistent, defensible readouts for (a) exec decisions and (b) AE/SE enablement materials. Current charts are inconsistent across decks and don’t reliably communicate cohort definitions, confidence, and gross margin impact. We also need a standard way to annotate when meter changes overlap with experiment windows (pre/post segmentation) so we don’t oversell conclusions. This ticket defines the “standard experiment readout visualization kit” used in: - Exec update: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/company/executive-update-pricing-and-metering-feb-2026.json - Sales readout draft deck: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/pricing-experiment-readout-slide-draft.json - Confluence pricing guidance pages (via screenshots / exports) Goals 1) Standard chart templates for experiment readouts: - Conversion (activation) lift - Retention / repeat usage - ARPU / net revenue per account - Gross margin overlay and/or cost-to-serve overlays - Plan mix / packaging movement (e.g., free → paid → committed) 2) Standard cohort + caveat framing built into the visual system: - Cohort summary chip (eligibility, timeframe, exclusions) - Metering caveat / “meter version” badge (pre/post fix) - Confidence & sample size callouts (where applicable) 3) Fast-to-produce exports that work in: - Slides (Google Slides) - Confluence pages - Optional: Redwood Console internal dashboards screenshots (stylistic alignment, not
title: Standardize pricing experiment readout visualizations (cohorts, confidence, margin overlays)

text: size callouts (where applicable) 3) Fast-to-produce exports that work in: - Slides (Google Slides) - Confluence pages - Optional: Redwood Console internal dashboards screenshots (stylistic alignment, not pixel-perfect) Non-goals - Designing new pricing pages / public marketing pages - Replacing analytics source-of-truth dashboards - Defining metric definitions (owned by PM/Finance: PM-1848) Proposed visualization kit (v1) A) Readout header block (always included) - Experiment name + variant summary - Dates (start/end) + rollout status - Cohort definition chip(s): “New self-serve”, “Existing paid”, “Sales-led excluded”, “EU-only”, etc. - Data health badges: - “Meter version: tokens_v2” / “pre-fix tokens_v1” - “Freshness: T+N hrs” (optional, for internal only) - Links (small) to cohort doc + analysis plan B) Primary KPI tiles (1 slide / page) - 3–5 tiles max with consistent structure: - Metric name - Control value - Variant value - Delta (absolute + relative) - N (accounts) and/or N (requests) depending on metric - Confidence indicator (see section E) C) Lift over time line chart (for conversion/activation) - X: time (day or week) - Y: conversion rate (or activation rate) - Two lines: Control vs Variant - Shaded “meter change window” overlay if applicable (light vertical band) - Annotations for rollout steps (flag ramp) - Footer with exclusions and known confounders (max 2 lines) D) Margin overlay chart (exec-required) Option 1 (preferred): Dual-axis with clear labeling and de-emphasis
title: Standardize pricing experiment readout visualizations (cohorts, confidence, margin overlays)

text: Annotations for rollout steps (flag ramp) - Footer with exclusions and known confounders (max 2 lines) D) Margin overlay chart (exec-required) Option 1 (preferred): Dual-axis with clear labeling and de-emphasis of secondary axis - Bars: net revenue / account (or ARPU) - Line: gross margin % (or cost per 1K tokens) - Grouped by: control/variant, optionally split by “model mix bucket” Option 2: Waterfall (when packaging changes shift multiple levers) - Start: baseline gross margin - Steps: change in revenue rate, change in cache-hit, change in model mix, change in retries/idempotency, etc. - End: net margin E) Confidence visualization rules (align with Sana + FP&A) - Always show N and time window. - If we have proper statistical treatment, show 90%/95% CI: - Use error bars on delta-only charts. - Avoid clutter on time series; use a small “CI available” tag + show CI on delta summary. - If we do not have reliable CI (e.g., heavily confounded), show “Directional” tag and require a caveat note. F) Cohort segmentation small multiples (when needed) - Up to 4 panels: - New vs existing - Self-serve vs sales-led (if included) - High volume vs low volume - US vs EU (only if region pricing differs) - Rule: if a segmentation panel flips the conclusion, it must be called out explicitly in the “Decision” slide. Visual language / style guidelines - Use Redwood internal deck style (neutral backgrounds, high contrast text). - Control = graphite/gray; Variant = Redwood green/teal (avoid “warning
title: Standardize pricing experiment readout visualizations (cohorts, confidence, margin overlays)

text: “Decision” slide. Visual language / style guidelines - Use Redwood internal deck style (neutral backgrounds, high contrast text). - Control = graphite/gray; Variant = Redwood green/teal (avoid “warning red” unless negative outcome specifically called out). - Use one consistent delta color treatment: - Positive = green - Negative = red - Neutral/noise = gray - Meter caveat badge should be visually distinct from confidence badge to prevent conflation. - Margin overlays must label axes clearly and use subtle gridlines. Accessibility - Color contrast must pass for text and key plot elements. - Do not rely on color alone for control vs variant (use line styles or point shapes). Deliverables 1) Figma file with: - Component library (tiles, header chips, badges) - Chart templates (time series, delta bars w/ CI, dual-axis margin overlay, waterfall) - Example slide layouts (Exec summary slide, Detailed KPI slide, Caveats/Decision slide) 2) “How to use” one-pager for PM/Ops (exporting charts for slides and when to choose each chart type) 3) Exportable slide assets (SVG/PNG) + layout guidance Acceptance criteria - Chris (Product Ops) can drop templates into the readout deck with minimal manual formatting (<30 min per experiment section). - Sana signs off that confidence display matches analysis approach (or “Directional” is enforced where CI is not valid). - Rishi signs off that margin overlay is readable and not misleading. - Jordan/Sales enablement confirms the charts are legible in a

text: description: Goal: refresh the system token set to introduce a small set of stable chroma anchors and a proportional typography velocity scale so components behave predictably across breakpoints and density modes. This work combines palette rationalization (anchoring key hues for brand/neutral/interactive states), a breathing-type scale that preserves optical sizes across 14–20px ranges, and companion spacing/radius/elevation adjustments targeted at dense tables and compact widgets. scope: - Create chroma anchors (6 anchors: neutral, brand, success, warning, danger, accent) with accessible deltas for light/dark modes. - Define a type velocity scale (steps that map to responsive breakpoints and compact mode). - Propose spacing and corner presets that align with the new type scale. - Rework minor elevation steps for table rows and floating controls to keep perceived depth minimal in dense layouts. - Deliver token files (JSON/SCSS), Figma token variables, migration instructions, and a migration PR template for component owners. acceptance_criteria: - New chroma anchors committed to tokens repo as v2.3 and referenced in Figma file 'DS/Tokens/ChromaAnchors' URL below. - Body text contrast >= 4.5:1 for default scales; interactive colors meet WCAG 2.1 AA. - Typography velocity scale defined and applied to Button, Input, Table row, and Card components in the component playground. - Spacing/radius presets included and a migration guide with codemods for 80% of core components. - Visual
title: Orchestrate chroma anchors and type velocity for responsive components

text: defined and applied to Button, Input, Table row, and Card components in the component playground. - Spacing/radius presets included and a migration guide with codemods for 80% of core components. - Visual regression run shows no >2% pixel diffs for key screens (Dashboard list, Table heavy, Compact forms). - PRs created for tokens and at least three component updates (buttons, tables, charts) with design sign-off. design_decisions: - Chroma anchors: choose 6 anchors only to limit cognitive complexity and interpolation edge cases. We will generate semantic ramps from anchors rather than hand-crafting every shade. - Typography: use modular scale with smaller ratios (1.125) for density-sensitive interfaces to reduce line-height jumps. - Spacing/Radius: favor proportional presets (e.g., 2,4,8,12) over pixel-exact values to simplify runtime sizing in compact mode. - Elevation: reduce number of elevation layers for dense surfaces to avoid noisy shadows; prefer subtle overlays for hover states. - Tradeoffs: interpolation simplifies maintenance but may produce hue shifts at extremes; we will lock key interactive colors as exceptions where necessary. deliverables: - tokens/v2.3/chroma-anchors.json (tokens repo) - Figma: 'DS/Tokens/ChromaAnchors' and 'DS/Components/Responsive-Playground' - Migration guide: docs/confluence/design/chroma-anchors-migration.md - Codemod: scripts/migrate-spacing-radius-codemod.js (example PR linked) - Component updates: PRs for button, table, chart; playground
title: Orchestrate chroma anchors and type velocity for responsive components

text: description: Design a compact, interactive visualization for tracing the lifecycle of a failed request through Redwood's serving stack. The Debug Path Visualizer shows the sequence of hops (client -> routing -> model shard -> fallback -> retry -> cache hit/miss -> response) with elapsed time, per-hop cost estimate, and a compact payload inspector. The goal is to make it fast for engineers and SREs to identify root cause (latency hotspot, model degradation, misrouted requests, serialization errors) and to support postmortem links and roll-forward actions (replay, force-fallback, pin model version). acceptance_criteria: Clickable path timeline that expands each hop into a detail pane Per-hop metadata: latency, error code, model variant, region, GPU pool id, batch id, cache status Inline cost estimate per hop and cumulative cost for the request Payload inspector modal showing request input, model prompts, and redacted sensitive fields with clear redaction markers Filters: time window, error taxonomy (transport/server/model), region, model family, request size Prototype validated in usability session with at least 5 engineers with >80% task success for diagnosing a synthetic fault decisions: Show aggregated cost only at first design pass; per-node exact dollars added as optional column to reduce clutter Redaction: mark fields as redacted and show structure but not values by default; allow role-based reveal in Private deployments Default view uses timeline orientation (left-to-right)
title: Debug Path Visualizer for Failed Requests

text: to reduce clutter Redaction: mark fields as redacted and show structure but not values by default; allow role-based reveal in Private deployments Default view uses timeline orientation (left-to-right) with option to switch to stacked list for long paths open_questions: How should we represent KV cache hits vs partial hits for multi-shard requests? Should retries be grouped into a single collapsible node or listed individually for timeline fidelity? What RBAC level is required to view unredacted payloads in Dedicated vs Hosted? tasks: Finalize hi-fi mocks for timeline and expanded detail pane Define hover states and microcopy for cost and redaction indicators Create accessible keyboard interaction spec and ARIA annotations Hand off tokens and annotation assets to Eng for prototype backend endpoints (trace API + payload-safe view) Run 2 usability sessions with SRE and Platform engineers and iterate comments: 2025-02-16 - Ava Patel: Kickoff notes — scoped to failed requests first (500/502/timeout) and model error classes; prefer incremental rollout behind feature flag. 2025-02-21 - Miguel Ramos: Technical feasibility check — ENG can expose per-hop metadata via trace API; need contract for 'cost_estimate' and 'redaction_map'. Creating ENG-33021 for API schema. 2025-02-25 - Sofia Nguyen: UX review — prefer inline sparklines for latency distribution per-hop instead of a separate modal to speed triage. 2025-02-28 - Priya Malhotra: Security note — payload inspector must default to redacted
title: Debug Path Visualizer for Failed Requests
